1.1 — Introduction | First Principles | Marshall v. Green (1875) ⇧ Index
🌳 "A tree is actually a warehouse of the fruits on it" — Marshall v. Green (1875) 1 CPD 35
The foundational axiom of this entire work on Allied Laws & GST
📝 This statement seems bewildering — but it is a legal truth declared by a court in 1875 and never disturbed since. A standing tree is itself a 'warehouse' of the fruits/timber on it — which are already 'goods' even before being severed. The point: legal reality is often counterintuitive, and language skills alone will mislead you.
THE CORE PRINCIPLE
When substance of any legislation concerns itself with goods — ascertaining whether that substance IS goods or not — flows from elucidation of tests established as 'first principles'
Whether those tests reside in Contract law or Property law
Whether application is for Income-Tax or GST purposes — they are inalienable
📝 'First principles' = foundational legal tests built up over centuries. They cannot be set aside merely because a new law (GST 2017) has been enacted — unless the new law makes a deliberate, express departure.
THE RULE OF DEPARTURE
UNLESS definition in GST law can be shown to make a deliberate departure from these tests founded on first principles — interpretation must be seasoned with authorities traceable to legislations as far back as 1872
Authorities left undisturbed since 1875 → apply to this new law of 2017
📝 GST is new — but its foundational concepts (goods, sale, transfer, service, lease) are old. Courts have built up a body of law since 1872. That law governs GST interpretation unless GST explicitly says otherwise.
1.2 — Anomalous State of Financial Statements ⇧ Index
📊 Financial Statements ≠ Facts | Disclosure ≠ Truth
📝 Financial statements contain only accounting treatment and disclosure — not the actual facts of the transaction. Relying on them for GST purposes misleads far more than it helps. Mismatch between financial statements and regulatory filings is NOT necessarily evidence of something being amiss.
WHAT FINANCIALS CONTAIN
Disclosure and accounting treatment of transactions
Taxpayers' assertions on facts about concluded transactions — tempered to meet needs of users of financial statements
Validated by external auditors under mandatory best practices/standards
📝 Auditors validate based on accounting standards — not legal substance. Financial statements are prepared for investors, creditors, regulatory filings — NOT for GST.
DANGERS OF RELIANCE ON FINANCIALS
Mismatch of data in financial statements vs regulatory filings = NOT indicator that anything is amiss
Reliance on financials = more disservice to detection of revenue leakage than help
New accounting standards (harmonized with IFRS) present taxable transactions EVEN LESS reliably than before
WHAT HAS EVIDENTIARY VALUE
Facts asserted before another regulator (Income Tax, SEBI, ROC, RBI, labour courts) — especially when GST is administered by those regulators
Assertions made in comparable legislations = invaluable evidentiary value
Technology-driven exchange of information making this comparison increasingly powerful
📝 A taxpayer's position taken before the Income Tax authority (about the nature of a transaction) has high probative value for GST — because both are private matters' legislations with comparable rules of construction.
⚠️ WARNING Taxpayers making a beeline to submit new material not forming evidence in support of allegations in notice = the fearful or ignorant. Allegations are no more than suspicion and wild guesswork. More years of this and GST will have been done in by administration.
1.3 — Facts are Inviolable | The Ultimate Shield ⇧ Index
🛡️ Facts are Inviolable — No Defence is Greater or Purer than Defence Based on Facts
📝 Facts are NOT what one believes to be true. Facts are NOT the reasons why something happened. Facts are assertions made — and the treatment of law turns on those facts. This is the most powerful concept in the entire book.
What Facts Are and Are Not
FACTS ARE NOT
What one believes to be true
The reasons why something transpired
What one would like it to be
Treatment of law
FACTS ARE
Assertions made → treatment of law turns on those facts
What can be proved
What bears relevance and establishes existence (or non-existence) of a thing
Assertions left undisputed by both sides → treatment in law becomes indisputable
FACTS & PROOF
Facts may be disputed → need proof
Undisputed facts do NOT require proof
Allegations + unimpeachable evidence = admittance as facts
Allegations left undisputed = admittance as facts
APPROBATE AND REPROBATE — KEY RULE
Taxpayer CANNOT approbate and reprobate as to the assertion on facts — things, state of things or existence (or non-existence) of those things or their state.

When taxpayer has asserted the existence of a certain thing (or state of things) before ONE regulator as a fact → for purpose of ANY other legislation → those facts remain UNALTERED → treatment attendant to those facts will FOLLOW as mandated under each of those legislations.
📝 Approbate and reprobate = you cannot blow hot and cold. If you told Income Tax that your transaction was a 'sale of services' — you cannot tell GST that the same transaction was a 'sale of goods'. Your earlier assertion binds you — and Revenue can cite it against you.
PRESUMPTION RULE Presumption runs AGAINST taxpayer's interpretation when:
(i) Two alternative tax treatments are possible (even if not permissible) AND
(ii) That adopted by taxpayer is more advantageous

Burden to impeach taxpayer's self-assessment rests on Revenue — though taxpayer is not obliged to disprove anything, is exposed to suffer the due process of challenge.
THE GOLD STANDARD
No defence is greater or purer than the defence based on facts.
No challenge bearing any innovative interpretation of law can prevail against facts that are inviolably established.

With house-keeping done — taxpayers may either revise self-assessment or stand their ground when challenge comes, because it will.
1.4 — Legal Fiction | Exhaustive, Not Illustrative | Boundaries of Schedule II ⇧ Index
🎭 Legal Fiction — Alteration of Facts, NOT Treatment | Express and Exhaustive
📝 Legal fiction is NOT the 'treatment' of a transaction according to legislative will. It is the ALTERATION OF FACTS before subjecting that transaction to the treatment otherwise applicable (to those altered facts). Example: Lease of goods is 'treated as services' — the facts are altered (it becomes 'services') and then the treatment applicable to services applies.
CANNOT
Legal fiction CANNOT be extrapolated as if illustrative → CANNOT extend to more situations than expressed
Legal fiction CANNOT be shorted → full extent of 'treatment' applicable to altered facts must be allowed
Legal fiction CANNOT be implied or imagined — howsoever logical or essential it may seem in a given context
📝 If legal fiction says 'lease of goods is treated as supply of services' — you cannot extend this to mean 'all leases are exempt like immovable property services'. The fiction applies only where expressly stated.
MUST
Legal fiction MUST be applied only for the 'purposes' for which it is provided — not extended beyond
Legal fiction is always exhaustive — unless (perilously) expressed to be illustrative
Full force of legislative mandate must be exerted — cannot be constrained
SCHEDULE II — CGST ACT — KEY EXAMPLE
Lease of goods = required to be 'treated' as supply of services due to legal fiction
BUT: will only be admitted as 'services' when any given provision demands 'treatment' to be applied — not so in other provisions where they continue to operate as 'goods'
💡 Boundary Example 1: E-way bill requirement — NOT a 'treatment'. Movement of goods under lease arrangement ALSO requires adherence to rigours of Section 68 and consequences of Section 129 CGST Act — despite legal fiction of 'services'.

💡 Boundary Example 2: Section 13(2)(b) — treatment of advance received. When lease rental received in advance — notwithstanding that object of supply are goods — treatment of advances received will NOT avail the exclusion available qua supply of goods vide Notification 66/2017-Central Tax dated 15 Nov. 2017.
Ut res magis valeat quam pereat — Aid to statutory construction where interpretation is carried out such that provision finds purpose for its existence and SURVIVES in the statute — rather than be rendered nugatory, superfluous and destroyed entirely.

Maxwell on The Interpretation of Statutes (12th Ed.): "In an Act of Parliament there are no such things as brackets any more than there are such things as stops. Before 1850 there was no punctuation in the manuscript copy of an Act which received the Royal Assent..."

Punctuation marks in statutes are still not reliably used or understood — cannot be the root cause of prevailing misinterpretation.
1.5 — Welfare Matters' Legislations | Purposive Interpretation | Not Comparable with GST ⇧ Index
⚖️ Welfare Matters' Legislations — Purposive Interpretation | Keep at Distance from GST
📝 Some laws exist to protect weaker parties — tenants, employees, consumers, beneficiaries. They are interpreted purposively (to advance the welfare object). GST is NOT a welfare legislation. It is a tax legislation subject to strict interpretation. Therefore, facts asserted under welfare legislations CANNOT be imported into GST.

WELFARE MATTERS' LEGISLATIONS
Object = redress matters of welfare
Interpreted by purposive rule
State speaks for weaker Party who is also beneficiary-cum-victim
Relationships between Parties of unequal bargaining power
📝 Examples: Rent Control Act, Labour Laws, Consumer Protection Act, RERA. These protect tenants, workers, consumers — State steps in for the weaker party.
GST / PRIVATE MATTERS' LEGISLATIONS
Object = regulation of private commercial relations / taxation
Interpreted by strict rule
Both Parties are knowledgeable, generally equal
Except to extent of anti-avoidance provisions
CRITICAL RULE — Welfare Legislation Facts = UNRELIABLE for GST
Taking assertion on facts under welfare matters' legislations and superimposing them in GST context to compel a tax treatment on inadmissible facts = misuse of that legislation + misapplication of GST legislation

When a legislation is identified as 'welfare matters' legislation → facts asserted and admitted there must be isolated and kept at a distance and considered unreliable for use in applying any treatment in GST.

Assertions on facts made in welfare matters' legislations are of little, if not any, assistance to illuminate the understanding of GST treatment.
FREEDOM OF CONTRACT Indian Contract Act: freedom (to contract) — NOT prudence (in contracting) — is the assurance. Courts must enforce the bargain Parties have reached and cannot supplant their own wisdom to that of the Parties, however imprudent those terms may appear to be.
1.6 — Private Matters' Legislations | Strict Interpretation | Comparable with GST ⇧ Index
🔒 Private Matters' Legislations — Strict Interpretation | Comparable with GST | Both Apples Test
📝 Private matters' legislations are those whose object is to redress matters of private interest — contract between two parties, taxation, property transactions. These are interpreted STRICTLY. GST is such a legislation. Assertions made under comparable private matters' legislations can be cited against taxpayer in GST — and vice versa.
WHY STRICT INTERPRETATION
Object = interests of specific, finite, knowledgeable and generally small population
Even when State is one of these stakeholders
Plain words employed by Legislature must be relied upon
Substitute express words with reader's fancies = affront to legislative labour
📝 'Strictness' is not hyper-technical or illogical — it is being true to the will and labour of Legislature. The best approach to advance the cause of private matters' legislations.
COMPARISON — 'BOTH APPLES' TEST
Select legislations for comparison: examine comparability FIRST
Both must be private matters' legislations
Both must have comparable rules of construction
→ THEN assertions on facts under one = valid to test/validate assertions under the other
📝 You can compare Income Tax assertions with GST assertions — both are private matters' legislations with strict interpretation. But you CANNOT compare RERA (welfare) assertions with GST — they are NOT both apples.
HOW TECHNOLOGY HAS CHANGED THIS
Unprecedented use of technology + native GST compliance = exchange of information efficient and default feature
API integration: comparison of contemporaneous data held by multiple information utilities = remarkable insights from assertions by taxpayers before various regulators
The 'race car of data mining' is coming
⚠️ CURRENT MISUSE Spate of demands raised based almost entirely on linear comparison of data held within different wings of Department of Revenue = 'armchair investigation'. This is far less intelligent than isolating private matters' legislations to locate assertions that can vindicate or annihilate the tax position adopted in GST.
1.7 — Golden Rule of Interpretation | Blackstonian Doctrine ⇧ Index
🏆 Golden Rule — Judiciary Declares What Interpretation Law Always Had
📝 The 'Golden Rule' of interpretation: a Judge does NOT lay down the law — the Judge lays down the INTERPRETATION THAT THE LAW ALWAYS HAD, and is only declared so now. This is Blackstonian doctrine. The law did not change on the day of the judgment — only our understanding of it was declared.
WHY 'GOLDEN'
Vests judiciary with power to declare the interpretation that any given statutory provision ought to have always been allowed
Judge declares — does not create
Strictness is 'golden' in interpretation of private matters' legislations
📝 This means: a 2024 Supreme Court judgment on a legal question is NOT 'new law' from 2024. It is a declaration of what the law meant since it was enacted. Retroactive in effect.
LIMITS OF INTERPRETATION
Very little room to 'feed missing pieces' in private matters' legislations
Separation of Judiciary and Legislature: legislation must be sent back to Legislature to restate original legislative intention
NOT permitted to use outlier words to 'rewrite' legislation in course of interpreting it
TRANSACTIONS — IN OR OUT
Transactions that fall within the pith of the legislative object = exposed to prescribed treatment
Transactions that fall outside the legislation = remain ousted from the treatment
Revenue cannot alter character of a transaction or 'pick bits out' to use them to impose a burden
AG v. Noyes (1881) 8 QBD 125 — Grove J
"The word 'evasion' may mean either of two things. It may mean an evasion of the Act by something which, while it evades the Act, is within the sense of it, or it may mean an evading of the Act by doing something to which the Act does not apply."

Revenue cannot pick bits out of a transaction and use them to foist a burden and reject others.
IRS v. Duke of Westminster (1936) AC 1 (HL) → Furniss v. Dawson (1984)
Development of judicial thought to lend support in matters of taxation — anti-avoidance carve out from strict interpretation rule — brought about where artificial device inserted in factual matrix to overcome treatment otherwise applicable which has no commercial purpose for existence but exists only to bring unjust advantage to perpetrator.
OECD BEPS: facts — strictly interpreted — cannot be easily jettisoned to deny tax advantages that the very statute permits.
1.8 — Judicial Notice | Section 56 Indian Evidence Act | Official Notice ⇧ Index
👁️ Judicial Notice — Section 56 Indian Evidence Act | Cross-Regulator Evidentiary Value
📝 Courts and quasi-judicial authorities can 'take notice' of the state of things — law, science, history, public policy — WITHOUT requiring evidence to be formally placed on record. This extends to assertions made by taxpayers before other regulators — under comparable legislations.
WHAT CAN BE NOTICED
Section 56 permits Court to take judicial notice of:
Law, science, history, extant public policy
Includes: assertions made by taxpayer before regulators under legislations whose rules of construction are comparable to tax legislation
OFFICIAL NOTICE — ADMINISTRATIVE AUTHORITIES
If judicial notice is permitted → official notice also permitted by Executive functionaries
Matters coming within operation of Section 56 need not find a place in the NOTICE but can be pressed into service in adjudication without violating Section 75(7) or in appeal without violating Section 107(11) CGST Act
📝 Adjudicating Officers and Appellate Authorities can take 'official notice' of taxpayer's assertions before other regulators — without those facts having been mentioned in the SCN/notice. This is a powerful tool for Revenue — and a risk for taxpayers who take inconsistent positions.
PROBATIVE VALUE
Statutory authorities permitted to derive evidence from assertions by taxpayer before any other regulator → 'high' probative value if they touch matters of interest to treatment applicable in GST
Just by showing other legislation is pari materia → Revenue satisfactorily discharges burden of proof to bring home allegation
PRACTICAL IMPLICATION Revenue can use taxpayer's own assertions from Income Tax/SEBI/ROC/RBI/Labour filings to support GST demand. Taxpayer can use their own consistent assertions from comparable legislations as defence.

Examples:
• Registration/non-registration of instrument creating interest in immovable property = relevant to ascribe certain meaning to contractual privity between Landowner and Developer
• Existence of master-servant relationship in Income Tax compliance = can guide course of treatment in locating reverse charge liability on the Company
1.9 — Divergence Essential | When Different Facts Before Different Regulators is Permissible ⇧ Index
🔀 Divergence Essential — When Two Legislations are Incomparable
📝 It is erroneous for taxpayer (OR Revenue) to attempt uniformity when two legislations are ex facie incomparable due to divergence of their 'objects'. If the objects are different — divergent facts before different regulators raises NO presumption of mala fides.
WHEN DIVERGENCE IS IMPERMISSIBLE
Both legislations are comparable (private matters') with similar rules of construction AND
Taxpayer asserts divergent facts AND
Advantage to taxpayer is unmistakable
→ Falsehood and perjury
📝 If you told Income Tax (a private matters' legislation comparable to GST) that the transaction was 'X' — you cannot tell GST the same transaction was 'Y' — especially if 'Y' gives you a GST advantage.
WHEN DIVERGENCE IS PERMISSIBLE
Objects of the two legislations are NOT the same
No imputation of mala fides due to apparent divergence
Where welfare matters' legislation is involved → taxpayer allowed latitude to assert divergent facts for purposes of GST
📝 If you told RERA (welfare legislation) something about your real estate project — GST cannot import those assertions as binding facts. The two legislations are incomparable — objects are different.
TEST TO IMPUTE MALA FIDES
Revenue must FIRST show: objects of both legislations are comparable with GST + both are private matters' legislations
In rebuttal: sufficient for taxpayer to justify that the other legislation relates to welfare matters

If Revenue cannot show this → any consequent advantage to taxpayer raises NO presumption against taxpayer's self-assessment — even when staring at divergent assertions on matters-of-fact.
1.10 — Imputation by Revenue | What Revenue Can and Cannot Do ⇧ Index
🔍 Imputation by Revenue — Facts vs Allegations | Revenue Cannot Introduce Facts
📝 Disproving taxpayer's self-assessment is NOT the same as feeding facts to support Revenue's interpretation. Revenue cannot manufacture facts. Facts must be discovered in investigation. Revenue's role is to find facts from taxpayer's own assertions — not to create them.
WHAT REVENUE CANNOT DO
Introduce facts that are not there
Make imputations of facts to a transaction
Feed facts to support Revenue's interpretation
Pick bits of a transaction and use them to impose burden while rejecting rest
📝 Revenue cannot say: "We believe this is a works contract" without finding facts that establish it. Belief is not a fact. Investigation must discover facts that are out there.
WHAT REVENUE CAN DO
Discover facts in investigation that unseat omissions in course of self-assessment
Cite taxpayer's own assertions before regulators of pari materia legislations
Use those assertions as 'high probative value' evidence to furnish missing pieces to erect edifice of demand
📝 Deft investigative prowess dips into taxpayer's own assertions before other regulators — the same facts, asserted elsewhere, become the building blocks of the GST demand.
ALLEGATION → FACT CONVERSION
Allegations become facts when:
(i) Well supported by evidence establishing existence/non-existence of things
(ii) Left undisputed

BUT: disputed allegations demolish reliability of any tax treatment canvassed via those allegations
1.11 — Both Wrong Answers | Adversarial Litigation | Notice on Trial ⇧ Index
⚖️ Both Wrong Answers — No Compulsion That One View Must Be Right
📝 In adversarial litigation, there is NO compulsion that one of the two rival views must be right. It is easily possible that BOTH are wrong. The right view may evade both sides for the time being. This must be accepted.
ADVERSARIAL LITIGATION FRAMEWORK
Adversarial litigation = 'notice on trial' — NOT taxpayer on trial
Adjudication and appellate remedy in GST = permitted only to examine the grounds and NOT the underlying transaction
Appellate Authority is NOT an Adjudicating Authority of higher rank
📝 The SCN/notice is what is on trial — not the taxpayer's integrity. If the grounds of the notice are bad — the notice fails — even if the right demand could have been made on different grounds.
TAXPAYER'S RIGHTS
Not obliged to 'bring forth the truth' or 'establish their innocence'
Welcome to entertain an interpretation that yields the least tax consequence
If challenged by alternate view → dismissal would NOT be violative of Constitutional mandate (No tax without authority of law)
📝 Tax omitted to be levied when authority of law existed = does NOT violate any Constitutional mandate. Taxpayer is free to take the least-tax position — Revenue must challenge it through due process, subject to limitation.
CURATIVE ATTEMPT
To uphold one of the two views = make a curative attempt of the grounds on which demand is made
Dismissal of any one view could easily result in omission to levy tax lawfully leviable
Such an outcome is acceptable in law even if not desirable
STATE'S CONSTITUTIONAL POSITION
No tax shall be levied without authority of law → doing so will be ultra vires the Constitutional mandate.
But tax omitted to be levied when authority of law existed = does no such violation.

Only by due process and subject to limitation can the State intervene. View adopted and view canvassed can both be wrong — the right view may evade both sides for the time being.
1.12 — References to Legislations | Scope of this Work ⇧ Index
📚 References — Scope, Limitations & Purpose of this Work
REFERENCES IN THIS WORK
Unless stated otherwise: reference to 'GST' = Central GST Act
In each chapter: reference to 'section' = statutory provision in THAT chapter's legislation (NOT CGST)
Examples = to illustrate the point of deliberation — NOT interpretation of universal application across diverse factual matrices
SCOPE OF DELIBERATIONS
NOT comprehensive analysis of allied laws considered
Merely an attempt to expose implications of interpretation applicable under those laws to the extent they bear relevance to GST and the treatment it allows
Large portions of allied laws omitted — not in the order those laws warrant
PURPOSE
To illuminate insights from allied laws that validate or annihilate their application in GST
All inadequacies are in this work and beg to be excused
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 1 | Background | Jurisprudential Foundations ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name. Chapter 1 principles are powerful weapons against SCNs based on financial statement data, cross-regulator allegation misuse, or overreach of legal fiction.
Marshall v. Green (1875) 1 CPD 35 — First Principles Doctrine
Common Law | Applicable in all GST disputes involving characterization of goods, standing crops, embedded property, tree-fruit-warehouse principle
Key Ratio / Principle
A tree is actually a warehouse of the fruits on it. This truth — about tree, fruit and warehouse — authoritatively declared in 1875 and left undisturbed since, is as applicable to GST 2017 as it was to the law of 1875. Unless definition in GST law can be shown to make a deliberate departure from tests founded on first principles, interpretation must be seasoned with authorities traceable to legislations as far back as 1872 and authorities left undisturbed since 1875 into this new law of 2017.
When to Use — (i) When Department classifies a transaction ignoring settled jurisprudence from Contract/Property law — argue: unless GST expressly departs from first principles, those principles govern. (ii) When Department applies a novel interpretation not supported by settled case law — cite Marshall v. Green for the proposition that 150 years of settled law cannot be overridden by mere administrative convenience. (iii) In any dispute about the nature of goods — standing crops, attached goods, tree produce — as a foundational authority.
Approbate and Reprobate Doctrine — Facts are Inviolable
Common Law Principle | Applicable in all cross-regulator GST disputes | ITC claims, nature of supply, RCM liability
Key Principle
Taxpayer cannot approbate and reprobate as to the assertion on facts — things, state of things or existence (or non-existence) of those things or their state. When taxpayer has asserted the existence of a certain thing (or state of things) before one regulator as a fact, then for purpose of any other legislation, those facts remain unaltered and the treatment attendant to those facts will follow as mandated under each of those legislations. No defence is greater or purer than the defence based on facts. No challenge bearing any innovative interpretation of law can prevail against facts that are inviolably established.
When to Use — (i) When Revenue cites taxpayer's Income Tax filing to support GST demand — DEFENCE: argue that the two legislations have different objects and rules of construction; show the other legislation is a welfare matters' legislation incomparable with GST. (ii) OFFENCE: cite taxpayer's consistent assertions before Income Tax (a comparable private matters' legislation) as binding facts supporting the GST position. (iii) When SCN is based on wild guesswork or unsubstantiated allegations — remind that allegations are no more than suspicion; facts are what can be proved.
Legal Fiction — Express and Exhaustive | Schedule II CGST Act
Statutory Principle — Central GST Act, 2017 | Applicable in e-way bill disputes, time of supply for lease, advance tax treatment
Key Principle
Legal fiction is the alteration of 'facts' before subjecting that transaction to the treatment otherwise applicable. Legislative fiction cannot be extrapolated as if it were illustrative, allowing to extend it to more situations than expressed. Legislative fiction is always exhaustive unless (perilously) expressing it to be illustrative. Legislative fiction cannot be shorted — the full extent of 'treatment' applicable to the altered facts must be allowed. And the legislative fiction must be applied only for the 'purposes' for which it is provided and not extended beyond. E-way bill not being a 'treatment' — movement of goods under a lease arrangement requires adherence to Section 68 and consequences of Section 129, notwithstanding the legal fiction of Schedule II para 1(b).
When to Use — (i) When Revenue seeks to extend Schedule II legal fiction beyond its stated purposes — e.g., claiming that all lease transactions are exempt from e-way bill requirements because they are 'services'. Cite: fiction applies only for specific purposes — not universally. (ii) When arguing about time of supply for advance lease rental — fiction of Section 13(2)(b) applies; exclusion under Notification 66/2017 for goods does not avail because fiction treats it as services. (iii) When Department tries to imply a legal fiction not expressly stated — argue: legal fiction cannot be implied or imagined howsoever logical it may seem.
AG v. Noyes (1881) 8 QBD 125 — Grove J | Revenue Cannot Pick Bits of Transaction
Queen's Bench Division, England | Applicable in ALL GST SCN/demand disputes where Revenue selectively uses parts of a transaction
Key Ratio / Principle
The word 'evasion' may mean either of two things. It may mean an evasion of the Act by something which, while it evades the Act, is within the sense of it, or it may mean an evading of the Act by doing something to which the Act does not apply. Revenue cannot alter the character of a transaction or to pick bits out of it and use them to foist a burden and reject others. Transactions that fall outside of the legislation remain ousted from the treatment. Strictness is 'golden' in interpretation of private matters' legislations and transactions that fall within the pith of the legislative object will be exposed to the prescribed treatment.
When to Use — (i) When SCN selectively picks parts of a composite transaction to attract tax while ignoring the rest — argue: Revenue cannot pick bits out of a transaction and use them to foist a burden. The whole transaction must be examined. (ii) When Revenue argues 'tax avoidance' based on purposive interpretation of GST — counter: GST is a strict interpretation legislation; purposive rule applies only in welfare matters' legislations. (iii) When challenging demands based on GSTR-2A/2B data comparison without examining the underlying transaction in full.
Section 56 — Indian Evidence Act | Judicial Notice / Official Notice | Pari Materia Doctrine
Indian Evidence Act, 1872 | Applicable in GST adjudication and appeals — cross-regulator assertion use
Key Principle
Section 56 of Indian Evidence Act permits a Court to take 'judicial notice' of the state of things — law, science, history or extant public policy — which includes assertions made by taxpayer of the facts or of things implying facts before regulators under legislations whose rules of construction are comparable to those used for construction of tax legislations. If judicial notice is permitted, official notice is also permitted to be taken by functionaries of the Executive. Matters coming within the operation of this section, even if the assertion which can be 'taken official notice of' need not find a place in the notice, but can be pressed into service in adjudication without violating section 75(7) or in appeal without violating section 107(11) of Central GST Act. Just by showing the other legislation to be pari materia, Revenue will have satisfactorily discharged its burden of proof.
When to Use — (i) DEFENCE — when Revenue cites taxpayer's assertions before another regulator without placing those documents formally in evidence — object that they need to demonstrate the other legislation is pari materia (comparable private matters' legislation) before they can rely on such assertions. (ii) OFFENCE — cite taxpayer's own Income Tax return / RERA filing / company law filing showing consistent facts to defeat an adverse SCN. (iii) When opposing SCN based purely on linear data comparison from GSTR/financial statements — argue that this is armchair investigation, not discovery of facts; allegations supported only by data mismatch are no more than suspicion.
Welfare vs Private Matters' Legislations — Divergence Doctrine
Jurisprudential Principle — Chapter 1, Background | Applicable whenever Revenue imports facts from RERA, Labour Laws, Rent Control, Consumer Forums into GST
Key Principle
Taking assertion on facts under welfare matters' legislations and superimposing them in the GST context to compel a tax treatment on inadmissible facts would be misuse of that legislation as well as misapplication of the GST legislation. When a legislation is identified to be welfare matters' legislation — then facts asserted and admitted there must be isolated and kept at a distance and considered unreliable for use in applying any treatment in GST. It is erroneous for taxpayer (or Revenue too) to attempt uniformity when the two legislations are ex facie incomparable due to divergence of their 'objects'. To impute falsehood and perjury, it would be necessary for Revenue to show that the objects are comparable with that of GST and both are private matters' legislations. And in rebuttal, sufficient for taxpayer to justify that the other legislation relates to welfare matters.
When to Use — (i) When Revenue imports RERA facts (e.g., classification of real estate project, nature of agreement) into GST demand — argue: RERA is a welfare matters' legislation (protects allottees); GST is a private matters' legislation with strict interpretation; the two are not comparable; RERA assertions cannot bind GST treatment. (ii) When Revenue uses Rent Control/Tenancy Act facts to characterize a lease for GST — argue: those legislations protect tenants (welfare); GST governs commercial transactions (private); incomparable. (iii) When taxpayer has divergent positions before RERA and GST — defend: the objects are incomparable — divergence is ESSENTIAL and raises no presumption of mala fides.
Notice on Trial — Not the Taxpayer | Both Wrong Answers Doctrine
Adversarial Litigation Principle | Applicable in all GST adjudication and appeals — Section 73/74 proceedings
Key Principle
Adversarial litigation is 'notice on trial' — and if the view adopted by taxpayer challenged by an alternate view canvassed could end with dismissal and that would not be violative of the Constitutional mandate to the State. No tax shall be levied without authority of law and doing so will be ultra vires this Constitutional mandate. But tax omitted to be levied when authority of law existed will do no such violation. Adjudication and appellate remedy in GST are permitted only to examine the grounds and not the underlying transaction. Appellate Authority is not an Adjudicating Authority of higher rank. Taxpayers are not obliged to 'bring forth the truth' or 'establish their innocence'. View adopted and view canvassed can both be wrong — and the right view may evade both sides for the time being.
When to Use — (i) At personal hearing — when Adjudicating Authority suggests taxpayer should 'explain the full picture' beyond what the SCN alleges — object: the notice is on trial, not the taxpayer; adjudication must examine grounds of the notice, not the underlying transaction. (ii) When Revenue raises entirely new grounds in the Order not mentioned in the SCN — argue: Appellate Authority is not an Adjudicating Authority of higher rank and cannot examine the underlying transaction; Order must be set aside. (iii) When both Department and taxpayer may have incorrect views — argue: the Constitutional mandate prohibits levying tax without authority of law; but tax omitted when authority exists is not a Constitutional violation. Taxpayer's least-tax interpretation is permissible and can only be overridden by due process subject to limitation.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 2a — Contract Act (Overview)

⚖️ Chapter 2 — Contract Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
2.1 Relevance to GST
2.2.1 Validity of Contract (§10)
2.2.2 Ostensible Competence (§11)
2.2.3 Lawful Authority
2.2.4 Economic Substance & Distinct Person
2.3.1 Existence by Formation
2.3.2 Counter-Offer
2.3.3 to 2.3.6 Manner / Implied / Form / Enforceability
2.3.7 Unequal Bargaining & Contra Proferentem
2.3.8 to 2.3.13 Statutory / Silence / Auction / Title
2.4.1 to 2.4.3 Consideration — Essential & Equitable
2.4.4 to 2.4.7 Absence / Existence / Stranger / Non-payment
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.1 — Relevance to GST | Vinculum Juris ⇧ Index
📜 Contract Act — Relevance to GST
Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Promulgated 145 years before GST
Every 'form' of supply in GST is one or other kind of contract
Transaction failing ingredients of valid contract → CANNOT come within GST treatment
📝 Every GST transaction — sale, service, lease, barter — is a contract at its core. If the underlying contract is invalid, the GST treatment simply does not apply. No valid contract = no taxable supply.
LATIN
Vinculum Juris
= Bond of legal obligations that contracting parties willingly enter into
📝 Vinculum juris literally means "chain of law." It is the invisible legal bond that ties two parties to their contract. It is voluntary — parties choose to be bound.
PRINCIPLE
Not for anyone — not even a Court — to deny Parties their bargain and its assistance in default
📝 Once parties freely agree, courts must honour the bargain. Courts cannot rewrite contracts just because one party is unhappy later.
SCOPE
Transactions involving supply flow out of commercial arrangements that are contractual — with some exceptions created by law
💡 GST on deemed supplies (Schedule I) = law creating contractual fiction where parties may not have contracted. Legal fiction overrides; no capacity argument runs.
RISK
No student of GST law can graduate without deep understanding of Contract law
2.2.1 — Validity of Contract | Section 10 ⇧ Index
✅ Section 10 — What Agreements are Contracts?
All agreements are contracts IF all ingredients present
Four Essential Ingredients for Valid Contract
①
Free Consent
of parties
②
Competent
to contract
③
Lawful
Consideration
④
Lawful Object
not void by law
PRESUMPTION
Where contract is purported to be enforceable → presumption runs in favour of validity unless some incurable or inseverable invalidity is discovered (minority, mistake etc.)
📝 Law presumes a contract is valid unless proven otherwise. The party challenging must show specifically what invalidity exists — vague allegations do not destroy a contract.
KEY DISTINCTION
Contract = not the 'form' of the document
but the 'consensus' about the object and expectation as to its enforceability in the event of breach or threatened breach
📝 It does not matter what you name the document. What matters is: did both parties agree on the same thing? Would a court enforce it if one party breaks the deal? THAT is the contract.
💡 A document titled "Memorandum of Understanding" between two parties who have agreed on price, quantity and delivery terms — is a binding contract. The label "MOU" is irrelevant. GST applies on the underlying taxable supply.
2.2.2 — Ostensible Competence | Section 11 | Distinct Person ⇧ Index
👤 Section 11 — Who is Competent to Contract?
STATUTE
Every person competent to contract who is:
(i) of age of majority | (ii) of sound mind | (iii) not disqualified by any law
✗ MINORS
Courts cannot allow relief to minors — would impute capacity to them
Contracts with minors = void ab initio due to incapacity
📝 A contract with a minor is dead from birth — not just voidable later. Even a court trying to be fair cannot pretend the minor had capacity to contract.
BUT
Courts may mould tortious relief outside contract law — on equitable principles — as Court sees fit, NOT as per terms of void contract
📝 Even though the contract is void, a court may still give some compensation to prevent unjust enrichment — but based on fairness, not on the contract terms.
GST — DISTINCT PERSON
'Distinct persons' are imputed with capacity to contract in GST law
Contractual relations imposed in: (i) §9(5) or (ii) Para 3, Schedule I
📝 In GST, different branches/registrations of the same company (same PAN) are treated as separate legal entities ("distinct persons"). GST law creates deemed contracts between them even if the branches never formally contracted.
💡 Head office in Delhi provides services to factory in Kanpur — GST deems this a supply between distinct persons even though both belong to the same company. Cross-charge is mandatory.
2.2.3 — Lawful Authority to Contract ⇧ Index
🔑 Lawful Authority — Prerequisite to Contract
RULE
Unless legitimate authority exists → no person can assert to be contracting for that object
Authority extends to both: Contractor AND Contractee (Recipient §2(93) CGST)
📝 Not just the supplier — even the receiver must have lawful authority to receive/procure what is being contracted. Without this authority, the contract itself is questionable and GST treatment cannot be assumed.
✓ WITH AUTHORITY
Person with lawful authority providing poor quality care → admitted within the definition
📝 Even if the quality is bad, legal authority is enough to bring the activity within the definition of supply. Quality is not the issue — authority is.
✗ WITHOUT AUTHORITY
Person providing high quality care but lacking lawful authority → NOT admitted
Admission into something presupposes existence of lawful authority
📝 Doing the right thing, the right way — but without legal authority — still disqualifies the activity from the relevant GST treatment. Authority is the threshold, not performance quality.
GST PRINCIPLE
Asserting any claim (clinical, charitable, educational) demands that person hold the necessary legal authority to support that assertion. Without lawful authority → nothing further remains to be examined.
💡 Eg.1: CA holding Certificate of Practice alone has lawful authority to discharge duties as Statutory Auditor — even if work is done by employees or sub-contractors. The authority vests in the CA, not in the worker.
2.2.4 — Economic Substance | Legal Fiction | Distinct Person (§2.2.5) ⇧ Index
🏛️ Economic Substance | Legislative Fiction | Distinct Person
Legislative Fiction
Not: fictional treatment of law
But: (i) alteration of facts by such fiction + (ii) subjecting altered facts to treatment applicable
📝 A legal fiction doesn't change the law — it changes the facts. GST treats distinct persons as if they were independent companies, then applies normal GST rules to those "altered" facts.
RULE
Where legislative fiction operates → No questions can be raised as to lack of capacity to contract
📝 Once law creates a fiction (like "distinct person"), you cannot argue "but we are the same company — no capacity to contract." The fiction overrides. Pay the GST.
💡 Eg.2: IGST Explanations 1 & 3 to §8 deny zero-rated benefit for export of services by distinct person in India to establishment outside India → deemed supply, RCM applies.
Distinct Person — Legal Fiction in GST
GST introduces sub-category of Legal Entity → elevates to status of independent Entity → calls it 'distinct person' (same PAN, different GSTIN)
Transactions inter se are liable to tax treatment 'as if' the transacting distinct persons were two distinct Legal Entities
Each transaction must be explained based on: (i) nature of transaction (ii) object of supply implicit (iii) imputed valuation
💡 Eg.5 & 6: HO in Delhi sends raw material to factory in Kanpur — costs incurred by HO cannot be retained by HO; must be charged to factory under suitable tariff classification with applicable GST.
Partnership Firm
Partnership firm is NOT a person
It is a collective noun for all Partners
Contracting in name of firm = contract between Contractee and each Partner acting through instrumentality of any one of them for all
📝 Unlike a company, a partnership firm has no separate legal existence. A contract with "M/s XYZ Firm" is really a contract with all the partners individually. Each partner is personally liable.
2.3.1 — Existence by Formation | Offer & Acceptance ⇧ Index
🤝 Formation of Contract — How a Contract Comes Into Being
Contract formed when Offer is Accepted — acceptance is merely of the terms offered, cannot alter them
TO LOCATE TAXABLE SUPPLY
→ First locate the 'Offer'
→ Acceptance merely of these terms
📝 In GST, the taxable supply arises from the contract. To find when and what was supplied, look for the moment of offer and acceptance — that is when the legal obligation was born.
NON-PAYMENT OF GST
Very often attributed to non-existence of contract on account of non-formation
💡 Eg.7: To explain non-taxability of payment for breach of contract — must show non-existence of contract with this as the object of the bargain.
Contract can be for 'not doing' — Abstinence is valid offer [§2(d) — consideration includes abstinence]. Unwritten contracts are no less enforceable than written contracts.
📝 A contract to NOT compete, NOT disclose, NOT build on certain land — "not doing" something is as valid as "doing." GST may still apply on consideration received for abstinence.
CAUTION
Contract formation more complex than convention — not limited to conventional modes of express offer acceptance
2.3.2 — Counter-Offer | Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. ⇧ Index
🔄 Counter-Offer — When Acceptance is NOT Acceptance
✗ COUNTER-OFFER
Altering the offer is NOT acceptance — it is a counter-offer
Making counter-offer destroys the original offer
📝 If a buyer says "I accept your offer but at ₹90 instead of ₹100" — this is not acceptance. The original offer is killed. Now the seller must decide whether to accept the new counter-offer.
Offeree must do nothing more than accept
To do anything more → voids the offer
✓ VALID ACCEPTANCE
Can be express or implied
Cannot be assumed
Can be without knowledge of Offeror (if offer does not require communication before performance)
Acting upon an advertisement = acceptance
One person accepting advertisement does not exhaust offer → each acceptor creates a separate contract with Offeror
💡 Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (1893) 1 QB 256: Company advertised £100 reward for flu after using product. Carlill used product, got flu, claimed reward. Held: General offer was valid, sincere (money deposited in bank) — not a mere puff. Each user who acts is a separate contract.
LOCATE CONTRACT
Demands locating offer AND acceptance
Offer may have conditions and timelines for expiration
📝 For GST disputes — to prove or disprove a taxable supply, locate: (1) what was the offer? (2) was it accepted? (3) did acceptance match the offer exactly? Only then does a valid contract exist.
2.3.3 to 2.3.6 — Manner of Formation | Implied Contract | Assumpsit | Enforceability ⇧ Index
📋 How Contracts are Formed & What Makes Them Enforceable
2.3.3 Manner of Formation
Traditional or modern manner — existence cannot be nebulous
Express contracts tested on: (i) words used (ii) conduct of parties — congruity
Actions speak louder than words — express contract will have to be discarded if it is a poor record of conduct pursuant to that contract
💡 CIT v. Motor and General Stores AIR 1968 SC 200: No authority — even a Court — may substitute what it believes ought to have been the terms unless express contract is shown to be fictitious or a sham.
2.3.4 Implied Contract
When something is put up for sale with a price → someone appropriates it → contract for sale is implicit by so appropriating
📝 If goods are displayed with a price and you take them, you've impliedly agreed to buy. No need to sign anything. Taking = acceptance.
LAMPLEIGH
Past consideration normally not consideration — BUT if (i) provided at request of Promisor AND (ii) with understanding it will be reciprocated → it will be admitted as consideration
Lampleigh v. Braithwait (1842) 3 QB 234
📝 Usually, something you did BEFORE the promise was made cannot be the consideration for the promise. Exception: if the other party asked you to do it, expecting to pay later.
INDIA RULE
Law in India is well established that enforceability once admitted trumps any uncertainty of consideration
2.3.4 Assumpsit
Assumpsit = Action on express or implied promise/contract not executed as prescribed, when acted upon in manner satisfying parties that binding obligations prevailed
📝 Assumpsit is a remedy for breach of an informal/unwritten promise. If you performed in good faith, you are entitled to remedy even without a formal sealed contract — based on the parties' conduct showing they were bound.
Assumpsit assures remedy even without sealed document since parties' conduct indicates consent to be bound by verifiable obligations
Defence of absence of contract has NOT succeeded for 650+ years and cannot be assumed to readily succeed now when contract-writing has made much progress
2.3.5 Test of Enforceability
Enforceability is the HEART of existence of any contract [§2(h)]
Anything affecting enforceability → contract becomes non est
📝 Section 2(h): An agreement enforceable by law is a contract. If you cannot enforce it, it is not a contract — it is just a promise. And section 2(g) makes the difference: void = not enforceable.
More efficient question to test contract: "Is the payment obligation enforceable?"
§2(g) Agreement not enforceable by law = void
§2(j) Contract ceasing to be enforceable = void when it ceases
💡 Eg.8: Best dealer award of ₹5 lakhs announced is NOT a gift but consideration for reciprocal promises performed. Enforceable = contractual = GST applicable.
2.3.7 — Unequal Bargaining Power | Contra Proferentem ⇧ Index
⚖️ Unequal Bargaining Power & Contra Proferentem Rule
REALITY
Not all contracts negotiated between equals
Supplier = large corporate; Recipient = dealer appointed by Supplier
Terms dictated by Supplier — not really open for negotiation
Every new condition agreed bears risk — outcome covertly tied to continuation of future relations
💡 Eg.13: Cement dealer buys at ₹400, sells at ₹350 — accumulates ITC. OEM gives ₹100/bag incentive without adjusting GST. Explanation must be tested for plausibility given unequal bargaining power before admitting it.
Contra Proferentem Rule
📝 Contra proferentem = "Against the one who put it forward." If a contract clause is ambiguous, it is interpreted against the party who wrote it. Don't draft tricky clauses hoping the other side didn't notice — backfires on you.
Ambiguous clauses in standard form contracts → interpreted against the Party who authored them
Terms of contract that bear advantageous tax consequences → interpretation adopted requires validation in light of unequal bargaining power
💡 If OEM's standard dealer agreement says "incentive is not related to sales" but OEM uses it to argue no GST — that clause will be read against OEM (the author) under contra proferentem.
2.3.8 to 2.3.13 — Statutory Contracts | Silence | Auction | Form | Record | Title ⇧ Index
📑 Special Types & Aspects of Contract Formation
2.3.8 Statutory Contracts
Legislature may curtail latitude → prescribe standard forms
Terms of contract contained in statute → tax treatment depends on statutory contract terms, NOT terms presented by parties
📝 In sectors like IRDA, RERA, Mining, Cable TV — the law itself prescribes the contract terms. Parties cannot deviate. GST follows what the law says, not what parties drafted.
💡 Eg.14–16: Tender + Letter of Acceptance = binding contract. Model Concession Agreements (NHAI), Cable TV interconnection rules — standard forms govern; parties cannot alter GST consequences by private arrangement.
2.3.9 Silence as Acceptance
Silence generally NOT acceptance
UNLESS: (i) Silence = communication of acceptance & acting on irrevocable offer is sufficient OR (ii) Express requirement in offer that rejection requires communication
📝 Silence = acceptance is the exception, not the rule. Compelling circumstances with no distortion of bargaining power required. Cannot assume GST supply just because one party stayed silent.
2.3.11 Auction
Auctioneer = agent, NOT involved in delivery of goods (∴ not Para 3, Schedule I)
Contract between Seller and Buyer directly
Fall of hammer affirms the contract
📝 Auctioneer is just a middleman facilitating the match between buyer and seller. The contract is between seller and winning bidder — auctioneer does not supply the goods.
💡 GST implication: Auctioneer charges commission for services separately. The winning bid creates a sale contract between seller and buyer — that is the taxable supply of goods.
2.3.10 Policy / Advertisement as Offer
Offer via advertisement → 'Silence PLUS performance' constitutes contract
Enforceability of such contract is key — must be admitted by Offeror, not explained away as 'puff'
💡 Eg.22–24: Discount code ads, interest-free education loan policies, gold coin schemes — all create binding enforceable contracts once acted upon. "It was just publicity" is not a defence in court or GST proceedings.
2.3.12 & 2.3.13 Record / Title
Written document need not bring contract into existence — verbal contract may be reduced to writing later as 'record'
Title not determinative — Documents carry titles to procure treatment; unless expressions tested for congruity with relations created, placing reliance is perilous
💡 Eg.26–31: "Lease Deed" not conferring possession → not a lease. JDA not registered → not a JDA. Movable barge supplier titled "Contractor" → cannot attract works contract treatment. Doctor's revenue share to hospital → not healthcare but rent.
2.4.1 to 2.4.3 — Consideration: Essential | Valuable | Equitable ⇧ Index
💰 Consideration — "The Price for Which the Promise is Purchased"
Lord Fredrick Pollock | §2(d) Indian Contract Act
📝 Consideration is what each party gives and receives in the deal. Seller gets money; buyer gets goods/services. Both sides must receive something — otherwise the contract is void. It is a two-way street.
§2(d) Consideration = at desire of promisor, promisee or any other person has done/abstained from doing, or does/abstains from doing, or promises to do/abstain from doing something — such act, abstinence or promise
2.4.1 Essential Considerations
Consideration = two-way street
Seller receives money; Buyer receives goods/services
Contract void if only Seller received consideration and NO consideration received by Buyer
CRITICAL
Non-payment of price does NOT render contract void
Absence of consideration makes contract void
Non-payment = default by Buyer → actionable cause to Seller
📝 Non-payment of GST invoice does not void the contract. The supply happened. The consideration was agreed. Non-payment is just a debt — giving rise to litigation rights, not making the transaction disappear.
EXECUTED vs EXECUTORY
Executed = performance complete → demand payment
Executory = performance pending → right to repudiate + damages for failure (non-payment)
📝 Executed contract: You delivered goods → demand payment. Executory contract: You haven't delivered yet → if buyer hasn't paid, you can walk away AND claim damages. Timing matters for GST liability too.
2.4.2 Valuable Consideration
'Nominal consideration' is NO consideration
Consideration must be 'reasonable' to be 'valuable'
'Reasonable' ≠ 'adequate' ≠ 'adequate consideration'
📝 "Reasonable" means worthy — not the same as "adequate" (enough). You don't need to pay market price, but you need to pay something genuinely meaningful.
Currie v. Misa (1875) LR 10 Ex 153
Consideration must be some "right, interest, profit or benefit" given OR some "forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility" suffered
📝 Benefit-detriment theory: Either the other party gains something (benefit) OR you lose something/refrain from something (detriment). Either is enough to count as consideration.
💡 This 'benefit-detriment theory' greatly illuminates understanding of 'tolerating act' situations in GST — e.g., tolerating a competitor's presence for payment is a taxable supply of service.
2.4.3 Equitable Exchange
Consideration must be equitable — (i) reasonable (ii) commensurate (iii) proportionate for the supply object agreed
📝 Valuable = worthy. Equitable = fair. Even if both parties agreed, a wildly unfair deal shocks the conscience of the court — which can then refuse to enforce it.
Consideration unconscionable → cannot be valuable, even if it was the bargain
Courts will NOT be led to enforce something that shocks its conscience
Any tax treatment based on assertion that unconscionable contract is freely consented → will NOT be admitted as valid → GST treatment extended without assistance from terms of such contract
💡 Eg.32: Lease Deed executed for nominal rent of ₹1 may be sufficient to register the document BUT is insufficient as valuable consideration for GST — valuation rules (§15) apply to determine correct value.
2.4.4 to 2.4.7 — Absence | Existence | Stranger to Contract | Non-Payment ⇧ Index
🔬 Consideration — Deeper Dimensions
2.4.4 Absence of Consideration
Stilk v. Myrick (1809) 2 Camo 317
Demand for additional consideration to perform obligations originally agreed = without any consideration
→ unenforceable
📝 You already agreed to do the job for ₹100. Midway, you demand ₹150. The extra ₹50 has no consideration — you are not doing anything new. The extra promise cannot be enforced.
Combe v. Combe (1951) 2 KB 215
Ex-wife suing for monthly allowance promised by ex-husband — no consideration flowing from ex-wife → no contract → suit rejected
CASES
Thorsen Car Ferries v. Wyemouth Portland BC (1977): Lack of consideration defence has no merit
Woodhouse AC Israel Cocoa Ltd. v. Nigerian Produce Marketing (1972): Businessmen know their own business best
2.4.5 Existence of Consideration
Absence of consideration makes contract void
Assertion about validity of contract → makes consideration real, albeit not in monetary form
📝 Even if you say the contract is valid — by saying so, you are admitting consideration exists, even if it isn't money. Non-monetary consideration is still consideration.
Existence of binding contract → disproves absence of consideration
Absence of consideration → disproves arrangement 'in course or furtherance of business'
💡 When costs incurred on 'business associates' are claimed to be 'business expenditure' — this contradicts claiming such outlay is a 'gift.' Either it is a business transaction (with consideration) or it is a gift (not in course of business).
Courts will NOT bargain for parties — Courts will only read terms of their bargain
Enforceability test → establishes existence of consideration
2.4.6 Stranger to Contract
PERMITTED
Stranger to contract may contribute towards consideration — although Stranger has no 'privity of contract'
📝 A third party (Stranger) can pay for the goods/services you are buying — even though the Stranger is not part of your contract with the seller. The seller gets paid; that's all that matters.
KEY RULE
Consideration = total amount received by Seller (whether from Buyer or any other person)
NOT just amount paid by Buyer
💡 GST: If Buyer's dealer pays ₹80 and Stranger pays ₹20 to Seller → consideration = ₹100. Taxable value = ₹100. Reason for Stranger's payment to be explained by Stranger (may itself be a separate taxable transaction).
Stranger's contribution cannot be unbeknownst to Promisee — Knowledge of Stranger's contribution must be attached to Promisee because supply will not take place but for Stranger's assurance
💡 Eg.43: Cost of food paid by Donor unknown to migrant workers who accepted food packets during Pandemic → NOT consideration contributed by Donor (Stranger). No contract between Donor and workers.
2.4.7 Effect of Non-Payment | Quid Pro Quo
Stoppage of payment → Promisor lawfully refrains from performing any obligations
Non-payment = surest test of existence that payment is consideration
📝 If stopping payment immediately allows the supplier to stop performing — that proves the payment IS the consideration. You stop paying → I stop supplying. That is quid pro quo.
Quid pro quo = "This for that"
Nature and object of the contract remain to be discovered
📝 Quid pro quo literally means "something for something." Every contract has a quid (what you give) and a quo (what you get). Understanding both is essential to determining GST treatment and valuation.
Performance without consideration = void
When performance is 'without consideration' → it is a gift or voluntary contribution → unenforceable
💡 Eg.44–46: OEM incentive to dealer to increase sales of stock already 'sold' by OEM = no consideration to OEM (Stilk v. Myrick). Cash-back by credit card company for fuel purchases = not voluntary contribution — it has consideration.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 2 | Contract Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Vinculum Juris Principle — Indian Contract Act, 1872
Foundational Principle | Applicable in all GST supply disputes
Key Ratio
Every 'form' of supply addressed in GST is one or other kind of contract that is entered into. A transaction that fails to satisfy the ingredients of a valid contract can never come within the treatment applicable in GST. Vinculum juris refers to the bond of legal obligations that contracting Parties willingly enter into and it is not for anyone, not even a Court, to deny Parties their bargain and its assistance in default.
When to Use — To challenge Department's demand where underlying transaction lacks essential ingredients of a valid contract (no free consent, no lawful object, void arrangement). Argue: no valid contract = no taxable supply = demand falls.
Section 10 — Presumption of Contract Validity
Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Para 2.2.1
Key Ratio
Where contract is purported to be enforceable, presumption runs in favour of its validity unless some incurable or inseverable invalidity is discovered in the contract — such as minority or mistake or something else. Parties intended to bring about binding legal obligations cannot be seen denying their contract. Flow of consideration implies validity of the contract.
When to Use — When Department challenges the existence or validity of a contract to deny GST exemption, zero-rating or ITC. Counter: presumption runs in favour of validity; burden is on Department to show specific incurable invalidity.
CIT v. Motor and General Stores | AIR 1968 SC 200
Supreme Court of India | 1968 | Para 2.3.3
Key Ratio
No authority, even a Court, may substitute what it believes ought to have been the terms of the contract unless the express contract is shown to be fictitious or a sham; and then proceed to ascribe meaning to their conduct with the terms of the arrangement based on the nature and object of such a contract.
When to Use — When adjudicating officer attempts to re-characterise or substitute the terms of a written contract based on what he thinks the parties "ought" to have agreed. Cite to bar such substitution and insist on terms as agreed.
Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. | (1893) 1 QB 256
Queen's Bench Division, England | 1893 | Para 2.3.2
Key Ratio
A general offer made by advertisement is not a mere puff — the sincerity of Offer was established when prize-money was published to be placed with a bank which persons acting upon the general offer would find acceptable. Acting upon the advertisement is acceptance. One person accepting does not exhaust the offer — each acceptor and the Offeror enter a separate contract.
When to Use — When OEM/Company argues that policy announcements, schemes, dealer communications or advertisements were "merely for publicity" and not binding contracts. Cite to establish that acting upon such offers creates enforceable obligations and attendant GST consequences.
Lampleigh v. Braithwait | (1842) 3 QB 234 | Past Consideration
Queen's Bench | England | Para 2.3.4
Key Ratio
Past consideration will still be admitted to be consideration in a present contract if it was (i) provided at the request of the Promisor and (ii) with the understanding that it will be reciprocated or made good under legal relations in due course. Law in India is well established that enforceability once admitted trumps any uncertainty of consideration.
When to Use — In disputes on dealer incentives, year-end schemes, retrospective discounts — where Department says "past performance is not consideration." Cite to establish consideration flowing from acts done at request of Promisor even if done before formal promise was made.
Currie v. Misa | (1875) LR 10 Ex 153 | Benefit-Detriment Theory
Court of Exchequer, England | Para 2.4.2
Key Ratio
To be valuable, consideration must be some "right, interest, profit or benefit" given to the other side, or some "forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility" suffered by the promising party. This benefit-detriment theory greatly illuminates understanding of 'tolerating act' situations in GST.
When to Use — In GST disputes on "tolerating an act or situation" as a taxable supply of service (Schedule II, Entry 5(e)). Cite to explain that suffering a detriment (tolerating) IS consideration — and the payment received IS taxable consideration for that service.
Stilk v. Myrick | (1809) 2 Camo 317 | No Consideration for Pre-existing Duty
Common Pleas, England | Para 2.4.4
Key Ratio
Demand for additional consideration to perform obligations originally agreed is without any consideration flowing. Incentive given by OEM to Dealer to increase sales of stock does not have any consideration to OEM because the stock is already sold by OEM to Dealer and to sell Dealer's own stocks is in Dealer's own interests.
When to Use — When OEM claims incentive paid to dealer is "without consideration" to avoid GST. Counter from assessee side: if incentive is for pre-existing duty to sell OEM's brand, there IS consideration. Alternatively, use from Department's side: incentive where Dealer sells their own stock = no consideration = no supply by Dealer to OEM = GST on incentive as independent supply.
Contra Proferentem Rule | Unequal Bargaining Power
Common Law Principle | Para 2.3.7 | Applicable in standard form contracts
Key Principle
Contra proferentem is the rule which states that contractual clauses contained in standard form contracts that are ambiguous shall be interpreted against the Party who authored them. Terms of contract that bear advantageous tax consequences render the interpretation adopted to require validation in the light of existence of unequal bargaining power.
When to Use — When OEM's standard dealer agreement, franchise agreement or any form contract has ambiguous clauses about GST, incentives, discounts or returns. Argue: clause authored by OEM/dominant party must be read against them. Any tax advantage claimed through such ambiguous clause deserves scrutiny.
Quid Pro Quo & Effect of Non-Payment | Section 2(d) ICA
Indian Contract Act Principle | Para 2.4.7 | Applicable universally
Key Principle
Stoppage of payment entitles Promisor to lawfully refrain from performing any obligations — this is the surest test of the existence that payment is consideration of contract whose nature and object remain to be discovered. After all, quid pro quo means 'this for that.' Performance without consideration is void. When performance is 'without consideration,' it would be a gift or voluntary contribution — and such arrangements are unenforceable.
When to Use — When assessee argues payment received is "gift" or "voluntary contribution" without GST liability. Cite to challenge: if performance stops when payment stops, it is quid pro quo = consideration = taxable supply. No such thing as "voluntary payment" in course of business.
Assumpsit Doctrine | Upton-on-Severn RDC v. Powell (1942) 1 All ER 220
English Court of Appeal | 1942 | Para 2.3.4
Key Ratio
When something is put up for sale and a price is attached to it, and someone appropriates (and consumes) that thing, then it is implicit by so appropriating that a contract for sale was intended. To say otherwise would excuse everyone who appropriates and denies existence of contract when payment is demanded. Defence of absence of contract has not succeeded for more than 650 years and cannot certainly be assumed to readily succeed now when contract-writing has made much progress and conduct of Parties unmistakably exposes their consent and binding obligations.
When to Use — When supplier or recipient tries to deny existence of contract to avoid GST — e.g., "we had no formal contract." Cite to establish that conduct of parties (placing order, delivering goods, accepting delivery) is itself evidence of an implied contract, and denial of contract when payment is demanded will not be entertained.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 2b — Consideration, Contract Doc & Object

⚖️ Chapter 2 — Contract Act | Consideration, Contract Document & Object of Contract

Source: GST Litigation Reference — Allied Laws | Para 2.4 to 2.6 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root Node
Level 1 Branch
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Negative
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
2.4.22 Cross Subsidization
2.4.23 Late Payment Charges
2.4.24 Non-Payment of Consideration
2.4.25 Sections 63 & 41
2.4.26 Bad Debts
2.4.27 Unilateral Write-Off
2.4.28 Bilateral Write-Off
2.4.29 Pre-Sales Discount
2.4.30 Post-Sales Discount
2.4.31 Special Discount
2.4.32 Annual Discount
2.4.33 Discount for Additional Obligations
2.4.34 Discount Without Additional Obligations
2.4.35 Voluntary Contribution / Largesse
2.4.36 Exception to Consideration
2.5.1 Documentation of Contract
2.5.2 Composite Document
2.5.3 Testing Composite Documents
2.5.4 Purpose of Composite Document
2.6.1 Objective of Object
2.6.2 Titles & Qualifications
2.6.3 Alteration of Object
2.6.4 Variation or Substitution
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.4.22 — Cross Subsidization ⇧ Index
🔀 Cross Subsidization — Loss Leaders in Product Portfolio
📝 A company may sell some products at a loss just to keep its distribution network alive — so that when it launches a profitable product, it can reach the whole market fast. This is commercial wisdom, not a contract problem.
WHAT IT IS
Product portfolio includes 'loss leaders' — products making low profits or even a loss
kept for commercial distribution reasons
💡 Eg.83: Lifebuoy (assumed loss-making) sold in high volumes so HUL can push LUX (high margin) swiftly across the market.
VALIDITY
No concern about validity or sufficiency of consideration of loss leaders — unless two products sold conjointly
📝 Adequacy of consideration is unquestionable once it is shown to exist and is valuable (not nominal). No regulator can demand linear margins across a product portfolio.
EXCEPTION — WATCH OUT
'Artificial subsidization' seen when:
Parties are same / related / acting-in-concert AND products sold conjointly
📝 When related parties bundle products to shift value from one to another — that is artificial and the Department can question adequacy of consideration in GST.
⚠️ BURDEN RULE
Where contract presented by Parties is rejected due to unreliability — burden is heavy on Department to reconstruct ingredients and derive consideration without travelling beyond the terms of the statute (Central GST Act).
📝 Department cannot simply reject a contract and make up a new consideration figure. It must stay within statutory limits when reconstructing value. This is a key defence in valuation disputes.
2.4.23 — Late Payment Charges ⇧ Index
⏱️ Late Payment Charges — Nature of Compensation for Delay
📝 When a buyer delays payment, the seller may demand extra money. Is this a new contract or just a term in the original contract? The answer changes the GST treatment — especially whether it is 'interest' (exempt) or a 'late payment charge' (taxable service).
SCENARIO A
Altogether new contract — delay in payment = object of new contract
Consideration = time value of delayed sum
Roles reversed: original Promisee (Buyer) becomes Promisor of new contract — the 'sum due' is now the object
📝 Even though roles swap, the nature of the transaction is still 'late payment charge' and NOT 'interest' — hence NOT exempt from GST.
💡 Eg.84: Equipment sold ₹100 crore, 3-month credit. Customer defaults → renegotiation → revised price for past performance of sale without original payment. This is a substituted new contract — not just variation.
SCENARIO B
Collateral contract within same composite document — secondary object = payment of incremental consideration for delay
Addressed by Sections 12(6) and 13(6) — deferment of time of supply for 'late payment charge' collected via debit note
📝 If the original contract itself already provides for a late payment charge — it is a secondary obligation within the contract. Time of supply defers to actual collection via debit note.
💡 Eg.85: Primary object = sale of equipment + 3-month credit. Secondary object in collateral contract within same document = bank lending rate on delayed payment beyond 3 months until actual payment.
KEY PRINCIPLE — Foakes v. Beer (1884)
Promise to alter schedule of payments in exchange for agreement to pay additional charge (time value of money) = upheld as binding and not unreasonable.
Late payment charge ≠ Interest. It remains taxable as a service under GST.
2.4.24 — Non-Payment of Consideration ⇧ Index
💰 Non-Payment vs. Absence of Consideration
📝 These are two very different things. Absence of consideration = void contract. Non-payment = valid contract, but the party who did not pay can be sued or performance can be demanded.
ABSENCE OF CONSIDERATION
Makes contract void ab initio
📝 If there was never any consideration agreed — the contract was born dead. No rights, no obligations.
NON-PAYMENT OF CONSIDERATION
Valid contract — non-defaulting Party has right to sue for recovery (monetary) or sue for performance (non-monetary)
🇮🇳 Indian Law vs English Law — Remission / Dispensing with Consideration
INDIA — PERMITS
Promisee may 'dispense with or remit' consideration
₹100 payable after 3 months → 'treated' as satisfied when ₹98 paid immediately
📝 In India, accepting lesser consideration is valid — it addresses time value of money AND brings certainty of settlement. Built into Corporate and Insolvency laws.
ENGLAND — DOES NOT PERMIT
Pinnel's Case (1602) — lesser consideration cannot satisfy the original
(this principle departed from in India)
KEY DISTINCTION — DISCOUNT vs REMISSION
Remission = Promisee (Recipient/Buyer) dispensing with Promisor's (Supplier's) performance = satisfaction of consideration
Discount = Promisor (Supplier) dispensing with post-quantified extent of money from pre-agreed consideration = allowed by Supplier
→ Discount does NOT come within Section 63 (remission). It is a Supplier's act.
📝 This is critical in GST: Remission = Buyer waives; Discount = Supplier reduces. Only Supplier-allowed discount (pre-sales or eligible post-sales) can reduce taxable value. Buyer's remission is a different concept entirely.
2.4.25 — Sections 63 & 41 of the Contract Act ⇧ Index
📜 Sections 63 & 41 — Remission and Third-Party Performance
SECTION 63
Promisee may dispense with or remit (wholly or in part) performance of promise, extend time, or accept any other satisfaction
📝 The RIGHT to forgive or reduce belongs to the Promisee (Buyer/Recipient) — NOT to the Promisor (Supplier). Supplier cannot grant remission to himself.
SECTION 41
When Promisee accepts performance from a Third Party — cannot afterwards enforce it against the Promisor
📝 If a stranger pays on your behalf and the creditor accepts — you (the debtor/Promisor) are freed from that obligation. But the stranger and creditor must explain and account for why/how this happened.
CRITICAL GST NOTE
Section 63 is NOT available to Promisor (Supplier). Supplier chooses to write-off receivable — that is a unilateral commercial decision, NOT remission under Section 63. Discount allowed by Supplier is Supplier's own act — it does NOT come within Section 63 principles.
2.4.26 — Bad Debts ⇧ Index
📉 Bad Debts — Commercial Write-Off of Receivables
📝 When a supplier decides that chasing payment is not worth it (due to business reputation, cost, or other reasons), the receivable is 'written off' as a bad debt. This is a commercial/accounting decision — it does NOT alter the original contract or the GST already paid.
WHY WRITE-OFF?
Suit for recovery not viable — not due to legal infirmity but due to: disrepute / future business opportunity / cost of litigation / other inexpediency
LEGAL STATUS
Write-off ≠ abandonment of legal right. Right to enforce recovery remains until limitation lapses.
On lapse of limitation — right to sue lost but right to accept voluntary payment remains (court can grant valid discharge)
GST IMPACT
Writing off does NOT alter the contract — it only leaves Promisee's performance outstanding.
Rule 37 of CGST Rules — restriction on ITC to Recipient (Buyer) — affects Recipient's entitlement to claim input tax credit
📝 Buyer who has not paid may lose ITC on that purchase. Rule 37 requires reversal of ITC if payment not made within 180 days from date of invoice.
2.4.27 — Unilateral Write-Off ⇧ Index
🔴 Unilateral Write-Off — Supplier's Independent Decision
📝 Supplier can write off a receivable without asking the Buyer's permission. After all, the Supplier is forfeiting his own right — the Buyer's consent is not needed. This is called 'unilateral' write-off.
GST TREATMENT
Tax treatment applied at time of supply does NOT require change. Unilateral write-off decision is independent of the earlier supply decision.
📝 GST was correctly paid at the time of supply. The Supplier's later decision to write off does not entitle him to a refund of GST already paid.
CREDIT NOTE
Supplier generates 'credit note' from accounting hygiene perspective.
BUT — credit note under Section 34 CGST Act is permitted only in a subset of circumstances — not for all business write-offs
📝 Section 34 does not control when credit notes must be issued in accounting. But for GST tax adjustment through credit note, Section 34 conditions must be satisfied.
Accounting Treatment of Amounts Written-Off (Unilateral)
(a) SUPPLIER (Promisor)
Reduce from 'Sales/Revenues'.
If year-end intervenes before write-off: report as 'expenditure' (not reduction from Sales of later year)
(b) RECIPIENT (Promisee)
No accounting treatment required to be made — this is Supplier's unilateral decision. Recipient's books untouched.
2.4.28 — Bilateral Write-Off ⇧ Index
🤝 Bilateral Write-Off — With Consent of Promisee
📝 When both parties agree to write off — the nature of the arrangement changes. Four possible explanations must be examined to determine GST consequences.
(a) Unilateral offer by Supplier to Recipient to settle — accepted by Recipient
(b) Bilateral variation of original contract — altering consideration to extent of write-down/write-off (requires 'consent')
(c) Substitution with new contract — new consideration stated as 'amount already paid' (must be bilateral — no new contract without consent)
(d) Second contract with reversed roles — cross-cancellation of supplies — Recipient performs new supply, settles outstanding consideration under first contract
⚠️ IMPUTED CROSS-CANCELLING SUPPLY
Based on implied contract — when receivable is written off bilaterally, it is not uncommon to impute a cross-cancelling supply by Recipient who has gained waiver of obligation. Tenable only if Recipient explains basis of bilateral settlement.
Accounting Treatment — Bilateral Write-Off
(a) SUPPLIER
Reduce from 'Sales/Revenues'. If year-end intervenes — report as 'expenditure', not reduction from later year's Sales
(b) RECIPIENT
Reduce from 'Purchases'. If year-end intervenes — report outstanding liability as 'other income', not reduction from later year's Purchases
2.4.29 — Pre-Sales Discount ⇧ Index
🏷️ Pre-Sales Discount — Mark-Down Before Sale
📝 Pre-sales discount is the mark-down from the published list price that the Supplier allows before the sale. The actual selling price is the list price 'net' of this discount — and THAT is the contractual price. This is NOT variation of consideration.
HOW IT WORKS
