List price less mark-down = actual selling price (pre-sales discount)
Discount on tax invoice = disclose to Customer that list price has been marked-down
Tax invoice ≠ the contract; it is a record of the contract
💡 Eg.86: MRP less 30% to Distributors — Distributors to sell not above MRP.
Eg.87: List ₹100, to Distributor ₹80, Industrial Customer ₹85, Government ₹75.
OUTSIDE THE TAX INVOICE
Discount allowed after sale is concluded = NOT pre-sales discount
= 'not discount' unless circumstances support it as discount at least not without further deliberation
📝 A discount offered after the sale is already a contract at list price — altering it after contract is entered is inconsistent with binding contract principles.
EXCESSIVE MARK-DOWN
Mark-down so excessive that list price 'net' of discount renders consideration nominal → contract void ab initio for lack of valuable consideration
💡 Eg.88: MRP ₹100, discount 100% → void.
Eg.89: List ₹10,000, discount ₹9,999 → void.
Eg.90: Buy one MRP ₹1,000, take another for ₹10 → examine carefully.
GST PRINCIPLE
Pre-sales discount: Contractual price = list price 'net' of discount = taxable value under Section 15(1) CGST Act, subject to disqualifications (relationship, extraneous consideration). Questioning reasons for pre-sales discount = questioning adequacy of consideration = barred in law.
2.4.30 — Post-Sales Discount ⇧ Index
🔄 Post-Sales Discount — After Contract Concluded/Executed
📝 Once a contract is concluded and performed, altering the consideration is impossible (lack of new consideration to Supplier). So any 'post-sales discount' must be carefully examined — it is actually one of five things.
(a) Unilateral waiver by Supplier
(b) Bilateral waiver — with consent of both Parties
(c) Composite consideration — contingency already built into contract price
(d) Cross-cancelling supplies and settlement
(e) Dispensation of consideration by Promisee (not by Promisor)
KEY — CONTINGENCY-BUILT DISCOUNT (Section 15(3)(b))
Essential elements that must be demonstrated for GST tax adjustment on post-sales discount:
#Essential Element
(a)Contingency must be known at time of contract
(b)Event forming contingency must be uncertain at time of contract
(c)Uncertain event must come to pass before final settlement of accounts (end of quarter/year)
(d)Contingency must NOT be controllable by either Party
(e)Measurement of effect on price must not be 'subject to' fresh negotiations
(f)No requirement to claim occurrence — occurrence must be undeniable and consequential effect pre-agreed
(g)When duration ends, contract price finally determined 'net' of contingency adjustment
📝 All 7 elements must be present. If any one is missing, tax adjustment under Section 15(3)(b) read with Section 34 and Rule 37 will NOT be available. Parties are masters of their bargain — multiple contingencies can be pre-agreed with different classes of Customers.
2.4.31 — Special Discount ⇧ Index
⚠️ Special Discount — Neither Pre-Sales Nor Post-Sales
📝 A 'special discount' that does not fit either the pre-sales or post-sales discount framework has a dangerous GST consequence — it implicitly admits that the tax adjustment available under Section 15(3)(b) and Section 34 is NOT applicable.
GST CONSEQUENCE
If asserted as 'special' discount — must be explained as one of:
(a) Unilateral waiver (b) Bilateral waiver (c) Cross-cancelling supply & settlement (d) Dispensation of consideration by Promisee (not Promisor)

Tax adjustment under Section 15(3)(b) read with Section 34 to Supplier AND Rule 37 to Recipient = NOT applicable
📝 Claiming 'special discount' without proper explanation is a trap — it forecloses the statutory route to GST adjustment. If the arrangement is genuinely one of the four categories above, it should be named and documented as such.
2.4.32 — Annual Discount ⇧ Index
📅 Annual Discount — Post-Supply Based on Annual Offtake
📝 Annual discounts based on the quantity purchased in a year are a very common trade practice — given in cash or by allowing Recipient to reduce from payable amount. These are post-sales discounts and must satisfy the same 'essential elements' test.
CASH FORM
Supplier pays money to Recipient
DEDUCTION FORM
Recipient reduces from any payable outstanding to Supplier
NON-MONETARY FORM
Overlaps with barter discussion — same enquiry into essential elements applies
MANDATORY Enquiry into 7 'essential elements' (from 2.4.30) is a must to admit or deny GST tax adjustment for annual discounts.
2.4.33 — Discount in Exchange for Additional Obligations ⇧ Index
🔃 Discount for Additional Obligations — Partial Barter / Composite Consideration
📝 When a Supplier gives a discount only if the Recipient performs some additional task — that is NOT really a discount. It is either partial barter (non-monetary consideration) or composite consideration. Using the word 'discount' is a misnomer — and if deliberate, it is misrepresentation to gain a tax advantage.
OPTIONAL OBLIGATION
Does NOT become a contingency → Cannot be equated with post-sales discount
COMPULSORY OBLIGATION
Makes up composite consideration → higher tax consequence to supply in each direction (roles reversed qua each object)
💡 Eg.91: OEM sells car ₹10 lakhs to Dealer + Dealer compulsorily performs services for OEM (₹50,000 value) — second contract whose consideration is intermixed with dues under first contract.

💡 Eg.92: CA engaged for ₹1 lakh/month + CA to share office with Company staff for ₹25,000/month — two separate contracts forcibly mixed.
TAX MISREPRESENTATION WARNING
Use of expression 'discount' where additional obligations are attached — is a misnomer. Where deliberate — tantamount to misrepresentation of facts to secure advantageous tax consequence. Tax treatment applicable to supply in each direction must be ensured.
2.4.34 — Discount Without Additional Obligations ⇧ Index
🚫 Post-Sales Discount — No Additional Obligations to Claim It
📝 If 'no one gives something for nothing' and there are no additional obligations to explain the discount — the only explanations are unilateral waiver, bilateral waiver, or dispensation by Promisee.
(a) Unilateral waiver
(b) Bilateral waiver
(c) Dispensation of consideration by Promisee (not Promisor)
GST CONSEQUENCE
Section 15(3)(b) conditions NOT satisfied → Tax adjustment NOT admissible.
Promisee (Recipient) suffers Rule 37 consequence — ITC reversal to extent of non-payment of discount.
Debate: Whether non-payment to extent of discount by Supplier is 'failure to pay' attracting Rule 37 — remains open question.
If discount is policy announcement bearing indicia of enforceable contract — must be explained as one of: post-sales discount, or voluntary charitable contribution not related to business.
2.4.35 — Voluntary Contribution or Largesse ⇧ Index
🎁 Voluntary Contribution / Largesse — Charity Not Related to Business
📝 Charity is generosity — giving something of value from the heart without expecting anything back. Acceptance of the gift is itself consideration to the Giver (Donor). But the Donee (receiver) CANNOT enforce a promise of a future gift — executory gifts are generally unenforceable.
ENFORCEABLE — WHEN
If Donee has incurred costs or losses relying on assurance by Donor — enforceable in tort
Assurance must be: in writing + executed as required by law
NOT ENFORCEABLE
Executory gifts and voluntary contributions — generally unenforceable
One who is to receive (Donee) cannot enforce yet-to-be-performed acts of generosity
GST CONTRAINDICATION
Assertion that something of value is given for nothing — places it in domain of voluntary contribution.
Remains to be tested for contradiction in assertions made under other allied laws.

If stock of finished goods (with MRP label) given away to potential Consumers as 'business promotion expense' — this is a contraindication of voluntary contribution (i.e., it is NOT a gift — it is promotional activity with business purpose).
💡 Eg.93: Stock of finished goods with MRP label given away to potential Consumers and accounted as 'business promotion expenses' — this is a contraindication of 'voluntary contribution'. GST may be leviable as deemed supply to self or promotional supply.
2.4.36 — Exception to Need for Consideration ⇧ Index
🔓 Exceptions to Consideration Requirement — Section 25 Contracts
📝 Contract without consideration is void. Section 25 carves out exceptions — but for GST deliberations, those exceptions have no bearing EXCEPT three specific ones.
(a) Contract of Agency
(b) Assignment
(c) Bailment
These three exceptions to need for consideration are relevant for GST analysis and are discussed separately in the book.
NOTE Discount must only be allowed by Supplier — not demanded by Buyer or allowed by an intermediary.
2.5.1 — Documentation of Contract ⇧ Index
📄 Contract Document ≠ The Contract Itself
📝 The document is just the paper on which the contract is written. The contract is the terms — offer, acceptance, consideration. One document can contain many contracts, and multiple documents can contain one contract.
SINGLE DOCUMENT
Can contain multiple contracts — multiple identical contracts — or continuation of obligations
MULTIPLE CONTRACTS TEST
Tested by: (a) Termination at end of each tenure (b) Satisfaction of payment (c) Obligation to return property (d) Option to commence new contract on identical terms
🔑 GST Implication — Single vs Multiple Contracts
SINGLE CONTRACT
ONE time of supply. GST on entire consideration due by first month's due date for entire duration.
💡 Eg.94: Non-compete agreement for 5 years, ₹10 crores — one contract, one consideration, fulfilled over entire tenure.
TWELVE CONTRACTS
TWELVE times of supply. GST paid on monthly consideration for each of the 12 lease terms.
💡 Eg.95: Non-compete for 5 years ₹2 crores per year, cancellable — 5 contracts on identical terms, each with its own time of supply.

💡 Eg.96: ESOP vesting period deferment — one contract with contingency to foreclose tenure and obligation to number of stock options.
2.5.2 — Composite Document ⇧ Index
📋 Composite Document — Primary + Collateral Contracts in One Document
📝 A single document can contain multiple different (unidentical) contracts that have some relevance for being together. One is the 'primary' contract and the other is 'collateral' or secondary. This has significant GST implications.
PRIMARY CONTRACT
Main object — the principal bargain
COLLATERAL CONTRACT
Secondary / side object — dependent on or related to primary
KEY PRINCIPLE There is nothing in Contract law that supports 'one-contract-one-document'. Commercially it is prudent to capture all arrangements in one document — but legally, arrangements may span multiple documents.
📌 Examples of Composite Documents
ExamplePrimary ContractCollateral Contract
Eg.97Construction of highwayMaintenance after completion of construction
Eg.9812 identical primary contracts for lease of equipment — in one document
Eg.99Provision of education for Class XII Board examsSpecial coaching for entrance exams
Eg.100Construction services by ContractorRecovery of cost of material supplied by Customer used beyond permissible wastage
Eg.101Sale of equipmentRefund of 20% consideration if equipment fails to perform for a certain duration/efficiency
📝 Circular 178/10/2022-GST (dated 3 Aug 2022) on 'composite supply' must be viewed in the light of these principles — one document may have multiple contracts, each requiring its own tax treatment.
2.5.3 — Testing Composite Documents ⇧ Index
🔍 Testing Whether Multiple Documents Form One Indivisible Arrangement
📝 When Parties spread their arrangement across multiple documents, the question is: are these truly independent contracts or is it one indivisible arrangement artificially split? Indivisibility is tested by looking at clauses in any or all of them.
3 Tests for Indivisibility
(a) CROSS-FALL BREACH
Breach/termination of one document automatically and instantaneously terminates all other supposedly independent documents
📝 If breaking one contract breaks all others — they are not really independent. They are one arrangement.
(b) REFERENCE TO CONTRACT SUM
Terms in any document requiring reference to 'contract sum' expressed as % — mobilization advance, retention, liquidated damages, etc. that refer to aggregate of all individual contracts
(c) COMPLETION DATE
Date of completion in all independent documents tied to date of completion of the last deliverable (installation + commissioning) across all contracts
⚠️ CONTRACT SPLITTING MISCHIEF
Artificially vivisecting an otherwise indivisible arrangement violates the unitary bargain of the Parties — especially when contracting Parties are same or related or acting-in-concert. Finding-of-fact as to object of each contract puts tax treatment to rest.
ExampleResult
Eg.102: Sale contract + annual maintenance after warranty — single documentTwo INDEPENDENT divisible contracts in one document
Eg.103: Lease of office premises + monthly maintenance of common areas — single documentTwo independent divisible contracts
Eg.104: Education for Class XII Board + mandatory coaching for entrance exams — single documentSingle INDIVISIBLE contract
Eg.105: Construction services + sale of key material of approved brand — single documentTwo independent divisible contracts
Eg.106: Sale of equipment + extended warranty (option exercised before sale) — single documentSingle indivisible contract
2.5.4 — Purpose of Composite Document ⇧ Index
⚡ Composite Document — Commercial Purpose vs Tax Mischief
📝 Combining contracts in one document is commercially wise — it protects both parties. But the same can be misused to reduce tax. The key is whether the splitting or combining is commercially driven or tax-driven.
LEGITIMATE — SINGLE CONTRACT
Equipment (₹100 Cr) + Installation (₹2 Cr) = single supply-cum-installation contract
Commercial reason: if Contractor fails to install, Customer still liable to pay full ₹100 Cr to Supplier — can't recoup from insolvent Contractor
MISCHIEF — CONTRACT SPLITTING
Clever contract-writing to optimize tax cannot be carried out contrary to trade customs which includes regulatory or technological impossibilities
💡 Eg.107: Due to GST tariff differences on sale vs installation — contracts may be split for fuel pumps (8413), air-conditioners (8415), aircraft (8802-8806).

💡 Eg.108: ITC admissibility on electronic installations — works contract for interior decoration with electronics embedded could be split, even if GST rate same as composite supply (9954).
REGULATORY BAR ON SPLITTING
Eg.109: Sale + servicing of aircraft — CANNOT be split due to regulatory restrictions and insurance voiding effect.
Eg.110: Installation of MRI equipment may ONLY be awarded to OEM — imprecision in installation may impair imaging and excuse recourse against OEM.

Contracts cannot be written contrary to trade customs. Insights into trade customs cannot be replaced by logical reasoning.
PERMISSIBLE SPLIT — WHERE SKILL IS COMMON
Eg.111: Air-conditioner (cooling system) installation — can be awarded to independent HVAC Contractors, not necessarily OEM — not mischief of 'contract splitting'.
Eg.112: DIY SKD-kits of furniture — instructions may obviate need for separate installation contract.
2.6.1 — Objective of Object of Contract ⇧ Index
🎯 Object of Contract — What Parties Have Consensus Ad Idem About
📝 Consideration and Object are different. Object = what the Promisor promises to perform. Consideration = what the Promisee pays for that performance. They must not be confused — though goods/services may serve as both depending on which side of the contract you look at.
OBJECT
What Promisor (Offeror) offers to perform — what Promisee (Offeree) demands
Object must be lawful
CONSIDERATION
Price agreed for performance of object
Flows to Promisor in satisfaction of performance
Consideration = object viewed from Promisee's angle
⚠️ CONSENSUS AD IDEM RULE
Object of contract = what Parties have consensus ad idem about. Opinion of the Party who knows least about the object is a reliable guide — because consensus cannot be about what one of them knows very little of.
Asserting that object was what is of 'greater value' is an error — there is no order in law to arrange objects by value in a composite contract document.
📝 In GST: You cannot say 'the principal supply is the one that is more expensive' unless that is what Parties actually agreed was the main object. Consensus ad idem = meeting of minds on the same thing.
💡 Eg.113: Automobile repair contract — object is 'services', NOT goods (parts replaced). Customer doesn't have knowledge to negotiate parts — service is the main object.

💡 Eg.114: OEM pays money to Reseller for promoting sale — object CANNOT be 'promoting sale of stock that already belongs to Reseller' because OEM has already received full payment. No consideration flows to OEM.
2.6.2 — Titles and Qualifications ⇧ Index
🏷️ Titles of Documents Do NOT Determine Object of Contract
📝 The name given to a contract document — 'Joint Development Agreement', 'Service Agreement', 'Sale Agreement' — does not decide what the contract actually is. The real object must be found from the substance of the terms, not the title.
MISCHIEVOUS TITLES
Titles used to procure advantageous tax treatment — discernment of object cannot be abdicated to title given to document
💡 Eg.115: Unregistered 'Joint Development Agreement' confers no interest in immovable property → legally it is ONLY an agreement for construction services where consideration = surrender of constructed units by Landowner (Contractee) to Developer (Contractor).
QUALIFICATIONS OF PROMISOR
Object of contract CANNOT be ascertained from qualifications of Promisor — erroneously object is often derived from circumstances surrounding transaction without giving due consideration to contractual relations
💡 Eg.116: Patient billed by Surgeon + portion shared with Hospital. Hospital-Patient contract is for 'healthcare services'. Surgeon-Hospital contract is for 'allowing use of infrastructure' — not healthcare services by Surgeon to Hospital.
OSTENSIBLE OBJECT ERROR
Common error: 'Ostensible object' — whether commercially expressed explanation accurately exposes legal relations qua the object, or is grossly mistaken and needs independent ascertainment of the lawful object.

Eg.117: Builder offers to pay 'rent' to apartment Buyers for duration of delay — apartment is not complete, so 'rent' is the common expression for liquidated damages for delay. Object = compensation, not rent.
📝 In GST: Calling something 'rent' or 'service charge' does not determine its taxability. The real legal nature of the payment determines the GST rate and exemption eligibility.
2.6.3 — Alteration of Object ⇧ Index
🔀 Alteration of Object — Substitution vs Variation
📝 When parties agree to change the original contract — is it just a 'variation' (modified same contract) or a 'substitution' (one contract killed and new one born)? The answer massively changes GST treatment — especially for debit/credit notes and advance received.
VARIATION
Pith of original contract remains intact
Alteration of quantity = variation
Same object, adjusted terms
📝 Think of it as the same river — you've just changed how much water flows. The river (contract) is still the same.
💡 Eg.118: Performance bonus to Employee = NOT a new contract but variation in consideration (salary) clause of original employment contract — enforceable.
SUBSTITUTION
Alteration of object itself = substitution
If variation is so extensive that a person of reasonable trade knowledge cannot recognize original contract → termination by substitution
📝 The original contract is dead. Rights and remedies under old contract = non est. Both parties must settle independently or under new contract.
💡 Eg.119: PO for rice but tax invoice for coal — substitution, not variation.

💡 Eg.120: PO for ready-mix concrete but invoice for concrete flooring works — substitution with new contract (new object + new consideration). Tax on advance received and subsequent treatment = vastly different.
STILK v. MYRICK PRINCIPLE
Sailors' demand for additional sum to bring vessel to shore — accepted by Captain in mid-sea — held NOT payable. Sailors performed originally agreed obligations only. Captain's promise was without consideration → not a valid variation or substitution.
📝 Key principle for GST: Debit note / credit note used in accounts does NOT compel or dictate the legal nature of the alteration. Whether it is variation or substitution is a question of FACT — discerned from arrangement and conduct of Parties. Revenue cannot assume or impute without evidence.
2.6.4 — Variation or Substitution — Price Variation ⇧ Index
💲 Variation in Price — Three Possible Explanations
📝 When the price changes after a contract is entered into, we must ask: why did it change? The three explanations have different GST consequences. Documents — meanings and implications for GST — cannot be abdicated to wisdom of commercial contract-writing but must be distilled from rights and obligations arising from the object of every contract.
(a) VARYING FACTOR
Directly affects ruling price per unit — must be tested for validity of assumption or assertion
📝 E.g., commodity price-linked contract — price varies with steel index. This is a genuine, verifiable market-driven variation.
(b) PREMIUM OR DISCOUNT
Agreed premium/discount over the ruling price per unit — has not otherwise varied except to extent of this supervening charge
(c) ANTICIPATED BREACH
Original contract permitted to survive — variation in price = extent of compensation demanded and accepted
Adjusted akin to cross-cancelling supplies — compensation = independent transaction with consideration flowing in opposite direction
📝 Example: Seller knows Buyer will not pay — accepts lower price as 'compensation' for anticipated breach. This compensation is an independent supply flowing in reverse — needs its own GST treatment.
FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE
Contract that cannot be performed may be: (a) terminated and substituted with new contract, OR (b) continued subject to variation by severance of terms that cannot be performed.
The attending treatment may not be the same in either case — and this demands careful study of the object of every contract.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 2 | Contract Act | Consideration, Document & Object ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Foakes v. Beer (1884) 9 App Cas 605
House of Lords, England | Applicable in GST disputes on late payment charges, interest, and time value adjustments
Key Ratio / Principle
Promise to alter schedule of payments (of previously agreed consideration) in exchange for agreement to pay additional charge akin to some measure of time value of money — upheld as not being unreasonable and binding force allowed to the alteration caused. Nature of such charge remains 'late payment charge' and not 'interest' — hence taxable and NOT exempt from GST.
When to Use — When Department argues that a 'late payment charge' is 'interest' and hence exempt from GST. Cite Foakes v. Beer to establish that time-value charge arising from delayed payment is a contractual late payment charge — a taxable service — and cannot be called interest merely because it is computed on a time-value basis.
Stilk v. Myrick (ibid) | Thomas v. Thomas (ibid)
Common Law | Contract Act Principles | Applicable in post-supply adjustment disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
Promise by Captain to pay additional amount to sailors who merely performed their existing contractual duty (bring vessel to shore) held NOT payable — promise lacked consideration. Post-performance alteration of consideration is impossible for lack of consideration to Promisor to accept lesser amount. Debit note or credit note in accounts does NOT compel or dictate the legal nature of an alteration — whether variation or substitution is a question of fact discerned from arrangement and conduct of Parties.
When to Use — When assessee or Department argues that a credit note / debit note changes the nature of supply or reduces taxable value. Cite to establish that financial records do not define legal nature — only the contract and conduct of Parties does.
Pinnel's Case (1602) 5 Co Rep 117
Common Law England | Departed from in Indian Law | Relevant for Remission & Compromise disputes
Key Principle
English law — payment of lesser sum cannot satisfy an obligation to pay a larger sum. This principle has been departed from in Indian law under Section 63 of Contract Act — accepting lesser consideration not only addresses time value of money but also brings about certainty about settlement. Compromise with creditors is not imprudent and is even advisable to save corporate assets — built into Corporate and Insolvency laws in India.
When to Use — When Department argues that accepting lesser consideration (compromise/settlement/IBC resolution) should attract GST on the original (full) consideration. Cite Indian departure from Pinnel to argue that Section 63 dispensation is valid and GST cannot be demanded on consideration that was legally remitted.
Section 15(3)(b) CGST Act — 7 Essential Elements for Post-Sales Discount
Statutory Provision | CGST Act 2017 | Read with Section 34 and Rule 37 CGST Rules
Key Ratio / Principle
Tax adjustment in respect of post-sales discount is available only if the contingency was known at time of contract, the event was uncertain, came to pass before settlement, was not controllable by either Party, the effect on price was not subject to fresh negotiations, occurrence was undeniable, and price was finally determined net of adjustment when the measurement period ended. Assertion that a 'special discount' is allowed implicitly admits that tax adjustment under Section 15(3)(b) read with Section 34 to Supplier and Rule 37 to Recipient will NOT be applicable.
When to Use — (i) To defend eligible post-sales discounts against Department's denial — argue all 7 elements are satisfied. (ii) To challenge excessive or unexplained post-sales discounts claimed by counterparty in ITC matters — show one or more elements are absent.
Consensus Ad Idem Principle — Object of Contract
Foundational Contract Law Principle | Applicable in all composite supply / principal supply determination disputes
Key Principle
Object of contract is that which contracting Parties have had consensus ad idem about. Opinion of that Party who knows least about the object is a reliable guide as to the real object of the contract — because consensus cannot be about that which one of them knows nothing or very little of. Object of contract cannot be identified from title of contract document — titles understood in common parlance are used and at other times mischievous titles may be used to procure advantageous treatment. Discernment of object cannot be abdicated to title given to the document.
When to Use — When Department determines principal supply based on contract title ('Joint Development Agreement', 'Works Contract') without analyzing actual terms. Argue: object must be determined from what Parties actually agreed — not from the label on the document. Especially powerful in JDA disputes where unregistered document is titled 'JDA' but legally is only a construction services contract.
Rule 37 CGST Rules — ITC Reversal and Bad Debts
CGST Rules 2017 | Read with Sections 16 & 34 | Bad Debt, Write-Off, and Discount Disputes
Key Principle
Writing off receivables does not alter the contract — it only leaves Promisee's performance outstanding. Decision to write off receivable does not abrogate right to enforce recovery. Since right to enforce recovery is neither automatic nor compulsory, right remains until limitation lapses. Consequences of restriction on credit under Rule 37 of Central GST Rules affect Recipient's entitlement to claim input tax credit. Unilateral write-off by Supplier generates credit note for accounting hygiene — but credit note under Section 34 is permitted only in a subset of circumstances — not for all business write-offs.
When to Use — (i) When Supplier's unilateral write-off is being mischaracterized as a bilateral arrangement triggering cross-supply. (ii) When Recipient's ITC is challenged under Rule 37 for non-payment — argue whether non-payment is 'failure to pay' or commercially agreed deferment. (iii) When Section 34 credit note eligibility is disputed.
Contract Splitting — Mischievous Contract Writing
GST Litigation Principle | Para 2.5.3 & 2.5.4 | Applicable in Works Contract / Composite Supply / ITC Disputes
Key Principle
Artificially vivisecting an otherwise indivisible arrangement violates the unitary bargain of the Parties — especially where contracting Parties are same, related, or acting-in-concert. Clever contract-writing to optimize tax cannot be carried out contrary to trade customs which includes regulatory or technological impossibilities. Finding-of-fact as to the object of each contract puts the tax treatment to rest. A deep study of the document is required — rushing to a conclusion based on the document presented by Parties is unbecoming of first principles of Contract law which cannot be denied or twisted just to canvass an additional tax demand.
When to Use — When Department alleges 'contract splitting' and seeks to combine two separate contracts into composite supply. Argue: indivisibility must be tested on three parameters (cross-fall breach, contract sum reference, completion date linkage). Where contracts are genuinely divisible, no mischief exists. Equally available to Department when assessee splits contracts for rate arbitrage.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 2c — Consideration (Para 2.4)

Para 2.4 — Consideration | Contract Act

Sections 2.4.8 to 2.4.21 | Source: Allied Laws — GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

🗺️ Legend
⬛ Root Node
🔵 Level 1 Branch
✅ Permitted / Positive
❌ Prohibited / Negative
⚠️ Condition / If / Unless
🟣 Optional / Pass-on Route
ℹ️ Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 Index — Para 2.4 Consideration (Sections 2.4.8–2.4.21)
2.4.8 Non-monetary Consideration
2.4.9 Negative Consideration
2.4.10 Composite Consideration
2.4.11 Extraneous Consideration
2.4.12 Subsidy
2.4.13 Profit Protection
2.4.14 Negative Margin
2.4.15 Compensation ≠ Consideration
2.4.16 Accounting Income ≠ Consideration
2.4.17 Flow of Consideration Undeniable
2.4.18 Condition ≠ Consideration
2.4.19 Private Motivation ≠ Consideration
2.4.20 Legal Fiction of Consideration
2.4.21 Bait-and-Hook Pricing
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.4.8 — Non-Monetary Consideration
⬆ Index
NON-MONETARY CONSIDERATION
Consideration need not be in money — GST recognises and values it
BARTER
Goods/Services exchanged for non-monetary consideration
📝 You give me 5 kg of rice; I give you 2 kg sugar. No money changes hands — but GST still applies on the value of what is given.
💡 Eg 47: In JDA — Developer provides construction services to Landowner. Landowner gives development rights to Developer. Construction service = non-monetary consideration for development rights.
EXCHANGE (IMMOVABLE PROPERTY)
Where transaction involves immovable property — also non-monetary
📝 In a JDA: land is immovable property. When Landowner gives land rights in exchange for construction — this is non-monetary consideration in immovable property form.
ACCOUNTING LIMIT
Where transaction is in non-monetary form — it will NOT be 'measured in monetary terms' — accounting entries are not permitted (money measurement concept)
📝 Standard accounting requires everything in money. Where consideration is non-monetary (like land for flats), accountants cannot pass entries — but for GST, Section 15(1) and 15(4) step in to compute taxable value.
GST MECHANISM
Section 15(1) → if price absent/not sole consideration
Section 15(4) → attracts 'deemed value' rules
Section 15(5) → anti-litigation safe haven
Munjaal Manishbhai Bhatt v. UOI (2022) — supported by Wipro Ltd v. ACC (2015)
📝 When non-monetary consideration is involved, GST cannot simply ignore it. Government prescribes rules to arrive at taxable value — these rules are an 'anti-litigation measure' meaning they protect taxpayers if applied correctly.
TEST Any provision imposing condition of 'payment' of consideration must be tested: is it applicable only where monetary consideration is involved, or does it require flow of non-monetary consideration too? Context of provisions gives guidance.
📝 Simply because a GST rule uses the word 'payment' — it does not automatically mean it applies only to cash transactions. The context must be read — sometimes non-monetary consideration also triggers those provisions.
2.4.9 — Negative Consideration
⬆ Index
NEGATIVE CONSIDERATION
The 'not doing' of something can be valid, valuable, and lawful consideration
VALID Abstinence / Forbearance / Not doing of an act
= Can constitute valid enforceable consideration
📝 If you pay someone ₹1 lakh to NOT compete with you for 5 years — the act of NOT competing is 'negative consideration.' It has real value and creates enforceable contract.
Eg 48: "Keep quiet for 1 hour, I'll give ₹100" — Peace/quiet = valid consideration (not impossibly invalid contract)
Eg 49: "Do not engage in this trade for 5 years after sale of business for ₹10 crores" — Not uncommon contract (non-compete)
Eg 50: Assignment of copyright including restriction on re-writing similar content — valid contract
GST EXPLORATION NEEDED
Much remains to be explored in GST regarding incidence of tax based on 'tolerating an act'
Ref: Circular 178 (to be discussed later)
📝 Under GST Schedule II, 'tolerating an act or situation' is deemed a service. The law on when negative consideration becomes taxable service is still evolving. Circular 178 gives guidance but gaps remain.
💡 GST Hearing: When Department alleges that non-compete payment is a taxable service — argue: (a) Is there a contract? (b) Is it really 'toleration' or a contract obligation? (c) Circular 178 test.
2.4.10 — Composite Consideration
⬆ Index
COMPOSITE CONSIDERATION
Mixed monetary + non-monetary — or barter vs. cross-cancelling distinction
BARTER / EXCHANGE
One contract — consideration is non-monetary — both supplies flow from same contract
Eg 51: 1 kg sugar for 3 kg rice = barter
📝 True barter: single deal, single price, goods or services exchanged for other goods/services. GST still sees two supplies here, both taxable on their respective values.
NOT BARTER
Two separate contracts — goods for services back — cross-cancelling supplies — receivable 'settled' with payable
Eg 52: ₹100 payable for sugar 'settled' against ₹100 for rice = cross-cancelling supply, NOT barter
📝 When two independent deals are 'netted off' — each still has its own price. Setting off one against the other is mere settlement of accounts — not barter. Both remain fully taxable at their own values.
💡 GST Hearing: Department treats netting-off of receivables/payables as barter to deny ITC — argue: Two contracts exist, two independent supplies, two separate GST events — not barter at all.
COMPOSITE
Monetary + non-monetary components together
Eg 53: 10 kg sugar for (i) 10 kg rice AND (ii) ₹700 cash = composite consideration
📝 When you receive both goods and cash for your supply — the total taxable value is the monetary component PLUS the GST-imputed value of the non-monetary component. Cannot tax only the cash part.
JUDICIAL PRINCIPLE — Chappell & Co. Ltd v. Nestle Co. Ltd (1960) AC 87
Court held: Consideration was NOT limited to money alone — it included aggregate of cash component AND cost to secure required number of chocolate wrappers.
Taxable value in GST of transactions involving monetary and non-monetary consideration cannot be addressed ignoring this underlying principle.
📝 In simple terms: If a customer paid both money AND collected 3 chocolate wrappers to get a music record — the wrappers also formed part of consideration. Same logic applies in GST — whatever the buyer must 'do' to get the supply, that also forms part of value.
💡 JDA Application: Landowner pays partly in money and partly in land/development rights. Both components form the taxable value for the Developer's construction service — Chappell principle directly supports this.
⚠️ PARTIAL BARTER — RULE
Where both monetary and non-monetary components are in ONE composite document flowing under ONE contract — it is 'partial barter' for the non-monetary part. Supplier remains Promisor and Recipient remains Promisee for Contract law — but must NOT be equated with cross-cancelling supplies / netting-off.
2.4.11 — Extraneous Consideration
⬆ Index
EXTRANEOUS CONSIDERATION
Additional payments in same direction between same parties — when can they be treated as extra consideration for original supply?
DEFAULT RULE
Additional payments flowing between same Parties in SAME direction (Buyer → Seller) CANNOT readily be imputed as additional consideration for original supply
📝 If a buyer pays the seller extra money beyond the contract price — you cannot automatically add that to GST taxable value. The law requires more proof before doing so.
TO IMPUTE AS EXTRANEOUS — MUST PROVE:
(a) Original contract and price was a SHAM
(b) Additional consideration can be demanded with NO obligation to perform
(c) If asserted as 'payment for nothing' — it is akin to voluntary charitable contribution
📝 Revenue must conclusively establish that the original contract price was deliberately kept artificially low, with extra payment being the real price. A high burden of proof is needed.
DOCTRINE OF ELECTION
If Revenue asserts additional payment = additional consideration for original supply — Revenue FORFEITS right to demand tax on another separate transaction between same parties
(Two tax positions on same amount = barred)
📝 Revenue cannot eat the cake and have it too. If they say extra payment is part of Supply A — they cannot ALSO say it is consideration for Supply B. They must pick one position and stick to it.
💡 Hearing Use: Where SCN demands GST on additional payment as extraneous consideration — argue doctrine of election: Revenue must choose — is this original supply or another supply? Cannot be both.
TDS / INCOME TAX INDICATOR
If TDS characterization differs from supply contract characterization — imputation that amount received is additional consideration for original supply (and the treatment applied to original transaction) will be DEFEATED
📝 If the same payment is treated as 'professional fees' under Income Tax (attracting TDS under 194J) but Revenue under GST tries to call it 'additional contract price' — the Income Tax characterization contradicts the GST imputation, weakening Revenue's case.
FREE ISSUE MATERIAL — REFERENCE
Metal Box India Ltd v. CCE (SC) — notional additions allowed in certain circumstances under Central Excise. GST departs: Section 15(2)(b) limits additions to price paid. Free-issue-material excluded from value where contract price was exclusive — unless quantity consumed exceeds permitted wastage levels. These distinctions cannot be treated as 'extraneous consideration' under Sec 15(2)(b).
2.4.12 — Subsidy
⬆ Index
SUBSIDY
Third-party (State or other) collects part of consideration — when is it includible in taxable value?
DEFINITION
Promisor collects part of consideration from a Third-Party (State or otherwise interested person)
= Subsidy
📝 When Government (or anyone else) steps in to pay part of the price on behalf of the buyer — that amount is still 'consideration' for the seller — paid by a Stranger. Total consideration = Buyer's portion + Stranger's subsidy.
EXCEPTION — Sec 15(2)(e)
Subsidy from Government contributors WOULD be excluded from value IF Sec 15(2)(e) applied
BUT: Subsidy from NON-Government contributors must be INCLUDED in taxable value
📝 GST Law excludes only Government subsidies from taxable value. If a private company (like OEM to Dealer) gives a subsidy/support — it must be added to taxable value. Govt subsidy exclusion is narrow.
WITHDRAWAL-FAILURE TEST
Would Promisor (Supplier) 'withdraw' from performing obligations if Payer (any payer) FAILED to pay aggregate consideration including subsidy amount?
If YES → Subsidy forms integral part of contractual consideration
📝 Ask: Would the Supplier have refused to sell if the subsidy was NOT assured? If yes — the subsidy is economically essential to the deal — and should be part of GST taxable value.
💡 Eg 54 & 55: OEM sells phones at ₹20,000. Distributor asks OEM to assure ₹5,000 per unit to cover loss. OEM agrees. If OEM's assurance withdraws (defaults) — A refuses to sell. Therefore ₹5,000 = part of consideration.
DIRECT BENEFIT TRANSFER SCHEMES
In DBT: subsidy payable to Buyer is directly credited to Seller. Test to identify Beneficiary: Is there any time interval between date of supply and date of entitlement to subsidy? If interval is explained away as administrative delay only → Seller is Beneficiary. If interval has independent conditions (vesting conditions) → Payee is NOT the Beneficiary.
Eg 56: Fertilizer at ₹1,000 imported; sold at ₹300. ₹700 subsidy — right to claim accrues on sale by Distributor to Agriculturist on 1st July 2023 — Beneficiary is Agriculturist, NOT Importer.
📝 In DBT schemes, just because subsidy money flows through Seller's account does NOT make Seller the beneficiary. The timing of entitlement (when the right to claim arises) identifies the true beneficiary. If subsidy vests only after sale to end-consumer — Seller is merely a pass-through agent.
2.4.13 — Profit Protection
⬆ Index
PROFIT PROTECTION
OEM assures minimum profit to Dealers on resales — is OEM's payment additional consideration?
Contribution by OEM aimed at protecting minimum profit levels on resales by Dealer — when resale price falls below purchase price. OEM either: (a) allows post-sale discount OR (b) enters collateral 'incentive contract' to put surplus in Dealer's hands.
📝 If a dealer sells below cost — OEM's payment to cover this loss is NOT a gift. It is economically part of the original supply transaction — and will be treated as additional consideration for taxable value purposes.
HOW IS A SCHEME EXPOSED?
(a) Dealers are selling BELOW COST and continuing to do so
(b) Dealers' losses empirically covered by compensation flowing under obscure arrangements
(c) Dealer's behaviour to sell below cost is OTHERWISE INEXPLICABLE if withdrawal-failure test were administered
📝 If a dealer is consistently selling at a loss, no one can explain why commercially — except that someone is compensating them secretly. This 'scheme' will be exposed under the withdrawal-failure test and the compensation will be added to taxable value.
DescriptionNormal 1Normal 2Normal 3
Selling Price100100100
Cost Price80100110
Margin200(10)
AssessmentNilBEPGrowth — INVESTIGATE
5 CONDITIONS for Year-End Incentive to be admitted as genuine (not profit protection)
(i) Incentives well known and quantum estimated accurately in advance
(ii) Net effect of negative margin + incentive gives yield above average margins
(iii) OEM must NOT have a say in Reseller's discounted pricing
(iv) Deeply discounted price must NOT have predatory effects on other resellers
(v) OEM's indifference to such pricing decision must be explainable
💡 Eg 57: OEM pays ₹5,000 per unit to compensate dealer loss at ₹20,000 selling price (dealer cost ₹25,000). Total consideration for OEM's supply = ₹20,000 + ₹5,000 = ₹25,000. Taxable value = ₹25,000.
2.4.14 — Negative Margin
⬆ Index
NEGATIVE MARGIN
Recurring sales with no or negative margins demand investigation — source of funds must be verified
Recurring sales with negative margins demand further investigation as prudence demands — resales without margins should not continue. When negative, business should be discontinued (due to unviability). Negative margin demands verification of 'source of funds' to finance this loss.
(a) CAPITAL
Resales made in anticipation volumes will pick up — current losses due to volumes below BEP — negative margins must be due to overheads only (not subsidy)
Eg 62: Revenue ₹100 Cr, COGS ₹150 Cr. Additional capital injected ₹50 Cr. Warrants for further ₹25 Cr per quarter.
📝 A startup or new entrant may accept losses for market share — but only if the losses are genuinely funded by their own capital investment (not by OEM secretly subsidising). To admit this: losses must be overhead-driven, not margin-driven.
(b) CROSS-SUBSIDISATION
Surplus from an associated transaction directly linked to volume of negative-margin sales, ensuring positive margins on combined basis
Eg 63: Food delivery — Food sales ₹100 Cr, COGS ₹120 Cr. Delivery charges revenue ₹40 Cr. Combined margins may be positive.
📝 If a business sells Product A below cost but makes good profit on Service B that always accompanies A — and the arrangement is transparent — combined view may show viable business. Revenue must examine the combined picture.
(c) PROFIT PROTECTION SCHEME
Beneficiary of higher volume of sales initiates another transaction ('incentive contract') that recoups losses
= Collateral arrangement → consolidated price taxable under original transaction rate
Eg 65: ECOM dictating deeply discounted price — sellers assured of incentives from ECOM to cover loss of normal profits.
📝 ECOM platforms (like Amazon, Flipkart) require sellers to offer heavy discounts. ECOM then compensates sellers through 'promotional support' or other schemes. Revenue will argue: the ECOM support is part of consideration for supply — taxable at supply's rate.
💡 GST Hearing: ECOM demands on below-cost pricing must be tested against withdrawal-failure: Would seller have listed products at loss WITHOUT ECOM assurance? If No → ECOM compensation is additional consideration.
DescriptionAbnormal 1Abnormal 2Abnormal 3
Selling Price100100100
Cost Price150150150
Margin(50)(50)(50)
YE Discount075—
OEM Support0—75
Revised Margin(50)2525
AssessmentBusiness UnviableVerify Discount PolicyInvestigate Terms of Incentive
2.4.15 — Compensation is NOT Consideration
⬆ Index
COMPENSATION ≠ CONSIDERATION
Using the word 'compensation' does NOT convert the payment into non-taxable amount
Criteria Consideration Compensation
Quantum Known in advance, agreed before supply Contingent upon quantum of damage
Determined by Promisor (Supplier) Promisee (Recipient)
Agreed by Promisee (Recipient) Promisor (Supplier)
Supply agreed but yet to be performed To be performed — obligations fulfilled Not to be performed yet — compensation due
Supply agreed and already performed Consideration due Consideration AND compensation both due
📝 If a supply is done and payment comes — it is consideration. If supply is NOT done but payment comes — it could be compensation. When BOTH supply is done AND extra payment comes for harm caused — it is consideration + compensation together. Cannot label the whole amount 'compensation' to escape GST.
INSURER PAYMENTS
Just because Insurer pays does NOT mean it is always 'compensation.' Must examine break-up:
(i) Actual loss
(ii) Opportunity loss
(iii) Reduction to extent of realizable value of wreck
Entire insurance settlement CANNOT be excluded from GST without examining heads.
📝 Insurance claims have multiple components. Some parts may include consideration for supply (e.g., reinstatement cost for services already agreed). Revenue will examine each head — not simply accept 'it's all compensation.'
ARBITRAL AWARDS
Compensation awarded by Arbitral Tribunal does NOT automatically eliminate consideration component. Must examine break-up — each component carries its own GST treatment. Tribunal/Court is only the forum to determine amount — PURPOSE for which payment is directed determines GST treatment.
📝 An arbitration award of ₹125 Cr may include: original work claim (₹900 taxable), additional works (₹200 taxable), rework costs (non-taxable as damages), pain & suffering (non-taxable), interest on delay (non-taxable). Cannot treat full ₹125 Cr as compensation.
💡 GST Hearing: Where SCN demands GST on full arbitral award — argue: break-up the award into components. Only those components relating to supply consideration attract GST. Pure damage components do not.
2.4.16 — Accounting Income is NOT Consideration
⬆ Index
ACCOUNTING INCOME ≠ CONSIDERATION
Not every income in P&L is consideration from a supply
GST is NOT a 'tax on turnover.' Impoverished understanding of accounting and contract law to assume every income in financial statements arises from accrual of consideration from a contract for outward supply liable to a certain treatment in GST.
📝 Just because something appears as 'income' in accounts does not mean GST is payable on it. GST is on 'supply' — and supply requires a contract, a promise, and consideration flowing from Promisee. Accounting entries are not contracts.
NOT CONSIDERATION
Eg 66: Creditors written back = 'other income' — NOT consideration from supply
Eg 67: Excess provision of earlier years written back = 'other income' — NOT consideration
Eg 68: Restatement gains in foreign currency = 'other income' — NOT consideration
📝 These are accounting adjustments — not actual receipts for any supply made. Revenue cannot demand GST on these merely because they appear in the P&L as income.
💡 GST Hearing: Department raises demand on creditors written back — argue: This is an accounting entry, not consideration for any supply. No contract, no supply, no GST. Burden on Department to show the supply that this is consideration for.
ACCOUNTING vs GST DIVERGENCE
Accounting standards (Ind AS) are now so sophisticated that income and consideration have lost correlation in several sectors.
Eg 69: Asset given on lease — capitalised in Lessor's books at historical cost AND ALSO capitalised in Lessee's books at NPV of future lease rentals.
= Same asset in two sets of books — but single supply/consideration exists
📝 Ind AS finance lease accounting creates double-entry income recognition that does not match GST reality. Cannot impute GST on accounting income that arises from standards-driven capitalization — it is not a second supply.
2.4.17 — Flow of Consideration Undeniable
⬆ Index
FLOW OF CONSIDERATION UNDENIABLE
Where actual inflow of monetary or documented non-monetary consideration occurs — it must be explained
Accounting income may not represent consideration — but actual inflow of monetary consideration or documented realization of non-monetary consideration beg to be explained. Rebutting allegation that every income = consideration from supply must be consideration and hence liable to disclosure (if not subject to) tax treatment, requires proper explanation.
📝 If money actually came in — you cannot simply say 'it's not consideration.' You must explain what it is. The burden shifts to the taxpayer to show that actual cash inflow is not from a supply.
NON-MONETARY — WHEN DOES IT 'FLOW'?
Non-monetary consideration is said to flow when the rights attendant to that transaction devolve in the eyes of applicable law onto the Promisor (Supplier)
Eg 70: Rights to develop land flow to Developer immediately upon registration of JDA — even if nothing done at project site
Eg 71: Notice of termination of lease where forfeiture of deposit agreed in Lease Deed → immediately results in realization of income out of refundable security deposit
Eg 72: Title passes at the fall of hammer in case of auction
📝 For non-monetary consideration — the moment of 'flow' is when legal rights transfer — not when physical possession changes or money changes hands. In JDA — rights flow on registration. In lease termination — rights to forfeit deposit flow on notice (not on cheque receipt).
💡 JDA GST Point of Taxation: Eg 70 confirms — taxable event for Developer's supply is registration of JDA, not completion of construction. Developer's consideration (development rights) flows on registration date.
2.4.18 — Condition is NOT Consideration
⬆ Index
CONDITION ≠ CONSIDERATION
Carlill v. Carbolic Smokeball Company — Classic leading case
CARLILL v CARBOLIC SMOKEBALL CO. — FACTS
Company advertised: use smokeball per instructions and get reward if you contract influenza. Carlill used smokeballs, contracted influenza, claimed reward. Court: Company's Offer → Carlill's Acceptance → by consuming smokeballs → valid contract. Contracting influenza was NOT consideration — it was the 'condition' to claim reward. Consideration = Carlill ingesting the smokeballs.
📝 Simple version: Company's offer was 'use our product and get reward if sick.' Carlill's act of USING the smokeballs = consideration (what she did). Getting sick = just the condition that proved she used them. Getting sick was NOT the price of the smokeballs — using them was.
REWARD NOT CONSIDERATION
Reward paid by Company is NOT consideration because consideration must flow FROM Promisee (Carlill) and NOT from Promisor (Company).
Consideration must be 'desired' by Promisor — what is desired must proceed from Promisee.
5-PART TEST — IS PAYMENT A CONDITION OR CONSIDERATION?
(a) Does non-delivery foreclose executory contract / discharge Promisor / result in breach by Promisee?
(b) Does separate and distinct consideration exist independent of this payment?
(c) Does it flow from Promisee (Acceptor) and NOT from Promisor?
(d) Does contract continue (with variation) on non-payment or compel termination?
(e) Is this sum 'valuable' as equitable exchange proportionate to obligations performed?
📝 If payment satisfies all 5 — it is consideration. If it fails even one — it could be a mere 'condition' for accessing the contract, not the price of the contract itself. Conditions do not attract GST as consideration.
💡 Eg 73: Zero-balance savings bank account = condition to contract — NOT consideration. Bank does not get consideration from zero-balance requirement — bank's motivation is commercial (idle funds). Eg 74: Filing fee to regulator = condition, NOT consideration for approval. Regulator may still reject. No refund on rejection.
CONSIDERATION EXAMPLES
Eg 75: Sitting fee = consideration for Director discharging duties
Eg 76: Entry fee for event = consideration (even if styled as 'fee')
Eg 77: Processing fee for loan application = consideration for processing — even if loan ultimately rejected due to document issues
Thomas v. Thomas (1842) 2 QB 851
Executor demanded widow remain unmarried to stay in matrimonial home. Singlehood = condition to continue occupation — NOT consideration. Consideration for accommodation must be examined elsewhere in the arrangement.
📝 Remaining single was a qualification/condition — not the price paid. The widow was not 'buying' the accommodation by staying single. This principle applies in GST: just because you must comply with a condition to access a supply does not mean the compliance = consideration.
2.4.19 — Private Motivation is NOT Consideration
⬆ Index
PRIVATE MOTIVATION ≠ CONSIDERATION
WHY a party enters a contract is irrelevant — WHAT is the price of the promise is the only question
Law is NOT interested in private motivations of each Party for entering into contract. Price at which the promise is purchased = consideration. Motivation may be noble (rob a bank to feed the poor) — but intention is wrong. Law attributes intention based on conduct, not private reasoning.
📝 Why you entered a deal is irrelevant to tax. What you promised and what you received = consideration. 'I did it for good reasons' is not a defence for anything legally, including GST treatment.
INTERNET ECONOMY TRAP
Where search, email or social platform is given 'free' — Users are NOT Customers. Users are the commodity traded between Operator and Advertisers.
Users pay by providing data and attention — but Operators are paid by Advertisers.
Tri-party/multi-party arrangements cannot be unbundled into simple bi-party ones for GST imputation.
📝 When you use Google for free — you are not the customer; you are the product. Google's customers are advertisers. The 'free service' to you is the bait to attract your data and attention which Google monetizes. GST imputation cannot assume you are the paying customer.
💡 Eg 79: Publishing newspaper costs ₹15 per copy. Advertisements contribute ₹14 per copy. Readers pay ₹2. The private motivation of Operator (to attract users for advertiser revenue) does NOT make the User's 'free' access = GST taxable supply at market value.
CORPORATE GUARANTEE TRAP
Holding company extends guarantee for subsidiary — motivation may be to avoid injecting own capital. This motivation CANNOT be imputed as consideration. Any imputation requires legal fiction — and legal fiction must be EXPRESS, not implied.
📝 Parent company guarantees child company's loan. Revenue says: 'Parent got benefit — so it's a service — GST payable.' But mere motivation to protect investment cannot constitute consideration. Law requires express fiction (like Schedule I) to deem it supply. Cannot be implied.
💡 Eg 78: Acquisition of sick company for ₹10 Cr based on fair value — motivation is potential highway access rights. Consideration = ₹10 Cr price. Motivation (future access value) is irrelevant for GST valuation.
JUDICIAL TEST — INTENTION
Intention is attributed by law from conduct of persons reasonably construed by a person of that trade with full knowledge of undisputed facts.
For failure to be explicit about intention — conduct is next best indicator.
Judicial task: NOT to discover private motivations BUT to decide what may reasonably and responsibly be concluded to be consideration by observing conduct.
📝 Courts don't read minds. They look at what you did, in the context of your trade, and draw reasonable conclusions. The standard is a reasonable business person in that trade — not the internal email you never sent to anyone.
CONTRACT WRITING IMPERATIVE
Contracts (even T&Cs) must be easy to understand and must declare the consideration plainly. Contract without consideration is void ab initio. T&Cs meant to be enforceable CANNOT be without consideration — even if consideration is non-monetary (like sign-up act).
Eg 80: Signing-up to use a platform = consideration. Physician agreeing to receive free samples + distribute to patients = consideration for Pharma company.
📝 If you offer something free but expect users to sign up, use, share data or refer others — those acts are your consideration. Write it in the contract plainly. Obscure T&Cs that hide consideration invite GST disputes where Revenue imputes its own value.
2.4.20 — Legal Fiction of Consideration
⬆ Index
LEGAL FICTION OF CONSIDERATION
Schedule I transactions — missing ingredients supplied by legal fiction — but within strict limits
PURPOSE OF SCHEDULE I
Transactions failing for missing ingredients of valid contract → Schedule I furnishes 'missing ingredients' to bring about trappings of valid contract → procure treatment applicable to valid contract
📝 Schedule I is like a legal 'repair kit' — it fixes a transaction that lacks one element (like consideration) by deeming that element to exist. Without this fiction, the transaction would not attract GST.
LIMITS OF LEGAL FICTION
Legal fiction CANNOT be extended beyond terms for which it is legislated.
Schedule I only furnishes ingredient of consideration — does NOT introduce a contract where NONE exists.
Revenue CANNOT use Schedule I to dictate underlying transaction of 'deemed supply' and impose tax treatment.
📝 Legal fiction has boundaries. Revenue cannot say: 'Schedule I makes everything between related parties a supply.' There must still be an actual arrangement/transaction that Schedule I then deems to be 'with consideration.' No arrangement = no supply = Schedule I inapplicable.
💡 GST Hearing: When Revenue invokes Schedule I for related-party transactions — argue: (a) Is there any actual arrangement? (b) Have parties acted with offer-acceptance? (c) Schedule I only fills the consideration gap — it cannot create the contract itself.
NON-MONETARY CONSIDERATION — NO LEGAL FICTION OPERATES
Transactions NOT involving monetary consideration do NOT operate under any legal fiction.
Monetary value of non-monetary consideration is to be arrived at as prescribed in Rules (not through fiction).
Taxable value based on legal fiction ≠ imputation about existence of consideration.
📝 Legal fiction fills the 'consideration exists' gap for Schedule I. But it does NOT create a monetary value for non-monetary consideration. The value of barter/exchange must be computed by the Rules separately — legal fiction is not a valuation tool.
RELATED PARTY RULE
For related-party transactions to be liable to GST: (a) transaction must be REAL (b) valid consideration must exist. Private motives cannot be treated as consideration just because parties are related and come within Schedule I. If related Parties have acted on their volition and no contract with 'offer-acceptance' attributes exist — merely being related cannot impute or impose existence of a contract.
Ratan Melting and Wire Industries (SC) 231 ELT 22 — Revenue can deviate from incongruous circulars if they run against first principles.
2.4.21 — Bait-and-Hook Pricing
⬆ Index
BAIT-AND-HOOK PRICING
Equipment sold at bait price — back-end profits from inevitable consumables — single or split transaction?
Customers who purchase equipment with nearly irreplaceable consumable are 'hooked' to brand for regular purchases. Equipment may be supplied at deeply discounted 'bait' pricing — back-end profits from inevitable consumable sales more than compensate for lost profits on equipment.
📝 Classic example: Sell a printer at ₹5,000 below cost — knowing customer MUST buy your cartridges for 5 years at high margins. The cartridge profits subsidise the printer loss. Revenue will ask: Is the printer deal and the cartridge deal really ONE transaction — or two?
RISK — CONTRACT SPLITTING
Question: Is this impermissible 'contract splitting'?
When UNRELATED persons are engaged — needs far more incriminating linkages to be admitted as a single transaction.
Eg 81: Aircraft engine sold at 'bait price' — OEM maintenance mandated for 30 years by regulations — single integrated arrangement
Eg 82: Cartridge non-interchangeable with other brands — captive consumable supply
📝 Revenue may argue that printer + cartridge supply is ONE transaction split artificially. But for unrelated parties, this needs strong evidence — like joint negotiations for both supplies at time of equipment purchase.
CONDITIONS FOR SINGLE TRANSACTION ADMISSION
(a) Decision to purchase consumables made AT TIME OF PURCHASE of equipment — joint negotiations entered for both
AND other incriminating factors showing single commercial objective
📝 If customer signed ONE agreement covering both equipment and consumables at time of purchase — with joint negotiation on total cost — Revenue can treat it as one transaction. But if consumable purchase was independent — separate transactions apply.
💡 GST Hearing: Where Department seeks to club equipment and consumable into one supply to change rate/value — argue: (a) Two separate contracts (b) No joint negotiation at time of equipment purchase (c) Consumable price determined independently in open market.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Para 2.4 | Consideration | Contract Act ⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, CESTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Chappell & Co. Ltd. v. Nestle Co. Ltd. (1960) AC 87
House of Lords | UK | Para 2.4.10
Key Ratio
Consideration is not limited to the money component alone — it is the aggregate of the cash component and the cost incurred by the buyer to secure the required non-monetary element. Taxable value in GST of transactions involving monetary and non-monetary consideration cannot be addressed ignoring this underlying principle.
When to Use — Where Department treats only monetary part of JDA/composite consideration as taxable value — argue: non-monetary component (development rights/land) must also be valued and included. Chappell principle mandates aggregation.
Munjaal Manishbhai Bhatt v. Union of India (2022) 138 taxmann.com 117 / 92 GST 327 / 62 GSTL 262 (Gujarat HC)
Gujarat High Court | 2022 | Para 2.4.8
Key Ratio
Section 15(5) of Central GST Act is a safe haven and an anti-litigation measure that cannot be lightly dismissed. The provision prescribes the manner in which non-monetary consideration is to be valued — and this prescription is binding on both the taxpayer and Revenue.
When to Use — In JDA disputes where Revenue seeks to value non-monetary consideration differently from the prescribed Rules. Cite to invoke the statutory protection of Section 15(5) and the anti-litigation purpose behind deemed valuation rules.
Carlill v. Carbolic Smokeball Company — Doctrine: Condition ≠ Consideration
Classic Common Law — Absorbed in Indian Contract Act | Para 2.4.18
Key Ratio
A condition to contract — even if it involves physical acts or payments — is not consideration. Consideration must be 'desired' by the Promisor and must proceed from the Promisee. Acts performed merely to satisfy a condition precedent to claiming a benefit are not the price of the promise and do not constitute consideration.
When to Use — Where Revenue characterises a 'condition' payment (zero-balance account, filing fee, entry qualification) as GST taxable consideration for a supply. Apply the five-part test: does non-payment foreclose the contract? Does separate consideration exist? Does it flow from Promisee?
Withdrawal-Failure Test — Doctrine for Subsidy / Profit Protection / Third-Party Payment
Jurisprudential Principle | Para 2.4.12 & 2.4.13
Key Principle
Whether a third-party payment (subsidy, OEM support, ECOM incentive) forms part of contractual consideration is tested by asking: Would the Promisor (Supplier) withdraw from performing obligations contained in the contract if any Payer failed to pay aggregate consideration including the third-party amount? If yes — the third-party payment is integral part of contractual consideration and taxable accordingly.
When to Use — In two situations: (a) Where assessee argues OEM support / ECOM incentive is not taxable — Department applies this test to add to taxable value. (b) Where assessee argues subsidy should be excluded — apply the test to show Supplier would still have supplied without subsidy (so it was truly gratuitous and NOT consideration).
Doctrine of Election — Revenue Cannot Take Two Positions on Same Payment
Legal Doctrine | Para 2.4.11 — Extraneous Consideration
Key Principle
By asserting that an additional payment received is additional consideration for original supply contract, Revenue forfeits its right to demand tax on another transaction between the same two Parties which may not share the same taxing ingredients as that applicable to the original supply contract. Revenue cannot take two tax positions on the same payment.
When to Use — Where SCN characterises an additional payment as both 'extraneous consideration for original supply' AND demands separate GST on the other transaction — argue: doctrine of election bars Revenue from taking both positions simultaneously. They must choose one and abandon the other.
Compensation ≠ Consideration — Arbitral Awards / Insurance Settlements
Principle from Para 2.4.15 | Applicable in works contract, construction, infrastructure disputes
Key Principle
An Arbitral Tribunal or Court is only the forum to determine the amount to be paid. The purpose for which the payment is directed — not the label on the award — determines the extent of consideration involved for supply made. Not all components of an arbitral award constitute consideration. Each component must be examined separately for its character — supply-related or damage-related.
When to Use — Where Revenue demands GST on full arbitral award received by contractor. Argue: break up the award into components. Claim for additional works (supply) → GST applicable. Rework costs, pain & suffering, interest for delay → NOT consideration for supply → GST not applicable.
CCE v. Ratan Melting and Wire Industries — 231 ELT 22 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | Para 2.4.20
Key Ratio
Revenue can (respectfully) deviate from incongruous Circulars if it runs against the grain of first principles tested for compliance with first principles. Similarly, the taxpayer can take advantage of a beneficial Circular but as permitted by the Apex Court in this case. Judicial authority is most desirable on issues of legal fiction and deemed supply.
When to Use — In Schedule I / legal fiction disputes where Revenue relies on a Circular to expand the scope of deemed supply. Argue: Circular cannot expand the statutory provision. Statute confines legal fiction. Ratan Melting allows Revenue to deviate from their own incongruous Circular — equally taxpayer can resist Circular that goes beyond first principles of Schedule I.
Section 15(2)(b) CGST Act — Limitation on Additions to Price
Statutory Provision | Para 2.4.11 — Extraneous Consideration
Key Principle
GST has made a significant departure from Central Excise in limiting additions to price paid — only to those instances falling under Section 15(2), particularly Section 15(2)(b). In Central Excise, valuation was the search for 'value or worth' of excisable goods. In GST the search is limited to contractual price for the object of supply. Free-issue-material and other extraneous payments not within Section 15(2)(b) cannot be added to taxable value.
When to Use — Where Revenue seeks to add free-issue-material, tools supplied to job-workers, or other items to taxable value. Counter: Under GST regime (unlike Central Excise), only those additions expressly permitted under Section 15(2) can be made to contractual price. Section 15(2)(b) is the ceiling — not a floor.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | GST Litigation Practice
📖 Ch 2d — Contract Act Part 2 (Para 2.6–2.10)

⚖️ Chapter 2 (Part 2) — Contract Act | Para 2.6–2.10

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
2.6.5 Valuable Consideration 2 — Substance vs Object
2.7.1 Privity — Contractual Relations
2.7.2 Locus Standi
2.7.3 Conjoint Purchasers & Fractional Ownership
2.7.4 Privity with Contractor's Workmen
2.7.5 Locus of Distinct Persons (DP-HO / DP-B)
2.7.6 Locus of Overseas Branches / PE
2.8.1 to 2.8.3 Breach — Types & Damages
2.8.4 to 2.8.7 Breach Types | Supplementary | Repudiation
2.8.8 to 2.8.10 Tortious Remedy | Election | Specific Relief
2.9 Indemnity — Bipartite | Consideration | Insurance | Damnation
2.10 Guarantee — Tripartite Contract
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.6.5 — Valuable Consideration 2 | Substance vs Object of Contract ⇧ Index
💰 Valuable Consideration 2 — Substance ≠ Additional Consideration
CST v. Bhayana Builders (P) Ltd. (2018) 91 taxmann.com 109
KEY DISTINCTION
Consideration can be 'in kind' — but when the 'substance' of a contract is NOT the 'object' of that contract → it is often mistaken to be additional consideration
(especially when abatement is allowed on gross consideration)
📝 "Substance" = the material/thing given freely to enable performance. "Object" = the actual service or goods being contracted. These are different. The substance is not extra payment — it is a tool needed to perform the object.
GST RULE
GST relies on contract price itself for valuation (unlike Excise 'assessable value' concept)
Must test whether any need to make valuation adjustments at all
📝 Under old Excise law (Metal Box India), 'freely issued material' added to assessable value. Under GST, the contract price IS the value. If something is given freely to enable service, it need not be added to taxable value.
PROHIBITED
Taxable value of object of supply cannot be marked-up by freely issued material/substance
(except for limited purposes of abatement calculation)
VARIATION CAUTION
Tax treatment in case of variation in price must carefully attend whether variation relates to original contract or creates new consideration arrangement
📝 If price changes — ask why. Is it a discount? A revision? Or a new supply at different rate? Revenue making assumptions about this without investigation can be fatal to the demand.
Example No.Substance (given freely)Object of ContractGST Position
Eg.121Air-conditioner given by Customer to SupplierInstallation services (object of contract)Substance ≠ additional consideration; not added to service value
Eg.122Raw material given freely to Job-workerJob-work services (object)Raw material = substance; not taxable as additional consideration to job-worker
Eg.123Amortized value of moulds given to Job-workerFinished product supply (object)Amortized value liable to be added to contract price of finished product — sale is object, not job-work
Eg.124Manuscript given freely to PrinterPrinting services (object)Object = printing service (not sale of books); paper, ink, labour added by Printer are part of service, not goods supply
2.7.1 — Privity of Contract | Contractual Relations ⇧ Index
🔗 Privity of Contract — Who Has Rights & Obligations Under a Contract?
📝 Privity = being a party to the contract. Only parties to a contract have rights and duties under it. An outsider (Stranger) cannot sue or be sued under a contract they are not party to — even if the contract affects them.
Names Don't Determine Relations
Relations determined by terms in the contract — NOT by names used in trade
Dealer = customer to Manufacturer; Buyer = customer to Dealer
Buyer and Manufacturer have NO contractual relations with each other
📝 Just because you call someone a "dealer" or "distributor" or "agent" does not define their tax treatment. Read the contract terms — that defines who owes what to whom.
💡 Eg.125: GST discharged by main-contractor on entire contract sum does NOT excuse sub-contractor from GST liability on his own supply. Privity between each is separate.
Roles Can Reverse
Same two Parties permitted to constitute and operate under divergent contractual relations
Supplier in one supply → Recipient in another supply (and vice versa)
📝 In the morning you sell goods to B (you are Supplier). In the evening B provides services to you (B becomes Supplier). Each transaction has its own GST treatment — do not mix up roles.
💡 Eg.126: Authorised service centre selling scrap and paying GST as supply of goods may be misunderstanding — scrap belongs to Customer (replaced parts). Selling another's goods = supply of services (authority to retain proceeds = rental/service).
Sub-Contractor Privity
Sub-contractor has privity with main-contractor only — NOT with Customer
One who does the work and one who has legal authority to do the work are NOT interchangeable
📝 Customer giving instructions directly to sub-contractor does not mean Customer has a contract with sub-contractor. Those instructions are "on account of" the main-contractor. Privity remains with main-contractor.
💡 Eg.127: OEM gives incentives directly to Resellers bypassing Distributors → this is a separate transaction from OEM to Resellers, not reduction in Distributor's purchase cost. GST implications of each transaction separately examined.
2.7.2 — Locus Standi | Who Can Bring Action? ⇧ Index
⚖️ Locus Standi — The Right to Bring Legal Action
📝 Locus standi = "place to stand." It means: do you have the legal right to bring this claim? Only a party with privity of contract and lawful authority under it has locus standi. A Stranger can contribute consideration but cannot sue under the contract.
HAS LOCUS
Party with privity of contract who has lawful authority under contract to bring action
Persons without contractual privity but involved in contributing consideration or fulfilling taxable supplies should be identified to avoid misreading the nature of underlying transactions
NO LOCUS
Stranger to contract has NO rights or remedies (other than benefits) under that contract
📝 A Stranger who pays consideration gets the benefit of the goods/services — but cannot sue the seller for breach. That right belongs only to the Buyer (party to the contract).
CUSTOMER → SUB-CONTRACTOR
When Customer gives instructions to sub-contractor bypassing main-contractor — must identify:
(a) Instructions 'on account of' main-contractor (employer)
(b) OR Customer has direct contractual relationship with sub-contractor
📝 If Customer and sub-contractor are dealing directly — check the contract. Is there a direct contract? Or are Customer's instructions actually flowing through main-contractor's authority? This determines privity and hence GST liability.
Any unusual activity bypassing express contractual lines of authority (issuing invoices, making payments, giving deductions) shakes certainty of contractual relations → requires re-examination before being admitted
📝 If the actual behaviour of parties is different from what the contract says — that is a red flag. Courts call this "conduct of Parties contrary to terms of contract." Investigate before assigning GST treatment.
2.7.3 — Conjoint Purchasers | Fractional Ownership | Chit Fund | ECOM ⇧ Index
🤝 Conjoint Purchasers | Pooling & Appropriation Arrangements
Fractional Ownership
CONDITION
Fractional ownership possible only if object is capable of fractional enjoyment
If object cannot be fractionally enjoyed → title is NOT absolute but restrained → transaction is less than sale (lease or license)
📝 You can jointly own a plot of land (enjoyable fractionally). You cannot jointly own one mobile phone in practical use. If the object cannot be shared, any "transfer" is really a limited right (license), not a sale.
💡 Eg.128: Apartment owners jointly own undivided interest in land. They have individual interest in nature of license to enjoy subject to similar interest of every other owner — NOT absolute title to any specific portion.
Invoice → Title Transfer Rule
Invoice issued to X → title transfers to X (not to A who receives delivery)
Payment made by X → payment 'on account of' obligation of A to X
📝 Whoever the invoice is raised on = whoever gets title. If goods go to A but invoice is to X — title passed to X, not A. A's use of X's goods must be explained by some other contract between A and X.
💡 Eg.129–131: Raw material delivered to A, invoice to C → C owns the material. Eg.132 (ECOM): Mobile phones sold through ECOM — title flows to Buyer (pays ₹20,000). ECOM's ₹5,000 is separate — settles Buyer's obligation, not a reduction of Seller's consideration.
Chit Fund — Key Features
Each subscriber → privity exclusively with Foreman only
Subscribers have NO contractual privity with each other
Subscription = liability of Foreman (investment held in trust)
FEATURES
(a) Subscription = Foreman's liability | (b) No privity inter se subscribers | (c) Foreman liable to repay with interest | (d) Default of one subscriber → Foreman makes good to others | (e) Subscriptions paid back = repayment of investment | (f) Gains = discount & interest only
💡 Eg.133: 10 persons × ₹1,000 = ₹10,000 pool. Subscriber A bids ₹450 discount. Foreman pays A ₹9,550 and ₹50 each to remaining 9. Test: Is Foreman an intermediary (not mutual association)? Payment held as liability = deposit, not income.
Tambola/Bingo — Contrast with Chit
Payment by Participant = NOT held as liability of Organizer
Total amount collected = income to Organizer
Organizer NOT liable to repay → NOT a deposit
Prize money = expenditure of Organizer
Participants DO NOT have privity with each other
📝 In Tambola, Organizer keeps all collections as income and spends on prizes and arrangements. In Chit Fund, Foreman holds collections as liability (must repay). This fundamental difference drives completely different GST treatment.
🔄 Testing Wheel — Questions to Disaggregate Pooling Arrangements
Participation contingent on payment or optional?
Payment non-refundable or held as deposit?
