Contractual privity exists with Participants inter se?
Existence of intermediary essential?
Intermediary — not a mutual association of Participants?
Reward is use of income or diversion of income?
2.7.4 — Privity with Contractor's Workmen | Payment Without Privity ⇧ Index
💳 Payment Without Privity — "On Account Of" Principle
PRINCIPLE
Payer is NOT necessarily owner unless title passes to Payer
Payment without privity = payment 'on account of' obligor — purpose of such payment remains to be explained
📝 When Contractee pays sub-contractor's workmen directly — it is NOT because workmen have any privity with Contractee. It is done to ensure project payments flow — "on account of" the main Contractor. Contractee is settling Contractor's obligation.
TRACE CONSIDERATION
GST incidence attracts not only when taxable supply has been made — but even when consideration towards a taxable supply yet to be made is collected
→ Trace the flow of consideration to locate the contract for supply
📝 Advance payment received BEFORE supply = still taxable (time of supply = date of advance). Track who pays, to whom, for what — that locates the supply and its GST treatment.
💡 Eg.136: Developer in JDA supplies constructed area → GST liable even BEFORE completion of construction, since consideration (development rights) received on date of registration. 'Time of supply' attracted on date of JDA registration.
Purpose of Stranger's payment must be explained as:
(i) Debt repayment involving repayment with interest
(ii) Payment to settle earlier obligation outstanding
(iii) New transaction to ensue with Buyer
💡 Eg.137: CRS companies pay incentive to IATA agents at start of FY based on past performance and future targets → 'time of supply' attracted since consideration collected BEFORE supply of services (advance payment rule).
💡 Eg.138: Cashback by Credit Card Issuer to Cardholder for using credit card at refuelling stations = completion of supply of use of credit card → tax incidence on Cardholder for making outward supply of services (not goods).
COMPLETENESS TEST
Title passes to only ONE of the parties (never to both)
Conduct of parties exposes errors in assertions about privity
No privity established merely by payment
📝 The one who gets the title is the one who is in the supply chain. Privity does not follow from the money — it follows from the contract and the title. Track the title, not just the cash.
2.7.5 — Locus of Distinct Persons | DP-HO & DP-B | Cross-Charge ⇧ Index
🏢 Locus of Distinct Persons — Which Branch is Supplier & Which is Sub-Contractor?
FRAMEWORK
DP-HO = Head Office (distinct person — HO)
DP-B = Branch executing the work (distinct person — Branch)
Key question: which distinct person is Supplier to Customer, and which is sub-contractor to HO?
📝 When a company has multiple GST registrations, each branch is a "distinct person." If HO signs the contract with Customer but Branch does the work — HO is Supplier, Branch is sub-contractor of HO. There is a deemed supply from Branch to HO (cross-charge) + HO to Customer.
STARTING POINT
If contract signed by Legal Entity citing location of distinct person where HO is situated → assertion is inviolable
HO = DP-HO = the Supplier with legal authority, responsibility and capability
💡 Eg.141: Bank HO in Kochi pays annual banking software license. HO does NOT resell license to branches. HO provides mixed supply of management supervision & oversight functions to all branches = separate taxable deemed supply to each distinct person (branch).
DETERMINATION CRITERIA
(i) Assertions in contracts with third-parties
(ii) Operational capability to perform contractual deliverables
(iii) Assertions before trade/banking regulators
(iv) Assertions before regulators in overseas jurisdictions
(v) Legal remedies to third-parties for warranties/indemnities
💡 Eg.142: HO Delhi, factory Jaipur, sales office Mumbai, after-sales support Ahmedabad, finance back-office Hyderabad. HO is NOT ISD — it is a 'fixed establishment' with outward supply capabilities. HO provides deemed 'management supervision & oversight' to all branches = separate taxable outward supply.
Transactions may be executed differently but not based on contracts as they exist — by causing alteration to terms of these contracts
GST implications cannot be bypassed by making cumbersome alterations to contracts
📝 You cannot restructure a contract purely to avoid GST cross-charge. The interplay of what was asserted to customers, regulators, and banks — compared with internal operational capability — determines which branch is doing what supply.
2.7.6 — Locus of Overseas Branches | Permanent Establishment (PE) ⇧ Index
🌐 Permanent Establishment Outside India — Legal Fiction & GST Consequences
📝 Permanent Establishment (PE) = a fixed place of business in a foreign country. Under IGST Act, if an Indian company has a PE abroad — that PE is treated as a "distinct person" separate from the Indian entity. This creates deemed supplies between HO and PE, and bars zero-rating of services from HO to PE.
PE ASSERTION = ESTOPPEL
Assertion before overseas regulator that 'place of business' with degree of permanence exists → hardly permits denying that place is NOT a place of business for GST law purpose
📝 If you told a foreign tax regulator "I have a permanent establishment in your country" — you cannot then tell Indian GST authorities "I have no establishment outside India." You are bound by your own assertion.
IGST — EXPLANATIONS 1 & 2 to §8
PE outside India = 'distinct person'
Services from HO to PE = NOT 'export of services' (zero-rating denied)
RCM applies on remittances to support PE outside India
📝 So if HO in India sends management services, software, staff to support PE outside India — this is NOT export of services. It is a supply between distinct persons. GST payable. No zero-rating. No LUT benefit.
💡 Eg.143: HO (legal authority, warranty, indemnity to Customer outside India) → fulfils contract de jure. PE executes de facto. Services by PE = capacity of sub-contractor to HO → taxable as import of services by HO.
INDEPENDENCE DOES NOT HELP
PE autonomously meeting operating costs from direct Customer contracts outside India → Independence does NOT imply PE avails legal identity from HO
HO provides management supervision to PE → NOT 'export of services' → disqualification of zero-rating
💡 Eg.140: IT services export signed by HO Bangalore. 300 FTEs in Bangalore + 200 in Hyderabad. Contract cannot be fractionally executed. Indemnity by Bangalore DP → Hyderabad DP sub-contracts to Bangalore DP by deemed supply. Deemed supply from Hyderabad to Bangalore → used in fulfilment of export contract by Bangalore.
2.8.1 to 2.8.3 — Breach of Contract | Misunderstanding | Damages | Hadley v. Baxendale ⇧ Index
🔨 Breach of Contract — Rights, Damages & GST Treatment
2.8.1 Common Misunderstanding
RIGHT TO BREACH
Breach is a RIGHT — as much as the duty to perform
Contractually parties enjoy various rights including 'right to breach'
📝 Breaching a contract is not always a wrongful act — it can be a strategic business decision. The law provides for this by specifying consequences of breach (damages, specific performance) right in the contract.
'Cordial breach' not uncommon — Parties continue amicably on same project while breach is worked out
Disharmonious pre-requisites to breach = poor appreciation of prudent contract-writing
IMPORTANT
CBIC Circular 178 misrepresents commercial wisdom in breaching a contract or preparations for 'cordial breach' — contracts are record of bargain, must concern itself with ALL aspects of bargain including non-performance terms
2.8.2 Contractual Damages
LIQUIDATED DAMAGES
Unless contract terminated — terms bargained must govern even in respect of quantum of damages
Damages agreed in contract = liquidated damages (bargained for)
📝 Liquidated damages = the amount agreed in the contract itself for breach. Not a penalty — a pre-agreed measure of loss. Courts enforce them as long as they are not punitive.
Damages within contract: (i) naturally arising from breach OR (ii) specially known as result of breach at time of contract
Damages outside contract (when contract terminated) → terms of contract contemplating damages go with termination → damages determined as Court sees fit (Section 73 ICA)
2.8.3 External Damages
Hadley v. Baxendale (1854) 9 Exch 341
"Damages which the other party ought to receive in respect of such breach of contract should be such as may fairly and reasonably be considered either arising naturally — according to the usual course of things from such breach — or such as may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both parties, at the time they made the contract, as the probable result of the breach of it."
— J Alderson B
📝 This is the foundational rule on damages. Two tests: (1) Did the loss naturally flow from the breach? (2) Was the loss in contemplation of both parties when they contracted? Only losses passing one of these two tests are recoverable.
Ex contractu damages (awarded by Court without contract terms) = unliquidated damages
NOT subject to tax treatment → did not arise from offer-acceptance → not consideration for supply
📝 Unliquidated damages (court-awarded, not pre-agreed) are NOT GST-able. They are compensation, not consideration for supply. They arise from tort/court order, not from a contract for supply.
💡 Quantum meruit claims do NOT attract GST — they are compensation for failure of performance, not consideration for supply to Promisor. Separate from liquidated damages which are bargained-for.
2.8.4 to 2.8.7 — Types of Breach | Supplementary | Withholding Suit | Repudiation ⇧ Index
⚡ Types of Breach & Post-Breach Contractual Arrangements
2.8.4 Types of Breach
FATAL BREACH
Goes to pith of contract → impairs project → renders continuation impossible
→ Right of repudiation to non-breaching Party
📝 Fatal breach = a breach so serious that the contract's fundamental purpose is destroyed. Like supplying aluminium when steel was contracted for. However, even fatal breach ONLY gives a RIGHT to repudiate — the non-breaching party may still choose to continue.
NON-FATAL BREACH
Breach can be remedied → remediation ensures breach does not recur
Non-fatal breaches can be several in a project
💡 Eg.145: Delay in submitting monthly invoices = non-fatal breach. Eg.146: Supplying steel when aluminium contracted = fatal breach unless Architect-approved design change + Contractee acceptance. Eg.147: 3-month delay in 60-month project = could be fatal but termination imprudent — court will not compel termination.
2.8.5 Supplementary Negotiation
Often contracts have only 'enabling clause' for breach — Parties negotiate supplementary contract on consequences
Supplementary contract deals with object that is executed (breach already occurred — not executory)
📝 A supplementary negotiation after breach creates a new contract covering what happens AFTER the breach. This is a separate executed contract — must be separately examined for GST treatment on what is being settled.
Composite contract document containing both primary and secondary (breach) objects → cannot receive selective treatment ignoring breach terms on basis of bias about 'soured relations'
2.8.6 Withholding Suit for Damages
STRATEGIC RIGHT
Non-breaching Party may forego right to sue for damages to negotiate better new contract → private motivation to secure advantageous bargain
Foregoing right to sue ≠ consideration for abstaining from action
📝 Choosing not to sue is a private business decision — not a "supply" for GST purposes. Unless parties are related, foregoing right to sue cannot be imputed as "consideration for supply."
BUT
Where composite document contains collateral contract for non-performance → damages accrue immediately on breach (inaction = default)
Forfeiting accrued damages ≠ foregoing right to sue
2.8.7 Repudiation & New Contract
Parties may agree to repudiate executory contract → enter into revised contract for same object
Similarity of consideration does NOT imply revised contract is on similar terms
Accrued damages under executed contracts must be paid — no suit necessary for determination
Accrued damages not yet paid may be settled (part/whole) as consideration owed under revised contract
📝 Old contract terminated by repudiation. New contract entered. But any damages already accrued under the old contract must still be settled — they don't disappear just because a new contract was signed.
2.8.8 to 2.8.10 — Tortious Remedy | Election of Remedy | Specific Relief ⇧ Index
⚖️ Remedies for Breach — Within Contract vs Outside Contract
2.8.8 Tortious Remedy
Breach of contract gives rise to remedy of:
(i) Continuation of contract + payment of accrued liquidated damages
OR
(ii) Repudiation + suit for unliquidated damages
CANNOT DO BOTH
As long as contract is NOT terminated → non-breaching party CANNOT simultaneously seek dual remedy (liquidated + unliquidated)
Remedy is within the contract or outside it — NOT possible for contract to subsist yet seek unliquidated damages
📝 You cannot have it both ways. Either you keep the contract alive (and claim agreed liquidated damages), OR you terminate it and go to court for full damages. Choose one path — not both.
Once liquidated damages agreed → Party-in-breach barred from disputing quantum
Acting outside terms of subsisting contract = affront to contract and barred in law
💡 Eg.148: Contract to transport horse for ₹5,000 — horse dies in transit. Party A cannot get relief beyond ₹5,000 (transporter's gain under contract) when seeking remedy WITHIN contract. Eg.149: Car damaged by construction projectile at paid parking — contract was for right-of-way to park, NOT insurance for car damage.
2.8.9 Election of Remedy
CHOOSE ONE
Non-breaching Party must decide:
(A) Remedy WITHIN contract → limited to liquidated damages in bargain
(B) Remedy OUTSIDE contract → must first TERMINATE contract + forego remedy available within
To operate outside contract (in tort):
(i) First terminate contract
(ii) Forego remedies available within contract
(iii) Bring action in tort + face defences including mala fides
📝 Termination = ending the contract entirely. Once terminated, all terms (including liquidated damages clause) are gone. Then you approach court for what it thinks is fair. Higher reward but higher risk — subject to court's discretion and challenges of bad faith.
Termination terminates in its entirety — hope of exemplary remedy in tort cannot be pursued if breach cannot be shown to be irredeemable
💡 Eg.150–151: Party A (horse) → (i) ₹5,000 in contract OR (ii) ₹5,00,000 in tort (value of horse). Owner M (car at parking) → (i) NO remedy under contract | (ii) remedy in tort against parking lot operator AND construction owner.
2.8.10 Specific Relief
Specific relief forms: (a) Recovery of property (b) Direction to perform obligations (c) Rescission for mistake (d) Injunction against threatened breach (e) Declaration of title
Tenant beyond lease term = not trespasser → liable for 'holding-over' (lawful possession, though not title) → cannot be dispossessed except by Court direction
📝 A tenant who stays beyond lease term is not a squatter. They had lawful possession under the lease. Even after expiry, they can only be evicted by court order, not by force. GST on "holding-over" rent continues to be payable.
Licensee staying after license termination = immediate trespasser
Agent disposing goods as her own = 'conversion' (akin to trespasser)
Trustee holds property in name but for beneficiaries — Trust property belongs to beneficiaries
SECTION 23 — SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE
Liquidated damages in contract NOT a bar to specific performance — contract can be specifically enforced even where liquidation sum named, if that sum was named only to secure performance (not give option to pay in lieu)
📝 A liquidated damages clause does not mean "I can pay money instead of performing." It means "if I fail, here is the pre-agreed compensation." The court can still order specific performance AND ignore the damages clause.
2.9 — Indemnity | Bipartite | Consideration | Insurance | Damnation ⇧ Index
🛡️ Indemnity — "No Loss Shall Arise from Acting on Our Assurances"
📝 Indemnity = a promise that if you suffer loss by acting on what I told you, I will compensate you. It is built into the primary contract — not a separate side deal. The Seller (Promisor) indemnifies the Buyer (Promisee) for acts of the Seller and any third party that affects the Buyer's enjoyment.
2.9.1 Bipartite Covenant
Indemnity is given by Promisor (Seller) for acts of Promisor AND any other person that may affect Promisee's (Buyer's) enjoyment
Indemnity is inextricable to the principal object in the primary contract
Indemnity is NOT a collateral contract — it is part of the primary contract consideration
Consideration in contract includes consideration to keep Promisee indemnified
📝 Indemnity is not a separate promise hanging on the side. It is woven into the price itself. When you buy something, part of what you are paying for is the seller's promise to indemnify you if things go wrong.
2.9.2 Consideration for Indemnity
UNLIKE GUARANTEE
No consideration necessary in contract of indemnity
Indemnification in India operates within very narrow compass:
(i) specific incident incurring loss AND (ii) causal relations with indemnity defined must be shown
📝 You don't need to pay extra to be indemnified — it is included in the main contract. But to CLAIM indemnity, you must prove: (1) the specific loss occurred, and (2) it happened because of what the indemnifier did or assured.
Gajanan Moreshwar Parelkar v. Moreshwar Madam Mantri AIR 1942 Bom 302:
Equity stepped in — once liability absolute, indemnified entitled to (i) pay claim directly OR (ii) pay into court sufficient money to constitute fund for paying claim
📝 Under old English law, you had to actually pay the loss before claiming indemnity. Equity corrected this — once your liability is certain, you can force the indemnifier to put money aside NOW (before you actually pay) to protect you.
2.9.3 Insurance Contract
Object of insurance = 'contingency' specified — NOT the 'article' over which contingency occurs
Merchantability of goods purchased = implied warranty (separate from insurance)
Promisor (Insurer) completes performance on executing the contract (not on event occurring)
📝 Insurance is not about the goods — it is about the risk event. The Insurer completes its part of the deal when it signs the policy (not when it pays the claim). The claim payment is just honouring a contingent obligation.
Consideration (premium) does NOT bear linear proportion — ₹100 for 1 month ≠ ₹600 for 6 months
Insurance = single contract; renewal = another contract (NOT extension of original)
💡 GST implication: Pawan's professional context — HDFC ERGO policy renewal is a NEW contract, not extension of old contract. Premium for each policy period taxable separately. Cashless medical insurance (Eg.155) = direct settlement of Insurer with hospital = completion of Insurer's obligation without Insured paying first.
2.9.4 Damnation
CONDITION
Indemnity-holder must be 'damnified' (actually suffered damage) to claim indemnification
If damage averted by extra care of indemnity-holder → indemnification NOT attracted
📝 "Damnified" = actually harmed. If you were supposed to be protected by indemnity but you took extra care and avoided the loss yourself — you cannot claim indemnity for a loss that didn't happen. Indemnity is not a profit mechanism.
SUBROGATION
Indemnifier avails right of subrogation — steps into shoes of indemnity-holder to sue for discharge/reduction of claim
Cannot give indemnity without being under obligation or locus to entertain demand
📝 Subrogation = "stepping into shoes." After Insurer pays your claim, Insurer takes over YOUR right to sue the wrongdoer. This is why Insurance companies can sue on your behalf after settling your claim.
2.10.1 — Guarantee | Tripartite Contract | Surety ⇧ Index
🤝 Guarantee — Special Contract Involving Three Parties
📝 A guarantee involves three parties: (1) the Creditor (who is owed), (2) the Principal Debtor (who owes), and (3) the Guarantor/Surety (who promises to pay if the Debtor defaults). Unlike indemnity (bipartite), guarantee is tripartite and is contingent on a default actually occurring.
TRIPARTITE
Special contract involving THREE Parties
Contract defines 'default' — on occurrence (which cannot be in doubt) → allows remedy to non-defaulting Party
CONTINGENT
Guarantee is contingent on occurrence of agreed-upon-default
Non-defaulting Party seeks remedy from:
(A) Party-in-default OR (B) Guarantor at volition/choosing of non-defaulting Party
📝 The creditor has a choice: chase the defaulting debtor directly, OR go straight to the Guarantor. The Guarantor cannot say "chase the debtor first." (Unless the guarantee specifically says so.)
vs INDEMNITY
Indemnity = consideration required in the main contract (included)
Guarantee = consideration required separately
Indemnity = bipartite; Guarantee = tripartite
Indemnity = inextricable from primary contract; Guarantee = collateral
💡 GST: Bank guarantee issued by Bank for Customer's performance obligation — Bank's commission/fee for issuing guarantee = taxable supply of financial service. Guarantee invoked on default = Bank honouring its contingent obligation (not a new supply).
Guarantor also called Surety
Distinction between Guarantee and Indemnity:
Guarantee = contingent on default; Indemnity = protects against loss from acting on assurances
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 2 (Part 2) | Para 2.6–2.10 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
CST v. Bhayana Builders (P) Ltd. | (2018) 91 taxmann.com 109/66 GST 320/10 GSTL 118 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | 2018 | Para 2.6.5 | Substance vs Object of Contract
Key Ratio
When the 'substance' of a contract is not the 'object' of that contract, it is often mistaken to be additional consideration — especially when abatement is allowed based on gross consideration. Even if for limited purposes of applying the abatement, value of freely issued material may be included, taxable value of the object of supply cannot be marked-up by this value adjustment. GST relies on contract price itself. Testing the object of contract is imperative to determine whether at all there is any need to make valuation adjustments.
When to Use — When Department seeks to add value of goods/material given freely to Supplier (moulds, raw material, AC units) to the taxable value of services. Cite to argue: substance ≠ additional consideration; GST is on contract price for the object of supply only.
Hadley v. Baxendale | (1854) 9 Exch 341 | Rule on Damages
Court of Exchequer, England | 1854 | J Alderson B | Para 2.8.3
Key Ratio
Damages which the other party ought to receive in respect of such breach of contract should be such as may fairly and reasonably be considered either arising naturally — according to the usual course of things from such breach of contract itself — or such as may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both parties, at the time they made the contract, as the probable result of the breach of it.
When to Use — When Department seeks GST on damages received by supplier for breach of contract. Counter: damages awarded by court (unliquidated) arise ex contractu — not from offer-acceptance — not consideration for supply — not taxable. Liquidated damages (bargained-for) may have different treatment — must trace the bargain.
Privity of Contract Principle | Sub-Contractor vs Customer
Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Para 2.7.1 | Applicable universally in sub-contracting disputes
Key Principle
Relations are not determined by names used in trade. Names given to contractual relations must be tested based on terms in the contract. Each contract creates different contractual relations. Sub-contractor has privity with main-contractor only and not with Customer. GST discharged on entire contract sum by main-contractor does not excuse sub-contractor from liability to tax. One who does the work and one who has legal authority to do the work are not interchangeable without offending the contract.
When to Use — (i) When assessee (sub-contractor) claims no GST liability because main-contractor paid GST — cite to show independent GST liability. (ii) When Department imputes contractual relations between non-contracting parties — cite to show privity must be tested from contract terms, not trade names.
Locus Standi — Contractual Authority to Bring Action
Common Law Principle | Para 2.7.2 | Applicable in agency, sub-contract, intermediary disputes
Key Principle
Locus standi refers to one who has lawful authority under a contract to bring any action. A Stranger to a contract is permitted to contribute towards consideration, yet not enjoy any rights or remedies (other than benefits) under that contract. Payment without privity implies payment 'on account of' obligor. Any unusual activity bypassing express contractual lines of authority shakes the certainty of contractual relations — it requires re-examination before being admitted as previously considered.
When to Use — When OEM/Manufacturer pays incentives/discounts directly to end-Reseller bypassing Distributor. Cite: this payment bypasses contractual lines of authority — it is a separate transaction from OEM to Reseller, not a reduction of Distributor's purchase cost. Examine each contract separately for GST treatment.
Distinct Person — Legal Fiction | DP-HO & DP-Branch Cross-Charge
IGST Act | CGST Act §2 | Para 2.7.5 | Applicable in all ISD / cross-charge / HO-Branch disputes
Key Principle
With legal fiction furnishing capacity to contract between branches of the same Legal Entity, the locus standi of each distinct person qua the Customer with whom the contract is entered into by the Legal Entity is of great importance. The assertions made by the Legal Entity in the contract with the Customer would be the most expeditious starting point. Five criteria determine which distinct person is Supplier and which is sub-contractor: (i) assertions in contracts with third-parties, (ii) operational capability to perform, (iii) assertions before trade/banking regulators, (iv) assertions before overseas regulators, (v) legal remedies offered to third-parties for warranties and indemnities.
When to Use — In cross-charge disputes between HO and branches. When HO argues it is merely coordinating (ISD) rather than supplying management services. Cite to establish: legal authority + warranty + indemnity asserted by HO to Customer = HO is Supplier of management supervision functions = taxable deemed supply from HO to branches.
Permanent Establishment — Overseas Branches | IGST Explanations 1 & 2 to §8
IGST Act Section 8 | Para 2.7.6 | Applicable in export of services / zero-rating disputes
Key Principle
When a Legal Entity asserts before an overseas regulator that a 'place of business' with some degree of permanence exists, it hardly permits denying that that place of business is NOT a place of business for purpose of GST law. Super-added to this is the fiction in Explanations 1 and 2 to Section 8 of IGST Act which ends any debate the moment Legal Entity asserts existence of PE in any overseas jurisdiction. HO legally provides management supervision and oversight services from India to PE — which is not 'export of services' due to disqualification of zero-rating in respect of transactions with overseas distinct persons.
When to Use — When Indian company claims zero-rating (LUT/export) for services to its overseas branch/PE. Cite to challenge: if PE exists (admitted before overseas regulator or assessed by them), IGST Explanations 1 & 2 to §8 bar zero-rating. Services from HO to PE = taxable supply between distinct persons.
Gajanan Moreshwar Parelkar v. Moreshwar Madam Mantri | AIR 1942 Bom 302
Bombay High Court | 1942 | Para 2.9.2 | Indemnity — Right Before Actual Payment
Key Ratio
The course of equity stepped in and mitigated the rigour of the common law. The court of equity held that when liability had become absolute, the indemnified was entitled either to pay of the claim or to pay into court sufficient money which would constitute a fund for paying off the claim whenever it was made. An indemnity might be worth very little indeed if the indemnified could not enforce his indemnity till he had actually paid the loss.
When to Use — In insurance-related GST disputes where issue is: when does the insurer's obligation crystallise? Cite to argue: once contingency has occurred and liability is absolute — insurer's obligation to indemnify is immediately enforceable. Cashless settlement (Eg.155) = insurer directly settling = completion of supply at that point.
Quantum Meruit & External Damages — Not Taxable as Supply
Indian Contract Act §73 | Para 2.8.3 | Applicable in court-awarded compensation disputes
Key Principle
When a Court awards damages, it is ex contractu — that is, without a contract or without the terms of bargain governing quantification. This award is not subject to any tax treatment as it did not arise out of offer-acceptance method. This is 'unliquidated damages.' Quantum meruit claims do not attract incidence of tax as it is not consideration for supply but compensation for failure of performance to Promisor.
When to Use — When Department raises GST demand on court-awarded damages or arbitration awards received by supplier for breach of contract by recipient. Cite: ex contractu / quantum meruit awards are not consideration for supply — they are compensation — not liable to GST.
Section 23 Specific Relief Act — Liquidated Damages NOT Option to Breach
Specific Relief Act, 1963 §23 | Para 2.8.10 | Applicable in liquidated damages / penalty disputes
Key Ratio
A contract, otherwise proper to be specifically enforced, may be so enforced, though a sum be named in it as the amount to be paid in case of its breach and the party in default is willing to pay the same — if the court is satisfied that the sum was named only for the purpose of securing performance of the contract and not for the purpose of giving to the party in default an option of paying money in lieu of specific performance. When enforcing specific performance under this section, the court shall not also decree payment of the sum so named in the contract.
When to Use — When Department argues liquidated damages clause = option to pay = taxable supply (tolerance of an act). Counter: §23 Specific Relief Act confirms liquidated damages are to SECURE performance, not give option to breach. The primary obligation remains performance, not payment. Liquidated damages ≠ consideration for tolerating breach.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 2e — Guarantee · Bailment · Agency (Para 2.10–2.12)

Para 2.10 · 2.11 · 2.12 — Guarantee · Bailment · Agency | Contract Act

Pages 92–108 | Source: Allied Laws — GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

🗺️ Legend
⬛ Root Node
🔵 Level 1 Branch
✅ Permitted / Positive
❌ Prohibited / Negative
⚠️ Condition / Test / If
🟣 Optional / Pass-on Route
ℹ️ Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 Index — Para 2.10 Guarantee · Para 2.11 Bailment · Para 2.12 Agency
2.10 Guarantee — Structure & Tripartite Relations
2.10.2 Agreed-upon-Default
2.10.3 Secondary Default Not Essential
2.10.4 Payment by Third-Party ≠ Guarantee
2.10.5 Non-Cash Remedy Guaranteed
2.10.6 Consideration to Guarantor
2.10.7 Recoupment by Guarantor
2.11 Bailment — Key Aspects in GST
2.12.1 Agency — Triumvirate Relations
2.12.2 Bipartite Relations
2.12.3 Formation of Agency
2.12.4 Consideration Not Necessary
2.12.5 Agency Commission
2.12.6 Section 202 — Agency with Interest
2.12.7 Actions in Excess of Authority
2.12.8 Acting on Own Account
2.12.9 Third-Party as Principal
2.12.10 Sub-Agent
2.12.11 Agent Indemnified
2.12.12–14 Agencies in GST / Sec 9(5) / Deemed Supplies
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.10 — Guarantee | Tripartite Structure
⬆ Index
CONTRACT OF GUARANTEE
Three contractual relations — Promisor · Promisee · Guarantor
(a) SUPPLY CONTRACT
Promisor (Seller) ↔ Promisee (Buyer)
Principal transaction of supply
(b) GUARANTEE CONTRACT
Promisor (Seller) ↔ Guarantor (Third Party)
Guarantor assures Promisor of Promisee's performance
(c) INDEMNITY CONTRACT
Guarantor ↔ Promisee (Buyer)
Guarantor's remedy against Promisee on default
ROLE CLARITY
Principal Debtor = Promisee (Buyer) — owes debt / consideration
Creditor = Promisor (Seller) — due to collect loan advanced / supplies made
Surety / Guarantor = Third Party — guarantees Promisor repayment by Promisee
Guarantor is NOT the Supplier — always the Promisor is Supplier
📝 In a GST context: Manufacturer (Seller/Promisor) sells to Buyer (Promisee). Buyer's bank or parent company acts as Guarantor assuring the Seller that payment will be made. Three separate legal relationships exist — each with its own GST implications.
HOW TRIPARTITE CONTRACT FORMS
Promisor offers supply → Promisee demands offer contain remedy against Guarantor (in case of default) → Promisor secures Guarantor's consent → Tripartite contract formed immediately upon acceptance of 'guaranteed offer' by Promisee
📝 The guarantee is not a separate document created later — it is a condition of the main supply contract. Once Promisee accepts the 'guaranteed offer' — all three relationships crystallize simultaneously.
Eg 156 A (manufacturer) sells machinery to B. A demands C guarantee timely PAYMENT of consideration by B.
→ C is Guarantor for payment obligation
Eg 157 A (manufacturer) sells machinery to B. B demands C guarantee timely DELIVERY of machinery by A.
→ C is Guarantor for delivery obligation (different direction)
REMEDY ROUTES
On default: Promisee may seek remedy against Promisor (specific performance) OR against Guarantor (repayment of guaranteed sum) — depending on agreed-upon-default terms
Remedy available against Guarantor is to Promisee or to Promisor — depending on default that is guaranteed
💡 Eg 158: "Sell goods to A, on A's default, B will pay" — B IS the Guarantor.
Eg 159: "Sells goods to A but collects payment from B" — B is NOT the Guarantor (B is merely payer / third-party obligor under different arrangement).
2.10.2 — Agreed-upon-Default
⬆ Index
AGREED-UPON-DEFAULT
Ambiguity in what constitutes 'default' voids the guarantee — not the rest of the contract
AMBIGUITY = VOID GUARANTEE
Ambiguity in nature and measure of default to give rise to action against Guarantor will void the guarantee — but NOT the rest of the contract
Consequent remedies against Promisor remain available
📝 If the guarantee document is vague about WHAT counts as a 'default' — the guarantee clause fails. But the supply contract itself survives — Promisee can still sue the Promisor (Seller) for breach — just cannot claim against Guarantor.
MULTIPLE DEFAULTS POSSIBLE
There can be more than one default — each may give rise to a different remedy based on the terms of the guarantee
📝 A guarantee may cover: (a) late payment; (b) partial payment; (c) non-delivery. Each attracts a different remedy. The guarantee must specify each default type clearly — otherwise the guarantee for that specific default is unenforceable.
WITH/WITHOUT RECOURSE CLAUSE
Dispute about whether agreed-upon-default occurred must be resolved before remedy is available.
'With recourse' clause = Promisee's claim of occurrence of default is final and binding — Guarantor must tender remedy first — dispute can be agitated later
📝 'With recourse' clause means: if Buyer says Seller defaulted — Guarantor must PAY FIRST and argue later. This protects the Buyer from Guarantor delaying payment by disputing the default. Very common in bank guarantees.
💡 GST Relevance: Guarantee invoked — Guarantor pays — this creates a cross-cancelling supply between Guarantor and Promisee. The GST treatment of Guarantor's payment must be determined separately from the original supply contract.
2.10.3 — Secondary Default NOT Essential
⬆ Index
SECONDARY DEFAULT NOT ESSENTIAL
Promisee need NOT exhaust remedies against Promisor before claiming against Guarantor
DIRECT CLAIM AGAINST GUARANTOR
Promisee CANNOT be directed to exhaust remedy against Promisor BEFORE exercising right against Guarantor.
No secondary default needed.
Guarantor's liability is coextensive with Promisor whose performance is the object of guarantee.
📝 'Coextensive' means: Guarantor's liability is as wide and deep as the Promisor's own liability. Buyer does not have to first sue the Seller, fail, and THEN claim against Guarantor. Guarantor can be directly approached on primary default.
BUT — PRIMARY DEFAULT MUST BE CERTAIN
Primary default cannot be uncertain. To perfect action against Guarantor:
(i) Promisee must document demand of performance by Promisor
(ii) By efflux of time OR inaction by Promisor — primary default stands established
📝 While you don't need to exhaust remedies against Seller first — you must be able to PROVE the default happened. This requires documentary evidence: demand letter, time given, non-performance established.
💡 GST Practice: Before invoking guarantee — ensure SCN / legal demand + non-payment for specified period is documented. Without this, Guarantor can dispute the 'default' itself and the guarantee action fails.
2.10.4 — Payment by Third-Party is NOT Guarantee
⬆ Index
PAYMENT BY THIRD-PARTY ≠ GUARANTEE
Flow of payment is NOT a reliable indicator of existence of Guarantee contract
CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Where a Third-Party accepts to pay the Promisor (Seller) in a contract — it is NOT a contract of Guarantee. Payer is liable to explain reasons for discharging obligations of Promisee (Buyer). This is NOT a bill-to-ship-to transaction where Payer is Promisee.
Obligation towards Promisor should NOT primarily be that of Guarantor for it to be a Guarantee contract.
📝 Just because a third party pays the Seller does NOT make that third party a Guarantor. It could be: parent company paying on behalf of subsidiary (not guarantee), employer paying for employee (not guarantee). The test is whether the third party is obligated to pay ONLY on a default by the primary obligor.
Eg 160: Employer making housing loan EMI payments to Bank with recourse to deduct from Employee's salary — does NOT make Employer the Borrower
📝 Employer pays bank on behalf of employee — but Employer is not the borrower. Employee (Promisee) remains the borrower. Employer is merely a conduit/facilitator — no guarantee relationship created.
Eg 161: Housing loan directly disbursed to Builder does NOT make Builder the Borrower
📝 Even if money goes directly to Builder — the Bank's contract is with the home buyer (Promisee). Builder is just the recipient of disbursement for the buyer's benefit — not the borrower or guarantor.
Eg 162–163: Holding company guarantees loan to WOS — but Holding company may become Borrower if loan sanctioned based on Holding's assets and WOS has insufficient credit on its own
→ Balance sheet ≠ best indicator of contractual relations — substance over form test applies
📝 If in substance the loan is based entirely on Holding company's assets and creditworthiness — and WOS couldn't get the loan on its own — Revenue/courts may treat Holding as the real Borrower (not mere Guarantor). Financial statements follow accounting rules, not contract law substance.
💡 GST Hearing: Where Revenue taxes corporate guarantee as 'service' by Guarantor — argue: (a) Is the guarantee true guarantee or is Holding the actual borrower? (b) Balance sheet treatment is not contractual reality. Substance over form must apply.
2.10.5 — Non-Cash Remedy Guaranteed
⬆ Index
NON-CASH REMEDY GUARANTEED
Guarantor providing non-monetary remedy = separate GST event between Guarantor and Promisee
Guarantor NOT being the Supplier — remedy allowed in contract for supply by Guarantor gives rise to tax treatment inter se Guarantor and Promisee — based on the transaction for which guarantee was given (which is a service, in GST).
Supply of goods being guaranteed by supply of services — tariff classification and other taxing ingredients cannot be applied interchangeably.
📝 When a Guarantor steps in and supplies goods (instead of money) to remedy default — this creates a new GST event between Guarantor and Buyer. The rate of tax on Guarantor's supply (goods) may be different from the original supply (also goods or services). Cannot assume same rate applies — separate GST computation needed.
CROSS-CANCELLING SUPPLY TRIGGER
Guarantee (non-cash remedy) triggers a cross-cancelling supply of obligation of Guarantor by way of services to Promisee.
Promisee must derive valid title to the guaranteed supply.
Omission to address tax treatment of each transaction (original supply + guaranteed supply) can be fatal to demand.
📝 When Guarantor performs — there are now TWO supplies: (1) Original supply (Promisor → Promisee) and (2) Guarantor's performance supply (Guarantor → Promisee). GST must be examined and discharged on BOTH separately. Revenue cannot treat them as one.
2.10.6 — Consideration to Guarantor
⬆ Index
CONSIDERATION TO GUARANTOR
Who pays the Guarantor? — Critical for GST on 'import of services' and reverse charge
SECTION 127 — CONTRACT ACT
"Anything done, or any promise made, for the benefit of the principal debtor, may be a sufficient consideration to the surety for giving the guarantee."
= Consideration for guarantee contract may simply be Promisor's (Seller's) willingness to enter into contract with Promisee (Buyer).
📝 The Guarantor does not NEED to be paid. The mere fact that Seller agreed to sell to Buyer (which benefits the Buyer/Principal Debtor) is sufficient consideration for the Guarantor's commitment. This is why corporate guarantees are often given without explicit fee — yet they are legally valid.
KEY: FLOW OF CONSIDERATION
Consideration to Guarantor flows from PROMISOR (Seller) — NOT from Promisee (Buyer)
Benefit arising to Promisee (Buyer) is NOT consideration to Guarantor
Consideration = Promisor's (Seller's) acceptance to enter into contract with Promisee (Buyer)
📝 The party who BENEFITS from guarantee (Seller gets assurance of payment) is the one who provides consideration TO the Guarantor — not the Buyer. This is crucial for determining: (a) Who is Recipient of guarantee service? (b) Is GST payable by Seller or Buyer?
💡 Eg 164 (Cross-border): Guarantor-Parent outside India guarantees loan to Subsidiary-Borrower. Consideration flows from Lender (Promisor) NOT from Borrower. Beneficiary (Subsidiary) cannot be Recipient liable to GST on 'import of services' — even if guarantee is taxable.
Eg 165 (Domestic) Guarantor-Parent inside India guarantees loan to Subsidiary-Borrower. Consideration to Guarantor flows from Lender. GST (if any) is payable on supply of guarantee between Guarantor and Lender — as consideration flows to Guarantor. Guarantor is also NOT Supplier but RECIPIENT of consideration flowing from Lender.
📝 This is the opposite of the usual assumption. Revenue often says 'Guarantor provides service to Borrower — Borrower pays GST.' But law says: consideration flows from Lender to Guarantor — so the GST relationship is between Guarantor and Lender, not Guarantor and Borrower.
GUARANTEE VALIDITY NOTE
Contract of guarantee must declare existence of consideration so as not to be void.
Guarantee can be given after contract is entered into between Promisor and Promisee if doubts arise as to recoverability of consideration — demand for Guarantee may be made as variation to original contract.
2.10.7 — Recoupment by Guarantor
⬆ Index
RECOUPMENT BY GUARANTOR
After Guarantor pays — Guarantor steps into Promisor's shoes → Right of Subrogation (Section 140)
RIGHT OF SUBROGATION — Sec 140
Once remedy extended and Promisee satisfied — Guarantor enters position of Promisee to claim recoupment against Promisor.
Tax treatment of recoupment transaction is NOT interchangeable with original supply promised.
📝 'Subrogation' means: after Guarantor pays Buyer's debt, Guarantor can turn around and recover from the Seller (Promisor) — as if the Guarantor were the Buyer. Guarantor is vested with ALL rights Promisor (Seller) enjoyed against Promisee (Buyer).
💡 GST Point: Recoupment by Guarantor from Promisor = settlement of debt owed to Guarantor — NOT a service inter se. So no fresh GST supply arises on recoupment — it is merely debt recovery, not a new transaction.
BACK-TO-BACK GUARANTEE — Sec 145
Promisee (Buyer) who enjoyed guarantee by Guarantor is liable to keep Guarantor indemnified.
Where Guarantor has EXPRESS contract with Promisee to reimburse claims settled towards Promisor (Seller) — such back-to-back guarantee is admissible.
Claim against Promisee = settlement of debt owed to Guarantor, NOT a service inter se.
📝 If Guarantor has separately contracted with Buyer for reimbursement — that contract (back-to-back guarantee) is valid. But when Guarantor claims reimbursement from Buyer — it is debt recovery, not a service — no fresh GST supply.
WARNING — PROMISOR MUST NOT WAIVE CLAIMS
Promisor (Seller) must NOT do anything that operates as waiver of claims against Promisee (Buyer) — as it destroys Guarantor's recourse against Promisee to recoup amount settled with Promisor.
📝 If Seller forgives Buyer's debt — Guarantor loses the right to recover what Guarantor paid on Buyer's behalf. Seller's waiver = destroys Guarantor's subrogation rights. Guarantor should ensure Seller does not waive without Guarantor's consent.
2.11 — Bailment | Key Aspects for GST
⬆ Index
BAILMENT
Physical custody without title transfer — sui generis — NOT sale, transfer, or agency
6 DEFINING ATTRIBUTES OF BAILMENT
(a) Requires physical custody
(b) Does NOT transfer title
(c) Made under contract or without one — but must be for specific and discernible purpose
(d) Liable to be returned after purpose is completed
(e) Further disposed as directed by Bailor (whose articles they are)
(f) Articles held for some other purpose after bailment purpose — imposes responsibilities of bailment if articles continue to remain in custody
📝 Simple test: Has title passed? No → Could be bailment. Is there a purpose for holding? Yes → Likely bailment. Will it be returned or disposed as per owner's direction? Yes → Bailment confirmed. Title staying with Bailor is the KEY distinguisher from sale.
✅ IS BAILMENT
Eg 166: Transporter
Eg 167: Auctioneer
Eg 168: Service centre (vehicle for repairs)
Eg 169: Job-work (goods sent for processing)
Eg 171: Returnable bottles (transport/delivery purpose — NOT rental of bottles or sale)
Eg 172: Refundable bottle deposit = bailment until time to return passes OR bottles not returned
💡 GST KEY: Job-work (Sec 143 CGST) is explicitly recognised as bailment. Goods sent for job-work without payment of GST return must come back within 1 year (goods) / 3 years (capital goods) — otherwise treated as deemed supply.
❌ NOT BAILMENT
Eg 170: Bank waiting to collect payment and handover Bill of Lading to overseas Purchaser = NOT Agent of Seller-Customer but a Bailee
(Bank holds document on bailment — not as agent)
NOT INTERCHANGEABLE
Bailment ≠ Agency (Bailee does not represent Bailor before third-parties)
Bailment ≠ does NOT attract legal fiction in Schedule I para 3 of CGST Act
Bailment is sui generis — a unique category of legal relations — cannot be equated with sale, transfer, or agency
📝 'Sui generis' = one of its own kind. Bailment stands alone — it is neither sale nor agency. Revenue cannot impute supply arising from bailment using Schedule I legal fiction applicable to agency — the two are legally distinct.
FREE ISSUE MATERIAL — BAILMENT PRINCIPLE
Goods given to Contractor as free-issue-material to carry out works = under bailment, NOT sale.
Goods MISUSED (wastage beyond norms) attracts remedy to compensate Customer — but NOT a transfer of property unless title remains with Contractor who is free to apply or misapply them.
If transfer is implicit to extent of wastage → transfer of property on all goods given as free-issue subject to verification of use or misuse.
📝 When a company gives steel to its contractor to build a factory — the steel is given on bailment, NOT sold. If contractor wastes steel beyond permitted limits — it is a breach of bailment, attracting damages — not a sale of goods. GST cannot be demanded on normal free-issue-material as if it were a sale.
💡 GST Hearing: Where Revenue demands GST on free-issue-material given to job-worker or contractor — argue: (a) No title transfer — bailment only. (b) Schedule I para 3 requires agency context — not present here. (c) Bailment is sui generis — no deemed supply arises on bailment per se.
2.12.1 — Agency | Triumvirate Relations
⬆ Index
AGENCY — TRIUMVIRATE RELATIONS
Section 182 — Agent brings about binding obligations between Principal and Third-Party
SECTION 182 DEFINITION
"An 'agent' is a person employed to do any act for another, or to represent another in dealings with third persons. The person for whom such act is done, or who is so represented, is called the 'principal'."
Agency = ability to bring about BINDING obligations between two others.
THREE SETS OF LEGAL RELATIONS — ALL THREE MUST EXIST FOR TRUE AGENCY
(a) Principal and Agent — Agent appointed, authority conferred
(b) Principal and Third-Party — binding obligations brought about by Agent's acts
(c) Agent and Third-Party — Agent interacts, asserts obligations Principal will fulfil
📝 True agency requires ALL THREE relationships to be real, independent, and substantive — none can be 'for show' only. Remedies to counter-parties must be real and within the realm of those relations only. This three-fold test is what determines if GST's 'intermediary' treatment applies.
AGENT'S KEY OBLIGATIONS
Authority must be vested and pursuant to authority — obligations must devolve on Principal.
Third-Party entitled to damages in case of Principal's refusal to perform.
Scope guided by assertions made by Agent and accepted by Third-Party.
Qui facit per alium, facit per se — he who acts through another, does the act himself.
📝 Latin maxim means: Principal is as responsible as if he had acted personally. When Agent brings about a contract — Principal is fully bound. Agent is NOT responsible for Principal's performance (unless acting on own account).
RELIABLE TEST FOR AGENCY
Existence of authority for Agent to bring about BINDING obligations for Principal with Third-Parties — which are to be FULFILLED BY PRINCIPAL and NOT by Agent.
It is NOT what actor is called — but the 'representative capacity' lawfully vested and exercised that is conclusive.
📝 Calling someone 'marketing agent' or 'sales agent' does NOT make them an Agent in law. The test: Can they BIND the Principal to Third-Parties in obligations the PRINCIPAL must fulfil? If yes → Agency. If Principal must do the work himself to discharge obligations → Agent is merely a facilitator, not a legal Agent.
💡 GST Intermediary: This test determines if Sec 2(13) IGST intermediary treatment applies. Without all three relations being real — taxpayer can resist intermediary classification and demand zero-rate / export treatment on their services.
2.12.2 — Bipartite Relations (False Agency)
⬆ Index
BIPARTITE RELATIONS — FALSE AGENCY
Expression 'agent/agency' used even when no triumvirate relationship intended
DEFAULT RULE Transactions where no triumvirate relationship exists but bear some indicia of agency will NOT be regarded as agency for GST purposes. It is NOT what the actor is called — but the 'representative capacity' that is conclusive.
NOT AGENTS
Eg 173: Canvassing agent (publishes offerings only — Third-Parties must directly enter contract with Principal)
Eg 175: Back-office agent (processes invoices, collects payments — no authority to bind Principal)
Eg 176: Advertising agency (publishes offering only — not authorised to bring about binding relations)
Eg 177: Detective agency (gathers info exclusively for Principal)
Eg 178: Security agency (NOT an Agent)
Eg 179: Broker (may procure leads but not authorised to conclude trade)
Eg 181: Advocate is NOT Agent but a Principal providing representation services to taxpayer (Principal) before Appellate Forum
📝 Advocates appear on behalf of clients — but they are NOT the client's agent in the legal sense. The Advocate is a Principal providing a service. This matters for GST: Advocates provide 'legal services' as Principals — not as Agents of clients.
ARE AGENTS
Eg 174: Collection agent receiving sum on money — does have authority to discharge debt owed by Third-Party to Principal
Eg 180: Land aggregators = canvassing Agents since sale deed will be executed in favour of Buyers (Principals) — agreement to sell entered into by Agent with Sellers (Third-Parties)
💡 Eg 180 JDA Relevance: If Developer acts as Agent of Landowner under a GPA — Developer is canvassing Agent for third-party purchasers. This is 'agency coupled with interest' (Sec 202) — cannot be terminated to evict Developer.
BIPARTITE WITH THIRD-PARTY ENGAGEMENT
Three scenarios where bipartite relations exist but involve some engagement with Third-Parties:
(a) Only collecting info passed to Principal (Liaison Offices, canvassing)
(b) Extended pre-sales engagement (market evaluation, strategic advice)
(c) Customer care — fulfilling Principal's after-sales obligations
→ None of these constitute TRUE agency for GST 'intermediary' purposes
2.12.3 — Formation of Agency
⬆ Index
FORMATION OF AGENCY
Agency is NOT created by contract — but by appointment and acceptance
KEY DISTINCTION
Agency is NOT created by contract (not listed in Section 25 exceptions) — it is created by 'appointment'. Appointment REFUSED does not create Agency. Agency formed by 'acceptance of appointment'.
Appointment must communicate with sufficient clarity: (i) scope (ii) scope limitations (iii) other factors for Agent's authority
📝 You cannot 'contract' someone into being your Agent without their consent. They must accept the appointment. This is important in GST — a company cannot unilaterally designate a subsidiary as its 'Agent' for purposes of avoiding GST without the subsidiary actually accepting and discharging agency duties.
(a) Express Appointment
(b) Holding-out in conduct towards Third-Parties
(c) Necessity
(d) Ratification of previously unauthorised actions
AGENT'S ACCEPTANCE CONDITIONS
Agent must accept appointment with full knowledge of authority to bring about binding relations between Principal and Third-Party. Third-Parties must know that assertions by Agent will be fulfilled by Principal.
2.12.4 — Consideration NOT Necessary for Agency
⬆ Index
CONSIDERATION NOT NECESSARY
Section 185 — No consideration is necessary to create an agency
SECTION 185 "No consideration is necessary to create an agency."
Although no consideration needed for formation — Agent is NOT responsible to bear costs when acting on behalf of Principal.
Principal is liable to reimburse Agent for costs incurred while discharging duties.
Agent is free to withhold monies belonging to Principal to the extent of reimbursements due.
📝 A parent company can appoint a subsidiary as its Agent without paying any fee — and this is a valid agency. But the subsidiary cannot be made to bear expenses of the agency out of its own pocket — Principal must reimburse all costs. Agent has lien over Principal's funds until reimbursed.
💡 GST Relevance: Even in an unpaid agency — if the triumvirate relations are real — Schedule II para 3 deemed supply between Principal and Agent may be attracted. The absence of consideration for the agency itself does not negate the deemed supply of goods between them.
2.12.5 — Agency Commission
⬆ Index
AGENCY COMMISSION
Remuneration may flow from Principal or from Third-Party — determines GST treatment
Remuneration permitted by special contract — must flow NOT from document that appointed Agent, but outside it. A single document with both (i) appointment and (ii) remuneration does not imply new legislative necessity of consideration for formation of Agency.
PAYER OF REMUNERATION — KEY FOR GST
'Payer of remuneration' is NOT the most reliable test of 'Recipient' of Agent's services.
Payer of consideration is the Recipient of supply (Section 2(93) CGST Act) — EXCEPT in case of Agency.
In Agency — Principal may direct Agent to collect remuneration from Third-Party who is NOT Agent's Principal.
📝 Normally: whoever pays = Recipient. But in Agency: Agent's fee may be collected from the Third-Party (Buyer), while Agent's actual Principal (Seller) remains the Recipient of the agency service. This creates GST complications — especially for import of services.
TWO GST CONSEQUENCES
(a) Payment 'on behalf of' Principal = attracts Section 15(2)(b) valuation adjustment for supply by Principal to Third-Party
(b) Payment NOT 'on behalf of' Principal = separate tax treatment as service directly by Agent to Third-Party
Eg 182: Travel Agent collects (i) commission from Airlines (ii) service charges from Passenger (iii) incentive from CRS companies
📝 Travel Agent has THREE income streams — each has different GST characterisation. Commission from Airlines (on behalf of Airlines' Principal service) — Sec 15(2)(b) applies. Service charge from Passenger — direct service by Agent. Incentive from CRS — another separate service. Cannot club all three.
💡 Key Case References: D. Pauls Consumer Benefit Ltd v. CCE TS-1003-CESTAT-2018(DEL)-ST and Kafila Hospitality and Travels (P.) Ltd. v. CST 2021-TIOL-159-CESTAT-DEL-LB — for development of law on agency commission under GST.
2.12.6 — Section 202 | Agency Coupled with Interest
⬆ Index
SECTION 202 — AGENCY COUPLED WITH INTEREST
Agent acquires 'interest' in property — cannot be evicted by termination of agency
Agent who acquires 'interest' in the property CANNOT be evicted by termination of contract with prejudice to such interest. This protection flows from Section 202. 'Interest' in context of immovable property must be identified through Transfer of Property Act.
📝 If an Agent invests money or effort and acquires a legal 'interest' in the property being dealt with — Principal cannot simply terminate the agency and throw the Agent out. This is 'agency coupled with interest' — it is irrevocable to the extent of that interest.
Eg 183 Underwriter (Agent) of IPO acquires interest if shares are allotted in the Company due to poor subscriber response.
→ Agency coupled with interest
Eg 184 — JDA CRITICAL Developer enters into JDA duly registered with Landowner along with General Power of Attorney (GST-cum-JDA) to act on behalf of Landowner in carrying out development = 'Agency coupled with interest'
📝 This is the legal basis why a Developer under a registered JDA+GPA cannot be simply evicted by the Landowner. The Developer has 'interest' in the property. Sec 202 protection kicks in — Landowner cannot terminate JDA and ask Developer to leave without legal consequences.
💡 JDA GST Impact: If Developer cannot be evicted → Developer's supply (construction services) cannot be characterised as void or incomplete. The taxable event of supply crystallises on registration of JDA — Developer's interest exists from that moment.
Eg 185 — LIMITATION ON TRANSFER Developer CANNOT transfer obligations to Sub-Developer EXCEPT by:
(i) Termination of JDA and substitution with new JDA, OR
(ii) Novation of JDA
Variation of JDA by replacing one Party = substantial variation → results in NEW JDA altogether — NOT a permissible variation
📝 Developer cannot simply 'pass on' the JDA to a Sub-Developer. This would require Landowner's fresh consent and a new registered JDA or valid novation. Unregistered assignment of JDA obligations = void under Registration Act (Section 17) — no legal interest passes.
💡 GST Practice: When Developer brings in a Sub-Developer — examine: Is there a registered tri-party agreement? If not — Developer remains sole contractual party with Landowner. Sub-Developer's supply is to Developer (not to Landowner) — different GST chain.
2.12.7 — Actions in Excess of Authority
⬆ Index
ACTIONS IN EXCESS OF AUTHORITY
Section 188 — Agent acting beyond authority is personally liable
SECTION 188
"An agent, having an authority to do an act, has authority to do every lawful thing which is necessary in order to do such act.
An agent having an authority to carry on a business, has authority to do every lawful thing necessary for the purpose, or usually done in the course, of conducting such business."
PERSONAL LIABILITY
Agent acting in excess of authority = personally liable.
Principal may repudiate Agent's actions OR ratify them.
Agent is NOT a Servant — Master-Servant relations do NOT exist. Principal answerable only for actions of Agents within scope of Agency.
📝 Agent going beyond what Principal authorised = Agent's own problem. Principal need not stand behind unauthorized acts. But if Principal ratifies (approves after the fact) — Principal is bound as if authorised from beginning.
EXCESS OF AUTHORITY EXAMPLES
Eg 186: Auctioneer cannot sell by private contract
Eg 187: Canvassing agent cannot make binding offer
Eg 188: Collection agent cannot give valid discharge
Eg 189: Customs broker cannot sell goods
Eg 190: Marketing Director of Company CANNOT authorise CA to appear before Tribunal in Company's case
💡 Eg 190 is directly relevant: Where Marketing Director (not authorised signatory) signs PCT-05 or GST appeal authorisation — Revenue may challenge authority. Ensure proper Board Resolution / Power of Attorney authorises the CA for GST hearings.
OWN ACCOUNT LIABILITY
Agent acting on 'own account' is liable to account to Principal for ALL profits in course of Agency.
Agent can act for more than one Principal — but qua each transaction with Third-Party — represents only ONE of several Principals.
2.12.8 — Acting on Own Account
⬆ Index
ACTING ON OWN ACCOUNT
When Agent steps out of agency role and acts as Principal for self
TEST FOR 'ACTING ON OWN ACCOUNT'
Agent acting for several Principals and engaging with Third-Parties WITHOUT identifying which Principal is to be bound → Agent acting on own account ONLY when:
(i) Agent makes unequivocal commitment about fulfilment of obligations
(ii) None of Principals who are represented are either informed or capable of performing those obligations
📝 When Agent does not reveal who their Principal is and takes personal responsibility for fulfilling obligations — Agent is 'acting on own account.' In GST — this shifts the tax liability from Principal to Agent because Agent has become the Supplier.
Eg 191: Land cannot be sold by Agent due to lack of title → Agent personally liable if purported to sell
Eg 192: Used car dealers (Agents) CAN sell 'on own account' without registration of vehicle in their name under MV Act
Eg 193: SIM card dealers (Agents) CAN sell 'on own account' since SIM cards are 'inactive' until linked with user number
Eg 194: Freight forwarders (Agents) CAN sell container space under opaque pricing formula even though they are not Liners
DEL CREDERE AGENT
Del credere Agents are NOT 'acting on own account' because obligations are still to be fulfilled by their Principals — except that these Agents have underwritten the receivables (guaranteed payment). This is a blend of agency and guarantee.
2.12.9 — Third-Party as Principal (Buying Agent)
⬆ Index
THIRD-PARTY AS PRINCIPAL — BUYING AGENT
Agency is not only about Selling — Recipient can also be Principal engaging a 'Buying Agent'
It is easily possible for Agent to represent the Recipient of supplies / Promisee → 'Buying Agent'. Principal = Promisee (Recipient) who engages Agent for vendor identification and management. Buying Agents engaged in: (i) identification (ii) award of contract (iii) performance / fulfilment (iv) testing, pre-shipment inspection and delivery.
GST treatment as 'intermediaries' applies to buying Agents equally as selling Agents.
📝 Most GST litigation focuses on Agents representing Sellers. But a Buyer can also appoint an Agent (like a procurement company) to act on their behalf. This 'buying agency' also attracts GST intermediary treatment — including the POS rules under IGST that limit zero-rating.
Eg 195 MNC shoe company setting up IT R&D Centre in Bangalore India — would actually be carrying out 'vendor management' activities for IT projects awarded to ITeS majors in India. Although styled 'IT R&D centre' — subsidiary would be engaged in 'intermediary' functions → GST treatment as intermediary applies → POS within India → not eligible for zero-rated benefit on exports.
2.12.10 — Sub-Agent
⬆ Index
SUB-AGENT
Delegatus non potest delegare — Agent cannot delegate authority delegated to them
LATIN MAXIM Delegatus non potest delegare — Agent cannot delegate authority delegated.
EXCEPT: by customs of trade / ordinary course of business / necessary implication where activities required cannot all be performed by Agent — delegation to Sub-Agent(s) is permitted.
There is NO privity of contract between Principal and Sub-Agent.
📝 If you appoint a CA as your Agent for GST litigation — the CA cannot simply hand over your case to another CA without your consent. UNLESS by customs of CA practice it is understood that delegation is normal (e.g., to an associate or employee). No direct relationship exists between you (Principal) and the other CA (Sub-Agent).
GST CONSEQUENCES WHEN SUB-AGENT INVOLVED
Agent stands in position of Principal for assertions made by Sub-Agent to Third-Parties.
Operation of legal fiction in Schedule II para 3 must be applied mutatis mutandis when Sub-Agent is involved.
When Sub-Agent admitted — Agent must be ousted from position for GST treatment of transaction by legal fiction.
There CANNOT be double compliance with legal fiction under (i) Section 9(5) or (ii) Section 52 — BUT there can be double compliance with GST treatment in Schedule I para 3.
2.12.11 — Agent Indemnified
⬆ Index
AGENT INDEMNIFIED
Section 222 — Indemnity by Principal is implied and express
INDEMNITY — SECTION 222
Indemnity by Principal is implied AND also express. Agent cannot be imperilled for not being able to expressly claim indemnity at time of accepting appointment.
Principal bears vicarious liability for actions of Agent within scope of Agency = respondeat superior
📝 'Respondeat superior' = let the master answer. Principal is vicariously responsible for what Agent does within the scope of the agency — just as an employer is responsible for employee acts within scope of employment.
WELFARE vs PRIVATE MATTERS DISTINCTION
Care must be taken to differentiate between interpretation of welfare matters' legislations and welfare aspects residing in private matters' legislations.
Vicarious liability of Principal provided actions of Agent are within scope of Agency.
📝 GST is a private matters' legislation — not a welfare statute. Principal's vicarious liability under GST for Agent's acts is limited to acts within the scope of Agency. Acts outside Agency scope = Agent's personal liability only.
2.12.12–14 — Agencies in GST | Section 9(5) | Deemed Supplies
⬆ Index
AGENCIES IN GST — FOUR CATEGORIES
Contract law determines existence & type of agency → GST treatment flows from type
Criteria9(5) ECOM52 TCS2(13) IntermediaryAll Others
RelationsThreeThreeThreeTwo
Appointed byPrincipalPrincipalPrincipalPrincipal
Collect gross trade valueAgentAgentPrincipalPrincipal
Agent's feePrincipalPrincipal *PrincipalPrincipal
Object of supplyServicesBothBothBoth
Obligated to PrincipalAgentAgentAgentAgent
Obligated to Third-Party **AgentPrincipalPrincipalPrincipal
Engage with Third-Party **AgentBothAgentPrincipal
* Cut-and-pay where Agent retains fee and remits balance to Principal    ** Customer or Supplier
SECTION 9(5) — ECOM OPERATOR AS FICTIONAL SUPPLIER
Where outward supplies are notified under Section 9(5) — Agent (ECOM) is liable to discharge GST "as if" such Agent were the Supplier. When Agent is treated "as" the Supplier → actual Supplier (Driver) is unseated → actual Supplier NOT liable to register even when threshold limit crossed (since entire output tax discharged by fictional Supplier ECOM).
Rate of tax discharged by fictional Supplier = same rate that would apply to underlying supply.
Fictional Supplier CANNOT also be liable to tax on service charges or convenience fees charged to actual Supplier or Customer.
📝 Swiggy/Zomato (ECOM) is treated as if they are the restaurant (Supplier) for restaurant services under Sec 9(5). Restaurant doesn't pay GST — Swiggy/Zomato does. But Swiggy/Zomato cannot ALSO charge GST on their 'delivery fee' or 'platform fee' separately — that would be double taxation on same transaction value.
2.12.14 — DEEMED SUPPLIES — SCHEDULE II PARA 3
Where Agent comes in possession of goods within scope of Agency — Schedule II para 3 CGST Act deems that:
(a) Principal has SOLD goods to Agent; AND Agent has RESOLD those goods to Third-Party
OR vice versa:
(a) Agent has PURCHASED goods from Third-Party; AND Principal has PURCHASED goods from Agent.
Financial records of Agent will show 'agency commission' as income — but GST records will show 'purchase-sale' transaction with ITC.
📝 Schedule II para 3 is the 'deemed supply' provision for goods through Agents. Even if no actual sale takes place between Principal and Agent — GST law deems one for goods passing through Agent's hands. This creates an additional GST supply in the chain — Principal must issue tax invoice to Agent and Agent to Third-Party.
💡 Eg 196: Advertisement agents — permitted to operate as (i) resellers paying output tax with ITC benefit OR (ii) agency paying tax only on commission earned.
Eg 197: Distributors of SIM cards — permitted to operate as (i) resellers paying output tax with ITC or (ii) agency paying tax only on commission. SIM card is NOT goods — Distributors are not TRAI licensees themselves.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Para 2.10 · 2.11 · 2.12 | Guarantee · Bailment · Agency ⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, CESTAT. State them confidently and cite the authority.
Consideration to Guarantor Flows from Promisor — NOT from Promisee
Para 2.10.6 | Section 127 Contract Act | Examples 164 & 165
Key Ratio
Consideration to Guarantor flows from Promisor (Seller/Lender) — NOT from Promisee (Buyer/Borrower). Benefit arising to Promisee is an incident of the transaction with Promisor — not consideration to Guarantor. Flow of consideration to Guarantor is NOT from Promisee but from Promisor. Therefore, Beneficiary cannot be Recipient liable to GST on 'import of services' of guarantee.
When to Use — When Revenue demands GST on corporate guarantee as 'import of services' from foreign parent — with Subsidiary as Recipient. Argue: Consideration flows from Lender (Promisor) to Guarantor — NOT from Borrower/Subsidiary. Borrower is beneficiary — not Recipient of guarantee service. GST, if any, between Guarantor and Lender — not between Guarantor and Borrower.
Guarantor's Liability is Coextensive — Secondary Default NOT Essential
Para 2.10.3 | Contract Act Principle
Key Ratio
Promisee cannot be directed to exhaust remedy against Promisor before exercising right against Guarantor. No secondary default by Promisor need be shown by Promisee to validly claim remedy against Guarantor. Guarantor's liability is coextensive with Promisor whose performance is the object of guarantee. Primary default cannot be uncertain — it must be documented by demand of performance and efflux of time or inaction by Promisor.
When to Use — Where Guarantor disputes liability arguing Promisee must first exhaust all remedies against Promisor. Cite to establish that Guarantor's liability is direct and coextensive — but also ensure primary default is documented with demand letter + non-performance evidence before Guarantor is approached.
Bailment is Sui Generis — NOT Sale, Transfer, or Agency
Para 2.11 | Contract Act | Schedule I Para 3 Exclusion
Key Ratio
Bailment is sui generis — a category of legal relations all of its own — and one that is not to be equated or treated as being synonymous with sale, transfer, or agency. Bailee does not represent Bailor before third-parties. Bailment does not attract legal fiction in paragraph 3, Schedule I of Central GST Act. Goods given to Contractor as free-issue-material to carry out works is under bailment and not sale. Goods misused (wastage) attracts remedy to compensate Customer — but not a transfer of property as no such transfer was contemplated.
When to Use — Where Revenue demands GST on free-issue-material given to job-worker / contractor / manufacturer. Cite to establish: (a) No title transfer — bailment only; (b) Schedule I Para 3 legal fiction is for Agency — not applicable to bailment; (c) Deemed supply cannot arise on goods given under pure bailment arrangement.
Reliable Test for True Agency — Authority to Bind Principal with Third-Parties
Para 2.12.1 | Section 182 Contract Act | GST Intermediary Test
Key Ratio
A reliable test of existence of agency would be existence of authority for the Agent to bring about binding obligations for Principal with Third-Parties, which are to be fulfilled by the Principal and not by the Agent. It is not what the actor is called but the 'representative capacity' that is lawfully vested and exercised that is conclusive. Existence of agency is not a matter of opinion but one of evidence — and the test being creation of binding obligations and not fulfilment of past obligations of said Principal.
When to Use — In any dispute about intermediary classification under Section 2(13) IGST Act. Where taxpayer is characterised as 'intermediary' — argue: Has taxpayer brought about binding obligations between Principal and Third-Parties that Principal must fulfil? If taxpayer is fulfilling own obligations directly — no agency exists — zero-rating on exports allowed.
Agency Coupled with Interest — Section 202 | JDA + GPA
Para 2.12.6 | Section 202 Contract Act | Example 184 | JDA Context
Key Ratio
Developer who enters into joint-development agreement duly registered with Landowner along with General Power of Attorney (GST-cum-JDA) to act on behalf of Landowner in carrying out development of the property — is 'agency coupled with interest'. Agent who acquires 'interest' in the property cannot be evicted by termination of contract with prejudice to such interest — protection flows from Section 202 of Contract law. Contract for transfer of interest in immovable property reduced in writing and duly registered constitutes 'agency with interest' that cannot be terminated without causing prejudice to such interest.
When to Use — Where Landowner seeks to terminate JDA mid-project and argues that supply by Developer has not been made / is incomplete. Argue: (a) Registered JDA+GPA = agency coupled with interest; (b) Landowner cannot evict Developer without legal consequence; (c) Developer's supply (construction services) crystallised on JDA registration — taxable event already occurred.
Section 9(5) — Fictional Supplier Cannot Bear Double Tax Liability
Para 2.12.13 | Section 9(5) CGST Act | ECOM Operators
Key Ratio
When Agent (ECOM) is seated "as" the Supplier for purposes of Section 9(5), then the actual Supplier (Driver/Restaurant) is unseated. When the entire transaction value is subjected to applicable rate of tax, the fictional Supplier cannot also be liable to tax on service charges or convenience fees charged to actual Supplier or to Customer. Rate of tax to be discharged by fictional Supplier is the same rate that would apply to the underlying supply. Creation of an independent stream of revenue (delivery charges, listing fees) to justify separate ITC credits must be tested on principles in Furniss v. Dawson.
When to Use — In ECOM GST disputes: (a) Where ECOM operator is also charged GST on platform/delivery fees separately from the Sec 9(5) liability — argue: double taxation on same transaction value; (b) Where actual Supplier (Driver/Restaurant) is also demanded registration/GST — argue: unseated by Sec 9(5) fiction — actual Supplier need not register if threshold not crossed independently.
Payer of Consideration = Recipient — EXCEPT in Agency
Para 2.12.5 | Section 2(93) CGST Act | Agency Commission
Key Ratio
Payer of consideration is the Recipient of supply under Section 2(93) of Central GST Act — in all cases EXCEPT in case of Agency. In Agency, Agent may collect remuneration from Third-Party by direction of Principal — while the Recipient of the agency service remains the Principal. 'Payer of remuneration' cannot therefore be admitted as the most reliable test of 'Recipient' of Agent's services in all cases. This distinction is critical where payment of Agent's remuneration is discharged by Third-Party — as it gives rise to GST demand from wrong person, especially in case of import of services.
When to Use — Where Revenue demands GST on 'import of services' or reverse charge from Subsidiary/Third-Party who merely paid the Agent's fee on behalf of Principal. Argue: Payer of fee ≠ Recipient of service in agency context. Recipient is the Principal who appointed the Agent. GST obligation falls on Principal — not the Third-Party payer.
Qui Facit Per Alium, Facit Per Se — Principal Bound by Agent's Acts
Para 2.12.1 | Latin Maxim | Respondeat Superior | Section 222 Contract Act
Key Ratio / Latin Maxim
Qui facit per alium, facit per se — He who acts through another, does the act himself. Agency is the instrumentality of the Principal. Principal bears vicarious liability (respondeat superior) for actions of Agent within scope of Agency. Principal may repudiate actions of Agent in excess of authority or ratify the same. Obligations asserted by Agent must be those which Principal has: (i) contractual capacity (ii) ostensible competence and (iii) lawful authority, to perform.
When to Use — In two scenarios: (a) Where Principal argues they are not bound by Agent's representation to Third-Parties — cite qui facit per alium to establish Principal's direct liability for Agent's acts within scope. (b) Where Revenue attributes Agent's action to Principal for GST evasion — ensure the action was within the scope of Agency before accepting vicarious liability — actions outside scope are Agent's personal liability only.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | GST Litigation Practice
📖 Ch 3a — Sale of Goods Act (Overview)

⚖️ Chapter 3 — Sale of Goods Act | Object, Conclusion & Not-a-Sale

Source: GST Litigation Reference — Allied Laws | Para 3.1 to 3.6 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root Node
Level 1 Branch
Valid / Permitted
Void / Prohibited
Condition / Unless / If
Alternative / Optional
Neutral / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
3.1 Relevance to GST
3.2.1 Goods — Definition
3.2.2 Future Goods
3.2.3 Ascertained Goods
3.2.4 Merchantability
3.2.5 Condition
3.2.6 Warranty
3.2.7 Condition as Warranty
3.3.1 Preliminary Aspects
3.3.2 Formation of Sale Contract
3.3.3 Adjusted Object
3.3.4 Passing of Title
3.3.5 Cross-Cancelling Sales
3.3.6 Job-Work
3.3.7 Manufacture
3.3.8 Goods Consumed in Service
3.3.9 Service Simplicitor
3.3.10 Reusable Service
3.3.11 Service to Goods
3.3.12 Goods to be Services
3.3.13 IPR
3.4.1 Limited Duties of Seller
3.4.2 Transportation Does Not Transfer Title
3.4.3 Appropriation to Contract
3.4.4 Loss Payee in Insurance
3.4.5 Sales Returns
3.4.6 Rejection of Sale
3.5.1 Sale-on-Approval Principles
3.5.2 Stock-in-Common
3.5.3 Fulfilment Centres
3.6.1 Transfer, Not Sale
3.6.2 Intention to Sell
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
3.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
📦 Sale of Goods Act — Relevance to GST
Law of Sale | Benjamin's Definition | 8 Forms of Supply
