📝 Sale of Goods Act applies only to 'goods' — not services or immovable property. But 8 of the supply forms under GST Section 7(1)(a) relate to goods. To understand those supplies, you must know Sale of Goods Act deeply.
BENJAMIN'S DEFINITION
Sale = Transfer of absolute or general property in a thing for a price in money
📝 Benjamin's works on Sale are foundational. A sale requires money as consideration. Non-monetary consideration = NOT a sale.
GST LINK
Sale of Goods Act originally in Sections 76–123 of Indian Contract Act — relocated. Contract law deliberations continue to apply subconsciously in Sale of Goods Act interpretation.
REUSABLE vs NON-REUSABLE SERVICE
Repairing equipment (non-reusable by Recipient) = skill of Supplier = license, not lease
Training in repair (reusable by Recipient) = skill of Supplier = not lease
💡 Eg.198: Supply of service of repairing equipment = supply of skill of Supplier that is non-reusable by Recipient — it is not a lease but a license.

💡 Eg.199: Training in repair = reusable by Recipient — not lease.
KEY Uniformity of tariff classification for goods and services undermines deep study of differences. But in disputes about valuation of services — these differences greatly assist defence to challenge Revenue's instances put forward as comparable.
3.2.1 — Goods — Statutory Definition & GST Comparison ⇧ Index
📋 "Goods" — Section 2(7) SOGA vs Section 2(52) CGST Act
📝 Both laws define 'goods' but with critical differences — especially on actionable claims and growing crops. The difference in a single 'comma' changes the legal meaning completely.
ElementSOGA Section 2(7)CGST Act Section 2(52)Significance
Actionable Claims Excluded — outside SOGA scope Included — expressly included in GST SOGA jurisprudence on actionable claims is inapplicable; but full force of prevailing jurisprudence must be extended for GST purposes
Stocks & Shares Included in definition Excluded (money & securities excluded) Transfer of shares is not supply of goods in GST
Growing Crop / Grass Comma before "and things" → growing crop & grass are goods even without agreement to sever No comma → words of qualification "agreed to be severed" apply to all including growing crops and grass Critical difference! Legislature deliberately deleted the comma in GST — changing meaning
STANDING CROP vs GROWING CROP
Standing Crop: Ceased dependence on land — completed growing — ready to be severed → already movable → goods
Growing Crop: Still dependent on land — continues growing → immovable → not goods until severed
📝 Standing crop = paddy that has ripened and stopped growing. It is physically still rooted but legally it is movable. Growing crop = paddy still growing and dependent on the soil for nutrients. This distinction is of great importance in GST — especially for agricultural produce.
3.2.2 — Future Goods ⇧ Index
🔮 Future Goods — Section 2(6) SOGA
📝 Future goods = goods yet to be made or acquired by the seller at the time of making the contract. You can contract for them — but the contract is really a 'promise to contract' and not a 'contract of sale' until the goods come into existence.
SECTION 2(6) "Future goods" means goods to be manufactured or produced or acquired by the seller after the making of the contract of sale
IF ACKNOWLEDGED AS FUTURE GOODS
Contract is non est — Parties cannot agree about a thing that does not exist
📝 If both parties openly admit the goods don't exist yet — there is no valid contract of sale. It is only an agreement to contract later.
💡 Eg.200: Contract to sell a cruise ship — specifications so unique that once built it is one-of-a-kind — cannot already exist — contract is something more (works contract).

💡 Eg.201: Contract for laptop could be valid sale even if OEM inventory is NIL — laptops of that specification exist in general trade.
IF NOT ACKNOWLEDGED
Parties may enter contract on Seller's assertion that goods exist (or will be ready by delivery date). Contract of sale valid for existing goods appropriated later.
📝 Seller says "I have it / will have it ready" — without disclosing non-existence. This allows a valid contract of sale for goods to be appropriated later from existing or new stock.
WORKS CONTRACT TEST Contracts for custom-built articles cannot be negotiated in vacuo. If goods are not in existence and must be manufactured to spec — these are contracts for works, not sale simplicitor. Custom-built goods forming object of negotiations = goods not in existence → contract for works.
3.2.3 — Ascertained Goods ⇧ Index
📌 Ascertained Goods — Setting Apart for Delivery
📝 'Ascertained' means 'set apart' for a specific contract — identified from the common stock. This does NOT yet pass title. Title passes only when Buyer appropriates or accepts them.
CANNOT
Goods set apart for Customer A CANNOT be diverted to Customer B (with earlier delivery date)
Setting apart is irreversible — pre-empts passing of title
SALE IN PRAESENTI
Can be entered into for existing goods even if unascertained from common stock
Ascertainment does NOT pass risks to Acceptor unless appropriated per terms of sale
📝 Unascertained goods does NOT mean future goods. Goods may exist in a common pile — just not yet identified for this particular contract.
3.2.4 — Merchantability ⇧ Index
✅ Merchantability — Fit-for-Purpose Test
📝 Merchantable quality = goods that do what they are designed and declared to do. If they fail this test, the identity of the goods themselves changes — they are no longer 'those goods'. This has massive implications for HSN classification and GST demands on expired/defective goods.
FIT-FOR-PURPOSE = MERCHANTABLE
Goods meeting purpose of their design and declared end-use → merchantable quality
Decided by trade and stated purpose in that trade
Custom-built goods: purpose defined in arrangement
UNFIT-FOR-PURPOSE
Cannot be identified as those goods
May be scrap or something else — NOT the identity of fit-for-purpose goods
Sale is void ab initio on discovery of unfitness
📝 Expired medicine is not 'medicine' — its identity flows from merchantable quality. Once unfit, it becomes a different article (scrap/waste) with a different HSN.
💡 Eg.202: Expired medicines returned to manufacturer are NOT medicines. Identity flows from implied merchantable quality / fit-for-purpose attribute.

💡 Eg.206: Old newspaper is NOT newspaper since not current — not fit-for-purpose as newspaper.

💡 Eg.207: Old motor vehicle IS still motor vehicle — fit-for-purpose as such.
GST CONSEQUENCE Where merchantability is in doubt — HSN code of merchantable quality goods CANNOT be applied. Where object of contract is NOT goods → supply of services in GST.

Sale using description bears implied warranty of fit-for-purpose. Sale is complete on delivery. If found unfit on inspection → sale void ab initio. Any tax treatment extended due to assumption of completion of sale → reversed on discovery of failure.
💡 Eg.203: Contract for healthcare services cannot be for 'sale of prosthetics' although price-longevity-benefits aspects may be discussed — object is NOT for sale of goods.

💡 Eg.204: Contract for food items preserved in sugar syrup in vacuum sealed container bearing MRP = sale of goods.

💡 Eg.205: Food bearing "best to consume within 3 days, no preservatives, handle with care, may be recycled" — contraindicates identity as goods (packaged for redistribution).
3.2.5 — Condition of Sale ⇧ Index
🔒 Conditions of Sale — Three Types
📝 A condition is central and substantive to the sale itself. Failure of condition denies the sale — even if already concluded. It is not just a 'rejection' (which requires subjective examination). Failure goes to the root of the transaction.
(a) CONDITIONS PRECEDENT
Sale does NOT arise unless conditions are fulfilled first
💡 Eg.208: Perfecting title by Seller before conferring title to Buyer = condition precedent.
(b) CONDITIONS CONCURRENT
To be shown to be fulfilled at time of passing title — simultaneous fulfilment
(c) CONDITIONS SUBSEQUENT
Sale concluded in anticipation that conditions will be subsequently fulfilled
💡 Eg.209: Sale of equipment subject to installation and testing = conditional sale (condition subsequent).

💡 Eg.210: Works contractor's bills 'subject to' approval by Project Engineer = NOT conditional sale. Approval = determination of completion.
KEY RULE Conditions are NOT: tendering of consideration by Buyer, conforming to description/sample. Conditions furnish occasion to constitute a sale. Failure of conditions → transaction non est. Waiver of fatal breach = substitution of contract.
3.2.6 — Warranty ⇧ Index
📋 Warranty — Section 14 Implied Undertaking as to Title
📝 Warranties are continuation of assertions made at the time of sale — essential to satisfaction of sale during agreed tenure. Warranty is a good indicator of the object of contract.
SECTION 14 — IMPLIED WARRANTIES (Unless different intention shown)
(a) TITLE
Seller has right to sell at time of sale (or will have right when property passes)
(b) QUIET POSSESSION
Buyer shall have and enjoy quiet possession of the goods
(c) FREE FROM ENCUMBRANCE
Goods free from any charge/encumbrance in favour of third party not declared or known to Buyer at time of contract
KEY CASE Kone Elevators India (P.) Ltd. v. State of TN [2014] 45 taxmann.com 150 (SC) — whether object of contract was sale simplicitor or works contract was NOT categorically decided by Apex Court but left to be determined based on terms of each contract and its stipulations. Shows great discernment in leaving room for play based on stipulations in each contract to guide the object and procure the tax treatment.
3.2.7 — Condition as Warranty ⇧ Index
🔄 Condition Treated as Warranty — Section 13
📝 Sometimes a breach (condition) can be 'saved' by treating the condition as a mere warranty — so the sale survives with a damage claim rather than full repudiation. This saves the executed portion of the contract.
Non-fatal breach or fatal breach waived by Promisee → saves the contract and Parties from re-tendering and accepting the variation caused
Provided for in Section 13 — treat condition as warranty: not to dilute rigours of performance in future but to avoid repudiation of contract and save executed portion
LIMITS
Waiver = like acquiescence
Does NOT alter contract in areas still executory
Does NOT create estoppel against future breach
Waiver of fatal breach → substitution of contract (NOT condition-as-warranty)
IMPORTANT Non-payment of consideration does NOT come within this legal fiction. Waiver applies only to Promisor's performance — not Promisee's payment obligation.
3.3.1 — Conclusion of Sale — Preliminary Aspects ⇧ Index
⚖️ Conclusion of Sale — Section 4 | Sale vs Agreement to Sell
📝 A 'sale' is complete only when property (title) transfers for money consideration. If transfer is to happen in future or subject to a condition, it is only an 'agreement to sell' — not a sale yet.
SALE
Property in goods transferred from Seller to Buyer NOW
Consideration = money (cash)
📝 Title passes immediately at point of sale. Once title is passed, sale is complete.
AGREEMENT TO SELL
Transfer to take place at future time OR subject to conditions to be fulfilled
→ Becomes a sale when time elapses OR conditions are fulfilled
NOT A SALE
Consideration other than money → NOT sale (Sale of Goods Act has no application)
Non-monetary consideration = barter/transfer — NOT sale
📝 But two cross-cancelling sales (each for money) whose money consideration is squared off does NOT become barter — each is still a sale.
TESTS APPLIED — Court History
(i) Principal object test — HAL v. State of Karnataka 1984 SC
(ii) Dominant intention test — Rainbow Colour Lab v. State of MP 2000 SC (doubted in ACC Ltd. v. CC 2001 SC)
(iii) Factual transfer test — article 366(29A) after 46th Constitutional Amendment
Leading position in CC v. Suzlon Energy Ltd. (CA 11400-11401/2018 SC) may need reconsideration.
💡 Eg.211: Contract for installation of air-conditioner = contract for services. Customer is obliged to make air-conditioner available for contractor to perform installation. No implying that air-conditioner is sold by Customer and purchased back via work contract.
3.3.2 — Formation of Sale Contract — Section 5 & 6 ⇧ Index
📝 Formation of Sale Contract — Goods Must Exist
📝 Sale is complete on delivery — not consumption. For the contract to form, the goods (object) must be in existence at the time of contract — present, ascertained, not future or unascertained. If goods don't exist, acceptance creates no contract.
SECTION 5 Contract of sale may be made by offer to buy or sell, for a price and acceptance. May be in writing, word of mouth, partly both, or implied from conduct of Parties.
SECTION 6 Goods must be existing goods (owned or possessed by Seller) OR future goods. If Seller purports to effect present sale of future goods → contract operates as agreement to sell only.
💡 Eg.213: Sale of door (wooden) — contract for sale of wooden door if door exists and is offered for sale to Buyer who is able to inspect and accept terms.

💡 Eg.214: Sale of door after production = promise to contract (if and) when production is complete and door (as described) comes into existence.

💡 Eg.215: Contract for wooden door yet to be produced but type of wood identified and confirmed from manufacturer's stock = (i) contract for sale of wood AND (ii) work order to produce door per specification — in two separate or single composite document.
KEY Contract for non-existent goods = only promise in praesenti to enter into contract in futuro. Terms negotiated = merely bargain of promise in praesenti.
3.3.3 — Adjusted Object ⇧ Index
🎯 Adjusted Object — Interplay of Sections 4(3) & 6(3)
📝 'Adjusted object' = the actual object of the contract after giving due consideration to what exists at the time of contract. The contract description may say 'wooden door' but the real (adjusted) object could be 'wood' + 'work order'. This adjustment is critical to determining the right GST treatment.
Once Adjusted Object is Identified — Further Check Required
CONTRACT IN PRAESENTI
Parties have asserted and acted upon document 'as if' a contract — adjusted object is exposed beyond title and descriptions
Established by enquiring into remedies for breach
POOR WORKMANSHIP TEST
Non-fatal breach (poor workmanship) tests:
(a) Risk of workmanship loss transferred already?
(b) Remedy capped to production contract or extends to materials?
(c) Cross-fall breach clauses converting production contract into composite agreement in praesenti?
KEY PRINCIPLE Tax treatment applicable to contract of sale of 'adjusted object' applies whether adjusted object resides in one contract or two contracts in a single composite document. Lee v. Griffin (1861) and Robinson v. Graves (1935) are landmark cases on adjusted object.
3.3.4 — Passing of Title — Section 19 ⇧ Index
🏛️ Passing of Title — Section 19 | Property Passes When Intended to Pass
📝 Title (ownership) does NOT pass automatically on delivery. It passes when Parties intend it to pass — determined from terms of contract, conduct of Parties, and circumstances. Right of rejection = title not yet passed. Once right of rejection passes — title cannot remain with Seller.
SECTION 19 For sale of specific/ascertained goods — property transfers to Buyer at such time as Parties intend it to be transferred. Sections 20–24 are rules for ascertaining that intention.
SALE BY DESCRIPTION
If knowledgeable in trade can recognize goods produced with words of description → Seller has complied
SALE BY SAMPLE
If goods match all relevant parameters of sample → Seller compliant. Matching decided by domain experts — not subjective satisfaction of Buyer
IF NOT IN CONFORMITY
Loss remains with Seller (Producer). Buyer has remedy for damages for delay without responsibility to pay for material used in producing non-conforming goods. Right of rejection enjoyed if contract is for sale simplicitor of goods already in existence.
INTERIM PROGRESS CLAIMS For contract for adjusted object (sale of wood + contract for production): title in wood passes on issuance. Risk of loss passes qua wood to Buyer. Producer stands to lose production charges for failure to meet description/sample. Hence — interim progress claims made and certified by independent reviewers.
💡 Eg.212: Glassware store sign "if you break it, you bought it" = NOT affirmation of passing of title as glassware knocked over was falling until it landed on floor. Sign merely cautions about liquidated damages applicable for breakage.
3.3.5 — Cross-Cancelling Sales & Settlement of Consideration ⇧ Index
🔀 Cross-Cancelling Sales — Two Independent Sales, Squared-Off Consideration
📝 When the same two parties sell different goods to each other and settle by squaring off the money amounts — this is NOT barter. Each sale is independent for money consideration. It becomes barter only if consideration for Goods A is denominated in equivalent quantity of Goods B.
CROSS-CANCELLING SALES ✓
Two independent contracts — each for money consideration — roles of Promisor/Promisee reverse per supply direction
Squaring off consideration ≠ barter
Each sale transacted for money denominated consideration
💡 Eg.216: Animal genetic material (HSN 0511, Nil tariff) sold to genetic engineering lab. Lab processes it, sells processed material (HSN 0511) back to same Customer. Net value added = processing charges (HSN 9988 @ 12% or 18%). Receivable and payable squared off.
WHEN SECOND SALE IS SHAM
If Buyer in first transaction obliged to resell back after processing + restriction on 'place and purpose of further use' — purported sale may be dismissed as sham
Such arrangements = job-work parading as two sales
💡 Eg.217: Miller of paddy into rice — both paddy and rice are exempt. But milling is liable to GST at 12% (registered Customer) or 18% (unregistered). Cross-cancelling sales is understandable but remains to be tested.
TEST — Is It Really Cross-Cancelling?
Must check: (a) Barter (goods/services) (b) Exchange (immovable property only) (c) Job-work — or vice versa

For second transaction to be admitted as independent sale:
(i) No restrictions on 'place and purpose of further use' after first purchase
(ii) Risk of loss assumed by Buyer (cum-Processor) after first purchase
(iii) Right of rejection enjoyed by Buyer in second transaction — Seller free to divert delivery to any other buyer until actual delivery
3.3.6 — Job-Work ⇧ Index
🔧 Job-Work — Treatment or Process on Goods of Another
📝 Job work = doing something to goods that belong to someone else. It is always a service — not a sale. Under GST, for the concessional tariff to apply, the person whose goods are being processed must be 'registered'.
WHAT IS JOB WORK
Every 'treatment or process' (not just transformation)
Includes: sorting, grading, packing, labelling, QC
May cause change — or simply perfect identity that was ambiguous
💡 Eg.218: Sorting/grading — not readily admitted as job work unless articles produced in mixed condition and grading separates them from common mix.
💡 Eg.219: Packing and labelling = treatment or process.
💡 Eg.220: QC of batch samples = treatment or process.
NOT JOB WORK
Repair = NOT job work (job work brings intended functionality to article for first time; repair restores impaired/deteriorated functionality)
Installation = NOT treatment or process even if manipulations carried out — purpose is to complete installation
📝 Job work brings NEW functionality. Repair RESTORES old functionality. Installation COMPLETES the item. Each has different GST treatment.
GST ELIGIBILITY For job work concessional tariff under Section 2(68) CGST: person whose goods are subject to treatment/process must be 'registered'. Schedule II Para 3 = treatment/process on goods belonging to another = service.
3.3.7 — Manufacture — Section 2(72) CGST ⇧ Index
🏭 Manufacture — Section 2(72) Central GST Act
📝 Manufacture creates a new article from input — one that is NOT functionally interchangeable with the input. Persons knowing the output must not be able to 'make-do' with input instead. Section 2(72) of CGST Act captures years of Central Excise judicial experimentation.
TEST FOR MANUFACTURE
Input (original article) and output (resultant article) must NOT be functionally interchangeable
Persons with knowledge of output must not be indifferent about using input instead
Positive action NOT required — inaction can be manufacture (fermentation of grape → wine)
NOT DETERMINATIVE
Ease of process — not determinative
Complexity of process — not determinative
Where users are indifferent → unlikely to be manufacture
📝 Transporting timber using elephants = not manufacture. Assembling computer using screwdriver technology = manufacture. It is about functional indifference, not effort.
3.3.8 — Goods Consumed in Service ⇧ Index
🔥 Goods Consumed in Service — NOT Sale of Goods
📝 If goods are used up in providing a service — that use is not a 'sale' of goods to the Customer. In GST, 'service' is a noun (anti-thesis of goods). If the object of contract is NOT goods, it is services.
'USED UP' = SERVICE
Goods consumed/exhausted in providing service = provision of service, NOT sale of goods to Customer
Often expected that goods be 'used up' for contract to be one of service
💡 Eg.221: Diesel 'used up' in passenger transport — but bus is NOT 'used up' (only 'used') — object is passenger transport service.

💡 Eg.222: Cement and sand 'used' in construction — NOT 'used up' in manufacture of RMC. Labour/consumables 'used up' in manufacture of RMC.

💡 Eg.223: Catalyst 'used up' in sample testing = consumed by Supplier — not sold to Customer.

💡 Eg.224: Electrodes 'used up' in welding steel sheets — not sold to Customer even though weld area bears deposits.
'USED' NOT 'USED UP' = GOODS
Goods continue to exist after contract = object not readily admitted as service
Goods used (not exhausted) suggest transfer of possession/right
💡 Eg.225: Prosthetics implanted in Patient = NOT sale of goods but healthcare service by Surgeon (MIOT Hospitals is an outlier).

💡 Eg.226: Self-driven cars on time-based rent = lease of car (lawful possession transferred to Customer).

💡 Eg.227: EV batteries on fixed rent/month — SIM-based control retained by Rental Co. → lawful possession NOT transferred → NOT lease of battery.
3.3.9 — Service Simplicitor ⇧ Index
🎭 Service Simplicitor — Service as a Noun in GST
📝 In English, 'service' is a verb — it signifies an action. But in GST, 'service' is defined as a noun in Section 2(102) — it is the anti-thesis of 'goods'. To be a service in GST, it suffices to show the object is NOT goods.
SERVICES AS VERB (Executory)
Services of Surgeon, Pilot, Driver, etc. — action provides desired result
These are services where action = the object
GOODS 'TREATED' AS SERVICES
Transactions involving goods that are 'treated' as supply of services by legal fiction — Schedule II
Object continues to be goods — but GST 'treats' supply as service
📝 Lease of goods = treated as service by Schedule II para 1(b) and 5(f). The goods don't stop being goods — only the supply is treated as service for GST.
3.3.10 & 3.3.11 — Reusable Service & Service to Goods ⇧ Index
♻️ Reusable Service → Service to Goods | TCS Ltd. v. State of AP
REUSABLE SERVICE
Services not consumed and exhausted once supplied — engender ability to exploit repeatedly
Teacher, Author, Designer, Copyrighter — remains long after the person
Translated into goods extending usability without dependence on person
📝 A teacher's lesson, once taught, can be recalled and used repeatedly. If recorded and distributed, it becomes goods (a product capable of extraction and storage).
SERVICE TO GOODS
When service becomes capable of extraction and storage suited for transmission → it is goods, not services
Author preparing manuscript = service; Publisher publishes book = goods
Research = service; Patent granted = goods (if sold); License of patent = service
📝 The moment a service is captured and packaged for independent use — it becomes goods. Manuscript becomes book. Research becomes patent. Software satisfies three attributes: utility, capable of being bought/sold, capable of being transmitted/stored.
💡 TCS Ltd. v. State of AP — "Indian law does not make distinction between tangible and intangible property. A 'goods' may be tangible or intangible. Goods = utility + capable of being bought/sold + capable of being transmitted/delivered/stored/possessed."

💡 Eg.228: Testing = service; spent samples not transferred to Customer — always belonged to Customer. Sending spent samples = only to validate report.

💡 Eg.229: Software testing services (software developed by others) = location-based service — 'place of supply' in India where lab is set up — NOT 'export of services'.
3.3.12 & 3.3.13 — Goods to be Services & IPR ⇧ Index
📱 Goods 'Treated as Services' (Legal Fiction) & IPR
GOODS TREATED AS SERVICES
Lease/license of goods — 'treated' as supply of services (Schedule II, paras 1(b) and 5(f))
They do NOT cease to be goods — GST superimposes legal fiction
Place of supply = location of supplier (not goods)
📝 This matters for inter-state vs intra-state determination. Without this legal fiction, monthly lease of goods to Customer in another State would shift from inter-state to intra-state mid-lease as goods are constructively returned and recollected.
IPR
Intangible movable property = goods. Intangibility does not impair movability or rights as real property.
Schedule II para 5(c) demands 'treatment' as services only when use is permitted temporarily
Permanent alienation of rights to IPRs → NOT legal fiction of services → it is GOODS
📝 Software license (temporary use) = treated as service. Software sold permanently (all rights transferred) = goods. Brand name and goodwill = goods. Actionable claims that are transferred = goods.
3.4.1 — Duty of Delivery — Limited Duties of Seller ⇧ Index
🚚 Duty of Delivery — Section 33 | Seller's Limited Duties
📝 The Seller's duty is not to proactively deliver at Buyer's doorstep. Once goods are produced/ascertained and set aside, Buyer must give delivery instructions and may be required to lift the material.
SECTION 33 Delivery = doing anything which Parties agree shall be treated as delivery OR which has the effect of putting goods in possession of Buyer or any person authorised to hold on his behalf.
BUYER COLLECTING ≠ TITLE PASSED
If Buyer retains right of rejection where quality established only after installation/testing → pre-shipment inspection does NOT pass title
DELIVERY ≠ TRANSIT RISK REMOVED
If sold on ex-works basis and goods match description/sample before transportation → Buyer bears transit risk
Seller delivering to Buyer does NOT excuse Buyer's transit risk
💡 Eg.230: Gold jewellery sold over-the-counter = cannot be subject to acceptance by Buyer at destination — contrary to customs of trade.

💡 Eg.231: OTC medicines without prescription = cannot be subject to acceptance at later point — vague and uncertain acceptance tests.
3.4.2 — Transportation Does Not Transfer Title ⇧ Index
🚛 Transportation ≠ Transfer of Title | Risk = Reliable Indicator
📝 Who arranges transport has nothing to do with when title passes. The surest test of 'terms of sale' is NOT who bears transportation responsibility — it is the point when 'risks and rewards' pass. After that point, Buyer no longer has right to reject delivery.
SUREST TEST
Point when 'risks and rewards' pass = point in time or location after which Buyer no longer enjoys right to reject delivery
Risks borne by either Party = reliable indicator of title held
RESERVATION OF RIGHT TO DISPOSAL — Section 25
Seller may reserve right of disposal until conditions fulfilled — even after delivery to carrier
If shipped by railway and bill of lading in Seller's name → prima facie Seller reserved right of disposal
Property does NOT pass until Seller's conditions are fulfilled
💡 Eg.232: Manufacturer of windmills refuses transportation responsibility but accepts installation, commissioning, testing. Buyer arranges transport. Title passes after commissioning tests pass. Risk of damage during transportation falls on Buyer. All other risks remain with Seller unless totally damaged during transport.

💡 Eg.233: Buyer allowed 100% refund for online purchase — sale completed on delivery if matching description/image. Sales return and refund = separate transactions.
3.4.3 — Appropriation to Contract — Section 15 ⇧ Index
📋 Appropriation to Contract — Sale by Description or Sample
📝 Goods readied for a contract must match either the description or the sample agreed. Pre-shipment inspection at Seller's location is a good indicator that Buyer accepts the goods and has no further right of rejection once delivered to site.
SECTION 15 Where sale by description — implied condition that goods correspond with the description. If also by sample — bulk must correspond with both description and sample (sample alone is not sufficient).
KEY Pre-shipment inspection at location of Seller is good indicator that Buyer carries out inspection and no longer holds right of rejection especially once goods are delivered to Buyer's site. But this depends on the 'mode' of identification contained in the contract.
3.4.4 — Loss Payee in Insurance ⇧ Index
🔒 Loss Payee in Insurance — Title is Risk | Risk is Title
📝 'Loss payee' = the person who collects insurance proceeds if goods are damaged. This person may or may not be the titleholder. Insurance interest is broader than risk and title — but locating the loss payee gives information about who holds title.
PRINCIPLE
Title is linked to risk. In fact: title = risk
Titleholder bears risks and must normally be loss payee where risk is insured
Locating loss payee → offers information about titleholder
LENDER AS LOSS PAYEE
A Lender may be named as loss payee where monies advanced to project and charge exists on all present and future assets of Borrower (Buyer)
Person collecting insurance compensation does NOT always hold title
3.4.5 — Sales Returns ⇧ Index
↩️ Sales Returns — Proof of Concluded Sale | Expired Goods HSN Issue
📝 Return of goods is actually MORE compelling proof that the sale was concluded than payment can evidence. A return implies the sale happened — goods went to Buyer with title — and now title is being retransferred back to Seller. This is a separate transaction.
HOW RETURNS HAPPEN
Seller collecting back goods with credit note, OR Buyer issuing tax invoice
Accounting treatment in either party's books = NO indicator of passing of title or contractual arrangement
EXPIRED GOODS RETURN — CRITICAL
Expired medicines returned = NOT cancellation of original sale but evidence original sale WAS concluded
Cannot apply HSN 30XX (medicines) — they are now unfit-for-sale
Full refund under HSN 9997
💡 Eg.234: Expired medicines returned to manufacturer = evidence original sale concluded. Cannot classify under HSN 30XX — they are not fit-for-sale medicines. If full refund collected → HSN 9997.
IMPORTANT Return on expiration of merchantability ≠ contingency attached to original sale. It would make original sale a 'consignment sale akin to sale-on-approval' — which belies validity of the original sale. Return on expiration = separate transaction, not reversal of original.
3.4.6 — Rejection of Sale ⇧ Index
❌ Rejection of Sale — NOT Sales Return | No Output Tax Due
📝 Rejection = Buyer says 'I don't accept these goods.' This is possible only if title has NOT yet passed. If title hasn't passed — there was no completed sale — and therefore NO output tax is due.
REJECTION ≠ SALES RETURN
Rejection of goods (title not yet passed) ≠ sales returns
Credit note may cancel tax invoice but does NOT indicate sale is complete
No output tax due when sales are rejected
Customs of trade prevail over innovative contract-writing
ITC IMPLICATIONS
Right of rejection = important factor to define passing of title and locate titleholder
Cannot be lightly considered
💡 Eg.235: Rejected goods lost in transit = (i) credit note for cancellation of sale + (ii) reversal of ITC on inputs consumed. But loss of goods sold (being returned) = output tax; no credit reversal required.

💡 Eg.236: Full refund by ECOM for defective goods delivered online — advising Customer NOT to return goods (high collection/disposal cost) = two transactions: (i) supply as sale of goods, and (ii) service involved in refund made to Customer by ECOM. Loss of goods = no credit note required since goods not returned.
3.5.1 — Sale-on-Approval — Section 24 ⇧ Index
✅ Sale-on-Approval — Section 24 | When Title Passes
📝 In a buyer's market, Seller cannot demand payment on delivery. Goods go to intermediary on approval — sale is complete only when resale occurs. This involves 'conversion' — instantaneous passing of title when goods are resold or treated as one's own.
SECTION 24 Goods sent on approval or 'on sale or return' — property passes to Buyer when:
(a) He signifies approval or acceptance or does any act adopting the transaction
(b) If he does not signify rejection — retains beyond fixed time (or reasonable time if no time fixed)
SECTION 31(7) CGST ACT
Supplier may deliver goods on-approval but 6-month time limit imposed to end uncertainty about status of approval
If damaged/resold → approval impliedly accepted immediately before damage/resale
GST — SUPPLIER
Goods impliedly approved → Section 31(7) requires tax invoice to be issued
GST — RECIPIENT
Recipient who accepted and appropriated goods → may be liable to credit reversal under Section 17(5)(h) OR payment of output tax on outward supply
💡 Eg.237: Demand for GST using HSN applicable to 'goods' assumes form of supply in wastage of goods is 'sale'. Since goods are wasted — there is no momentary transfer before (excess) consumption — defence need only show it is not 'sale' for demand to fail. Limitation may run out before revised notice is issued with HSN applicable to 'services'.
3.5.2 — Stock-in-Common | ATF Aviation Fuel ⇧ Index
✈️ Stock-in-Common — ATF / Aviation Turbine Fuel Principle
📝 ATF is stored in shared reservoirs at airports by multiple Oil companies. The intermediary manages these shared stocks. This creates a unique legal question — who passes title and when — especially since stocks are intermixed without quality/quantity differentiation.
WHO IS THE SELLER
Privity of contract = Oil Co. and Carrier (qua ATF)
Intermediary = storage-and-handling service provider
Holding/handling stock-in-common does NOT pass title to Intermediary
Without title, Intermediary CANNOT confer title to Carriers
BUYER'S INDIFFERENCE TEST
Principle extends beyond ATF only if Customers in those sectors are:
(i) Agnostic to identity of owners of stock-so-intermixed AND
(ii) Indifferent to possible quality variances in the intermixed stock-in-common
📝 If Customers are NOT indifferent (they care who owns the stock or its quality) → operation of 'agency of necessity' through conduct would apply (Schedule I para 3 CGST Act).
KEY CASES PST Energy 7 Shipping LLC v. OW Bunker Malta Ltd. (2016) UKSC 23 — not a sale contract but a sui generis supply contract analogous to sale. South Australian Insurance Co. Rendell (1869) LR 3 PC 101 — intermixing of stock-in-common NOT a sale but mere bailment. Qualified in Mercer v. Craven Grain Storage Ltd. (1994) to accommodate sale contracts by applying buyer's indifference principle.
3.5.3 — Fulfilment Centres ⇧ Index
📦 Fulfilment Centres — GST Legal Fiction Risk
📝 Fulfilment centres (third-party warehousing + value-added services) create real peril about which legal fiction applies — canvassing agent, fulfilment agent, or collection agent. Misconstruing the type of intermediary exposes taxpayers to wrong compliance obligations.
FULFILMENT AGENT
Schedule I para 3 — legal fiction applies — liable for compliance
Due to dissimilarities of stocks of different Suppliers at given location
NOT AFFECTED
Canvassing agent, collection agent, agents of stocks-in-common = para 3, Schedule I legal fiction does NOT affect them
WARNING Value-added services blur lines between canvassing agent and fulfilment agent. Legal fiction in para 3 Schedule I CGST does NOT have universal application beyond first principles. Revenue-neutral tax outflow does not justify applying legal fiction where it does not apply. Legal fiction can neither be glossed over when attracted nor applied when it does not apply.
3.6.1 — Not-a-Sale | Transfer, Not Sale ⇧ Index
🚫 Not-a-Sale — Transfer of Property ≠ Sale
📝 Not every transfer of property is a 'sale'. Sale requires the INTENTION to cause a sale — money consideration — AND all essential ingredients of a sale. If any essential ingredient is missing — transfer of property may still happen but it is NOT a sale.
TRANSFER — NOT SALE
Confiscation of offending articles
Forfeiture of pledged articles
Subrogation of insured articles on claim settlement
NON-MONETARY CONSIDERATION
Transfer for consideration expressly in non-monetary form = NOT sale
But IS a transfer for GST purposes, nevertheless
Accounting treatment = good indicator of Supplier's intention behind transaction
💡 Eg.238: Blankets given by Company to old-age home residents may be (i) sold with tax invoice + receivable written off, OR (ii) purchase price expensed in books of Company.
GOODS CONSUMED IN SERVICE — NOT SALE
Rashtriya Ispat Nigam Ltd. v. CTO 1990 (77) STC 182 (AP) — amount debited towards use of construction equipment belonging to Customer made available to Contractor = NOT lease — 'right to use' cannot be inhibited by place or purpose of further use. If amount debited were consideration for transfer of right to use, Contractor should have been free to take equipment and use it in any other project.
FOOD IN RESTAURANT — NOT SALE
Northern India Caterers (India) Ltd. v. LG Delhi AIR 1978 SC 1591 — supply of food in restaurant = service, not sale simplicitor. Items presented for consumption, not redistribution. Take-away: items of food presented for consumption but packed for take-away — suited for consumption but NOT for 'storage and redistribution'.
3.6.2 — Intention to Sell ⇧ Index
🎯 Intention to Sell — Cannot Be Substituted by GST Concepts
📝 The 'intention' to cause a sale must exist for a transaction to be a sale. Private motivations do not make up consideration. Intention to cause a sale must not be substituted with composite/mixed supply concepts in GST — those concepts determine classification, time, and place of supply — NOT the underlying form of supply.
HOW INTENTION IS EXPLAINED
Intention = immediate explanation of actions
Accounting treatment = reflection of Supplier's intention = good indicator
COMPOSITE/MIXED SUPPLY
Only determines: classification, time and place of supply, and other attendant treatment
Does NOT affect the underlying form of supply (as a sale)
NOT A SALE
Goods consumed in provision of some other service = NOT a contract for sale of goods
Private motivations ≠ consideration
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 3 | Sale of Goods Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Kone Elevators India (P.) Ltd. v. State of TN [2014] 45 taxmann.com 150 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | Applicable in all Works Contract / Composite Supply / Goods vs Service disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
Whether the object of contract was sale simplicitor or works contract was not categorically decided by the Apex Court but left to be determined based on terms of each contract and its stipulations. This decision shows great discernment in leaving room for play based on stipulations in each contract to guide the object of said contract and procure the tax treatment.
When to Use — When Department categorically classifies a supply as 'goods' or 'works contract' based on the label/title of the contract document. Cite Kone Elevators to establish that the Apex Court itself declined to make categorical rules — the object must be determined from the terms of each contract. Powerful against mechanical classification.
Rashtriya Ispat Nigam Ltd. v. CTO 1990 (77) STC 182 (AP) | Affirmed in State of AP v. Rashtriya Ispat Nigam Ltd. 2002 (126) STC 114
Andhra Pradesh High Court | Affirmed by Supreme Court | Applicable in construction equipment / machinery lease disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
Amount debited towards use of construction equipment belonging to Customer made available to Contractor for Customer's project was NOT a lease under the earlier tax regime because 'right to use' cannot be inhibited by place or purpose of use. If amount debited were consideration for transfer of right to use, Contractor should have been free to take the construction equipment and use it in Customer's project or any other project. Since restriction on 'place and purpose of further use' was applicable, amount debited could not satisfy 'test of transfer' of right — whether limited only to use or unlimited and absolute — to be liable to tax.
When to Use — When Department demands GST on amounts debited/charged for use of equipment/machinery made available by Customer for a specific project. Cite Rashtriya Ispat Nigam to argue: restriction on place and purpose of use means there is no 'transfer of right to use' — hence not a lease — GST on supply of goods not applicable.
Northern India Caterers (India) Ltd. v. LG Delhi AIR 1978 SC 1591
Supreme Court of India | Applicable in Food & Beverage / Restaurant / Hotel service GST disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
Supply of items of food in the context of consumption does not meet the exacting standards of Sale of Goods Act to be a sale simplicitor. Items presented for consumption and not in a manner suited for redistribution. Food supplied in a restaurant is a service and not sale simplicitor of goods. Where food is extended to the convenience of one's home (take-away), it is presented for consumption but packed suitably for take-away — not for storage and redistribution.
When to Use — (i) When disputing GST on restaurant food as 'sale of goods'. (ii) When distinguishing restaurant food from packaged food sold for redistribution to determine correct HSN and tax rate. (iii) Particularly useful to distinguish canteen supply from outside catering for ITC eligibility under Section 17(5).
Tata Consultancy Services Ltd. v. State of AP 2004 (178) ELT 22 (SC) / (2005) 1 SCC 308
Supreme Court of India — Constitution Bench | Applicable in Software / IPR / Intangible Goods disputes
Key Ratio / Principle (Variava J.)
Indian law does not make any distinction between tangible property and intangible property. A 'goods' may be a tangible property or an intangible one. It would become goods provided it has the attributes thereof having regard to (a) its utility; (b) capable of being bought and sold; and (c) capable of being transmitted, transferred, delivered, stored and possessed. If a software whether customized or non-customized satisfies these attributes, the same would be goods.
When to Use — (i) When arguing that software, IPR, brand names, or other intangibles are 'goods' for GST purposes. (ii) When distinguishing permanent alienation of IPR (goods) from temporary license (service treated as service under Schedule II). (iii) In disputes about e-commerce software products — whether supply is goods or services.
NM Goel and Co. v. STO 1990 taxmann.com 1352 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | Applicable in Construction Material Waste / Debiting of Material Costs disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
Amounts debited to Contractor's accounts must be treated as a sale by Customer to Contractor which appears to be supported by the fact that the contract scope and price was not sans these key material and Contractor was free to otherwise procure said key material of acceptable quality and decline to use material made available by Customer. The consideration was the 'valuable consideration 2' — freedom to reject material provided by Customer.
When to Use — In construction disputes where Customer debits Contractor for excess material usage and Department argues this constitutes a 'sale' attracting GST. Cite to show the conditions for deemed sale — Contractor must have been free to procure material independently. If Contractor had no choice but to use Customer's material, no sale occurred.
HAL v. State of Karnataka 1984 taxmann.com 905 (SC) | Rainbow Colour Lab v. State of MP 2000 SC | ACC Ltd. v. CC 2001 SC
Supreme Court of India — Evolution of Sale vs Works Contract Tests
Key Ratio — Three Tests Applied Over Time
(i) Principal Object Test (HAL v. State of Karnataka 1984) — Is the principal object goods or service? (ii) Dominant Intention Test (Rainbow Colour Lab 2000) — What was the dominant intention of the Parties? This was doubted in ACC Ltd. 2001. (iii) Factual Transfer Test (post 46th Constitutional Amendment via article 366(29A)) — Actual factual transfer of title determines the nature of the supply. It is key to locate the object of contract to identify the conclusion of that contract.
When to Use — When Department applies an outdated or incorrect test to characterize a supply. Show the evolution: dominant intention test is now doubted; the correct test post-46th Amendment is factual transfer. Limiting settled position in CC v. Suzlon Energy Ltd. (CA 11400-11401/2018) may need reconsideration — cite this to show the law is still evolving and categorical positions are unsafe.
Section 31(7) CGST Act — Sale-on-Approval | HSN Mismatch Defence
Statutory Provision | CGST Act 2017 | Applicable in wastage, goods sent on approval, and ECOM disputes
Key Principle
Demand for GST using HSN applicable to 'goods' assumes that form of supply in wastage of goods is 'sale'. Since goods are wasted, there is no momentary transfer before (excess) consumption — defence need only show it is not 'sale' for demand to fail. Limitation may run out before revised notice is issued with HSN applicable to 'services'. Section 31(7) permits delivery on-approval with 6-month cap on uncertainty — implied acceptance once goods damaged or resold.
When to Use — (i) When SCN demands GST on wastage of goods treating it as 'sale' with goods HSN — argue no momentary transfer, hence not sale — HSN should be for services if anything. Limitation argument is powerful: by the time Department issues corrected notice, limitation may have lapsed. (ii) In goods-on-approval disputes regarding time of supply under Section 31(7).
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 3b — Sale of Goods Act (Para 3.6–3.9)

⚖️ Chapter 3 – Sale of Goods Act | Para 3.6–3.9

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Sale
Prohibited / NOT a Sale
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
3.6.3 Barter — Not a Sale
3.6.4 Gift — Not a Sale
3.6.5 Sample Units — Not for Sale
3.6.6 Demo / Trial Units vs Sample
3.6.7 to 3.6.9 Defacement | Destructive Testing | Disposal
3.7.1 Deemed Sales — Legal Fiction
3.7.2 46th Amendment | Key Cases
3.7.3 101st Amendment — GST
3.8.1 Price | Section 64A | Indirect Tax Burden
3.8.3 to 3.8.5 Firm Price | GMP | Open Book Contracts
3.8.6 to 3.8.9 Transportation Cost | Security Deposit | Retention | Appropriation
3.8.10 to 3.8.12 Condition | Warranty | Nemo Dat
3.9 Supply Involving Services | Intermediary Test
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
3.6.3 — Barter, Not a Sale ⇧ Index
🔄 Barter — Non-Monetary Consideration That Is NOT a Sale
📝 A sale requires money consideration. Barter is when you exchange goods for goods (or goods for services). No money changes hands. Both are valid contracts — but they are treated differently in law and in GST. Barter ≠ sale.
BARTER ≠ SALE
Consideration in non-monetary form ≠ barter necessarily
Barter cannot involve immovable property (that would be 'exchange')
Consideration must be in money to be a sale
📝 If A gives rice to B and B gives wheat to A — that is barter. No money. Cannot be a sale. But GST still applies on each supply in the barter — both A and B are suppliers to each other.
💡 Eg.242: A gives 10 kg rice in return for 6 kg wheat by B = barter. Eg.243: A gives dinner every day to student in return for student maintaining accounts of hotelier = barter of goods for services.
BARTER vs CROSS-CANCELLING SUPPLY
If NOT cross-cancelling sales → it IS barter
Cancelling payment for each = NOT barter
Proof that monetary consideration was involved flows from assertions by Parties
📝 Cross-cancelling supply: A sells goods to B (GST invoice issued), B sells goods to A (GST invoice issued), they "net off" the payments. This looks like barter but is really two separate sales with settlement of consideration. GST applies on each supply separately.
STRUCTURE
Barter = ONE contract with ONE offer (by Promisor) and ONE acceptance (by Promisee)
Consideration payable by Promisee is 'in kind' (non-monetary)
Roles of Promisor and Promisee do NOT interchange
📝 In a barter, there is ONE agreement — not two. The price is just paid in goods instead of money. But if there are two separate contracts (each party selling to the other), it is cross-cancelling supply — not barter.
DISTINCT PERSONS
Legal fiction of distinct persons demands identification of existence of 'implied contracts' in the nature of supply without consideration OR supply in the form of barter
📝 Between HO and branch, even without money flowing, a supply may exist — either as a deemed supply (Schedule I) or as barter. Both attract GST. The label "no money transaction" does not escape GST.
3.6.4 — Gift, Not a Sale ⇧ Index
🎁 Gift — Agreement Without Consideration | Exception to Sale
📝 A gift is a transfer of property without consideration to the Donor. The intention is to transfer ownership, NOT to effect a sale. But in business, "gift" is a misnomer — if there is a business connection, it cannot be treated as a voluntary gift.
GIFT ≠ SALE
Gift = agreement but NOT contract (no consideration to Donor)
Intention to transfer property — NOT to effect a sale
Test: 'modality' of gifting must be examined
📝 Gift requires: (1) voluntary giving, (2) no compulsion on Donee, (3) no business connection between Donor and Donee. If any of these fail — it is not a gift.
EXCEPTION — §25 Contract Act
Equitable remedy available to Donee who incurs cost or loss on assurance by Donor who intended NOT to honour the commitment made
IN BUSINESS
Gift is a misnomer in business
If business connection exists between beneficiary of 'gift' and enterprise → NOT to be readily admitted as gift without further investigation
📝 Giving away goods to business associates or product users = NOT a gift. There is a proximate business nexus. The "gift" is in fact a transfer for non-monetary consideration — remains subject to GST valuation rules.
💡 Eg.244: Agricultural equipment distributed 'free of charge' to Agriculturists = NOT a gift — business connection exists. Eg.245: Medicines given 'free' to Physicians = NOT without consideration to Company. Eg.246: Buy-one-take-two = NOT gift — transfer of property tested for monetary consideration.
GST — GIFTS vs SAMPLES
For GST purposes: gifts or samples have special treatment
BUT ONLY whether they are 'disposed' — NOT on 'sale' or 'transfer' in course of business for consideration (albeit non-monetary form)
📝 GST Schedule I: gifts to employees above ₹50,000 per year are deemed supply. Medicaments to Physicians marked "not for sale" — tested for whether it is disposal (gift) or transfer (consideration in non-monetary form). The answer determines GST treatment.
💡 Eg.263–264: Packaged water 'not for sale, for consumption on Indian Railways' / Medicaments 'not for sale, Physicians samples' — must distinguish: are these gifts/disposals OR transfers with non-monetary consideration? Assertions under other allied laws about deductibility belie any gift claim.
3.6.5 — Sample Units vs 3.6.6 Demo / Trial Units — Key Distinctions ⇧ Index
🔬 Sample vs Demo vs Defaced — Critical Distinctions for GST
Feature Sample Units (3.6.5) Demo / Trial Units (3.6.6) Defaced Units (3.6.7)
Return expected? NO — not expected to be returned YES — expected to be returned; attracts demand for price if retained NO — defaced = unfit for sale; for-show only
Offered for sale? NO — not offered for sale YES — offered for sale-on-approval NO — rendered unfit-for-sale
Fit for sale? YES — fit-for-sale though not meant-for-sale YES — always fit-for-sale NO — impairs merchantable quality; cannot be object of sale contract
Quantity NOT in commercial quantities YES — presented in commercial quantities Below merchantable quality
Form of presentation NOT in same form as commercial units YES — same form as commercial units Modified/defaced form
Interchangeable with commercial units? NO YES — interchangeable NO
Carried as asset/stock? NO — not carried as assets YES — capitalized or accounted as revenue expenditure; disposed as 'pre-owned' at marked-down price N/A — defaced and unfit
Accounting Revenue expenditure Capitalized or revenue expenditure Revenue expenditure
GST Position Not a sale — distributed for anticipation of further sales. ITC rules apply on distribution. Can attract GST on sale-on-approval; when retained by customer, price is due NOT merchantable quality → cannot be object of sale. If sold as scrap — GST on scrap sale.
EXAMPLES
Sample: Eg.247: Shampoo sachet — fit-for-sale in quality but NOT capable-of-sale in quantity. Eg.248: Tester bottles marked 'sample' in non-standard quantities under Legal Metrology Act.
Demo: Eg.249: Cars with registration mark and 'demo car' sticker — NOT sample. Eg.250: Medicaments distributed to Physicians — fit-for-use but not-for-sale → demo or trial units (NOT samples).
Defaced: Eg.251: Shirts without buttons/collar cut — defaced for research. Eg.252: Mobile phone sent for software upgrade testing — back cover excluded. Eg.253: Defaced shirt sold as scrap for waste cloth / pillow fills. Eg.254: Expired medicines → cannot be sold, must be incinerated.
3.6.8 Destructive Testing | 3.6.9 Disposal — Not a Sale ⇧ Index
🧪 Destructive Testing & Disposal — Two More Non-Sale Categories
3.6.8 Destructive Testing
Testing may result in irreversible change, destruction or total consumption of goods
Goods used/consumed in destructive testing = service of testing — NOT goods for sale
📝 When a biscuit is tasted to test for quality parameters — the biscuit is consumed, not sold. The activity is a testing service. The biscuit is the input to the service, not the output of a sale.
TWO SITUATIONS
(A) Destructive testing in production process → No tax treatment for GST
(B) After completion of production → Credit reversal may be involved
ALWAYS
Destructive testing = location-dependent service → NOT zero-rated supplies in GST
Unless special dispensation under §13(13) of IGST Act
💡 Eg.255–256: Biscuits tasted from batch / Cigarettes, beverages tasted → No sale of goods. Eg.257: Ready-mix cement drawn for testing → already treated as sale; no abatement for quantity drawn for testing (testing is quality of works contract by Contractor). Eg.258: Cars taken for speed/crash testing → scrapped, sold as metal HSN 7204.
3.6.9 Disposal — Not a Sale
'Disposal' ≠ synonym for 'sale'
Disposal = articles that are unfit-for-sale
Seller looks at: avoidance of cost of retention
Buyer looks at: salvage value
📝 When a Seller is trying to get rid of something (end-of-life goods) and Buyer sees raw material value — that is disposal. There is no "sale" in the traditional sense because the goods are unfit for their original purpose.
TEST
Articles 'not for sale' does NOT render them 'unfit' for sale
IF articles are 'not for sale' but 'fit' AND given away → NOT disposal but 'transfer' (consideration in non-monetary form)
💡 Eg.259: Computers (end-of-life) sold to e-waste company with software defaced = disposal. Eg.260: Same computers given to Training Centre where use continues as computers = SALE (use continues as original purpose, even though fully depreciated). Eg.261–262: Shampoo sachets in magazines / Perfume testers = disposal (below merchantable quantity / exposure to utility only).
3.7.1 — Deemed Sales | Purpose of Legal Fiction ⇧ Index
🏛️ Deemed Sales — Legal Fiction Furnishing Missing Ingredients of Sale
📝 A "deemed sale" is not actually a sale under the Sale of Goods Act. Law creates a fiction — treating certain transactions AS IF they were sales, to bring them into the tax net. But the fiction only supplies the missing ingredient (e.g., "transfer of title") — all other ingredients of a sale must still exist independently.
WHY FICTION?
Instances where benefits of ownership were enjoyed, utility exploited BUT tax on sales bypassed due to innovative contract-writing → prompted legal fiction to overcome gaps and expose transactions to tax
MECHANISM
Sale is 'deemed' by legislative fiction:
(i) transfer NOT a sale but 'deemed' sale by fiction
(ii) fiction furnishes 'missing' ingredients to constitute a sale proper
📝 Fiction adds what is missing. But it does NOT excuse inquiry into other taxing ingredients. All other conditions for a sale must still be satisfied — fiction only covers the one missing piece.
LIMITS OF FICTION
Legal fiction cannot trespass other restrictions in law
Legal fiction can even be absurd and yet be lawful
Fiction must be limited in application only to cases to which it is to apply
💡 Eg.265: Sale of goods in high-seas CANNOT be deemed to be sale of goods in India (fiction cannot trespass territorial restriction). Eg.266: Sale of securities CAN be deemed to be exempt supply for purposes of reversal of common credit. Eg.267: Lease of goods can be deemed to be supply of service — but movement of goods under lease still requires e-waybill. Eg.268: Sale of food in course of any service can be deemed supply of service — but sale of food NOT for any service (redistribution) remains sale of goods.
3.7.2 — 46th Constitutional Amendment | Key Cases | Works Contract History ⇧ Index
📜 46th Constitutional Amendment — Furnishing Missing Ingredients for 6 Transaction Types
📝 Before the 46th Amendment (1982), many transactions involving goods — like works contracts, restaurant food, hire-purchase — could not be taxed as "sales" because they lacked one or more ingredients of a sale (e.g., transfer of title as movable goods). The 46th Amendment to Article 366(29A) fixed this by furnishing the "missing ingredients" for 6 specific transaction types.
6 Transaction Types Covered by Article 366(29A)
(i) Compulsory Acquisition
(ii) Works Contract
(iii) Hire-Purchase
(iv) Lease
(v) Transactions in goods involving mutuality
(vi) Food & Beverage consumed via a service in restaurant
CaseCitationKey Ruling
State of Madras v. Gannon Dunkerley (GD-1)AIR 1958 SC 560Definition of 'sale' in Sales Tax law = meaning in Sale of Goods Act. Economic exploitation of 'risks and rewards' not fitting statutory sale = not taxable as sale. No sales tax on food in restaurants / compulsory acquisition.
Builders' Association v. UOI (BAI-1)1989 SCALE (2) 76846th Amendment upheld. GD-2 examined value abatement and rate issues.
K Raheja Development Corporation v. State of Karnataka[2005] 2 STT 178 (SC)Development of apartments = Developer acting as contractor qua construction for apartment Buyers = works contract liable to tax.
L&T v. State of Karnataka[2013] 38 taxmann.com 98 (SC)Tripartite agreements also involve construction → works contracts liable to tax — but only from date of contract with Buyer, not for works completed previously.
Kone Elevators India v. State of TN[2014] 45 taxmann.com 150 (SC)After 46th Amendment, no distinction remains between sale simplicitor and works contract. Overruled earlier AP v. Kone Elevators decision.
NM Goel & Co. v. STO (NMG)AIR 1989 SC 285Extended incidence of tax to 'free supplies' — but in GST context, debiting contractor's account does not travel beyond terms of (i) privity (ii) object of contract (iii) consideration.
CCE v. Dai Ichi Karkaria Ltd.1999 (112) ELT 353 (SC)Cost of goods sold reduces when Modvat credit availed (profits increase); increases when inadmissible Modvat credit becomes admissible (profits decrease). Basis for ITC impact on pricing.
⚠️ IMPORTANT FOR GST
Treatment flowing from 46th Amendment does NOT pollinate understanding of three GST categories (Schedule II / Schedule III / composite vs mixed supply). Authority to levy GST flows from Article 246A — separate from Article 366(29A). Cross-pollination is NOT permitted in law. They operate in different constitutional domains.
📝 Just because something was a "deemed sale" under old Sales Tax law does NOT automatically decide its GST treatment. GST has its own separate constitutional foundation (Article 246A + 101st Amendment). Do not import 46th Amendment jurisprudence into GST interpretation.
3.7.3 — 101st Constitutional Amendment | GST Constitutional Framework ⇧ Index
🏛️ 101st Amendment — GST Constitutional Framework | 3 Categories of Supply
📝 The 101st Amendment (2016) introduced GST by amending the Constitution. It created brand new taxing powers under Article 246A — completely separate from existing Article 246 / 366(29A) powers. GST law operates on its OWN constitutional foundation. The 46th Amendment is an "aid to construction" of old taxing entries — NOT a guide to GST entries.
Three GST Treatment Categories for Transactions Involving Supply of Goods or Services
CATEGORY I — Schedule II
Transactions involving goods declared to be subject to treatment as supply of service or vice versa
📝 Schedule II: The law says "this is supply of service" (even though it looks like goods supply, e.g., works contract, restaurant food). Or vice versa. You follow Schedule II — period.
CATEGORY II — Composite/Mixed
Supply tantamount to supply of service or goods based on terms of supply → composite supply or mixed supply
📝 Composite supply: A principal supply + naturally bundled ancillary supplies. The whole is treated as the principal supply. Mixed supply: Independent items combined for a single price — treated as whichever attracts highest GST rate.
CATEGORY III — Schedule III
Transactions omitted from being admitted as supply at all when listed in Schedule III (neither goods nor services)
📝 Schedule III: "Not a supply at all." Sale of land, actionable claims (other than lottery etc.), services by employee to employer — these are outside GST entirely. Not even a supply to be taxed.
CRITICAL
Article 366(29A) = aid to construction of taxing entries in legislations traceable to Article 246
Article 246A = brand new taxing powers for GST
These two cannot cross-pollinate. GST law has its OWN constitutional independence.
3.8.1 Price | 3.8.2 Section 64A | Indirect Tax Burden | Chotabhai Jethabhai ⇧ Index
💰 Price & Valuable Consideration | Section 64A | Who Bears the Tax?
3.8.1 Price
§2(10) SGA
"Price" = money consideration for sale of goods
Price in cash or permitted negotiable instruments (NOT in kind — that is barter)
§15(1) CGST
'Sole consideration' is disqualification of price — if there is any consideration apart from or extraneous to price → price disregarded → §15(4) valuation rules apply
📝 If the price is the ONLY thing the Buyer gives — §15(1) applies and price = taxable value. But if Buyer also gives something else (land, goods, services, time of payment terms) — that "extra" disqualifies the price, and GST rules determine value.
3.8.2 Section 64A — Tax Escalation Clause
§64A(1)(a)
If tax imposed/increased AFTER contract is made → Seller may add to contract price the amount equivalent to such tax or increase → entitled to be paid and to sue and recover
§64A(1)(b)
If tax decreased/remitted AFTER contract → Buyer may deduct from contract price → NOT liable to pay or be sued for such deduction
LIMITATION
§64A applies only to: (a) Customs/excise on goods (b) Tax on sale/purchase of goods
NO amendment to cover services — not in 2012, not in 2017 (GST introduction)
§64A NOT applicable to GST on services
📝 §64A was designed for goods taxes in pre-GST era. Parliament never extended it to services. Using §64A to claim GST variation on service contracts is legally unsound — it has no application.
💡 Eg.269: Tractors exempted from Central Excise (duty = 8709) → Selling Price increased → Govt reintroduced duty at 0.25% to ease cost pressures → credit accumulation ensued. Eg.270: Where rate inversion not feasible → refund mechanism under Rule 89(5) CGST Rules.
Who Bears Indirect Tax?
Chotabhai Jethabhai Patel Sons v. UOI AIR 1962 SC 1006
"Direct tax: demanded from the persons who (it is interested or desired) should pay it. Indirect taxes are those which are demanded from one person in the expectation and intention that he shall indemnify himself at the expense of another." — John Stuart Mill
Apex Court held: This is NOT a statutory definition. Tax is that levied by statute based on precise 'Taxable Event' on the Payer named in the statute. Right to recoup from Buyer is a contractual remedy — NOT statutory right
📝 GST is legally the Seller's liability (Payer named in statute). The Seller's right to collect GST from Buyer is a contractual right — based on the contract terms. If the contract is silent on "taxes," Seller cannot demand reimbursement from Buyer using §64A (which covers goods taxes, not services).
3.8.3 Firm Price | 3.8.4 GMP Contracts | 3.8.5 Open Book Pricing ⇧ Index
📋 Types of Price Arrangements in Contracts
3.8.3 Firm Price Contracts
Price variations allowed in long-duration contracts for:
(a) WPI inflation (b) Volatile inputs (cement, steel, oil, freight) (c) Changes in taxes on supply (d) Any others
FIRM PRICE = NO ESCALATION
Seller liable to bear changes in cost including taxes and statutory levies
NOT even §64A can rescue Promisor in firm price contracts
GST (new tax not in existence at time of contract) → CANNOT be claimed as adjustment
📝 If the contract says "firm price" — the Seller absorbs ALL cost changes including new taxes like GST. The Buyer's argument: "You accepted the risk when you agreed to firm price." Courts have entertained force majeure-like arguments but there is no universal rule.
💡 GST introduced after contract was signed at 'firm price' → Seller CANNOT demand GST from Buyer as "new tax" adjustment. The risk of new taxes was accepted with the firm price. Consensus ad idem touched.
3.8.4 GMP (Guaranteed Maximum Price) Contracts
Costs incurred in respect of inward supplies from various vendors → complete transparency to Buyer
Sale by Seller to Buyer for consideration limited to maximum price guaranteed
Transparency of cost does NOT imply direct transfer of title from vendors to Buyer
Title: Vendor → Seller (first transfer) → Buyer (second transfer)
Two separate titles = two separate GST supplies
📝 Even in a GMP contract where Buyer sees all Seller's invoices and costs — title still passes twice. The Seller buys from vendors, then sells to Buyer. Two supplies = two GST events. Seller is not merely a "coordinator" — Seller holds title between the two transfers.
💡 Plurality of transfers (title passes by accession in works contracts) was rejected by SC in State of AP v. L&T Ltd. (2008) 9 SCC 191 — confirmed in L&T v. Dy CCT (CA) 2956/2007. Multiple title transfers are supported by express legislative intendment in GST statutory provisions.
3.8.5 Open Book Pricing
Full transparency assured by Seller to Buyer as to actual costs incurred + agreed coordination fee = 'contract price'
Object: Seller not prejudiced by uncertain cost of components required to fulfil requirement of Buyer on single-point responsibility
📝 "Open book" means: I'll show you all my bills + add a coordination fee. Whatever it costs, that's the contract price. Used for complex projects where Buyer wants cost certainty but Seller cannot predict input costs.
CAUTION
Object of contract must NOT be misconstrued as individual components — if contract is for 'plant' and pricing is 'open book', object remains sale of 'plant', NOT of 'components'
CCE v. Shirke Construction Equipment 1997 (95) ELT 644: "What was being transported was not parts of crane but crane in parts"
💡 Eg.272: Cement (HSN 2523, 28%) delivered to site as 'secured advance' → billed as HSN 9954 Construction Services comprising cement delivered to site → GST at 9954 rate, NOT 2523. Eg.273: Batteries for generator → invoiced as HSN 8501 supply of generator (batteries as part supplies for installation) → GST at 8501 rate (18%), NOT 8507 (28%).
3.8.6 Transportation Cost | 3.8.7 Security Deposit | 3.8.8 Retention | 3.8.9 Appropriation of Payment ⇧ Index
💳 Special Payment Arrangements — GST Treatment
3.8.6 Transportation Cost = Price
When Seller bears obligation of disposal of scrap/debris → Seller avoids incurring cost by permitting Buyer to bear those costs
Buyer's cost incurred = Price of scrap/debris (even though Seller collects nothing)
📝 If Buyer agrees to clear and transport debris that Seller would otherwise have to clear — Buyer is effectively paying the "price" by incurring those costs. Obligations of Seller met by Buyer = consideration. 'Transportation charges' is a misnomer — it is the price of the goods.
💡 Eg.274: Tenant (Seller) offers to sell interior works 'as is' to Demolishing Contractor (Buyer) for ₹1. Buyer incurs ₹5,00,000 for demolishing and clearing (costs Seller ought to have incurred). Seller liable to discharge GST as composite/mixed supply. 'Transportation charges' paid by Buyer = price of goods sold.
3.8.7 Security Deposit
RULE
Consideration does NOT include deposits paid to secure performance of obligations
Security deposit ≠ payment for supply
📝 A security deposit is held by Supplier as security — not as payment. Until it is "applied as consideration" (per §2(31) CGST Act), it is NOT taxable. The moment Supplier applies it against dues — it becomes consideration and attracts GST.
§2(31) CGST
Deposit shall not be considered payment for supply UNLESS supplier applies such deposit as consideration for the said supply
Key word: "as" consideration (not "towards" consideration)
💡 Eg.280: Rent overdue, lease authorises Landlord to adjust deposit — but additional Covid time given → deposit remains deposit, NOT consideration. Eg.281: Tenant informs intent to vacate + authorises adjustment during notice period → deposit extent adjusted = becomes rent collected in advance → GST applicable. Eg.282–284: Covid rent — tenancy subsisting = GST on accrued rent; tenancy terminated = no GST; 50% discount = waiver = non-payment, NOT discount.
3.8.8 Retention Money
Retention of some portion of payment due to secure Buyer's interest in event of defects in quality of goods sold
Retention ≠ Unpaid creditor
Retention admits: (i) completion of sale (ii) settlement of full extent of payment due (iii) collection of security deposit
Accounting: Trade Creditors Dr. 100 / To Security Deposit Cr. 100
📝 Retention money is NOT unpaid consideration — it is a converted security deposit. The sale is complete. GST has already accrued on full contract price. The retained amount is just a security held by Buyer until defect liability period ends.
3.8.9 Appropriation of Payment
§59 ICA
Decision to appropriate payment against specific invoices = right of Buyer
Seller CANNOT apply payment received based on oldest invoices unless Buyer has issued specific instructions
📝 If you owe money on 5 different invoices, YOU (the Buyer) decide which invoice to pay off first. The Seller cannot unilaterally apply your payment to the oldest invoice. This matters for GST — which invoice is "paid" determines whether time-of-supply rules are satisfied.
§60 ICA
Where Buyer omitted to intimate and no circumstances indicating which debt to discharge → creditor may apply at his discretion to any lawful debt actually due — whether recovery barred by limitation or not
3.8.10 Condition & Warranty | 3.8.11 Warranty & Breach | 3.8.12 Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet ⇧ Index
📋 Condition vs Warranty vs Nemo Dat — Rights in Sale Contracts
3.8.10 Condition vs Contingent Contract
§12 SGA
Condition = stipulation essential to main purpose → breach gives right to treat contract as repudiated
Warranty = stipulation collateral to main purpose → breach gives claim for damages only (NOT right to reject goods)
📝 Condition: If the seller breaches it, the buyer can cancel the whole contract and return the goods. Warranty: If breached, buyer can only claim compensation — cannot reject the goods. The difference is huge in practice.
CAUTION
Whether stipulation is condition or warranty depends on construction of contract — not on what it is called
A stipulation may be a condition though called a warranty in the contract
CONDITIONAL SALE ≠ CONTINGENT CONTRACT
Sale is complete with title transferred even if conditions attached
BUT: if contingency attached to contract of sale → sale cannot be complete, title retained
📝 Pre-condition (condition precedent) prevents the contract from being born. Post-condition (condition subsequent) may restore title after it passed. The GST "time of supply" depends on when title actually passes — which depends on whether condition is precedent or subsequent.
3.8.11 Warranty and Breach
Merchantability = condition (not warranty) — determined by custom of trade
Warranty extends to: title of Seller, several other aspects
Warranty = obligation attendant to sale
CAN BE
Warranty can be 'contracted out' by Seller (excluded by express agreement)
CANNOT BE
Warranty CANNOT be 'contracted into' by third-Party
WARRANTY FULFILMENT — GST
OEM warranty replacement of parts → No GST applies since price of OE already includes potential cost of warranty replacements
BUT: GST payable on replacement parts used by fulfilment intermediary (no obligation to Customer + 'bill to ship' transaction)
💡 Eg.285: OEM warranty obligation lies with OEM — fulfilled via Dealer intermediary. No additional consideration to OEM (included in OE price). But Dealer using tax-paid parts for replacement = appears to be sale of those parts to OEM. Eg.286: Customer modifications void OEM warranty. Eg.287: Extended warranty by Dealer = maintenance contract (not warranty) — starts after OEM warranty period ends.
3.8.12 Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet
"He who does not have cannot give" — refers to capacity of Seller to confer valid title to Buyer unless Seller first secured valid title (condition precedent)
📝 You cannot sell what you do not own. If a Seller does not have good title to goods, any purported sale is non est — the "sale" never happened. Buyer is not obliged to verify Seller's title — that is Seller's condition to contract, not a warranty.
If Seller without title → sale concluded is non est → goods delivered are liable to be recovered and restored
GST discharged on such void sale = wrong tax paid
💡 Eg.288: Job-worker cannot sell scrap if Customer did not authorize transfer of title in residual scrap from raw material during job-work conversion. Eg.289: Insurer has authority to sell wreck (subrogation). Eg.290: Developer CANNOT sell Landowner's share of constructed units even if GST discharged — Landowner is liable for GST. Eg.291: Co-owners must each pay GST on their share of rent — one co-owner cannot discharge for all.
3.9 — Supply Involving Services | Lease | Barter | Sale of Services | Intermediary Test ⇧ Index
🔧 Supply Involving Services — Lease, Barter, Sale & Intermediary Authority Test
3.9.1 Lease of Goods
DEEMED SERVICE
No actual service in leasing — but treated as supply of services in §1(b) and §5(f) of Schedule II to CGST Act (limited purpose of preserving 'place of supply' and instantaneous return/re-lease rules)
📝 Leasing goods is economically more like a sale (you have the goods, you use them). But law treats it as service — so that "place of supply" rules (location of service provider) apply, and so that each monthly lease period is a fresh supply.
💡 Eg.292: Lease of equipment for 1 year = 12 agreements with identical terms. GST payable on EACH monthly lease rental — NOT on aggregate rental over entire tenure. Each month = fresh supply = separate GST event.
3.9.2 Barter with Services
Services can form good and sufficient consideration in a barter — whether involving other goods or other services
Care must be taken that it is NOT interchanged with:
(i) Cross-cancelling supply + settlement of consideration
(ii) Private motivations treated as consideration for supplies actually made
📝 If A provides healthcare to B and B provides use of computers to A — this could be barter of services for services. But if A and B both invoice each other and net off payments — it is two separate taxable supplies (cross-cancelling), not barter. The structure and intent determine the answer.
3.9.3 'Sale of Services' — Not Possible
Expression 'sale of services' = incorrect
Permitting enjoyment of benefits from an executed contract = assignment — not resale of services
Service is NOT the contract — it is the object of a contract that is NOT goods
📝 You cannot "resell" a service the way you resell goods. If you buy a hotel room and let someone else use it — you have "assigned" your benefit, not "resold" the service. The hotel still provides the service. The hotel is the Supplier — not you.
💡 Eg.293: Lease of building fully-paid by A, B permitted to occupy on A's order — NOT resale of leasing services (A has no authority to sub-lease). Eg.294: Hotel accommodation fully paid, guests P and Q — NOT resale of accommodation services. Eg.295: Reinsurance = NOT another contract without Insured's concurrence — Insurer settles full claim then reclaims from Reinsurer.
🔍 Four-Factor Intermediary Authority Test for Service Resellers
📝 For an intermediary to be treated as independently supplying a service (and use the same HSN as the actual service provider), they must satisfy ALL FOUR conditions. If any one is missing, they are merely a facilitator — and can only charge commission under intermediary HSN.
①
Lawful Authority
Legal right to supply this specific service
②
Physical Capability
Operational capacity to actually perform
③
Contractual Obligation
Legally bound to deliver the service to Customer
④
Vicarious Liability
Liable for negligence, misconduct, deficient quality of the service
Intermediary OfferingLawful AuthorityPhysical CapabilityContractual ObligationVicarious LiabilityCan Resell?
Hotel stayNONONONONO — intermediary only
Travel ticketNONONONONO — intermediary only
Health careNONONONONO — cannot claim HSN 9993
AdvertisementNONOYESNOPARTIAL — can resell space (goods); commission on services
SIM cardNONOYESNONO
Chit fundNONONONONO
Pre-paid cardNONONONONO
Motor carNOYESYESNOLIMITED — physical capability allows some contractual obligation
TV etc.NOYESYESNOLIMITED
LPG cylinderNONONONONO
LoansNONONONONO
InsuranceNONONONONO
CONCLUSION
Where (i) lawful authority AND (ii) operational capability form condition to the contract → resale NOT permissible → intermediary acts as instrumentality for underlying services → CANNOT assert independent supplies of these services → must operate under intermediary HSN (commission only).

All differentiating factors located ONLY within Explanatory Notes to classification of services in GST — NOT from first principles of Sale of Goods Act.
💡 Eg.296: IATA Agent reselling flight tickets at marked-up price → tour package remains service to be supplied by Airline, NOT by Agent. Eg.297: ECOM reselling hotel room-nights → ECOM is intermediary (identity of property is of essence in contract; ECOM asserts no ownership of property). ECOM cannot use HSN 9963 for hotel accommodation. Eg.299: Health-tech company aggregating physicians → cannot use HSN 9993 (no lawful authority, no vicarious liability). Eg.300: Advertisement agency reselling newspaper space → can act as reseller of space (goods) with full GST + ITC, OR as intermediary discharging GST only on commission.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 3 | Sale of Goods Act | Para 3.6–3.9 ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
