State of Madras v. Gannon Dunkerley & Co. (Madras) Ltd. (GD-1)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1958 SC 560 | Para 3.7.2 | Foundational Deemed Sale Case
Key Ratio
The definition of 'sale' in Sales Tax legislation must be interpreted within the meaning of Sale of Goods Act. Economic exploitation of 'risks and rewards' attendant to ownership that does not fit the statutory definition of sale was held not liable to sales tax. No sales tax was validly leviable on food served in restaurants (Associated Hotels, Northern India Caterers) and compulsory acquisition (New India Sugar, ONGC, Vishnu Agencies).
When to Use — When challenging Revenue's attempt to impose GST by characterising a transaction as "deemed sale" based on economic substance alone. Cite to argue: the legal definition controls, not economic exploitation of benefits. The 46th Amendment exists precisely because this case denied such broad characterisation — and GST has its own separate constitutional foundation under 101st Amendment.
Kone Elevators India (P) Ltd. v. State of TN
Supreme Court | [2014] 45 taxmann.com 150 | Para 3.7.2 | Works Contract
Key Ratio
After the 46th Constitutional Amendment, no distinction remained between sale simplicitor and works contract. Any contract for supply and erection of goods which involves transfer of property in goods is a works contract subject to tax on the goods component.
When to Use — In disputes about whether a supply + installation contract is a works contract or a sale simplicitor for GST classification. Cite to establish that post-46th Amendment (and now under GST Schedule II Entry 6), the distinction is settled — composite supply of works contract is treated as supply of services.
Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet — Capacity to Transfer Title
Common Law Maxim | Para 3.8.12 | Sale of Goods Act Section 14 | Applicable universally in title disputes
Key Principle
He who does not have cannot give. This refers to the capacity of the Seller to confer valid title to the Buyer unless the Seller first secured valid title — which is a condition precedent. If Seller is without title, the sale concluded is non est and goods delivered are liable to be recovered and restored. Developer cannot sell units falling to Landowner's share in a joint development project — Landowner is the proper person liable to discharge GST on such supply.
When to Use — (i) When Developer discharges GST on Landowner's share of flats — argue Developer cannot supply what Landowner owns; Landowner must pay GST. (ii) When job-worker purports to sell residual scrap from customer's raw material — without express title transfer authorization from Customer, job-worker has no title to scrap = cannot supply = GST on scrap may not arise from job-worker.
Section 64A — Sale of Goods Act | Tax Escalation Limited to Goods Taxes Only
Sale of Goods Act Section 64A | Para 3.8.2 | Applicable in price variation disputes under GST
Key Principle
Section 64A of the Sale of Goods Act deals with amount of increased or decreased taxes to be added or deducted in contracts of sale. The provision applies only to: (a) any duty of customs or excise on goods and (b) any tax on the sale or purchase of goods. No amendment has been made in section 64A to cover services — not even in 2012 when sweeping changes were made to tax on services, and not in 2017 when GST was introduced. This principle is pressed forward all too often when relief is prayed in suits for variation in contract price due to legislative action.
When to Use — When Seller of services demands GST from Buyer as "additional payment" citing §64A. Counter: §64A has never been extended to cover service taxes or GST on services. No statutory basis exists to claim GST as an addition to contract price under §64A for service contracts. The right to collect GST from Buyer is purely contractual — dependent on the terms of the contract.
Chotabhai Jethabhai Patel Sons v. UOI | AIR 1962 SC 1006
Supreme Court of India | 1962 | Para 3.8.1 | Who Bears Indirect Tax
Key Ratio
John Stuart Mill's definition of indirect tax — as one demanded from one person in expectation that he shall indemnify himself at the expense of another — is NOT a statutory definition. Tax is that which is levied by statute based on occurrence of a precise Taxable Event and Legislature is at liberty to foist this burden on any Payer it chooses, subject only to some nexus of Payer with Taxable Event. Right to recoup the incidence from Buyer, where Payer is Seller, is not a statutory right but a contractual remedy — Payer (Seller) has no statutory right to demand reimbursement by Buyer, not even when there is a presumption against Seller in section 49(9) of Central GST Act, if their contract were to be silent on 'taxes'.
When to Use — When Seller demands GST from Buyer claiming "GST is Buyer's burden as it is an indirect tax." Counter: GST is legally the Seller's statutory liability. Seller's right to collect from Buyer is purely contractual. If contract is silent on taxes, Seller cannot demand GST from Buyer as a legal right — only as a contractual claim (if any).
CCE v. Shirke Construction Equipment (P) Ltd. | 1997 (95) ELT 644 (Tri. - Pune)
CESTAT Pune | 1997 | Para 3.8.5 | Equipment in Parts = Whole Equipment
Key Ratio
What was being transported was not parts of crane but crane in parts. When components of a plant/equipment are delivered over a short interval of time by caravan of trucks for installation and commission, the supply is of the whole plant/equipment — not of individual components. This principle continues in Rule 55(5) of Central GST Rules.
When to Use — When Department seeks to tax plant/machinery components at higher rate (e.g., 28%) by treating each component separately, when the contract is for supply of the whole plant (e.g., 18%). Cite to argue: delivery in parts ≠ supply of parts; supply is of the whole plant at the applicable rate. But this rule applies only where delivery is over a short interval — not where components are delivered over extended periods (then equipment-in-parts billing must be used).
Section 2(31) CGST Act — Deposit vs Consideration | "Applied As" Consideration
CGST Act 2017 | Section 2(31) | Para 3.8.7 | Applicable in all security deposit / advance payment disputes
Key Principle
A deposit given in respect of the supply of goods or services or both shall not be considered as payment made for such supply unless the supplier applies such deposit as consideration for the said supply. The Legislature has used the expression "as consideration" instead of "towards consideration" — by referred to the appropriation of deposit "as" consideration based on the terms of contract. Referring to "towards" consideration would be at the volition of Supplier. The distinction is crucial: deposit remains deposit until the contract terms themselves trigger its application as consideration.
When to Use — (i) When Department seeks GST on security deposit received from tenant/customer at time of receipt. Counter: §2(31) explicitly excludes deposits from consideration — GST arises only when applied as consideration per contract terms. (ii) When Landlord retains deposit despite Covid waiver — cite Eg.280: deposit remains deposit and does NOT become consideration merely because contract authorises adjustment.
Four-Factor Intermediary Authority Test — Lawful Authority + Physical Capability + Contractual Obligation + Vicarious Liability
Para 3.9.3 | Sale of Goods Act Allied to GST | Applicable in all intermediary / reseller / ECOM disputes
Key Principle
Where lawful authority and operational capability form conditions to the contract, resales are not permissible and intermediary acting as instrumentality for underlying services to be performed by one with this authority and that capability cannot assert independent supplies of these services. Service is not the contract — it is the object of a contract that is not goods. The expression 'sale of services' is incorrect because permitting enjoyment of benefits to arise from an executed contract is 'assignment' and not resale of services. Lawful authority to provide actual services can be via the instrumentality of a third-party provider — but this requires services to become 'actionable claim' and be resold — and actionable claims in GST are 'goods', not services.
When to Use — (i) Against ECOM operators claiming HSN 9963 for hotel accommodation resales (cite: ECOM has no lawful authority, physical capability, contractual obligation or vicarious liability). (ii) Against health-tech aggregators claiming healthcare exemption (HSN 9993). (iii) Against travel agents claiming to be airlines. (iv) Against insurance intermediaries claiming to be insurers. In each case, check the 4-factor test — all four must be satisfied for independent supply characterisation.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 4 — Partnership Act

Chapter 4 — Partnership Act

Para 4.2 · Peculiarities of Partnership | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📋 Table of Contents

4.1 Relevance to GST 4.2.1 Firm not an Entity 4.2.2 Mutuality 4.2.3 AOP 4.2.4 Pooling of Resources 1 4.2.5 Remuneration 4.2.6 Pooling of Resources 2 4.2.7 Diversion by Overriding Charge 4.2.8 Conversion ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal

4.1 · Relevance to GST

⬆ Index
Partnership Act & GST — Relevance
Firm = Partners
KEY
Firm is a collective noun for all Partners
Transactions inter se
Partner ↔ Partner
or Partner ↔ Firm
IF
Not easy to locate when
supply arises
Two Transaction Types
TYPE 1
Independent of Firm
TYPE 2
As instrumentality of Firm
Governing Law
RULE
Partnership Act 1932 —
Strict Construction

4.2.1 · Firm not a Legal Entity

⬆ Index
Firm — Legal Character
Not a Legal Entity
PRINCIPLE
Being a collective noun,
Firm has no independent
legal personality
Transactions with Firm =
multiple individual
transactions with each Partner
Legal Fiction Exception
FOR TAX
Treated as a 'person'
under Income Tax, GST
and other laws
NOTE
Does NOT alter
non-entity status
under Partnership Act
Section 4 — Definition
Partnership = relation between persons
agreeing to share profits of business
carried on by all/any acting for all
Partners = individually
Firm = collectively
Firm Name = business name
Origin of Firm
KEY
Inter se arrangement
of Partners brings
Firm into existence
CANNOT
Firm does NOT exist
at decision-making stage
(before Partners are Partners)
Who Can Be Partner?
WHO
A Company can be
a Partner
CANNOT
A Firm cannot be
a Partner in another Firm
Partners in one Firm
CAN be Partners
in another Firm
Firm Property
Held in Firm name but
legally belongs fractionally
to each Partner
RULE
Distributed by operation
of law — not by
Partners' consent
CANNOT
Firm cannot be shareholder
in limited company —
needs Partners as instruments

4.2.2 · Mutuality

⬆ Index
Mutuality Principle
Core Rule
MAXIM
"No one can profit
out of oneself"
Where persons are 'one',
inter se transactions
are ignored
Association Examples
Eg 301: Members' contributions
to Association = receipts,
NOT income
Eg 302: Interest earned
by Association from
investing contributions = income
Vanishing Point of Mutuality
WHEN
Partner steps outside
her capacity as Partner
and conducts business with Firm
Eg 303: Partner supplies
raw material to Firm
Eg 304: Partner leases
personal property
on rent to Firm
When Mutuality Applies
RULE
Mutuality applies ONLY
to transactions where
Partners act in their
capacity as Partners

4.2.3 · AOP vs Firm

⬆ Index
Association of Persons (AOP) vs Firm
AOP ≠ Firm
KEY DISTINCTION
Firm must have a Deed
AOP is created by contract,
not by status
Referring to someone
as 'Partner' does NOT
imply existence of Firm
RULE
AoP is explicitly NOT
a Firm
GST Override on Mutuality
AMENDMENT
Section 7(1)(aa) inserted
w.e.f. 1st July 2017
(notified 1st Jan 2022)
Activities/transactions by person
(other than individual) to its
members/constituents or vice versa
for cash, deferred payment
or other valuable consideration
Explanation: Person and its
members/constituents = TWO
separate persons; inter se supply
deemed from one to another
Court Challenge & Scope Limit
PENDING
Whether legal fiction
passes muster in Court
remains to be seen
LIMITATION
Fiction limited to
monetary consideration only:
(i) Cash (ii) Credit
(iii) Negotiable instruments
'Words of rank' rule of
construction: does NOT
cover non-monetary
valuable consideration

4.2.4 · Pooling of Resources 1

⬆ Index
Pooling of Resources — Partner & Firm
Core Rule — No Remuneration
PRINCIPLE
No remuneration can be
demanded for taking part
in business of Firm
Partners pool resources,
share profits subject to
exclusions/restrictions in Deed
All Payments = Distribution of Profits
Eg 305: Salary to working
partner = distribution
of profits
Eg 306: Commission
to partner = distribution
of profits
Eg 307: Interest on capital
= distribution
of profits
Partner as Independent Supplier
WHEN
Partner steps outside
relationship as Partner
Contract = Purchase Order
or commercial contract
(NOT the Deed)
Partner acts as
independent Supplier,
not in capacity of Partner
Accounting entry:
Purchases Dr. 100
To Creditors (Partner) Cr. 100
Creditor also a Partner
KEY
Fact that Creditor is also
Partner is NOT relevant
and does NOT attract mutuality
TEST
Examine the capacity
in which Partner is
transacting with Firm

4.2.5 · Remuneration

⬆ Index
Remuneration — GST & Income Tax Treatment
No Right to Remuneration
RULE
Partner not entitled to
remuneration for participating
in Firm's business
Tax Treatment of Remuneration
KEY
Tax treatment does NOT
follow nomen juris
(name assigned)
By whatever name called —
distribution to Partners
is always 'sharing profits'
All modes = distribution
of profits; no supply
as long as Partner
acts in capacity of Partner
Income Tax — Pre-tax Deduction
IT Act permits remuneration
under specific heads +
pre-tax deduction
NOTE
Only for limited purposes
of availing IT treatment;
still distribution of profits
Source vs Application
CRITICAL DISTINCTION
'Source' of income ≠ 'Use' of income
Distribution of profits
= application of income
RESULT
Distribution of profits
does not arise from supply
SC — CIT Bihar v. Ramniklal Kothari
SC held: business of firm = business of partner; profits of firm = profits of partners; expenditure by partners to earn such shares = admissible deduction [1969] 74 ITR 57 SC
CIT v. RM Chidambaram Pillai
Lindley on Partnership: Firm has no legal recognition; law looks to partners; property of firm = their property; debts of firm = their debts. A partner cannot be debtor or creditor of his own firm. (1977) 1 SCC 431

4.2.6 · Pooling of Resources 2

⬆ Index
Asset Contribution by Partner — GST Implications
Asset Brought into Firm
Partner's capital account
credited with money value
of asset contributed
Accounting entry:
Asset Dr. 100
To Partner's Capital Cr. 100
Capital Credit = Two-fold Transaction
IF
Capital account is credited,
it implies two-fold
commercial arrangement
LEG 1
Asset Dr. 100
To Creditors Cr. 100
Supply of asset → GST liable
LEG 2
Creditors Dr. 100
To Partner's Capital Cr. 100
Liability taken over → NOT GST liable
Best Practice for Deed
ADVICE
Capital contribution best
left in Deed — NOT as
payable subsumed into capital
Immovable Property Transfer without Sale Deed
Common structure used:
(i) Contribute property to Firm
(ii) Credit money value to capital
(iii) Induct new Partner at valuation
(iv) Exit or dilute existing Partner
RISK
Absence of Sale Deed
attracts scrutiny —
raison d'etre questioned
(Suraj Lamp case AIR 2012 SC 206)

4.2.7 · Diversion by Overriding Charge

⬆ Index
Diversion by Overriding Charge
Mechanism
Deed may provide for
'source' of income to be
diverted by overriding charge
NATURE
Operates as an
encumbrance on the source
itself — not on the income
Effect on Income
Income is reduced
to extent of this charge
Only net income
belongs to Firm
Example
Eg 308: Admitted share of
Firm's revenue to be paid
towards goodwill to spouse
of deceased Partner

4.2.8 · Conversion

⬆ Index
Conversion of Firm to LLP / Company — GST Treatment
GST Applies Regardless of Concessions
RULE
Concessions allowed on
'conversion' do NOT
override GST treatment
TRIGGER
Where PAN changes,
GST treatment applies
Two GST Treatment Paths
PATH 1
Transfer as
going concern
PATH 2
Formation of new
distinct person after
closure of existing
Unpaid Liability after Conversion
NOTE
With unpaid liability
determined after
conversion date —
liability is joint and several
GST makes 'much ado
about nothing' — demands
tax treatment under
respective GSTIN of
transferor and transferee

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 4

⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name and citation directly before the Adjudicating Authority or Appellate Forum.
CIT, Bihar v. Ramniklal Kothari
Supreme Court of India | 1969 | [1969] 74 ITR 57 SC
"The business of the firm was the business of the partner and the profits of the firm were the profits of the partners, and the expenditure incurred by partners in earning such shares was admissible for deduction in arriving at the total income."
When to Use: When Department argues that profit distribution to partner constitutes a separate taxable supply — use this ratio to establish that firm's business IS the partner's business; profits are not income from supply.
CIT v. RM Chidambaram Pillai & Ors.
Supreme Court of India | 1977 | (1977) 1 SCC 431
"The firm as such has no legal recognition. The law, ignoring the firm, looks to the partners composing it. What is called the property of the firm is their property, and what are called the debts and liabilities of the firm are their debts and their liabilities. A partner cannot be either debtor or creditor of the firm of which he is himself a member, nor can he be employed by his firm."
When to Use: When demand is raised treating partner-firm transaction as supply between two distinct persons — use this to argue that a partner cannot be creditor/debtor of his own firm; no supply relationship between partner and firm inter se.
Suraj Lamp and Industries (P.) Ltd. v. State of Haryana & Anr.
Supreme Court of India | 2012 | AIR 2012 SC 206
Transfer of immovable property by inducting a partner, contributing property and subsequently exiting the earlier partner, without executing a proper Sale Deed, attracts scrutiny on the raison d'etre of the arrangement — particularly where absence of Sale Deed is the apparent objective.
When to Use: When opponent alleges that immovable property transfer via partnership route without Sale Deed was done to evade stamp duty or tax — note that SC has flagged this arrangement; alternatively use to argue that if proper Deed exists, transaction is legitimate.
Section 7(1)(aa) — CGST Act (Legal Fiction on Mutuality)
Parliament — inserted w.e.f. 1st July 2017 (notified 1st Jan 2022)
Even the statutory legal fiction under Section 7(1)(aa) is limited to transactions for "cash, deferred payment or other valuable consideration" — which by the 'words of rank' rule of construction covers only monetary consideration: (i) cash, (ii) credit, and (iii) negotiable instruments. It does NOT extend to non-monetary valuable consideration.
When to Use: When Department seeks to apply Section 7(1)(aa) to non-monetary transactions between association and members — argue that the statutory fiction itself has a built-in limitation and cannot be stretched beyond monetary consideration.
Lindley on Partnership (2) — cited by Apex Court
Authoritative Treatise — cited in CIT v. RM Chidambaram Pillai (1977) 1 SCC 431
"The firm is not recognised by English lawyers as distinct from the members composing it. Any change amongst the partners destroys the identity of the firm. In point of law, a partner may be the debtor or the creditor of his copartners, but he cannot be either debtor or creditor of the firm of which he is himself a member."
When to Use: As a supplementary citation to reinforce the non-entity status of a firm before adjudicating officers who may be unaware of the foundational principle — demonstrates that this position is supported by English partnership law as adopted by the Apex Court.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 4 — Partnership Act, Para 4.2
📖 Ch 5a — Transfer of Property Act (Part 1)

Chapter 5 — Transfer of Property Act

Sections 5.1 to 5.5 | Pages 167–193 | Source: Allied Laws — GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

🗺️ Legend
⬛ Root Node
🔵 Level 1 Branch
✅ Permitted / Positive / IS
❌ Prohibited / Negative / NOT
⚠️ Condition / Test / If / Unless
🟣 Optional / Pass-on Route
ℹ️ Neutral Reference / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 Index — Chapter 5 | Transfer of Property Act
5.1 Relevance to GST — Aspect Theory
5.2.1 Statutory Definitions — Immovable Property
5.2.2 Affixation — Manner & Purpose Test
5.2.3 Standing Timber
5.2.4 Growing Crop
5.2.5 Intangible Immovable Property
5.2.6 Benefits to Arise from Land
5.2.7 Profit-à-prendre
5.2.8 Land — A Specie
5.2.9 Registration
5.2.10 Movable Property
5.2.11 Actionable Claim
5.3.1 Transfer — Validity
5.3.2 Possession
5.3.3 Document
5.3.4 Non-Transferable Rights (Section 6)
5.3.5 Title, Right or Interest
5.3.6 Consideration for Transfer
5.3.7 Rule Against Perpetuity
5.4.1 Sale — Valid Sale (Section 54)
5.4.2 Risks and Rewards (Section 55)
5.5 Mortgage — Nature, Types & GST
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
5.1 — Relevance to GST | Aspect Theory
⬆ Index
GST & IMMOVABLE PROPERTY — RELEVANCE
It is erroneous to think GST cannot apply to transactions involving immovable property
ASPECT THEORY — Province of Madras v. Boddu Paidanna (AIR 1942 FC 33) affirmed in Atiabari Tea Co. v. State of Assam (AIR 1961 SC 232)
Central Excise duty applies for taxable event of manufacture — Sales tax applies for taxable event of sale of those manufactured articles. Selling price may form basis for quantification of BOTH — yet there is NO double taxation. This is the simplest presentation of 'aspect theory' of taxation.
📝 Same thing, two different taxes — each looking at a different 'aspect' of the same transaction. Stamp duty looks at the instrument of conveyance. GST looks at the consideration for the transaction. Paying stamp duty does NOT buy immunity from GST on immovable property deals.
THREE ROUTES FOR EXCLUSION FROM GST — ALL THREE MUST BE SHOWN
(a) Levy must be expressly made inapplicable — like securities, money, alcoholic liquor, 5 petro-products
(b) Transactions kept out by Schedule III
(c) Statutory exemption notified under Section 11
📝 Payment of stamp duty in respect of immovable property transactions does NOT procure immunity from GST unless one of these three situations is shown. Stamp duty and GST operate on different aspects — both can validly apply.
💡 GST Hearing: Where assessee argues 'we paid stamp duty so GST should not apply' — cite Aspect Theory. Stamp duty is on instrument of conveyance. GST is on consideration for transaction. Two different aspects — no double taxation — both validly applicable.
5.2.1 — Statutory Definitions | Immovable Property — Three Acts
⬆ Index
IMMOVABLE PROPERTY — THREE STATUTORY DEFINITIONS
Definitions cannot be substituted — inclusions cannot be shunted, exclusions cannot be forcibly included
Transfer of Property Act (Sec 3)Registration Act (Sec 2(6))General Clauses Act (Sec 3(26))
"Immovable Property" does NOT include standing timber, growing crops or grass; "Immovable Property" includes land, buildings, hereditary allowances, rights to ways, lights, ferries, fisheries or any other benefit to arise out of land, and things attached to the earth, or permanently fastened to anything which is attached to the earth, but not standing timber, growing crops nor grass; "Immovable Property" shall include land, benefits to arise out of land, and things attached to the earth, or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth;
KEY JUDICIAL PRINCIPLE — Tarakeshwar Sia Thakurji v. Dar Das Dey (1979) 3 SCC 106
Definition in Section 3(26) of General Clauses Act applies to Transfer of Property Act since definition in Section 3 is in the negative (drawing authority from Shantabai v. State of Bombay AIR 1958 SC 532).
📝 Since TPA's own definition only says what immovable property does NOT include — you must look at General Clauses Act for what it IS. GCA definition includes land, benefits from land, things attached to earth, permanently fastened things. This is the POSITIVE definition that governs.
💡 GST Hearing: When determining whether a transaction involves 'immovable property' for Schedule III — use GCA's inclusive definition (land, benefits from land, things attached). Do not limit to only TPA's negative definition which only tells you what is excluded.
5.2.2 — Affixation | Manner and Purpose Test
⬆ Index
AFFIXATION — MANNER AND PURPOSE TEST
'Able' in immovable = character of the thing, NOT capability of person or process to move it
THE TEST — TWO PARTS
Character → 'Manner and Purpose' of affixation — Is the thing fastened inextricably bonded to land or building?
Capability to move → 'Strength and ability' — Whether a person/process CAN physically move it (IRRELEVANT)
📝 The test is NOT whether YOU CAN remove it — the test is WHY it was fixed there and HOW it was fixed. Even if something can be easily dismantled — that doesn't make it movable. Also: 'to be unearthed, with or without damage, is no reason to doubt the results of the manner and purpose test.'
IS IMMOVABLE PROPERTY
Eg 309: Hut
Eg 310: Gypsum partition walls with aluminium/wooden frame
Eg 316: Projector room in cinema theatre
Eg 315: Acoustic enclosure is part of DG set — but BUILDING (not part of DG set)
IS MOVABLE PROPERTY
Eg 311: Ducts for air-handling (low-side)
Eg 312: ATM installed in building
Eg 313: DG installation — although bolted to concrete floor — movable (purpose of affixation = operate DG, not to bond to land)
Eg 314: Container houses used for dwelling or office
📝 DG set bolted to floor is still movable — because its PURPOSE is to generate power (which it can do anywhere), not to permanently become part of the building. Bolting is for stability during operation, not for inextricable bonding to the earth.
BUILDING ENCLOSURE TEST
Factory building may be constructed and machinery installed inside. OR machinery installed and protective enclosure added. Test: Is the enclosure-building:
(a) Means of operating the machinery? → Movable
(b) Premises where machinery is operated? → Immovable
Eg 315: Acoustic enclosure = part of DG set (movable)
Eg 316: Projector room = building (immovable)
TIER 2 AFFIXATION Article 'attached to that which is embedded to earth' = also immovable — provided it satisfies 'method and purpose' test.
CCE v. Solid and Correct Engineering Works 2010 (252) ELT 481 (SC): "It is nobody's case that the attachment of the plant to the foundation is meant for permanent beneficial enjoyment of either the foundation or the land in which the same is embedded."
📝 If the plant is attached to foundation only for operational stability (not for permanent enjoyment of the land) — it is movable property. This SC judgment is the key ratio in plant/machinery vs immovable property disputes in GST.
5.2.3 — Standing Timber
⬆ Index
STANDING TIMBER — Tree vs Standing Timber
Critical distinction for GST classification — immovable vs movable property
TREE = IMMOVABLE PROPERTY
Tree depends on land for nourishment and growth. By 'manner and purpose' test — tree is immovable since it is not intended for felling. Grows from and depends on land.
Eg 317: Tomato plant bearing fruit = tree
Eg 318: Teakwood plant more than 6 cm girth = tree
Eg 319: Sprouted seeds = plant (NOT tree)
Eg 320: Coconut sapling = plant (NOT tree)
📝 A living tree rooted in the earth and still growing = immovable property. It draws sustenance from the land. Its purpose is to grow — not to be cut. Transfer of a growing tree = transfer of immovable property (requires registration if value > ₹100).
STANDING TIMBER = MOVABLE PROPERTY
Standing timber = tree that is READY FOR FELLING. Has reached end of dependence on land for nourishment. Left to itself — may continue or die — but unlikely to derive any more benefit from land.
When taken for filling — would NOT remain immovable property.
Tree excludes plants — scientific and plant tests provide distinction.
📝 When a tree has served its purpose, matured fully, and is ready to be cut — it becomes 'standing timber' — movable property. The transaction is then for goods (timber), not immovable property. GST at goods rate applies — Schedule III (land/building exclusion) does NOT apply.
💡 GST Hearing: Royalty for forest timber rights — characterise as: (a) Immovable property rights (if trees still growing) or (b) Goods supply (if standing timber ready for felling). State of MP v. Orient Paper Mills AIR 1977 SC 687 — royalty = feudalistic euphemism for 'price' of timber.
5.2.4 — Growing Crop
⬆ Index
GROWING CROP — Movable Property
Growing crop excluded from immovable property — but object of contract test determines treatment
GROWING CROP = MOVABLE PROPERTY
On the other side of their life — which standing timber has already reached. Growing crops excluded from immovable property definition.
OBJECT OF CONTRACT TEST
Land sold WITH growing crop on it = whole is inseparable 'object' of contract → combined object = immovable property.
Eg 321: Lease of land with fruit-bearing trees for 6 months = price for fruit ready for harvest. Note restriction against felling.
📝 If the POINT of the deal is to get the land AND the crop together — treat it as immovable property. If the point is to get only the fruits — it is movable property (goods).
MARSHALL v. GREEN TEST (1875)
"Where purchaser is to derive benefit from further growth and nutriment from land → contract is for interest in land (immovable). But where vegetation is over or parties agree thing sold shall be immediately withdrawn → mere warehousing of the thing sold → contract is for goods."
📝 Future growth expected → immovable property deal. Immediate severance expected → goods deal. The test is the state of growth at time of contract and the intended purpose of the purchaser.
Eg 322: Lease of land for 5 years = rights to harvest over multiple seasons. Note restrictions against felling those trees — especially if that variety has more than 10 fruit-bearing years and much still remain.
→ Land leased to enable harvest of growing crop over term
5.2.5 — Intangible Immovable Property
⬆ Index
INTANGIBLE IMMOVABLE PROPERTY
'Property' is the RIGHT qua the thing — not the thing itself. Rights over immovable things = immovable (but intangible) property
FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE
Property is NOT the land or building (or goods). Property is the right qua those things. If the thing over which rights exist is immovable → it is immovable (but possibly intangible) property. If the thing is movable → it is movable property.
Property is commonly (erroneously) referred to as the very thing itself — nothing is farther from truth.
📝 You don't OWN a piece of land. You own the RIGHTS over that piece of land. The rights ARE the property. When those rights are over immovable things (land, buildings) — those rights are themselves immovable property — even if they are intangible (like development rights, fishing rights, lease rights).
Eg 323 Right to USE is an intangible right over land — can be detached from ownership and transferred under a 'lease'
Eg 324 Fishing rights = intangible right over land (lake) — transferred under a lease of lake. Lake refers to water covering the land underneath
Eg 325 Right to construct and develop over land = itself an intangible immovable property → JDA grants this right → Developer receives intangible immovable property
IMPORTANT: GREATER RIGHT SUBSUMES LESSER RIGHTS
Greater rights comprise several lesser rights. When greater right is admitted 'object of transfer' → no treatment may be extended to lesser rights that also transfer (they were not the 'object of transfer').
Eg 330: Absolute title to land includes right to use, right to license, right to develop. Sale of land (greater right) being absolute → right to lease (lesser right) is also transferred.
Eg 331: Ownership of shares (greater right) subsumes all other shareholder rights (lesser rights). Transfer of lesser rights cannot give rise to any tax treatment independent of that which transfer of greater right attracts.
📝 When you buy land (greatest right) — you automatically get all lesser rights too. You don't separately owe tax on each lesser right. Similarly, when GST excludes transfer of land (Schedule III) — all rights INCLUDED in that land transfer also get excluded — they are not separately taxable.
💡 GST Hearing: Revenue tries to tax each 'right' separately within a land sale (development rights, FSI, etc.) — argue: Transfer of land (greater right) subsumes all these lesser rights. If land transfer is excluded under Schedule III — all lesser rights follow the same treatment.
5.2.6 — Benefits to Arise from Land
⬆ Index
BENEFITS TO ARISE FROM LAND
'Benefits to arise' = FUTURE benefits — what lies in present is the 'right to those benefits'
KEY DISTINCTION
Reference is to 'future benefits' — benefits 'yet to' arise. What lies in the PRESENT is the 'rights to those benefits' — intangible right in present — enforceable and allows holder to enjoy benefits when they come into existence.
Transfer of 'benefits to arise from land' = transfer of the RIGHT IN PRESENT to enjoy in future — NOT transfer of the benefits themselves.
📝 You cannot transfer 'tomorrow's fruits' — fruits don't exist yet. But you CAN transfer TODAY the RIGHT to collect those fruits when they grow. That right is PRESENT and intangible immovable property. When you sell 'rights to future harvest' — you are selling the right (immovable) — not the future fruits (movable).
Eg 326: Fruits on tree = benefits from land. Right to permit harvesting = benefits to arise from land
Eg 327: Ore in land = benefits from land. Right to permit mining = benefits to arise from land
📝 Mining rights = intangible immovable property. When Government grants mining lease — it is granting intangible immovable property. GST cannot apply as if it were a service. Schedule III (land exclusion) may apply — subject to specific GST provisions.
CERTAINTY TEST
Where there is UNCERTAINTY of yield but CERTAINTY of exclusive rights to exploit → transfer of rights to future goods = benefits to arise from land → intangible immovable property.
Where there is CERTAINTY of yield → it is sale of goods (future goods contract).
📝 Mining right: quantity of ore uncertain → intangible immovable property. But confirmed fixed-tonnage ore sale contract → sale of goods (future goods).
Eg 328: Fruits from next three harvests can be transferred TODAY by entering into non-cancellable lease of land duly registered for three years
Eg 329: Irrevocable power of attorney along with interest in land CAN be executed
5.2.7 — Profit-à-prendre
⬆ Index
PROFIT-À-PRENDRE
French: Right of 'taking away' — a determinate or indeterminate group of persons enjoys right to take produce from another's land
DEFINITION — Saiyad Wajid Ali Shah v. Umrai Bibi (1904) 1 ALJ 621
"A profit-à-prendre is a right to remove and appropriate any part of the soil belonging to another, or anything growing upon or attached to the soil or the purpose of the profit to be gained from the property thereby acquired — that is, a right to take gravel, stone, trees and so forth."
Eg 332: Right to draw water from river/lake/well
Eg 333: Right to catch fish in river or lake
Eg 334: Right of passage through field of another
KEY DISTINCTION — LEASE vs PROFIT-À-PRENDRE
Shantabai v. State of Bombay AIR 1958 SC 532:
"In a lease, one enjoys the property but has NO right to take it away. In a profit-à-prendre one has a licence to enter on the land, not for purpose of enjoying it, but for REMOVING something from it — a part of the produce of the soil."
Lease is intangible immovable property where possessory rights transferred.
Profit-à-prendre = right to take away — before taking = intangible immovable property.
📝 In a mining lease — if you go onto land to ENJOY and operate the mine as business → LEASE. If you go in ONLY TO EXTRACT ore and take it away → PROFIT-À-PRENDRE. The two have different legal and GST implications.
💡 GST Hearing: Revenue classifies mining arrangement as 'service' or 'goods supply.' Argue: this is profit-à-prendre = intangible immovable property. Transfer of profit-à-prendre rights requires registration — unregistered arrangement = non est (Registration Act Sec 17 + 49 apply).
PROFIT-À-PRENDRE vs EASEMENT
Profit-à-prendre is MUCH MORE than easement — easements INCLUDE profit-à-prendre.
Profit-à-prendre that is NOT included in easement = 'profit-à-prendre in gross' — exists independent of dominant heritage — is intangible immovable property and can be lawfully transferred.
5.2.8 — Land — A Specie of Immovable Property
⬆ Index
LAND — ONE SPECIE OF IMMOVABLE PROPERTY
Equating Schedule III Para 5 to every specie of immovable property would be expanding the words of Schedule III
CRITICAL GST PRINCIPLE
Land is NOT the whole of the discussion about immovable property. There are MANY different 'immovable properties' — land is just ONE of them. To equate paragraph 5, Schedule III to EVERY SPECIE of immovable property — whether tangible or intangible — would be an attempt at expanding the words of said Schedule III.
Eg 335: Planet does not make up the entire universe. Universe is made of billions of stars, planets and other heavenly bodies. Planet is NOT the whole of the universe.
📝 Schedule III Para 5 excludes 'sale of land' and 'sale of building.' This exclusion does NOT automatically extend to ALL immovable property. Development rights, mining rights, fishing rights, profit-à-prendre, benefits from land — these are OTHER species of immovable property — their GST treatment must be separately determined. Cannot hide behind Para 5 Schedule III for all of these.
UNIVERSE OF IMMOVABLE PROPERTY Includes: (1) Land (earth) (2) Building (3) Things attached (4) Profit-à-prendre (5) Rights to exploit (benefits from land). Land and building are just two of many things that make up this universe. Immovable property contains vast number of 'rights' that are less than 'title' but which procure lawful enjoyment akin to ownership.
5.2.9 — Registration
⬆ Index
REGISTRATION — SINE QUA NON FOR IMMOVABLE PROPERTY TRANSFER
Registration = record of title — NOT itself assurance of title
VOID WITHOUT REGISTRATION
Immovable property (tangible or intangible) conveyed WITHOUT registered written instrument = VOID
Unregistered record is no record
Registration does not itself assure title — only records it
Lease for more than one year = void unless in writing and duly registered (Section 107)
📝 This is the key rule: you CANNOT transfer immovable property without a registered document. The transfer is not merely unenforceable — it is VOID. Nothing passes. JDA without registration = zero legal effect for Developer.
EXCEPTIONS
Verbal contracts = permitted
Customary gifts like Hiba = even recognized
BUT: Where non-testamentary instrument conveys interest, right or title → registration is sine qua non (indispensable)
Asserting existence of rights demands production of registered written record
📝 'Sine qua non' = without which nothing. Registration is absolutely necessary for non-testamentary transfers of immovable property. Absence raises presumption AGAINST the person asserting title to property.
11-MONTH LEASE PRACTICE
This is the reason for popular practice of 11-month lease arrangements that are not written or not stamped and registered. Lease for more than 1 year must be registered. Practitioners avoid registration by keeping below 12 months.
💡 GST Impact: 11-month unregistered lease arrangement = valid in law (no interest in immovable property passes — it is merely a license). But if the document actually transfers 'possessory rights' it is a lease (Eg 368 — license agreement that transfers possessory rights = lease, not license).
5.2.10 — Movable Property
⬆ Index
MOVABLE PROPERTY
General Clauses Act Sec 3(36) + Registration Act Sec 2(9) — all property except immovable property
IS MOVABLE PROPERTY
Eg 336: Electricity
Eg 337: Human blood
Eg 338: Air
Eg 339: Goodwill
Eg 340: Brand name
Eg 341: Software
Eg 342: Pets
Eg 343: Livestock
Also: Standing timber, growing crops, grass, fruit upon and juice in trees
NOT MOVABLE PROPERTY
Eg 344: Wildlife (not property at all)
Eg 345: Leasehold rights (not movable property)
Eg 346: Building (not movable property)
Eg 347: Pre-paid expense (not property)
Eg 348: Insurance policy (not property)
Eg 349: Personal club membership (not property)
Eg 350: Crypto-currency (not property)
Eg 351: Non-fungible tokens (not property)
📝 Being 'movable' alone is NOT sufficient to qualify as movable property. Must bear all attributes of a RIGHT qua the thing. Rights must be enforceable. Crypto-currency's decentralised existence makes it unenforceable → not property. Merely because a market exists does not make the thing 'property.'
TEST FOR 'PROPERTY'
It must bear all attributes of a RIGHT qua the thing (land, building, goods). Unless 'proprietary rights' can be exercised over the object — being 'movable' alone will not suffice.
Chose-in-possession = stock on hand
Chose-in-action = holder of endorsed bill of lading
Mere right to sue = NOT chose-in-action
Right to property that becomes available when successful in suit → chose-in-action
5.2.11 — Actionable Claim
⬆ Index
ACTIONABLE CLAIM
Beneficial interest in movable property NOT yet in possession — expressly included in GST Sec 2(52) definition of 'goods'
SECTION 3 DEFINITION
"Actionable claim means a claim to any debt, or to any beneficial interest in movable property not in the possession, either actual or constructive, of the claimant, which the Civil Courts recognise as affording grounds for relief, whether such debt or beneficial interest be existent, accruing, conditional or contingent."
📝 'Action' required to actualise the 'claim.' You have a CLAIM to something — not the thing itself. The Civil Court must be willing to entertain a suit on it. If the claim is unenforceable (e.g., crypto claim) — it is NOT actionable. Actionable claim is always 'in respect of' movable property — NOT immovable property.
IS ACTIONABLE CLAIM
Eg 352: Lottery-ticket
Eg 353: Bills receivable
Eg 354: Accrued rent
Eg 355: Accepted insurance claim
Eg 356: Retired Partner's capital balance
Eg 358: Bills discounted
Eg 359: Right to pension under EPF Act on superannuation by contributors
Transferable Development Rights (TDR) issued to land-losers
💡 TDR is actionable claim since it is actionable if underlying interest in real property were to be denied. GST on TDR must be examined as GST on supply of actionable claim — which is expressly 'goods' under Sec 2(52) CGST Act.
NOT ACTIONABLE CLAIM
Eg 357: Overdue rent = debt itself (chose-in-action) — NOT actionable claim
Eg 360: Right to pension of ex-servicemen = non-transferable property
Shares and securities (do not bear interest in movable property)
Duty credit certificates (DEPB, REP licences) — are goods simplicitor, NOT actionable claim (Yasha Overseas v. CST, SC 2015)
Digital wallets = NOT actionable claims (stored value pre-paid instrument)
Crypto-currency = NOT actionable claim (decentralised, unenforceable)
📝 Duty credit certificates (like DEPB) are like 'prize-winning ticket after the draw' — the prize is certain, value known. They are goods, not actionable claims. Cannot be traded as actionable claims. GST treatment = supply of goods at their face value.
GST SIGNIFICANCE
Actionable claims are expressly included in definition of 'goods' in Section 2(52) of Central GST Act — overriding their exclusion from Sale of Goods Act definition. Trade in actionable claims (which is the area of attention in GST) requires FREE TRANSFERABILITY. Actionable claim is always 'in respect of' movable property — NOT immovable property and much less intangible immovable property.
5.3.1 — Transfer | Validity (Section 5)
⬆ Index
TRANSFER — VALIDITY
Section 5: Transfer must be of property IN EXISTENCE — not property yet to come into existence
SECTION 5 — TRANSFER DEFINED
"Transfer of property means an act by which a living person conveys property, in present or in future, to one or more other living persons, or to himself, or to himself and one or more other living persons."
'In present or in future' qualifies the ACT of conveying — NOT the 'property.' Property must exist in present.
📝 'In future' refers to when the conveyance HAPPENS — not the property being conveyed. So you can agree today to convey property later — but the property must already exist at time of actual conveyance. You cannot convey an apartment under construction — it does not exist yet.
VALID TRANSFERS — INTERESTS IN PRESENT
Eg 361: Developer secures 'interests' in land under registered JDA (right to construct and develop) — VALID
Eg 362: Lessee has 'possessory rights' over user of property — VALID
Eg 363: Bank holding mortgage has 'absolute right but contingent title' over property as security — VALID
Eg 364: Co-parceners interests in joint-property becomes perfected on death of karta or partition — VALID
💡 Eg 361 is critical: Developer's interest under registered JDA = interest in existing land (not the apartment yet to be built). This interest is PRESENT and VALID. This is why GST's taxable event for Developer = date of JDA registration, not completion of apartment.
INVALID 'TRANSFERS'
Eg 374: Agreement-holder does NOT have any interest in apartment (or share of land) yet to be completed. Transfer of apartment under-construction = object yet to come into existence → NOT valid transfer
Transfer to non-existent/unborn person = invalid
Transfer of property that is yet to come into existence = invalid
Eg 373: Sale Deed with contingency delaying possession does NOT convey absolute title to Buyer
📝 This is the legal basis for the Supreme Court position on flat bookings: The apartment does not exist yet — so 'booking' of an apartment is NOT a transfer of immovable property. Premium collected on such transfer is NOT excluded from GST by Para 5, Schedule III. (Confirmed in Eg 390 from 5.4).
INGREDIENTS FOR VALID TRANSFER
(i) Object in present
(ii) Intended to be conveyed
(iii) Inter vivos including juridical persons — must be shown to exist
Where conveyance requires registration — that too must exist.
Nemo dat quod non habet: Transferor can convey only the same extent and quality of title held. Cannot give more than what they have.
5.3.2 — Possession
⬆ Index
POSSESSION
Lawful control to occupy and use — possession ≠ custody — cannot be lost when custody handed to third-party
LAWFUL POSSESSION
Possession = lawful control to occupy and use.
Can be actual or constructive but must be lawful.
Eg 365: Lessor conveys 'possessory right' to Lessee — Lessor CANNOT be in occupation though retains right of re-entry and resume possession on termination/cancellation
Eg 366: Sale and lease arrangement — Buyer acquires title, transfers lawful possession to Lessee (previous Owner). Seller retains 'reversionary right' as Lessor.
📝 When Lessor gives possession to Lessee — Lessor loses the right to physically occupy even though they still own the property. This is why disturbing a tenant requires legal process — eviction by force = illegal, attracts restoration of possession and suit for ejectment.
NOT POSSESSION
Carrier, warehouse-keeper, finder of lost goods = custody but NOT possession
Eg 367: Caterer serving food inside factory = only permission pursuant to catering contract — NOT possession of factory premises
Adverse possession = possession retained AGAINST right of lawful owner — wrong possession
Holding-over beyond lease term = accountable for mesne profits in ejectment suit
📝 'Mesne profits' = profits earned between date of rightful termination and date of actual handing over. Occupant holding over beyond lease must compensate true owner for this period — this is NOT rent but damages for wrongful occupation.
5.3.3 — Document
⬆ Index
DOCUMENT OF TRANSFER
Object of transfer known not from title to document but from effects produced by obligations accepted in bargain
Document may be an expression of requirements of the arrangement — but interests, rights and title to be conveyed must be extracted from effects produced from obligations accepted in the bargain. Once executed, registered, and not disputed as mistake — full effects follow de hors titles and descriptions.
📝 What matters is NOT what the document is CALLED — but what it DOES. A document titled 'License Agreement' that actually transfers possessory rights is a LEASE — not a license. Revenue must look at substance of what rights are conveyed, not the name of the document.
Eg 368: License agreement seeking to transfer 'possessory rights' = LEASE, not license
Eg 369: General Power of Attorney seeking to appoint Attorney with irrevocable authority to sell or develop and sell property = conveys 'interest' in property to such Attorney
Eg 370: Sale Deed for land conveys trees and building situated on such land
Eg 371: Lease Deed for land conveys after-constructed buildings to Lessor on determination of lease
Eg 372: Sale of tenanted building conveys obligations attendant to lease to Buyer
FATAL DEFECTS
Inadequately stamped document of conveyance = conveys NOTHING
Unregistered document validly stamped = also conveys NOTHING
Both stamp AND registration are mandatory for valid conveyance of immovable property.
5.3.4 — Non-Transferable Rights | Section 6
⬆ Index
NON-TRANSFERABLE RIGHTS — SECTION 6
"Property of any kind may be transferred except as otherwise provided" — Section 6 lists what CANNOT be transferred
(a) Chance of heir-apparent succeeding to estate / legacy on death of kinsman / mere possibility of like nature — CANNOT be transferred
(b) Mere right of re-entry for breach of condition subsequent — CANNOT be transferred except to owner of property affected
(c) Easement — CANNOT be transferred apart from dominant heritage
(d) Interest in property restricted in enjoyment to owner personally — CANNOT be transferred
(dd) Right to future maintenance — CANNOT be transferred
(e) Mere right to sue — CANNOT be transferred
(f) Public office / salary of public officer — CANNOT be transferred
(g) Military, naval, air-force, civil pensions — CANNOT be transferred
(h) No transfer opposed to nature of interest affected, or unlawful object/consideration (Sec 23 ICA), or to person legally disqualified
JDA KEY EXAMPLES
Eg 374: Agreement-holder does NOT have any interest in apartment/share of land yet to be completed → transfer of such apartment under-construction is for object yet to come into existence → NOT valid transfer
Eg 384: Transfer of interest of Developer 1 to Developer 2 in earlier JDA = VOID without registered amendment deed to original JDA
Eg 385: Transfer of leasehold rights ('rights of user' held by Lessee 1) to Lessee 2 = also NOT valid transfer (non-transferable)
📝 These examples are critical for GST. Revenue often demands GST on booking of under-construction apartments treating it as 'transfer of immovable property.' Eg 374 establishes: no valid transfer exists because the apartment does not exist — therefore Schedule III Para 5 exclusion does NOT apply — GST IS applicable on premium collected.
5.3.5 — Title, Right or Interest
⬆ Index
TITLE — RIGHT — INTEREST
Three concentric concepts — Interest (innermost) → Right (middle) → Title (outermost)
DEFINITIONS
Interest = Any lawful claim over property — broadest and most inclusive
Right = Superior interests and inferior title — intermediate layer
Title = Absolute rights over the WHOLE of the property — outermost layer
Interests can be secured by ANY lawful arrangement without necessarily acquiring title. Where title (greater right) is lawfully secured → all incidents to title by way of interests (lesser rights) automatically available (unless prior encumbrance — Section 8).
📝 Think of concentric circles: Title is the biggest circle (absolute ownership). Inside it are Rights (e.g., possessory right, mortgage right). Inside rights are Interests (smaller enforceable claims like lien, charge). You can have an 'interest' in property WITHOUT having title — like a banker with mortgage, or a tenant with lease.
KEY EXAMPLES
Eg 375: Acquisition of title → right of occupation and use automatically derived. If property already under registered non-cancellable lease → title acquired absolute but occupation continuing with Lessee (reverts to Purchaser on determination of lease)
Eg 376: Seller remains absolute title-holder even after Agreement to Sell — until Sale Deed executed and registered
Eg 365: Lessor conveys possessory right to Lessee
📝 Eg 376 is fundamental: Agreement to Sell creates NO interest in property for the Buyer. Seller is still the title-holder. Only the Sale Deed (registered) transfers title. Until then — Buyer has only contractual right to demand specific performance — nothing more.
💡 GST Critical — Eg 377: Agreement to sell land gives NO 'interest' to Buyer even if registered. Agreement to Sell ≠ Sale Deed. Buyer only has right to demand Sale Deed. This supports GST position that booking premium is for service — not for immovable property transfer.
NON EST IN LAW
Eg 378: Transfer Deed after Agreement to See is executed but before Sale Deed = NON EST in law. So-called Buyer A (transferring to Buyer B) has NO 'interest, right or title' that exists in present to lawfully be subject matter of transfer.
Eg 377: Agreement to sell land gives NO 'interest' to Buyer even if agreement is registered. Agreement to Sell by itself does NOT create any interest, right or title to Buyer (see Section 54 — discussed in 5.4).
📝 The chain of title must be unbroken and registered. An intermediate 'transfer' by someone who has only an Agreement to Sell is void — they are passing title they don't have. This voids the entire sub-transaction.
REVERSIONARY RIGHT + REMAINDER INTEREST
Reversionary right = recovery of interests/rights to property after termination/completion of term for which they were conveyed — Lessor has reversionary right of occupation after lease term.
Remainder interest = right of son in testamentary succession subject to life interest bequeathed to widow — perfected and becomes absolute on demise of widow.
5.3.6 — Consideration for Transfer
⬆ Index
CONSIDERATION FOR TRANSFER
Insufficient consideration voids transfer — Section 6(h) — JDA-specific applications
KEY RULES
Consideration may be monetary or non-monetary.
Insufficient consideration = voids transfer (unless contract asserts extraneous consideration in non-monetary form).
Payment of stamp duty based on circle rate = NOT substitute for actual consideration.
Absence of consideration hits Section 6(h) → void ab initio (barring gift).
JDA CONSIDERATION — CONFIRMED
Eg 380: Landowner CANNOT claim to be transferring title to land when entering into JDA with Developer — (consideration for Landowner's 'transfer' = Developer's construction services)
Eg 381: Developer CANNOT claim to be providing construction services in respect of units falling to Landowner's share without consideration — (consideration = development rights/land given by Landowner)
Eg 382: Developer acquires 'interest' in land once JDA is executed and registered (see Section 202 Indian Contract Act)
💡 These three examples together establish the complete GST edifice for JDA: (1) Landowner makes taxable supply (land/development rights) for consideration (construction service value). (2) Developer makes taxable supply (construction services) for consideration (development rights). (3) Both transactions are fully taxable — neither side can claim it is 'without consideration.'
JOINT OWNERSHIP CONSIDERATIONS
Extent of contribution towards consideration = extent of 'interest' in transfer of each contributor.
Joint ownership = singularity of title and possession by all contributors — devolves on survivor entirely.
Co-ownership = determinate, distinct, unchanging share in title — singular possession over entire property — death results in succession to heirs (not surviving co-owners).
Eg 383: Land contributed by Partner A and machinery by Partner B = joint venture (not JDA) — depends on accounting treatment.
📝 Whether assets contributed to a JV are 'transferred' to the JV firm depends on whether they are treated as the firm's assets in accounts. If credited to firm's asset accounts = transfer. If merely 'available for use' but acknowledged in deed but NOT treated as transfer = individual assets remain.
5.3.7 — Rule Against Perpetuity
⬆ Index
RULE AGAINST PERPETUITY
Section 14 — Embargo against creating 'interest' after lifetime of persons to a present transfer
THE RULE
Even if absolute title cannot be created in present to property yet to come into existence — Section 14 places embargo against creating 'interest' after lifetime of one of the persons to a transfer made presently.
Innovative contract-writing causing interests to be created in future = VOID ab initio.
PERMITTED
Irrevocably transferring property in present to a Trust = permitted even if beneficiaries are persons not yet born — transfer is complete between Transferor and Transferee (Trust)
VOID — JDA EXAMPLES
Eg 384: Transfer of interest of Developer 1 in earlier JDA to Developer 2 = VOID without registered amendment deed
Eg 385: Transfer of leasehold rights by Lessee 1 to Lessee 2 of 'rights of user' (non-transferable) = also NOT valid transfer
📝 Any arrangement that creates interests in property beyond the natural life of the persons involved in the present transfer is void under rule against perpetuity. Also, Section 114 of Indian Succession Act has a similar prohibition.
5.4.1 — Sale | Valid Sale | Section 54
⬆ Index
SALE — VALID SALE (SECTION 54)
"Sale is a transfer of ownership in exchange for a price paid or promised or part-paid and part-promised"
SECTION 54 — THREE ELEMENTS OF VALID SALE
(1) Parties to contract — must have capacity to contract AND authority to contract qua the object of proposed transfer
(2) Object of transfer — must be identified by tracing nature and extent of interest, right or title held by Transferor
(3) Price — consideration in monetary or non-monetary form — price need not form the whole of consideration
(4) Act of transfer — for tangible immovable property ≥₹100 or reversion or intangible → registered instrument only. For tangible <₹100 → registered instrument OR delivery of property.
📝 All four elements must be present for a valid sale of immovable property. If ANY is missing — no valid sale occurred. 'Contract for sale' does NOT itself create any interest in or charge on property — it merely gives right to demand conveyance.
AGREEMENT TO SELL ≠ SALE
Agreement-holders have NO 'interest' in immovable property — whether in existence or yet to be constructed.
Agreement to transfer (non-existent) interest = non est
Premium/consideration flow on booking = to be explained as service at gross value of premium plus advance paid.
Eg 376: Seller remains absolute title-holder after Agreement to Sell until Sale Deed executed and registered.
Eg 390: Customer booking of apartment before OC = no 'interest' in apartment not yet in existence → Premium collected NOT excluded by Para 5, Schedule III → GST applicable.
📝 This is the GST position on flat booking: The apartment doesn't exist at booking stage. No valid transfer of immovable property. Premium is for construction service — fully taxable under GST. Para 5 Schedule III exclusion (sale of building) does NOT apply until after OC/CC is obtained and Sale Deed executed.
PRICE — KEY RULES
Uncertainty of price is FATAL — voids transfer.
Contract specifying METHODOLOGY of determination of price = valid (if methodology itself is certain).
Price need not form whole of consideration — non-monetary component also valid price.
Selling price lower than previous purchase = NOT indicator of inadequacy unless seller asserts mistake or lack of free consent.
Eg 386: NPV of committed cashflow from rental of tenanted property discounted at WACC of 12% = valid price.
Eg 387: NPV of PROJECTED cashflow from rental of property = invalid price (speculative).
💡 Eg 391: GPA-holder for plot of land paid ₹10 crore to Owner and executed Sale Deed in favour of Buyer for ₹15 crore. GPA had no interest, right or title to convey. Gains by GPA = liable to GST WITHOUT relief of exclusion from Para 5, Schedule III — because GPA was never the valid title-holder.
EIGHT FORMS OF SUPPLY — SECTION 7(1)(a)
8 forms listed cannot be expanded
Each form is pregnant with meaning — none superfluous
May overlap but cannot be interchanged or misapplied
Presence of "such as" does NOT authorize ejusdem generis rule
Only permits sub-species of permitted forms
Other 'forms' in law may convey interests/rights but NOT included due to inapplicability of ejusdem generis
Consideration is must even in non-monetary form — Schedule I only eclipses absence of consideration
Transaction must be "in course or furtherance of business"
5.4.2 — Risks and Rewards | Section 55
⬆ Index
RISKS AND REWARDS — SECTION 55
Section 55 — Rights and liabilities of buyer and seller — nemo dat quod non habet baked in
SECTION 55(2)
"The seller shall be deemed to contract with the buyer that the interest which the seller professes to transfer to the buyer subsists and that he has power to transfer the same."
Nemo dat quod non habet = one cannot give what one does not have — baked into Section 55(2). Seller impliedly warrants that he has the interest/title to convey.
📝 When a Seller sells property — the law implies a warranty: 'I have this title and I have the power to transfer it.' If this is false (e.g., title has defects, there are encumbrances) — buyer has remedies under this implied warranty without needing to separately prove fraud.
ACCOUNTANTS vs LAW — RISKS AND REWARDS
What accountants call 'passing of risks and rewards attendant to ownership' — is succinctly laid out in Section 55 — providing enforceable terms that govern contractual relations overriding any innovative contract-writing.
Understanding must be compared with Contract Law and Sale of Goods Act to confirm harmony of first principles in private matters' legislations where strict interpretation applies.
💡 GST Point: Under Ind AS, revenue recognition depends on 'transfer of risks and rewards.' But for GST — time of supply is governed by the TPA/Contract law framework — not accounting. Accountants may recognise revenue at project completion but GST supply may have occurred at JDA registration date or booking date. These are different timelines.
5.5 — Mortgage | Nature, Types & GST Implications
⬆ Index
MORTGAGE — LOSS OF TITLE BY TRANSFER OF INTEREST
Mortgage conveys 'interests' to Mortgagee — Mortgagor retains title — relationship is always debtor-creditor
STRUCTURE
Mortgagor = one who holds title and offers as security (Transferor)
Mortgagee = one who advances money and receives property as security (Transferee)
Relationship between parties = ALWAYS debtor-creditor
Title can be LOST in mortgage by default — to this extent relevant in GST to admit principles about Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act.
SIX TYPES OF MORTGAGE
TypeTitlePossessionKey FeatureGST Note
(a) Simple MortgageOnly Interest (not title)Remains with MortgagorMortgagee has right to dispose property to recover debtSale under lawful authority — title in name of Mortgagor
(b) Mortgage by Conditional SaleLess than absolute (only rights)Remains with MortgagorCondition (repayment by date) voids sale — no retransfer neededSatisfaction of condition voids mortgage — not a supply
(c) Usufructuary MortgageTitle remains with MortgagorGiven to MortgageeMortgagee lets out property and recoup interest/principal from rentsGST issue: Imputed rent from Mortgagee to Mortgagor (Sec 65A) + actual rent from Mortgagee to third-parties — TWO separate GST events. Possession gained by Mortgage NOT by lease.
(d) English MortgageAbsolute but with retransfer obligation on repaymentNot with MortgageeMortgagor liable to debt — subject to retransfer without demur on repaymentTitle transfers to Mortgagee — if default, title perfected
(e) Deposit of Title DeedsInterest only (no document)Remains with MortgagorAct of depositing title deeds creates interest — registration not required (Section 59)No registration requirement — but no absolute title transfer
(f) Anomalous MortgageMortgage is to immovable property what pledge/pawn is to movable property. Mortgages governed by TPA; pledge/pawn by Contract law.
ACCOUNTING ANOMALY — GST IMPACT
Mortgagor (borrower) retains 'title' to property — recorded as asset and debt as loan-term borrowings.
Mortgagee (lender) retains 'interest' in property — NOT recorded as asset but debt advanced as current asset.
In Usufructuary Mortgage: Mortgagee gains by letting out property (possessory rights) for rent/profit. For GST: imputed value of rent due from Mortgagee to Mortgagor + actual value of rent earned by Mortgagee from third-parties = TWO GST events. Possession by Mortgagee NOT by way of lease (the only form of supply that must be shown to attract incidence of tax). Possession gained by Mortgage → cannot impute GST on costs/accessions made during possession.
📝 Key GST point on usufructuary mortgage: When Mortgagee holds possession and lets out property — the rent received is income from possessory rights under mortgage — NOT under a lease. Since the 'form of supply' (lease) is not present — the Schedule II entry for 'renting of immovable property' may not apply to Mortgagee's possession-based income.
CHARGE vs MORTGAGE
Charge on property = encumbrance — NOT a transfer — NOT mortgage — NOT pawn or pledge — NOT lien (Section 100).
Charge is encumbrance NOT in nature of 'interest' in property.
Repayment discharges encumbrance unless Parties intended charge to continue ('floating charge').
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 5 | Transfer of Property Act ⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, CESTAT. State them confidently and cite the authority.
Aspect Theory — Stamp Duty ≠ Immunity from GST
Province of Madras v. Boddu Paidanna AIR 1942 FC 33 | Affirmed in Atiabari Tea Co. v. State of Assam AIR 1961 SC 232 | Para 5.1
Key Ratio
Central Excise duty applies for the taxable event of manufacture whereas Sales tax applies in respect of the taxable event of sale of those manufactured articles. Although selling price may form basis for quantification of both, there is no double taxation. This is the simplest presentation of the 'aspect theory' of taxation. Payment of stamp duty in respect of transactions involving immovable property does not procure immunity from the incidence of GST without showing that these transactions fall within one of the three situations listed for definitive exclusion.
When to Use — When assessee argues 'stamp duty paid therefore GST not applicable.' Cite to establish: two different taxes on two different aspects — stamp duty on instrument, GST on consideration. Three situations for definitive exclusion from GST must be shown (express levy inapplicability / Schedule III / Section 11 exemption). Stamp duty payment fulfils none of these.
Land is Only ONE Specie — Schedule III Para 5 Cannot Be Expanded
Para 5.2.8 | Immovable Property Universe Principle
Key Ratio
Land is NOT the whole of the discussion about immovable property. There are many different 'immovable properties' and land is just one of them. To equate paragraph 5, Schedule III of Central GST Act to every specie of immovable property — whether tangible or intangible — would be an attempt at expanding the words of said Schedule III. Immovable property is a universe of things and land or building are just two of the many things that make up this universe.
When to Use — When assessee claims that development rights, mining rights, fishing rights, profit-à-prendre, FSI rights etc. are all covered by Para 5 Schedule III (sale of land exclusion). Argue: Para 5 covers only 'land' and 'building' as transferred. All other species of immovable property (intangible — rights, benefits from land) must be separately examined for GST treatment. Cannot hide all immovable property dealings under Para 5.
Affixation — Manner and Purpose Test | CCE v. Solid and Correct Engineering Works
CCE v. Solid and Correct Engineering Works 2010 (252) ELT 481 (SC) | Para 5.2.2
Key Ratio
"It is nobody's case that the attachment of the plant to the foundation is meant for permanent beneficial enjoyment of either the foundation or the land in which the same is embedded." Character of the thing ('manner and purpose') determines immovability — not capability of any person or process to move it. To say that the thing affixed can be easily dismantled does not undermine the objective of its affixation. There is nothing in the 'manner and purpose' test that requires impairment on dismantling as necessary to admit permanence in its affixation.
When to Use — In disputes on whether plant/machinery is immovable property (attracting ITC restriction under Section 17(5)(d)) or movable property (ITC allowable). Cite to argue: Purpose of bolting DG/plant to foundation is operational stability — not permanent beneficial enjoyment of land. Therefore = movable property, not immovable. ITC allowable.
Standing Timber — State of MP v. Orient Paper Mills | Royalty = Price for Timber
State of MP v. Orient Paper Mills Ltd. AIR 1977 SC 687 (SC) | Para 5.2.3
Key Ratio
"We are satisfied that despite its description, the deed confers in truth and substance a right to cut and carry timber of specified species. Till the trees are cut, they remain the property of the owner. Once the trees are severed, the property passes. The 'Royalty' is a feudalistic euphemism for the 'price' of the timber. The question is not so much as to what nomenclature would aptly describe the deed but as to whether the deed results in sale of trees after they are cut. The answer has to be in the affirmative."
When to Use — In forest/timber royalty disputes where Revenue characterises royalty as 'service' or 'licence fee.' Argue: royalty for timber rights = price for goods (timber once cut and severed). Once trees become standing timber (ready for felling) — they are movable property (goods). Tax treatment should be as goods supply, not service.
Profit-à-prendre — Lease vs Right to Take Away | Shantabai v. State of Bombay
Shantabai v. State of Bombay AIR 1958 SC 532 | Para 5.2.7
Key Ratio
"In a lease, one enjoys the property but has no right to take it away. In a profit à prendre one has a licence to enter on the land, not for the purpose of enjoying it, but for removing something from it, namely, a part of the produce of the soil." Profit-à-prendre is the right to take away and not the chattel taken away. This right of taking away (before anything is taken away) is intangible immovable property that can lawfully be transferred.
When to Use — In mining/quarrying/fishing rights disputes. Where Revenue treats these rights as 'services' subject to GST as licence/permission. Argue: profit-à-prendre = intangible immovable property. Transfer of such rights = transfer of immovable property — requires registration — unregistered arrangements are void. GST on 'transfer of immovable property' must be examined under the immovable property framework, not as routine service supply.
Agreement to Sell Creates NO Interest — Apartment Booking Premium is Taxable
Section 54 TPA | Para 5.4.1 | Examples 377, 390 | GST — Schedule III Para 5
Key Ratio / Principle
Agreement to sell land gives no 'interest' to Buyer even if the agreement is registered. Agreement to Sell by itself does not create any interest, right or title to Buyer. Agreement-holders have no 'interest' in the immovable property, whether in existence or yet to be constructed. Customer booking of apartment before OC in a real estate project — having no 'interest' in apartment not yet in existence — cannot assert to transfer any immovable property — is void and non est. Premium collected on such transfer is NOT excluded from incidence of tax due to paragraph 5, Schedule III of Central GST Act.
When to Use — When assessee claims that apartment booking premium is excluded from GST under Schedule III Para 5 (sale of building). Cite to establish: (a) Apartment does not exist at booking stage; (b) Agreement to Sell creates no interest in property; (c) No valid transfer of immovable property; (d) Premium is for construction service — fully taxable. Para 5 Schedule III exclusion applies only after OC/CC obtained and Sale Deed registered.
Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet — Baked into Section 55(2) TPA
Section 55(2) TPA | CIT v. Motor and General Stores (P.) Ltd. AIR 1968 SC 200 | Para 5.4.2
Key Ratio
The seller shall be deemed to contract with the buyer that the interest which the seller professes to transfer to the buyer subsists and that he has power to transfer the same. Nemo dat quod non habet — one cannot give what one does not have — is baked into Section 55(2) of Transfer of Property Act. Holder of lesser right cannot purport to confer a greater right. Holder of lesser right can acquire the remainder to perfect title.
When to Use — Where GPA-holder or Developer (without proper title) executes Sale Deed claiming to transfer title. Cite nemo dat: GPA-holder has no title to convey. Any 'transfer' by GPA-holder = void as the interest does not subsist in GPA-holder. Gains by such GPA-holder cannot claim Schedule III Para 5 exclusion. (Eg 391: GPA paid ₹10 Cr to Owner, executed Sale Deed for ₹15 Cr to Buyer — GST payable on ₹15 Cr without Schedule III benefit.)
Benefits from Land — Right in Present — Mining Rights as Intangible Immovable Property
Para 5.2.6 | Ananda Behara v. State of Orissa AIR 1956 SC 17 (referenced in Chapter 6)
Key Principle
Benefits 'to arise' from land refers to future benefits — what lies in the present is the 'rights to those benefits' — an intangible right in present that is enforceable. This 'right in present' to 'enjoy in future' is the only kind of transfer that Transfer of Property Act permits for future benefits attached to immovable property. Uncertainty of yield but certainty of exclusive rights to exploit resources from land = transfer of rights to future goods being benefits to arise from land = intangible immovable property rights.
When to Use — Where Revenue demands GST on grant of mining lease, fishing rights, or other natural resource exploitation rights as 'service' or 'licence.' Argue: these are 'benefits to arise from land' — the rights granted are intangible immovable property — transfer requires registration — Schedule III treatment for immovable property must be examined. Cannot simply classify as 'service supply of royalty/licence.'
Section 6 — Objects of Transfer Must Exist | Non-Est Principle | JDA Sub-contracting
Section 6 TPA | Para 5.3.4 | Examples 374, 384, 385
Key Principle
Any claim to property or right that cannot be transferred per Section 6 will not be actionable claim. Transfer of interest of Developer 1 in an earlier joint-development agreement in favour of Developer 2 is void without a registered amendment deed to original JDA. Innovative contract-writing that causes interests to be created in the future which are void ab initio. The list in Section 6 illuminates the ingredients needed to constitute a valid transfer — innovative contract-writing will come to naught if the ostensible object of contract comes within the list in Section 6, especially in arrangement to enter into joint-development of immovable property.
When to Use — Where Developer attempts to transfer JDA rights to Sub-Developer without registered amendment. Cite Eg 384: Transfer void without registered amendment. Sub-Developer's supply is therefore to the Developer (not Landowner) — different GST chain. Also use when Revenue tries to demand GST on 'transfer' of non-transferable leasehold rights — cite Eg 385: non-transferable rights cannot be the object of a valid transfer — no GST supply arises.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | GST Litigation Practice
📖 Ch 5b — Transfer of Property Act (Subrogation, Lease, Exchange, Gift)

⚖️ Chapter 5 — Transfer of Property Act

Subrogation (§5.5) | Lease (§5.6) | Exchange (§5.7) | Gift (§5.8) | Assignment (§5.9) | Para 5.5–5.9 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root
L1 Branch
Valid / Permitted
Void / Prohibited
Condition / If / Unless
Alternative / Optional
Neutral / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
5.5 Subrogation
5.6.1 Lease — Formation
5.6.2 Possession vs License
5.6.3 License
5.6.4 Consideration — Premium & Rent
5.6.5 Non-Payment
5.6.6 Lease-Free Usage
5.6.7 Sub-Lease
5.6.8 Sub-Lease by Assignment
5.6.9 Death of Lessee
5.6.10 Settlement on Determination
5.6.11 Lease-cum-Sale
5.6.12 Transferable Interest
5.6.13 Non-Transferable Interest
5.7 Exchange — Section 118
5.8.1 Gift — Nature of Transfer
5.8.2 Non-Monetary Consideration
5.8.3 Property in Gift
5.8.4 Title
5.8.5 CSR
5.8.6 Grant
5.9.1 Assignment — Purpose
5.9.2 Novation
5.9.3 Endorsement
5.9.4 Loss Payee
5.9.5 Nominee
5.9.6 Subrogation (Assignment)
5.9.7 Actionable Claims
5.9.8 Intellectual Property
5.9.9 Prohibition
5.9.10 Consideration for Assignment
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📌 PARA 5.5 — SUBROGATION
5.5 — Subrogation — Section 92 TPA ⇧ Index
🔄 Subrogation — Section 92 | Stepping into Mortgagee's Shoes
📝 Subrogation = when someone pays off a debt on another's behalf and then steps into the original lender's position — acquiring all rights, remedies and safeguards the lender had. The person who redeems the mortgage gets the same rights as the mortgagee whose mortgage he redeemed.
SECTION 92 Any person referred to in Section 91 (other than mortgagor) and any co-mortgagor shall, on redeeming property subject to mortgage, have — as regards redemption, foreclosure or sale of such property — the same rights as the mortgagee whose mortgage he redeems may have against the mortgagor or any other mortgagee. The right conferred = right of subrogation.
WHO CAN SUBROGATE
Person who advances money with which mortgage is redeemed → subrogated to mortgagee's rights if mortgagor has by registered instrument agreed that such person shall be so subrogated
📝 Only one who has 'interest' in the property can avail right of subrogation on redemption. Stranger or person without locus does NOT get subrogation even if they pay off the debt.
💡 Eg.392: Insurer paying off cost of repairs avails title to wreck.

💡 Eg.393: Company paying off repairs from accident caused by Employee's negligence = only right of recovery (no subrogation — no interest in vehicle).

💡 Eg.394: Unsecured hand-loan taken to redeem a mortgage or pledge = does NOT cease being unsecured — no right of subrogation avails.
WHO CANNOT SUBROGATE
Person with obligation to redeem → merely performs what they were already obliged to do
Person acting voluntarily, charitably, or for other reasons
Stranger without locus standi
📝 Three conditions to subrogate: (i) have locus standi (ii) not have prior obligation so to do (iii) act in expectation of this right — not voluntarily or charitably.
GST NOTE All interests (to property) pass via subrogation but subrogation is NOT one of the 'forms' of supply in Section 7 of Central GST Act. Purposive construction is not allowed in GST which is exclusively reserved for welfare matters' legislations. Subrogation in context of sale of goods and insurance — particularly relevant in GST disputes.
📌 PARA 5.6 — LEASE
5.6.1 — Lease — Formation | Section 105 ⇧ Index
🏠 Lease — Section 105 | 6 Essential Elements
