📝 Lease = separating the 'right to use' from absolute ownership. Lessor keeps title and reversionary right — but carves out the right to use and transfers it to Lessee exclusively for a certain time. This 'carve out' is called the 'demise'.
SECTION 105 Lease of immovable property = transfer of right to enjoy such property, made for certain time (express/implied/perpetuity), in consideration of price paid or promised, or money/share of crops/service or any other thing of value, to be rendered periodically or on specified occasions by transferee, who accepts transfer on such terms.
1. LESSOR
Transferor (owner) who retains title and reversionary right
2. LESSEE
Transferee who accepts the demise and holds possessory right
3. DEMISE
The 'carve out' from Lessor's absolute title — the right to use — transferred to Lessee. Object of lease = demise, not property
4. TERM
Certain time — lease for uncertain time = lifetime of Lessee determines duration
5. PREMIUM
One-time price for interest in demise = salami. Accrues on date of transfer. Non-refundable.
6. RENT
Recurring payments for user of property. Accrues over duration. Payable while Lessee in actual occupation.
Deliberations on Lease — 5 Tests (though Act pertains to immovable property):
(i) Number of persons involved
(ii) Formation — express or implied
(iii) Ostensible object of contract
(iv) Test of possessory rights transferred
(v) Consideration
5.6.2 — Possession — Lease vs License vs Permission ⇧ Index
🔑 Possession — The Core Test of Lease | Interest in rem
📝 To constitute a lease, the object of transfer must be to permit 'possessory rights' — and nothing else. Everything else (all other rights of ownership) remains with Lessor. Lease is not a mere contract — it creates an 'interest' in the property enforceable against the whole world.
LEASE = POSSESSION
Lessee has possessory right + interest in rem (against whole world including Lessor)
Lessor excluded from possession during lease term
Lease is heritable (can be inherited)
📝 Even perpetual lease does NOT convert to absolute title — Lessor retains reversionary right. Lessee's right is possessory, not ownership.
LICENSE = PERMISSION (NOT POSSESSION)
Ingress/egress granted — no demise — no 'carve out' from Lessor's title
Appearance of enjoyment of benefits attendant to demise does NOT render license into lease
Lease vs license: discerned from terms of contract, NOT appearance or assertions
💡 Eg.395: Caterer entering Customer's property for event = granted 'permission' to enter and 'use' — NOT 'possession'.

💡 Eg.396: Persons entering cinema theatre = granted 'permission' to enter — do NOT get 'possession' of seat they occupy.
JOINT LESSEE RULE Two transfers of the SAME demise to two SEPARATE Lessees is not possible. Either: (a) latter lease is void — Lessor was denuded by first lease, OR (b) former lease terminated (if terms permit) so second Lessee can get same demise.
5.6.3 — License vs Lease | 5 Principles to Differentiate ⇧ Index
🔓 License — Permission Without Demise
📝 Where possession is NOT granted, Lessor's title remains absolute — there is no 'carve out'. Any ingress or egress granted is a 'license'. This is critical in GST — lease (of immovable property) is exempt in many cases; license is taxable.
5 Principles to Differentiate Lease from License
#PrincipleWhat to Look For
(a)Intention of PartiesSubstance of document examined to locate 'object of contract' — not form or appearance
(b)Interests, Rights & TitleIntention tested on interests sought to be transferred AND those retained
(c)Enforceable InterestValidate if interest created is enforceable without great deal of evidence — Parties do not contest existence of 'demise'
(d)ExclusivityExclusivity of 'right' against whole world AND exclusivity of 'interest' against Transferor (Lessor)
(e)Nothing ContraryNothing exists indicating intention was otherwise — contrary assertions, if any, not sustainable in law
💡 Eg.397: Hire of truck on exclusive basis for fixed price + fixed duration + price payable even when truck idle = lease (not goods transport service).

💡 Eg.398: Hotel accommodation for 10 days including 2 days outstation travel = NOT lease of property (no demise).

💡 Eg.399: Issuing book from library = permission to read, NOT right to copyright of author.

💡 Eg.400: Paying guest (PG) = NOT lease but license.

💡 Eg.401: Serviced apartment = NOT lease but license.

💡 Eg.402: Operator of PG or serviced apartment may acquire 'possessory rights' under a lease — and allow occupants under a license.

💡 Eg.403: Occupation of co-working spaces = under a license.

💡 Eg.404: Licensee under void contract = may be in adverse possession. But Lessee on termination of lease = answerable for holding-over and tenancy-at-sufferance.
5.6.4 — Consideration — Premium vs Rent | CIT v. Panbari Tea ⇧ Index
💰 Consideration in Lease — Premium (Salami) vs Rent
📝 Lease has two kinds of money consideration — Premium (one-time, for the interest in demise) and Rent (recurring, for the user of property). These are legally distinct, have different GST treatment, and different consequences on non-payment.
FeaturePREMIUM (Salami)RENT
NatureOne-time price for interest in the demise — non-recurringRecurring payment for user of property
When AccruesOn date of transfer (execution of lease)Rateably over duration — provided Lessee in actual occupation
Non-PaymentAborts contract of transfer of lease — no demiseGives cause of action for termination due to breach by Lessee
Refundable?Non-refundable (unless mortgage — debt is repayable)Not applicable (recurring obligation)
Security Deposit / Advance RentNOT Premium — returnable / pre-paymentAdvance Rent = pre-payment. Security deposit = NOT consideration
RenewalFresh Premium required for second leaseRent applicable under both first and second lease
GST Time of SupplyTriggered on execution (instantaneous)Periodic — accrues as Lessee uses property
SALAMI INDICIA (CIT v. Panbari Tea / Raja Bahadur Kamakshya Narain Singh of Ramgarh)
(1) Single non-recurring character AND (2) payment prior to creation of tenancy. Consideration paid by tenant for being let into possession — can be neither rent nor revenue — it is a capital receipt in the hands of the landlord.
GST CRITICAL Purported lease without Premium but only Rent = NOT a lease but a rental arrangement akin to license where only permission for access and use is allowed. Agreement to lease which does not convey the right and interest (demise) = promise in present to enter into (contract of) lease in future.

Rent includes: collection charges, postage, property upkeep, Lessor's staff salary, property tax, any enhancement from time to time — if non-payment of these causes cessation of lease, they are Rent. If no such cessation, they are NOT Rent.
USUFRUCTUARY MORTGAGE CAUTION Transfer of possession by way of lease falls within scope of supply in Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act. Usufructuary mortgage = possession transferred, only principal repayable without interest. Both demand strict interpretation — no Court can gloss over form of transaction to impose tax treatment based on substance unless contract shown to be a sham (CIT v. Motor and General Stores ibid).
5.6.5 — Non-Payment of Rent | Bai Mamubhai Trust v. Suchitra ⇧ Index
⚠️ Non-Payment of Rent — GST Continues | Court Receiver Ruling
📝 Non-payment of rent does not excuse GST treatment — GST accrues on accrual of rent. Reduction of rent requires contractual variation. What the documents cannot deny, Parties cannot assert. When documents are undeniable, contrary assertions cannot be lawfully entertained.
NON-PAYMENT
Does NOT excuse GST treatment on accrual
Falling into arrears does NOT remove GST liability
Waiver of arrears does NOT eclipse the demise
📝 Lessor cannot demand increase in rent when Lessee's business is booming. Lessee cannot demand reduction when business suffers. Terms agreed must be respected.
REDUCTION OF RENT
Requires variation to contract (bilateral agreement)
Reduction without variation = write-off
3 essentials: (i) interests, rights and title (ii) consideration agreed (iii) documentation of original or altered terms
KEY CASE — Bai Mamubhai Trust v. Suchitra [2019] 109 taxmann.com 200
(a) Court Receiver NOT liable to obtain GST registration — amounts collected are compensation or mesne profits (para 72) — subject to eventual determination
(b) Court Receiver conducting affairs of Owner in representative capacity = liable and must comply with section 92 CGST Act
(c) Defendant (Tenant) not liable to GST on reverse charge — Court is not the supplier of immovable property
(d) GST on rent eventually recovered in suit would NOT be extinguished in hands of Plaintiff (Owner) unless decretal amount determined to be compensation/mesne profits
This decision is authority on GST liability being linked to 'nature' of payments made.
5.6.6 — Lease-Free Usage | Bilateral Waiver Test ⇧ Index
🆓 Lease-Free Period — Not Absence of Consideration | Bilateral Waiver
📝 'Lease-free period' is a misnomer if the demise already exists. If Lessee is vested with the interest, any free period is not an absence of consideration — it is either bilateral waiver of accrued consideration or a cross-cancelling supply. Absence of consideration renders the contract void ab initio.
IF DEMISE EXISTS IN PRESENT
'Lease-free period' = bilateral waiver of accrued consideration (not absence of consideration)
Could also be cross-cancelling supply with settlement
💡 Eg.405: Lease with 5-year lock-in + 3 months lease-free occupation = case of bilateral waiver.

💡 Eg.406: Lease to celebrity-Lessee without lock-in + 1 year lease-free = rental receivable being set-off with endorsement fee payable to celebrity.
IF DEMISE NOT YET PERFECTED
Lessee awaiting perfection of title by Lessor (per Section 55(2)) by date of commencement
GST treatment depends on whether demise exists in present or not
5.6.7 — Sub-Lease | Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet ⇧ Index
🔗 Sub-Lease — Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet | Lessee Cannot Give More Than He Has
📝 Nemo dat quod non habet = 'one cannot give what one does not have'. A Lessee can only sub-lease if the primary lease transferred BOTH primary right of user AND secondary right to alienate the primary right. Section 108(o) imposes an inherent restriction by way of 'description of purpose'.
LAWFUL SUB-LEASE — PRE-REQUISITES
(i) Valid primary lease not yet determined
(ii) Existence of greater interest (secondary right) under original lease
(iii) Sub-lease must be for duration 'less than or equal to' original lease
(iv) Interest returned back to Lessee on determination of sub-lease
📝 If primary lease does not transfer greater rights, even Lessor's consent outside the primary lease = variation of contract (not permission to sub-lease in law).
RESTRICTIONS ON SUB-LEASE
Section 108(o) — purpose restriction: cannot use for purpose other than that for which leased
Section 108(j) — Lessee shall NOT cease to be subject to liabilities attaching to lease by reason of transfer
Sub-lease cannot be alienation of Lessor's interests
💡 Eg.407: Lease for office = restricted from being put to use as factory.

💡 Eg.408: Lease for residence = restricted from use as hospital.

💡 Eg.409: PG = not sub-lessee but licensee.

💡 Eg.410: Serviced apartment = not sub-lessee but licensee.

💡 Eg.411: Operator of PG/serviced apartment may acquire possessory rights under a lease and allow occupants under a license — not sub-lease.
SUB-LEASE ≠ SUB-CONTRACT (WRONG COMPARISON)
(a) No rights flow from main-contractor to sub-contractor — only responsibilities and obligations flow
(b) Direction of consideration flow is OPPOSITE — main-contractor pays sub-contractor (for work done) whereas sub-lessee pays consideration to Lessee (for use of demise)
WHAT PURPORTED 'SUB-LEASE' MAY ACTUALLY BE
(a) Assignee (of Lessee) in occupation; OR
(b) Servant or nominee of Lessee with waiver by Lessor to object; OR
(c) Partnership with sub-lessee and Lessor continuing in de jure occupation through Firm

Use of expression 'sub-lease' + availability of 'consent' of Lessor do NOT ipsi dixit affirm sub-lease when it is actually assignment or partnership.
5.6.8 — Sub-Lease by Assignment of Lease ⇧ Index
📋 Sub-Lease = Assignment of Lease 'For Consideration' | GST HSN Implication
📝 Once a lease is executed, Lessor is denuded of possession. Right to use the demise held by Lessee can be enjoyed by Lessee or any nominee. Sub-lease = enjoyment by another actor 'for consideration'. The test of privity is key — sub-lessee has NO privity with Lessor.
PRIVITY CHAIN
Lessee liable to Lessor by privity of contract
Sub-lessee liable to Lessee by privity of estate (personal obligation as assignee)
NO privity between sub-lessee and Lessor
GST IMPLICATION
Sub-lease = assignment of lease 'for consideration' → must operate under a different HSN than primary lease
Errors in locating locus standi of transacting persons = defence against tax demands based entirely on flow of payments
Wrong tax paid demands right tax to be paid again — refund if admissible
📝 Sub-lessee paying Rent even directly to Lessor = discharging obligation of Lessee qua Lessor AND sub-lessee's own obligation qua Lessee (sub-lessor).
5.6.9 — Death of Lessee | Lease Cannot Survive Lifetime ⇧ Index
⚰️ Death of Lessee — Lease Terminates | GST Treatment Guided by Titles = Erroneous
📝 'Interest' acquired under a lease cannot survive the lifetime of the Lessee. Death abates all things. And when lease cannot survive Lessee's death, any sub-lease built on that primary lease also cannot survive.
DEATH OF LESSEE
Terminates the lease — interest cannot survive lifetime of Lessee
Also terminates sub-lease — existence of primary lease is must for sub-lease
Continuing sub-lessees in occupation = irregular (holding-over)
LESSOR'S DEATH
Lessor's lifetime NOT relevant — Lessor's estate is heritable
Lessee can still return interest acquired to successors-in-interest of (now deceased) Lessor
GST WARNING Tax treatment guided by titles and descriptions or even provisions of Income-Tax law (under which TDS is deducted) = erroneous. GST treatment must be guided by the nature of the demise and the legal reality of the lease arrangement.
5.6.10 — Settlement on Determination of Lease ⇧ Index
🏁 Settlement on Determination — Security Deposit, Excess Wear & Tear
📝 When lease ends, settling the security deposit and outstanding advance rent creates new rights and obligations. Deduction towards wear and tear is not admissible — but compensation for EXCESSIVE wear and tear is NOT mere compensation — it is consideration flowing under a collateral contract or new agreement.
NORMAL WEAR AND TEAR
NOT admissible as deduction
Lessee bound to restore property (Section 108(m)) subject only to changes caused by reasonable wear and tear or irresistible force
EXCESSIVE WEAR AND TEAR
NOT mere compensation — consideration under collateral contract in composite document OR new contract
New contract where 'new consideration due' settled by deduction from security deposit or advance rent returnable = GST applicable on terms of new bargain
💡 Eg.412: Deposit forfeited (accepted by Lessee) = liable to GST being collateral contract agreed in composite lease document.

💡 Eg.413: Notice-to-vacate with adjustment of rental + adjustable security deposit = immediately attracts 'time of supply' provisions — taxed without awaiting accrual of rent.

💡 Eg.414: Advance rent NOT being security deposit = already attracted 'time of supply' provisions.
5.6.11 — Lease-cum-Sale | Restricted Title Arrangements ⇧ Index
🏗️ Lease-cum-Sale — Two Possible Legal Forms
📝 'Lease-cum-sale' is a popular expression but legally it can be TWO very different arrangements — each with completely different GST consequences. The underlying contractual relations must be studied for each variant before applying tax treatment.
TYPE A — LEASE WITH OPTION TO PURCHASE
Lessee has demise but NOT title
Only AFTER stated conditions satisfied (payment + infrastructure + duration of operations) will Parties execute Sale Deed
Two transactions: (i) Lease → lesser right (ii) Sale → greater right (title + reversionary right)
Title perfected when lesser rights merge with greater rights
TYPE B — CONDITIONAL SALE
Original sale complete — title transfer to Purchaser valid
Condition subsequent: if conditions (ii), (iii), (iv) not satisfied → re-transfer title back by another sale
Discovery of violation = voids original sale
GST KEY QUESTIONS for Lease-cum-Sale / Long-term Lease
(a) Entry 41 of Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (exemption for long-term lease)
(b) Grant of development rights by owner = equated with Premium paid on long-term lease (Notification amendments)
(c) Admissibility of exclusion in paragraph 5, Schedule III CGST Act (sale of land)

All lease-cum-sale arrangements are NOT the same — tax treatment cannot be assumed without studying underlying contractual relations of each variant.
💡 Eg.420: GST paid on Premium in respect of lease of land is NOT eligible for ITC due to Section 17(5)(c) and (d) CGST Act, where land is procured for construction of factory building. But when benefits of unexpired term (remaining 45 years) are assigned for ₹45 crore consideration — GST will still be attracted, notwithstanding that credit was not availed when lease for 50 years was entered into and Premium of ₹50 crore was paid.
5.6.12 — Transferable Interest | Section 108 Rights & Liabilities ⇧ Index
🔄 Transferable Interest — Section 108 | Authority to Alienate
📝 Section 108 lays down various 'rights and liabilities' of Lessor and Lessee that illuminate the nature and extent of any authority to alienate. Tax treatment is greatly altered if authority to alienate is absent. Key clauses for GST are (d), (j), (m), (o), (p) and (q).
ClauseRight/LiabilityGST Implication
(d)Any accession to property during lease = deemed comprised in lease; reversionary interest of Lessor enhanced by accessionAccession belongs to Lessor on determination — not Lessee
(j)Lessee may transfer absolutely or by mortgage or sub-lease the whole or any part of his interest. Transferee may again transfer it. But Lessee shall NOT cease to be subject to liabilities attaching to lease by reason of such transferGST treatment of consideration must operate under different HSN for sub-lease arrangement. Sub-lease is actually assignment for consideration.
(m)Lessee bound to keep and restore property in as good condition as at time of possession — subject to reasonable wear and tear or irresistible force. Allow Lessor inspection at all reasonable times.Excess wear and tear → new contract → own GST treatment
(o)Lessee may use property as person of ordinary prudence. Must NOT use for purpose other than leased, or sell timber, pull down buildings, work mines or quarries not open at time of leaseProfit-à-prendre barred in lease simplicitor. Purpose restriction = key to GST classification
(p)Must NOT correct permanent structure without Lessor's consent (except agricultural purposes)Construction by Lessee only if expressly permitted — such additions belong to Lessor and must be handed over on determination
(q)On determination of lease, Lessee bound to put Lessor in possession of the propertyFailure to return = tenancy-at-sufferance attracting liability
NON-TRANSFERABLE — KEY RULE Leasehold rights are non-transferable to the extent that NO interest remains with Lessee. Lessee necessarily retains some small interest in the demise sufficient to answer Lessor in respect of obligations under Section 108(d) to (q). Any new arrangement between Lessee and third-Party cannot outlast term of primary lease AND must have as its object something inferior to that of object of primary lease.

Industrial lands (for development of infrastructure and production of specified industrial products): speculation by entering into new arrangement styled as 'sub-lease' = impermissible. Tax treatment applicable to such new arrangement ≠ treatment applicable to primary lease.
💡 Eg.415: Lease of school building cannot be used as hospital — undertaking to restore and return building at end of term.

💡 Eg.416: Lease of residential building cannot be used as warehouse where workmen are housed at night.

💡 Eg.417: Trees planted by Lessee pass to Lessor (just as trees planted by Lessor had passed to Lessee).

💡 Eg.418: Building constructed by Lessee must be demolished on return of Demise. With Lessor not objecting, Lessee may vacate Building which then accedes to Lessor.
5.6.13 — Non-Transferable Interest | Premium Accrues on Execution ⇧ Index
💎 Non-Transferable Interest | Premium — One-Time Capital Receipt
📝 Premium is the consideration for the interest transferred by contract of lease. It accrues instantaneously on execution — just like any consideration agreed by Promisee in any contract. Premium's deferment or amortization for accounting purposes does NOT imply it accrues proportionally over the lease term.
PREMIUM — KEY PRINCIPLES
One-time, non-recurring → completes transfer on execution
Non-refundable (unlike security deposit)
Does NOT accrue rateably over duration
Accounting amortization ≠ legal accrual
💡 Eg.419: Premium of ₹50 crore for 50-year lease = money consideration (price). If terminated after 5 years, Lessor not obliged to refund any part. Premium paid once — benefits enjoyed over 50 years.
WHEN LEASE IS CANCELLED
NO right to reclaim proportionate Premium for unexpired duration
Lessee has no surviving 'interest' to transfer — except obligation to return demise to Lessor (Section 55(2) mutatis mutandis)
To recoup opportunity loss: enter new arrangement with Lessor's consent to permit Transferee to enjoy remaining benefits
📝 Lessor's consent for new arrangement = only to examine if new arrangement violates end-use conditions fundamental to primary lease — NOT to permit 'novation' of lease (being transfer of interests in property, which is an executed contract).
📌 PARA 5.7 — EXCHANGE
5.7 — Exchange — Section 118 | Antithesis of Sale ⇧ Index
🔀 Exchange — Section 118 | Mutual Transfer of Title
📝 Exchange = two persons mutually transfer ownership of one thing for ownership of another. It is the antithesis of sale — sale requires money; exchange requires a thing (not money alone). Money can be used to balance an unequal exchange without voiding it.
SECTION 118 "Exchange" defined — When two persons mutually transfer the ownership of one thing for the ownership of another, neither thing or both things being money only, the transaction is called an "exchange". A transfer of property in completion of an exchange can be made only in the manner provided for transfer of such property by sale.
WHAT EXCHANGE CAN BE OF
(a) Tangible immovable property
(b) Intangible immovable property
Reference to 'sale' (Section 54) → exchange limited to immovable property
Jurisprudence under Section 54 applicable mutatis mutandis
📝 Exchange must be of 'title in its entirety'. Reference to 'ownership' bars exchange of mere interest/rights without transfer of title. Transferor must have necessary title to exchange — vice versa.
MONEY TO BALANCE
Exchange of one immovable property for another + some money = still valid exchange
Money to bring about 'equitable' balance = permissible
But exchange 'being money only' = NOT exchange
Money impairs 'barter' — does NOT impair 'exchange'
📝 This is strict interpretation applicable to private matters' legislation — Property law. Involvement of money in barter = invalid barter. But involvement of money in exchange = permissible (as long as both properties are transferred).
REGISTRATION Registration requirement (discussed in Chapter 6 — Registration Act) and effects of non-registration mutatis mutandis apply to exchanges. Non-registration = exchange void. GST cannot be applied on exchange where foundational document is void.
📌 PARA 5.8 — GIFT
5.8.1 — Gift — Nature of Transfer | Section 122 ⇧ Index
🎁 Gift — Section 122 | Transfer Without Consideration
📝 Gift = transfer voluntarily and without consideration. The essence of contract (consideration) is absent — so assertions about gifts can be easily tested for admissibility. Three requirements: (i) transfer (ii) accepted by Donee (iii) during lifetime of Donor.
SECTION 122 "Gift" = transfer of certain existing movable or immovable property made voluntarily and without consideration, by one person (Donor) to another (Donee), and accepted by or on behalf of Donee during the lifetime of Donor while he is still capable of giving. If Donee dies before acceptance = gift is void.
GIFT CAN BE OF
(a) Tangible immovable
(b) Intangible immovable
(c) Tangible movable
(d) Intangible movable
💡 Eg.421: Right to use (lease) can be object of gift.
Eg.422: Discharge of overdue mortgage = money gift.
Eg.423: Copyright can be object of gift.
Eg.424: Holiday package (travel and stay) can be object of gift.
HOW EFFECTED — Section 123
Immovable property gift: registered instrument signed by/on behalf of Donor + attested by at least 2 witnesses
Movable property gift: registered instrument OR delivery
NOT A GIFT
Executed gift = enforceable; cannot be revoked (Section 126)
Will = NOT gift — executory and cannot be accepted before death of Donor
Executory gifts = generally unenforceable (empty promises for no consideration)
📝 Gift must be of 'certain' property — not uncertain or mysterious. Relinquishment of right to claim any property is also 'gift' when without consideration.
TAX TREATMENT OF GIFTS Follows completed transactions since no consideration is contemplated. Completed transactions tested based on: (i) object of transfer (ii) relevance to business of Donor (so-called) (iii) form that consideration could take (iv) relations/proximity of Parties to justify 'gift' (v) most reasonable or imminent implications in case of omission of delivery by Donor.
5.8.2 — Non-Monetary Consideration | Gift = NOT a Gift ⇧ Index
⚠️ Non-Monetary Consideration — Anti-Thesis of Gift
📝 Existence of non-monetary consideration (any obligation, discharge, expectation, or compulsion) renders the gift void. Presence of any form of obligation — even discharge of obligation — upsets the assertion of voluntary contribution. Presumption LIES AGAINST gift claim when made in course of business.
CONSIDERATION IS UNDENIABLE when:
(a) Discharge from an obligation
(b) Acceptance is expectation of so-called Donor

Gratification to Donor is NOT consideration. Gratification = mental satisfaction. But any form of obligation or compulsion that is suitably discharged upsets assertion of voluntary contribution.

Absence of consideration = matter of special knowledge. Burden of proof rests on person making the assertion. Presumption lies against the assertion especially when made in course of business (Thorsen Car Ferries and Woodhouse AC Israel Cocoa Ltd.).

'Contract without consideration' is non est in law — absence of consideration and contract cannot be used in the same sentence.
💡 Eg.425: Advertisement as 'open offer' requiring Acceptor to act = enforceable contract — NOT a gift (consideration exists).

💡 Eg.426: Company's new policy announcement to gift prize to best performing Dealer = enforceable contract for consideration — NOT voluntary contribution in nature of gift.

💡 Eg.427: Physician accepting medicine samples = NOT gift.

💡 Eg.428: Pharma company distributing medicines to old-age home counting against CSR obligation = NOT gift.

💡 Eg.429: Food distributed to migrating workers = gift.

💡 Eg.430: Advertisement of 'feed-a-child' project = voluntary contribution by Donor, not by NGO organizer.

💡 Eg.431: Donor's business and names put on display = renders gift questionable.

💡 Eg.432: Free units given by OEM to Dealer = NOT gift.

💡 Eg.433: Buy-one-take-two scheme = NOT gift.
5.8.3 — Property in Gift | 5.8.4 Title | 5.8.5 CSR | 5.8.6 Grant ⇧ Index
📋 Property / Title / CSR / Grant — Gift-Related Concepts
PROPERTY (5.8.3)
Must be in 'existence' — AND 'certain' (ascertained/knowable, not mysterious)
Future property cannot be gifted
Relinquishment of right to claim any property = gift if without consideration
📝 Future goods (like Sale of Goods Act) — cannot be subject of a gift. The property must exist in the present.
TITLE (5.8.4)
Ascribing title as 'gift' = NOT determinative
Gift given in course of business = contradiction of sorts — belies very premise of 'business'
All necessary requirements for formation of gift and legal consequences must be located
📝 CSR activities: even CSR activities cannot be admitted as 'gift' — consideration flows by way of discharge of obligation (Companies Act Section 135(5)).
CSR (5.8.5)
CSR obligation = obligation whose retirement = consideration
Denial of ITC under Section 17(5)(fa) CGST Act = limited to goods/services 'used for activities relating to CSR obligation' — NOT to goods/services 'supplied' in retirement of these obligations
📝 CSR outlay in excess of threshold + without benefit of set-off with future years' obligation could probably meet definition of 'gift' — otherwise NOT a gift.
GRANT (5.8.6)
Ind AS 20 — two accounting methods: (a) Income approach (b) Capital approach
Grant is generally NOT a gift — terms of grant must be examined
Donee's actions of accepting and putting to use = Donor's expectations forming consideration for transfer
📝 Donee cannot be mere instrumentality of Donor. Donor must be beneficiary of gift. Gift may be made to a Trust — but Donor/nominees/persons acting-in-concert cannot be Trustees.
📌 PARA 5.9 — ASSIGNMENT
5.9.1 — Assignment — Purpose | Benefits of Executed Contract ⇧ Index
📋 Assignment — Section 130 | Transfer of Benefits of Executed Contract
📝 Assignment = transferring the benefits you are entitled to receive from an already-performed (executed) contract — to another person. Assignment is not merely collecting money on behalf of the Promisor — it gives the Assignee the right to enjoy those benefits themselves.
OBJECT OF ASSIGNMENT
Benefits arising from an executed contract which Promisor (Assignor) is entitled to enjoy
Assignee permitted by deed to enjoy benefits otherwise enjoyed by Promisor
NOT merely to collect consideration on behalf of Promisor
💡 Eg.436: On payment of Premium, interest in property is transferred and right of user (of that property) = the 'benefits' which form object of assignment.

💡 Eg.437: Collecting consideration from Promisee = 'collection agent' — NOT assignment of benefits of executed contract.
WHAT ASSIGNMENT IS NOT
Assignment does NOT require consent of Promisee (only novation does)
Assignment ≠ Remission of performance (Sections 63 and 41)
Assignee NOT answerable for obligations of Assignor (Promisor)
Assignment = 'form' — object and purpose of assignment remains to be discovered in each transaction
💡 Eg.438: Sub-lease of possessory rights by Lessee (with primary and secondary rights) to sub-lessee = takes form of assignment.

💡 Eg.439: Transfer of copyright from author to publisher = takes form of assignment.

💡 Eg.440: Rights on settlement of insurance claim = takes form of subrogation-cum-assignment.
ASSIGNMENT REQUIRES ALL CONTRACT INGREDIENTS Assignment is also by contract. Motivation to permit Assignee to enjoy benefits is private to Assignor — but assignment is not barred. Consideration for assignment takes forms that assignment gives to the underlying arrangement. Contracts whereby Promisor agrees to permit Assignee to enjoy benefits otherwise enjoyed by Promisor, for good and sufficient reasons — assignment is only the 'form' in which those contracts are undertaken.
PROPERTY LAW CONTEXT Entire law on assignment located in Section 130 of this Act — applicable jurisprudence mutatis mutandis guides assignment of contract for purposes of Contract law. Once obligations are performed (contract executed), all that remains is for Promisor to collect consideration from Promisee = debt (if monetary). Right to collect consideration = actionable claim.
💡 Eg.434: Contract for sale cannot be without a price.
💡 Eg.435: Contract for lease cannot waive obligations listed in Section 108.
5.9.2 — Novation — Assignment vs Novation vs Variation vs Substitution ⇧ Index
🔄 Novation — Substitution of Contract with New Parties
📝 Novation = substitution of contract with new contract AND new Parties (at least a new Promisor). The consideration for novation = discharge of the previous contract. Novation is NOT mere variation — it goes to the pith and substance of the original bargain.
Khardah Company Ltd. v. Raymon & Co. (I) (P.) Ltd. AIR 1962 SC 1810
"As assignment of a contract might result by transfer of either of the rights of the obligations thereunder. As a rule, obligations under a contract cannot be assigned except with the consent of the promise and then it is a novation resulting in substitution of liabilities. On the other hand, rights under a contract are assignable unless the contract is personal in its nature or the rights are incapable of assignment."
ConceptDefinitionConsent of Promisee?Previous Rights/Liabilities
AssignmentTransfer of benefits of executed contract to Assignee. Assignee acts 'in name of' AssignorNOT requiredRemain on original parties (Assignor continues answerable to Promisee)
NovationNew contract + new Promisor replacing original. Consideration = discharge of previous contract. Transferee in novation acts 'in own name'YES — requiredDischarged — none remain on previous Promisor
SubstitutionSame Parties replace old contract with new one for good and sufficient reasons. Pith and substance so altered it cannot be regarded as variation of originalBoth Parties must agreeNone remain on substitution
VariationAlteration of original contract without affecting core/pith/substance. Continuity maintained — rights and liabilities accrued to date of variation remainBoth Parties must agreeAccrue up to date of variation; intact for the rest
SECTION 62 — Indian Contract Act Effect of novation, rescission, and alteration of contract — If the parties to a contract agree to substitute a new contract for it, or to rescind or alter it, the original contract need not be performed.
5.9.3 — Endorsement | 5.9.4 Loss Payee | 5.9.5 Nominee ⇧ Index
📄 Endorsement, Loss Payee & Nominee — Related to Assignment
ENDORSEMENT (5.9.3)
Negotiation of enforceable claims (cheques, drafts, bill of lading, railway receipt) = accepted mode of authorizing Attorney to give valid discharge to Promisee
Assignment permitted only by Promisor endorsement = another form of assignment
Object involved = actionable claim
💡 Eg.441: Factoring services involves assignment of receivables.
Eg.442: Subrogation involves assignment.
Eg.443: Insurance policies are assigned along with claim.
LOSS PAYEE (5.9.4)
Designating any person as 'loss payee' in insurance policy (before claim) = assignment of benefits (compensation) from policy to be retained and enjoyed by Assignee
Lender may demand to be named as loss payee where asset is financed
Insurer = Assignee of benefits from executed insurance policy
NOMINEE (5.9.5)
Nomination = Agency — NOT assignment
Nominee is mere Receiver — NOT Assignee
Receiver can give valid discharge to Promisee
Receiver must account for receipts to Promisor
Servant appointed = NOT assignment — Promisor accepts through Servant's instrumentality
Servant NOT entitled to retain benefits
📝 Endorsement = appoints nominee (not assignment of benefits of executed contract). Nominee receives on behalf of Promisor — does not become Assignee entitled to keep what is received.
5.9.6 — Subrogation (in Assignment context) | 5.9.7 Actionable Claims ⇧ Index
⚖️ Subrogation + Actionable Claims — Assignment Framework
SUBROGATION IN ASSIGNMENT (5.9.6)
Subrogation = itself NOT assignment but operates along with assignment
Equitable remedy — substantive law located in Section 92 TPA and in Insurance law
Subrogation allows stepping into shoes of another's rights — operates alongside assignment where benefits are also transferred
ACTIONABLE CLAIMS (5.9.7)
Transfer of actionable claims = takes 'form' of assignment
Promisor permits Assignee to enjoy benefits attendant to actionable claim
Assignment pre-supposes another contractual privity between Assignor and Assignee
Two contracts involved: (a) Promisor-Promisee (b) Promisor-Assignee
NO contractual privity between Assignee and Promisee
📝 Assignment is merely the 'form' — the object and nature of actionable claim (addressed in Section 3 of this Act) determines the GST treatment.
5.9.8 — Intellectual Property | 5.9.9 Prohibition | 5.9.10 Consideration ⇧ Index
💡 IPR Assignment, Prohibition & Consideration
IPR (5.9.8)
Copyrights, Patents = cannot be separated from owner
Assignment for consideration = 'form' in which holders 'transfer' IPRs in favour of Buyer for consideration
Salary paid to employees may already include consideration towards any IPRs developed during employment
💡 Eg.444: Contract of sale between Author and Publisher = actually an assignment in respect of copyright in the publication.

💡 Eg.445: Goodwill transferred with business = takes with it assignment of trademarks representing that goodwill.
PROHIBITION (5.9.9)
Assignment imposes prohibition on holders of certain IPRs — assurance that Transferors will operate within the prohibition makes assignment effective
Prohibition may operate as collateral contract where primary contract is brand endorsement
💡 Eg.446: Author undertakes NOT to reproduce artistic/literary work for any other publisher.

💡 Eg.447: Inventor undertakes prohibition not to exploit invention elsewhere.

Note: Prohibition may be primary contract for brand endorsement — additional consideration paid to abstain from endorsing competitor's offerings.
CONSIDERATION (5.9.10)
"Rose by any other name, would smell just as sweet"
Consideration for assignment = properly characterized based on object of transfer in deed of assignment
💡 Eg.448: Consideration for assignment of IRP = royalty.

💡 Eg.449: Consideration for assignment of possessory rights = sub-lease rental.

💡 Eg.450: Consideration for assignment of mineral ore deposits = royalty.

💡 Eg.451: Consideration for accepting a prohibition = 'agreeing to the obligation to refrain from an act, or to tolerate an act or a situation......'
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 5 | Transfer of Property Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
CIT v. Panbari Tea Co. Ltd. AIR 1965 SC 1871 | Raja Bahadur Kamakshya Narain Singh of Ramgarh v. CIT [1943] 11 ITR 513 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | Applicable in all GST disputes on Premium (Salami) vs Rent distinction
Key Ratio / Principle
The indicia of salami are (1) its single non-recurring character and (2) payment prior to the creation of the tenancy. It is the consideration paid by the tenant for being let into possession and can be neither rent nor revenue but is a capital receipt in the hands of the landlord.
When to Use — (i) When Department demands GST on the 'Premium' (salami) portion of a lease treating it as 'rent' and arguing it should be taxed as recurring rent. Cite Panbari Tea to establish Premium is a one-time capital receipt — not periodic rent. (ii) When determining the time of supply for long-term lease Premium — it accrues instantaneously on execution, not rateably over the term. (iii) In disputes about Notification 12/2017-Central Tax entry 41 where Premium on long-term lease of industrial plots is involved.
Bai Mamubhai Trust v. Suchitra [2019] 109 taxmann.com 200/2019 (31) GSTL 193 (Bom.)
Bombay High Court | Applicable in GST disputes involving Court-appointed receivers, mesne profits, tenancy-at-sufferance
Key Ratio / Principle
Court Receiver is not liable to obtain GST registration and discharge applicable GST since the amounts collected are not in the nature of lease rental but in the nature of compensation or mesne profits (para 72), subject to eventual determination on disposal of suit, and there can be no notional contract imputed between Court Receiver and Tenant (para 75). Court Receiver would, however, be liable to pay GST had the Court Receiver conducted the affairs of Plaintiff (Owner) in representative capacity. This decision is not an authority to eclipse section 92 of Central GST Act but an authority on GST liability being linked to 'nature' of payments made.
When to Use — (i) When GST is demanded on Court Receiver collections — argue these are mesne profits/compensation, not rent — GST liability linked to nature of payments. (ii) When distinguishing 'rent' from 'compensation for use and occupation' in landlord-tenant disputes before FAA/GSTAT. (iii) When arguing that Tenant is NOT liable to GST on reverse charge since Court is not the supplier of immovable property lease.
Khardah Company Ltd. v. Raymon & Co. (I) (P.) Ltd. AIR 1962 SC 1810
Supreme Court of India | Applicable in Novation, Assignment, and Sub-Lease GST disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
As assignment of a contract might result by transfer of either of the rights of the obligations thereunder. As a rule, obligations under a contract cannot be assigned except with the consent of the promisee and then it is a novation resulting in substitution of liabilities. On the other hand, rights under a contract are assignable unless the contract is personal in its nature or the rights are incapable of assignment.
When to Use — (i) When Department argues that a change in contractual parties constitutes 'novation' attracting fresh GST liability. Cite Khardah to distinguish: if only rights are transferred = assignment (no fresh GST liability on Assignor); if obligations also transferred with Promisee's consent = novation (fresh contract, fresh GST treatment). (ii) In IBC resolution plan disputes — whether assignment of rights under contracts in resolution plan = novation (requiring all creditors' consent) or mere assignment (not requiring consent). (iii) In sub-lease disputes to show sub-lease = assignment for consideration with its own GST HSN.
Section 92 TPA — Subrogation Doctrine | GST Non-Applicability
Statutory Provision — Transfer of Property Act, 1882 | Applicable in Insurance, Sale of Goods, and Mortgage disputes
Key Principle
All interests (to property) pass via subrogation but subrogation is NOT one of the 'forms' of supply in section 7 of Central GST Act. Section 7 is not allowed purposive construction which is exclusively reserved for welfare matters' legislations or welfare aspects residing in private matters' legislations such as tax or Contract law or Transfer of Property Act. For subrogation right to arise: person entering to redeem must (i) have locus standi (ii) not have prior obligation so to do and (iii) act in expectation (express or implied) of this right and not voluntarily or charitably.
When to Use — (i) When Department demands GST on insurer's subrogation recovery — argue subrogation is NOT one of the 8 forms of supply; purposive construction of Section 7 is not permissible. (ii) When challenging the levy of GST on insurance recoveries from third parties. (iii) When advising clients in financing arrangements where loss-payee designation is used — to clarify that compensation flow via subrogation is not a taxable supply.
Lease vs License — 5 Principles | Section 105 TPA
Transfer of Property Act, 1882 | Applicable in ALL renting of immovable property disputes — commercial, residential, co-working, hotel, hospital
Key Principle
To constitute a lease, the object of transfer must be to permit 'possessory rights' and nothing else — everything else, being all other rights of ownership, must remain with Lessor. Lease is not a mere contract but an 'interest in rem' — against the whole world. Where possession is not granted, Lessor's title remains absolute — any ingress or egress granted is a 'license'. Whether an arrangement is one of license or lease must be discerned from the terms of contract and not from its appearance or any misinformed assertion by the Transferee. Titles and descriptions are not determinative of the real object of contract. Procedure for termination of contract and remedies available on termination is a good test of the nature of interests, rights and title that was held.
When to Use — (i) When GST exemption for 'renting of immovable property for residential purposes' is contested — argue presence/absence of demise. (ii) When hotel/PG/co-working arrangements are classified as 'lease' for Schedule III exclusion — argue these are licenses (no demise, no possessory rights). (iii) When challenging GST on operator of serviced apartment / PG claiming 'lease' arrangements — show arrangement is sub-licensing, not sub-lease. (iv) In any SCN reply where the nature of the arrangement (lease vs license) is the foundational question for taxability or exemption.
Gift — Consideration Test | Thorsen Car Ferries | Woodhouse AC Israel Cocoa Ltd.
Common Law Principle | Applicable in GST disputes on free samples, CSR activities, business promotion gifts, OEM-dealer schemes
Key Principle
Absence of consideration being a matter of special knowledge, burden of proof rests on person making such assertion. In fact, presumption lies against such assertion, especially when made in the course of business. Absence of consideration and contract cannot be used in the same sentence because 'contract without consideration' is non est in law. Existence of any form of obligation or compulsion that is suitably discharged upsets assertion about voluntary contribution. Gift given in the course of business is a contradiction of sorts — it belies the very premise of 'business'. CSR activities undertaken by a corporate entity cannot be admitted as 'gift' for the reason that consideration flows by way of discharge of obligation albeit under Companies Act.
When to Use — (i) When Supplier claims 'gift' to avoid GST on free samples — argue presumption lies against, especially in business context — obligation to distribute samples or promotional items is itself consideration. (ii) When OEM gives 'free units' to dealers claiming gift — cite that commercial motivation/obligation upsets gift claim. (iii) When disputing Schedule I para 2 applicability to 'gifts' between related parties — use this principle to test whether underlying transaction is truly voluntary and without consideration. (iv) CSR — ITC restriction under Section 17(5)(fa) applies to goods 'used for activities relating to CSR obligation' — NOT to goods 'supplied' in retirement of CSR obligations (cite the distinction).
Non-Transferable Interest in Lease | Section 108(j) TPA | Eg.420
Transfer of Property Act, 1882 | Applicable in Long-term Lease, Industrial Plot Sub-lease, SEZ, MIDC disputes
Key Principle
Leasehold rights are non-transferable to the extent that no interest remains with Lessee. Lessee necessarily retains some small interest in the demise sufficient to answer Lessor in respect of obligations under section 108(d) to (q). As such, leasehold rights are not alienable in their entirety and any new arrangement between Lessee and third-Party cannot outlast term of primary lease. Where industrial lands are leased for development of infrastructure and production of specified industrial products, speculation by entering into new arrangement styled as 'sub-lease' is impermissible. Terms of this new arrangement will attract tax treatment applicable to such new arrangement and not mimic the treatment (incidence and benefit of input tax credit) applicable to primary lease. GST paid on Premium in respect of lease of land may not be eligible for ITC (Eg.420) — but assignment of unexpired term for consideration still attracts GST.
When to Use — (i) When MIDC/GIDC/industrial plot lessees seek to 'sub-lease' and claim the same GST treatment as original lease — argue this is a new arrangement with different HSN and different tax treatment. (ii) When Lessee assigns benefits of long-term lease for consideration — GST is attracted on consideration for such assignment even if ITC was not availed on original lease Premium. (iii) When disputing ITC reversal on long-term lease Premium — argue Section 17(5)(c)/(d) applies only to goods, not services; use clause (j) of Section 108 to show Lessee's right to transfer was part of the interest originally transferred.
Exchange — Section 118 TPA | Non-Monetary Immovable Property Transfer
Transfer of Property Act, 1882 | Applicable in JDA (Joint Development Agreement), Land for Apartment swap disputes
Key Principle
Exchange = mutual transfer of ownership of one thing for ownership of another, neither thing or both things being money only. Exchange must be of 'title in its entirety' — reference to 'ownership' bars exchange of mere interest or rights without transfer of title. Exchange is limited to immovable property since reference to 'sale' demands applicability of Section 54 jurisprudence mutatis mutandis. Requirement of registration and effects of non-registration mutatis mutandis apply to exchanges. Money paid to balance an unequal exchange does not impair validity of the exchange (unlike barter where money involvement impairs validity).
When to Use — (i) In JDA disputes where Landowner 'exchanges' land for constructed apartments — argue this is an 'exchange' of immovable property for immovable property — Section 118 governs — full transfer of title in land against full transfer of title in apartments. (ii) When challenging GST on land-for-apartment swaps — if the exchange is registered, both transfers are of immovable property and sale of land (Schedule III) vs sale of under-construction apartments (taxable) must be separated. (iii) When arguing that money paid to balance an exchange (such as Landowner paying part cash in addition to land for disproportionate area of apartments) does not change the fundamental nature of the transaction as an exchange.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 6 — Registration Act

⚖️ Chapter 6 — Registration Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
6.1 Relevance to GST
6.1A Ananda Behara Case
6.2.1 Statutory Definition of Immovable Property
6.2.2 Nature of Right
6.2.3 Dating Back
6.2.4 Holding Out
6.2.5 Evidentiary Value
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
6.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
⚖️ Registration Act — Relevance to GST
§17(1)(b) | Compulsory Registration of Documents
Non-registration of compulsorily registrable documents
is FATAL to the anticipated contractual relations
📝 If a document that must be registered by law is left unregistered — the contract it creates simply does not exist in law. There are no rights, no obligations, no remedies.
STATUTE
Section 17(1) — Documents which registration is compulsory
(if property situate in India, executed on/after commencement of Act)
§17(a)
Instruments of gift of immovable property
📝 Even if you gift land/building — it must be in writing & registered, else gift is invalid.
§17(b)
Non-testamentary instruments that create/declare/assign/limit/ extinguish any right/title/interest in immovable property ≥ ₹100
📝 Any sale deed, assignment, lease (over ₹100) of land/building must be registered. Most GST real-estate disputes arise here.
§17(c)
Instruments acknowledging receipt/payment of consideration for creation/assignment/extinction of such right
📝 A receipt/acknowledgement for payment towards an immovable property deal — also needs registration.
§17(d)
Leases of immovable property — year to year, term exceeding 1 year, or reserving yearly rent
📝 Any lease for more than 1 year must be registered. Short-term monthly/quarterly leases may escape. Critical for GST on renting of immovable property.
💡 A 3-year commercial shop lease on unregistered agreement → Schedule III exclusion (immovable property) cannot be claimed; GST implications open.
GST LINK
Tax treatment — exclusion u/s Schedule III Para 5 or limitation u/s Schedule II Para 5(b) — cannot be lightly applied or assumed without valid registered contract.
Contractual relationships cannot be assumed without giving due attention to registration.
6.1A — Ananda Behara v. State of Orissa | AIR 1956 SC 17 ⇧ Index
🏛️ Ananda Behara v. State of Orissa
AIR 1956 SC 17 — 5 Judge Bench | Vivian Bose J
📝 Landmark Supreme Court case showing how failure to register a compulsorily registrable document destroys all legal rights — even fundamental rights cannot save you.
📋 Facts of the Case
Raja of Parikud owned Lake Chilka
Royal estate ceded to State of Orissa via Orissa Estates Abolition Act, 1951
Fishing licenses sold for large sums to various persons before estate vested in State
PROBLEM
Sales were oral — no writing, no registration
STATE ACTION
State of Orissa refused to recognise these oral fishing licenses & proposed re-auction — being the new title holder of Lake Chilka
PETITION
Licensees approached SC under Article 32 — alleged infringement of fundamental rights
🔍 Court's Analysis — Two Possibilities Tested
IF Licenses = rights to future goods (fish)
Then → Contract of sale of goods = Personal right of licensees
State is merely refusing to honour obligation
(not a party to contract)
→ No fundamental right violation
IF Licenses = benefits from land & fish = profit-à-prendre
📝 Profit-à-prendre = A right to take something (like fish, timber, minerals) from another person's land. It is treated as immovable property in Indian law.
Then → Immovable property
Requires writing + registration (Sec 54, TPA)
Oral sale → void
⚖️ COURT HELD — Vivian Bose J (5-Judge Bench):

"The sales in this case were oral: there was neither writing nor registration. That being the case, the transactions passed no title or interest and accordingly the petitioners have no fundamental right that they can enforce."
📝 In plain terms: You paid money, you fished for years — but without a registered document, you own nothing. No court, not even the Supreme Court, can help you enforce what was never legally created.
GST APPLICATION
Tax treatment (Schedule II / Schedule III) based on inadmissible or unregistered documents cannot be applied. Rights and remedies of parties to an unregistered but compulsorily registrable instrument do not even merit consideration in law.
6.2.1 — Statutory Definition of "Immovable Property" [Section 2(6)] ⇧ Index
🏗️ "Immovable Property" — Section 2(6)
Registration Act | Authoritative for Property Law purposes
📝 This definition is the authoritative one for property law. Each item in the list has different legal consequences — painting all transactions with the same brush would be wrong.
Included Element Legal Meaning / Notes Plain English GST Relevance
Land Terra firma or top-layer of the earth The ground itself — soil, surface. Sale of land → Schedule III exclusion (no GST)
Building Passive structures put up for enjoyment and utilization of land Any structure erected on land — office, factory, shop. Renting of building → taxable under GST as service
Hereditary Allowances Allowances that are heritable Rights that pass from parent to child automatically — like village headman rights or watam rights. Rare in GST context; relevant in Maharashtra (vatandar rights)
Rights to Ways Passageways for ingress and egress Right of way — e.g., right to cross neighbour's land to reach a road. Transfer/assignment of right of way may be exempt as immovable property
Lights Passageways for entry of light (and air) Right that your window gets sunlight — neighbour cannot build a wall blocking it. Rarely arises in GST; easement rights generally outside scope
Fisheries Rights to take away fish not in existence (profit-à-prendre) Right to catch fish from a water body — the fish don't yet exist when the right is granted. This is immovable property. Key issue: Assignment of fishing rights → immovable property → registration mandatory → unregistered = void
Any other benefit to arise out of land Akin to fisheries — both (i) fisheries and (ii) any other benefit from land are "benefit to arise out of land" (disjunctive 'or') Mining rights, quarrying rights, timber cutting rights — all benefits arising from land = immovable property. Mining lease → immovable property right → GST on royalty / lease premium separately analysed
Things attached to the earth * Based on 'manner and purpose' test — for functionality NOT stability. Not a benefit from land (Oxford comma rule applies) A machine bolted to factory floor for using it (functionality) → attached to earth. But a statue embedded in garden for stability → may be movable. Test is why it was attached. Embedded plant & machinery — attached for functionality → immovable → ITC blocked u/s 17(5)(d) CGST Act
Permanently fastened to anything attached to earth Secondary attachment — things attached to things attached to earth A shelf bolted to a wall which is attached to earth. Secondary/derived attachment. Relevant for ITC eligibility — whether structure is "plant and machinery" or "civil structure"
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE  It is important to identify 'what' is the immovable property that forms the object of the contract — for sale, lease or other arrangement. Legal consequences vary based on 'what' the object is. Titles/descriptions in contracts serve trade needs but do not dictate legal consequences.
6.2.2 — Nature of Right ⇧ Index
🔍 Nature of Right — What Exactly is Being Transferred?
Transfer must be examined to identify — Title OR Right to goods-of-future
✓ TITLE
Absolute & all-encompassing
Includes:
• Land surface
• Mineral deposits on surface (sand)
• Minerals under surface (iron ore)
📝 Full ownership — you own the soil, what's on it, and what's under it.
CONDITION
Goods-of-future that may be severed & transferred
(e.g., standing timber, minerals to be extracted)
OR
Merely transfer of right to goods-of-future
(e.g., fishing right, quarrying right)
⚠️ CAUTION
Titles and descriptions in contracts are meant to serve the needs of trade — they do NOT dictate legal consequences.
Enquiry into legal consequence is must to ascribe correct tax treatment.
📝 Just because a contract says "sale of goods" does not make it a goods transaction. You must look at the substance — is it really immovable property? The label doesn't matter; the legal nature does.
💡 Example: A contract titled "Timber Supply Agreement" — if standing trees are to be cut and supplied, it is sale of goods. But if right to enter land and take timber (profit-à-prendre) is granted, it is immovable property requiring registration.
6.2.3 — Dating Back ⇧ Index
📅 Dating Back — Effect of Timely vs Late Registration
📝 Registration can be done after signing the document — but there is a window of 4 months. If you register within 4 months, the registration "dates back" to the day you signed. If you miss the 4-month window, the document is treated as if it never existed.
GENERAL RULE
Transfer is complete on date of execution of the document
✓ IF REGISTERED WITHIN 4 MONTHS
Registration "dates back" to date of execution
Transfer is valid and complete from date of signing
📝 Retrospective validity — as if you registered it the same day you signed it.
💡 JDA signed on 1 Jan 2024, registered on 15 Apr 2024 (within 4 months) → valid from 1 Jan 2024 for GST and TPA purposes.
✗ IF TIME ALLOWED PASSES WITHOUT REGISTRATION
Omission renders execution non est
(does not exist in law)
📝 Non est = Not existing. The document is treated as if it was never signed at all. The transfer is void.
Transfer executed becomes void
Instruments Covered — Transfer under Non-Testamentary Instrument includes:
(i) Slump Sale Agreement
(ii) Scheme of Arrangement
(iii) Resolution Plan (NCLT approved)
(iv) Global Merger / Cross-border transfer involving Indian immovable property
⚠️ GLOBAL MERGERS
Transfer of property pursuant to global merger requires local Indian country arrangement to meet exacting standards of Indian law to confer valid title to Transferee.
Compulsions of trans-continental deals cannot be in derogation of the mandate in Indian laws.
📝 Even if two foreign companies merge overseas, if Indian immovable property is involved, the transfer of that property must comply with Indian Registration Act and TPA. A foreign court order is not enough — Indian registration is separately required.
GST CONSEQUENCE
Tax treatment applicable to a valid transfer will not flow if the instrument is unregistered.
Compulsory registration makes transfer void if instrument left unregistered.
6.2.4 — Holding Out ⇧ Index
🚫 Holding Out — Acting on an Unregistered Document
📝 "Holding out" means acting as if a right exists — even when it doesn't legally exist. Parties to an unregistered but compulsorily registrable document have no rights — and courts will not help them. Worse, if you pay tax based on such a document, you have paid the wrong tax.
THE PROBLEM
Unregistered documents which are compulsorily registrable do NOT procure the rights they contemplate
Acting out those rights = Mistake of fact
(void ab initio)
📝 Void ab initio = Void from the very beginning. The contract was never born — there was never any valid right.
No one will know / take note of this nullity → Tax position adopted on such document is wrong tax
Unregistered documents = non est even if uncontested
💡 A developer collects GST from buyer under an unregistered JDA. Later discovered JDA is void. GST collected was on a transaction that legally never happened — wrong tax paid, refund blocked by limitation.
THE CURE
Non-registration is curable by an adjudication order (court order validating the contract)
Only after adjudication does the contract attain legal existence
📝 A court can sometimes save an unregistered document — but until it does, the document is dead in law. You cannot assume the court will save it.
⚠️ DANGEROUS GST SCENARIO
Assuming tax treatment applicable to a valid registered document would apply to an unregistered document = Paying wrong tax.

Expecting refund of wrong tax is subject to limitation (not covered by Section 77 CGST Act — which deals only with wrong head, not wrong transaction).

You paid GST on something that legally never happened. You cannot easily get it back.
📌 Practical Examples from the Chapter
Eg.452
Unregistered JDA where seller accepts consideration as constructed units of equivalent value → merely construction agreement
Eg.453
Supplementary JDA remaining unregistered in respect of a registered JDA → non-fulfilment of original obligations — not a new agreement
Eg.454
Registered construction agreement at concessional GST rate → additional amounts for modification works do not alter rate — scope & obligations under original agreement unchanged
6.2.5 — Evidentiary Value of Unregistered Documents [Section 49] ⇧ Index
📄 Section 49 — Effect of Non-Registration
Evidentiary Value of Unregistered Documents
📝 Even if a document is not registered, can it at least be shown as evidence in court? Section 49 answers this. The answer is: almost never — except in two narrow situations.
✗ CANNOT — Unregistered document SHALL NOT:
(a) Affect any immovable property comprised therein
📝 Cannot create/transfer/extinguish any right in the property.
(b) Confer any power to adopt
(c) Be received as evidence of any transaction affecting such property or conferring such power
📝 Cannot even be shown to a court as proof. It is as if the document never existed — not just void but invisible to law.
✓ EXCEPTIONS — Unregistered document MAY be admitted as evidence in:
(a) Suit for specific performance
(Chapter II, Specific Relief Act, 1877)
📝 If you want a court to force the other party to complete the contract (not just pay damages), you can show the unregistered document as evidence of the contract's existence.
(b) Collateral transaction not required to be effected by registered instrument
📝 For a side/separate transaction that does not itself need registration — unregistered document can be shown. E.g., prove a loan was given, using an unregistered mortgage as background evidence of the loan relationship.
✗ BUT NOT to evidence the arraignment involving part performance (Section 53A, Transfer of Property Act)
📝 Part performance = When you took possession of property and improved it based on an unregistered agreement — Section 53A of TPA protects you against the seller. But unregistered document cannot even be shown for this defence in court (Section 49 bars it).
GST — JDA SPECIFIC APPLICATION
Interest to GPA-holder flows from the Joint Development Agreement (JDA).
Omission to register JDA → makes the 'interest' void ab initio.
Legal consequences to Developer = non est since facts about the arrangement itself cannot be admitted from an unregistered instrument.
Rights, remedies and safeguards of parties do not even merit consideration.
Tax treatment based on inadmissible evidentiary value of such documents cannot be applied.
📝 In simple terms: If a developer starts construction under an unregistered JDA — the developer has no legal interest in the land. GST cannot be computed on a "deemed supply" that is itself based on a void legal arrangement. The entire tax edifice built on an unregistered JDA is legally unsound.
💡 Example 455: Booking of apartment during launch event — creates no interest in Buyer. It is merely "intent to purchase" even if deposit is paid. Cancellation gives only right to refund (or forfeiture if agreed).

💡 Example 456: Agreement to sell — even though creating no interest in apartment — triggers legal fiction in Para 5(b), Schedule II (CGST Act) if booking amount collected (5:95 scheme) and remainder collected after project completion.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 6 | Registration Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Ananda Behara v. State of Orissa
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1956 SC 17 | 5-Judge Constitution Bench | Vivian Bose J
Key Ratio / Principle
"The sales in this case were oral: there was neither writing nor registration. That being the case, the transactions passed no title or interest and accordingly the petitioners have no fundamental right that they can enforce."
Further Ratio on Profit-à-prendre
"Now a 'sale' is defined as a transfer of ownership in exchange for a price paid or promised. As a profit a prendre is immovable property and as in this case it was purchased for a price that was paid it requires writing and registration because of section 54 of the Transfer of Property Act. If a profit a prendre is regarded as tangible immovable property, then the 'property' in this case was over ₹100 in value. If it is intangible, then a registered instrument would be necessary whatever the value."
When to Use — When Department or assessee claims tax rights/exemptions based on an oral or unregistered arrangement involving immovable property. Cite to establish that unregistered transactions pass NO legal title or interest — and therefore GST treatment applicable to a valid registered transaction cannot flow.
Section 17 — Registration Act, 1908
Statutory Provision | Applicable across GST disputes on real estate / immovable property
Key Principle
Non-registration of documents compulsorily registrable under Section 17 is fatal to the anticipated contractual relations from that contract. Tax treatment — whether claiming exclusion under Schedule III Para 5 or limitation under Schedule II Para 5(b) — cannot be lightly applied or assumed to go unchallenged when applied to unregistered documents. Contractual relationships cannot be assumed without giving due attention to registration, when it is an essential requirement to make those contracts valid.
When to Use — When challenging Department's demand which rests on an unregistered JDA, development agreement, or lease document. Argue: the foundational document is void — no valid taxable event arises — entire demand falls.
Section 49 — Registration Act, 1908 | Non Est Doctrine
Statutory Provision | Read with Section 17 | Applicable in all JDA / real estate GST disputes
Key Ratio
Unregistered (but compulsorily registrable) documents are non est in law and remain so even if they are uncontested. Assuming the tax treatment otherwise applicable had the document been duly registered would be akin to paying the wrong tax and expecting that the right tax will not be demanded and refund of wrong tax subject to limitation. Tax treatment based on inadmissible evidentiary value of unregistered documents cannot be applied.
When to Use — When assessee or Department relies on unregistered JDA, development agreement or supplementary agreement as basis for GST computation. Cite to nullify the evidentiary basis of the entire demand/claim.
Dating Back Principle — Section 17 read with Registration Act
Legal Principle | Registration Act, 1908 | Para 6.2.3
Key Principle
When registration is completed within the time allowed (four months from execution), registration 'dates back' to the date of execution and leaves execution valid and complete. However, omission to register within the allowed time renders the execution non est — it voids the transfer executed. Compulsory registration makes transfer void if instrument is left unregistered. Tax treatment applicable to a valid transfer will not flow if the instrument is unregistered.
When to Use — In disputes about timing of taxable supply in real estate (JDA registration date vs execution date). Also useful where Department argues supply arose on execution date — counter: if registration was within 4 months, effective date dates back; if unregistered, no supply at all.
Profit-à-prendre Doctrine — Immovable Property Test
Common Law Doctrine | Affirmed in Ananda Behara (SC 1956) | Applicable in mining/fishery/forestry GST disputes
Key Principle
A profit à prendre is immovable property. It requires writing and registration under Section 54 of the Transfer of Property Act. Whether tangible (over ₹100 in value) or intangible — a registered instrument is necessary. Oral assignment of fishing rights, mining rights, or quarrying rights passes no title or interest in law.
When to Use — In disputes involving mining lease, fishery rights, timber rights where Department or assessee characterises the arrangement as "goods supply" to claim different GST rate. Argue: this is profit-à-prendre = immovable property = registration mandatory = unregistered arrangement is void = entire tax computation on such arrangement is without legal basis.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 7 — Easements Act

⚖️ Chapter 7 — Easements Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / NOT applicable
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
7.1 Relevance to GST
7.2.1 Essentials of Easement
7.2.1B Easement vs Profit-à-Prendre vs License vs Public Road
7.2.2 License — §52 | Bare vs With Interest
7.2.3 Sub-License | 7.2.4 Termination
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
7.1 — Relevance to GST | Intangible vs Tangible Immovable Property ⇧ Index
🌿 Easements Act — Relevance to GST
Intangible Immovable Property ≠ Tangible Immovable Property
CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Intangible immovable property cannot be equated with tangible immovable property
Tax treatment will also be different based on nature of underlying transaction
📝 "Immovable property" is not just land and buildings. Rights over land (like easements, licenses) are also immovable property — but of an intangible kind. GST treats them differently depending on whether the benefit has already arisen or is yet to arise.
TWO CATEGORIES
Benefits already arisen from land
→ Supply of goods

Benefits yet to arise from land
→ Supply of services
📝 An existing fishery right (fish already swimming in the pond) = supply of goods (present goods). A right to fish in future seasons = supply of services (future benefit). The timing of the benefit determines goods vs services classification.
TAX RISK
Omission to pay correct tax exposes taxpayer to a second demand
Relief by refund of wrong tax — but NOT without peril of time lapse
(Limitation clock runs on wrong tax refund)
📝 If you pay GST under the wrong head (e.g., goods instead of services or vice versa) — you will get a second demand for the correct tax AND may lose the refund of wrong tax if time limit for refund has passed. Both risks hit simultaneously.
WHY THIS CHAPTER MATTERS
Familiarity with easements will refine GST treatment when consideration is paid:
(i) To refrain from obstructing pre-existing easements
(ii) For transfer of easements
📝 If someone pays you to NOT block their right of way — what is that payment for GST? Tolerating an act? Transfer of immovable property right? Goods or services? This chapter answers that — and the wrong answer costs heavily in litigation.
7.2.1 — Essentials of Easement | Dominant & Servient Heritage ⇧ Index
🏡 Easement — Lawful Advantage to Dominant Heritage Operating as Lawful Disadvantage on Servient Heritage
📝 An easement is a right that one piece of land (dominant heritage) has over another piece of land (servient heritage). The owner of the dominant land can use a part of the servient land in a specific way. The servient landowner cannot stop this — even though they own the land. It is a burden that "runs with the land" — whoever owns the servient land must honour it.
Five Essentials of an Easement (from the Example of A & C)
(a)
Dominant Heritage
Land of A
(benefits from easement)
(b)
Servient Heritage
Land of C
(bears the burden)
(c)
Easement itself
Lawful, determinate, unchanging and enforceable
e.g. Pathway through C's land
(d)
Beneficial Enjoyment
Of A's land
(purpose the easement serves)
(e)
A and C are different persons
(merger of A's and C's land would cancel easement — lesser rights merge with greater)
Key Rules Governing Easements
✓ HERITABLE
Easement is heritable — goes with A's land on sale, lease or succession
Does NOT belong to A personally — attached to A's LAND
📝 If A sells her land, the easement goes with the land to the new buyer automatically. The new owner enjoys the same pathway right. A cannot "take the easement with her" separately — it stays with the land.
✗ A CANNOT
Construct on the easement area
Only use the 'right of way' as it is
✗ C CANNOT
Obstruct A's right of way
Build high-rise blocking air & light
Build wall across the pathway
📝 The rights are narrowly defined. A gets what was granted — no more (cannot expand the easement). C must tolerate the easement — cannot reduce or block it.
RELIEF AVAILABLE
Permanent injunction against attempt to obstruct customary easements
Direction to demolish obstructions already put up
C's title is absolute — but ALWAYS subject to customary easements
📝 Courts will grant injunctions to protect easements. Even though C owns the land absolutely, that absolute ownership is always "subject to" the easement. C cannot buy away this burden even if C has full title.
CUSTOMARY EASEMENTS
Villagers passing through lands of landholders along river/lake banks
Types:
(a) Right of way
(b) Right to air and light
(c) Right to draw water from river or lake
(d) Right not to undertake construction affecting stability of neighbour's building
⚠️ IMPORTANT LIMIT
Easement does NOT permit exploitation of sub-soil over the easement area
Sub-soil belongs to the servient heritage (owner C's land)
📝 If A has a right of way across C's land — A can walk/drive across it. But A cannot drill for water, mine minerals, or build underground infrastructure in that strip of land. The sub-soil belongs to C. The easement is only on the surface use.
💡 GST Application: Payment by new landowner for "not obstructing existing right of way" = GST on tolerating an act/situation (Schedule II, Entry 5(e))? OR transfer of easement right (intangible immovable property)? The answer depends on whether what is being paid for is: (i) a new grant of easement OR (ii) simply refraining from obstructing an already-existing easement. These attract different GST treatments.
7.2.1B — Easement vs Profit-à-Prendre vs License vs Public Road — Critical Distinctions ⇧ Index
Feature Easement Profit-à-Prendre License (Bare) Public Road
Linked to Dominant Heritage? YES — inseparably attached to dominant land NOT necessarily — can be free-standing right (not linked to dominant land = NOT easement) NO — personal right of Licensee NO — no dominant heritage to public
Nature Encumbrance on servient land — NOT profit-à-prendre; cannot be detached from servient land When linked to dominant heritage AND exercised over servient heritage = easement.
When free-standing = profit-à-prendre (immovable property)		 Only makes user of Licensor's property lawful. No interest created (bare license). Public road — no dominant heritage; no easement framework
Can be terminated? NO — goes with dominant land on sale, lease, succession Depends — if free-standing, yes; if linked to dominant land, follows easement rules YES — bare license terminable by terminating underlying contract N/A
Creates interest in property? YES — interest similar to charge on servient land YES — immovable property; requires writing + registration (§54 TPA) NO — bare license creates NO interest in property NO
Registration required? YES — heritable, registered with land YES — writing and registration mandatory (Ananda Behara SC 1956) NO — bare license needs no registration
(but license with interest requires registration)		 N/A
GST Treatment Payment to refrain from obstructing = GST on tolerating act OR transfer of intangible immovable property — depends on facts Existing goods (fish in existence) = supply of goods.
Future goods (fish to arise) = supply of services		 Bare license = supply of goods (access to immovable property — not interest in it).
License with interest = supply of service (interest in immovable property)		 N/A
7.2.2 — License | §52 Easements Act | Bare License vs License with Interest ⇧ Index
🔑 License — §52 Easements Act | "Making Use of Property Lawful"
SECTION 52 DEFINITION
"Where one person grants to another, or to a definite number of other persons, a right to do, or continue to do, in or upon the immovable property of the grantor, something which would, in the absence of such right, be unlawful, and such right does not amount to an easement or an interest in the property, the right is called a license."
📝 A license simply makes it legal for the Licensee to be on/use the Licensor's property. Without the license, the Licensee would be a trespasser. But the license gives NO ownership or interest in the property — just permission. The moment the license is revoked, Licensee must leave.
Two Types of License — Critical Distinction
BARE LICENSE
Creates NO interest in property
Only makes access and user of property lawful
Revocable by terminating underlying contract
📝 Bare license = pure permission. Like a visitor's pass. You are allowed to be there. But you have no right to be there independently. The host can ask you to leave anytime.
GST
Tax treatment of bare license = supply of goods
(access to immovable property — not interest in it)
Whether under license or sub-license
📝 Bare license grants access to immovable property — treated as supply of goods in GST (not services). This is counterintuitive but follows from the fact that no interest in immovable property is created. Leasing/renting that creates interest = service.
💡 Eg.458: LCO does NOT grant any license over common infrastructure to subscriber — LCO provides services using its own infrastructure. Eg.459: Access to river to practice rowing = license (not easement). Eg.460: Contractor entering project site = license (even if RMC plant is set up on site — permitted use only). Eg.461: Purchase of additional car parking slot in common area of apartment = license (not easement) provided Transferor has vested title.
LICENSE WITH INTEREST
Creates an interest in property (like profit-à-prendre)
Cannot be revoked to prejudice Licensee's interest
