Becomes irrevocable once acted upon (§202 Indian Contract Act)
📝 License with interest = more than just permission. It creates a real right — like a right to take crops or fish. Once the Licensee has invested in acting upon the license, the Licensor cannot simply terminate it. It has become irrevocable.
REGISTRATION
If license with interest asserted → requirement of registration (§17 Registration Act) is attracted to make assertion lawful
In absence of registration → presumption runs in favour of bare license only
📝 If you claim your license gives you an "interest" in the property — you must register it. Without registration, the law presumes it is only a bare license (no interest). And unregistered instruments conveying interest are non est (Registration Act Chapter 6).
💡 Eg.457: License of rubber estate for one harvest to tap latex = license with interest (current harvest — goods in existence — NOT profit-à-prendre which relates to future goods). Eg.462: License of land to cultivate crop until its harvest = NOT license but easement (crop is Transferee's own — not sale of goods in existence for one season; NOT profit-à-prendre when multiple seasons allowed).
⚠️ LABEL CAUTION
Consideration styled 'license fee' does NOT by that fact alone create any interest
Legal consequences flowing from the contract must support such conclusion
📝 Just because you call the payment a "license fee" does not make it a license. Just because you call it a "lease" does not make it a lease. The contract terms and legal consequences must be examined — not the label on the invoice.
LEASE vs LICENSE
Lease = transfer of possessory right to Lessee + conveyance of interest in Lessor's property
License = only makes user of Licensor's property lawful
Lease ≠ License
📝 A lease gives you possession and an interest in the property. A license only gives you permission to be there. If your landlord can throw you out without a court order — it is a license. If they need a court order — you have a lease (possessory rights).
💡 GST: Renting of immovable property (lease) = taxable service. Bare license to use premises = supply of goods (access to immovable property). The distinction determines HSN, rate, and GST treatment entirely.
7.2.3 Sub-License | 7.2.4 Termination of License ⇧ Index
🔄 Sub-License & Termination — GST Implications
7.2.3 Sub-License
PERMITTED IF
Contract of license permits greater right to Licensee
(i.e., contract allows sub-licensing)
📝 You can only sub-license if your license agreement expressly allows it or gives you rights greater than bare permission. If your contract is silent — you cannot sub-license by default.
GST
Tax treatment of bare license = supply of goods — whether under license OR sub-license
Same GST treatment flows to sub-license as to original license (if bare)
📝 If original license is bare (no interest created) — the sub-license is also bare. GST = goods. If original license has interest — sub-license also has interest. GST = services. The nature flows through.
7.2.4 Termination
Bare License
Terminated by terminating the underlying contract
(work or service contract)
Simple — no formalities needed
📝 End the contract → license ends. No registration or special formality needed to terminate a bare license.
License with Interest
Termination involves restoration of interest back to Licensor
Requires: terms flowing from contract of license OR another instrument in writing, duly registered
📝 If a license with interest is terminated — the interest must be formally transferred back to the Licensor. This needs a registered written instrument — just like the original grant needed registration. Termination by email or verbal agreement = insufficient.
GST ALERT
Secure consent for termination = yet another transaction attracting separate tax treatment
NOT merely unwinding the former transaction
📝 When you pay someone to agree to terminate a license with interest — that payment is NOT just "reversing" the original license. It is a NEW transaction — the Licensee is tolerating termination for consideration. This has its OWN GST event — it is NOT exempt merely because the original transaction was already taxed.
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS (IPRs)
License involving IPRs must be tested if they are incorporeal rights over:
(i) Immovable property → legal requirements for immovable property apply
(ii) Movable property → legal requirements for movable property apply
📝 A copyright over a song = incorporeal movable property. A building plan/architectural design registered as IPR = may have immovable property characteristics. The termination requirements (and GST treatment) depend on which category the IPR falls into. This is not settled by the label "license fee" on the invoice.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 7 | Easements Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Easement Principle — Intangible Immovable Property ≠ Tangible Immovable Property
Easements Act, 1882 | Para 7.1 | Applicable in all immovable property GST disputes
Key Principle
Intangible immovable property cannot be equated with tangible immovable property, and tax treatment will also be different based on the nature of the underlying transaction. Benefits already arisen from land are supply of goods, and benefits yet to arise from land are supply of services. Omission to pay the correct tax exposes taxpayers to a second demand, with relief by way of refund of the wrong tax — but this is not without the perils of time lapse.
When to Use — When Department characterises all immovable property-related transactions as "services" (renting/leasing) and demands service tax / GST at service rate. Counter: nature of right matters — is the benefit already arisen (goods) or yet to arise (services)? Right of way being granted for existing fish in a pond = goods; right to fish for future seasons = services. Wrong classification = wrong tax + limitation peril.
Section 52 Easements Act — License Definition | Bare License Creates No Interest
Easements Act, 1882 | Section 52 | Para 7.2.2 | Applicable in all license vs lease vs easement disputes
Key Ratio
A license is where one person grants to another a right to do something in or upon the immovable property of the grantor which would in the absence of such right be unlawful, and such right does not amount to an easement or an interest in the property. A bare license creates no interest in the property — it only makes the access and user of the property lawful. Tax treatment of bare license will be supply of goods and not supply of services — whether under license or sub-license — since it is not an interest in immovable property.
When to Use — (i) When Department demands GST on "license fee" as supply of service (renting of immovable property). Counter: if no interest in property is created — it is a bare license = supply of goods — different HSN and rate apply. (ii) When assessee tries to treat license revenue as "goods" to claim input tax credit on inputs used — cite to distinguish bare license from lease/renting which creates interest.
License vs Lease — Possessory Right vs Permission
Easements Act §52 + Transfer of Property Act | Para 7.2.2 | Applicable in all renting/licensing disputes
Key Principle
Lease, being transfer of possessory right to Lessee and conveyance of interest in property of Lessor, is not a license which only makes user of Licensor's property lawful. Consideration being styled 'license fee' does not by that fact alone create any interest unless the legal consequences flowing from the contract support such a conclusion. In the absence of registration of a written instrument conveying interest via license over a property, presumption runs in favour of a bare license only.
When to Use — When Licensor claims "I gave only a license, not a lease" to escape higher GST rate or registration requirements. Counter: if the arrangement gives Licensee possessory rights and interest in property → it is a lease regardless of what the document says. AND conversely, when Department treats a bare license as a lease — cite to show no possessory right or interest was transferred = bare license = supply of goods, not renting of immovable property service.
License with Interest — Registration Mandatory | Presumption for Bare License
Easements Act §52 read with Registration Act §17 | Para 7.2.2 | Applicable in license with interest / profit-à-prendre disputes
Key Principle
Where assertion of license with interest is made, the requirement of registration in terms of section 17 of the Registration Act is attracted to make this assertion lawful. In the absence of registration of a written instrument conveying interest via license over a property, presumption runs in favour of a bare license. A license with interest such as profit-à-prendre (discussed in Ananda Behara v. State of Orissa AIR 1956 SC 17) cannot be revoked to prejudice the Licensee's interest — and once an irrevocable license with interest is granted and acted upon (section 202, Indian Contract Act), the Licensor is estopped from revocation.
When to Use — (i) When assessee claims "license with interest" in property to justify certain tax treatment without a registered instrument — cite: no registration = presumption of bare license only, NOT license with interest. (ii) When assessee claims fishing/mining/timber-cutting rights are "licenses" (not profit-à-prendre) to avoid registration requirement — cite: if linked to dominant heritage and exercised over servient heritage, it is easement; if free-standing with future goods = profit-à-prendre requiring registration.
Easement vs Profit-à-Prendre — When Profit-à-Prendre Becomes Easement
Easements Act, 1882 | Para 7.2.1 | Read with Registration Act Chapter 6 and Ananda Behara SC 1956
Key Principle
Easement being the benefit attached to A's land by imposing encumbrance on C's land is not profit-à-prendre as the easement cannot be detached from C's land. It is only an encumbrance not similar to a charge on property but similar to an interest in C's land that A's land enjoys. Profit-à-prendre not linked to a dominant heritage is not an easement — but when it is linked to a dominant heritage and exercised over a servient heritage, it becomes an easement. Easements do not belong to A personally — they are attached to A's land and go with it on sale, lease or succession.
When to Use — In mining lease, fishery rights, quarrying right disputes — to precisely identify whether the right is: (i) easement (attached to dominant land, non-detachable, heritable with land), (ii) profit-à-prendre free-standing (immovable property, needs registration, future goods = services for GST), or (iii) license to take current harvest (goods in existence = supply of goods for GST). Each attracts entirely different GST treatment. Precision in characterisation prevents wrong-tax demands.
Termination of License with Interest — Separate Taxable Transaction
Easements Act | Para 7.2.4 | Applicable in consent-termination and license surrender disputes
Key Principle
Termination of a bare license may be accomplished by terminating the underlying contract. But termination of a license with interest involves restoration of interest back to Licensor and requires terms flowing from the contract of license or another instrument in writing, duly registered. Special consideration for secure consent for termination is yet another transaction attracting separate tax treatment — not merely unwinding the former transaction.
When to Use — When payment is made to terminate a lease/license and question is: is this taxable? Counter to "it is just reversal": termination of a license with interest = a new transaction (transferring interest back to Licensor) = separate GST event. The consideration paid for consent to terminate = consideration for tolerating loss of the license interest = taxable supply. Cannot be treated as a mere "reversal" of original supply.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 8 — Limitation Act

⏳ Chapter 8 — Limitation Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

"When a statute requires a thing to be done in a particular manner, it must be done in that manner or not at all." — Nazir Ahmed v. King Emperor AIR 1936 PC 253
Permitted / Valid
Time-barred / Prohibited
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
8.1 Relevance to GST | Fatal vs Non-Fatal Lapse
8.2.1 Right or Remedy | Limitation vs Prescription
8.2.2 Purpose of Limitation | Interest Reipublicae
8.2.3 Mandatory or Directory
8.2.4 Condonation — Every Day Must Be Explained
8.2.5 General or Special Limitation
8.2.6 Amendment of Limitation
8.2.7 Acquiescence | Validity of Service of Notice
8.2.8 Prescription — Acquisitive & Extinctive
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
8.1 — Relevance to GST | Fatal vs Non-Fatal Lapse of Timeline ⇧ Index
⏳ Limitation Act — Relevance to GST
Timelines for nearly every activity — by Taxpayer AND by Proper Officer
WHY IT MATTERS
GST has timelines for nearly every activity
Lapse of timelines asserted to affect validity of proceedings
Care must be taken to locate a FATAL lapse vs a NON-FATAL lapse
📝 Not every missed deadline kills a proceeding. Some timelines are strict (fatal — missing them destroys the action). Others are directory (non-fatal — missing them causes inconvenience but does not destroy the action). You must know which is which to mount or defend a limitation challenge.
FATAL vs NON-FATAL
Distinction lies in:
(i) Does it destroy vested rights?
(ii) Is there necessary intendment that proceedings MUST stop once timeline lapses?
📝 If the law says "proceedings SHALL stop after X days" AND missing it destroys someone's right — it is fatal. If the law says "should be done within X days" without consequences for missing — it is directory and non-fatal.
NATURE OF THIS LAW
Limitation Act = private matters' legislation
= substantive law on procedures
Rules of strict interpretation apply — especially in taxation
📝 The Limitation Act is not a technicality — it is a substantive protection for both taxpayer and Department. Courts apply it strictly, especially in tax law. Neither party can ignore limitation and expect the court to overlook it.
GST TIMELINES TABLE
Taxpayer:
→ Claim ITC
→ Claim refund
→ File appeal against adverse order

Proper Officer:
→ Issue notice for demand
→ Issue notice rejecting refund
→ File departmental appeal
→ Initiate revisionary proceedings
📝 Both sides have timelines. The Proper Officer cannot issue SCN without limit, cannot appeal without limit, cannot initiate revision without limit. And the taxpayer cannot claim ITC, refund, or file appeal without respecting the timelines. The clock runs for everyone.
8.2.1 — Right or Remedy | Limitation vs Prescription | Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields ⇧ Index
⚖️ Limitation Denies the REMEDY — Prescription Destroys the RIGHT
📝 This is the most fundamental distinction in limitation law. Limitation only bars you from going to court — it does not erase the underlying right. Prescription is stronger — it actually destroys the right itself. In GST: limitation bars the Department from issuing SCN after time limit, but the government's right to tax may still exist theoretically. However, once limitation lapses, the counter-party gets a VESTED RIGHT that cannot be taken away.
LIMITATION
Procedure to take legal action in respect of a vested right
Lapse → denies the remedy (cannot go to court/adjudicate)
Does NOT destroy the underlying right
📝 The underlying right (e.g., right to demand tax) still exists — but you cannot enforce it in law. The remedy is gone. Like having a debt that you cannot sue for — the debt exists but the court won't help you collect it.
PRESCRIPTION
Statutory provision declaring vested right to be extinguished or acquired
Inaction for prolonged period → alters vested rights
Destroys the right ITSELF
📝 Prescription is more devastating than limitation. Not only can you not sue — the right itself is gone. Like adverse possession: after 12 years, you don't just lose the right to sue for your land — you actually LOSE TITLE to the land.
KEY RULE
Where affirmative action is required, omission to take action creates a vested right to counter-party once limitation lapses
Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. 1962 AIR SC 361
📝 This is the most powerful rule in limitation law for GST practice. Once the Department misses its limitation period for issuing SCN — the taxpayer gets a VESTED RIGHT that the demand will not be made. This right cannot be lightly unseated.
5 STEPS — AWARENESS
(i) Locate timelines for affirmative action
(ii) Tests to identify timelines NOT in nature of limitation
(iii) Additional tests for timelines operating as prescription
(iv) Legal effects of extensions under §168A or circulars
(v) Assistance available as legal defence against defaults
8.2.2 — Purpose of Limitation | Interest Reipublicae | Jai Mangal Oraon ⇧ Index
Interest Reipublicae Ut Sit Finis Litium
"It is for the general welfare that a period be part to litigation"
📝 This Latin maxim means: "It is in the public interest that there be an end to litigation." The law cannot allow disputes to hang in the air forever. Limitation forces parties to act promptly — or lose their right to act.
Three Reasons Courts Have Given for Limitation
(a) Dormant claims have more cruelty than justice
(b) Evidence could be lost to prove a stale claim
(c) Person with a good cause must pursue it diligently
VESTED RIGHTS SURVIVE
Vested rights NOT destroyed — only procedural remedy denied
Matter of public policy and prudence that bars assistance to dormant claims of BOTH parties
📝 Limitation protects both sides — not just the taxpayer. If the taxpayer files a late refund claim, limitation bars it. If the Department issues a late SCN, limitation bars that too. Both are governed equally.
MAXIMUM LIMITATION — GST
No State action can survive beyond maximum limitation in §74(10)
Certainty made explicitly clear in §75(10) of Central GST Act
Unlimited time = infinite uncertainty
📝 Section 74(10) + 75(10): adjudication MUST be completed within the maximum prescribed period. If not, the demand is deemed concluded as declared in §75(10). This is a hard stop — no extensions beyond this.
💡 Eg.463: Refund cannot be filed after 2 years (subject to extension vide NT 13/2022-CT, 9/2023-CT). Eg.464: ADT1 cannot be issued starting 6 years ago. Eg.465: MOV7 cannot be issued once goods reach destination. Eg.466: APL1 cannot be filed after 4 months of Order.
NO SPECIFIC LIMITATION — STILL LIMITED
In absence of limitation for a specific case → action cannot be permitted without time limit
Jai Mangal Oraon v. Mira Nayak AIR 2000 SC 2276: Reasonable time must be read into authority to take action
Relief admissible can be denied due to inordinate delay
📝 Even where the law does not specify a limitation period — you cannot act indefinitely. Courts will read a "reasonable time" into the authority. What is reasonable depends on the facts — but inordinate delay will be punished even without a specific period.
DELAY = WAIVER = LACHES
Delay in seeking statutory remedy = limitation
Delay in seeking equitable remedy = laches
Delay is equivalent to waiver
Delay informs counter-party that relief is not desired → consequent action available may be initiated
📝 Laches = sitting on your rights for too long in equity. Even if no strict limitation period applies, if you wait unreasonably — courts deny equitable relief. And your delay tells the other side "you're okay with the situation" — they may then take actions (like issuing garnishee orders) based on that assumption.
💡 Eg.467: Balance in ECrL remains even if refund not filed in time. Eg.468: Payment made 'under protest' via DRC3 will be lost unless refund application filed until disposal of protest. Eg.469: DRC13 may be issued if appeal not filed within 3 months from adjudication order. Eg.470: DRC13 may be issued within 1 month of best judgement assessment when evasion suspected.
8.2.3 — Mandatory or Directory | Abuse of Law | Taxpayer NOT Obliged to Volunteer ⇧ Index
📋 Mandatory vs Directory — When Does a Timeline Kill the Proceeding?
MANDATORY Limitation
Limitation must be applied even if NOT pleaded — to avoid defective adjudication
Adherence = mandatory; Application by counter-party = mandatory
Ignorance of one party does NOT authorize counter-party to derive advantage due
📝 Mandatory limitation = even the adjudicating authority MUST apply it suo motu. You don't need to raise it — the adjudicating officer must notice it and apply it. Confirming a time-barred demand is illegal — even if the taxpayer didn't raise the limitation argument.
Language test: "no appeal shall be filed…unless…" → appeal without pre-deposit does not exist in law → non est
(Eg.488: §107(11) CGST Act)
💡 Eg.473: SCN for 2017-18 demand cannot be issued after 30 Sept 2023 (without extension). Eg.474: SCN for 2017-18 issued after 30 Sept 2023 cannot be overlooked by adjudicating authority and confirmed to save the demand. Eg.481: Adjudication not completed within §73(10)/74(10) = deemed concluded under §75(10). Eg.484: Appeal filed after §107(4) time = must be dismissed.
DIRECTORY Limitation
Instances of contradiction in statute indicate limitation is directory
Substantive provisions must prevail over procedural provisions where there is imminent conflict
All parts of statute must operate in harmony
📝 Directory limitation = missing the deadline causes inconvenience but doesn't invalidate the action. If the statute gives a "late cut" penalty (like SEIS/MEIS) — the limitation is directory, not mandatory. The action is still valid, just penalised.
TESTS FOR DIRECTORY
Nature of remediation provided is good indicator:
(a) "Late cut" proportionate to delay → directory
(b) Substantive vs vesting condition conflict → substantive prevails
💡 Eg.478: Belated SEIS/MEIS credit claims entertained with proportionate 'late cut' — limitation is directory. Eg.479: Retrospective §7(1)(aa) amendment effective 1 July 2017 without saving clause for §16(4) — taxpayers who had no liability cannot be hit. Eg.480: §16(2)(b) is substantive / vesting condition → §16(4) time limit cannot start before supplies can possibly be received completely. Eg.485: Appeal not disposed within §113(4) time = still valid and must be addressed.
Language test: "…shall not be entitled to take input tax credit……" ≠ "no credit shall be taken…unless taken within…"
(Eg.489: §16(4) CGST — language is entitlement-based, NOT absolute prohibition)
Counter-Party's Rights & Obligations
COUNTER-PARTY IS NOT OBLIGED TO
Volunteer relief to the other party
Notify the other party of their default
Reverse ITC without SCN (Eg.475)
Repay erroneously sanctioned refund without SCN (Eg.476)
📝 The law does not require you to help your adversary. If the Department missed its limitation — you are not required to tell them. If the taxpayer availed excess ITC — Department must issue SCN; taxpayer need not reverse voluntarily without being asked.
ABUSE OF LAW
Until limitation lapses, counter-party is liable NOT to take any 'permitted action' that could prejudice taxpayer's remedies
Taking premature action = abuse of law
📝 The Department cannot take recovery action while the appeal limitation period is running — even if it is "permitted" by some provision. The taxpayer still has the right to appeal. Premature recovery undermines that right. Taking action that is "not permitted" = abuse of law.
💡 Eg.471: Recovery action (without good reason) before expiration of appeal limitation against adjudication order = abuse. Eg.472: Blocking credits under Rule 88A for belated returns to enforce demand under §16(4) bypassing notice procedure = abuse of law.
8.2.4 — Condonation | Every Day of Delay Must Be Explained ⇧ Index
📄 Condonation of Delay — Not a Right, Not an Emotional Appeal
📝 Condonation is not a favour granted routinely. There is NO vested right to seek condonation. The counter-party (who gets the benefit of limitation lapsing) has a vested right — and condonation is taking away that right. Courts require objective justification — not sympathy.
What is Condonation?
§5 Limitation Act
Provides a RIGHT that delay be condoned (discretionary)
§14 Limitation Act
Provides a REMEDY where sufficient cause shown (effect of filing before incorrect forum)
📝 Section 5 says: you CAN seek condonation if there is sufficient cause. Section 14 gives a specific remedy — if you filed in the wrong court in time, the time spent there is excluded from limitation. Two separate provisions — two different situations.
SUFFICIENT CAUSE = TWO REQUIREMENTS
(i) Shown to explain reasons for delay
(ii) Justifying why power MUST be exercised
How to File — Objective Standards
EVERY DAY OF DELAY MUST BE EXPLAINED
Condonation will NOT be allowed routinely unless every day of delay from end of statutory period is explained
PLEADING REQUIREMENTS
(a) Pleadings must be precise — explanation of each day of delay + justification to invoke power
(b) Assertions must be evidenced — affidavit or other real and plausible proof with strong prima facie grounds
📝 "We had staff issues" or "Portal was down" — vague, not accepted. You need specific dates: "from X to Y, the authorised signatory was hospitalised — here is the medical record." Each day must be accounted for with contemporaneous evidence.
AFFIDAVIT RULE
With risk of perjury, affidavits may be used
Where contemporaneous evidence is available → affidavits must be avoided — those material documents must be brought on record
📝 If you have a contemporaneous document (hospitalization record, bank statement, portal screenshot with error) — file the document. Don't rely on an affidavit saying "I remember that…" — affidavits for things that can be proved by documents invite perjury risk and are weak evidence.
Test for Granting / Rejecting Condonation
Satisfaction with reasons for delay is NOT subjective agreement with explanation
Two tests:
(i) Plausibility of reasons
(ii) Connection of those reasons with duty to file within statutory limitation
📝 The adjudicating authority must objectively assess: (1) is the reason believable? (2) does the reason actually explain WHY the filing was delayed (connection between reason and delay)? Not every valid reason automatically justifies delay.
REJECTION MUST BE SPEAKING ORDER
Rejection of condonation requires a 'speaking order' — subject to appeal and judicial review
Seriousness must be given to affirmative action in making application
Lack of seriousness = basis for rejection
📝 If the authority rejects your condonation application — they must give reasons in writing (speaking order). A one-line rejection is not enough. And you can appeal the rejection — it is a separately reviewable order.
8.2.5 — General or Special Limitation | §3 Limitation Act | Time-Barring is Fatal ⇧ Index
📋 General vs Special Limitation | §3 Limitation Act | Applicability to GST
Hierarchy of Limitation Periods
RULE 1
Any "suit, appeal or application" cannot operate without a limitation — firstly
RULE 2
Where limitation in special law is different from this Act → limitation in special law prevails
📝 GST is a special law. The specific limitation periods in CGST Act (e.g., 3 months for appeal under §107, 3 years for SCN under §73, 5 years under §74) prevail over the general Limitation Act. But §3 and §29 of Limitation Act still apply to GST proceedings.
§3 Limitation Act — Mandatory Dismissal
Section 3 makes it mandatory for the suit, appeal or application to be DISMISSED even if the question about time-barring has NOT been pleaded
📝 Time-barring is not a defence that can be waived — it is not something you need to raise. The court/authority MUST apply it. Even if the taxpayer says "I don't mind, please hear my case even though it is late" — the authority MUST dismiss it.
FATAL CONSEQUENCE
Person without realizing notice issued is time-barred → cannot acquiesce to proceedings → then suffer demand → due to omission to plead time-bar
Time-barring is fatal to the proceedings
📝 Even if you respond to a time-barred SCN (without raising limitation) — the demand is still void. You didn't "waive" limitation by responding. The authority should have applied it suo motu. You can raise limitation even in the appeal stage.
§29 Limitation Act — Extends to All Special Laws
§29 makes §3 apply to every other special law
§§4 to 24 apply to special laws to the extent they may apply
Deep study of rights, remedies and safeguards in §§4–24 essential for GST practice
📝 §§4 to 24 of the Limitation Act contain important provisions like: exclusion of time when applicant was minor/insane (§6), exclusion of time for appeals filed in wrong forum (§14), extension when court is closed (§4), etc. These apply to GST proceedings by virtue of §29.
💡 Eg.482: Audit must be concluded within time specified in §65(4). Eg.483: Goods seized must be returned within §67(7) time. Eg.484: Appeal filed after §107(4) time = must be dismissed (cannot be entertained). Eg.485: Appeal not disposed within §113(4) time = valid and must be addressed in due course.
8.2.6 — Amendment of Limitation | §168A Extension | Retrospective Effect ⇧ Index
📝 Amendment of Limitation — Does Extension Revive a Lapsed Right?
GENERAL RULE
Rules of procedure including limitation applicable on date when 'cause of action' arose will apply when action is taken subsequently
📝 If your cause of action arose on 1 April 2018 — the limitation rules in force on that date govern, even if you take action later. You cannot apply a later, more generous limitation period retroactively to an action that was already time-barred.
EXCEPTION — PROSPECTIVE EXTENSION
Amendment extending period of limitation may be taken retrospectively
Made applicable to actions yet to be taken with additional limitation
BUT: where underlying right was taken under unamended law → extension will NOT revive the right
📝 If the law extends limitation from 3 years to 5 years — cases where the 3-year period had NOT yet expired can benefit from the extension. But if the 3-year period had ALREADY EXPIRED before the amendment — the right is dead, and the extension does NOT bring it back to life.
SECTION 168A — GST SPECIFIC
Extension of limitation NOT revived UNLESS expressly restored by Legislature or by Government under §168A of Central GST Act
§168A = special power to extend timelines for specific categories in force majeure situations
📝 Section 168A (inserted post-Covid) gives Government power to extend GST timelines in force majeure situations (like pandemic, natural disasters). But this power must be specifically exercised — it does not automatically extend all timelines. And extension under §168A cannot revive rights already extinguished.
8.2.7 — Acquiescence | Validity of Service | Notice on Common Portal | §160(2) CGST ⇧ Index
📬 Acquiescence — Allowing Limitation to Lapse = Most Effective Way of Rejecting Remedy
What is Acquiescence?
Knowledge of:
(i) Outcome being adverse
(ii) Availability of opportunity of remediation
(iii) Facilities to seek redressal
AND still doing nothing
📝 Acquiescence = "I knew, I could have acted, I chose not to." It is the strongest form of accepting the outcome. Once you acquiesce — courts say "you have accepted the outcome." But IGNORANT person cannot be assumed to have acquiesced.
ESCAPE from §160(2)
§160(2) CGST has limited application
Acquiescence built into §160(2) can be overcome if:
(i) Lack of knowledge demonstrated
(ii) Passive concealment of rights/remedies/safeguards by Revenue exposed
📝 Section 160(2) CGST says proceedings are not invalid for minor procedural defects if no one objected. But this "acquiescence" in §160(2) can be defeated — if you show you didn't know about the defect, or if Revenue actively concealed information about your rights.
Acquiescence Cannot Be Lightly Admitted
Especially when non-service of notice or order is pleaded in appeal
Appellate Authority is OBLIGED to examine 'validity' of service of notice, order or communication
📝 If you say "I never received the notice" — the appellate authority MUST examine whether service was valid. They cannot say "you are raising this late, so you have acquiesced." Non-service prevents limitation from starting to run — how can you acquiesce to something you never knew about?
Validity of service ≠ just the 'mode' of service listed in §169 CGST Act
Validity of service touches validity of commencement of due process of law to make any demand
NOT a mere question of fact — it is a question of law
📝 Service is not just "did the email reach?" Service is: was the chosen mode the most expeditious way to PUT THE PERSON ON NOTICE? If the Department chose the slowest, least effective mode — and then says "limitation ran from date of service via that mode" — that is a fraud on the taxpayer's rights.
Service = Question of Judiciousness
"Service is a question of judiciousness in selecting the 'mode' that is most expeditious manner of 'putting at notice' for the counter-Party to respond. Fairness in due process of law will not evade any person when a least expeditious mode of service was selected in 'putting at notice'."
Appellate Authority enjoined with duty to examine fairness in adjudication must bring this examination with validity of service
💡 Eg.486: Notice served on Common Portal — limitation commences on date when Noticee came to KNOW of the notice (not upload date). Eg.487: Order served via e-mail — appeal may be filed on date when Appellant came to know about the order when DRC13 was served on the Bank (first actual knowledge).
8.2.8 — Prescription | §25 Easements Act | §27 Limitation Act | Acquisitive & Extinctive ⇧ Index
⚖️ Prescription — Statutory Provision Declaring a Vested Right to Be Extinguished or Acquired
📝 Prescription is different from limitation. Limitation only bars the remedy. Prescription actually changes the legal position — it either creates a NEW right (acquisitive prescription) or destroys an EXISTING right (extinctive prescription). Inaction for the prescribed period causes this change by operation of law — no court order needed.
Acquisitive Prescription
Right is ACQUIRED
§25 Easements Act
Access and use of light/air/way/water peaceably enjoyed as easement, as of right, without interruption, for 20 years → right becomes absolute and indefeasible
📝 If you have peacefully used a pathway over your neighbour's land as if it were your right — for 20 uninterrupted years — you have ACQUIRED an easement by prescription. No document, no registration — just 20 years of peaceful use. The right is now absolute.
20 YEAR PERIOD
Taken to be period ending within 2 years next before institution of suit where claim is contested
Government property → 30 years
INTERRUPTION
Not an interruption unless:
(i) Actual discontinuance of possession by obstruction by act of another person
(ii) AND such obstruction submitted to or acquiesced in for one year after claimant has notice
📝 If someone puts up a wall blocking your path — that is NOT automatically an interruption. Only if you accept/acquiesce to the blockage for 1 full year after knowing about it. If you protest and litigate within 1 year — the 20-year clock continues running.
Extinctive Prescription
Right is EXTINGUISHED
§27 Limitation Act
At determination of period limited for any person for instituting suit for possession of any property → his right to such property shall be extinguished
📝 If you don't sue for possession of your property within the limitation period — you don't just lose the RIGHT TO SUE. You actually lose TITLE to the property. This is extinctive prescription. Adverse possession works this way — after 12 years, the original owner loses title.
GST APPLICATION
Deep study of prescription can help locate:
Extinctive prescription in §16(4) CGST (ITC timeline) vs §65(4) (audit timeline)
Test depends on construct of statutory provision
📝 Is §16(4) limitation (ITC claim within time) a limitation (bars remedy but right survives) or prescription (extinguishes the ITC right itself)? Language of §16(4) says "shall not be entitled to take" — this reads as extinctive prescription, not mere limitation. Once the period expires, the entitlement itself is gone.
💡 Eg.488: §107(11) CGST — "no appeal shall be filed…unless…" → appeal without pre-deposit does NOT exist in law → non est (extinctive — right to file appeal without pre-deposit is extinguished). Eg.489: §16(4) CGST — "shall not be entitled to take ITC" → NOT "no credit shall be taken unless" → entitlement-based (extinctive of the entitlement, not just the remedy).
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 8 | Limitation Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Nazir Ahmed v. King Emperor | AIR 1936 PC 253
Privy Council | 1936 | Para 8.1 | Foundational Rule of Statutory Procedure
Key Ratio
When a statute requires a thing to be done in a particular manner, it must be done in that manner or not at all.
When to Use — The opening blow in every limitation/procedural challenge. When Department has not followed the prescribed procedure (SCN not issued within time, not in prescribed form, not served in prescribed manner) — cite this Privy Council maxim to establish that the procedural deviation is not a technicality but goes to the root of the action's validity.
Ramlal v. Rewa Coalfields Ltd. | 1962 AIR SC 361
Supreme Court of India | 1962 | Para 8.2.1 | Vested Right Created by Lapse of Limitation
Key Ratio
Where affirmative action is required, omission to take such action creates a vested right to counter-party once limitation lapses. Limitation is a procedure to take legal action in respect of a vested right (or actionable cause). Lapse of limitation denies the remedy in law (to take this action) but does not destroy the right. Prescription destroys the right itself.
When to Use — (i) When challenging time-barred SCN: argue the lapse of §73 or §74 limitation has created a VESTED RIGHT in the taxpayer that no demand will be raised — this right cannot be lightly unseated. (ii) When opposing condonation application by Department: cite to argue the taxpayer's vested right (acquired by lapse of limitation) must prevail and condonation cannot routinely unseat it.
Interest Reipublicae Ut Sit Finis Litium — Purpose of Limitation
Latin Maxim | Para 8.2.2 | Universally applicable in all limitation disputes
Key Ratio
It is for the general welfare that a period be put to litigation to ensure that legal remedies are kept alive for a definite duration of time. Dormant claims have more cruelty than justice; evidence could be lost to prove a stale claim; and a person with a good cause must pursue it diligently. No State action can survive beyond the maximum limitation in section 74(10) whose certainty is made explicitly clear in section 75(10) of Central GST Act.
When to Use — When Department argues that limitation should be condoned because "the demand is large" or "tax evasion is serious." Counter: public interest itself demands finality to litigation — the seriousness of the demand does not override the limitation. Courts have specifically held that §74(10) + §75(10) create a hard stop on State action — no exception for "serious" cases.
Jai Mangal Oraon v. Mira Nayak | AIR 2000 SC 2276
Supreme Court of India | 2000 | Para 8.2.2 | Reasonable Time Even Without Specific Limitation
Key Ratio
In the absence of limitation for a specific case, action cannot be permitted to be taken without time limit. Reasonable time must be read into this authority to take action. Relief admissible can be denied due to inordinate delay.
When to Use — When Department takes action under a provision that has no specified limitation period (e.g., certain revisionary powers, certain rectification orders). Argue: even without a specific period, "reasonable time" must be read in. Action taken after inordinate and unexplained delay must be struck down — courts will deny relief even where no specific limitation exists.
Section 3 Limitation Act — Mandatory Dismissal | Time-Barring is Fatal
Limitation Act, 1963 | Section 3 | Para 8.2.5 | Applicable to all GST proceedings via §29
Key Principle
Section 3 of the Limitation Act makes it mandatory for the suit, appeal or application to be dismissed even if the question about time-barring has not been pleaded. Time-barring is fatal to the proceedings. A person without realizing that the notice issued is time-barred cannot acquiesce to the proceedings and then suffer the demand due to omission to plead time-bar. Section 29 of the Limitation Act makes section 3 apply to every other special law (including GST) and sections 4 to 24 apply to the extent they may apply.
When to Use — (i) When adjudicating officer confirms a time-barred demand saying "taxpayer did not raise limitation in reply to SCN." Counter: §3 requires the authority to apply limitation suo motu — taxpayer's silence does not cure the time-bar. (ii) When challenging a confirmed demand in appeal: limitation can be raised for the first time in appeal — it is not too late, because the authority should have applied it on its own.
Condonation — Every Day of Delay Must Be Explained | Speaking Order Required
Limitation Act §5 | Para 8.2.4 | Applicable in all appeal/application condonation matters
Key Principle
No vested right exists in seeking condonation. Lapse of limitation procures a vested right to counter-party which cannot be lightly unseated. Every day of delay must be explained. Condonation will not be allowed routinely unless every day of delay from the end of the statutory period of limitation is explained. Condonation is not an emotional and subjective appeal — it is an objective one where: (a) pleadings must be precise with explanation of each day of delay and justification to invoke power; and (b) assertions must be evidenced with affidavit or other real and plausible proof with strong prima facie grounds. Rejection of condonation applications requires a 'speaking order' that is subject to appeal and judicial review of the validity of rejection.
When to Use — (i) When opposing Revenue's condonation application for filing departmental appeal late: cite to argue vested right of taxpayer (from lapse of Revenue's limitation) cannot be casually unseated; Revenue must explain every day with contemporaneous evidence. (ii) When filing condonation: cite the standards to ensure your own application meets the objective test — plausibility + connection between reason and delay.
Acquiescence & Validity of Service | Limitation Runs from Date of KNOWLEDGE
Para 8.2.7 | §169 CGST Act | Applicable in all portal service / e-mail service / knowledge disputes
Key Principle
Acquiescence cannot be lightly admitted, especially when non-service of notice or order is pleaded in appeal. The Appellate Authority is obliged to examine the 'validity' of service of notice, order or communication. Validity of service is not limited to the 'mode' of service listed in section 169 of Central GST Act. Service is a question of judiciousness in selecting the 'mode' that is most expeditious manner of 'putting at notice' for the counter-Party to respond. Fairness in due process of law will not evade any person when a least expeditious mode of service was selected. Validity of service touches the validity of commencement of due process of law to make any demand — it is not a mere question of fact.
When to Use — (i) When Department argues appeal is time-barred because the order was uploaded on the portal on date X and taxpayer did not appeal within 3 months. Counter: limitation ran from date taxpayer came to KNOW of the order (e.g., when DRC-13 was served on bank). Portal upload ≠ valid service unless taxpayer had actual/constructive knowledge. (ii) When defending against "acquiescence" arguments: ignorant taxpayer cannot acquiesce; Revenue must prove knowledge of outcome + opportunity + facilities for redressal — all three must be present.
Abuse of Law — Premature Action Before Expiry of Appeal Limitation
Para 8.2.3 | Applicable in all premature recovery / garnishee / credit blocking matters
Key Principle
Until limitation lapses, counter-party is liable NOT to take any 'permitted action' that could prejudice taxpayer's remedies. When statute permits appeal and appeal holds hope of certain relief, Adjudicating Authority cannot undermine appellate process by taking permitted action prematurely — rendering consideration of facts and merits by Appellate Authority empty and otiose. When permitted action must wait, taking action that is not permitted is abuse of law. Recovery action (without good and sufficient reasons) before expiration of limitation to file appeal against adjudication order = abuse of law. Blocking of credits under Rule 88A in respect of credit claimed in belated returns to enforce demand due to section 16(4) bypassing due process of issuing notice of demand in respect of credit wrongly availed = abuse of law.
When to Use — (i) When Department takes recovery action (garnishee, bank attachment) while appeal limitation period is still running. File petition/stay citing abuse of law — the taxpayer still has a subsisting right to appeal and the recovery action undermines it. (ii) When Department blocks ITC under Rule 88A without issuing SCN — cite abuse of law; due process requires notice before blocking.
Section 16(4) CGST — Extinctive Prescription vs Limitation | Language Test
CGST Act Section 16(4) | Para 8.2.8 | Applicable in all ITC time-bar disputes
Key Principle
Deep study into the nature of 'prescription' can help locate if there are any extinctive prescriptions in GST — say the timeline prescribed in section 16(4) of Central GST Act. The language employed in section 16(4) of CGST Act does not state something like "no credit shall be taken unless it is taken within…" but expresses the timeline in the words "shall not be entitled to take input tax credit". Section 16(2)(b) is a substantive provision and a vesting condition which operates as a divesting condition if not satisfied. Time limit to claim credit under section 16(4) cannot occur earlier than the time by when supplies can possibly be received completely.
When to Use — When challenging Department's demand for reversal of ITC under §16(4) time-bar: the §16(4) timeline is tied to §16(2)(b) — the vesting condition. ITC cannot be claimed before supplies are received. Therefore the time limit for claiming ITC cannot start before all supplies are received. Also useful to argue that §16(4) limitation is "directory" in nature because §16(2)(b) is substantive — substantive provisions prevail over procedural ones where there is conflict.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 9a — Income-tax Act (Overview)

📊 Chapter 9 — Income-tax Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Danger
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
9.1 Relevance to GST
9.2.1 Person
9.2.2 Liaison Office in India
9.2.3 Permanent Establishment
9.2.4 Overseas Branch Office
9.2.5 Vodafone Amendment
9.2.6 Section 56
9.2.7 PE Types
9.3.1 Accounting Income
9.3.2 Taxable Income
9.3.3 Presumptive Income
9.3.4 Income Attributed to PE
9.3.5 ALP
9.3.6 ICDS
9.3.7 Non-income
9.3.8 Exempt Income
9.3.9 Diversion by Overriding Charge
9.3.10 Section 199 / TDS
9.3.11 Foreign Tax Credit
9.3.12 Unexplained Credits
9.4.1 Asset
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
9.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
📊 Income-tax Act — Relevance to GST
Why IT-Act matters in GST disputes
⚠️ DANGER 1
Assertions before IT-authorities = unimpeachable evidence against taxpayer in GST
📝 Whatever you admitted to the income-tax officer about your turnover, income or transactions — GST department can use those same admissions against you. You cannot take back what you said in one forum.
💡 Turnover offered for IT purposes is greater than GSTR returns → Department issues demand notice — hundreds of thousands of such cases exist.
BEYOND LINEAR
Other matters beyond turnover comparison — tax positions taken for IT expose underlying factual position in GST
📝 It's not just about turnover numbers. Cross-border reporting, domestic IT-law positions, and GAAR all expose facts that affect GST treatment.
✓ SAFE ZONE
Except anti-avoidance (GAAR / presumptive taxation) — all other IT provisions demand strict interpretation similar to GST
📝 Good news: income-tax law (except GAAR and special presumptions) is read strictly — just like GST. So arguments on strict construction apply equally in both laws.
9.2.1 — Person [Section 2(31)] ⇧ Index
👤 Person — IT-Act vs GST Concept
Incidence: Tax on 'person'; Measure: 'income'
IT-ACT
'Global person' — legal entity whose global income is exposed based on proximate connection with India
📝 Income-tax sees you as one global person — all your income worldwide is taxable in India if you have a connection with India. Even income earned abroad can be caught.
GST-ACT
'Distinct person' — legal fiction vivisects person into multiple distinct taxable entities (HO, BO, branches)
📝 GST does the opposite — it splits one entity into many 'distinct persons.' HO and Branch Office are treated as separate persons for GST purposes even though they are one legal entity.
TYPES OF 'PERSON' u/s 2(31)
Firm | Association of Persons (AoP) | Body of Individuals (BoI) | Artificial Juridical Person (AJP)
Firm does NOT survive demise of all Partners — but admitted as taxable entity for GST overlap purposes
📝 A partnership firm is not a legal entity in strict law — it dissolves when all partners die. But for tax purposes, it is recognised as a taxable entity. This matters in GST because the 'person' who is registered for GST is based on PAN — and PAN is issued to these entities.
VEIL-LIFTING
GST rarely lifts the veil. Section 122(1A) separately exposes persons behind the entity to punitive action — in addition to Section 122(1) and 122(2)
📝 'Lifting the corporate veil' means looking behind the company to hold the directors/owners personally liable. GST rarely does this — but Section 122(1A) is the exception where individuals behind the entity face personal punishment.
9.2.2 — Liaison Office in India ⇧ Index
🏢 Liaison Office ('LO') in India
Also: Representative Office / Rep Office — Office of foreign legal entity
📝 An LO is a small window of a foreign company in India — allowed to promote business but NOT to earn income. Think of it as a notice board, not a shop counter.
✓ PERMITTED ACTIVITIES
(FEMA Schedule II, Reg. 4(b))
(i) Representing parent/group companies in India
(ii) Promoting export/import from/to India
(iii) Promoting technical/financial collaborations
(iv) Communication channel between parent & Indian companies
✗ NOT PERMITTED
'Income generating activities' in India — costs must be met from inward remittances only
📝 The LO cannot earn a single rupee in India. It must be funded entirely from its foreign parent. If it earns anything in India — it crosses the line and becomes taxable.
⚠️ GST TRIGGER
If IT-inquiry exposes LO indulging in income-generating activities → falls within 'business' u/s 2(17) CGST Act → compulsory registration u/s 24
📝 Once an LO is found doing business (even if illegal under FEMA), GST must be applied. The FEMA violation is irrelevant to GST — if there's income, there's GST.
💡 Eg.490: LO doing marketing activities, employees paid commission on sales referrals — income-generating = GST exposure.
Eg.491: LO with 50 employees → business-like activities → tax on income attributable.
Eg.492: LO services = intermediary services; POS within India → taxable, not zero-rated.
9.2.3 — Permanent Establishment (PE) in India ⇧ Index
🌐 Permanent Establishment ('PE')
Article 5, UN Model Double Tax Convention | Branch/Project Office set up in India by overseas entity
📝 A PE is when a foreign company is so active in India that Indian law treats it 'as if' it has a taxable presence here — even though it is not registered here. It's a legal fiction — a deemed Indian entity.
PE INCLUDES
Branch office, Project office — set up in India by overseas legal entities
PE DOES NOT INCLUDE
Liaison office | Separate legal entity (Associated Enterprise) — unless it acts as instrumentality of overseas entity generating unreported income
📝 A separate Indian subsidiary is NOT a PE — unless the subsidiary is actually running the foreign company's business secretly and not reporting that income as its own.
GST Consequence of PE Admission / Allegation
(a) Income attributable to PE → satisfies 'turnover from outward supplies' → HSN, POS, exemptions, tariff credits apply
(b) Cross-cancelling supplies within 'net' income → examined separately
(c) Taxable value of adjustments required — Sec 15(4) CGST + Rules 27-31
KEY RULE
Taxable person = PE and NOT the overseas legal entity. Place of supply is unlikely to be outside India for a PE in India.
💡 Eg.493: Malaysian entity's Project Office in India → secured highway contract from NHAI → PO is PE → construction services to NHAI have POS in India for the Malaysian entity acting through PO.
⚠️ ARTICLE 5(4) — What is NOT PE (OECD Model)
(a) Storage/display/delivery of goods
(b) Stock maintenance for storage/display
(c) Stock for processing by another enterprise
(d) Fixed place for purchasing goods / collecting info
(e) Preparatory or auxiliary activities only
(f) Combination of (a)-(e) if overall = preparatory/auxiliary
📝 Even if Art. 5(4) says "not a PE" — the underlying transactions must still be unbundled and treated under GST as applicable. The exclusion from PE status does not mean exclusion from GST.
ART. 5(5) — Deemed PE via Agent
Person (not independent agent) habitually exercising authority to conclude contracts in Contracting State on behalf of enterprise → enterprise deemed to have PE
ART. 5(6) — NOT PE
Enterprise carried on through broker, general commission agent, or any other agent of independent status acting in ordinary course of business
📝 Art 5(5) and 5(6) exclusions expose GST implications that taxpayers miss — where this exclusion is availed, underlying transactions must be unbundled for GST treatment.
9.2.4 — Overseas Branch Office (BO) ⇧ Index
🌍 Overseas Branch Office ('BO')
Indian entity establishing BO outside India | Section 8, IGST Act — Distinct Person Fiction
📝 Indian companies are allowed to open branch offices abroad. But under GST (Section 8, IGST), the HO in India and BO outside India are 'distinct persons' — meaning any service from HO to BO is a supply and vice versa.
Section 8, IGST Act — Two Explanations for 'Distinct Person'
Explanation 1
Establishment in India + any other establishment outside India → distinct persons. Also within States (inter-state) and within business verticals.
Explanation 2
Person carrying on business through branch or agency or representational office in any territory → treated as having an establishment in that territory
⚠️ CRITICAL GST RULE
Export of services from HO (India) to BO (outside India) is NOT admissible to zero-rating benefits u/s 16(1) read with Section 2(6), IGST Act.
Condition (v) of Section 2(6): Supplier and recipient must NOT merely be establishments of a distinct person (Explanation 1, Section 8).
📝 Normally, services exported abroad = zero-rated (no GST). But when the recipient abroad is your own BO — the export is NOT zero-rated. You must charge GST on HO-to-BO services. This is a huge trap for Indian IT/consulting firms with overseas branches.
HO–BO Spectrum — 3 Case Analysis
CriteriaCase A (HO supplies)Case B (Shared)Case C (BO supplies)
Contract with CustomerHOHOBO
Legal authority to performHOHOBO
Physical infrastructureBOBOBO
Human resourcesBOBOBO
Quality assurance-oversightHOHOBO
Working capitalHOHOBO
Indemnity and warrantyHOHOBO
Experience and expertiseHOHO+BOBO
Locus to sue CustomerHOHO+BOBO
Defendant in suit by CustomerHOHO+BOBO
KEY TESTS to determine HO vs BO as supplier: (a) Privity of contract — against HO or BO? (b) HO's unilateral authority to reassign from BO1 to BO2 without Customer consent? (c) Indemnity/warranty expected against HO or BO?
💡 Eg.495: Contract by BO (Singapore) + work by HO (Chennai) → supply by HO to BO not zero-rated.
💡 Eg.496: Contract by HO (Delhi) + work by BO (Germany, deputed staff) → import of services by BO from HO → billed to BO without zero-rating.
💡 Eg.497: Contract by HO (Mumbai) + work by BO (London, local UK employees) → project costs billed to HO = import of services.
💡 Eg.498: Contract by BO (Sydney) + work by BO → no supply between HO and BO. BO revenues = Case C, independent.
9.2.5 — 'Vodafone' Amendment [Taxation Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021] ⇧ Index
📜 Vodafone Amendment — Section 9(1)(i), IT Act
Taxation Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021 | Indirect Transfer Taxation
📝 This is the law that came after India lost the Vodafone case — it allows India to tax transactions between two non-Indian companies if the deal's value substantially comes from Indian assets. Called 'indirect transfer' taxation.
WHAT IT DOES
Two non-resident entities transact — if substantial value of transaction = attributable to establishment in India → tax charge attracted in India
📝 Example: Company A (Cayman Islands) sells its shares to Company B (Mauritius). If those shares derive most of their value from Indian assets — India can tax this transfer even though no Indian party is involved.
GST BOUNDARY
This amendment is NOT to transplant IT legal fictions as facts for GST. Transaction remains that of two overseas entities — does not engender GST treatment due to limitation of 'taxable territory'
📝 Even though IT can tax this deal, GST cannot. The transaction is between two overseas parties — there is no Indian 'taxable territory' involved in the GST sense. IT fiction stays within IT law.
Explanation 5, Section 9(1)(i)
An asset/capital asset being share or interest in company registered/incorporated outside India → shall be deemed situated in India if it derives, directly or indirectly, its value substantially from assets located in India
9.2.6 — Section 56(2) — 'Income from Other Sources' Legal Fictions ⇧ Index
💰 Section 56(2) — Deemed Receipts as Income
Where any person receives on/after 1st April 2017 from any person(s)
📝 Section 56(2) catches gifts, property received below fair value etc. and treats them as income — even though they are not earnings from business. This creates a legal fiction that creates 'income' without any real transaction. GST asks: if IT taxes it as income, does a taxable supply exist?
(a) Sum of Money
Without consideration, aggregate > ₹50,000 → entire aggregate value taxable as income
(b)(A) Immovable Property
Without consideration, stamp duty value > ₹50,000 → stamp duty value = income
(b)(B) Immovable Property
For consideration BUT stamp duty value exceeds consideration AND excess > higher of ₹50,000 or 5% of consideration → excess = income
GST VALUATION TRIGGER
Where IT admits/assesses tax on gains from transaction price being lower than fair value → the rejected transacted price would attract GST valuation review if said transaction is otherwise liable to GST
📝 If IT officer rejects your transaction price as too low and taxes the fair value — GST cannot ignore this. GST valuation rules (Section 15) also permit rejection of transaction value if parties are related/connected. So IT's finding about undervaluation opens GST valuation door too.
💡 Eg.500: ESOP discount to employees of other distinct persons/subsidiaries → GST on supply as ESOP expense — NOT the securities transaction itself.
💡 Eg.501: ESOP charge from overseas entity to employees of Indian subsidiary where employer-employee relations do not exist → GST applicable.
9.2.7 — Types of Permanent Establishment (PE) outside India ⇧ Index
🌐 Forms of PE — Indian Entity Outside India
Dissimilar treatment: Outbound vs Inbound leg of transaction
(a) Fixed location
(b) Construction
(c) Service
(d) Dependent agency
(e) Subsidiary
KEY NOTE
PE exists but transactions are at arm's length price (ALP) → Article 5(4) OECD → existence of PE may be ignored for income-tax. But GST is NOT obliged to extend same courtesy — GST will latch on to PE existence and impose treatment due.
📝 ALP (Arm's Length Price) = fair market price between unrelated parties. If a transaction that creates a PE is priced at fair market rate, IT ignores the PE. But GST doesn't care about ALP — if a PE exists, GST tax is due regardless.
💡 Eg.504: Payment to Agent (Agency PE) at ALP → implies existence of supply AND intermediary services — POS may be in India.
💡 Eg.505: Payment to Contractor (Service PE) at ALP → implies supply of installation/other services → location-dependent, not export.
💡 Eg.506: Fee for Installation PE at ALP → supply of lease/license services → location-dependent.
9.3.1 — Accounting Income vs GST Turnover ⇧ Index
📒 Accounting Income — The Gap with GST Turnover
Rules of accounting ≠ GST turnover | Differences are explainable
📝 Accounting income and GST turnover are two different things, measured by different rules. Just because they don't match does NOT mean one of them is wrong or that tax has escaped assessment.
TIMING DIFFERENCES
Differences that normalize over time when multi-year data is taken
💡 Eg.502: Contractor invoices admitted as IT revenue but not recognised under % completion method until threshold reached — resolves by project end.
Eg.507: 100% depreciation in IT — asset still in use = No GST effect.
PERMANENT DIFFERENCES
Differences that remain permanently — explainable by dissimilar treatment rules
💡 Eg.503: Depreciation rate in books vs IT rate → permanent difference — no GST effect.
⚠️ INCOME ≠ TURNOVER
Income may be reported due to nature of accounting treatment — e.g., Supplier's account balance reversal as 'income' after renegotiated payments.
This is probably the simplest example of income that does NOT represent turnover — renegotiated liability written back ≠ outward supply.
📝 If you renegotiated a supplier's bill downward — the balance no longer payable gets written back as 'income' in your P&L. The IT officer sees 'income'. But this was never a 'sale' — you didn't supply anything. Income here ≠ turnover for GST.
DEFENCE PRINCIPLE
Existence of difference between accounting income and turnover in tax returns is no indicator, much less presumption, that turnover has escaped assessment.
Most prudent validation: confirm timing difference + collect multi-year cumulative data.
💡 Eg.508: No GST on provisions created towards expenses.
💡 Eg.509: No GST on reversal of provisions as 'other income'.
💡 Eg.510: No GST on 'unbilled revenue' where tax invoice not yet issued.
💡 Eg.511: No GST on reversals in 'unbilled revenue account' since GST already paid on tax invoices.
9.3.2 — Taxable Income vs GST Turnover ⇧ Index
💼 Taxable Income — Even Further from GST Turnover
Taxable Income = Accounting Income − Excludible + Includible amounts
📝 Taxable income is even further from GST turnover than accounting income. It excludes some accounting income and includes some non-accounting items — making it an unreliable basis for GST computation.
ADMISSIONS IN IT = GST FACT
Additions to taxable income admitted by taxpayer → directly relatable to turnover that may not have been admitted for GST — admissions are assertions on facts
📝 What you admitted in your IT return as additional income can be used against you in GST to show you had undisclosed turnover. Admissions in one law cannot be insulated from implications in another.
BURDEN GAP
IT: presumption on unexplained credits → imputes income, lighter burden on Revenue (11 taxing ingredients not needed)
GST: 'no supply, no tax' — all 11 taxing ingredients must be established
📝 In IT, Revenue can demand tax by pointing to unexplained bank credits. In GST, Revenue must prove there was an actual supply, HSN, value, taxable person, etc. — all 11 ingredients. Much harder for Revenue in GST than in IT.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Facts admitted or accepted in income-tax cannot be without examining implications in GST — and vice versa. Contest of any demand in IT cannot be insulated from implications in GST. Admission (even reluctantly) is an assertion on facts.
9.3.3 — Presumptive Income ⇧ Index
📋 Presumptive Income — Small Taxpayers
Permitted NOT to maintain books; discharge IT on % of gross revenue
📝 Small taxpayers in IT can avoid bookkeeping by paying tax on a fixed % of their revenue (presumed income). They don't need to maintain records for IT. But for GST, they still need full record-based reporting.
⚠️ GST CANNOT FOLLOW IT
No information derivable about inward/outward supplies from tax returns of presumptive taxpayers.
For GST purposes, taxpayers may operate on record-based GST reporting while operating on record-less IT reporting.
These two regimes are independent — IT presumption does NOT translate to GST.
📝 Just because IT accepted your turnover on presumptive basis doesn't mean GST must accept the same. GST demands actual supply records, invoices, and returns — regardless of IT treatment.
9.3.4 — Income Attributed to PE ⇧ Index
🔢 Income Attributed to PE — GST Relevance
PE admitted or imputed in investigation
ADMITTED PE
Attribution based on functional analysis, industry standards, case-specific adjustments → arrives at 'income of PE'
📝 When a company admits a PE, tax officers calculate how much income should be attributed to that PE using complex formulas. For GST, this income = turnover from outward supplies — so the full GST treatment must follow.
IMPUTED PE
Where PE not admitted but imputed in investigation → outcome of investigation must be considered to determine whether taxable supplies existed in relevant FY
📝 Even if the company denies having a PE, if IT investigation alleges one — this allegation itself triggers a GST review of whether taxable supplies happened. The denial in IT cannot stop GST from examining the facts.
BOTH ENDS RULE
Income of PE = Expense of HO and vice versa → GST treatment must be extended to both ends of the transaction.
Zero-rating is NOT admissible between HO in India and BO outside India.
Tax liability on 'import of services' applies.
📝 If PE earns, HO pays. If HO earns, PE pays. Both sides of the coin must be examined for GST. You cannot apply GST on the income side but ignore the expense side — or claim zero-rating for HO-to-BO transactions.
SAFE HARBOUR RULE
Any PE admitting income not below the 'safe harbour' threshold → safe from further income attribution inquiry. GST still independently applies on actual supplies.
9.3.5 — Arm's Length Price (ALP) ⇧ Index
⚖️ Arm's Length Price (ALP) — IT vs GST Position
Where transaction constitutes PE but payment is at ALP
📝 ALP means pricing a transaction as if between unrelated parties at fair market rates. In IT, paying ALP gives immunity from PE consequences. But in GST — no such immunity. GST looks past ALP and applies full tax treatment.
IT POSITION
ALP payment → existence of PE eclipsed → income-tax satisfied with tax collected on ALP basis → PE treatment ignored
GST POSITION
ALP is NOT a defence in GST. GST will latch on to PE existence and impose treatment due, notwithstanding that IT is satisfied with ALP. ALP compliance = clear assertion that PE EXISTS.
📝 Paying ALP-based tax actually confirms for GST purposes that a supply relationship exists. By paying ALP, you have admitted the PE. GST then demands the full tax — independent of what IT accepted.
GST CONSEQUENCE OF ALP
Non-application of IT treatment due to ALP = clear indicator + positive assertion of fact that GST must extend scope of enquiry to discover:
(i) Tariff classification | (ii) Nature of supply | (iii) Taxable value — since existence of supply is indubitable
9.3.6 — ICDS (Income Computation and Disclosure Standards) ⇧ Index
📊 ICDS — Adjustments from Accounting to Taxable Income
Financial Statements → ICDS-adjusted → Taxable Income
📝 ICDS are special income standards that override normal accounting rules for tax purposes. A company's financial statements are first prepared under accounting standards, then 'adjusted' per ICDS to arrive at taxable income. These ICDS adjustments are mostly irrelevant for GST.
10 ICDS Issued — Topics of Interest
(a) Accounting policies
(b) Inventory valuation
(c) Construction contracts
(d) Revenue recognition
(e) Fixed assets
(f) Forex rate changes
(g) Government grants
(h) Securities
(i) Borrowing costs
(j) Provisions & contingencies
GST POSITION
Tax treatment depends on 'time of supply' — where tax invoice issued per time of supply, differences between revenue in financial statements and GST returns will NOT result in escapement of revenue.
Anxiety from linear year-comparison is misplaced — multi-year data cures timing differences.
9.3.7 — Non-income (Section 10) ⇧ Index
🚫 Non-income — Section 10 Exclusions
IT excludes certain accounting income from being 'income' — NOT same as GST exemption
📝 Section 10 items are NOT taxed in IT — but this is not because they are 'exempt'. IT excludes them from the very definition of income. GST doesn't care about this IT exclusion — if the underlying transaction is a supply, GST applies independently.
IT POSITION
Section 10 items → excluded from 'income' → NOT taxable. Express provision declaring certain income as 'not income' → implies those transactions ARE outward supplies treated 'as if' they were not income
📝 Section 10 is a legal fiction. It pretends certain transactions didn't produce income. But this fiction stays within IT law only.
GST POSITION
IT legal fiction cannot pollinate treatment due to underlying transaction in GST. GST may apply on outward supply which IT-law continues to disregard as income.
📝 GST applies on what actually happened — not on what IT decided to call 'income' or 'non-income'. Existence of Section 10 fiction is evidence that a supply occurred — it just escaped IT, not necessarily GST.
💡 Eg.512: Income from charitable activities not admitted as income in IT (based on 'application' of income). But GST allows exemption to 'source' of income from charitable activities. Definition of charity is different in both statutes — examine independently.
💡 Eg.513: Surplus from specified infrastructure projects = exclusion from IT income, but no equivalent exclusion from taxable outward supplies under GST.
9.3.8 — Exempt Income ⇧ Index
🎁 Exempt Income — Chapter VI-A Special Deductions
Expenditure disallowed in IT but claimed as special deduction → GST trigger
📝 In IT, exemption means subtracting expenditure from income to arrive at 'net taxable income'. Chapter VI-A allows special deductions for expenses not otherwise admissible. When these are claimed, it is a signal for GST review.
GST TRIGGER
Expenditure NOT allowed as deduction against income AND claimed as special deduction (voluntary or in assessment) = positive assertion to review GST treatment applicable
📝 If IT disallows your expense but you still get a special deduction — GST must review whether that expenditure represents an inward supply on which ITC or reversal obligations arise. Contradiction in facts between IT and GST is itself a finding.
💡 Eg.514: Corporate gifts where ITC reversed u/s 17(5)(h) — claimed as business expenditure in IT (Ch. IV, Sections 28-44D) — contradiction in facts between IT and GST claims.
💡 Eg.515: IT payable on annual lettable value of house property → NOT liable to GST due to notional income being taxed in absence of actual transaction of letting-out.
9.3.9 — Diversion of Income by Overriding Charge ⇧ Index
⚡ Diversion by Overriding Charge
Income diverted before it reaches recipient's hands
📝 'Overriding charge' is when a portion of income is legally diverted to another person before the first person even gets it. Like a third party having a prior claim on your income. This diversion is NOT an expenditure — it is income of the original earner being shared at source.
MUTUALITY
Contribution by members for collective benefit → NOT taxable income as members = association are the same person
OVERRIDING CHARGE
Diversion → becomes income of another → NOT expenditure of former person → NOT liable to TDS & compliance
📝 Because the diverted income was never the first person's income (it was diverted before reaching them), it is NOT their expenditure. So no TDS obligation on the diversion amount.
GST — NOT OBLIGED TO ADMIT SAME TREATMENT
GST not obliged to admit same treatment in view of exceptions in Chapter XVI, CGST Act.
Refer: Mumbai HC in Bai Mamubai ibid
💡 Eg.516: Subscription collected by Foreman is not 'income' of Chit Fund — it is liability.
💡 Eg.517: Pool money collected by wager operator is not 'liability' of Organizer — prize distributed = application, not diversion.
💡 Eg.518: Outgoing partner's goodwill from future Firm receipts = income of Partner, not Firm.
💡 Eg.519: Income deposited in JV common bank account = income of each venturer in agreed ratio, not JV.
9.3.10 — Section 199 / TDS — Tax Deduction at Source ⇧ Index
💳 TDS / Section 199 — Timing of Deduction vs GST
40%+ of IT collection via TDS | Over 40% of IT revenue from source deduction
📝 TDS means the payer deducts tax from the payment before releasing it to the payee. The deducted amount goes to the Government in the name of the payee. Section 199 deals with when that credit should be allowed to the payee.
TDS MECHANISM
Payer deducts TDS even before Payee performs promise → credits based on PAN of Payee → Payee gets credit 'as if' deposited by Payee
📝 TDS happens even before the supplier has delivered anything. The customer deducts tax on the estimated bill. The supplier gets credit for tax already deducted — even if the service hasn't happened yet.
GST KEY PRINCIPLE
IT Act itself admits unreliability of 'timing' of tax deduction → deduction in earlier years is NO PROOF of turnover escaping assessment of that year.
(Investigation cannot be completed after SCN issued; adjudication cannot be on grounds not in notice — Section 75(7) CGST Act)
📝 Just because TDS was deducted in Year 1 on income that materialises in Year 3 — doesn't mean Year 1's GST return under-reported turnover. IT itself says timing of deduction is approximate. GST must accept this.
9.3.11 — Foreign Tax Credit Income ⇧ Index
🌏 Foreign Tax Credit — Indicator of Outward Supply
FTC claimed in India → income existed from outward supplies
📝 When an Indian company earns income abroad and pays tax there, it can claim credit of that foreign tax against Indian IT. This foreign tax credit (FTC) is a signal — it proves income was earned from outward supplies, which GST must examine.
FTC Analysis — Three Case Framework
Case A / Case B
FTC = accurate reflection of ownership of underlying transaction → HO made outward supply → full GST treatment on inward and outward supplies applies
Case C
Income earned and received outside India by overseas PE/BO → FTC claim = contra-indicator — cannot be taxed in India, similar to high-sea sales (extra-territorial)
📝 If the overseas branch earned and kept the income abroad (Case C), the FTC claim is actually a contradiction — that income can't be taxed in India for GST. Like high-sea sales — turnover of Indian entity but not GST-liable due to situs outside India.
INVESTIGATION TOOL
Study of IT returns filed by Indian entities in all foreign jurisdictions → map to:
(a) Income offered to tax in those jurisdictions → and
(b) Expenditure claimed against such income in those jurisdictions
→ illuminates GST treatment due in India under IGST Act standards
9.3.12 — Unexplained Credits ⇧ Index
❓ Unexplained Credits — IT vs GST Treatment
Imputed income arises from credits not offered as taxable income
📝 When there are bank credits that can't be explained, IT treats them as income and taxes them. In GST, unexplained credits do NOT automatically become taxable supplies — because 'no supply, no tax' is a fundamental GST principle.
IT POSITION
Unexplained credits → imputed as income → taxed under IT → no deduction allowable → burden on taxpayer to explain
GST POSITION
'No supply, no tax' — GST imputation of income does NOT ipso facto imply it arose from taxable outward supplies. Unaccounted purchases don't imply sales at mark-up attracting GST.
📝 In GST, Revenue must prove an actual supply happened. IT can presume income from unexplained credits — GST cannot make the same jump without establishing a supply transaction. 11 taxing ingredients must be present.
ONLY LEEWAY IN GST
Section 35(6), CGST Act — stock differences (only shortage) may be presumed to have been supplied. But even this is a rebuttable presumption — not an irrebuttable assumption.
💡 Eg.520: Inward supplies in GSTR-2A not belonging to taxpayer, not claimed as credit in GSTR-3B → cannot be forced as purchases and output tax demanded at some contemporaneous selling price. Contrary to law.
💡 Eg.521: Cash found in IT search proceedings → CANNOT be presumed to be from taxable outward supplies to demand output tax.
9.4.1 — Asset vs Expenditure ⇧ Index
🏗️ Asset — 'Real Property' Test
Asset must be 'real property' — anything else is only expenditure
📝 In IT, an 'asset' must have real, enduring value — it is 'real property'. Anything else, however much it costs, is only expenditure. You can capitalise it in your books, but it is NOT an asset in law. This matters for depreciation, ITC, and block of assets in GST.
GST RELEVANCE
Whether an expenditure is an 'asset' or 'expense' determines:
→ ITC eligibility under Section 16, CGST Act
→ ITC block under Section 17(5)(d) — immovable property attached to earth
→ Whether 'plant and machinery' exclusion applies to ITC block
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 9 | Income-tax Act ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision/case name. Chapter 9 has NO court judgments cited — Arsenal below is principle-based from the chapter.
Principle: IT Admissions = Unimpeachable GST Evidence
Chapter 9, Para 9.1 | GST Litigation Reference | CA Jatin Christopher methodology
Key Ratio / Principle
Assertions made before income-tax authorities are readily inferred to be unimpeachable evidence against the taxpayer. Tax positions taken for purposes of income-tax law, domestic and cross-border reporting, can expose the underlying factual position and make the taxpayer suffer the treatment due in GST. Admission, even reluctantly, is an assertion on facts. Facts, after all, are the whole point. What has been admitted in income-tax cannot be without examining implications in GST and vice versa.
When to Use — When taxpayer argues IT-turnover mismatch is irrelevant to GST demand. Counter: admissions in IT proceedings are evidence of underlying facts that GST cannot ignore. Also use when Department raises GST demand solely based on higher IT turnover — press for 11-ingredient supply analysis, not mere linear comparison.
Principle: IT Legal Fiction Cannot Transplant to GST
Chapter 9, Para 9.2.5 / 9.3.7 | Vodafone Amendment Context
Key Ratio / Principle
Legal fiction in income-tax is relevant to locate the boundaries of that legal fiction and not to transplant the assertions arising from legal fiction as facts for purposes of GST. The transaction, for GST purposes, continues to remain that of two overseas entities and notwithstanding the income-tax implications in India, it does not engender any GST treatment due to limitation of 'taxable territory' applicable. Legal fiction in income-tax cannot impact underlying admitted fact for purposes of GST or any other law for that matter.
When to Use — When Department imports an income-tax finding (e.g., Vodafone amendment, Section 56 fiction, PE imputation) directly into GST demand without independently proving the supply. Argue: IT legal fiction stays within IT law — it cannot become GST taxable event without independent supply analysis.
