Principle: 'No Supply, No Tax' — GST vs IT Unexplained Credits
Chapter 9, Para 9.3.12 | Section 35(6), CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
'No supply, no tax' is an inalienable truth in GST. If incidence of GST could arise on every income, then GST would itself become another kind of 'tax on income'. Admission of imputation of income in income-tax proceedings does not ipso facto imply that it has arisen from taxable outward supplies. Unaccounted purchases do not imply that sales at a certain mark-up must have occurred and attract imposition of GST. Only leeway in GST can be found in Section 35(6) where stock differences (only shortage) is permitted to be presumed to have been supplied — but even this is a rebuttable presumption.
When to Use — When GST demand is based entirely on unexplained credits in IT proceedings, search-and-seizure findings, or GSTR-2A mismatches. Press for proof of actual supply. Section 35(6) presumes only shortage — not income-tax additions.
Principle: Accounting Income ≠ GST Turnover — Difference is Explainable
Chapter 9, Para 9.3.1 | Govind Saran Ganga Saran v. CST AIR 1985 SC 1041
Key Ratio / Principle
Income does not imply turnover. Existence of difference between accounting income and turnover reported in tax returns is no indicator, much less any presumption, that turnover has escaped assessment. Differences exist and are explainable either as timing differences that will normalize when cumulative years' data is considered, or as permanent differences that remain explainable due to dissimilar treatment. All taxing ingredients in income-tax and even in GST must be shown to exist. Implicit reliance on accounting income is well endowed to bring overconfidence in foisting logical demands to face public humiliation without adducing material to show how income appearing in financial statements represents turnover.
When to Use — Primary ground when Department issues SCN comparing GSTR-3B with ITR turnover and demands GST on difference. Cite to establish that difference ≠ evasion. Demand multi-year data. Press for supply-by-supply proof.
Principle: ALP Compliance = GST Supply Admission — Treatment Due
Chapter 9, Para 9.3.5 | Article 5(4), OECD Model Tax Convention
Key Ratio / Principle
Non-application of income-tax treatment due to existence of Arm's Length Pricing (ALP) is a clear indicator and a positive assertion of fact that GST must extend the scope of enquiry to discover the tariff classification, nature of supply, and taxable value — since existence of supply is indubitable. GST will latch on to the fact — existence of PE — and impose the treatment due, notwithstanding that income-tax law has long abandoned this fact being satisfied with the treatment based on ALP.
When to Use — In cross-border disputes involving PE, BO, Agency PE where Department/taxpayer relies on ALP pricing as discharge of all obligations. Counter for taxpayer: if ALP-based IT filing admitted a PE, GST now must independently examine all supplies. Counter for Department: ALP compliance = supply admitted, now establish HSN, rate, value.
Principle: TDS Timing ≠ Proof of Turnover Escapement
Chapter 9, Para 9.3.10 | Section 199, Income-tax Act | Section 75(7), CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
The Income-tax Act itself admits the unreliability of 'timing' of tax deduction. This is noteworthy for GST purposes — deduction of tax in earlier years is no proof of turnover escaping assessment of that year. Investigation cannot be continued after show cause notice is issued. And adjudication cannot be made on grounds not existing in the notice (Section 75(7) of Central GST Act).
When to Use — When Department argues that TDS deducted in Year X on payment implies GSTR under-reporting in Year X. Counter: TDS timing is unreliable even by IT's own admission (Section 199). Also cite Section 75(7) CGST Act — adjudication must be on grounds in SCN only.
Principle: HO to BO — NOT Zero-Rated Export
Chapter 9, Para 9.2.4 | Section 2(6) read with Section 16(1), IGST Act | Section 8, IGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Export of services from Head Office (HO) in India to Branch Office (BO) outside India is not admissible to zero-rating benefits under Section 16(1) read with Section 2(6) of the Integrated GST Act. Condition (v) of the definition of 'export of services' requires that the supplier and recipient must not merely be establishments of a distinct person in accordance with Explanation 1 in Section 8. Capacity to contract, legal and operational authority to contract, expectation of indemnity and warranty, within the legal fiction of distinct persons, cannot be glossed over and must be relied upon to guide the identity of the de jure supplier.
When to Use — When Indian IT/ITES/consulting company claims zero-rating on services billed to its own overseas BO. Firmly counter: the supply is not 'export' as per Section 2(6)(v) — HO and BO are establishments of the same distinct person. Full GST applies on HO-to-BO billing.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 9b — Expenditure & Transfer | Income Tax & GST

⚖️ Chapter 9 — Expenditure & Transfer | Income Tax Act & GST

Para 9.4 to 9.6 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root / L1 Node
Permitted / Valid / Allowed
Prohibited / Void / Disallowed
Condition / If / Unless / Example
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
9.4.1 Assets vs Property — Capital Goods
9.4.2 Sunk Cost
9.4.3 Disallowance
9.4.4 Deemed Income
9.4.5 TDS — General
9.4.6 Section 194R — Perquisites
9.4.7 TDS by Mistake
9.4.8 Section 195 — Overseas Remittances
9.5.1 Transfer — Capital Asset
9.5.2 Negative Fiction (Section 47)
9.6.1 Transfer Pricing — Benchmark
9.6.2 Transfer Pricing — Adjustment
9.6.3 CbCR
9.6.4 Domestic TP & GAAR
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
9.4.1 — "Assets" vs "Property" — Capital Goods in GST (Para 9.4) ⇧ Index
📦 Assets vs Property — What qualifies as "Capital Goods" in GST?
Para 9.4 | Income-Tax Act Chapter 9
Core Distinction: Not all 'Assets' (accounting) = 'Property' (legal)
📝 In accounting, 'assets' is a broad word — it includes WIP, prepaid expenses, and even things that have no legal ownership. But in law, 'property' is something where you have real rights against the whole world. Only legal property can be 'capital goods' in GST.
ACCOUNTING ASSETS
Represent enduring benefits expected beyond year of acquisition
NOT necessarily 'real property'
Virtual digital assets, prepaid insurance, WIP costs carried forward — are 'assets' in books but NOT 'property' in law
📝 Eg. Prepaid insurance is shown as asset in balance sheet — but it gives no real ownership right. It cannot be enforced against the world.
💡 Eg.522: Prepaid insurance = not 'asset' for property law. Eg.523: WIP costs = deferment of expenditure, not asset. Eg.526: Lease Premium = not asset though benefits extend beyond current year.
REAL PROPERTY (Legal)
Transferee has rights to exclusion of the whole world
Suit can be brought in Court of law against infringement
All property will be 'assets' if enterprise expects enduring benefits
But NOT vice versa — all assets are NOT property
💡 Eg.524: Inventory (RM, WIP, FG) = assets. Eg.525: Tools & spares = assets even if not inventorized.
Capital Goods in GST — Three Rules
RULE 1
Only goods can be capital goods in GST — not services
(even if services are capitalized in books)
💡 Eg.531: Building constructed = 'capital goods' in GST even though construction = service in GST (Schedule II fiction helps).
RULE 2
'Temporary' structures cannot be capitalized → NOT capital goods for GST
💡 Eg.527: Mud-stone wall = temporary = not capital goods. Eg.528: Cement replacement of mud wall = still not capitalized. Eg.529: Renovation of stadium pavilion = no increase in seating/life = not capital goods.
RULE 3
Schedule II fiction — certain goods 'treated' as services → WILL be capital goods for GST because fiction applies only to 'treatment' not for all purposes
💡 Eg.530: One-time royalty for exhibition rights of Sony Pictures films = capitalized but NOT capital goods in GST (services, not goods).
NOTE Investments in stocks/shares = 'assets' in financial statements but NOT capital goods in GST. Basis for capitalization in books is outsourced by GST to accounting rules and standards of disclosure in audited financial statements.
9.4.2 — Sunk Cost ⇧ Index
🕳️ Sunk Cost — Definition, GST Impact & ITC Consequences
📝 A sunk cost is money spent with the hope of earning income — but that hope has now ended. The expenditure is a dead loss. It cannot be carried forward as a tax loss, and it raises ITC reversal issues in GST.
What is a Sunk Cost?
Unfructified expenses = costs incurred in anticipation of revenue OR for creation of revenue-generating assets — aborted due to change in circumstances
AS26 demands these be expensed as 'period costs'
Cannot be capitalized (no longer supports capitalization)
'Period costs' = expenses that do not justify being capitalized and not necessarily connected to revenue earned in that financial year
Income Tax Treatment of Sunk Costs
NOT ADMISSIBLE
Cannot be carried forward as losses against taxable income of future years
📝 You cannot offset a dead expenditure against profits you make next year. The loss is locked in — it does not travel forward.
NOT DEDUCTIBLE
Not deductible under Section 37 of Income-tax Act
Business is not the same entity when income-generating capacity is reached
EXCEPTION
Section 35D of Income-tax Act — period costs allowed to the extent provided therein
GST Treatment of Sunk Costs — ITC Angle
ON GOODS
Sunk costs akin to 'write off' — attract Section 17(5)(h) CGST Act
Only to extent they relate to goods, NOT services
📝 If you wrote off stock (goods), ITC reversal kicks in u/s 17(5)(h). But if you wrote off a service cost, same treatment does not apply. Section 17(5)(h) covers goods only.
ON SERVICES
Sunk costs on services fail 'use in business' test — Section 16(1)
Must receive same treatment as goods qua Section 17(5)(h) — alternate views exist
📝 The 'use in business' test is strict — if the expenditure was ultimately not used in business (aborted), ITC cannot be retained even on services.
INCOME TAX LINK
If income-tax disallows expense as 'sunk cost' in assessment → cannot ignore this for GST ITC computation
Attendant GST treatment must follow
💡 Illustration (a): Company buys Story A and Story B films for ₹2cr each. Only Story B released. Cost of Story A disallowed u/s 37 — but may be carried as 'asset' in books till production happens. NOT deductible until then.
KEY CASES ON INADMISSIBILITY
Western India Vegetable Products (Bom HC 1954) | Mazagaon Dock Ltd (SC 1958) | Tuticorin Alkali Chemicals (SC 1997) | CIT v. Saurashtra Cements (Guj HC 1973) | CIT v. Ralliwolf (Bom HC 1980) | K Sampath Kumar v. CIT (Mad HC 1986)
Finding: Unfructified costs do not satisfy 'use in business' test — disallowance in income-tax is not something to be taken for GST purposes automatically.
9.4.3 — Disallowance of Expenditure ⇧ Index
🚫 Disallowance — Income-Tax Disallowance & its GST Consequences
📝 When income-tax authorities disallow an expense (say, for personal use or TDS failure), the question arises — does this disallowance create a new taxable event in GST? The answer is: disallowance does NOT create outward supply or new GST liability by itself.
TYPE 1
Voluntary disallowance or disallowance for personal use
(e.g. 25% office rent, 10% bus lease disallowed)
Must be examined for most appropriate GST tax treatment
Refer earlier deliberations on 'sunk costs'
💡 Eg.532: 25% of office rental disallowed for personal use. Eg.533: 10% of company bus lease disallowed for personal use.
TYPE 2
Disallowance for failure to deduct TDS — Section 40(a)(ia)
Entire expenditure disallowed; added back to taxable income
📝 Section 40(a)(ia): If you pay a resident without deducting TDS and TDS is not paid by due date — 30% of such payment is disallowed. The disallowance adds to your taxable income — but does NOT create outward supply liability in GST.
BUT Disallowance resulting in additional taxable income to Payer CANNOT tantamount to turnover from outward supply to attract GST incidence
💡 Eg.534: Remittances to overseas supplier not subject to withholding tax (u/s 195) — disallowed in income-tax — but GST-RCM credit will NOT be denied. Eg.535: Omission to deduct TDS u/s 194J will NOT disentitle credit of GST paid on invoice.
GST TREATMENT
Could be any of:
(i) Section 17(1) or 17(5)(g)
(ii) Section 7(1)(a) read with Para 4(b) Sch II
(iii) Section 7(1)(c) read with Para 1, Sch I CGST Act
📝 The correct GST treatment of the disallowed expense depends on the nature — whether it is blocked credit, deemed supply, or schedule-based classification. There is no single automatic answer.
9.4.4 — Deemed Income ⇧ Index
💡 Deemed Income — Income-Tax Fiction vs GST Reality
📝 'Deemed income' is income-tax's way of taxing something that does not really exist — like taxing a transfer that never gave real money. This legal fiction works only for income-tax purposes and does NOT automatically create GST liability.
WHAT IS IT
Income that does not exist is admitted as 'taxable income' in certain circumstances:
• Failure to satisfy post-conditions
• Deferment of capital gains tax of earlier years
• Income of other persons taxed in hands of given taxpayer
GST RULE
Legal fiction in income-tax operates ONLY for purposes of income-tax law
Does NOT fertilize GST treatment 'as if' such income represents taxable turnover of outward supplies
📝 Just because income-tax says 'this is income', GST cannot be demanded as if there was an actual supply. The fiction stays within income-tax law — it does not cross over to GST.
9.4.5 — TDS — General Principles & GST Implications ⇧ Index
📋 TDS — Income-Tax Deduction & its Role in GST Enquiry
MECHANICS
Omission to deduct TDS when deductible → Disallowance of payment + Imputed as income of Payer
TDS rates: 1–10% (usually)
Aggravated consequences push Payers to 'conservatively' deduct
📝 Payers are scared of TDS disallowance — so they often over-deduct TDS under a 'safe' category without thinking about implications to Payee.
THIRD PARTY DATA
Third-party data (TDS) is more reliable than self-assessment in GST enquiry
Section 199 remedy available to Payee
Payees less inclined to debate with Payers when collection is priority
📝 When the Department finds TDS filed under a certain HSN category, they use it as evidence — it is third-party data filed by Payer, considered more reliable than self-assessment by the taxpayer.
CRITICAL LIMITATION
No HSN tariff presumption can be raised against taxpayer in GST based on TDS category adopted by Payer
TDS category = means nothing for GST HSN classification
TDS is applied on provisions made in Payer's books — not completion of supply
📝 The HSN code used by Payer for TDS does NOT determine GST HSN. The Supplier (Payee) self-assesses HSN under Section 59 — Payer cannot override this. Using TDS HSN against Payee is a fatal and erroneous presumption.
9.4.6 — Section 194R — Perquisites & GST as Outward Supply ⇧ Index
🎁 Section 194R — TDS on Perquisites & Cascading GST Implications
Finance Act, 2022 | Effective 1 July 2022
📝 Section 194R introduced TDS @ 10% on 'perquisites' given to business associates (not employees). This is not a gift — it is business distribution that creates a barter transaction with profound GST consequences.
Mechanics of Section 194R
10% TDS on 'perquisite' given to any person (other than employee) in course of business
Perquisite = (i) cash (ii) goods or services (iii) partly both
Taxpayer must first procure goods/services in its own name (even if delivered directly)
Inward supply invoice → to taxpayer
Then hold in stock OR use in making outward supply
Output tax payable immediately when goods/services are 'distributed' to Suppliers or other business associates
GST Analysis — Is this a Gift or a Supply?
NOT A GIFT
Distribution to business associates = NOT gift attracting reversal u/s 17(5)(h)
Gift or free samples = two ways to 'dispose of' goods — not applicable here
📝 A gift is given without any business reason. But distributing perquisites to business associates has a business purpose — it is 'reciprocated' by the associate's distributive services. So Section 17(5)(h) reversal does NOT apply.
CONSIDERATION TEST
Distribution to business associates = ipso facto 'reciprocity'
= Non-monetary consideration under Section 2(31)(b) CGST Act
For distribution to be truly WITHOUT consideration → given to persons NOT associated with business
📝 Section 2(31)(b): Consideration includes monetary value of any act or forbearance for inducement of supply. Since business associates reciprocate by their services, there IS consideration — just non-monetary.
BARTER TRANSACTION
This is a barter:
(i) Inward supply of 'distributive trade services' (HSN 9961/9962) from business associate
+ (ii) Outward supply of goods/services distributed
Both have SAME time of supply — admitted in TDS filings
💡 When 194R TDS is deducted admitting distribution of goods to business associates → it is outward supply → liable to GST as barter. Recipient also liable for outward supply of distributive services.
⚠️ AUDIT TRIGGER
Verification of TDS u/s 194R must be integral part of GST audit u/s 65 or scrutiny u/s 61 CGST Act — by comparing Form 26AS/AIS with returns furnished.
9.4.7 — TDS by Mistake — Timing & HSN Classification Issues ⇧ Index
⏱️ TDS by Mistake — When TDS is Deducted Wrongly or Prematurely
📝 Every TDS deduction is not a perfect determination of income in Payee's hands. TDS is triggered by accounting entries and provisions — not by actual income or completion of service. This creates 'timing differences' with GST implications.
TIMING ISSUE
Any person who makes a provision or records a transaction must deduct TDS at source — accounting for liability does not require invoice from counter-party
TDS is to ensure completeness of accounting in Payer's books
💡 Eg.536: Statutory audit for FY 2022-23 completed between Jun-Sep 2023. But audit fee provision (with TDS) is already in FY 2022-23 books even though no work started. TDS credit flows to Auditor in 2022-23 itself. When invoice issued (Aug 2023), no fresh TDS — unless shortfall due to higher billed amount.
HSN CATEGORY MISTAKE
Payer deducts TDS u/s 194J interpreting services as 'professional/technical'
But contractor's actual HSN = 9954 (construction) not 9985 (consultancy)
HSN self-assessment by Supplier under Section 59 CGST — cannot be left to Payer
📝 If Payer TDS under wrong HSN category — Supplier cannot be penalized. Payer deducts TDS to avoid Section 40(a)(ia) disallowance — but has no duty to 'accurately' determine character of income or tax period to which it pertains.
CRITICAL PRINCIPLE
Difference between GSTR-9 and GSTR-1/3B does NOT establish any unpaid GST — because tax period in which TDS is deducted cannot determine TOS for discharge of GST
💡 Eg.537: GSTR-9 vs GSTR-1/3B difference ≠ unpaid GST. TDS tax period ≠ Time of Supply for GST discharge.
9.4.8 — Section 195 — Overseas Remittances & RCM ⇧ Index
🌐 Section 195 — Withholding Tax on Overseas Remittances & GST POS / RCM
📝 When you pay an overseas supplier, you must withhold tax (TDS) — this is called withholding tax. The category under which you withhold TDS does NOT determine your GST liability or RCM treatment. GST has its own rules for Place of Supply.
Withholding by deducting from amount payable OR grossing up to net amount paid = transacted price
Overseas Payee may claim credit per home country tax laws
Payer likely adopts advantageous or conservative income characterization to optimize withholding requirement
Income characterization affects withholding tax rate — not GST
KEY PRINCIPLE
GST POS and applicability of 'import of services' is NOT dependent on income characterization adopted for withholding tax
Income characterization + underlying motivations of Payer → considered while independently arriving at POS and corresponding RCM liability
📝 GST does not outsource POS / RCM determination to income-tax characterization. The two are independent exercises — though income-tax data can assist in GST classification.
9.5.1 — Transfer — Capital Asset (Section 50C) ⇧ Index
🏛️ Transfer — Capital Asset & Section 50C — Stamp Duty Valuation vs GST
📝 Section 50C says: if you sell land/building for less than stamp duty value, income-tax will tax you on stamp duty value. This is anti-evasion. But does this change the GST value? The answer requires careful analysis — rejection of transaction price by one regulator is a signal for GST too.
INCOME TAX — Section 50C
Income-tax definition of 'capital asset' = riddled with legal fiction
Securities = capital assets in income-tax → excluded from GST (not goods/services)
Contracts for sale of immovable property — 'substance prevails over form' even before TPA fruition
📝 Income-tax is a 'private matters' legislation where strict interpretation applies — but large-scale evasion has forced purposive construction even here.
SECTION 50C — Stamp Valuation
Where consideration < Stamp Duty Value → Stamp duty value deemed as full consideration for Section 48 purposes (capital gains computation)
GST IMPACT
Where transaction price rejected by one regulator → indicator that transacted price may not be 'sole consideration' for Section 15(1) CGST Act
GST operates on 3 disqualifications before resort to valuation rules
Rejection by one regulator cannot be glossed over while admitting GST treatment based on transaction price
📝 If income-tax says 'your sale price is artificially low', GST should sit up and take note — the valuation may need re-examination under Section 15 CGST Act. You cannot just say 'GST goes by contract price' ignoring the stamp duty valuation rejection.
9.5.2 — Negative Fiction — Section 47 Income Tax Act ⇧ Index
🔄 Negative Fiction — Section 47 & Its Proof of Transfer for GST
📝 Section 47 of Income-tax Act says certain transactions are NOT a 'transfer' for income-tax purposes — so capital gains does not apply. But claiming this exemption is itself proof that a real transfer occurred — which then has GST consequences.
WHAT IS SECTION 47
Negative fiction = Though transactions are in fact transfers, they will NOT be admitted as 'transfer' for income-tax Act
Includes: (iv) Transfer of capital asset by company to subsidiary [if parent holds 100%] | (v) Transfer by subsidiary to holding [if 100%] | (xiv) Sole proprietor → Company succession
📝 Section 47 basically says: "We know this is a transfer, but we are choosing to exempt it from capital gains for income-tax purposes." This is a deliberate fiction — not a denial that transfer happened.
GST CONSEQUENCE
Taxpayer claiming Section 47 exclusion = Proof that transfer for GST purposes has occurred
Attendant GST tax treatment must be applied
Section 47A rescinds legal fiction in certain instances
📝 You cannot claim Section 47 exemption in income-tax AND simultaneously deny that there was any supply in GST. The claim proves the transfer happened — GST must follow.
💡 Holding-subsidiary capital asset transfer exempted u/s 47(iv) → proves transfer for GST → Section 7(1)(a) supply may arise → need to examine Schedule I for related party supply without consideration.
SECTION 93 CGST
Unusual provision — GST liability prior to demise of property determined subsequent to demise → payable by successors-in-interest or from estate of deceased
Section 47A rescission of legal fiction in certain instances
9.6.1 — Transfer Pricing — Benchmark ⇧ Index
🌍 Transfer Pricing — Benchmark & GST Disclosure Implications
📝 Transfer pricing rules require companies doing international transactions with related parties (Associated Enterprises) to prove their prices are 'arm's length' — comparable to what unrelated parties would charge. These disclosures are a goldmine for GST scrutiny.
WHY IT MATTERS
International transactions impact tax revenues more significantly than domestic ones — one limb is beyond jurisdiction of Income-tax Act → more critical to monitor and benchmark
Mandatory 'Two-Point Disclosure' for International Transactions with AEs
(a) Transactions with Associated Enterprises and their details
(b) Disclosure of methodology of comparability used to establish correctness and completeness of transacted price
TWO ASSERTIONS IMMENSELY RELEVANT FOR GST
(a) Fact that such transactions with AEs exist
(b) Nature, classification and value of these transactions

Admission made before income-tax authorities via 'two-point' disclosure exposes verification of treatment applied to these transactions and validation of sufficiency under GST law.
📝 Whatever you disclosed in Transfer Pricing documents to income-tax — you cannot take a different position in GST. The admission is on record. GST does NOT outsource tax treatment to income-tax, but the admission is binding.
9.6.2 — Transfer Pricing — Adjustment & GST Impact ⇧ Index
⚖️ TP Adjustment — When Income-Tax Says Price Was Wrong
📝 When income-tax finds that your inter-company transaction price was less than what arm's length would require, they adjust it upward — and you pay more income-tax. But this adjustment also means GST was computed on the wrong (lower) price. Consequences follow.
MECHANISM
Taxpayer offers adjustment to income-tax (not required to alter inter-company transaction price)
Pays additional income-tax on assumption net margin was X%
💡 Eg.538: Net income from transaction must be 10% over cost but actually 6%. Taxpayer offers to pay income-tax on additional 4% net margin — but does not change actual transaction price.
GST CONSEQUENCE
Higher margin admission → inter-company price ought to have been higher
GST computed on actual (lower) transaction price — requires corresponding treatment 'as if' adjusted transaction price was subject to it
Effect: NOT to impose 'double fiction' — but deny benefits like zero-rating or refund or impose additional tax where applicable
📝 Accepting TP adjustment = asserting that actual transaction price was NOT OMV. So GST treatment must follow adjusted price — not actual billed price. If tax incidence applies, it applies on adjusted amount.
💡 Eg.539: Adjustment of price of export of services will NOT attract GST — adjusted price also qualifies for zero-rating. Eg.540: Adjustment of commission to India intermediary → additional tax on adjusted amount payable to agent in India.
PROCESS
Each adjustment demands examination of GST treatment applicable to underlying transaction
Apply relevant tax treatment 'as if' transacted at adjusted price
9.6.3 — Country-by-Country Report (CbCR) — Form 3CEAA ⇧ Index
🗺️ CbCR — Global Disclosure & GST Compliance Exposure
📝 The OECD introduced Country-by-Country Reporting (CbCR) to fight tax base erosion. Multinationals must file three-tier documentation. The data disclosed is incredibly valuable for GST scrutiny — because what you admit globally cannot be denied locally in GST.
Three-Tier Documentation Required
(a) Country-by-Country Report
(Form 3CEAA)
(b) Master File
(Form 3CEAB)
(c) Local File
Transaction-level detail
Form 3CEAA — Key Disclosures (GST-Relevant)
#Disclosure RequiredGST Significance
(i)Nature of business or businessesConfirms classification of supplies
(ii)Important drivers of profitsReveals what services/goods are being used
(iii)Supply chain for 5 largest products/services + others >5% group revenueDirectly maps to GST supply chain — nature, classification, value
(iv)Important service arrangements among group membersInter-company service agreements — GST import of services / RCM
(vi)Transfer pricing policies for allocating service costs and pricesBasis of GST value of inter-company transactions
(viii)Functions, assets and risks analysis of constituent entitiesFAR analysis — determines GST classification of transactions
(ix)Description of business restructuring, acquisitions, divestmentsTOGC / slump sale → GST supply or exempt?
⚠️ KEY INSIGHT
Assertions made under income-tax law cannot be denied under GST law. Where CbCR compliance is not applicable (below threshold), the nature of this exercise still exposes taxpayers to internally evaluate GST treatment applicable had compliance been required.
9.6.4 — Domestic TP & GAAR (Section 95) — GST Treatment ⇧ Index
🏠 Domestic TP & GAAR — Anti-Avoidance Measures & GST Correction
DOMESTIC TP
Section 95 — 'General Anti-Avoidance Rules' from Jan 2017 applicable from FY 2017-18 onwards
Anti-avoidance measure → purposive interpretation applies
NOT strict interpretation
📝 GAAR allows income-tax to look through artificial arrangements that exist only for tax avoidance. Strict interpretation (letter of law) gives way to purposive interpretation (spirit of law).
WHEN TP/GAAR FINDS ARTIFICIAL ARRANGEMENT
Transactions in income-tax detected as including artificial entities with no economic substance → 'Real and effective transaction' is restated
Tax treatment in GST will need to follow these new facts
📝 If income-tax finds a sham transaction — the real transaction underneath it has GST consequences. Even if GST was already paid on the sham transaction, the correct GST must now apply to the real transaction.
GST CORRECTION REQUIRED
Income-tax proceeding detecting artificial facts → Correcting GST treatment already applied
Tax may have already been discharged on asserted (artificial) facts
Legal fiction in income-tax cannot alter GST facts — but where artificial facts are overturned → GST treatment must be corrected
📝 GAAR findings are about facts (not fiction) being restated to reflect reality. So GST follows the real facts — not the artificial ones — even if GST was already paid on artificial facts. This may require refunds or additional demands.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 9 | Expenditure & Transfer | Income Tax Act & GST ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision/case name.
Assets vs Property — Capital Goods Test
Para 9.4 | GST Litigation Reference | Chapter 9
Key Principle
Not all 'assets' in books of account are 'property' in law. All property will be 'assets' if enterprise expects enduring benefits — but all assets are not property. 'Accounting assets' are not 'assets' in law. Real property is that which the transferee has rights to the exclusion of the whole world and suit can be brought in a Court of law against infringement. Only goods can be capital goods in GST — even if services are capitalized in books, they will NOT be capital goods.
When to Use — When Department claims ITC disallowance treating an item as 'immovable property' or 'not capital goods' — counter by establishing whether the item is goods, whether capitalized, and whether the fiction in Schedule II applies to it. Key: GST does not outsource capitalization to any other law — it follows accounting rules.
Sunk Cost — Section 17(5)(h) CGST Act — Goods vs Services
Para 9.4.2 | Section 17(5)(h) CGST Act | Section 16(1) CGST Act
Key Principle
Sunk costs are akin to 'write off' and attract the operation of section 17(5)(h) but only to the extent they relate to 'goods' and NOT to 'services' under this section. Sunk costs which relate to 'services' fail to satisfy 'use in business' test in section 16(1) and must receive the same treatment as in section 17(5)(h) as goods do. Finding under income-tax that unfructified costs do not satisfy 'use in business' test is not something to be taken as determinative for GST purposes — but the finding cannot be left without applying the attendant GST tax treatment.
When to Use — (i) When Department demands ITC reversal on written-off goods, cite Section 17(5)(h) — applicable to goods not services. (ii) When assessee claims ITC on aborted project services, counter: 'use in business' test fails — ITC liable for reversal.
Income-Tax Disallowance ≠ Outward Supply / GST Turnover
Para 9.4.3 | Section 40(a)(ia) Income-tax Act | Section 7 CGST Act
Key Principle
Disallowance of expenditure resulting in additional taxable income to Payer cannot tantamount to turnover from outward supply to attract incidence of GST by such taxpayer. Omission to deduct TDS under section 194J will not disentitle credit of GST paid on invoice. Remittances to overseas supplier not subject to withholding tax disallowed in income-tax scrutiny assessment — GST-RCM credit will not be denied.
When to Use — When Department attempts to demand GST on amounts added back to income due to TDS failure disallowance — argue: disallowance creates income-tax consequence, not GST outward supply. Two different events, two different consequences.
TDS Category / HSN under TDS ≠ GST HSN Classification
Para 9.4.5 | Section 59 CGST Act | Section 40(a)(ia) Income-tax Act
Key Principle
No presumption about HSN tariff can be raised against taxpayer in GST based on category under which Payer of consideration has deducted TDS. Determination of HSN code is based on self-assessment under section 59 of Central GST Act by the Contractor and cannot be left to the mercy of the Contractee. Category under which TDS is applied means nothing for GST. TDS is applied on provisions made in Payer's books of anticipated expenditure — it does not even indicate completion of supply by Payee.
When to Use — (i) When GST officer uses TDS category (e.g., 194J professional services) to demand GST under that HSN. (ii) When GSTR-1/GSTR-3B vs GSTR-9 difference is attributed to TDS period — demolish the presumption that TDS period = Time of Supply.
Section 194R — Distribution of Perquisites = Barter Transaction in GST
Para 9.4.6 | Section 194R Income-tax Act | Section 2(31)(b) CGST Act | HSN 9961/9962
Key Principle
Distribution of perquisites to business associates is NOT a gift to attract reversal of input tax credit under section 17(5)(h) of Central GST Act. Distribution to business associates ipso facto indicates 'reciprocity' that forms inducement for taxpayer in non-monetary form of consideration as per section 2(31)(b) of Central GST Act. When TDS under section 194R is deducted admitting distribution of goods or services to business associates, such distribution will be liable to GST as 'outward supply' — this transaction is actually a barter comprising inward supply of 'distributive trade services' (HSN 9961/9962) reciprocated by distribution of said goods or services.
When to Use — In GST audit/scrutiny when 194R TDS data surfaces in AIS/Form 26AS. Argue: distribution = outward supply = barter. For assessee: challenge whether Section 17(5)(h) reversal is correct — it is NOT a gift. For Revenue: demand GST as outward supply.
Section 47 Income-Tax Exclusion = Proof of Transfer for GST
Para 9.5.2 | Section 47 Income-tax Act | Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act | Schedule I CGST Act
Key Principle
When a transfer is completed under the substantive law applicable, this fact must be admitted to apply the tax treatment due under other laws including income-tax law. Taxpayers claiming exclusion by section 47 negative fiction is proof enough that a transfer, for purposes of GST, has occurred — and the attendant tax treatment must be applied.
When to Use — When holding-subsidiary capital asset transfer is exempted from capital gains in income-tax u/s 47(iv) or (v) — use this to establish in GST that a transfer has indeed occurred. Then examine Schedule I — supply without consideration between related parties is taxable. Section 47 exclusion = admission of transfer = GST consequences must follow.
Transfer Pricing Adjustment — GST Value Must Follow Adjusted Price
Para 9.6.2 | Transfer Pricing Rules | Section 15 CGST Act | Section 50C Income-tax Act
Key Principle
Where transfer pricing adjustment admits higher margin, it implicitly means inter-company transaction price ought to have been higher than actually transacted. GST compliance has been made based on actual transaction price and not adjusted transaction price — requires a corresponding treatment 'as if' the adjusted transaction price and not the actual transaction price was subject to the treatment. Accepting adjustment before income-tax authorities is an assertion that actual transaction price is not OMV as understood in GST. Effect of this adjustment will NOT be to impose a 'double fiction' — but denying benefits otherwise admissible such as zero-rating or refund of credits or applying additional tax where applicable.
When to Use — Where assessee has accepted TP adjustment in income-tax proceedings — use to argue in GST that the OMV of transaction was higher, inviting GST demand on the adjusted higher price. Conversely, where export service is adjusted — GST zero-rating still applies on adjusted price.
CbCR / Form 3CEAA Disclosures — Binding Admissions for GST
Para 9.6.3 | Form 3CEAA | Sections 286, 92D Income-tax Act | Rules 10DA, 10DB
Key Principle
Assertions made under income-tax law cannot be denied under GST law. Data forthcoming in Form 3CEAA is unbelievable for taxpayer and tax administration reviewing GST compliances to verify and validate the correctness and completeness. Where any taxpayer asserts that CbCR compliance is not applicable for any reason such as transactions below threshold limit or other criteria, the nature of this exercise exposes taxpayers to internally evaluate GST treatment applicable had this compliance been applicable to them or when this compliance does become applicable.
When to Use — During GST audit u/s 65 or special audit u/s 66 for multinational group taxpayers. Demand Form 3CEAA disclosures as part of GST audit evidence. Supply chain, service arrangements, pricing policies disclosed in 3CEAA are binding admissions — use to verify GST classification, value, and RCM compliance for import of services.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Para 9.4–9.6 | Income Tax Act & GST
📖 Ch 11 — Special Economic Zones Act

🏭 Chapter 11 — Special Economic Zones Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Concession Allowed
Prohibited / Void / Concession Denied
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
11.1 Relevance to GST — Section 53 Fiction
11.2.1 Developer and Unit
11.2.2 FTWZ
11.3.1 Authorized Operations & End Use
11.3.2 Concessions — Specified Officer's Role
11.3.3 Non-Processing Area
11.3.4 Supplies to SEZ — Outliers
11.3.5 Input Tax Credit to Units
11.3.6 Debonding
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
11.1 — Relevance to GST | Section 53, SEZ Act — 'Territory Outside India' Fiction ⇧ Index
🏭 SEZ Act — Relevance to GST
Section 53 | Remarkable Legislative Fiction | 'Territory Outside India'
📝 The SEZ Act does something extraordinary — it takes a physical area inside India and declares it to be 'outside India' by law. This is not geography — it is a legal fiction. And this fiction has enormous consequences for GST zero-rating, ITC, and concessions.
Section 53(1): A Special Economic Zone shall, on and from the appointed day, be deemed to be a territory outside the customs territory of India for the purposes of undertaking the authorized operations.

Section 53(2): A Special Economic Zone shall, with effect from such date as Central Government may notify, be deemed to be a port, inland container depot, land station and land customs stations under Section 7 of the Customs Act, 1962.
📝 In plain terms: Even though GIFT City (Gandhinagar) or Kandla SEZ are physically in India — for legal purposes they are treated as if they were in a foreign country. Goods going into the SEZ = export. Goods coming out of the SEZ = import. This is why supplies to SEZ = zero-rated.
PILLAR 1
Assertions made by Developer of the zone (or Unit in zone) and admitted by regulator → mutatis mutandis reliable for GST purposes
📝 Mutatis mutandis = with necessary changes. What the Developer told the regulatory authority (SO) can be used in GST proceedings with appropriate adaptations. Developer's admissions are GST evidence.
PILLAR 2
'End use' of inward supplies — as attested by Specified Officer (SO) — has significant bearing on tax treatment applicable in GST
📝 'End use' means: what is the supply actually going to be used for? The Specified Officer verifies this. If used in authorized operations in processing area → concession allowed. If not → GST at full rate.
PILLAR 3 — TAX CONCESSIONS
Tax concessions permitted under SEZ Act (and GST law) apply to:
(i) Supplies made into processing area only
(ii) For use in specified activities of the Unit
📝 Concessions are not blanket — they are narrow. Only supplies into the processing area (not the non-processing area) AND only for authorized operations get zero-rating / duty exemption. Both conditions must be satisfied.
NATURE OF SEZ ACT
SEZ Act is a 'private matters' legislation but deeply involves public welfare — it relates to public land given to develop the zone.
Being a private matters legislation — it demands strict interpretation, similar to GST.
📝 'Private matters legislation' means it primarily deals with rights and obligations between private parties (Developer, Units). But since public land is involved, the obligations are of public nature too — making regulatory compliance critical and non-negotiable.
11.2.1 — Developer and Unit ⇧ Index
🏗️ Developer vs Unit — Roles & Obligations
🏢 DEVELOPER
✓ DOES
License u/ss 3 & 4 → creates infrastructure on land → leases industrial plots to Units → earns lease rental + maintenance charges
✗ DOES NOT
Developer does NOT export goods or services. Does NOT engage in sale of land or manufacture of specified products.
📝 Developer is a landlord — he builds infrastructure and rents it out to factories (Units). He does NOT make finished goods or export anything. Only the Units export.
Zone Types
Sector specific (natural resources / economic balancing) OR General purpose
🏭 UNIT
✓ MUST DO
Obtain approval u/s 15(9) → undertake authorized operations only → manufacturing or other permitted processes identifiably related to specified sector → net foreign exchange earner
⚠️ DTA SALES
Domestic sales by Units → permitted with much hesitation — extensive pre-conditions, post-conditions and restrictions applicable to DTA sales
📝 Units are the factories inside the SEZ. Their primary obligation is to EXPORT and earn foreign exchange. Selling domestically (to Domestic Tariff Area — DTA) is the exception, not the rule, and comes with heavy conditions.
11.2.2 — Free Trade and Warehousing Zone (FTWZ) ⇧ Index
📦 FTWZ — Free Trade and Warehousing Zone
Special zone notified for 'trading and warehousing' activities
📝 FTWZ is a special type of SEZ for trading and warehousing — not manufacturing. Think of it as a giant bonded warehouse complex where goods can be stored, traded and re-exported without Indian customs duties.
FTWZ may be configured as:
(i) Zone on its own
(ii) Demarcated area in a multi-product zone
(iii) Demarcated area within a sector-specific zone — NOT more than 20% of processing area
FTWZ UNITS — Export Obligation Satisfied By:
(i) Exporting imported goods | (ii) Exporting goods from other SEZ | (iii) Exporting domestically procured goods

Developer earns income from letting-out area to Units set up in FTWZ
11.3.1 — Authorized Operations & End Use Review ⇧ Index
🔍 Authorized Operations — End Use is the Key
Section 6, SEZ Act | Letter of Approval (LoA) | Specified Officer (SO)
📝 Every Unit in the SEZ gets a Letter of Approval (LoA) listing its exact operations and finished products. Only inward supplies used for those listed operations get concessions. The Specified Officer (SO) is the gatekeeper — he decides if each inward supply qualifies.
Section 6 — Demarcation of SEZ areas:
(a) Processing area — for setting up Units for activities being manufacture of goods or rendering of services
(b) Area exclusively for trading or warehousing purposes
(c) Non-processing areas — for activities other than (a) or (b)
Section 7 — Exemption for supplies to SEZ:
Any goods or services exported out of, or imported into, or procured from the Domestic Tariff Area by (i) a Unit in a Special Economic Zone or (ii) a Developer shall, subject to terms, conditions and limitations, as may be prescribed, be exempt from the payment of taxes, duties or cess under all enactments specified in the First Schedule.
End Use Determination — Two-Stage Process
STAGE 1 — Pre-Procurement
Review of Purchase Order by SO → value judgment whether inward supply is 'for use in authorized operations'
📝 Before the goods are even ordered, the SO reviews the purchase order. He decides: is this supply needed for the Unit's listed operations? If yes → concession tentatively allowed at PO stage.
STAGE 2 — On Arrival
Examination upon arrival of supplies for confirmation at entry into zone → allows said concessions
📝 When goods actually arrive at the SEZ gate, SO again verifies. Only after this final confirmation are concessions officially allowed. Both stages must pass — PO approval alone is not sufficient.
⚠️ DIFFICULTY IN DETERMINATION
Determination of 'end use' may not be easy every time. There are interstices where some instances may pose serious problem — risk that concessions may be extended where not to be, OR concessions may be denied where they ought to be allowed.
📝 The end use review is not mechanical — it involves judgment calls. Wrong calls can hurt taxpayers both ways: either getting concessions they shouldn't have (liability later) or being denied legitimate concessions (ITC/refund issues). This is the core tension in SEZ tax disputes.
Rule 27 — Import and Procurement: A Unit or Developer may import or procure from the Domestic Tariff Area without payment of duty, taxes or cess (or procure from DTA after availing export entitlements, or procure from other Units in same or other SEZ, from EOU, STPI, EHTP units) — all types of goods including capital goods (new or second hand), raw materials, semi-finished goods (including semi-finished jewellery) component, consumables, spares goods and materials for making capital goods required for authorized operations — except prohibited items under Import Trade Control (Harmonized System) Classifications.
11.3.2 — Concessions & the Specified Officer's Role ⇧ Index
✅ Concessions — Two Officers, One Decision
Zone-Customs (Min. of Finance) + Specified Officer (Min. of Commerce)
📝 Two different officers from two different ministries must work together to allow concessions. Zone-Customs officer handles the goods-in process. The Specified Officer (SO) from Ministry of Commerce determines end-use eligibility. Both must agree.
Import of Goods — Concession Flow
Port of Entry
Port-Customs permits movement out of port — marks as "SEZ Cargo"
Zone-Customs inspection at entry point → appraises consignment, documents nature / quantity / value
END-USE REVIEW
SO reviews → confirms 'use in authorized operations' → concession allowed
DOMESTIC PROCUREMENT
Subject to end-use review by SO via 'endorsement' of invoice issued by respective suppliers → permitted for use in authorized operations
📝 When goods are bought domestically (not imported), the SO endorses the supplier's invoice to confirm the goods are for authorized SEZ operations. This endorsement is the trigger for zero-rating the DTA supplier's invoice.
IT SECTOR — UNIFIED LIST
For IT sector, a unified list of services is published for guidance of SO — but SO is permitted to disagree on a case-specific basis.
Review of end use and sanction of concession is not a matter of procedure but a substantive question involving objective appraisal of the transaction.
Unified list does not render SO's review nugatory — SO is not expected to act under dictation.
📝 The published list is only a guide — it's not a binding list. The SO has independent mind. Even if a service is on the list, the SO can still deny concession if the facts of a particular case don't justify it. And vice versa — even if not on the list, SO can allow if facts support it. Facts first, list second.
11.3.3 — Non-Processing Area ⇧ Index
🏘️ Non-Processing Area — Open Zone, No Concessions
Freely used by all Units AND users from DTA | Rules 10, 11, 11A, 12
📝 The non-processing area is like a township within the SEZ — it has schools, hospitals, entertainment. Anyone can use it — SEZ units AND ordinary DTA people. But this area does NOT get duty/tax concessions.
✓ NON-PROCESSING AREA CAN HAVE
Infrastructure | Schools | Hospitals | Entertainment facilities
📝 The non-processing area supports the living needs of Unit employees and their families. It is a civilian township — not a factory zone.
✗ CONCESSIONS ONLY FOR PROCESSING AREA
Concessions allowed for use in processing area only — demarcated under Rules 10, 11, 11A and 12.
Non-processing area supplies → NO concession
📝 If cement is ordered for building a hospital in the non-processing area — zero-rating does NOT apply. Only supplies physically entering and used in the processing area (factory zone) get the concession.
PRE-SCRUTINY MECHANISM
Zone Customs Officer carries out pre-scrutiny of every inward supply into zone → verifies whether it is meant for:
(a) Use in authorized operations — processing area installation, OR
(b) Other operations — non-processing area
Restricted on concession found in rules.
11.3.4 — Supplies to SEZ — Outlier Cases ⇧ Index
⚠️ Supplies to SEZ — Two Conditions for Zero-Rating
DTA supplies must satisfy: (1) Billing to Unit in zone AND (2) Entry of goods into zone for pre-entry inspection by SO
📝 Zero-rating of DTA supplies to SEZ is not automatic. Two conditions must BOTH be satisfied: (1) the invoice must be billed to the SEZ Unit, and (2) the goods must physically enter the zone for SO's inspection. If either condition fails — no zero-rating.
Outlier Cases — Neither Qualifies for Zero-Rating
OUTLIER (a) — Billed to DTA Contractor
Cement supplied for renovation works in zone, billed to Contractor (in DTA) but shipped to zone
📝 Goods go TO the zone but the bill goes to a DTA contractor — not to the SEZ Unit. Zero-rating fails on Condition 1: billing must be to the SEZ Unit.
💡 A DTA construction company doing renovation inside SEZ orders cement. Bill = DTA contractor. Ship = SEZ. → NOT zero-rated. Contractor pays full GST.
OUTLIER (b) — Billed to Unit but goods go to DTA
Cement (Client-provided material) required for manufacture of RCM by Contractor → billed to Unit in zone but shipped to Contractor's batching plant (DTA)
📝 Bill goes to the SEZ Unit (Condition 1 satisfied) but goods never enter the SEZ (Condition 2 fails). Zero-rating requires BOTH billing to Unit AND entry into zone.
💡 SEZ Unit orders cement for its construction project. Cement billed to Unit but sent to contractor's plant outside the zone for batching. → NOT zero-rated. Entry into zone condition fails.
RULE
Zero-rated supply demands that billing must be to Unit in the zone as well as entry of goods must be into the zone for pre-entry inspection by SO.
Both conditions are mandatory. Failure of either condition = full GST applicable.
11.3.5 — Input Tax Credit to Units | SO's Refusal Effect ⇧ Index
💳 ITC to SEZ Units — Complex Interplay
Section 16(1)(b) IGST (amendment pending notification) | Section 16(2) IGST | Section 17(2) CGST
📝 The ITC rules for SEZ Units are unusually complex because two different statutory provisions create a tug-of-war: the provision that denies concession and the provision that allows ITC and refund on zero-rated supplies. Understanding this interplay is critical in disputes.
CURRENT POSITION (Amendment to Sec 16(1)(b) IGST NOT yet notified)
ALL supplies to Unit are zero-rated — the only hurdle is end-use review by SO.
If SO declines concession → supplier is NOT permitted to apply zero-rated benefit on tax invoice → supplier must charge applicable IGST on invoice.
📝 Right now, until the amendment is notified, every supply to an SEZ Unit is treated as zero-rated by default. But if the SO says 'no concession' — the supplier cannot zero-rate the invoice. He must charge full IGST. The Unit then receives a IGST-charged invoice.
KEY QUESTION: Can Unit claim ITC when SO refused concessional benefits?
SCENARIO A — SO Refuses Concession
SO declines → Supplier charges IGST → Unit pays IGST → Unit claims ITC on IGST paid → Unit makes further zero-rated supplies + claims refund of ITC of IGST
📝 Here is the paradox: SO says supply is NOT for authorized operations. Supplier charges IGST. Unit claims ITC on that IGST. Unit then exports and claims refund of ALL its input ITC — including the IGST paid on supplies SO refused to allow concession! What SO directly barred, the ITC route makes indirectly available.
ALTERNATIVE — DTA Supplier Payment Option
Where SO allows, DTA suppliers have option of 'payment of IGST' in respect of supplies to Unit used in authorized operations appraised by SO
📝 As an alternative to zero-rating, DTA suppliers can choose to charge IGST and let the Unit claim ITC and refund. This is the 'payment of IGST' route — it achieves same economic result as zero-rating but through the ITC mechanism.
⚠️ PARADOX — DRAFTSMAN'S GAFFE?
Ab initio exemption denied by SO is undone in the form of zero-rated benefits allowed to Unit via ITC scheme.
IGST on domestic procurement + IGST on import of goods continue to enjoy ITC and refund against exports — notwithstanding SO's refusal.
End use review really works only for basic customs duty on imports where SO declines duty concession.
📝 The SO's refusal only truly bites on Customs Duty on imported goods. For IGST — whether on imports or domestic procurement — Units can still get the benefit via ITC and refund route. This makes the SO's role in IGST matters largely academic. Units need to proceed with caution despite this apparent loop.
SECTION 16(2) IGST — THE OVERRIDE
Section 16(2) of Integrated GST Act allows input tax credit subject to restrictions in Section 17(5) CGST — effectively overrides the embargo against claiming ITC on any inward supply used in making zero-rated supplies (exports and supplies to SEZ).
📝 Section 16(2) IGST is a powerful provision — it allows ITC even on supplies that would otherwise be blocked (like cement, construction services for factory building) when the ultimate outward supply is zero-rated (SEZ supplies or exports).
ITC ELIGIBILITY CONCLUSION
Nothing in Section 16(1) CGST Act denies ITC to Units where SO refuses concession — inward supplies to Units satisfy:
(i) Used in course or furtherance of business, AND
(ii) Benefit of Section 16(2) IGST extends to all zero-rated supplies — not limited to exports only.
11.3.6 — Debonding ⇧ Index
🔓 Debonding — Domestic Sale from SEZ Processing Area
Section 30, SEZ Act | Concession enjoyed → repaid at current rates on domestic sale
📝 'Debonding' means a Unit in the SEZ decides to sell goods domestically (to DTA) that came into the SEZ without paying duties. When this happens, the Unit must pay back the concessions it enjoyed — at current duty rates, not the rates when goods entered.
Section 30 — Removal of goods to DTA:
(a) Any goods removed from a Special Economic Zone to the Domestic Tariff Area shall be chargeable to duties of customs including anti-dumping, countervailing and safeguard duties under the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, where applicable, as leviable on such goods when imported; and
(b) the rate of duty and tariff valuation, if any, applicable to goods removed from a SEZ shall be at the rate and tariff valuation in force as on the date of such removal, and where such date is not ascertainable, on the date of payment of duty.
WHAT IS DEBONDED
Goods where duties and taxes NOT paid on inward supply (whether import or domestic procurement) — whether capital goods or inputs — in processing areas
RATE — CURRENT, NOT HISTORICAL
Duty payable on domestic sale = at rates in force as on date of removal — NOT at rates when goods entered SEZ
(or date of payment if removal date not ascertainable)
📝 If customs duty rate on a machine was 5% when it entered the SEZ, but is 20% when the Unit decides to sell it domestically years later — the Unit pays 20%. Current rates apply. This is a significant commercial risk for Units planning DTA sales of old assets.
GST Aspects of Debonding
GOODS — Purchase Returns
Inward supplies admitted into zone have been used → 'purchase returns' to DTA permitted only by way of termination of supplies + credit note u/s 34 CGST Act
📝 Since goods already entered the zone (and consumed), a 'purchase return' is possible only if the supply itself is terminated. The SEZ Unit issues a credit note under Section 34 — which reverses the supply. This is the formal legal mechanism.
SERVICES — Different Rule
Services supplied by Units to DTA are liable to GST on outward supplies — no special treatment or exemption on DTA service sales
📝 If an SEZ Unit provides a service to a DTA customer — full GST applies on that service. The SEZ fiction ('territory outside India') works for goods movement, but outward service supplies to DTA are straightforward taxable services.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 11 | Special Economic Zones Act ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision. Chapter 11 is principle-based — no court judgments cited in the chapter. Use these in disputes involving SEZ zero-rating denials, ITC claims, SO refusals, and debonding assessments.
Principle: Section 53 Fiction — SEZ is 'Territory Outside India'
Section 53, SEZ Act, 2005 | Applicable in all SEZ-related GST disputes
Key Ratio / Principle
A physical area ('zone') is demarcated and declared in Section 53 of Special Economic Zones Act, 2005 to be a 'territory outside India'. This is such a remarkable legislative fiction that without grasping the need for such provision in the statute, its implications can hardly be appreciated. Supplies made into processing areas alone and used in authorized operations only are entitled to concessions and privileges enabled by this Act.
When to Use — When Department disputes zero-rating of supplies to SEZ or when a refund of ITC on SEZ supplies is denied. Establish the statutory foundation: Section 53 makes SEZ a 'deemed foreign territory' — supplies to SEZ = exports. Zero-rating is mandated by law, not a discretionary benefit.
Principle: Two Conditions for Zero-Rating — Billing AND Entry into Zone
Para 11.3.4 | Section 7, SEZ Act | Section 16, IGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Zero-rated supply demands that billing must be to Unit in the zone as well as entry of goods must be into the zone for pre-entry inspection by Specified Officer. Neither condition alone is sufficient — both conditions are mandatory and cumulative. Where billing is to a DTA contractor (not the Unit) though goods go to zone — zero-rating fails on the billing condition. Where billing is to the SEZ Unit but goods do not enter the zone — zero-rating fails on the entry condition.
When to Use — When DTA supplier's refund claim is rejected by Department. Verify whether both conditions are satisfied on the facts. Use offensively when Department wrongly denies refund despite both conditions being satisfied. Use defensively when Department seeks to zero-rate a supply that fails either condition — to protect Revenue.
Principle: SO's Review is Substantive — Not Procedural. Unified List is Only a Guide.
Para 11.3.2 | Section 7, SEZ Act | Rule 27, SEZ Rules
Key Ratio / Principle
Review of end use and sanction of concession is not a matter of procedure but a substantive question involving objective appraisal of the transaction. The Unified list published does not render the Specified Officer's review nugatory — the SO is not expected to act under dictation. The Unified list is only a guide and facts in each specific case need to be given due consideration.
When to Use — When SO refuses concession relying only on the fact that the service/supply is not on the Unified List for the IT sector (or other sector). Counter: Unified List is not exhaustive or binding. SO must apply mind to the facts and conduct objective appraisal. Refusal merely on the basis that the supply is not on the Unified List is unsustainable without substantive end-use analysis.
Principle: SO's IGST Refusal is Undone by ITC Scheme — Section 16(2) IGST Override
Para 11.3.5 | Section 16(2), IGST Act | Section 17(5), CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Ab initio exemption that is denied by SO is undone in the form of zero-rated benefits being allowed to Unit. Section 16(2) of Integrated GST Act allows input tax credit subject only to restrictions in section 17(5) of Central GST Act and effectively overrides the embargo against claim of input tax credit on any inward supply that is used in making zero-rated supplies (exports and supplies to SEZ). There is nothing in section 16(1) of Central GST Act that these inward supplies to Units fail to satisfy — used in the course or furtherance of business — or to fall out of section 16(2) of Integrated GST Act that extends benefits of input tax credit (and consequent refund) to all zero-rated supplies and not limited to exports only.
When to Use — When Department argues that since SO refused concessional benefit to a particular supply, the Unit cannot claim ITC on IGST paid on that supply. Counter firmly: Section 16(2) IGST extends ITC benefit to all zero-rated supplies — the SO's refusal of end-use concession does not extinguish the Unit's ITC right. The embargo in Section 17(5) CGST is overridden by Section 16(2) IGST when outward supply is zero-rated.
Principle: Debonding — Current Rate Applies, Not Entry Rate
Section 30, SEZ Act | Para 11.3.6
Key Ratio / Principle
Goods on which duties and taxes are NOT paid on their inward supply, whether by way of imports or domestic procurement and whether capital goods or inputs, into processing areas of the zone — domestic sale requires repayment of concession enjoyed earlier but at current rates. The rate of duty and tariff valuation applicable to goods removed from a Special Economic Zone shall be at the rate and tariff valuation in force as on the date of such removal, and where such date is not ascertainable, on the date of payment of duty.
When to Use — When a SEZ Unit disputes the duty rate applicable on debonded goods. The law is clear: the rate on the date of removal from SEZ applies — not the historical rate when goods entered. Also useful in challenging assessments where the Department applies an incorrect rate or valuation in debonding proceedings.
Principle: Developer's Regulatory Admissions are GST Evidence — mutatis mutandis
Para 11.1 | Section 53, SEZ Act | GST Litigation Reference
Key Ratio / Principle
Assertions made by Developer of the zone (or Unit in the zone) and admitted by regulator are mutatis mutandis reliable for purposes of GST. The 'end use' to which inward supplies made by the Developer are put to, as attested by Specified Officer, has a significant bearing on the tax treatment applicable in GST.
When to Use — When establishing the nature and purpose of supplies made to SEZ Developer/Units in a GST hearing. What the Developer declared to the SO about end use of inward supplies is reliable GST evidence. Also use when Department attempts to treat Developer's infrastructure services as taxable — counter with SO's certification of end use for authorized operations.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 12 — Companies Act & GST | Corporate Identity, IBC

⚖️ Chapter 12 — Companies Act & GST | Corporate Identity, IBC & Insolvency

Para 12.1 to 12.4 | Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root / L1 Node
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Risk
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
12.1 Relevance to GST — Distinct Person Fiction
12.2.1 Fiduciary Duties — Corporate Veil
12.2.2 Professional Directors & RCM
12.2.3 Holding Out — Director Identity
12.2.4 Branches — Distinct Person & HO-BO
12.2.5 Overseas PE
12.2.6 Agency by Ratification
12.2.7 Accounts & Consolidation — ELF vs CFS vs GST
12.3.1 Merger — Section 87 GST
12.3.2 Liquidation Estate
12.3.3 Recovery Action — Section 89 CGST
12.3.4 Offences by Companies
12.4.1 IBC — Contours & Swiss Ribbon
12.4.3 Rainbow Papers — GST as Secured Creditor
12.4.4 India Resurgence ARC
12.4.5 Vidarbha Industries
12.4.6 IBC Amendment Act 2023
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
12.1 — Relevance of Companies Act to GST — 'Distinct Person' Fiction ⇧ Index
🏢 Companies Act & GST — Corporate Identity & 'Distinct Person' Fiction
📝 A company is an artificial person — it acts through humans. GST creates an even finer fiction: even a single company can be split into multiple 'distinct persons' based on separate GST registrations. Each registration is treated as a separate entity for GST purposes — like a separate shop within the same company.
CORPORATE IDENTITY
Company = Artificial legal entity with 'capacity to contract' independent of founders, shareholders & directors
Role of functionaries (directors, managers) = sub-set of corporate authority
📝 Company has its own legal identity. Shareholders own it. Directors run it. But the company is the real contracting party — not the human beings behind it.
DISTINCT PERSON FICTION
GST's 'distinct person' fiction subdivides a single corporate entity into multiple distinct persons based on each GST registration
New location = 'fixed establishment' → derives 'capacity to contract' by operation of this fiction
📝 Even if a company has only ONE GST registration — if it has a location with indicia of 'fixed establishment', that location is treated as a distinct person. This creates GST supply even between branches of the same company.
💡 Branch Offices in different cities = distinct persons → supplies between them (stock transfers, management support) = taxable supply in GST even if not sales in books.
COMPLIANCE DUTY
Self-assessment in GST demands self to conduct assessment — NOT argue about unacceptability of enacted statutory provisions
Branch Offices operate as sub-contractors to Registered Office of same corporate entity
📝 Treating all branch offices as one entity and ignoring GST distinct person fiction = clear omission to apply deliberate legislative will. A company cannot say "we are one entity" to escape GST on branch-to-branch supplies.
12.2.1 — Fiduciary Duties — Corporate Veil & Piercing ⇧ Index
🛡️ Corporate Veil — When Separate Identity is Upheld vs Pierced
📝 The 'corporate veil' means a company is separate from its owners and managers. Courts normally respect this separation. But in cases of fraud, sham transactions, or anti-avoidance — courts will 'lift the veil' and look at the real persons behind the company.
GENERAL RULE
Salomon v. Salomon & Co. Ltd. (1897) AC 22 — Company separate from shareholders
LIC v. Escorts Ltd. AIR 1986 SC 1370 — strict interpretation upheld
Misfeasance falls on corporate entity — not past the veil
📝 The company bears all consequences of its actions — not the shareholders or directors personally. The gain from misadventure must accrue to the corporate entity, directly or indirectly.
EXCEPTION — VEIL LIFTED
Fraud, sham transactions, anti-avoidance → purposive construction applies
'Piercing the corporate veil' or 'lifting the corporate veil'
Cases where lifted:
(a) Santanu Ray v. UoI 1989 — active concealment by Director
(b) Jyothi Ltd. Kanwaljit Kaur Bhasin 1987 — actions against public welfare
(c) Workmen v. Associated Rubber Ind. 1986 — prevent refusal to meet employees' welfare
💡 GST Application: If a company routes transactions through subsidiaries/associates to claim artificial exemptions — this could trigger 'veil lifting' by GST authorities under anti-avoidance principles.
MALA FIDES RULE
Mala fides in action by corporate functionaries → imputed to corporate entity
Gains from misadventure must accrue to corporate entity directly or indirectly
Incompetent tax compliance ≠ mala fides
📝 If directors make shady deals — the company bears the GST consequences. But if a company just made a wrong tax decision due to ignorance — that is NOT mala fides. Indirect gains (like winning contracts by underpricing) count.
TURQUAND RULE Royal British Bank v. Turquand (1856) — Third parties are entitled to assume actions authorized by Board already in accordance with MoA. Assertion of corporate authority = assumed. Question of legal capacity to contract and ostensible authority = given.
📝 When a third party contracts with a company, they do not need to verify internal board resolutions — they can assume the person they dealt with had authority. This protects innocent third parties in GST disputes about who was authorized to make a supply.
12.2.2 — Professional Directors — RCM Liability on Company ⇧ Index
👔 Professional Directors — Reverse Charge Liability & Withdrawal-Failure Test
📝 Directors are NOT owners — shareholders are. Professional directors are employees (master-servant) or independent contractors (principal-to-principal). Their services attract GST RCM on the company. But only services provided in their 'capacity as director' attract this RCM — not other services they provide.
RCM RULE
Services of directors liable to GST on reverse charge basis on corporate entity
BUT — only services in their 'capacity' as directors alone attract this liability
📝 RCM applies only when the director is acting as a director. If the same person provides a separate service (like renting office space), that is a separate transaction — RCM rules differ.
WITHDRAWAL-FAILURE TEST
Ask: If non-payment of consideration — will it bring about breach of terms of directorship?
If YES → RCM applies (service in capacity as director)
If NO → directorship continues but performance fails under other contract → RCM does not apply on company
📝 Test: Would non-payment end the directorship? If yes — it's a directorial service attracting RCM. If non-payment only fails some other contract (say, lease) — that is not a director service and RCM does not apply on the company for that.
💡 Eg.541: Director owns office, leases to Company. Non-payment → quit notice under Section 106. Directorship unaffected. Hence lease rental does NOT attract RCM on Company.

💡 Eg.542: Sitting fee, Nomination-cum-Remuneration Committee fee, Audit Committee fee — all in director capacity → RCM applies on Company.
DIRECTORS NOT PERSONALLY LIABLE
Directors are not answerable for legal consequences arising to corporate entity that may be suboptimal or adverse — even in spite of best efforts
📝 A director who does their best but the company still ends up with a bad tax outcome is not personally liable for that outcome. Personal liability arises only for gross neglect, misfeasance, or breach of duty.
12.2.3 — Holding Out — Who Qualifies as 'Director' for GST RCM ⇧ Index
🔍 Holding Out — 'Director' for RCM Must be Admitted Under Companies Act
📝 Just because someone calls themselves a 'director' does not mean GST RCM applies. The reference to 'director' in GST law means persons admitted as director under Companies Act — not merely holding out to be one.
NOT SUFFICIENT
'Director' designated without being appointed on Board of corporate entity
Merely holding out to be director ≠ director for GST RCM purposes
📝 If a company calls someone 'Director – Business Development' but they are not on the Board of Directors under Companies Act — they are NOT a 'director' for GST RCM. They are just an employee.
TWIN CONDITIONS FOR RCM
(i) Payments made to persons admitted as 'director' under Companies Act
(ii) Such payment is in their 'capacity' as director and not otherwise
📝 Both conditions must be satisfied: (1) Officially a director on the Board + (2) The service is in that directorial role. If either condition fails — RCM does not apply on the company for that payment.
12.2.4 — Branches — Distinct Person & HO-BO GST Supplies ⇧ Index
🏬 Branches — GST Distinct Person Fiction & Head Office / Branch Office Supplies
📝 When a company has branches — third parties contract with the corporate entity, not the branch. But for GST, each branch is a distinct person. So when Head Office provides services to Branch (like management, IT, HR), that is a GST taxable supply — even though it is the same company.
THIRD PARTY ANGLE
Third-parties contract with corporate entity — are entitled to remain agnostic to fact that they interface with a Branch Office
BUT when 'distinct person' fiction is admitted — corporate entity must explain identity of distinct person making supplies
💡 Eg.543: Contract signed by Registered Office Delhi. Goods manufactured in Bhubaneshwar. Tax invoice from Delhi → implies purchase of goods by RO from Factory + onward sale. OR: invoice from Factory → implies marketing services by RO supplied to Factory.
HEAD OFFICE → BRANCH = TAXABLE SUPPLY
'If good for goose, good for gander' — if banking companies' HO taxed on management oversight services to Branches, same applies to ALL corporate entities
Definition of 'fixed establishment' in Section 2(50) CGST acknowledges result of corporate decision — no legal fiction
📝 A company cannot say 'our branches are us — so no supply'. The company itself chose to establish branches for business reasons. GST follows that corporate decision. Section 2(50) defines 'fixed establishment' without any legal fiction — it is a factual acknowledgement.
💡 Eg.544: Bank Head Office in Kochi — Board meets there, Corporate Legal/IT/HR/Finance/Tax located there — no retail banking. This = outward supply of management oversight & supervision services by HO to all Branch Offices (FAQ 55 & 56, CBIC to BFSI, 27 Dec 2018).
12.2.5 — Overseas PE — Permanent Establishment & GST ⇧ Index
🌐 Overseas PE — Board Resolutions as Admission & GST Consequences
📝 When a company constitutes an overseas Permanent Establishment (PE) — either voluntarily declared or found by enforcement — Board resolutions prove this fact. The income attributed to that PE in income-tax may not matter for GST — but the existence of PE itself triggers GST treatment.
PE CONSTITUTED
Declared voluntarily or result of enforcement action by regulators
Board resolutions = clear admission that PE exists overseas
INCOME ATTRIBUTED
Attribution of income to PE triggers treatment based on legal fiction qua each PE
Income attributed need NOT be accepted for GST purposes
BUT admission of existence of PE = sufficient assertion of fact for GST to impose treatment
📝 GST does not follow income-tax's income attribution to PE. But if you admit there IS a PE — GST will step in and determine what supplies flow to/from that PE and impose GST treatment accordingly.
12.2.6 — Agency by Ratification — Corporate Liability & GST ⇧ Index
🔗 Agency by Ratification — Joint Ventures & Corporate GST Exposure
📝 When corporate functionaries deny a master-servant relationship with the company, their transactions may still attract GST on the company through 'agency of necessity' or 'ratification'. If there were reasons to excuse the corporate entity from vicarious liability — GST treatment is still inescapable.
Corporate functionaries deny master-servant relationship → transactions carried under authority of corporate entity → exposed to onerous GST treatment
Agency of necessity / ratification → corporate entity suffers underlying tax treatment
Even if excused from vicarious liability → GST treatment in GST is inescapable
💡 Eg.545: EL&T-GAMUN bid for public infrastructure contract, assert 'no AOP'. Both are corporate entities. Tax treatment for (i) indivisible packages within overall contract works (ii) TDS allocation by NHAI (iii) common costs — remain to be explained. Any misstep = full GST incidence as each entity being 'sub-contractor' or 'intermediary' of combined entity (not being an AOP).
12.2.7 — Accounts & Consolidation — ELF vs CFS vs GST ⇧ Index
📊 Accounts & Consolidation — Financial Statements vs GST Records
📝 Accounting standards require consolidated financial statements. But GST operates on disaggregated individual entity data. A mismatch between financial statements and GST records is normal and expected — it does NOT by itself prove non-payment of GST.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Purpose of preparing financial statements is NOT to meet needs of GST law. No presumption can be raised based on mismatch of information appearing in financial statements and GST records — unless adverse inference is (i) left undisputed or (ii) accepted.
Income / Disclosure in Financial Statements NOT amounting to GST Turnover Turnover for GST Purposes NOT appearing in Financial Statements
Write-back of provisions created in earlier yearsBarter (goods and services) not accounted as purchase or sale
Write-back of balances due to creditors no longer payable after renegotiationsSupply of development rights by Landowner in exchange for constructed apartment units by Developer
Expense incurred in previous year revised and reduced in current yearAdvance received for project held as current liability — not satisfying 'pure agent' tests
Gains from restatement of outstanding balances in foreign exchangeSales to subsidiaries and associate entities are net off from purchases (COGS)
Gains from disposal of securities or land and completed buildings or other non-GST articlesProfit on sale of capital goods above WDV retained in balance sheet as capital reserve
Government subsidy receivedGrants received reported under capital method and not income method retained in balance sheet
Write-off of loans by Lender on settlement in insolvency resolution proceedings or other debt restructuringShare of expenses incurred in jointly-created assets adjusted from carried value of assets
Restatement of impairment loss provision in previous yearsDecommissioning business assets
ELF vs CFS vs GST — Key Transaction Comparison
TransactionELFCFSGST
Sales to third-party customersYESYESYES
Stock transfers to branches *NONOYES
Sales to subsidiaries *NONOYES
Management support to branches *NONOYES
Back-office support to subsidiaries *NONOYES
Inter-branch financial support **NONOYES
Inter-company loans to subsidiariesYESYESYES
Inter-branch capital goods transfersNONOYES
* Even when tax invoice issued after admitting outward supplies.    ** Notional value of interest admitted on outward supply.
⚠️ CRITICAL DISCLOSURES IN FINANCIAL STATEMENTS WITH GST BEARING
(a) Merger of different branches due to consolidation or discontinued operations
(b) Shifting of business assets (where ITC availed) to other States for project execution
(c) Estimated value of stock-in-trade given away to business associates for promotional purposes
(d) Housing loan / education / overseas assignment policies containing terms of enforceable contract
(e) Acquisition with ongoing GST litigation of determined demands / contingencies
(f) Acquisition via Resolution Plan from NCLT — treatment of write-down of assets & liabilities
💡 Eg.546: Turnover in audited financial statements does not match turnover in GST Annual Returns → GST appears to be payable on positive difference of the two turnovers.
💡 Eg.547: Other income appearing in financial statements not already subjected to GST → GST appears payable on such income.
12.3.1 — Merger — Section 87 CGST Act & Status Quo ⇧ Index
🔀 Merger — NCLT Approval vs GST Section 87 Status Quo Rule
📝 In a merger, there are two dates — the 'effective date' (when shareholders approve) and the 'approval date' (when NCLT formally approves, months or even a year later). Income-tax and financial statements restate everything to effective date. But GST does NOT — it requires status quo to be maintained throughout.
INCOME TAX / FINANCIAL STATEMENTS
Financials revised and restated 'as if' merger complete on effective date
Income-tax returns similarly revised
'Dating back' concept — merger completed on effective date
GST — SECTION 87 STATUS QUO
