GST compliance in respect of outward supplies and ITC — reported as that of Transferor without any restatement into Transferee's GST returns
Even if restatement done for income-tax / other statutes — GST status quo maintained
📝 From effective date to approval date — Transferor continues to file GST returns and pay GST. Transferee does NOT absorb those transactions into its GST returns. This is the opposite of income-tax treatment.
NUMERICAL ILLUSTRATION
Company A + Company B Merger
DescriptionCo. ACo. B
Before effective date50150
After effective date up to approval100200
After date of merger—75
Turnover per GST returns150425
Turnover per revised financials50525
Two Remarkable GST Points in Mergers
BEFORE APPROVAL — Section 81
Transfer of assets with intent to defraud Revenue are VOID unless made for (i) adequate consideration (ii) good faith (iii) without notice of pending proceedings, duly permitted by Proper Officer (Additional Commissioner)
AFTER APPROVAL — Section 85
Liability of Transferor — whether determined before or after effective date of merger — will be JOINT AND SEVERAL liability of Transferor and Transferee
📝 After merger — both companies are jointly liable for past GST dues of the Transferor. The Transferee cannot escape by saying 'we did not exist then'. This applies to merger, slump sale, amalgamation, and demerger.
12.3.2 — Liquidation Estate — Section 88 CGST & IBC ⇧ Index
🏚️ Liquidation Estate — GST Liability in Winding Up
📝 When a company winds up, all its assets form the 'liquidation estate'. GST dues are a charge on this estate. Section 88 CGST applies for corporate entities — but HUF/individuals/firms face Section 93 CGST, which can pursue successors-in-interest even after death of proprietor.
LIQUIDATION ESTATE
Assets of company from which to discharge liabilities at liquidation
Subject to value erosion in carried value of those assets
Gains from settlement of creditors for lesser amount (haircut) = NOT liquidation estate
SECTION 93 CGST — INDIVIDUALS/HUF/FIRMS
Liability determined subsequent to demise of proprietor/partner → encumbers liquidation estate
Recoverable from legal heirs or successors-in-interest
Right to sue (by Revenue) survives demise of defendant
📝 For individuals and HUFs — GST can pursue the legal heirs even after the proprietor dies. 'Death abates all things' in CPC (Order 22 Rule 1) — BUT Revenue's right to sue survives demise through Section 93.
CORPORATE ENTITIES — Section 88 CGST
Section 88 applies in case of liquidation
Resolution by restructuring of dues to financial credits — plan approved by NCLT prevails per IBC
12.3.3 — Recovery Action — Section 89 CGST — Directors of Private Companies ⇧ Index
💰 Section 89 CGST — Recovery from Directors of Private Companies
📝 Section 89 CGST allows Revenue to recover GST dues from Directors of private companies personally — but only if very specific conditions are met. All five conditions must be satisfied. If even one fails — personal recovery cannot proceed.
Five Conditions — ALL Must Be Satisfied for Director Recovery
(a)
Person must be a Director at any time during the disputed period
(b)
Demand must be made against the Company
(c)
Demand must become final after exhausting appellate remedies
(d)
Company must be unable to meet obligations AND omission by Revenue to take pre-emptive recovery action notwithstanding
(e)
Director must be found guilty of (i) gross neglect (ii) misfeasance OR (iii) breach of duty — directly resulting in non-recovery of Company's dues
SCOPE Applies only to private companies AND private companies converted to public companies (but up to date of such conversion). Does NOT apply to public companies.
📝 This provision is a safety net for Revenue when a private company evades and cannot pay. But it is not a sword against every director — the burden is on Revenue to prove gross neglect/misfeasance/breach. An innocent director who did their best is protected.
12.3.4 — Offences by Companies — Natural Person vs Juristic Person ⇧ Index
⚖️ Offences by Companies — Who Bears Criminal Prosecution?
📝 A company (juristic person) cannot go to jail. So when a company commits a GST offence — only a monetary fine can be imposed on the company. The natural persons (directors, managers) who actually committed the offence must be prosecuted individually. But can they be prosecuted if the gains went to the company?
BASIC RULE
Offences by juristic persons = perpetrated by natural persons
Company = can only be fined (monetary fine)
Natural persons involved = must be prosecuted individually
JURISPRUDENTIAL QUESTION
If natural person discharged official duties AND gains belong to juristic person → permissible to prosecute natural person merely because no remedy in law to prosecute juristic person?
Perhaps statute must find remedy for this lacuna until then excuse natural persons
📝 Lacuna = A gap or defect in the law. Here — company commits offence, gains go to company, but company cannot be jailed. Natural person who is merely an obedient officer should arguably not bear criminal prosecution for company's offence.
DEFENCE AVAILABLE
Omission to implicate natural persons under Section 122(3) CGST = good defence against imposition of penalty under Section 122(1) on juristic persons
Natural persons = at best guilty of being abettor — NOT principal offender
GST law has nothing that shifts prosecution burden from juristic person to natural person
📝 Mousell Brother v. London & North Western Rail Co. (1916-17): To ascertain whether a particular Act of Parliament makes principal liable — regard must be had to object of statute, words used, nature of duty, the person upon whom it is imposed, and the person upon whom penalty is imposed.
12.4.1 — IBC Contours & Swiss Ribbon — First Principles ⇧ Index
🏦 IBC — Insolvency & Bankruptcy Code 2016 — Structure & Swiss Ribbon Principles
📝 IBC 2016 replaces equity-holders (shareholders) who proved unworthy of running the business with debt-holders (creditors) to preserve economic value. The Apex Court in Swiss Ribbons (2019) laid down the first principles of IBC — distinguishing financial and operational creditors, establishing CIRP process validity, and resolving constitutional challenges.
IBC CIRP Process — Key IBC Sections
SectionDescription
7, 9 and 10Application to commence CIRP process
22IRP may be continued by CoC to act as RP
17, 18 and 23IRP to replace Board of Directors of CD
14 up to 31(2)Moratorium on all pending suits
30Recommendation to CoC of a Resolution Plan
31Approval of Resolution Plan by NCLT
33(1) and 34Confirmation of unviability of CIRP → recommending liquidation of CD
35, 36 and 38Completion of liquidation
53Restatement of dues and settlement (haircut) — waterfall provision
54Dissolution on liquidation
Swiss Ribbons (P.) Ltd. v. UOI — 2019 | 101 taxmann.com 389 | 152 SCL 365 (SC)
JUSTIFICATION FOR IBC
Focus = revival and continuation of CD instead of liquidation
Separate ownership from management if creditors' interest not secured
Proceedings are NOT adversarial to CD but protective of their interests
FINANCIAL vs OPERATIONAL CREDITORS
Operational creditors = trade-linked, limited to transactions they enter into
Financial creditors = not privy to transaction-level activities → sufficient distinction exists inter se
Exclusion of operational creditors from CoC = not offending UNCITRAL Guidelines
CLAIM vs DEFAULT
Financial creditor must prove 'default'
Operational creditor must only 'claim' dues
Well-laid out in separate statutory provisions
SECTION 29A — Exclusion
Only 'connected' persons are excluded — no vires challenge
ILC Report recommended exclusion of MSME by enacting Section 240A
12.4.3 — Rainbow Papers — GST as Secured Creditor in IBC ⇧ Index
💎 STO v. Rainbow Papers (P.) Ltd. — MANU/SC/1109/2022 — GST Dues as First Charge
📝 The big question in IBC vs GST: Are Government tax dues (GST) 'secured creditor' dues that must be paid before other financial creditors? Rainbow Papers said YES — because Section 82 CGST Act makes GST a first charge on property. But this conflicts with IBC waterfall and must be read carefully.
SECTION 82 CGST
"Tax to be first charge on property" — Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in any law for the time being in force, save as otherwise provided in IBC 2016 — any amount payable by taxable person on account of tax, interest or penalty = first charge on property of such taxable person
📝 Section 82 says GST dues are FIRST charge on all property of the taxable person — like a super-lien. BUT it expressly saves IBC 2016 (the opening words 'save as otherwise provided in IBC'). So IBC provisions override Section 82.
GVAT ACT — Rainbow Papers Facts
State = secured creditor under GVAT Act
Section 3(30) IBC defines 'secured creditor' = creditor in favour of whom security interest is created
Such security interest could be created by operation of law
Definition of 'secured creditor' in IBC does NOT exclude any Government or Governmental Authority
DILUTION OF RAINBOW PAPERS
For GST purposes: affirmative action by Liquidator expected in law
NOT from Resolution Professional when CIRP process is initiated
Only when Corporate Debtor cannot be salved and liquidation ordered by Appellate Authority
Result: GST as first charge operates only in LIQUIDATION — not during CIRP
📝 Rainbow Papers is diluted for GST: Govt. dues are 'secured creditor' but only in liquidation. During CIRP (resolution process), the waterfall provision under Section 53 IBC governs — and Govt. dues rank lower. Recourse to Section 82 CGST during CIRP resolution stage = uphill task.
12.4.4 — India Resurgence ARC — Dissenting Financial Creditor Rights ⇧ Index
🔑 India Resurgence ARC (P.) Ltd. v. Amit Metaliks Ltd. — (CA) 1700/2021
📝 When a dissenting financial creditor has security — can they enforce that security independently? Or are they limited to what the resolution plan gives them? India Resurgence ARC clarifies that 'equitable treatment' means equal treatment among the same class — not unlimited rights to enforce security against the resolution plan.
PRINCIPLE
Equitable treatment of creditors = equitable treatment among SAME CLASS of creditors + protection from each other
Creditors cannot be imbued with greater rights in bankruptcy proceedings than under general law
Rateable distribution of realizable value (Section 53) cannot be partial including priority and value of security held
DISSENTING CREDITOR — PAYMENT
Payment to dissenting financial creditor = only by handing over quantum of money OR allowing recovery by enforcement of security interest — as per entitlement of such dissenting financial creditor
Conditioned by extent of value receivable by him (Jaypee Kensington ratio)
POLICY PRINCIPLE (SC)
If appellate propositions accepted → result would be that rather than insolvency resolution and maximization of value of assets of CD, the processes would lead to MORE LIQUIDATIONS with every secured financial creditor having option to stand on dissent
📝 Apex Court's policy concern: If every dissenting secured creditor can blockade a resolution plan, we will never have resolutions — only liquidations. That defeats the entire purpose of IBC to revive businesses.
12.4.5 — Vidarbha Industries — NCLT Must Examine All Attendant Factors ⇧ Index
⚡ Vidarbha Industries Power Ltd. v. Axis Bank Ltd. — 2022 SCC Online SC 841
📝 Vidarbha Industries (converted to IPP) entered IBC due to commercial tariff dispute (fuel cost increase). The question: Should NCLT automatically admit CIRP when debt and default are proved? Or should it examine all surrounding factors? SC held: NCLT MUST apply its mind to all relevant factors before initiating CIRP.
NCLAT's ERROR
NCLAT erred — only required to see whether there was a debt and CD had defaulted in making repayment
Existence of financial debt + default → only gives financial creditor RIGHT to apply for CIRP
Does NOT automatically initiate CIRP
📝 NCLAT was wrong to mechanically admit CIRP just because debt and default were proved. That is just the trigger — not the full analysis NCLT must do.
SC's DIRECTION
NCLT required(d) to apply its mind to relevant factors including:
• Feasibility of initiation of CIRP
• Against electricity generating company operated under statutory control
• Impact of MERC appeal pending in SC
• Order of APTEL referred above
• Overall financial health and viability of CD under existing management
GST CONSEQUENCE
Any claim of default including those of Commissioner (u/s 88 CGST) was rendered subject to waterfall provision in Section 53 CGST
AND to inquiry into merits of case occasioning the default
Recourse to Section 82 CGST = uphill task to recover legitimate dues
📝 For GST: After Vidarbha, even if GST Commissioner files claim in CIRP — NCLT will examine the merits of the underlying GST demand. The claim goes through waterfall (Section 53 IBC). Section 82 CGST first-charge becomes very difficult to enforce during CIRP.
12.4.6 — IBC Amendment Act 2023 — Key Changes ⇧ Index
🔧 IBC Amendment Act 2023 — Eight Proposed Changes (MCA Circular 18 Jan 2023)
(a) Increased reliance on data furnished by IUs (Information Utilities)
(b) Application under Section 7 must be admitted as soon as default is established without entering into reasons for default
(c) Pre-packaged framework for resolution
(d) Isolation of projects with delinquency rather than expose entire legal entity to CIRP process
(e) Review of waterfall provisions to provide (i) creditors up to liquidation value in order of priority (ii) surplus distributed rateably in respect of unsatisfied claims (iii) residue to equity holders
(f) Inclusion of guarantor's assets in pool of assets for resolution
(g) All unsecured creditors to be treated on par INCLUDING Government
📝 This is a major GST concern — if Govt. (GST dues) treated as unsecured creditor on par with others, recovery in IBC proceedings becomes very limited.
(h) Immunity to resolution applicant from legacy dues to Government
📝 Resolution applicant (new buyer) gets protection from previous GST/tax demands of the Corporate Debtor. This makes resolution plans more attractive.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 12 | Companies Act, IBC & GST ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name and provision.
Salomon v. Salomon & Co. Ltd. (1897) AC 22
House of Lords | UK | Foundation of Corporate Separate Identity Principle | Upheld in LIC v. Escorts Ltd. AIR 1986 SC 1370
Key Principle
A company has a separate legal identity from its shareholders — it is not their agent. Corporate consequences fall on the corporate entity and do not travel past the corporate veil. Strict interpretation of separate identity can only be overcome by express statutory provisions involving fraud or sham transactions where purposive construction applies — referred to as 'piercing the corporate veil'.
When to Use — (i) When GST authorities attempt to hold directors/shareholders personally liable for company's GST dues without invoking Section 89/122 CGST specifically. (ii) When authorities try to consolidate group companies as one entity for GST assessment ignoring distinct person registrations. (iii) Defence: Salomon principle bars recovery from directors unless specific grounds of Section 89 are established.
Distinct Person Fiction — Section 25(4) & (5) CGST Act | Section 2(50) CGST Act
Para 12.1 | CBIC FAQ 55 & 56 to BFSI dated 27 Dec 2018
Key Principle
Legal fiction of 'distinct person' in GST subdivides the corporate entity into multiple entities. Where any new location of the corporate entity bears the indicia of a 'fixed establishment', then notwithstanding that no separate GST registration has been obtained for such location, it will derive 'capacity to contract' by operation of this fiction. Branch Offices operating as sub-contractors to the Registered Office of the same corporate entity — treating them as dependent and inseparable Offices would be a clear omission to apply the deliberate legislative will. Self-assessment in GST demands self to conduct assessment and not argue about unacceptability of enacted statutory provisions.
When to Use — (i) When demanding GST on stock transfers / management support / back-office services between branches of same company. (ii) When assessee argues 'we are one entity, no supply between branches'. Counter: Distinct person fiction operates mandatorily — HO services to branches = outward supply = GST payable.
Director Services — Withdrawal-Failure Test — Section 9(3) CGST Act read with Notification 13/2017-CT(R)
Para 12.2.2 | Section 9(3) CGST Act | Eg.541 & Eg.542
Key Principle
Services of directors are liable to GST on reverse charge basis but only services in their 'capacity' as directors alone attract reverse charge liability on the corporate entity. By applying the 'withdrawal-failure' test — whether non-payment of said consideration will bring about a breach of terms of directorship or directorship will continue but performance of obligations under some other contract will fail — must be examined. For RCM to apply: (i) Payment must be to persons admitted as 'director' under Companies Act and (ii) such payment is in their 'capacity' as director and not otherwise.
When to Use — (i) When SCN demands RCM on all payments to persons designated as 'director' regardless of capacity — challenge: apply withdrawal-failure test; not all payments to directors attract RCM. (ii) Eg.541: Office lease by director to company — non-payment → quit notice, directorship unaffected → NO RCM. (iii) Eg.542: Sitting fee / Committee fees → RCM applies.
Financial Statements ≠ GST Records — No Presumption from Mismatch
Para 12.2.7 | Section 133 Companies Act | AS-21 / Ind AS-110 | Eg.546 & Eg.547
Key Principle
Purpose of preparing financial statements is not to meet the needs of GST law. No presumption can be raised based on mismatch of information appearing in financial statements and GST records, unless such adverse inference is left undisputed or accepted. Data appearing in financial statements (ELF or CFS) do not facilitate any inference about information that would imply non-payment of GST as tax treatment operates of 'time of supply' and 'deemed supply' principles that are diametrically opposed to the disclosure requirements in standards of accounting.
When to Use — (i) In GST audit u/s 65 or scrutiny u/s 61 when officer proposes demand based on financial statement turnover being higher than GSTR returns turnover. Counter: financial statements follow accounting standards — not GST time-of-supply. Mismatch is normal and expected. (ii) Stock transfers, management support, subsidiary sales — appear in GST but NOT in ELF/CFS — explain each item in the table.
Section 87 CGST Act — Merger Status Quo — GST vs Income-Tax Restatement
Para 12.3.1 | Section 87 CGST Act | Sections 81 & 85 CGST Act
Key Principle
Section 87 of Central GST Act requires status quo be maintained. GST compliance in respect of outward supplies and input tax credit on inward supplies during the interval of time between effective date and date of approval will continue to be reported as that of the Transferor without any restatement into the GST returns of Transferee, notwithstanding that such a restatement has been carried out for purposes of income-tax and other statutes. Liability of Transferor — whether determined before or after effective date of merger (or any other form of transfer) — will be joint and several liability of Transferor and Transferee (Section 85).
When to Use — (i) In GST notices post-merger demanding GST from Transferee for Transferor's period — show Section 87 status quo: Transferor filed all GST returns. (ii) Caution to Transferee in due diligence: Section 85 — joint and several liability applies. Check all Transferor GST litigation before merger. (iii) For tax invoice disputes between effective date and approval date — identity of supplier = Transferor.
Section 89 CGST Act — Five Conditions for Director Recovery — ALL Must Be Satisfied
Para 12.3.3 | Section 89 CGST Act | Private companies only
Key Principle
Dues of private company can be recovered from Directors only where: (a) Person must be a Director at any time during the disputed period; (b) Demand must be made against the Company; (c) Demand must become final after exhausting appellate remedies; (d) Company must be unable to meet its obligations and omission by Revenue to take pre-emptive recovery action notwithstanding; (e) Director must be charged with and found to be guilty of gross neglect, misfeasance, or breach of duty, directly resulting in such non-recovery of Company's dues. This mandate is found in Section 89 of Central GST Act and applies only to private companies and private companies converted to public companies but up to date of such conversion.
When to Use — When Revenue issues notice to Director personally for company's GST dues — challenge: (i) Which of the five conditions are satisfied? (ii) Was demand first exhausted against company? (iii) Is demand 'final' (all appeals exhausted)? (iv) Company still exists and has assets — pre-emptive recovery not attempted. (v) Was Director proved guilty of gross neglect specifically? All five must be present — if any one fails, director recovery is premature and illegal.
Offences by Companies — Natural Person Cannot be Principal Offender — Section 122(3) CGST
Para 12.3.4 | Section 122 CGST Act | Mousell Brother v. London & North Western Rail Co. (1916-17) All ER Rep 1101 | J Atkin
Key Ratio — J Atkin in Mousell Brother
Prima facie a principal is not to be made criminally responsible for the acts of his servants, yet the Legislature may prohibit an act or enforce a duty in such words as to make the prohibition or the duty absolute; in which case the principal is liable if the act is in fact done by his servants. To ascertain whether a particular Act of Parliament has that effect or not regard must be had to the object of the statute, the words used, the nature of the duty laid down, the person upon whom it is imposed, the person by whom it would in ordinary circumstances be performed, and the person upon whom the penalty is imposed.
When to Use — (i) When GST authorities prosecute Directors/Managers for offences where gains accrued to the company. Defence: There is nothing in GST law that shifts burden of prosecution from juristic person to natural person. Natural person = at best an abettor, not principal offender. (ii) Omission to implicate natural persons under Section 122(3) CGST = good defence against penalty on juristic person under Section 122(1).
Section 82 CGST vs IBC Waterfall — Rainbow Papers Diluted by Vidarbha
STO v. Rainbow Papers (P.) Ltd. MANU/SC/1109/2022 | Vidarbha Industries Power Ltd. v. Axis Bank 2022 SCC Online SC 841 | Section 82 CGST Act | Section 53 IBC
Key Principle
Section 82 CGST Act makes GST dues a first charge on property of taxable person — but expressly saves IBC 2016 ("save as otherwise provided in the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016"). Rainbow Papers held State = secured creditor under Gujarat VAT Act — Committee of Creditors cannot secure their own dues at cost of statutory dues owed to any Government or Governmental Authority. However, Vidarbha dilutes this: affirmative action by Liquidator is expected in law — NOT from Resolution Professional when CIRP process is initiated, only when Corporate Debtor cannot be salved and liquidation is ordered. Result: Section 82 CGST first-charge operates primarily in liquidation — not during CIRP resolution process.
When to Use — (i) For GST Commissioner filing claim in CIRP — cite Rainbow Papers: GST = secured creditor, not to be subordinated to unsecured financial creditors. (ii) For Resolution Applicant or Resolution Professional opposing GST claims — cite Vidarbha: NCLT must examine feasibility factors; Section 82 claim is subject to waterfall (Section 53 IBC) during CIRP. (iii) IBC Amendment 2023 Proposal (g): Unsecured creditors including Government to be treated on par — if enacted, Rainbow Papers position may be further weakened for GST.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter 12 — Companies Act & Insolvency | GST Litigation Reference
📖 Ch 13 — Securities Contracts Act

📜 Chapter 13 — Securities Contracts Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Outside GST
Prohibited / Taxable / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
13.1 Relevance to GST
13.2.1 Shares & Debentures — Definition of Securities
13.2.2 Derivatives
13.2.3 Options in Securities
13.2.4 ESOP
13.2.5 Share-Based Settlement
13.2.6 ESOP of Parent Co.
13.2.7 Important Authorities
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
13.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
📜 Securities Contracts Act — Relevance to GST
Identifying Transactions Outside GST Purview
📝 Securities law tells us which instruments are "securities" — and securities are excluded from both "goods" and "services" under GST. So understanding securities law directly helps you know when GST does NOT apply.
Understanding securities laws helps identify transactions that are outside the purview of GST
✗ NOT SECURITIES
Actionable claims — excluded from GST via Schedule III Para 6
📝 Actionable claims (like lottery tickets per Sunrise) are not securities — they get their own exclusion route under Schedule III.
✗ NOT SECURITIES
REP license & licenses under FEMA (Foreign Trade Act 1992) — neither actionable claims nor securities though 'traded'
📝 Just because something is traded in the market does not make it a security. The legal form decides — not the trading activity.
✗ NOT SECURITIES
TDR certificates granted to 'land losers' — not securities, yet traded
📝 Transfer of Development Rights (TDRs) look like a tradeable instrument — but legally they are not securities. Easy to mislabel — heightens need for care.
⚠️ CAUTION
Ease with which articles can be misleading — need to call out 'securities' specifically
Statutory definition overrides logical reasoning
📝 Common sense logic can mislead. Only the statutory definition of "securities" under Securities Contracts Act decides the issue — not trade practice or labelling.
FORM vs SUBSTANCE
Speculative trade with no intent to take delivery of object → guided by the 'form' transaction takes rather than its 'substance'
📝 In derivative trading, parties never intend to actually take/give goods. The "form" (securities trade) governs — not the substance (underlying commodity). So it stays outside GST.
💡 Trading in commodity futures (gold/silver) on exchanges — even though underlying is a commodity, derivative form keeps it as a security = outside GST.
GST PRINCIPLE
GST treatment cannot be misapplied bona fide
Securities law is regularly amended to include more species as tradable securities pervade industry
📝 Even if you genuinely believe something is not a security — if it legally is, GST cannot be applied. Good faith is not a defence to misapplication. Always check current law.
PRIVATE MATTERS
Securities law is a private matters' legislation
Assertions made before securities regulators will pollinate tax treatment in GST
📝 What you tell SEBI, NSE, BSE, RBI about your transaction will be used against you (or for you) in GST proceedings. Consistency across regulators is critical.
💡 A company classifies its instrument as a derivative before SEBI. Revenue cannot then say it's a goods transaction for GST purposes — the SEBI disclosure is evidence.
13.2.1 — Shares & Debentures | Statutory Definition of "Securities" [Section 2(h)] ⇧ Index
📑 Section 2(h) — "Securities" Include
Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956
📝 This is the master definition. If an instrument falls within this list — it is a "security" and therefore excluded from both goods and services under GST. Existence of a tariff HSN entry does not override this exclusion.
Clause What is included Plain English GST Position
§2(h)(i) Shares, scrips, stocks, bonds, debentures, debenture stock or other marketable securities of a like nature in or of any incorporated company or other body corporate The basic corporate securities — equity shares, preference shares, debentures, bonds of any company. The backbone of capital markets. Excluded from goods & services — outside GST. Transfer of shares = no GST.
§2(h)(ia) Derivative Futures, options, swaps — instruments whose value is derived from an underlying asset (stock, commodity, index, currency). Outside GST — specifically covered under securities definition. Test: intent not to take delivery.
§2(h)(ib) Units or any other instrument issued by any collective investment scheme to investors Units of a Collective Investment Scheme (CIS) — pooled investment vehicles regulated by SEBI. Units = securities = outside GST.
§2(h)(ic) Security receipt as defined in clause (zg) of Section 2 of SARFAESI Act, 2002 Receipts issued by Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs) when they securitise non-performing loans. Used in banking/ARC transactions. Security receipts = securities = outside GST. ARC transactions involving these are not taxable.
§2(h)(id) Units or any other instrument issued to investors under any mutual fund scheme Mutual fund units — what you receive when you invest in an MF scheme (equity fund, debt fund, hybrid, etc.). MF units = securities = outside GST. Sale/purchase of MF units not taxable.
§2(h)(ii) Government securities G-Secs, T-Bills, State Government bonds — sovereign debt instruments. Government securities = securities = outside GST.
§2(h)(iia) Such other instruments as may be declared by Central Government to be securities Residual category — Central Government can expand the list by notification. Reason why securities law is "regularly amended". Any Central Government declared instrument = security = automatically outside GST.
§2(h)(iii) Rights or interest in securities Not just the security itself — even rights IN securities (e.g., right to receive dividend, right to subscribe in rights issue) are also securities. Rights & interests in securities = outside GST. ESOP (options) — treated as options in securities — falls here.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE — HSN ENTRY DOES NOT OVERRIDE
Securities are excluded from definition of 'goods' as well as 'services' in GST. Even though there is a tariff entry HSN 997171 relating to 'holding equity control in a subsidiary company' — existence of such tariff entry does NOT dictate taxability in derogation of the express exclusion of 'securities' from the scope of GST.

Legislative wisdom cannot be made light of, nor can missing ingredients be 'furnished by imagination'. Existence of tariff entry only arouses suspicion about possible taxability — it is only the taxing ingredients clearly laid down in the statute that will bring the transaction within the scope of charging provision.
13.2.2 — Derivatives ⇧ Index
📉 Derivatives — Trading Without Intent to Take Delivery
Section 2(ac) — Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act
📝 A derivative is a contract whose value comes from something else (stock price, commodity price, index). You speculate on price movement — you never actually take the underlying goods. This "no delivery intent" is what keeps derivatives firmly inside the securities exclusion and outside GST.
Speculation about price changes without intention to take delivery → approved in form of derivatives' trading
THE DECISIVE TEST
Criterion of utmost relevance = 'intention not to take delivery' of the underlying commodities
→ Keeps transaction squarely planted within the exclusion available to securities from any treatment in GST law
📝 The moment you intend to actually receive the goods (like physically taking gold in a futures contract) — it could be argued it steps outside pure derivative territory. The no-delivery intent is the anchor.
💡 Commodity derivatives — trading in metals, spice, financial instruments on NCDEX/MCX — even though financial commitments are with reference to commodities, these do NOT become goods transactions for GST. The derivative form prevails.
STATUTE — Section 2(ac) "derivative" includes:
(A) A security derived from a debt instrument, share, loan (secured or unsecured), risk instrument or contract for differences or any other form of security
📝 A contract for differences = you and counterparty settle the difference in price — no actual delivery of shares/goods. Classic derivative.
(B) A contract which derives its value from the prices, or index of prices, of underlying securities
📝 Nifty/Sensex futures and options — their value is derived from the index of underlying stock prices. Classic definition of financial derivatives.
💡 Nifty 50 options trading — value derived from index of 50 underlying stocks. Clearly within §2(ac)(B) = security = outside GST.
⚠️ CAUTION — COMMODITY REFERENCE ≠ GOODS TRANSACTION
Just because financial commitments are made or accepted with reference to commodities (metals, spice, financial instruments, etc.) does NOT bring these transactions out of securities and into goods so as to become exposed to GST.
13.2.3 — Options in Securities ⇧ Index
⚖️ Options in Securities — Section 2(d)
Warrants, Teji, Mandi, Put, Call — All Securities
Warrants — NOT stock units but covenants-with-contingency(ies) that give rise to rights to claim stock units
📝 A warrant is a promise (covenant) with conditions (contingency) — when conditions are met, you can claim the stock. It is not a stock itself but a right to get stock. This right is itself treated as a security.
✓ GST POSITION
Options in securities are also 'securities' and treated exactly as securities in GST
→ Outside GST
THE TEST
Test of securities is NOT whether they are traded —
but that they are:
(i) Recognized in law  &  (ii) Tradeable even though among a small group of persons with interest
📝 Even if an instrument is traded in a very small, restricted circle — as long as it is recognized as a security in law and is capable of being transferred — it qualifies as a security. Breadth of market does not matter.
STATUTE — Section 2(d) "option in securities" means:

A contract for the purchase or sale of a right to buy or sell, or a right to buy and sell, securities in future, and includes a teji, a mandi, a teji mandi, a galli, a put, a call or a put and call in securities
📝 The list is exhaustive of traditional Indian market terms — Teji (call option), Mandi (put option), Teji Mandi (straddle). All are recognized as options in securities = excluded from GST.
💡 Stock exchange traded Nifty Call Options (teji), Reliance Put Options (mandi) — all fall within §2(d) = securities = outside GST. No GST on option premium received/paid.
13.2.4 — ESOP (Employee Stock Option Plan) ⇧ Index
👔 ESOP — Employee Stock Option Plan
SEBI (ESOP & ESPP) Guidelines 1999 | Options in Securities
📝 ESOP is simply the "option" to purchase employer's shares at a discounted/fixed price, after fulfilling certain service conditions (vesting). Since options in securities = securities = outside GST, ESOP schemes are generally outside GST's reach.
ESOP = Several variants commonly referred as 'ESOP'. Simplest = 'option' to purchase designated equity instruments
ELIGIBLE
Employees participant in an approved plan are entitled to benefit
VESTING
Based on vesting criteria as determined under the plan
📝 Vesting = conditions to be fulfilled (like serving 3 years) before the option can be exercised. Before vesting, the option is "unvested" and cannot be exercised.
ATTRACTION
Discount compared to traded price or fair value + potential upside on exit under the plan
📝 Employee gets shares cheaper than market price — the discount is the real benefit. Plus any appreciation in share price between grant and exercise date.
📊 Four ESOP Variants — SEBI Guidelines 1999
ESOP
Employee Stock Option Plan
Right to purchase shares at predetermined price after vesting
ESPP
Employee Stock Purchase Plan
Employee regularly contributes; company buys shares at discount at end of period
RSU
Restricted Stock Unit
Promise to deliver shares after vesting — no option, actual shares granted
SAR
Stock Appreciation Right
Cash settlement for the appreciation in share value between grant date and exercise date — no actual shares issued
💡 An employee is granted ESOP for 1,000 shares at ₹100 per share (current market ₹150). After 3-year vesting, she exercises the option — buys at ₹100, market is ₹250. Gain = ₹150 per share. This is capital gain/perquisite under Income Tax — NOT a GST event. The option = security = excluded.
13.2.5 — Share-Based Settlement ⇧ Index
💰 Share-Based Settlement — Contract Denominated in Money, Settled in Stock
ICAI Guidance Note on Accounting for Share-Based Payments
📝 There is a pre-existing contract in money terms (say ₹100 for consultancy). But instead of paying cash at settlement, the company allots shares of equivalent value. GST arises on the underlying supply (consultancy), not on the share allotment.
Scenario A — Contractor/Non-Employee
Contract for supply of goods/services denominated in monetary terms
↓
At settlement: equivalent value of stock units allotted instead of cash
GST INCIDENCE
GST arises on first leg (supply of goods/services)
Share allotment = merely mode of payment = no separate GST
📝 Think of it like barter — you supplied consultancy worth ₹100, paid by shares worth ₹100. GST is on the consultancy supply. The share allotment is not a separate supply event.
💡 Journal: Consultancy charges Dr. 100 / To Creditor Cr. 90 / To TDS payable Cr. 10. Then: Creditor Dr. 90 / To Share capital Cr. 90. GST on ₹100 consultancy — not on share allotment.
Scenario B — Employee
Unlike contractors, when employees are allotted stock units —
first leg of transaction was not recorded but now made mandatory
(income-tax deduction deferred when plan is 'recognized')
✓ NO GST
No incidence of GST on first leg which is excluded from tax vide Schedule III Para 1 (services by employee to employer in course of employment)
📝 Schedule III Para 1 says services by employee to employer = not a supply. So no GST on salary-in-shares either. The employee supplies service, employer pays in shares — both legs outside GST.
💡 Journal: Salaries Dr. 200 / To Salary payable/bank Cr. 140 / To TDS payable Cr. 60. Then: Salary payable Dr. 140 / To Share capital Cr. 140. No GST anywhere — employer-employee = Schedule III exclusion.
ICAI GUIDANCE
Large number of entities in India involved in share-based payment transactions. ICAI Research Committee revised 'Guidance Note on Accounting for Share-based Payments' to align with Ind AS 102 — covering both employees and non-employees, including group-wide share-based payment transactions (grants by parent company to employees of subsidiary company).
13.2.6 — ESOP of Parent Co. (Cross-Charge Scenario) ⇧ Index
🏢 ESOP of Parent Co. — SubsiCo Buys Plan from ParentCo
Cross-Charge | No Privity Between ParentCo and SubsiCo Employees
📝 When a parent company's ESOP is extended to employees of a subsidiary — there is no direct contract between the parent and the subsidiary's employees. The subsidiary "buys the plan" from the parent and uses it to reward its own employees. This creates a cross-charge transaction between ParentCo and SubsiCo.
Three-Party Structure — Unlike Own-Stock ESOP
NO PRIVITY
No privity of contract between ParentCo and employees of SubsiCo
📝 Privity of contract = direct contractual relationship. ParentCo and SubsiCo employees never directly contract with each other — it flows through SubsiCo.
MECHANISM
SubsiCo 'purchases the plan' from ParentCo & expends it to reward its own employees
💡 Journal in SubsiCo: Share-based plan expenses Dr. 100 / To ParentCo Cr. 100. Then ParentCo: ParentCo Dr. 100 / To Bank Cr. 100. SubsiCo pays ParentCo for the plan.
OPTION PRICING
Option pricing model must be applied to arrive at fair value of stock units involved in the plan
📝 Black-Scholes or similar model needed to value the options — not just the market price of shares. Illustration in book oversimplifies valuation aspect.
ICAI GUIDANCE NOTE (Sept 2020) — CROSS-CHARGE
ICAI requires a cross-charge from ParentCo of monetary value of cost incurred under share-based compensation plan when extending plan to employees of SubsiCo.
This cross-charge must be paid through banking channel.
📝 The cross-charge from ParentCo to SubsiCo for the ESOP cost — this is a service (supply of management/HR service) from ParentCo to SubsiCo. It may attract GST. The assertion before SEBI/ICAI about the nature of this cross-charge will pollinate the GST treatment.
💡 ParentCo (US) cross-charges SubsiCo (India) ₹1 crore for ESOP plan. Is this an import of service by SubsiCo from ParentCo attracting GST under reverse charge? Likely yes — but depends on whether it is a management support service or a capital contribution. ICAI guidance note says it is a capital contribution to SubsiCo — which affects GST analysis.
ACCOUNTING TREATMENT (ICAI — Subsidiary financial statements)
Under 'share-based payment transaction' definition — Subsidiary receives goods or services in a share-based payment arrangement.
Transaction classified as equity-settled transaction because Subsidiary 'has no obligation to settle the transaction with the supplier'.
Although Subsidiary is not party to agreement with its employees — it still records a cost. Accounting treatment represents that Subsidiary has received a capital contribution from Parent, which Subsidiary has then 'spent' on employee remuneration.
📝 In simple terms — the parent gives the subsidiary a gift (capital contribution) in the form of bearing the ESOP cost. The subsidiary books this as an expense against the employees' benefit. The "capital contribution" characterization is important for GST — capital contributions are generally not supplies and therefore not taxable.
13.2.7 — Some Important Authorities ⇧ Index
⚖️ Jurisprudence on Securities, Actionable Claims & Goods
Identifying the Line Between Securities / Actionable Claims / Goods
📝 Courts over decades have wrestled with the question: Is this instrument a "good"? An "actionable claim"? Or a "security"? The answer determines whether sales tax / GST applies. These cases form the landmark chain on this issue — each case either followed, distinguished or overruled the earlier one.
Case Citation Subject Held Overruled / Distinguished GST Relevance
H Anraj v. State of TN 1986 taxmann.com 838 (SC) Lottery tickets Lottery tickets have two aspects: (i) right to participate in draw & (ii) right to claim prize money. Lottery tickets = 'goods' not 'actionable claim'. Exigible to sales tax. Base decision — later doubted by Sunrise Established "two rights" theory — each aspect of an instrument must be separately examined
Vikas Sales Corporation v. CCT 1996 taxmann.com 1126 (SC) / AIR 1996 SC 2082 REP licenses REP licenses are 'goods' because they have value in and of themselves and do not represent some other property like a title document. Liable to sales tax. Approved Anraj. Impliedly overruled by Sunrise Associates REP licenses = goods in Vikas. But post-Sunrise & Yasha — position is complex. FEMA licenses under Foreign Trade Act are neither securities nor actionable claims.
Sunrise Associates v. NCT [2006] 4 STT 105 (SC) Lottery tickets Doubted correctness of Anraj. Rejected "two rights" theory. Held lottery tickets are indivisible composite right = 'actionable claims' NOT exigible to sales tax. Impliedly overruled Vikas Sales also. Doubted Anraj; impliedly overruled Vikas Sales Actionable claims = Schedule III Para 6 exclusion in GST. Sunrise supports argument that composite instruments are actionable claims — outside sales tax and possibly GST.
Yasha Overseas v. CST [2008] 2008 taxmann.com 1747 (SC) REP licenses & DEPB (i) Sunrise did NOT overrule Vikas Sales re: REP licenses. (ii) DEPB is exactly like REP licenses — has value of its own — cannot be considered actionable claim. DEPB = goods = exigible to sales tax. Distinguished Sunrise from Vikas Sales DEPB = goods in Yasha. Relevant for any Government-issued tradeable certificate/scrip that has intrinsic value — may be 'goods' not 'actionable claim'.
Sodexho SVC India Ltd. v. State of Maharashtra 2015 SCC Online SC 1291 Prepaid meal coupons Prepaid meal coupons are 'not goods'. Consistent with Sunrise line Meal vouchers / food coupons = not goods. Relevant for gift voucher / prepaid card GST debate.
Idea Mobile Communications Ltd. v. CCE 2011 (23) STR 433 (SC) SIM cards SIM cards are 'services' not 'goods'. (Affirmed Escotel Mobile Communications v. UOI (2002) 126 STC 475 (Ker), considered BSNL v. UOI (2006) 6 SCC 1 and Imagic Creative (P) Ltd. v. CCT 2009 (2) SCC 614 re: 'right to use goods' in network per State of UP v. UOI (2003) 3 SCC 239) SIM card as service — multiple precedents SIM cards = services. Relevant where Department tries to classify software media / activation codes as goods for higher rate.
⚠️ YASHA PARA 42 — KEY TEST FOR LOYALTY POINTS / PPIs

Yasha put forward in para 42 a criteria for locating the distinction between:
(a) Loyalty points — not stored value — NOT Prepaid Payment Instruments (PPIs)
(b) PPIs — stored value under Payment & Settlement Systems Act, 2007

Test: If they are permitted free transferability to become marketable commodities — they would soon become market commodities and be sold/bought as 'goods'.
This must be read together with statutory restrictions to discount coupons which are not PPIs.
📝 In plain terms: Loyalty points (like airline miles, credit card reward points) cannot be freely transferred/sold. They are restricted. So they are NOT goods — they are not PPIs either. But if a prepaid instrument can be freely transferred and traded — it starts looking like a good. The free transferability test from Yasha para 42 is the dividing line.
💡 Credit card reward points — cannot be sold to a third party, non-transferable. = Not goods = Not PPIs = Outside GST (accumulated, not redeemed). BUT a prepaid gift card that can be freely gifted/sold = may be a PPI = different GST treatment applies under RBI PPI Master Directions.
YASHA — PARAS 41 & 42 (KEY ANALOGY)

Para 41: "We may observe here, if DEPB (or for that matter REP license) has to be compared with a lottery ticket, it can only be compared with a lottery ticket that has won the prize..."

Para 42: "If any more analogies are to be given one might compare DEPB with prepaid meal tickets or prepaid petrol coupons or accumulated flying miles. A meal ticket, a petrol coupon or flying miles credit has its own intrinsic value. If permitted free transferability those would soon become market commodities and would be sold and bought for their value as 'goods'."
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 13 | Securities Contracts Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Section 2(h) — Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 | HSN Entry Does Not Override Exclusion
Statutory Provision | CGST Act Schedule III Para 6 | Applicable in all securities-related GST disputes
Key Principle
Securities are excluded from the definition of 'goods' as well as 'services' in GST. Existence of a tariff entry such as HSN 997171 relating to 'holding equity control in a subsidiary company' does not dictate taxability in derogation of the express exclusion of 'securities' from the scope of GST by carving securities out of goods and services. Legislative wisdom cannot be made light of, nor can missing ingredients be 'furnished by imagination'. Existence of tariff entry only arouses suspicion about possible taxability and it is only the taxing ingredients clearly laid down in the statute that will bring the transaction within the scope of charging provision.
When to Use — When Department raises demand on any share/debenture/derivative transaction citing an HSN entry or tariff classification. Argue: the express exclusion of securities from goods and services cannot be overridden by the mere existence of a tariff entry. Legislative exclusion prevails over tariff classification.
Derivatives — Intention Not to Take Delivery | Form Prevails Over Substance
Legal Principle | Section 2(ac) SCRA | Applicable in commodity futures / options GST disputes
Key Principle
Just because financial commitments are made or accepted with reference to commodities (metals, spice, financial instruments, etc.) does not bring these transactions out of securities and into goods so as to become exposed to GST. The criterion of utmost relevance is 'intention not to take delivery' of those commodities which keeps the transaction squarely planted within the exclusion available to securities from any treatment in GST law. Speculative trade with no intent to take delivery of object of contract is guided by the 'form' transaction takes rather than its 'substance'.
When to Use — When Department argues that commodity derivatives (gold futures, silver futures, agricultural commodity options) are "goods transactions" subject to GST. Cite to establish: no delivery intent = derivative form governs = security = outside GST.
Test of Securities — Recognition in Law + Tradeability
Legal Principle | Section 2(d) SCRA | Options in Securities | Applicable in ESOP / Warrant GST disputes
Key Principle
Test of securities is not whether they are traded but that they are (i) recognized in law and (ii) tradeable even though among a small group of persons with interest. Options in securities are also 'securities' and treated exactly as are securities in GST. Warrants are not stock units but covenants-with-contingency(ies) that give rise to rights to claim stock units — and are securities for all purposes.
When to Use — When Department argues ESOP options are not "securities" because they are not traded on exchange, or warrants are not securities because they are not yet exercised. Cite to establish: recognition in law + transferability (even restricted) = security = outside GST.
Sunrise Associates v. NCT | [2006] 4 STT 105 (SC)
Supreme Court of India | 2006 | Lottery Tickets — Actionable Claims | Doubted Anraj; Impliedly overruled Vikas Sales
Key Ratio
Lottery tickets are indivisible composite rights and as such are 'actionable claims' not exigible to sales tax. The "two rights" theory propounded in H Anraj — dividing a lottery ticket into right to participate and right to claim prize — was rejected as incorrect. Rights in an instrument cannot be artificially bifurcated to bring them within the taxing net when the instrument as a whole represents an actionable claim.
When to Use — When Department tries to split a composite financial instrument into multiple "aspects" to find a taxable component. Cite Sunrise to argue: composite rights cannot be artificially bifurcated. The instrument must be examined as a whole — if it is an actionable claim, Schedule III Para 6 exclusion applies in GST.
Yasha Overseas v. CST | [2008] taxmann.com 1747 (SC) | Free Transferability Test
Supreme Court of India | 2008 | DEPB / REP Licenses / Loyalty Points Distinction
Key Ratio — Para 42
If any more analogies are to be given one might compare DEPB with prepaid meal tickets or prepaid petrol coupons or accumulated flying miles. A meal ticket, a petrol coupon or flying miles credit has its own intrinsic value. If permitted free transferability those would soon become market commodities and would be sold and bought for their value as 'goods'.
Application — Free Transferability Test
The distinction between loyalty points and prepaid payment instruments (PPIs): if permitted free transferability to become marketable commodities — this must be read together with statutory restrictions to discount coupons which are not PPIs. Non-transferable loyalty points = not goods = not PPIs = outside GST scope of supply.
When to Use — (i) In disputes on credit card reward points, airline miles, loyalty programmes — cite para 42 to argue non-transferable = not goods = outside GST. (ii) In DEPB/scrip trading disputes — cite Yasha to show scrips with intrinsic tradeable value = goods. (iii) In PPI disputes — use free transferability test to distinguish taxable vs non-taxable instruments.
GST Treatment Cannot Be Misapplied Bona Fide | Private Matters Legislation
Legal Principle | Para 13.1 | Securities law as private matters' legislation
Key Principle
GST treatment cannot be misapplied albeit bona fide. Securities' law is a private matters' legislation and assertions made before those regulators will pollinate tax treatment in GST. Securities' law is very specific and one that is regularly amended to include more species as these tradable securities pervade industry.
When to Use — When assessee claims they "genuinely believed" an instrument was not a security and applied GST incorrectly. Cite: bona fide belief is not a defence. Also use to leverage SEBI/RBI disclosures — if assessee told SEBI this is a derivative, Revenue cannot argue it's a goods supply for GST. Consistency principle.
Schedule III Para 1 — Employee Services | Share-Based Salary No GST
CGST Act 2017 | Schedule III Para 1 | Read with ICAI Guidance Note on Share-Based Payments
Key Principle
Services by an employee to an employer in the course of or in relation to his employment are not a supply under Schedule III Para 1 of CGST Act. When employees are allotted stock units (ESOP) as part of remuneration, there is no incidence of GST on the first leg of the transaction — the employee-employer transaction — which is excluded from tax. Requirement to deduct income-tax on such plan is deferred when the plan is 'recognized'. The share allotment is merely the mode of settlement of salary obligation — not a separate supply event.
When to Use — When Department raises demand on ESOP discount as a taxable supply (either as goods — shares — or as services — perquisite). Cite: employee-employer services under Schedule III Para 1 = not a supply = no GST. Share allotment = mode of salary payment = no separate supply.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 14 — Sovereign Commerce

⚖️ Chapter 14 — Sovereign Commerce

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Consideration
Prohibited / Not Consideration / GST N/A
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
14.1 Relevance to GST
14.2 Instrumentalities
14.2.1 Government
14.2.2 Statutory Body
14.3 Transactions
14.3.1 Stock-in-Trade
14.3.2 Consent and Permit
14.3.3 Direct Transfer of Benefits (DBT)
14.3.4 Statutory Compensation
14.4 Statutory Functions
14.4.1 No Competition from Private Enterprise
14.4.2 Fee is Condition
14.4.3 Yield or Proportionality
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
14.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🏛️ Sovereign Commerce — Relevance to GST
Article 298 | Constitution of India
CONSTITUTION
Article 298 — Executive power of Union and each State shall extend to carrying on of any trade or business, acquisition/holding/disposal of property and making of contracts for any purpose
📝 The Constitution permits the Government to do business just like any private company. So Government activities are NOT automatically free from GST — they must be examined transaction by transaction.
⚠️ CRITICAL WARNING
Do NOT assume that all activities by Government (Central or State) are free from GST. Where 'business' activities are undertaken, tax treatment WILL apply to Government or its instrumentalities (government agency or entity).
GST is NOT Payable ONLY When:
(a)
Object of contract (supply) is securities, money, alcohol for human consumption or 5 petro products
📝 These are outside GST scope entirely — not a Government exemption, but a product/supply exclusion applicable to everyone.
(b)
Transaction is listed in Schedule III
(neither supply of goods nor services)
📝 Schedule III lists activities that are completely outside GST — like sale of land, services of an employee to employer.
(c)
Supply is exempt under Section 11 of CGST Act
📝 Government may be notified as exempt supplier — but this must be a specific notification, not assumed.
EXEMPTION — ENTRY 3
Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) dated 28 Jun 2017 — Sovereign functions listed in Entry 3
Described as: "functions entrusted to" Panchayath under Article 243G OR Municipality under Article 243W
📝 The exemption is NOT limited only to Panchayath/Municipality activities. It is 'supplier agnostic' — any person can supply these specified services to a Government recipient and get the exemption.
KEY OBSERVATIONS on Art. 243G & 243W
(a)Topics listed in Schedules XI and XII — broad fields, NOT precise transactions
(b)Topics amenable to expansion or constriction based on nature of activity and its effect in advancing the listed topics
(c)Activities must fall in the 'pith' of the topic
(d)Incidental activities — even if essential or expedient — but NOT forming 'pith' of topic, will NOT be included
(e)Supply 'to' Government must be direct — NOT indirect via main-contractor or intermediate persons
(f)Activities forming 'pith' of the topic must be the principal object of contract for supply
📝 'Pith' means the essential core. If the activity is merely incidental or ancillary to the core sovereign function — it is taxable. Example: Data collection for database management is severable from sovereign activities and therefore NOT exempt.
💡 Entry 4 and 5 of same notification applies when Government itself is the Supplier of services. Goods supplied TO Government-Recipient: taxability based on nature of goods, NOT status of Recipient.
14.2 — Instrumentalities ⇧ Index
🏢 Instrumentalities — Who Qualifies as Government?
📝 Sovereign functions can be discharged through any instrumentality which would be an extended arm of the Government. BUT — not every entity set up by statute or Government is 'Government' for GST exemption purposes. A detailed examination is required.
14.2.1 — Government
Section 2(17)
"Business" includes any activity or transaction undertaken by Central Govt, State Govt, or any local authority in which they are engaged as public authorities
📝 Even when acting as 'public authorities', Government activities fall within 'business' for GST — requiring closer examination of exclusions and exemptions.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Sovereign functions can only be performed by the Sovereign — but NOT all functions by the Sovereign become sovereign functions
📝 The mere fact that Government is doing something does NOT make it sovereign. Exclusivity of the State in undertaking an activity must be traceable to a sovereign privilege — not a mere statutory embargo/monopoly.
⚠️ Activities in competition with private enterprise — even if prohibited for private enterprise — make the activity non-governmental in nature
DEFINITIONS — Notif. 11/2017-CT(Rate)
Governmental Authority [Expln.(ix)]
Authority/board/body set up by Act of Parliament or State Legislature, OR established by Govt with 90%+ participation by equity/control to carry out functions entrusted to Municipality (243W) or Panchayat (243G)
Government Entity [Expln.(x)]
Authority/board/body including society, trust, corporation — set up by Act of Parliament/State Legislature OR established by Govt with 90%+ participation by equity/control to carry out any function entrusted by Central Govt, State Govt, UT or local authority
📝 Key difference: Governmental Authority must perform 243G/243W functions. Government Entity performs any function entrusted by any Government — wider scope.
14.2.2 — Statutory Body
NOT AUTOMATIC
Formation by 'an Act of Parliament' alone does NOT make it Government without examining:
(i) Ownership
(ii) Management
(iii) Liquidation Estate
📝 Three-part test: Who owns it? Who manages it? Who bears the losses if it shuts down? Unless finances roll up into the Consolidated Fund — it is NOT Government.
TESTS MUST BE SATISFIED:
Entity's finances must roll up into Consolidated Fund OR obligations must operate as a charge on Consolidated Fund
💡 A PSU with independent finances and no Consolidated Fund charge is NOT Government — its transactions are fully taxable even if set up by Act of Parliament.
EXCLUSIVITY TEST
State's exclusivity to undertake activity must be traceable to a sovereign privilege — not merely a statutory embargo (prohibition on private competition)
📝 Statutory embargo = law banning private players. Sovereign privilege = inherent right that only a sovereign can exercise (like issuing currency, maintaining army). The former does NOT create a sovereign function; only the latter does.
14.3 — Transactions ⇧ Index
💼 Sovereign Transactions — Nature & Tax Treatment
14.3.1 — Stock-in-Trade ⇧ Index
🌍 Stock-in-Trade — Natural Resources Belonging to the People
PRINCIPLE
Natural resources that belong to the People form the stock-in-trade for Government to exploit and engage in 'trade or commerce' for the collective benefit
📝 The Government does not own natural resources for itself — it holds them in trust for the People. When it exploits them commercially, that is sovereign commerce — but it is still 'commerce'.
Where land belongs to the State — benefits to arise from land also belong to the State:
Standing timber in forests
Sand on banks of rivers
Oil in deep reserves
Spectrum for telecommunication
🔍 Nature of Sovereign Contract
Sovereign contract is NOT a contract between contractor and State.
It is a contract between contractor and the People — State only acts for the People in negotiating and performing.
📝 The State is merely an agent of the People. This means the 'object of contract' must be examined from People's perspective — not from Government's convenience.
TEST — OBJECT OF CONTRACT
Examine 'object of contract' when there is an enforceable obligation that the State accepts to come under. Terms indicating intention to come under a viniculum juris (bond of legal obligation) — sufficient to bring about a contract.
📝 Viniculum juris = legal bond. If the State commits to do something and can be held liable for breach — that is a contract, not a mere administrative permission.
Whether object of contract is Goods or Services — depends on:
(i) Object of contract is existing (even if unascertained) — but NOT a thing of the future yet to come into existence
📝 Goods must exist (or be certain to exist) at the time of contracting. If it is purely future and uncertain — it may be a service.
(ii) Yield is unknown but not ambiguous or unlikely — and commitment of consideration is certain and known
📝 Even if exact quantity is unknown (like fish to be caught, ore to be mined) — if consideration is defined and the yield is reasonably certain — it qualifies as a goods contract.
POST-MOTILAL PADAMPATH
State is no longer estopped from going back on its promise — this makes the promise sovereign and NOT contractual.
But determination of sovereign contracts remains exposed to the same tests: binding obligations, right to breach, remedies for breach.
📝 In Motilal Padampath, SC held Government cannot be stopped by promissory estoppel. So Government's promise ≠ always a contract. But even sovereign contracts must satisfy ordinary contract tests to determine if consideration exists.
14.3.2 — Consent and Permit ⇧ Index
📋 Consent and Permit — Fee v. Consideration
When is a Government Payment 'Consideration' for GST purposes?
✗ NOT CONSIDERATION
Payment is NOT consideration when:
Amount prescribed bears NO correlation to value proposition involved
Non-payment is fatal to lawful authority to undertake the activity
📝 If you can't proceed without paying — it's a regulatory fee, not a voluntary payment for a service received in return. No supply = no GST.
If not traceable to Article 298 — it is NOT consideration (it is a levy)
📝 Art. 298 is the source of Government's commercial power. Payments for regulatory/sovereign actions don't flow from Art. 298 and are therefore not consideration.
✓ IS CONSIDERATION
Payment IS consideration when it is traceable to a genuine bilateral arrangement
Reciprocity is the touchpoint — quid pro quo evidences consideration
📝 Quid pro quo = something for something. Genuine two-way exchange = consideration. One-way levy = NOT consideration.
HISTORY
Debate over 'fee v. consideration' substantially resolved — liquor license fee debate abandoned when Government withdrew claim that license fee was consideration (26th GST Council, 10 Mar 2018)
📝 This means: liquor license fees are NOT consideration for GST — they are regulatory fees. Government itself conceded this. Can be cited in hearings where Department tries to tax similar permit/license fees.
14.3.3 — Direct Transfer of Benefits (DBT) ⇧ Index
💰 Direct Transfer of Benefits (DBT) — GST Treatment of Subsidies
Purpose: Prevention of pilferage and unintentional denial of admissible claims
OLD METHOD
Subsidies disbursed to intermediaries who have met policy requirement and make claims with evidence of delivery of goods/services to intended beneficiaries
📝 Old method = money goes through middlemen → pilferage risk + delay in reaching real beneficiary.
DBT METHOD
Disbursement directly to beneficiary or on account of beneficiary to any Payee
📝 DBT = Direct Benefit Transfer. Money goes directly to the person who is supposed to get it — with OTP verification added by Government for identity confirmation.
NATURE OF SUBSIDY
Subsidy = contribution by Government of part or whole of consideration — to discharge the obligation of Beneficiary towards Supplier.

Government adds OTP verification of Beneficiary → then disburses subsidy to Supplier.
📝 The subsidy is part of the price the beneficiary owes the supplier — Government pays it directly. So the supplier still has full consideration (partly from buyer, partly from Government as subsidy). GST implications: subsidy forming part of consideration is taxable (to extent includible in value u/s 15 CGST).
💡 Example (549): Importer sells fertilizer → Distributors → Dealer → Agriculturist (intended Beneficiary of Government subsidy scheme). Importer sells at subsidized price. Beneficiary's OTP verification affirms identity of end-use. Government disburses subsidy to Importer under DBT scheme.
⚠️ CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Payee ≠ Beneficiary
Care must be taken to locate 'beneficiary' of subsidy scheme. Payee is NOT necessarily the Beneficiary.

If there is none — it becomes inevitable that subsidy is meant for Beneficiary and only disbursed to Payee.

Refer earlier deliberations on locus standi of Payee vis-à-vis Payer.
📝 Locus standi = right to be a party. The Payee (who receives money) may not be the true Beneficiary (who the scheme intends to help). This matters for GST — subsidy in hands of Payee/Supplier may be taxable even though Beneficiary ultimately benefits.
14.3.4 — Statutory Compensation ⇧ Index
⚖️ Statutory Compensation v. Statutory Contract
📝 Not all payments under a statute are compensation. Some statutory obligations create genuine contracts with reciprocal obligations — the test is whether offer-acceptance operates and whether a price is paid for the promise.
REFERENCE Circular 178/10/2022-GST dated 3 Aug 2022 (Para 7) — Coal Mines (Special Provisions) Act, 2015 — Remediation for prior allottees
STATUTORY COMPENSATION
Payment by Government under statute is NOT consideration but compensation
Circular 178 context: absence of prior agreement with allottees = sheet anchor that payment offered by Government under this statute is NOT consideration but compensation
📝 If there was no agreement before the statute forced the payment — it is remediation/compensation, not a freely negotiated price. Government is making good a loss, not buying a service.
Termination of contract ab initio due to defects in conditions precedents (qualification, selection) — creates unusual facts where Government role (leading allottees to imperil themselves) belies basic premise of contract law
STATUTORY CONTRACT
Contracts CAN reside in statute — terms residing in legislation or delegated legislation
Where offer-acceptance operates AND there is a price at which that promise is purchased → MUST be a contract
📝 Form of contract (whether in a statute or a formal agreement) is NOT as relevant as the fact that binding obligations are brought about. Substance over form applies.
KEY LEARNING
Contractual obligations can flow from statute. Terms residing in statutory provision do NOT make reciprocal obligation anything less than consideration — apply 'withdrawal-failure' test
WITHDRAWAL-FAILURE TEST
To examine the relationship: Does non-payment result in non-permission? Does cancellation result in non-refund of charge already paid?
If YES → indicators of consideration, NOT fee.
📝 This is the practical test to distinguish fee from consideration in any hearing. If Government can withdraw service on non-payment AND keeps the amount on cancellation — it behaves like a contract, making the payment 'consideration' liable to GST.
14.4 — Statutory Functions ⇧ Index
⚖️ Statutory Functions — When Government Activity Escapes GST
14.4.1 — No Competition from Private Enterprise ⇧ Index
🚫 Statutory Function — No Private Competition Possible
DEFINING FEATURE
Functions NOT open to competition from private enterprise — NOT because not lucrative but because undesirable by private enterprise but necessary for public welfare → State undertakes such functions
📝 Private players don't avoid these activities because there's no money — they avoid them because private enterprise would not voluntarily bear these burdens. Only the State, owing duty to People, takes them up. These are true sovereign functions.
⚠️ SEVERABLE DATA FUNCTIONS — NOT SOVEREIGN
Work done by State akin to data collection, sorting, and development of database for further use in State activities — if severable from those latter State activities — such data management functions will NOT be statutory functions
📝 If the data collection/management task can be contracted out to a private party without compromising the sovereign activity — it is not sovereign. Only what is inseparable from the core sovereign function qualifies for exemption.
💡 Example (550): Spectrum license fee is consideration.
💡 Example (551): Profit-petroleum is consideration.
14.4.2 — Fee is Condition ⇧ Index
📋 Fee is Condition — Not Consideration
PRINCIPLE
Consideration must be valuable to be valid — NOT merely an enquiry into adequacy, but a test of the nature of payment
FEE (Not Taxable)
Payment is disproportionate to work done
Organization's functioning is NOT dependent on payments collected
Organization is underwritten by State — finances not dependent on fees collected
📝 If the Government body would function even without collecting these fees (because State funds it) — the fee is merely a condition of permission, not consideration for a service.
Non-payment considered non-compliance (passes withdrawal-failure test) — still remains a condition, NOT consideration
💡 Example (552): MCA filing fee is NOT consideration.
💡 Example (553): Building plan sanction fee is NOT consideration.
CONSIDERATION (Taxable)
Proportionality of payment to service or gains derived — good guide that payment IS consideration
📝 If there is a reasonable ratio between what you pay and what you receive — it looks like a genuine commercial transaction. That makes it consideration liable to GST.
14.4.3 — Yield or Proportionality ⇧ Index
⚖️ Yield or Proportionality — Consideration Indicator Test
PRINCIPLE
Quid pro quo is evidence of existence of consideration. Reciprocity is the touchpoint of all contracts. Contract can flow from statute or tender + letter of award or simple work order.
📝 Quid pro quo = something for something. If there is reciprocity — there will be a contract. Form does not matter — whether terms are in a statute, a tender, or a simple work order.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE
Payment of prescribed charge does NOT procure any more or any less but the exact extent of statutory permission that requires payment of this charge. NOT every payment made to a statutory authority is 'consideration'.
📝 If paying ₹100 only gets you a license (permission) — and that license gives you nothing more — it is a fee for statutory permission, not consideration for a service. The size of the payment doesn't matter; what you get in return is what matters.
WITHDRAWAL-FAILURE TEST — TWO LIMBS
(i) Does non-payment result in non-permission?
(ii) Does cancellation result in non-refund of charge already paid?
📝 Apply both limbs. If (i) alone — likely a fee. If both (i) and (ii) — strong indicator of consideration. Non-refund on cancellation is the decisive factor — it shows genuine 'purchase' of a service/right.
Proportionality of Impost — Consideration Indicators:
✓ INDICATOR OF CONSIDERATION
Royalty paid in proportion to yield
💡 Example (554): ₹100 per ton — indicative of consideration.
💡 Example (555): Rent at 15% of sub-lease — is consideration.
✗ CONTRA-INDICATOR
Charge prescribed being non-linear to yield
💡 Example (556): 0.25% per ₹1,00,000 of share capital is a rateable fee — NOT consideration.
💡 Example (557): ₹100 per sft of proposed building construction is NOT consideration.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 14 | Sovereign Commerce ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision/authority.
Article 298 — Constitution of India | Government as a Business Entity
Constitutional Provision | Applicable across all GST disputes involving Government / PSU / Govt Entity
Key Principle
Article 298 permits the Union and each State to carry on trade or commerce. Therefore, it is important not to make any assumption that all activities by the Government (Central or State) are free from GST. Where 'business' activities are undertaken, tax treatment will apply to Government or any instrumentality of the Government, such as a Government agency or entity.
When to Use — When assessee argues blanket GST exemption merely because the other party is a Government entity. Cite to establish that Government acting commercially is NOT automatically exempt — each activity must be examined for sovereign character.
Entry 3, Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) — Sovereign Function Exemption | 'Pith' Test
GST Exemption Notification | Applicable in disputes about sovereign function exemption claims
Key Principle
Topics listed in Schedules XI and XII to Articles 243G and 243W are broad fields and not precise transactions, amenable to expansion or constriction based on the nature of activity under consideration. Activities must fall in the 'pith' of the topic. Incidental activities, even if essential or expedient, but not forming the 'pith' of the topic, will NOT be included within the scope of the given topic. Supply 'to' Government must be direct and not indirect via main-contractor or other intermediate persons. Activities forming the 'pith' of the topic must be the principal object of contract for supply.
When to Use — When Department or assessee claims exemption under Entry 3 of Notification 12/2017. Cite to argue/counter that the specific activity must fall in the pith of the sovereign function — incidental activities remain taxable. Also use when supply is routed through sub-contractors (supply not 'direct' to Government).
Sovereign Privilege v. Statutory Embargo — Sovereign Function Test
Legal Principle | Chapter 14 | Applicable in disputes involving PSU / Government body claiming sovereign status
Key Principle
Sovereign functions must be performed by the Sovereign. But functions by the Sovereign do not all become sovereign functions. Exclusivity by State to undertake an activity must be traceable to a sovereign privilege and not a statutory embargo. Activities undertaken in competition with private enterprise — even if prohibited but not impossible — make the activity non-Governmental.
When to Use — When a Government entity claims its activities are sovereign and therefore exempt. Argue: mere statutory exclusivity (monopoly by law) is NOT a sovereign privilege. The activity must inherently belong only to sovereign — like issuing currency, maintaining armed forces, exercising police power.
Fee v. Consideration — Withdrawal-Failure Test
Legal Principle | Chapter 14.4.2 & 14.4.3 | Applicable in disputes involving Government fees, levies, permits
Key Principle
Consideration must be valuable to be valid. This is not an enquiry into adequacy of consideration but a test of the nature of payment. If the payment is disproportionate to the work done, such that the organization's functioning is not dependent on the payments collected and is underwritten by the State, the payment is a 'fee' and not 'consideration'. Proportionality of payment (to the service or gains derived) is a good guide of this payment being consideration and not fee. The withdrawal-failure test helps locate if (i) non-payment results in non-permission or (ii) cancellation results in non-refund of charge already paid.
When to Use — (a) When Department taxes Government fees (MCA filing fees, building sanction fees, spectrum fees) as consideration. Counter: these are fees, not consideration — body is State-funded, fees are disproportionate to cost, and pass only (i) limb of withdrawal-failure test. (b) When assessee claims spectrum license fee or royalty is exempt. Counter: these are proportional to yield → consideration → taxable.
Quid Pro Quo — Proportionality as Consideration Indicator
Legal Principle | Section 14.4.3 | Applicable in royalty / mining / spectrum disputes
Key Principle
Quid pro quo is evidence of existence of consideration. Reciprocity is the touchpoint of all contracts. Royalty paid in proportion to yield is a good indicator of consideration but charge prescribed being non-linear to yield is a contra-indicator of consideration. Not every payment made to a statutory authority is 'consideration'. Payment of prescribed charge does not procure any more or any less but the exact extent of statutory permission that requires payment of this charge.
When to Use — In mining lease, royalty, spectrum license disputes. If royalty is per ton of extraction (linear to yield) → consideration → taxable. If fee is flat per sft of building or per ₹1 lakh of capital (non-linear to yield) → NOT consideration → not taxable as GST supply.
DBT — Payee ≠ Beneficiary | Subsidy as Part of Consideration
Legal Principle | Section 14.3.3 | Applicable in subsidy / DBT / fertilizer / welfare scheme disputes
Key Principle
Nature of subsidy being contribution of, a part or whole of the consideration by the Government to discharge the obligation of Beneficiary towards Supplier, is an efficient place for use of DBT. Care must be taken to locate 'beneficiary' of the subsidy scheme. Payee is not necessarily the Beneficiary. If there is none the it becomes inevitable that the subsidy is meant for Beneficiary and only disbursed to Payee.
When to Use — In disputes about GST on subsidy received by manufacturers/importers under government welfare schemes. Argue: subsidy discharged Government's obligation to beneficiary — it forms part of consideration u/s 15 CGST Act — therefore taxable in hands of Supplier (Payee). Or conversely, if subsidy truly belongs to Beneficiary and Payee is merely a conduit — examine locus standi carefully before taxing.
Statutory Compensation v. Statutory Contract — 26th GST Council Concession
Legal Principle | Circular 178/10/2022-GST | 26th GST Council (10 Mar 2018) | Chapter 14.3.4 & 14.3.2
Key Principle
Contractual obligations can flow from statute. Merely because its terms reside in a statutory provision does not make the reciprocal obligation anything less than consideration — by applying the 'withdrawal-failure' test to examine the relationship. Absence of a prior agreement is the sheet anchor of the interpretation that payment offered by Government under the statute is not consideration but compensation.
