The debate over 'fee v. consideration' in liquor license context was abandoned when Government withdrew its claim that license fee was consideration in hurried conclusions in 26th GST Council deliberations on 10 Mar 2018.
When to Use — In disputes about coal block allotment compensation (Circular 178). Also cite the 26th GST Council concession in any hearing where Department tries to treat regulatory license fees as consideration. Government's own concession that liquor license fee is NOT consideration sets a strong precedent for all similar regulatory fees.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 15 — Minerals Laws

⛏️ Chapter 15 — Minerals Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Correct
Prohibited / Void / Wrong
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
15.1 Relevance to GST
15.2.1 Compulsory Exaction
15.2.2 Regulatory Background & Popular Misconceptions
15.2.3 Licenses
15.2.4 Unauthorized Sub-lease
15.2.5 Welfare Contribution (DMF/NMET)
15.3.1 Statutory Demand — Royalty as Tax?
15.3.2 Recoupment of Incidence
15.3.3 Development of Authorities
15.3.4 Present Controversy & GST Rate
15.3.5 Royalty as Tax or Not — Comparison
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
15.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
⛏️ Minerals Laws — Relevance to GST
MMDR Act 1947 | Mines Act 1952 | Royalty | Licenses
Minerals are vast — not just deep-earth iron ore or copper ore.
Sand from riverbed = mineral. Oil reserves = mineral. Gravel = mineral (minor).
📝 Even taking sand from a river is "mining" and that sand is a "mineral." This chapter covers GST implications across ALL such extractions — not just big industrial mines.
OWNERSHIP
Land owner owns mineral reserves ON or UNDER that land
UNLESS mineral rights are reserved in the grant
WHEN STATE GRANTS LAND
Rights to mineral deposits are reserved by State
MMDR Act authorizes State to lay claim & permit excavation for a 'price'
📝 State owns the minerals under land it has granted. It charges a 'price' (royalty) for allowing extraction. This 'price' is the central GST controversy.
CERTAINTY TEST
Marshall v. Green ibid
Fruit-tree = warehouse of fruits.
Certainty of yield = essential test
Fruits in present
= Sale of Goods
Fruits in future
= Intangible immovable property (benefit to arise from land)
📝 Mineral deposits exist — but their exact yield is uncertain. Because yield is uncertain, a mining contract is NOT a simple sale of present goods. It is a contract for benefits to arise from land.
CONSENSUS AD IDEM
For a valid contract:
Kind & quality of mineral must be known
AND yield must be precise & free of contingency
📝 Both parties must agree on exactly what mineral, what quality, and how much will be extracted. If yield is uncertain, there is no meeting of minds on "how much" — making this a future-goods / service contract.
GST CORE PURPOSE
Understanding compulsory exactions (royalty, DMF, NMET) is necessary to determine:
(i) Tax treatment of those payments, AND
(ii) Whether the contract is for supply of goods (ascertained mineral) or supply of services (benefit to arise from land)
MMDR Act = private matters' legislation → strictly interpreted on form AND substance of contract
15.2.1 — Compulsory Exaction (Royalty) ⇧ Index
💰 Compulsory Exaction — Royalty & Related Payments
Constitutional Entries | MMDR Act 1947 | Article 246A
CONSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK
Entry 23 (List II) / Entry 55 (List I) — Schedule VII of Constitution
Once Parliament enacts legislation → States are denuded of their taxing power
MMDR Act 1947 = Parliament's deliberate legislative step for development & tax on minerals
📝 When Parliament passed the MMDR Act, States lost the power to independently tax minerals. However, GST power comes from Article 246A — so GST and MMDR Act operate simultaneously without one overriding the other.
Three Factors that make Royalty = Consideration for Supply
✓ 1
Offer-Acceptance
Statutory contract with terms fixed by law
✓ 2
Valuable-Proportionate
Rate based on quantity and quality of mineral extracted
✓ 3
Reciprocal-Enforceable
Non-payment → breach → contract terminable
(Withdrawal-failure test)
Royalty = Consideration for which Supply?
OPTION A
Supply of Goods
Mineral deposits ascertained & pre-specified quantum limited to excavation
(present goods — certainty of yield)
📝 If both parties know exactly how much will be extracted — it is a sale of present goods and royalty is the price of goods.
OPTION B
Supply of Services
Benefits to arise from land of unascertained quantum of deposits — limited only by duration/term of contract
(future goods)
📝 If yield is uncertain and the contract runs for a fixed term (say 20 years), it is a supply of services — the right to extract — and royalty is consideration for that service.
💡 Eg. 558: GST is 'levied' u/s 9 CGST Act when 'taxable event' (supply) occurs.
Eg. 559: Royalty is NOT 'levied' under MMDR Act, 1947 — it is consideration, not a tax levy.
⚠️ WITHDRAWAL-FAILURE TEST
If Licensee does not pay royalty → Licensee is in breach → Contract is due for termination
This seals the conclusion: royalty is consideration — because non-payment of consideration = breach of contract, not evasion of tax.
📝 If royalty were a tax, non-payment would attract tax penalties — not contract termination. The fact that non-payment triggers contract termination proves it is contractual consideration, not a tax.
15.2.2 — Regulatory Background & Popular Misconceptions ⇧ Index
📚 Regulatory Background — Popular vs Correct Understanding
Mines Act 1952 | MMDR Act 1947 | Statutory Definitions
Two Statutes — Different Purposes
MINES ACT, 1952
Labour & safety regulations in mines
MMDR ACT, 1947
Development — exploitation & conservation — and regulation of mines & minerals
📝 Gravel, sand, clay, nuclear material, natural gas, petroleum — all are 'minerals' under MMDR Act. Not just iron ore or copper.
⚠️ IMPORTANT Any GST deliberation requires fresh perspective — away from common sense preconceptions — to appreciate the workings of this law.
❌ Popular & Erroneous Understanding ✅ Correct & Statutory Meaning
Mineral does not include gravel, stone, sand and clay Mineral includes all of them as minor minerals
Mining means deep excavation Taking (winning) mineral deposits in the Earth is mining — even river sand collection
License means use of the land where minerals are deposited License is profit-à-prendre — that is, to extract and 'take away'
License to prospect means permission to extract License to prospect is only to explore, locate and prove (existent, extent and quality) of mineral reserves — NOT extraction
Mining lease is rent for the land containing mineral deposits Mining lease is the price of minerals that are taken away and sold
Mineral reserves belong to the Operator of the mine Mineral reserves belong to the Nation
Mine operator who extracts the minerals automatically has the right to sell in the market State has the right to mineral deposits — Mine operator must purchase from State and then sell in the market
Royalty (Section 9) is statutory fee paid to State by Mine operator Royalty is the 'price' for purchase of minerals from the State
Dead rent (Section 9A) is rent for the land and not for mineral deposits All payments are for mineral deposits — Dead rent is a minimum amount to be paid even when no extract is done
District Mineral Foundation contribution (Section 9B) is a donation paid to the DMF Foundation All payments are for mineral deposits — a specific amount to be paid towards objects of the Trust
National Mineral Exploration Trust charges (Section 9C) is another donation paid to the Trust All payments are for mineral deposits — a specific amount to be paid towards objects of the Trust
Each payment is for a different 'object' of contract Every payment is essentially for the minerals — which is the only object of every contract here
⚠️ GST LITIGATION ALERT  Department often treats royalty, dead rent, DMF, NMET as separate obligations for separate supplies. The correct position: ALL are payments for minerals — the one and only object of the contract. Separate treatment = erroneous tax position.
15.2.3 — Licenses (Prospecting, Mining, Transferability) ⇧ Index
📋 Licenses — Nature, Stages, and Legal Character
Two Stages Before Revenue Arises from Mining
STAGE 1
Prospecting License
Locate existence of viable mineral deposits
Explore → locate → prove (existence, extent, quality)
📝 Prospecting = only looking. You are NOT allowed to extract. All expenses here are 'sunk costs' — calculated risks. Capitalization only if viability supports it (AS26 / IndAS38-106).
STAGE 2
Mining License
After proving viable reserves → License for actual extraction granted
📝 Only after reserves are proven does extraction begin. Revenue arises only after successful extraction and sale of minerals.
Nature of Mining License — Key Legal Character
TRANSFERABLE
Mining license itself is transferable
NOT TRANSFERABLE PROPERTY
But it is not transferable property
📝 You can transfer the license — but you are not transferring 'property.' It is a right (jus ad rem) — a right to a right — not an outright property title.
JUS AD REM
Only a 'right to a right' — not a vested right in itself
📝 Jus ad rem = right to acquire property, not ownership itself. Vested right arises only AFTER extraction and settlement of all encumbrances (royalty + dues).
STATE DISCRETION
State has authority to decline permission — making license only an authority, not a vested right
DURATION & CHARGES
Duration of license can be increased or decreased — does not disturb any vested right (since there is none).
Royalty and DMF/NMET charges are variable and linked to quantity extracted, whether sold or lost in-transit, and whether market rates are favourable or not.
📝 Collection of transfer charges does not imply mining license is a vested right. The license is an authority to extract — not ownership. Once collection of transfer charges is permitted, Transferee acts on their own and Transferor is divested of locus.
💡 Long-term lease of land with right to 'take away' unascertained quantity of natural resources → supply is NOT 'lease' of present goods. Lease being the 'documentation style' for supply of present AND future goods → supply of services under GST.
15.2.4 — Unauthorized Sub-lease ⇧ Index
🚫 Unauthorized Sub-lease — GST Consequences
THE PROBLEM
Licensees may not operate the mine but enter into illegal and unenforceable sub-lease with sub-Licensee
— without consent of regulators
📝 Some mine license holders don't mine themselves — they hand over operations to a sub-licensee without State permission. This sub-lease is illegal and creates complex GST issues.
LEGAL CONSEQUENCE
All transactions by sub-Licensee are on behalf of and as instrumentality and servant of the Licensee
📝 The sub-licensee is treated in law as merely the agent/servant of the original licensee. Every act (extraction, sale) is attributed to the original licensee.
PAYMENTS FLOW
Payments made towards royalty or DMF/NMET charges → will be 'on account of Licensee' and not on account of sub-Licensee
AFTER VALID TRANSFER
Once permission to transfer is granted → Transferee acts on own account → Transferor is divested of any locus under the transferred license
⚠️ GST LITIGATION ALERT
Sub-licensee cannot claim independent GST registration or input tax credit on purchases for mining operations — as all transactions are legally those of the original Licensee. Wrong GST position adopted by sub-licensee = demand on Licensee.
15.2.5 — Welfare Contribution (DMF / NMET) ⇧ Index
🌿 Welfare Contribution — Sections 9B & 9C MMDR Act
District Mineral Foundation | National Mineral Exploration Trust
SECTION 9B — DMF
District Mineral Foundation
Obligation to contribute towards environmental restoration, afforestation and rehabilitation
SECTION 9C — NMET
National Mineral Exploration Trust
Contribution towards mineral exploration objectives of the Trust
Legal Character of DMF / NMET Contributions
INSEPARABLE FROM ROYALTY
DMF/NMET is inseparable from and co-extensive with royalty
📝 DMF and NMET are not standalone donations. They move with royalty — if royalty is consideration for minerals, so are DMF and NMET. They cannot be treated differently.
NATURE = SURCHARGE
Operates as a surcharge because authority to collect and power to apply proceeds are vested with the Government and its instrumentalities
📝 A surcharge = additional charge on same base. Since Government controls both collection and application, this is a surcharge on royalty — not an independent tax or donation.
⚠️ GST IMPLICATION
If royalty = consideration for supply of minerals → DMF/NMET (being inseparable surcharges on royalty) = also part of consideration for the same supply.
Department cannot treat DMF/NMET as independent taxable services to attract separate GST — they form part of the same supply value.
15.3.1 — Statutory Demand of Dues: Is Royalty a Tax? ⇧ Index
⚖️ Royalty — Tax or Consideration? The Core Controversy
India Cements → Kesoram Industries → MADA v. SAIL → 9-Judge Bench
📝 This is the most important controversy in mineral taxation. If royalty is a tax, then GST cannot be levied on top of it (tax on tax). If royalty is consideration, GST applies. The Supreme Court has been wrestling with this for decades.
ORIGIN OF CONTROVERSY
India Cements Ltd. v. State of TN — AIR 1990 SC 85
Para 34 (7-Judge Bench): "...royalty is a tax, and as such a cess on royalty being a tax on royalty, is beyond the competence of the State Legislature because section 9 of the Central Act covers the field..."
📝 The 7-judge bench in India Cements appeared to say royalty = tax. But this was later found to be possibly a typographical/inadvertent error in the judgment.
CORRECTION ATTEMPT
State of WB v. Kesoram Industries Ltd. — AIR 2005 SC 1646 (5-Judge Bench)
Held: There was a typographical error in para 34 of India Cements.
"We hold that royalty is NOT tax. Royalty is paid to the owner of land who may be a private person... A private person owning the land is entitled to charge royalty but not tax."
📝 Kesoram said India Cements para 34 was a typo — the sentence should read 'royalty is NOT a tax.' A private person can charge royalty (as income from land) but cannot levy taxes. Only State can levy taxes.
CONFLICTING BENCHES
Mineral Area Development Authority v. Steel Authority of India Ltd. (MADA v. SAIL) — (2011) 4 SCC 450
Conflict between 5-judge bench (Kesoram) and 7-judge bench (India Cements) → Matter referred to 9-judge bench for final resolution
📝 Since a 5-judge bench (Kesoram) cannot overrule a 7-judge bench (India Cements), the Supreme Court referred the question to a 9-judge bench. This is the largest possible bench — the final word.
REFERENCE QUESTION
"Whether the majority decision in State of West Bengal v. Kesoram Industries Ltd. (2004) 10 SCC 201, could be read as departing from the law laid down in the seven Judge Bench decision in India Cement Ltd. v. State of Tamil Nadu (1990) 1 SCC 12?"
PRESENT STATUS
Matter is sub judice before 9-judge bench (MADA v. SAIL).
Government has NOT waited — notified GST rate on royalty (Circular 164/20/2021-GST dated 6 Oct 2021) → GST payable on royalty on Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM) as services supplied by Government.
💡 GST Rate on Royalty under RCM — Three stages:
HSN 997337 Entry 17(viii) @ 5% → 1 Jul 2017 to 31 Dec 2018
HSN 9991 Entry 29 @ 18% → 1 Jan 2019 onwards
HSN 997337 Entry 17(viii) @ 18% → 1 Jul 2017 to 31 Dec 2018 (revised)
Recipient (Mine operator) pays GST under RCM on royalty paid to Government.
15.3.2 — Recoupment of Incidence ⇧ Index
🔄 Recoupment of Incidence — Cost-Plus Contracts & GST
Right to Recoup Tax from Customer — Statutory Right or Contractual Remedy?
NOT A STATUTORY RIGHT
Right to recoup tax discharged by Seller is NOT a statutory right (as held in Chotabhai Jethabhai Patel & Sons)
MERELY CONTRACTUAL
It is merely a contractual remedy — arising only if the contract provides for it
COST-PLUS CONTRACT ANALYSIS
Where consideration is determined on costs incurred (Open-book or GMP basis) → Costs incurred by Supplier are transparent to Customer
→ Does NOT shift obligation to discharge those costs to Customer
→ Costs remain obligations of Supplier
📝 Just because the customer can see your costs does not mean the customer is now obligated to pay them directly. The supplier still owes those costs — the contract merely determines consideration based on them. Rule 33 CGST Rules (pure agent test) makes clear who bears the obligation.
CONSIDERATION IN SUCH CONTRACT
= Gross value of all costs + agreed mark-up
Without relying on any rule of valuation
= Plain meaning of consideration being the price at which the promise is purchased
Tested on anvil of 'withdrawal-failure' consequences
📌 Practical Examples from the Chapter
Eg.560
Salary of employees forming bases for cost-plus contract does NOT move Employer-Employee relationship to Customer merely because Customer bears the cost of salaries paid
Eg.561
Factory rent forming bases for cost-plus contract for job-working does NOT make Customer liable to pay lease rent to Owner of factory building contrary to terms of lease agreement
Eg.562
Corporate Debtor is NOT liable to discharge GST-RCM on legal charges paid by Insolvency Professional as part of CIRP process — even though costs are first met out of liquidation estate — since no privity is created with Advocate and Corporate Debtor
15.3.3 — Development of Authorities (Timber / Bamboo Cases) ⇧ Index
🌳 Development of Authorities — Present Goods vs Future Goods
Orient Paper Mills | Titaghur Paper Mills | Marshall v. Green
📝 These cases establish the critical distinction between: (a) sale of present goods (tree already standing = goods ready for harvest) and (b) supply of services / future goods (right to extract over many years = benefit from land). This distinction directly controls GST rate and treatment.
MARSHALL v. GREEN — FOUNDATION PRINCIPLE
A tree is a warehouse of fruit on it.
Contract for sale of fruit on a tree = sale simplicitor of goods (fruit) — in addition to expending labour to harvest.
📝 If the object of contract is existing, identifiable, harvestable goods (fruit on the tree, standing timber) — it is sale of present goods. Recoupment of royalty in addition to price of mineral = yet another reason to assert royalty is a tax (but this is a wrong reason — Kesoram rejected this).
Key Distinction — Orient Paper vs Titaghur Paper
ORIENT PAPER MILLS
AIR 1977 SC 687
State of MP — bamboo & salai wood — 'lease deed' style document
Duration: 20 years
SC held: Levy of sales tax valid & proper
The deed in truth & substance confers right to cut & carry timber
"Till the trees are cut, they remain property of the owner. Once trees are severed, property passes."
📝 'Royalty' used in the deed was a "feudalistic euphemism for the 'price' of the timber." But the question was whether the deed results in sale of trees after they are cut.
TITAGHUR PAPER MILLS
AIR 1985 SC 1293
State of Orissa — bamboo & standing trees — 'bamboo contract' & 'timber contract'
Duration: 13 years
SC overruled Orient Paper
Key distinction: Duration extended to future goods — object of contract not limited to present goods
"Royalty was consideration for all the rights conferred under the contract though calculated according to quantity of bamboos felled"
📝 Because the contract ran for 13/20 years covering future growth — it was not sale of present goods. It was a right to take future goods = benefit from land = service.
✓ CRITICAL GST PRINCIPLE FROM TITAGHUR
Present goods (ascertained, certain yield) → Sale of goods → Taxed as sale rate
Future goods (unascertained, uncertain yield, long-term contract) → Supply of Services → Taxed as service rate

"A chameleon may change its surroundings but document is not a chameleon to change its meaning according to the purpose of the statute with reference to which it falls to be interpreted."
📝 In plain terms: If both parties know what mineral and how much — it is present goods (sale). If yield is uncertain and extraction happens over years — it is future goods / services. The label on the document (lease deed, royalty, supply agreement) does NOT change the legal character.
💡 Sand mining contract for 3 years — specific quantities to be extracted per month = present goods (ascertainable) = sale of goods.
vs.
Iron ore mining lease for 20 years — extraction as per reserves found = future goods (unascertainable) = service = GST on RCM at 18%.
15.3.4 — Present Controversy & GST Rate on Royalty ⇧ Index
🔥 Present Controversy — MADA v. SAIL Sub Judice & Government's GST Notification
IF ROYALTY = TAX
→ GST CANNOT be levied on royalty
→ Levy of GST on royalty is unconstitutional
→ Entire RCM demand on royalty would fall
IF ROYALTY = CONSIDERATION
→ GST IS applicable on royalty
→ RCM on mine operator is valid
→ Government's circular is correct
GOVERNMENT'S CURRENT POSITION
Notwithstanding MADA v. SAIL being sub judice:
• Notified GST rate on royalty
• Circular 164/20/2021-GST dated 6 Oct 2021 — royalty payable on RCM as services supplied by Government
• Mine operator pays GST on royalty paid to State Government under RCM
HSN Entry Rate Remarks
997337 17(viii) 5% 1 Jul 2017 to 31 Dec 2018
9991 29 18% 1 Jan 2019 onwards
997337 17(viii) 18% 1 Jul 2017 to 31 Dec 2018 (revised)
⚠️ LITIGATION PENDING
Final answer on royalty = tax or not is awaited from 9-judge bench.
Until then: GST on royalty under RCM at 18% is Government's position — and mine operators are paying it.
If 9-judge bench holds royalty = tax → refund of entire GST paid on royalty will be a massive litigation issue (limitation, unjust enrichment).
💡 A mine operator paying ₹100 crore royalty annually to State = paying ₹18 crore GST under RCM on top of it. If Supreme Court eventually holds royalty = tax, the mine operator's refund claim of ₹18 crore per year will face limitation and unjust enrichment bars.
15.3.5 — Royalty as a Tax or Not — Full Comparison ⇧ Index
⚖️ Royalty — Is it a Tax or Consideration? Both Sides Compared
📝 Both sides have arguments that are not wholly unfounded. The book presents both fairly and then concludes: royalty is consideration (not a tax) because State is custodian of natural resources and manner of contracting by statute does not change the contractual nature of the relationship.
❌ Arguments that Royalty is a TAX ✅ Arguments that Royalty is CONSIDERATION
It is imposed by statute It is a statutory contract — statute sets the terms, but it is still a contract
It is collected on quantum of extraction It is collected on quantum of extraction — this is a rule of proportion (quid pro quo for minerals taken)
Tariff varies based on quality or grade of extraction Tariff varies based on quality or grade — this is quid pro quo (price varies with what you get)
It requires filing of periodic returns Periodic returns = objective measure of quantity supplied (like billing cycles in a supply contract)
It attracts penalties in case of deviation or compliance failure Enforceability does not alter terms of statutory contract — all contracts have penalty clauses
Dues may be recovered as arrears of land revenue All State dues are debt liable to similar recovery action — this is a recovery mechanism, not proof of tax nature
Disputes follow due process of adjudication with appellate remedy Equitable remedies for grievance redressal to avoid arbitrariness in State action — exists in all contract disputes with State too
✓ AUTHOR'S CONCLUSION
Royalty is NOT a tax — it is consideration for a statutory contract.
State is custodian of natural resources. Manner of contracting (via statute + periodic returns + recovery as arrears) does NOT alter the contractual nature of relations.
State is empowered to engage in trade under Article 298. Statutory contract with periodic returns and transparency / legal remedies do not alter the bond of legal relations (vinculum juris) created between Licensee and State over natural resources as the object of their contract.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 15 | Minerals Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
India Cements Ltd. v. State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1990 SC 85 | 7-Judge Bench
Key Ratio (Para 34 — the controversial paragraph)
"...royalty is a tax, and as such a cess on royalty being a tax on royalty, is beyond the competence of the State Legislature because section 9 of the Central Act covers the field and the State Legislature is denuded of its competence under entry 23 of list II. In any event, we are of the opinion that cess on royalty cannot be sustained under entry 49 of list II as being a tax on land. Royalty on mineral rights is not a tax on land but a payment for the user of land."
When to Use — If arguing royalty = tax (to challenge GST on royalty under RCM). Cite this 7-judge bench para. Note: Kesoram called this a typographical error — so use with awareness of the ongoing 9-judge bench reference in MADA v. SAIL.
State of West Bengal v. Kesoram Industries Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2005 SC 1646 | 5-Judge Bench
Key Ratio — Royalty is NOT Tax
"We hold that royalty is not tax. Royalty is paid to the owner of land who may be a private person and may not necessarily be State. A private person owning the land is entitled to charge royalty but not tax. The lessor receives royalty as his income and for the lessee the royalty paid is an expenditure incurred. Royalty cannot be tax. We declare that even in India Cement it was not the finding of the Court that royalty is a tax."
When to Use — Primary ratio to challenge any Department's argument that GST on royalty is invalid because royalty is itself a tax. Argue: Kesoram (5-judge bench) held royalty = NOT tax = GST applicable on royalty as consideration for supply of services by Government.
State of MP v. Orient Paper Mills Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1977 SC 687
Key Ratio — Substance over Nomenclature
"We are satisfied that despite its description, the deed confers in truth and substance a right to cut and carry timber of specified species. Till the trees are cut, they remain the property of the owner, namely the appellant. Once the trees are severed, the property passes. The 'Royalty' is a feudalistic euphemism for the 'price' of the timber."
When to Use — When Department or assessee argues the contract document's label (lease deed, royalty agreement, supply agreement) determines GST treatment. Counter: Substance of the contract determines the nature of supply — not its title or description. 'Royalty' was merely a feudalistic word for 'price.'
State of Orissa v. Titaghur Paper Mills Co. Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1293
Key Ratio — Present Goods vs Future Goods | The GST Rate Determinant
"Thus, in coming to the conclusion that the term 'royalty' used in the document before it was merely 'a feudalistic euphemism for the price of the timber', the Court overlooked the fact that the amount of royalty payable by the respondent was consideration for all the rights conferred upon the respondent under the contract though it was to be calculated according to the quantity of the bamboos felled, and the Court also overlooked the fact that this was made further clear by the provision for payment of a minimum royalty."

"A chameleon may change its surroundings but document is not a chameleon to change its meaning according to the purpose of the statute with reference to which it falls to be interpreted and if documents having the same tenor are not to be construed by courts in the same way, it would make for great uncertainty and would introduce confusion, leaving people bewildered as to how they should manage their affairs so as to make their transactions valid and legal in eye of the law."
When to Use — In disputes where Department treats a long-term mining arrangement as 'sale of goods' to charge goods rate. Argue: if yield is unascertained and contract extends to future goods (like bamboo in Titaghur for 13-20 years) → it is supply of services → 18% GST under RCM. Document's label is irrelevant — substance is what matters.
Profit-à-prendre / License Nature Doctrine — MMDR Act, 1947
Statutory Principle | MMDR Act 1947 | Affirmed across multiple SC decisions
Key Principle
Mining license is profit-à-prendre — that is, a right to extract and 'take away.' It is jus ad rem (a right to a right) — not transferable property and not a vested right in itself. Vested right arises only after extraction and settlement of all encumbrances (royalty and other dues). Collection of transfer charges does not imply mining license to be a vest right in itself. All payments — royalty, dead rent, DMF, NMET — are for mineral deposits which is the only object of every contract here.
When to Use — When assessee argues mining license is 'sale of immovable property right' (to claim Schedule III exclusion) or when Department treats DMF/NMET as independent taxable services. Counter: license = profit-à-prendre = right to extract, not property. All payments = one object = minerals.
Vinculum Juris Doctrine — Royalty as Statutory Contract
Legal Principle | MMDR Act 1947 | Para 15.3.5 of the book
Key Principle
Royalty is not a tax since State is custodian of natural resources and manner of contracting does not alter the contractual nature of relations brought about by statute. Since State is empowered to engage in trade under Article 298, statutory contract with periodic returns and transparency legal remedies do not alter the bond of legal relations or vinculum juris that is created between Licensee and State over the natural resources as the object of their contract. Royalty is consideration for a statutory contract — not a tax levied under taxing power.
When to Use — In RCM disputes on royalty where mine operator challenges GST liability. Use to rebut argument that royalty = tax = no GST. Argue: vinculum juris of contract exists between Licensee and State → royalty = consideration → GST under RCM is valid. Also useful to rebut unjust enrichment claims when seeking refund of wrongly paid GST if royalty is ultimately held to be a tax by the 9-judge bench.
Recoupment Principle — Chotabhai Jethabhai Patel & Sons
Legal Principle | Para 15.3.2 | Cost-Plus Contract GST Analysis
Key Principle
The right to recoup tax from the Customer is not a statutory right but merely a contractual remedy. In a cost-plus contract where consideration is determined based on costs incurred, costs incurred by Supplier do not shift the burden of those costs (and obligation to discharge those costs) to Customer — they remain obligations of Supplier. Consideration in such a contract would be the gross value of all costs plus the agreed mark-up, without relying on any rule of valuation to reach this result — which flows from a plain meaning of consideration being the price at which the promise is purchased.
When to Use — When mine operator or subcontractor in cost-plus mining contracts claims ITC or disputes GST on cost components (employee salaries, factory rent, legal charges in CIRP) included in billing. Counter: cost-pass-through does not create new taxable supply or new principal-agent relationship. Eg. 560, 561, 562 support this.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 16 — Legal Metrology Act

⚖️ Chapter 16 — Legal Metrology Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
16.1 Relevance to GST
16.2.1 Weight or Measure
16.2.2 Caveat Emptor
16.2.3 Caveat Venditor
16.2.4 Packed Commodities
16.2.5 Pre-package
16.2.6 Non-Standard Packing
16.2.7 Free Units
16.2.8 Buy-One-Take-Two
16.2.9 Combo Package
16.2.10 Label Alteration
16.3.1 FSSAI
16.3.2 Food Business Operator
16.3.3 Label of Claims
16.3.4 Licensing of FBO
16.3.5 Goods or Services
16.4.1 Explosives — Statutory Terms
16.4.2 Stock Loss
16.4.3 Stock Gains
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
16.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
⚖️ Legal Metrology Act, 2009 — Relevance to GST
Welfare Legislation | Facts → Illuminates GST Treatment
📝 Legal Metrology Act regulates weights, measures, and labelling of packed goods. What a seller declares on a label or to metrology authorities is a "positive assertion of fact" that cannot be resiled from in GST proceedings. Facts from this law illuminate GST treatment — but legal consequences of this law are NOT transplanted into GST.
WHY RELEVANT
Goods in packed condition — Buyers cannot validate terms of offer since goods are not amenable to examination without loss of merchantability
📝 When goods are sealed/packed, you cannot open and verify quantity/quality without destroying the package. So the seller must disclose everything on the label — consumer cannot rely on pre-purchase tests.
VALUE TO GST
Assertions made to regulators under LMA 2009 = invaluable source of facts that illuminate understanding about facts needed to apply treatment in GST
📝 What you declare on the label (MRP, weight, quantity) and to metrology inspectors is a sworn statement of fact. GST authorities will use these very facts to apply the correct tax treatment.
💡 A seller declares goods as 'pre-packaged' on a MRP label. In GST, pre-packaged attracts specific rate. Seller cannot then say to GST officer that goods are 'loose/unpackaged' to claim lower rate.
⚠️ CRITICAL DISTINCTION
LMA is a welfare legislation → purposive construction
GST is a private matters' legislation → strict interpretation only
Facts: transplantable ✓
Legal consequences of LMA: NOT transplantable ✗
📝 LMA protects consumers. GST is a revenue law. You can use facts from LMA in GST — but you cannot say "since LMA exempts X, GST also exempts X." The legal consequences are separate.
NON-RESILING RULE
After making assertions before one regulator, taxpayer cannot resile from it before GST authorities
📝 You cannot say one thing to the Metrology Inspector and the opposite thing to the GST officer. Consistency is mandatory — your label declarations bind you in GST.
GST LEGAL FICTION — LIMIT APPLICATION
Services become goods in trade and goods are treated as services in GST. It is important that this distinction between goods and services is not lost. Legal fiction must be limited to the confines of its scope and purpose. Any fiction in GST applicable to 'pre-packaged' goods must be applied strictly — not stretched by inference from LMA.
16.2.1 — Weight or Measure (Standards) ⇧ Index
📏 Weight or Measure — Standard Quantities
Legal Metrology (National Standards) Rules, 2011
PROHIBITION
Articles offered for sale are NOT permitted to be packed in non-standard quantities
📝 You cannot sell rice in a 700-gram pack if the standard quantities prescribed are 500g, 1kg, 2kg, 5kg, etc. Non-standard pack = violation.
STANDARD
Standard quantities specified in LM (National Standards) Rules 2011
Unit of measurement must also be as prescribed
COMPLEX UNITS
Complex units of measure also applied in trade → 'equivalent' units in standard weight or measure must also be expressed
📝 When you import in one unit and sell in another (e.g., square meters → square feet), the equivalent in standard units must also appear on the label. The conversion cannot be exact.
💡 Eg.563: Carpet tiles imported in sq.m. but sold in sq.ft. — stock differences will result due to equivalence not being exact.
💡 Eg.564: LPG imported in tonnes but sold in litres after bottling.
⚠️ GST — UQC RULE
Answers must be found and books maintained based on the 'UQC' (Unit Quantity Code) for GST purposes but without violating the substantive law applicable to the article.
After making assertions before one regulator, taxpayer cannot resile from it before GST authorities.
📝 UQC in GST invoices and returns must match the standard units of LMA. If you told metrology inspector goods are sold in kilograms, your GST invoice UQC must also be KGS — not some other unit to claim a different value or rate.
16.2.2 — Caveat Emptor ⇧ Index
👀 Caveat Emptor — "Let the Buyer Beware"
Indian Contract Act Section 17 | LMA Override for Packed Goods
📝 Caveat emptor = "Let the Buyer beware" — buyer bears risk of purchase. But this principle is severely limited for packed goods because buyers physically cannot examine goods without destroying the package. So statute steps in and forces disclosure by seller.
GENERAL RULE
"Let the Buyer beware" — no burden on Seller to make disclosure beyond terms of offer
EXCEPTION 1 (Contract Act)
Latent defects (hidden defects) must be disclosed by Seller to avoid repudiation for fraud
📝 Latent defect = a hidden defect the buyer cannot find on ordinary inspection. Seller must reveal it. If not disclosed — it is fraud and contract can be repudiated.
EXCEPTION 2 (NO duty)
Seller NOT answerable for non-disclosure of patent defects — which a knowledgeable Buyer from the trade could detect on examination Seller facilitates
📝 Patent defect = obvious defect visible on inspection. If it's visible and you still buy — seller is not liable. The test is: would a trade professional see this defect?
LMA OVERRIDE
Goods in packed condition — NOT amenable to thorough verification of quantity → This Act mandates Seller to make full and true disclosure
📝 For packed goods, you cannot even see or test the quantity. So caveat emptor fails — the Act forces the seller to disclose everything on the label: quantity, weight, MRP, ingredients, etc.
QUALITY TEST
Quality is that which is fit-for-purpose (discussed earlier) when sale is by description.
16.2.3 — Caveat Venditor ⇧ Index
⚠️ Caveat Venditor — "Let the Seller Beware"
Vicarious Liability | Misleading Presentation
📝 The opposite of caveat emptor. "Let the Seller beware" — the seller bears the risk of misrepresentation. Sellers must be mindful of how they present their goods. Misleading label or packaging = liability.

Imposes liability on Sellers and producers to be mindful of misrepresentation or misleading appearance of strict presentation of information material to buying decision
REMEDY
Available to Buyer under this Act OR in Consumer Forum
CAUTION
This principle exposes Seller to vicarious liability
Higher degree of accountability for those in better bargaining position
📝 Vicarious liability = liability for the actions of others in your supply chain. A manufacturer can be held liable for a distributor's misleading label if the manufacturer knew or was responsible.
GST PRINCIPLE
While compensation may be determined based on the promise, tax treatment would depend only on the actual object of a contract
📝 Even if a seller pays compensation for misrepresentation — GST is charged on what was actually supplied (the real object), not what was promised in a misleading manner. Promise ≠ taxable event. Actual supply = taxable event.
16.2.4 — Packed Commodities ⇧ Index
📦 Packed Commodities — Three Distinct Definitions
Retail Package / Standard Package / Wholesale Package | PC Rules
📝 "Packed" is not the same as "packaged commodity." The law draws sharp distinctions — mixing these up in GST can cause major errors. GST treatment (e.g., pre-packaged and labelled rate) can only be availed when the correct statutory definition is satisfied.
Retail Package
Rule 2(k) — PC Rules
Package intended for direct sale to consumer at retail
📝 A packet you buy at a shop for yourself — bread, milk, biscuits. Meant for end consumer use.
Standard Package
Rule 2(p) — PC Rules
Package conforming to standard quantities prescribed
Wholesale Package
Rule 2(q) — PC Rules
Package for wholesale trade — not direct retail sale
📝 A wholesale carton of 12 bottles supplied from manufacturer to distributor. Not a retail package.
KEY DISTINCTION
Anything packed is NOT a package
Packed commodities ≠ Packaged commodities
📝 Just because something is physically put in a wrapper or container does NOT make it a "packaged commodity" under the law. It must satisfy the statutory definition — including standard quantity, standard unit, and MRP labelling requirements.
⚠️ GST MISCHIEF — CRITICAL
GST treatment (e.g., concessional rate on food items in unpacked condition) cannot be availed unless articles are:
(i) Packaged commodity as per the statutory definition, AND
(ii) Conforming to circumstances of presentation in these statutory definitions

Suffice to state the much mischief can occur by omitting to differentiate between these.
💡 Eg.565: Aluminium can of beverage emptied and found short on quantity or containing sand/contaminants = breach of PC Rules.
💡 Eg.566: PET bottles of beverage found substantially short (half-bottle) = breach of PC Rules but not grievous breach due to defect (quantity shortage being a patent defect).
💡 Eg.567: Pre-package of gulab jamun in sealed wrapper with MRP, included in a 'thali meal', has lost its identity as a pre-package. PC rules not applicable.
16.2.5 — Pre-Package ⇧ Index
🏷️ Pre-Package — Definition & GST Consequence
Section 2(l) | Section 11 | Section 18 | LMA 2009
📝 A pre-package is a package where the buyer CANNOT verify the quantity at the time of purchase because the goods are enclosed. This inability to verify is what triggers the legal obligations of labelling, standard units, and MRP declaration. Critical for GST since "pre-packaged and labelled" attracts specific rates.
Pre-package = Articles placed in a package so as to render them incapable of verification by customer at time of making the purchase
Such package being form of presentation
→ demands package be in 'standard' units of weight or measure
Section 11 — prohibition on use of weight/measure not standard (as prescribed in Sec 8)
Section 18 — disclosures mandated in respect of pre-packages as defined in Sec 2(l)
⚠️ KEY CONSEQUENCE
When any package is NOT conforming to 'pre-package' and complying with requirement of use of 'standard' units of weight/measure → implies that the article is presented in unpacked condition and the treatment applicable to 'unpacked package' is inescapable.
📝 If your package does not meet the pre-package definition (no MRP, non-standard quantity, no statutory disclosures) — it is treated as if the goods are LOOSE/UNPACKAGED in GST. You cannot claim the lower rate for packaged goods AND also fail to comply with labelling norms.
📌 Key Examples on Pre-Package (Sec 2(l))
ExampleIs it a Pre-Package?Reason
Eg.568: Diwali assortment box of sweet-meats✗ NOT pre-packageAssortment — variable contents, no standardised quantity
Eg.569: New Year chocolate assortment box — box bears MRP, individual sub-packs have NIL MRP✓ Pre-packageBox bears MRP — MRP label on main package sufficient
Eg.570: Medicines distributed to Physicians — WITHOUT MRP, with "Physician's samples, not for sale" label✓ Pre-packageException for physician samples — no MRP required
Eg.571: Jam bottled with MRP ₹X (MRP ₹Y for CSD Canteen)✓ Pre-packageBears MRP — meets requirements
Eg.572: Bread bearing label "for sale by Indian Railways"✓ Pre-packageFor resale — pre-package
Eg.573: Bottled water bearing label "not for sale, specially packed for serving on Indian Airlines"✗ NOT pre-packageNot for further redistribution — only for in-flight service/consumption
Eg.574: Sweet meats sold with 'piece' as unit of price✗ NOT pre-package'Piece' is not a standard unit of weight/measure
16.2.6 — Non-Standard Packing ⇧ Index
📏 Non-Standard Packing — When is it Permitted?
Institutional / Industrial Exceptions | MRP Dual Requirement
GENERAL RULE
When goods are offered for sale in a pre-package → they CANNOT be in anything but a standard package and presented in standard units of weight or measure
EXCEPTION 1
Grains sold in bags more than 25 kg → non-standard package is permitted
📝 Bulk packing of grains for institutional/industrial buyers is exempt from the standard quantity requirement. 26kg rice bag = non-standard but permitted.

💡 Eg.575: Rice bag of 26 kg is non-standard package even if suited for storage and re-distribution and not merely for handling until consumption.
EXCEPTION 2
Assertion by Seller that goods are NOT for further sale OR meant for exclusive institutional sales → use of non-standard packing does not attract statutory embargo
📝 If you sell only to factories, hospitals, government canteens (institutional buyers) — and not for further resale — you can use non-standard pack quantities.

MRP DUAL REQUIREMENT
When non-standard unit/price used → MRP must be presented for:
(i) Unit of sale AND
(ii) Equivalent for standard unit of weight/measure
📝 If selling a 250g pack where standard unit is KG — MRP must show both "₹100 per package" AND "₹400 per kilogram."

💡 Eg.578: Article with standard unit = kilogram, pre-package contains 250g → MRP must state (i) ₹100 per package AND (ii) ₹400 per kilogram.
EXCEPTIONS TO ENTIRE LAW
Rigours of this law do NOT apply to:
(i) Institutional consumer, or (ii) Industrial consumer — as defined in rule 3
(iii) Articles listed in rule 26 of PC Rules
GST ASSERTION RULE
Significance of the label and disclosure made is a positive assertion when anything contradictory is asserted to avail an advantageous tax treatment in GST.
💡 Eg.576: Lassi in a take-away cup is NOT a pre-package at all. Cup is not for storage and re-distribution but handling until consumption.
💡 Eg.577: Unequal heights of contents in two bottles of beverage = standard volume of contents when presented in packed conditions. Appearance of difference due to varying thickness of walls and base of glass bottles.
16.2.7 — Free Units ⇧ Index
🆓 Free Units — "Free" is a Contradiction Under LMA
Section 18 | No provision for selling without MRP | Circular 92/11/2019-GST
📝 Under LMA, once goods are admitted to be within the operation of this Act (pre-packaged and bearing MRP), they are EITHER offered for sale OR not offered for sale. There is no middle ground of "free" — if it bears an MRP, it is for sale. Calling it "free" is a legal contradiction.
CORE PRINCIPLE
Goods admitted to being within operation of this Act and put up for sale with necessary label being MRP — when asserted to be 'free' = contradiction.
Under this Act either it is offered for sale OR NOT offered for sale. No third category exists.
Section 18 — Declarations on pre-packaged commodities: "(1) No person shall manufacture, pack, sell, import, distribute, deliver, offer, expose or possess for sale any pre-packaged commodity unless such package is in such standard quantities or number and bears thereon such declarations and particulars in such manner as may be prescribed. (2) Any advertisement mentioning the retail sale price of a pre-packaged commodity shall contain a declaration as to the net quantity or number of the commodity contained in the package."
⚠️ FREE SAMPLES — MEDICAMENT EXCEPTION
Rule 65(18) of Drugs & Cosmetics Rules allows relaxation — "no drug intended for distribution to the medical profession as free sample" bearing label on container as specified.

BUT: This permission under Drugs and Cosmetics Rules is NOT a classification but a general approval for specified end-use.
GST law is obliged only to examine whether there is reciprocity in accepting the medicaments as 'free' and any dilution in legal significance in respect of these medicaments.
📝 Even if Drugs Act says it's a "free sample," GST must independently examine: is there really no consideration? Is there some indirect benefit flowing back (reciprocity)? If yes — it is a supply in GST even if called "free." The Drugs Act permission does not create a GST exemption.
GST Circular 92/11/2019-GST dated 7 Mar 2019 — Para 2(A)(i): "Accordingly, it is clarified that samples which are supplied free of cost, without any consideration, do not qualify as 'supply' under GST, except where the activity falls within the ambit of Schedule I of the said Act."
AUTHOR'S CRITIQUE
Reference to 'free' in a welfare matters' legislation implies that expression does not override Contract law to create a new class of contracts valid even without consideration.
Consideration exists albeit in non-monetary form. The ironic and evasive interpretation is evidence in GST Circular 92 itself.
📝 The author points out that Circular 92 is internally inconsistent — it says "free = no supply" but the LMA position is that "free" is a contradiction for goods that bear MRP. Non-monetary consideration (brand exposure, reciprocal obligation) may still exist. The circular is a litigation-avoidance measure, not a principled interpretation.
16.2.8 — Buy-One-Take-Two (Promotional Schemes) ⇧ Index
🛒 Buy-One-Take-Two — Three Options Analysed
MRP ₹999 per article | PC Rules Rule 6 | GST on Promotional Schemes
📝 A common "buy-one-get-one-free" scheme. The chapter analyses three ways to structure the invoice and shows that only ONE option (C) is both LMA-compliant AND GST-correct. The popular choice (Option A) is actually wrong on both counts.
DescriptionOption AOption BOption C ✓
Invoice value: First unit1,0001,0001,000
Invoice value: Second unit01,0001,000
Sub-total1,0002,0002,000
Less: discount—(1,000)—
GST @ 12%120120240
Less: discount (post-GST)——(1,000)
Total1,1201,1201,240
✗ OPTION A — WRONG
Second unit invoiced at ₹0
Would be true ONLY if two units were packed together and carried MRP ₹1,000 for two
→ LMA (PC Rules Rule 6) requires each unit to be packed into single package carrying single MRP
📝 Option A is what most businesses do. But it is wrong — two separately packed items with individual MRPs cannot be combined as if only one item is being sold. The label (MRP ₹999 per item) is a positive assertion under LMA.
✗ OPTION B — IMPOSSIBLE
Expansive meaning of 'consideration' under §2(31)(b) CGST Act + limited scope for deduction for 'discount' in promotional scheme + 'withdrawal-failure' test → Option B impossible to be admitted in GST
📝 A promotional scheme discount is not the same as a trade discount. The "withdrawal-failure" test asks: can the buyer walk away without taking the second unit? In a "buy-one-take-two" scheme — no. So the "free" unit has consideration in non-monetary form.
✓ OPTION C — CORRECT
Both units invoiced at MRP
GST on full value (₹2,000)
Discount of ₹1,000 given after GST
Compatible with LMA and GST law
📝 Charge full MRP for both units. Charge GST on full value. Then give a cash discount of ₹1,000 after GST. Total paid by customer = ₹1,240. This respects the MRP label assertion AND correctly computes GST.
💡 Circular 92/11/2019-GST advises reversal of ITC where the assertion is that the articles are given away freely. Option C avoids this by treating both units as sold at MRP and giving a post-GST discount.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Terms of a promotional scheme cannot be viewed in isolation as to the tax treatment to be applied without regard to positive assertions made on the label under this Act and PC Rules.
16.2.9 — Combo Package ⇧ Index
🎁 Combo Package — Several Units in Single Package
PC Rules Rule 6(5) | Object of Contract must be Identified
📝 When you pack multiple different items together in one box/package, the labelling requirements multiply. The critical GST question is: what exactly is the "object of contract" — are you selling the bundle as a single thing, or is each component a separate thing? The label answers this.
Where several units are presented in single package → labelling requirements become much more, and important to identify from the label: "what is the object of contract of sale?"
PC Rules Rule 6(5): "Where a commodity consists of a number of components and these components are packed in two or more units, for sale as a single commodity, the declaration required to be made under sub-rule (1) shall appear on the main package and such package shall also carry information about the other accompanying packages or such declaration may be given on individual packages and intimation to that effect may be given on the main package and if the components are sold as spare parts, all declarations shall be given on each package."
GST IMPLICATION
The label under PC Rules 6(5) determines whether the combo package is:
(a) A single composite supply (one GST rate), OR
(b) Multiple individual items (each with its own GST rate)

Terms of a promotional scheme cannot be viewed in isolation without regard to positive assertions on label.
💡 A combo pack of shampoo + conditioner sold as "Hair Care Kit" with single MRP → Rule 6(5) requires declarations. If the label says "combo" and single MRP — likely composite supply. If individual MRPs are also shown — individual supplies, each with their own rate.
16.2.10 — Label Alteration ⇧ Index
🏷️ Label Alteration — What Can Be Changed & What Cannot
PC Rules Rule 6(3) | MRP Sticker Rules | GST Implications
📝 Labels are printed in advance — sometimes circumstances change (price changes, law changes, rate changes). Rule 6(3) deals with what can be altered by sticker and what cannot. Particularly important for GST when GST rates change mid-product-lifecycle.
GENERAL RULE (Rule 6(3))
It shall NOT be permissible to affix individual stickers on the package for altering or making declaration required under these rules
📝 You cannot put a sticker on a package to change any of the required declarations — like changing the weight, changing the ingredients list, changing the net quantity, etc.
EXCEPTION — MRP REDUCTION ONLY
For reducing the Maximum Retail Price (MRP) — a sticker with the revised lower MRP (inclusive of all taxes) may be affixed
The sticker shall NOT cover the MRP declaration made by manufacturer/packer on the label
📝 The only permitted sticker alteration is to REDUCE the MRP. You can put a sticker showing ₹80 over a label that says ₹100. But the original ₹100 must still be visible underneath — not covered.
CONTEXT — CHANGES IN LAW
Labels printed in advance — admitted interval between packing and shelf sale. During this interval — changes in law (including GST rate changes) can affect claims made including 'old' and 'new' claims on label. Rule 6(3) demands disclosure of both.
📝 If GST rate changes from 12% to 5% after a batch is packed — the old MRP (inclusive of 12% GST) and new MRP (inclusive of 5% GST) must both be disclosed. The MRP reduction sticker is the vehicle for this disclosure.
⚠️ GST IMPLICATION
Assertions by taxpayer that goods are 'free' or 'coupled' with some other articles cannot be glossed over while considering the tax treatment to apply.

Superadded to articles of food — compliance also required with labelling requirements under:
• Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954
• Seeds Act, 1966
• Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940
16.3.1 — FSSAI (Food Safety Standards Authority of India) ⇧ Index
🍽️ FSSAI — Statutory Definition of "Food" & GST Implications
Food Safety Standards Act, 2006 | Section 3(1)(j)
📝 FSSAI monitors food production, distribution and labelling. What qualifies as "food" under FSSAI standards is an assertion of fact critical for GST — because "food" attracts specific rates (often Nil or 5%). But FSSAI license ≠ "meant for resale." MRP label = "meant for sale." These are different assertions.
Section 3(1)(j) — "Food" means any substance, whether processed, partially processed or unprocessed, which is intended for human consumption and includes primary food to the extent defined in clause (zk), genetically modified or engineered food or food containing such ingredients, infant food, packaged drinking water, alcoholic drink, chewing gum, and any substance, including water used into the food during its manufacture, preparation or treatment but does not include any animal feed, live animals unless they are prepared or processed for placing on the market for human consumption, plants, prior to harvesting, drugs and medicinal products, cosmetics, narcotic or psychotropic substances.
FSSAI LICENSE ≠ FOR RESALE
Grant of FSSAI license does NOT imply articles of food are suited for distribution
It only implies article conforms to standards prescribed and Seller is within regulatory oversight
📝 FSSAI license = you are regulated, not that your goods are ready for retail sale. A laboratory can have FSSAI license for test samples — does not make those samples "goods for sale."
MRP = MEANT FOR SALE
FSSAI license does NOT imply article is 'meant for resale' or otherwise
UNLIKE affixing of MRP which necessarily implies the article is 'meant for sale'
📝 MRP on a product = positive assertion = "this item is for sale." FSSAI license has no such implication. The distinction matters for GST rate applicability and supply classification.
💡 Eg.579: Hookah and cigarette are NOT food even if ingested.
💡 Eg.580: Chewing tobacco is NOT food even though it is eaten.
16.3.2 — Food Business Operator (FBO) ⇧ Index
🏭 Food Business Operator (FBO) — Who is covered?
Section 3(1)(n) & (o) | FSSA 2006
Section 3(1)(n) — "food business" means any undertaking, whether for profit or not and whether public or private, carrying out any of the activities related to any stage of manufacture, processing, packaging, storage, transportation, distribution of food, import and includes food services, catering services, sale of food or food ingredients.

Section 3(1)(o) — "food business operator" in relation to food business means a person by whom the business is carried on or owned and is responsible for ensuring the compliance of this Act, rules and regulations made thereunder.
FBO includes — Manufacturer, Packers, Wholesalers, Distributors and Sellers in the last-mile
FOOD INCLUDES — READY-TO-EAT CATEGORIES
(a) Ready-to-eat foods  |  (b) Ready-to-cook foods  |  (c) Heat-and-eat foods  |  (d) Cold salads and assembled-foods

EXCLUDES: Articles uncooked and intended for use in further preparation — grain, sugar, oil, dairy products, meat, fruits, vegetables, etc.
📝 The "food" definition in FSSA is specifically for food business purposes — ready-to-consume preparations. Raw agricultural produce intended for further cooking is NOT food for FSSAI purposes. This distinction feeds into GST classification: restaurant/catering service vs supply of raw food goods.
16.3.3 — Label of Claims ⇧ Index
📋 Label of Claims — Section 23 FSSA
No False or Misleading Claims Permitted on Food Labels
Section 23(1) — "No person shall manufacture, distribute, sell or expose for sale or despatch or deliver to any agent or broker for the purpose of sale, any packaged food products which are not marked and labelled in the manner as may be specified by regulations: Provided that the labels shall not contain any statement, claim, design or device which is false or misleading in any particular concerning the food products contained in the package or concerning the quantity or the nutritive value implying medicinal or therapeutic claims or in relation to the place of origin of the said food products."
No false/misleading statement on label re: quantity, nutritive value, medicinal/therapeutic claims, or place of origin
Section 23(2): FBO must ensure labelling & presentation of food — including shape, appearance, packaging, packaging materials, manner of arrangement, setting in which displayed — does not mislead consumers
GST LINK
Positive assertions on food labels (contents, composition, whether preservative-free, shelf life) are facts used to determine whether supply is goods or services and applicable GST rate
📝 A label saying "preservative-free, short shelf life, not for redistribution" will be used by GST to determine: is this food prepared for immediate consumption (restaurant service) or packaged goods for sale?
16.3.4 — Licensing of FBO ⇧ Index
📜 Licensing of FBO — Sine Qua Non & Exception
Section 31 | Food Safety Standards Act, 2006
📝 Sine qua non = absolutely essential (without which, not). No one can run a food business without a license. But there is a carve-out for small/petty food sellers — they only need to register, not obtain a full license. This exception reveals a key principle: "presentation" determines whether food is goods or services.
Section 31(1): No person shall commence or carry on any food business except under a licence.

Section 31(2): Nothing contained in sub-section (1) shall apply to a petty manufacturer who himself manufactures or sells any article of food or a petty retailer, hawker, itinerant vendor or a temporary stall holder or small scale or cottage or such other industries relating to food business or tiny food business operator; but they shall register themselves with such authority and in such manner as may be specified by regulations, without prejudice to the availability of safe and wholesome food for human consumption or affecting the interests of the consumers.
KEY INSIGHT — PRESENTATION = GOODS or SERVICES
Street-hawkers who sell articles of food and drinks are not supplying them for further distribution. Even though someone may 'take away' and resell them — the nature of the earlier transaction involving articles of food for consumption by Buyer remains.

From exception in Section 31 — it is clear that 'presentation' is key to determination of whether articles of food are goods or services.
📝 A pav bhaji hawker sells food for immediate consumption. Even if someone buys 10 plates and sells them ahead — the hawker's transaction is a "service" (food for consumption), not sale of "goods" for distribution. Presentation (for consumption vs for redistribution) is the decisive test for GST classification.
16.3.5 — Goods or Services (Food) ⇧ Index
🍱 Food — Goods or Services? The Presentation Test
Circulars 27/1/2018-GST | 92/11/2019-GST | 164/20/2021-GST
📝 This is one of the most litigation-prone areas of GST. Tax treatment of food is sensitive to whether it is classified as goods (multiple rates: 0%, 5%, 12%, 18%) or services (typically 5% restaurant rate). Classification error is not covered by Section 77 CGST (wrong head, same taxpayer) — it is an irrecoverable error.
⚠️ CRITICAL GST WARNING
Tax treatment in GST is sensitive to whether articles of food are goods or services.
Any error in classification as goods or services can prove too costly for Supplier:
• Demand for correct tax
• Risk of losing the wrong tax due to lapse of limitation
Classification error is NOT a case that comes within the relief available under Section 77 of CGST Act.
📝 Section 77 CGST gives relief when you pay CGST/SGST instead of IGST (or vice versa) — wrong head but same transaction. But goods vs services is NOT a "wrong head" error — it is a wrong classification entirely. No Section 77 relief. Demand + limitation risk both arise.
Key Tests — When is food "goods" vs "services"?
Preservative-free?
Short shelf life due to absence of preservatives or susceptible to accelerated oxidation / putrefaction
Packing only for handling and not for sustained storage
Not available in places of re-distribution
→ SERVICE
All four factors point to food prepared for consumption, not redistribution = Restaurant/catering service
📝 Chole batura from Kakke-da-Dhaba — not sold at Big Bazar. Only for immediate consumption. No redistributable shelf life. = Service (5% GST, restaurant rate).
💡 Eg.584: Chole batura from Kakke-da-Dhaba is not available in Big Bazar Stores = service.
Eg.583: Haldiram's bhujia — with statutory disclosures including MRP for sale at Big Bazar without restriction on further sales = goods.
CIRCULAR 164/20/2021-GST
Ice cream parlours sell already manufactured ice cream and do NOT cook/prepare — supply of ice cream as goods, not service → 18% GST
📝 The Government carved out ice cream parlours from restaurant treatment because they resell pre-manufactured ice cream. This is a litigation-avoidance circular, not a principled first-principles interpretation per the author.
💡 Circular 27/1/2018-GST: Any service by way of serving food or drinks including by a bakery = Section 10(1)(b) composition levy = 5% GST.
Circular 164/2021: Cloud kitchens/central kitchens = "restaurant service" = 5% GST (no ITC).
💡 Eg.581: Bottled aerated beverage sold by bottler to Indian Railways = sale of GOODS (presentation not for consumption but for resale/distribution by IR to Passengers).
💡 Eg.582: Sale of bottled beverage by Indian Railways to Passengers = not for resale or distribution — for consumption → may be SERVICE element involved.
💡 Eg.585: Pan (betel leaf with lime mix) lacks shelf-life once prepared — any packing is only for handling until consumption.
💡 Eg.586: Rosogolla in sugar syrup in sealed container = suitable for storage and redistribution = goods.
💡 Eg.587: MacDonald burger prepared in-situ = box only for handling until consumption = service.
16.4.1 — Explosives — Statutory Terms of Contract ⇧ Index
💥 Explosives Act, 1884 & Rules, 2008 — Positive Assertions on Facts
LPG | Flammable & Combustible Substances | Statutory Contracts
📝 Explosives Act applies to flammable and combustible substances — including LPG. This law imposes restrictions on manufacture, storage, bottling, and distribution that operate as "positive assertions on facts" for GST purposes. Contracts for LPG must comply with this law — you cannot contract out of it.
STATUTE OVERRIDES CONTRACT
Restrictions imposed by this law prevails over any innovative contract-writing — terms of contract are compelled to comply with this law
📝 You cannot write a contract that says "LPG sold ex-works, buyer collects." The law mandates door delivery. Statutory obligation prevails over any contractual attempt to avoid it.
💡 Eg.588: LPG cylinder cannot be sold on 'ex works' basis due to mandate that licensed Bottler-Dealer must supply consumer LPG on 'door delivery' basis.
LPG — STANDARD QUANTITY
Pre-package of domestic LPG cylinder must have 14.2 kilograms (Gas Cylinder Rules, 2004)
📝 Domestic LPG is sold in 14.2 kg cylinders — this is a statutory standard. You cannot sell domestic LPG in 5kg, 19kg, or 47.5kg cylinders and claim domestic LPG GST rate.
💡 Eg.589: Pre-package of domestic LPG cylinder must have 14.2 kilograms (Gas Cylinder Rules, 2004).
Eg.590: LPG cylinder of 5, 19 or 47.5 kg — cannot enjoy concessional GST applicable to 'domestic LPG for PDS' as these quantities allowed only for non-domestic use.
LPG — WHAT IS IT?
Nothing called 'liquefied petroleum gas' — trade name for hydrocarbons that liquefy when pressurized and gasify when depressurized. Odourless — domestic LPG sold after adding 'ethyl mercaptan' (rotten cabbage odour)
📝 LPG is a trade name, not a chemical compound. It is a mixture of propane, butane, etc. When you claim "domestic LPG" rate in GST — the statutory label (14.2 kg) and the declared end-use matter. Content alone does not determine rate.
16.4.2 — Stock Loss (Liquid Fuels) ⇧ Index
📉 Stock Loss — Evaporation, Calibration & GST Consequences
Petrol | Diesel | LPG | CNG | Kerosene | Section 17(5)(h) | Section 16(2)(b) CGST
📝 Liquid fuels lose quantity during transport (evaporation). When "dispatch quantity" is more than "delivered quantity" — two GST questions arise: (1) Can excess ITC be reversed under Section 17(5)(h)? (2) Can recipient still claim ITC under Section 16(2)(b) for goods not actually received? Industry norms (not public standards) govern acceptable deviation.
CAUSE 1
Evaporation during transportation — loss of stock in transit
CAUSE 2
Calibration losses — calibration device may not show same reading due to inherent error due to permitted tolerance in different flow-meters
CAUSE 3
Pressure changes affect measurement of liquid fuels (liquified under pressure) — alteration of ambient pressure results in changes in stock quantities
⚠️ GST QUESTION
Dispatch quantity > Delivered quantity → whether Section 17(5)(h) CGST Act applies
Recipient: How to satisfy Section 16(2)(b) CGST Act to avail ITC where GST was paid on inward supplies?

Industry norms of stock loss are well established and not a publicly available standard but industry standards of deviation that is allowed.
Any GST treatment based on stock verification similar to a trader's inventory would be riddled with errors — normalizing for such factors must first be done before any real stock difference is established.
📝 You cannot simply compare dispatch quantity vs delivered quantity in a straight line and demand GST reversal or deny ITC. First, calibration tolerances, evaporation norms, and pressure variation must be adjusted. Only after that normalisation can a "real" stock loss (as opposed to technical/industry-standard loss) be identified.
16.4.3 — Stock Gains (LPG Residue) ⇧ Index
📈 Stock Gains — The LPG Residue Phenomenon
Domestic LPG | 14.2 kg Mandate | Residual LPG | Misleading Stock Comparison
📝 Unlike other goods, stock gains (having MORE stock than expected) are also possible for liquid fuels — specifically LPG. Consumers replace cylinders before completely emptying them. Refilling stations fill only up to 14.2 kg. The residual gas from returned cylinders creates a "stock gain" for the bottling plant. This is a structural industry reality, not fraud.
The Residue Mechanism
Consumer reorders as soon as flame starts to flicker → cylinder not emptied before exchange → 100 grams LPG residue per cylinder
Refilling Mandate
Bottling plants mandated to fill only 14.2 kg → fills exactly that much → but 100g residue already in cylinder → effectively only 14.1 kg fills in
Scale of Gain
50,000 cylinders × 1.5 cycles/month × 100g residue = 7.5 tonnes/month stock gain
(for a single bottling plant)
⚠️ GST IMPLICATION
Stock gains must be accounted for in financials. Where is this stock gain accounted in the financials?
Stock gains compounded with stock losses (from other factors) create accounting predicament — true not just for LPG but also for:
• Ready-mix concrete
• Hot-mix (asphalt)
• Scores of other products

Liner comparison yields misleading results if industry norms and realities are ignored. GST treatment based on such comparison would be riddled with errors.
📝 GST officers doing stock verification of LPG bottlers who treat stock gains as "unaccounted goods" liable to GST at maximum rate — without first accounting for the structural residue gain and other factors — are making a fundamental error. Industry norms must be applied first.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 16 | Legal Metrology Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the statutory provision or principle.
Facts-Not-Law Transplant Rule — LMA Assertions in GST
Legal Principle | Para 16.1 | LMA 2009 is Welfare Legislation | GST is Private Matters Legislation
Key Principle
Legal Metrology law is a welfare legislation and demands purposive construction, but for limited purposes of GST, which is a private matters' legislation and strict interpretation alone applies, assertions by Seller to either regulator is only facts and not any treatment of law that is to be transplanted from Legal Metrology law to GST law. Assertions on facts under this law can greatly assist in examining the tax treatment under that law.
When to Use — When Department attempts to import legal consequences of LMA (e.g., LMA penalty for non-standard packing = deemed unpackaged = lower GST rate) into GST directly. Counter: only facts travel, not legal consequences. GST must apply its own strict interpretation.
Non-Resiling Rule — Positive Assertion Before One Regulator Binds Before GST
Legal Principle | Para 16.1 | UQC | Label Declarations
Key Principle
After making assertions before one regulator, taxpayer cannot resile from it before GST authorities. The significance of the label and disclosure made is a positive assertion when anything contradictory is asserted to avail an advantageous tax treatment in GST.
When to Use — When assessee claims goods are "loose" before GST officer but declared as "pre-packaged with MRP" on label. OR when assessee claims goods are "standard packaged" before LMA inspector but claims "non-packaged" lower rate before GST. The label and regulator declaration binds.
Pre-Package + Non-Conformity = Unpacked Treatment Inescapable
Legal Principle | Section 2(l), Section 11, Section 18 | LMA 2009
Key Principle
When any package is NOT conforming to 'pre-package' and complying with the requirement of use of 'standard' units of weight or measure, implies that the article is presented in unpacked condition and the treatment applicable to 'unpacked package' is inescapable. Assertions to the contrary, even for GST purposes, cannot be admitted and the tax treatment to apply to such altered assertions must be validated and applied.
When to Use — When a taxpayer claims a packaged commodity rate but their packaging does not comply with LMA standards (non-standard quantity, no MRP, non-standard unit). Argue: non-compliant package = treated as unpacked = unpacked rate applies — the taxpayer cannot simultaneously violate LMA and claim GST benefit meant for LMA-compliant packages.
Free Units — MRP Bearing Goods Cannot be "Free" | Section 18 LMA
Legal Principle | Section 18 LMA 2009 | Circular 92/11/2019-GST dated 7 Mar 2019
Key Principle
Goods admitted to being within the operation of this Act and put up for sale with necessary label being MRP, when asserted to be 'free' is a contradiction. Under this Act either it is offered for sale or NOT offered for sale. Reference to 'free' in a welfare matters' legislation implies that expression does not override Contract law to create a new class of contracts that are valid even without consideration. Clearly, consideration exists albeit in non-monetary form.
When to Use — When assessee claims "free samples" supplied with MRP label are not supply under Schedule I CGST (related party). Counter: MRP-bearing goods cannot be called free. Section 18 LMA mandates MRP on goods offered for sale — the MRP label is the assertion. Non-monetary consideration exists = it is a supply.
Buy-One-Take-Two — Option C is the Only Correct GST Treatment
Legal Principle | Para 16.2.8 | PC Rules Rule 6 | Section 2(31)(b) CGST | Circular 92/11/2019
Key Principle
Terms of a promotional scheme cannot be viewed in isolation as to the tax treatment to be applied without regard to positive assertions made on the label under this Act and PC Rules. Option A (second unit at zero) is incongruous with PC Rules Rule 6 making labelling applicable to 'each' package, requiring single MRP per unit. Option B (discount before GST) bears indicia of consideration in non-monetary form applying the 'withdrawal-failure' test making it impossible in GST. Option C (both units at MRP, GST on full value, discount after GST) is compatible with LMA and GST law.
When to Use — In promotional scheme GST disputes. When Department demands GST on full MRP value of "free" unit under scheme, or when assessee claims GST benefit on discount that contradicts MRP labels. Cite to establish correct Option C treatment: GST on full MRP of all units; discount is a post-GST commercial decision.
Presentation Test — Food as Goods vs Services | Section 31 FSSA Exception
Legal Principle | Para 16.3.4 & 16.3.5 | Circulars 27/1/2018, 92/2019, 164/2021
Key Principle
From the exception in Section 31 of the Food Safety Standards Act, it is clear that 'presentation' is key to determination of whether articles of food are goods or services. Street-hawkers who sell articles of food and drinks are not supplying them for further distribution — the nature of the transaction involving articles of food for consumption by Buyer remains. Tax treatment in GST is sensitive to whether the articles of food are goods or services and any error in classification can prove too costly for the Supplier due to demand for correct tax with risk of losing the wrong tax due to lapse of limitation. Classification error is not a case that comes within the relief available under Section 77 of CGST Act.
When to Use — In food/restaurant GST disputes. (i) Against demand at goods rate on food served for consumption — cite presentation test, it is a service. (ii) Against demand at service rate on pre-packaged food for redistribution — it is goods. (iii) To warn taxpayers: classification error = Section 77 unavailable = compounded demand + limitation risk.
LPG Domestic Rate — 14.2 kg Standard is Non-Negotiable | Gas Cylinder Rules 2004
Legal Principle | Para 16.4.1 | Gas Cylinder Rules, 2004 | Explosives Rules, 2008
Key Principle
Pre-package of domestic LPG cylinder must have 14.2 kilograms as per Gas Cylinder Rules, 2004. Restrictions imposed by Explosives law prevails over any innovative contract-writing and the terms of contract are compelled to comply with this law. Terms of contract being dictated by statute implies that contracts must be 'subject to' restrictions imposed by this Act and attendant Rules. LPG cylinder of 5, 19 or 47.5 kilograms cannot enjoy concessional GST tariff applicable to 'domestic LPG for PDS' due to these quantities being allowed only for non-domestic use.
When to Use — When assessee claims domestic LPG concessional GST rate on non-standard cylinder sizes (5kg, 19kg, 47.5kg). Cite: Gas Cylinder Rules 2004 mandates 14.2 kg for domestic use. Non-standard size = non-domestic use = commercial LPG rate applies. Statutory standard prevails over contractual designation.
Stock Loss/Gain — Industry Norms Must Be Applied First | Cannot Do Liner Comparison
Legal Principle | Para 16.4.2 & 16.4.3 | Section 17(5)(h) & Section 16(2)(b) CGST Act
Key Principle
Industry norms of stock loss are well established and not a publicly available standard but industry standards of deviation that is allowed. Any GST treatment based on stock verification similar to a trader's inventory would be riddled with errors. Normalizing for such factors must first be done before any real stock difference is established. Liner comparison yields misleading results if industry norms and realities are ignored.
When to Use — When Department raises demand on LPG/fuel stock losses (ITC reversal under Section 17(5)(h)) without adjusting for evaporation, calibration tolerance, and pressure variation norms. Argue: industry-standard losses are not "destruction of goods." Normalisation is a pre-condition to identifying any real loss. Demand without normalisation = riddled with errors.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 17 — Real Estate (RERA)

🏗️ Chapter 17 — Real Estate (Regulation & Development) Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Valid / Permitted / Taxable
Prohibited / Void / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
17.1 Relevance to GST
17.2 Essentials
17.2.1 Tabulation of Expressions
17.2.2 Apartment
17.2.3 Project
17.2.4 Construction & Works Contract
17.2.5 Land into Plots
17.2.6 Commencement
17.2.7 Completion
17.2.8 Occupation
17.2.9 Escrow
17.2.10 Standard Terms
17.2.11 Defects Liability
17.3 Extensions
17.3.1 RREP
17.3.2 Illegal Construction
17.3.3 Pseudo Completion
17.3.4 Non-RERA Project
17.3.5 Self-Development
17.3.6 Industrial Units
17.3.7 Joint Development
17.3.8 Redevelopment
17.3.9 Approved Resolution Plan
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
17.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🏗️ RERA — Relevance to GST
Real Estate (Regulation & Development) Act, 2016
LIMITED RELIANCE
GST has relied upon RERA only to select certain definitions and use them for prescribing tariff rates to residential projects commencing after 1 Apr 2019 under the 'new rate regime' (or projects that transitioned due to non-election of old rate regime)
📝 GST does not adopt RERA wholesale. It only borrows certain specific definitions for limited tariff purposes. The rest of RERA (being a welfare law) does NOT govern GST treatment.
⚠️ IMPORTANT DIVERGENCE
RERA = welfare legislation → subject to purposive construction
GST = tax legislation → demands strict construction
