📝 Purposive construction = interpret the law to achieve its social purpose (used in welfare laws). Strict construction = interpret exactly what the words say (used in tax laws). A Court's liberal interpretation of RERA does NOT automatically carry over to GST.
Two Ways Definitions can be Adopted — Two Baskets:
BASKET 1 — BY REFERENCE
Legislature defines expression in one law 'by reference' to another law → any amendment or Court declaration in the referred law is automatically absorbed in the referring law
📝 Like a live link — if RERA definition changes, the GST meaning also changes automatically.
BASKET 2 — BORROWED & RECITED
Definition is borrowed and written out in the legislation itself → unless that legislation is also separately amended, the two laws go their separate ways
📝 Like a snapshot — only captures the RERA meaning at the time of borrowing. Later RERA amendments do NOT affect the GST meaning unless GST is also amended.
KEY PRACTICAL RULE
(a) Purposive construction applicable to RERA provisions does NOT apply to GST provisions which demand strict construction
(b) Expressions legislated by reference must be located in the two baskets to know whether amendment/declaration must be accommodated in GST too
17.2.1 — Tabulation of Expressions — GST Adoption from RERA ⇧ Index
Clause Expression in GST Notification Reference to RERA Implication
(xiii)Apartment booked before 31 MarDoes NOT borrowGST has its own independent definition — RERA amendments irrelevant
(xiv)ApartmentBorrows Section 2(e)RERA meaning applies — smallest saleable unit (not just residential)
(xv)ProjectRefers to other termsComposite — REP or RREP — see 17.2.3
(xvi)Affordable residential apartmentDoes NOT borrowGST's own definition — price-based or area-based criteria
(xvii)PromoterBorrows Section 2(zk)RERA definition of Promoter applies — includes landowner who books for sale
(xviii)Real Estate Project (REP)Borrows Section 2(zn)Development of building/land into apartments or plots for sale — includes common areas
(xix)Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)Does NOT borrowGST invention — REP where commercial carpet area ≤ 15% of total carpet area
(xx)Ongoing ProjectDoes NOT borrowGST's own definition
(xxi)Commencement CertificateDoes NOT borrowGST's own meaning — see 17.2.6
(xxii)Development WorksDoes NOT borrow (defined in RERA Sec 2(t))GST has own meaning — see 17.2.4
(xxiii)External Development WorksDoes NOT borrow (defined in RERA Sec 2(w))Roads, water supply, sewage outside plot — essential for benefit of plot
(xxiv)Internal Development WorksDoes NOT borrow (defined in RERA Sec 2(zb))Works within project
(xxvi)Carpet AreaBorrows Section 2(k)RERA definition applies — net usable floor area excluding walls, balconies
(xxvii)Real Estate Regulatory AuthorityBorrows Section 20(l)Same authority as RERA
(xxix)Residential ApartmentMakes reference but does NOT borrowGST has its own meaning
(xxx)Commercial ApartmentDoes NOT borrowGST's own definition — any apartment that is not residential
(xxxi)Floor Space IndexDoes NOT borrowGST's own definition
⚠️ RULE Always check whether GST 'borrows' or 'recites' each RERA definition — this determines whether a later RERA amendment or Court declaration automatically affects GST treatment.
17.2.2 — Apartment ⇧ Index
🏢 "Apartment" — Smallest Saleable Unit [RERA Sec 2(e)]
COMMON MISCONCEPTION
'Apartment' is often equated with 'dwelling unit' — this is WRONG
CORRECT MEANING
'Apartment' = smallest saleable unit in a project
📝 This is wider than just a flat. It includes any unit that can be sold separately in the project — irrespective of what it is used for.
Residence / Flat
Office
Shop
Godown
Plot
(smallest saleable unit)
PLOT vs LAND
Undeveloped 'land' ≠ 'plot' in RERA. Plot = smallest saleable unit after development of land.
Development works need NOT be 'in' the plot — includes external development works carried out outside the plot but essential for the benefit of the plot (roads, water supply, sewage, drainage, electricity, solid waste — in periphery or outside the project).
📝 A plot is sold only after the land has been developed. Even if the development works (like road, drainage, transformer) are outside the actual plot area — their completion is necessary before the plot can be said to be complete.
17.2.3 — Project ⇧ Index
📋 "Project" — Real Estate Project (REP) & RREP
DEFINITION — GST
"Project" = Real Estate Project (REP) OR Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)

REP [RERA Sec 2(zn)] = Development of building/apartments OR converting existing building OR development of land into plots/apartments — for purpose of selling all or some — includes common areas, development works, all improvements, structures, easements, rights & appurtenances

RREP [GST invention, not in RERA] = REP in which carpet area of commercial apartments ≤ 15% of total carpet area of all apartments in REP
📝 RREP is GST's own creation — RERA does not have this term. It is a mix-use project where commercial element is minor (≤15%). This determines whether the lower 1%/5% rate applies or the higher 12% rate applies.
CRITICAL — 'FOR SALE' TEST
Project is one that is 'meant for sale' — this fact is borne out in RERA registration requirement under Section 3 (construct in negative — registration is sine qua non for Promoter to proceed with making any actual or implied offers)
📝 Sine qua non = absolutely essential. RERA registration is not optional. Even if the Promoter omits to register — if any unit has been offered for sale and accepted by even one customer before completion — GST treatment applicable to a 'Project' is inescapable.
LAND ≠ PLOT — KEY PRINCIPLE
Conversion of 'land into plots' is NOT beyond the definition of 'project'.
Land cannot be equated with plot.
Sale of land → excluded from GST by Schedule III
Sale of plot → excluded from GST by Schedule III BUT only after completion of development works
This is true of a building too — excluded from GST by Schedule III BUT only after completion of its own development works — evident in qualifying words "subject to paragraph 5(b) to Schedule II"
📝 The Schedule III exclusion for sale of land/plot/building only kicks in AFTER completion. Before completion = taxable as construction service under Schedule II para 5(b). This is the entire basis for GST on under-construction real estate.
Unmistakable Aspects — Land into Plots (confirmed by Manishbhai Munjaal Bhatt, Guj HC):
(a) Land ≠ plot; plot emerges only after completion of development works
(b) Construction is involved in development works
(c) Price paid is not for plot alone but for ALL elements involved in purchase
(d) Price after reducing 1/3 abatement is MORE than attributable to value of land alone — true even for buildings sold above certain price
ABATEMENT RULE — Manishbhai Munjaal Bhatt
One-third statutory abatement allowed ONLY when 'actual value' attributable to land is unknown. Where actual value of land is not ascertainable or is unreliable → statutory abatement (1/3) is unquestionable by Revenue.
Using actual value higher than 1/3 in the agreement is not free from challenge due to interlinkage with construction agreement and advantageous tax consequence.
📝 If you specify a higher land value in the agreement (e.g., 40% instead of 33.33%) to reduce tax — Revenue can challenge it, especially if it is linked to a separate construction agreement. Statutory 1/3 is the safe harbor when land value cannot be independently ascertained.
17.2.4 — Construction & Works Contract ⇧ Index
🔨 Construction & Works Contract — GST Definition & Scope
Construction — Wider Than Visible
Includes super-structure AND sub-structure:
• Underground water & power lines
• Foundation for any super-structure
• Sub-surface infrastructure
• External development works
📝 Construction is not just the visible building. Even underground pipes, foundations, and external roads/drains form part of 'construction' for GST purposes.
Construction can be:
• Civil (brick & mortar)
• Metal (fabrication)
• Wood (assembly)
Any works resulting in a structure bringing about an immovable property
📝 Works contract under GST is about what it produces (immovable property) — not how it is built. A pre-fabricated steel structure that becomes immovable when installed is 'construction'.
PRINCIPAL SUPPLY TEST
In composite supply → construction (resulting in immovable property) must be the principal supply
Civil foundation for DG equipment → NOT construction (incidental to DG supply)
Culvert for water flow → IS construction (principal object is the structure)
📝 Test: What is the customer primarily buying? If it is the structure — construction is principal. If it is the equipment and the foundation is merely incidental — not a works contract.
Works Contract [Section 2(119) CGST]
GST Works Contract = limited to:
(i) Construction, fabrication, etc.
(ii) Resulting in immovable property
For apartment construction — contract of immovable property wherein transfer of property in goods (in goods or some other form) is involved in execution — appearing in Sec 2(119)
Cannot be limited to only constructed area (in which Buyer has absolute title).
Must also include constructed common areas in which Buyer has non-exclusive rights
📝 The works contract covers both (i) the flat itself AND (ii) the common areas like corridors, lifts, parking, club — even though buyer does not own common areas exclusively. Both form part of the 'contract of immovable property'.
COMMON AREAS — RERA INSIGHT
Goods in common areas not extinguished — they exist physically. Relinquishment of common areas to regulator perfects Buyer's title. Transfer of property in those goods is indirectly to Buyer (as their rights permit). Relinquishment is 'on account of' Buyer — so as to perfect Buyer's title
📝 When developer transfers common areas to housing society/regulator — it is really being done for the Buyers' benefit. The goods in those areas (tiles, pipes, lifts) are transferred indirectly to Buyers. Hence includible in works contract value.
17.2.5 — Land into Plots ⇧ Index
🗺️ Land into Plots — GST Treatment
GST TREATMENT
Conversion of land into plots = a 'project'. GST allows abatement of one-third to total value to account for land exclusion (Schedule III).
Remaining value (after abatement) = towards amenities, brand and quality assurance that Buyer enjoys.
📝 When you buy a plot in a developed township, you're not just buying land. You're also paying for the roads, club, landscaping, water supply, and brand value. GST taxes that portion — not the land value. Object of contract is what the Buyer admits to.
RERA PARITY
RERA expects same extent of rigorous compliance for a project whether it is a building or a plot. No support can be derived from RERA to differentiate GST treatment for plot development.
📝 You cannot argue that plot development should get softer GST treatment just because it is plots and not flats. RERA treats both equally — and so does GST.
17.2.6 — Commencement ⇧ Index
📅 Commencement — Legal & Physical Both Required
DEFINITION
Commencement = (i) Legal authority (grant of consent to undertake development works) AND (ii) Material alteration of ground realities

Juridically: grant of consent to undertake development works = actually undertaking development works
📝 GST makes a special definition of commencement — especially important when a project had already commenced before 1 Apr 2019 (new rate regime). The old rate regime continues only if both legal permission AND physical start had happened before the transition date.
STATUTORY DEFINITION — Apartment Booked Before 31 Mar 2019
An apartment booked on or before 31 Mar 2019 shall mean an apartment which meets ALL THREE conditions:
(a) Part of supply of construction which has time of supply on or before 31 Mar 2019; AND
(b) At least one instalment has been credited to bank account of registered person on or before 31 Mar 2019; AND
(c) Allotment letter or sale agreement or any other similar document evidencing booking has been issued on or before 31 Mar 2019
📝 All three conditions must be satisfied simultaneously. Missing even one condition means the apartment is NOT treated as booked before 31 Mar 2019 — and the new rate (1%/5%) applies instead of old rate (8%/12%).
17.2.7 — Completion ⇧ Index
✅ Completion — Actual vs Legal
ACTUAL COMPLETION
Works are physically completed
LEGAL COMPLETION
Works completed AND tested to meet standards of the regulator (Completion Certificate by Competent Authority)
📝 Unlike commencement, completion is not only actual but ALSO legal. Physical completion alone is not enough — you need the Completion Certificate from the authority certifying it meets sanctioned plan standards.
RERA Sec 2(q)
"Completion certificate" = certificate, by whatever name, issued by competent authority certifying that real estate project has been developed according to sanctioned plan, layout and specifications as approved by competent authority under local laws
(definition NOT referred by GST)
GST TEST FOR COMPLETION
Para 5(b), Schedule II uses expression "completion" → fitness-for-purpose must be established NOT by standards of any Engineer but by those of a Buyer (being one who knows lesser of the two contracting Parties — ostensible object of contract)
📝 GST looks at completion from the buyer's perspective — is it fit for purpose in the eyes of a lay person buying a home? Not technical engineering standards. This is consistent with 'ostensible object of contract' discussed earlier.
17.2.8 — Occupation ⇧ Index
🏠 Occupation Certificate vs Completion Certificate
RERA Sec 2(zf)
"Occupancy certificate" = certificate, by whatever name, issued by competent authority permitting occupation of any building, which has provision for civic infrastructure such as water, sanitation and electricity
(definition NOT referred by GST)
COMPLETION CERTIFICATE
Development works completed per sanctioned plan
Certain finishing works may be PENDING
(aesthetic appeal — tiles, paint, finishing)
OCCUPATION CERTIFICATE
Physical construction complete + utilities connected
(water, sanitation, electricity)
Involves additional tests before certifying suitable for occupants
CLEAR DIFFERENCE
Completion certificate can be granted even when certain finishing works (that address aesthetic appeal only, NOT functionality or utility that defines building identity) are pending. Occupation certificate requires utilities to be connected and building to be fit for occupation.
📝 In GST, 'first occupation' of the building (not necessarily OC) triggers the Schedule III exclusion — bringing an end to GST on new bookings after that date. Completion certificate is only evidence — not conclusive if facts show otherwise.
17.2.9 — Escrow ⇧ Index
💰 Escrow — Ring-fencing of Project Funds
RULE
Funds collected for one project cannot be utilized for anything except:
• Repayment of working capital injected earlier
• Other requirements of THAT specific project
• Limited relaxation for administrative overheads
📝 This is RERA's ring-fencing mechanism — customer payments for Project A cannot be diverted to Project B or to promoter's personal use. Protects buyers from diversion of funds.
MONITORING
Funds collected = subject to 'end use' monitoring based on utilization reports duly certified — submitted online to the regulator.
Chapter III of RERA addresses rights and obligations of Promoters.
17.2.10 — Standard Terms of Contract ⇧ Index
📜 Standard Terms — Welfare Legislation Prevails
RERA IMPACT
Statute prescribes 'standard form' of contracts — Parties no longer free to devise their own form and format of agreements. Statute allows limited leeway for Promoters to devise own terms and conditions.
📝 Pre-RERA: Promoters wrote one-sided contracts. Post-RERA: Standard form is mandated. Terms in the statutory contract require 'substance' to prevail over any anomalous or onerous 'form' of contract (being a welfare legislation).
RULE — contra proferentem
Rule of contra proferentem applies when one of the Parties devised the standard form of contract — ambiguities interpreted against the drafter (Promoter).
📝 Contra proferentem = against the one who wrote it. If the contract is ambiguous, courts interpret it against the Promoter who drafted it. In GST disputes — standard RERA contract terms provide the factual foundation that can neither be lightly denied nor contradicted.
GST VALUE
Rights and remedies flowing to Parties tempered by restrictions imposed in RERA provide much needed facts to apply GST treatment to specific transactions. One cannot lightly deny rights clearly admitted in this law or that some payments made or waived is NOT consideration but compensation.
17.2.11 — Defects Liability ⇧ Index
⚠️ Defects Liability — GST Implications
RERA Sec 14(3)
Promoter obliged to indemnify for quality of product sold. Defects must be cured at Promoter's expense for 5 years from date of handing over possession.
📝 This 5-year defects liability period starts from date of handing over possession — NOT from date of completion certificate. The Promoter bears the cost — no additional charge can be levied on Buyer for defect rectification during this period.
MULTI-TOWER PROJECTS
Promoter may designate each tower or phase as separate project for RERA purposes → starts and ends defects liability period per tower/phase
📝 This helps Promoters manage defects liability by tower — not entire project. But GST treatment is affected by 'date of completion' of each project identified by a separate RERA registration.
PARTIAL COMPLETION — FAQs
FAQs (F.No.354/32/2019-TRU dated 7 May 2019) Q29, Q33, Q34 — allude to 'final completion certificate' as actual completion for applying exclusion in Para 5, Schedule III
📝 Where partial completion certificates are granted: CBIC FAQs treat the final (not partial) completion certificate as the trigger for Schedule III exclusion. Earlier partial certificates do not end GST liability.
⚠️ CAUTION
Care must be taken not to misconstrue commercial presentation of the Project with the RERA identity of each Project. A commercially presented as single project may be multiple separate RERA registered projects — affecting GST completion date.
17.3.1 — RREP (Residential Real Estate Project) ⇧ Index
🏘️ RREP — GST Invention | Rate Determination
IMPORTANT RREP is an invention of GST law — there is NO such expression in RERA. It is a mix-use project where commercial units form part of the residential development.
✓ RREP
Commercial carpet area ≤ 15% of total carpet area
GST on commercial apartment: 5% (Entry 3(ib)) — applicable to commercial apartment in RREP under new rate regime, subject to other conditions
📝 Low commercial component = project treated as predominantly residential. Commercial units also get the lower residential rate (5%).
✗ NOT RREP
Commercial carpet area > 15% of total carpet area
GST on commercial apartment: 12% (Entry 3(if)(i)) — applicable to commercial apartment in REP under new rate regime, with ITC (old rate: also 12%)
📝 High commercial component = project treated as REP (not RREP). Commercial units taxed at 12% — but with ITC under new rate regime (not available to RREP).
⚠️ DETERMINATION OF 15%
Determination is a question of fact — best ascertained on completion. Tax treatment may be adopted based on approvals during construction. No fait accompli — perils of short payment if actual construction does not satisfy 15% threshold.
📝 Fait accompli = done deal. Even if you adopted the 5% rate based on approved plans, if actual construction ends up exceeding 15% commercial — you owe the differential tax. Revenue will not accept approved plans as conclusive if actual construction differs.
17.3.2 — Illegal Construction ⇧ Index
⚖️ Illegal Construction — GST is Inescapable
CONSEQUENCES of Construction in Breach of Approvals
• Direction to demolish excess constructed area
• Payment of fine in lieu of demolition
• Denial of completion certificate — for those units or entire Project
RERA APPROACH
Illegality or mere irregularity of construction = matter to be determined under 'competent authority'. RERA being welfare legislation strives to secure interests of Buyers — NOT to uphold 'Town Planning' goals.
⚠️ GST RULE — INESCAPABLE
If units constructed are sold → GST treatment is inescapable — whether those units are illegal or merely irregular.
📝 GST does not care about legality of construction. If you sold it — you collected consideration — GST applies. Illegality is a RERA/Town Planning problem, not a reason to escape GST.
COMPLETION CERTIFICATE NOT CONCLUSIVE
Denial of completion certificate by 'competent authority' does NOT eclipse operation of Para 5(b), Schedule II to Central GST Act.
Incidence of GST will end once project reaches completion — certificate is only evidence.
Proof of "first occupation" = end to incidence of tax on new bookings → attracts exclusion in Para 5, Schedule III.
📝 Even for illegal buildings where the competent authority refuses to give a completion certificate — GST ends when the building is actually first occupied. 'First occupation' is an alternate, factual evidence of completion for GST purposes.
17.3.3 — Pseudo Completion ⇧ Index
⚠️ Pseudo Completion — Premature Completion Certificate
PROBLEM
Very common for real estate projects to secure completion certificate even BEFORE 'habitable completion' — due to policy decisions linked to practical realities in each region and satisfaction of 'competent authority' in different States.
CONTRA-INDICATOR
Flow of input tax credits in GSTR-2B reports = contra-indicator of assertion about completion
📝 If you're still getting ITC on purchases post-completion date, it shows construction is ongoing. Revenue can use this as evidence to disprove the 'completion' assertion.
MINOR WORKS — SAFE
Minor finishing works — overcoat of paint, club finishes, minor defects — will NOT impair the 'date of completion' to avail exclusion from GST in Para 5, Schedule III
📝 Cosmetic finishing works after substantial completion do not restart GST liability. They are not 'construction' in the project delivery sense.
⚠️ DOUBLE WHAMMY RISK
Where inward supplies are substantial (not just finishing works) → Schedule III exclusion will evade AND "first occupation" referred in Para 5(b), Schedule II also denied.
Bookings after 'pseudo' completion date will attract GST.
Mere reversal of ITC will NOT suffice — reversal of credit = double whammy.
[Apex Court in Motor and General Stores: assertion in document disproved by actual turn of events or conduct of Parties]
📝 Double whammy = you pay GST on bookings AND you lose ITC already claimed. Reversing ITC does not solve the problem if GST is also due on new bookings — you end up paying both.
17.3.4 — Non-RERA Project ⇧ Index
🏗️ Non-RERA Project — GST Still Applies
REFERENCE — RERA Sec 3(2)
Projects that do not require RERA registration = those covered by Section 3(2) of RERA. GST treatment applicable to a 'Project' will apply to these too which are 'for sale' Projects.
📝 Just because a project is too small for RERA registration (below prescribed limits) does not mean it escapes GST. If it is a 'for sale' project, GST applies equally.
⚠️ ARTIFICIAL BIFURCATION — SHAM TRANSACTION
Artificial bifurcation of sales where (i) land is sold to Customer AND (ii) construction agreement is with Customer's spouse or close relative → guidance from Motor and General Stores and other terms — expose the two documents to comprise an indivisible arrangement.

'Testing composite documents' equally applies when Parties assert independent contracts bearing all indicia of a single composite and conjoint arrangement.
📝 Common tax planning: sell land to buyer, give separate construction contract to buyer's wife/relative — to split the transaction and escape GST. Courts (and the book) hold this is a sham — both documents together form one composite supply of construction, fully taxable under GST.
17.3.5 — Self-Development ⇧ Index
🏠 Self-Development — No Output Tax Liability
RULE
Construction for self does NOT attract any output tax liability. Credit will be restricted due to Section 17(5)(c) and (d) of Central GST Act.
📝 If you build for yourself (not for sale) — no GST output liability. But you also cannot claim ITC on inputs used for such construction — blocked credit under Section 17(5)(c)/(d). A trade-off.
⚠️ CO-OWNERS — SPLIT BILLING SHAM
Co-owners of joint family property engaging in self-development may instruct contractor to issue invoices to each co-owner separately.
Transfer of construction works by contractor is indivisible — fractional transfer of property in works is NOT possible in all aspects.
There will always be indivisible components → renders 'split billing' a sham → attracts alternate consequences in law.
📝 You cannot split a single construction contract into parts by issuing separate invoices to 5 co-owners. The construction of the building is one indivisible act. The split-billing arrangement will be treated as a sham and the real transaction will be taxed.
CO-OWNER AS DEVELOPER
One of co-owners will be the Developer in (i) self-development qua own share AND (ii) joint-development with all others qua their share — contractor operating as sub-contractor to this Developer (refer Section 202, Indian Contract Act on agency)
📝 The co-owner who manages the development is the 'Developer' — both for his own portion (self-development) and for others' portions (joint-development as agent). The contractor bills the Developer, who is principal. Others' invoices do not make them separate principals.
17.3.6 — Industrial Units ⇧ Index
🏭 Industrial Units — RERA & GST Rate Treatment
SCOPE
Unless other laws occupy this subject — RERA continues to apply. 'Apartment' covers shops, offices, godowns, etc.
Residential and commercial development in RREP → special dispensation under new rate regime.
Commercial and industrial development → continues to operate under old rate regime.
DEVELOPMENT RIGHTS — KEY DISTINCTIONS
• Reverse charge liability on development rights → only to 'for sale' projects
• Exemption from tax on development rights on 'residential' projects only
• All commercial and industrial development → eligible for ITC → will NOT avail any exemption from tax on development rights
• Where development is 'not for sale' → exemption applicable to 'for sale' development and its deferment until completion → will NOT be applicable
📝 Industrial/commercial development is fully in the ITC regime — no preferential treatment. The special new-rate regime benefits (1%/5% + no ITC) and the development rights exemption apply only to residential 'for sale' projects.
17.3.7 — Joint Development ⇧ Index
🤝 Joint Development — Supply A + Supply B
STRUCTURE
Developer does NOT invest in purchasing land — enters arrangement with Landowner to jointly develop a 'project'.
Developer secures interest (but not title) in the immovable property — under a registered instrument (Sec 17 Registration Act) — and undertakes development.
Both Developer and Landowner = designated as 'Promoter' for RERA purposes.
📝 Developer gets a right to develop (not ownership) — registered JDA. Both parties become Promoters under RERA — sharing obligations to buyers. Consideration to Landowner = sharing of area or revenue from constructed units.
Landowner
Supply A →
Development Rights
→
← Supply B
Construction Services
←
Developer
GST ON JDA
Whether project is 'for sale' (RERA applicable) or 'not-for-sale' (RERA not applicable) — in a joint development arrangement, the transaction inter se will be liable to GST:

Supply A: Landowner → Developer = Development rights (taxable with deferment until completion for 'for sale' residential projects)
Supply B: Developer → Landowner = Construction services (taxable — Landowner's share of constructed units)
📝 Both legs of the JDA are taxable. Landowner pays GST on transfer of development rights. Developer pays GST on construction services provided to Landowner (for Landowner's share of flats). The deferment mechanism applies only to residential 'for sale' projects — taxed at time of completion or first booking, whichever earlier.
17.3.8 — Redevelopment ⇧ Index
🔄 Redevelopment — GST Treatment
CONCEPT
Redevelopment by current owners = self-development (discussed at 17.3.5).
Redevelopment where increased FSI is the reason → Joint-development arrangement arises: current owners 'exchange' their units → Developer acquires interest in additional FSI (+ TDRCs purchased, if any).
📝 Redevelopment adds FSI (Floor Space Index) — allows building a bigger building. Old residents give up their old flat, Developer builds new bigger building, old residents get back a new flat + extras. Developer keeps additional units to sell.
Consideration to Current Owners (aggregate of):
(a) New constructed units
(b) Cost of relocation until completion
(c) Incremental size of units allocated
(d) Additional constructed units allocated
(e) Any cash consideration
GST TREATMENT TO DEVELOPER:
(a) Tax on supply of development rights — subject to limited exemption
(b) Tax on supply of construction area involved in the exchange
(c) Tax on sale of constructed units to new Buyers
CURRENT OWNERS — GST NIL
Current owners will NOT be liable to any GST unless additional units received in the exchange are offered for sale before completion of Project.
📝 Old residents getting their new flat back in exchange for their old flat = no GST for them. GST only if they start selling their newly received additional units before the project is complete (becoming Promoters themselves).
CASE LAW Adityaraj Builders v. State of Maharashtra WP 4575/2022 (Bom.) — Permanent Alternate Allotment Agreement providing legal standing to current owners to secure absolute title to new apartment unit and undivided interest in common area and land = affirmed NOT to attract stamp duty again. But does NOT apply to additional units allotted along with additional consideration from Developer.
17.3.9 — Approved Resolution Plan (IBC) ⇧ Index
⚖️ Approved Resolution Plan — RERA + IBC Interface with GST
STRUCTURE
Resolution Applicant permitted to offer plan to acquire a delinquent project under Resolution Plan for approval by Committee of Creditors under IBC.
Once approved → Resolution Applicant steps into shoes of Corporate Debtor in respect of:
• All joint-development interests in any immovable property belonging to other Landowners as Developer
• In respect of any owned land included in beleaguered Project
📝 Resolution Applicant = takes over the insolvent developer's role completely. It is as if the Resolution Applicant had originally entered into all the contracts from the beginning — subject only to variations approved in the Resolution Plan.
Variations to be Addressed (with Tax Treatment):
(a) Cash injected into Project to repay loans
(b) Treatment of 'haircut' in outstanding loans — (i) loans assumed after reduction OR (ii) loans reduced after agreeing to assume them
(c) Cost of assumed obligations WITHOUT additional consideration payable by Buyers in the Project
⚠️ TIME OF SUPPLY — DO NOT ACCELERATE
Obligations assumed under a Resolution Plan = NOT consideration received in advance.
NCLT direction to comply with terms approved by CoC → obligations to be complied sometime in the future (not instantaneously on date of approval).
Terms may or may not eventually be fulfilled → or modified by Creditors.
Care must be taken: do NOT accelerate 'time of supply' to date of approval of Resolution Plan — time of supply = date of compliance with obligation when Creditor grants 'discharge' to Resolution Applicant.
📝 GST liability does not arise on the date the NCLT approves the Resolution Plan. It arises only when each specific obligation is actually discharged/performed. Accelerating the time of supply to the approval date would be legally incorrect and may lead to paying GST prematurely on obligations that may never be performed.
PRINCIPLE
RERA and IBC — both being welfare matters' legislations — cannot dictate 'time of supply' in GST which is a private matters' legislation. GST Act provisions on taxing ingredients have full play as mandated by Legislature.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 17 | RERA ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision/authority.
RERA — Limited Borrowing by GST | Purposive vs Strict Construction
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.1 | Applicable in all RERA-GST intersection disputes
Key Principle
RERA, being a welfare legislation, is subject to purposive construction. Declaration of law under RERA does not alter the strict construction application to terms used in GST once it is shown that they come within the operation of the statutory definition. Expressions that have been legislated by reference must be located in the two baskets to know whether their amendment or declaration must be accommodated in GST too. GST has relied upon RERA only to select certain definitions and use them for purposes of prescribing tariff rates — reliance is no more than that.
When to Use — When Department or assessee cites a Court's RERA interpretation to argue for a different GST treatment. Counter: purposive construction of RERA does NOT carry over to GST which demands strict construction. Also use when arguing RERA amendment does not automatically change GST meaning (borrowed vs recited basket argument).
Land ≠ Plot — Schedule III Exclusion Only After Completion
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.2.3 | Para 5(b) Schedule II & Para 5 Schedule III CGST Act
Key Principle
Land cannot be equated with plot. Sale of land is excluded from GST by Schedule III and so is sale of plot but only after completion of development works. This is true of a building which is also excluded from GST by Schedule III but only after completion of its own development works, which is evident in the qualifying words "subject to paragraph 5(b) to Schedule II". Conversion of land into plots is not beyond the definition of 'project'. Construction is involved in development works. Price paid is not for plot but for all elements involved in purchase.
When to Use — When assessee argues sale of plotted development is sale of 'land' (Schedule III) and not liable to GST. Counter: land ≠ plot; plot emerges only after development works; price includes construction elements; Schedule III exclusion available only after completion. Also use when Department challenges 1/3 abatement on plot sales.
Manishbhai Munjaal Bhatt — Abatement Rule (Gujarat HC)
Gujarat High Court | Ibid. | Chapter 17.2.3 | Statutory abatement of 1/3 for land value
Key Principle
One-third abatement is allowed only when 'actual value' attributable to land is unknown. Where actual value of land is not ascertainable or is unreliable, statutory abatement (one-third) will be unquestionable by Revenue authorities. Use of actual value by specifying a certain value greater than one-third in the agreement is not free from challenge due to interlinkage with agreement for construction and the inherent advantageous tax consequence that follows.
When to Use — When Revenue challenges the 1/3 statutory abatement or when assessee specifies inflated land values in the agreement. Revenue cannot challenge 1/3 abatement unless land value is ascertainable by independent means. Conversely, if assessee has specified higher land value (say 40%) in the sale agreement — Revenue can probe the interlinkage with the construction agreement.
Apartment = Smallest Saleable Unit | Works Contract Covers Common Areas
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.2.2 & 17.2.4 | RERA Sec 2(e) | CGST Sec 2(119)
Key Principle
'Apartment' refers to 'smallest saleable unit in a project' — this may include office, residence, shop, godown or even a plot. Reference to the words "contract of immovable property wherein transfer of property in goods is involved in the execution of such contract" appearing in Section 2(119) of Central GST Act cannot be limited to constructed area in respect of which Buyer has absolute title AND must exclude constructed common areas in respect of which Buyer has non-exclusive rights. It would be disharmonious with this new law to hold that property in those goods has vanished or vapourized when they continue to exist physically in the common areas.
When to Use — When Department argues GST on works contract should exclude common area construction (since buyer has no exclusive title). Counter: Works contract covers the entire project including common areas — goods in common areas do not vanish. Also use to establish that 'apartment' in a mixed development includes commercial units too, not just residential.
Illegal Construction — GST Inescapable | Completion Certificate Not Conclusive
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.3.2 | Para 5(b) Schedule II & Para 5 Schedule III CGST Act
Key Principle
If units constructed are sold, GST treatment is inescapable, whether those units are illegal or merely irregular. Denial of completion certificate by 'competent authority' does not eclipse operation of paragraph 5(b), Schedule II to Central GST Act. Incidence of GST will end once project reaches completion. Certificate from competent authority is only evidence of completion. If recommended evidence is unavailable, this does not imply corroborative evidence is inadmissible. Proof of "first occupation" brings an end to incidence of tax on new bookings after that date and attracts the exclusion allowed in paragraph 5, Schedule III to Central GST Act.
When to Use — (a) When assessee argues that since construction is illegal (no completion certificate), there is no 'supply' and no GST liability. Counter: GST is inescapable on sale of units regardless of legality. (b) When assessee argues completion certificate is not available (illegal building) and therefore Schedule III exclusion does not apply. Counter: 'First occupation' is alternative proof of completion — completion certificate is only evidence, not conclusive.
Pseudo Completion — ITC Flow as Contra-Indicator | Motor & General Stores
Apex Court — Motor and General Stores (ibid) | Chapter 17.3.3 | GSTR-2B Analysis
Key Principle
Flow of input tax credits in GSTR-2B reports is a contra-indicator of assertion about completion. Where inward supplies are substantial, the exclusion allowed by paragraph 5, Schedule III will evade and "first occupation" referred in paragraph 5(b), Schedule II will also be denied. Bookings after this 'pseudo' completion date will also attract GST and mere reversal of input tax credit on these units will not suffice — reversal of credit would entail double whammy. As per Motor and General Stores: any assertion in a document is disproved by actual turn of events or conduct of Parties — facts will flow from those events or conduct and not the document.
When to Use — When assessee claims completion certificate was obtained and tries to apply Schedule III exclusion to subsequent bookings — while GSTR-2B still shows substantial ITC flowing in. Use GSTR-2B data as contra-indicator to disprove the completion assertion. Also use in adjudication to argue that where completion certificate is manipulated to avoid GST on new bookings, the facts (continuing ITC, ongoing construction) override the document.
Artificial Bifurcation / Split Billing — Sham Transactions
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.3.4 & 17.3.5 | Motor and General Stores (Apex Court)
Key Principle
Artificial bifurcation of sales where land is sold to Customer and construction agreement is with Customer's spouse or close relative — guidance from Motor and General Stores and other terms expose these two documents to comprise an indivisible arrangement. 'Testing composite documents' equally applies when Parties make assertion about independent contracts which bear all the indicia of a single composite and conjoint arrangement. Transfer of construction works carried out by contractor are indivisible — fractional transfer of property in works carried out is not possible in all aspects. There will always be some indivisible components that renders 'split billing' a sham.
When to Use — (a) When developer separates land sale and construction agreement to escape GST (selling land to buyer, construction contract to buyer's relative). Cite to establish composite and conjoint nature of indivisible arrangement. (b) When co-owners try to split contractor invoices to escape GST on self-development. Cite to show split billing is a sham — indivisible components remain.
Resolution Plan — Time of Supply NOT Accelerated to Approval Date
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.3.9 | IBC + RERA + CGST Act Interface
Key Principle
Obligations assumed under a Resolution Plan is not consideration received in advance for the reason that the direction of NCLT to comply with the terms approved by CoC are obligations to be complied sometime in the future — which may or may not eventually be fulfilled. Care must be taken not to accelerate 'time of supply' to the date of approval of Resolution Plan but to the date of compliance with obligation when Creditor would grant 'discharge' to Resolution Applicant. RERA and IBC, both being welfare matters' legislations, cannot dictate 'time of supply' in GST which is a private matters' legislation. GST Act provisions on taxing ingredients have full play as mandated by Legislature.
When to Use — When Department raises a demand that GST is payable on the date of NCLT approval of Resolution Plan. Counter: obligations under Resolution Plan are future commitments — time of supply arises only when each obligation is actually discharged/performed. RERA/IBC cannot dictate GST's time of supply. Use to push back demands for GST on 'haircut' or 'cash injection' at the time of approval rather than at actual compliance.
RREP — 15% Commercial Threshold | Fait Accompli Warning
Legal Principle | Chapter 17.3.1 | New Rate Regime — Notification 11/2017-CT(Rate) as amended
Key Principle
RREP is an invention of GST law with no corresponding expression in RERA. It is a mix-use project where commercial units form part of residential development. Determination of 15 per cent is a question of fact best ascertained on completion although tax treatment adopted may be based on approvals. No fait accompli follows perils of short payment, if any, of taxes due as per approvals if actual construction does not satisfy this condition — the 15 per cent threshold.
When to Use — (a) When assessee claims RREP rate (5%) but commercial area exceeds 15% on completion. Revenue's position: actual construction governs, not approved plans — short payment of tax is recoverable. (b) When assessee argues RREP definition from RERA — counter: RREP has no RERA source; it is purely GST's own invention and must be interpreted strictly per GST notifications.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 18 — Carrier Laws

🚛 Chapter 18 — Carrier Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Wrong
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
18.1 Relevance to GST
18.2.1 Agent — Who is a Carrier?
18.2.2 Mode of Transport
18.2.3 Forwarding Note — GTA Trigger
18.2.4 Reverse Charge Liability
18.3.1 Delivery v. Transport
18.3.2 Movement v. Journey
18.3.3 Interception of Conveyance
18.3.4 Complicit in Illegal Transport
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
18.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🚛 Carrier Laws — Relevance to GST
Carriage by Road Act 2007 | GTA | Composite vs Mixed Supply
Who is a Carrier?
✓ CARRIER IS
Agent of owner of goods
May be appointed by Consignor OR Consignee
Object of contract = ferry goods from Point A to Point B
= Transportation simplicitor
📝 A carrier's one and only job is moving goods from one place to another. If the object of the contract is anything more than this — it may not be a carrier at all.
✗ CARRIER IS NOT
Seller offering to transport their own goods
— Offering to transport does NOT convert Seller into Carrier
📝 If a seller delivers their own sold goods to the buyer's place, the seller is NOT acting as a carrier. Their contract is for sale, not for transportation.
💡 Eg.591: ECOM operator delivering purchased goods = NOT transporter — value-added services extend beyond transportation.
Eg.592: LPG dealership delivering cylinders = NOT transporter even on door-delivery terms.
Eg.593: Courier service = IS a transporter — entitled to transport time-sensitive goods door-to-door.
Nature of Carrier's Engagement — Two Modes
MODE 1
Time Basis
Carrier engaged for a period of time
📝 You pay the carrier for their availability over a duration — whether or not goods move. E.g., a truck hired on monthly contract.
MODE 2
Activity (Trip) Basis
Carrier engaged per trip / per consignment
📝 You pay the carrier per delivery trip. GST tariff classification and RCM liability are directly affected by which mode applies.
⚠️ COMPOSITE vs MIXED SUPPLY — CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Value-added services by carrier must be incidental to transportation to be 'composite supply'
(Transportation = principal supply)

Value-added services cannot be substantive — if substantive, transaction becomes 'mixed supply' (taxed at highest rate)
📝 A carrier can offer loading, packing, insurance as incidental services — that's composite supply (taxed at GTA rate). But if carrier also provides warehousing, labelling, e-commerce logistics as main services — it becomes mixed supply taxed at the highest applicable rate.
NATURE OF CARRIER LAWS
Welfare matters legislations → exposed to purposive construction
BUT mandatory requirements in these laws prevail
Any contractual arrangement to bypass these requirements → will fall foul with GST treatment
Legal fiction of 'distinct persons' in GST must be overlaid with mandatory requirements of Carrier law
📝 Carrier laws protect the public interest. You cannot contract out of them. If your transport arrangement tries to bypass these mandatory requirements (e.g., avoid GTA classification), GST will still apply as if those requirements were met — because they are deemed to exist by law.
18.2.1 — Agent: Independent Carrier vs GST Intermediary ⇧ Index
🔍 Carrier as 'Agent' — What This Means in GST
✓ CARRIER IS
'Agent' of the goods owner
(carries goods on behalf of owner)
📝 Carrier acts as agent in the sense that it represents the owner's interest in getting goods safely transported. But this 'agency' has a very specific and limited meaning.
✗ CARRIER IS NOT
'Intermediary' as understood in GST
(Para 3, Schedule I, Central GST Act)
📝 In GST, 'intermediary' means someone who arranges supply between two parties — like a broker or agent earning commission. A carrier is NOT this. A carrier supplies transportation directly.
✗ NOT ANSWERABLE TO
Tax treatment in Para 3, Schedule I
(principal-agent supply)
OR
Compulsory registration u/s 24(vii) CGST Act
(agents of taxable supply)
📝 Section 24(vii) requires compulsory registration for persons making supply on behalf of others (agents). A carrier does NOT supply goods on behalf of the owner — it only transports them. So carrier does not need compulsory registration just because of that provision.
PAYER OF REMUNERATION ≠ DETERMINATIVE OF PRINCIPAL
Who pays the carrier is NOT determinative of who the Principal is
Duty of carrier is determinative of nature of services supplied
📝 Even if the Buyer pays freight (as in CIF contracts), the carrier's principal can still be the Seller who appointed the carrier. The payment does not decide the relationship — the duty (what the carrier is obligated to do under the contract) decides it.
⚠️ KEY TEST
If contractual obligations (duties) are of anything but transportation — or many things IN ADDITION to transportation → must determine the object of contract without assuming it is transport of goods (even if transport is one of the duties)
📝 Just because a contract involves transportation does not mean the contract IS a transportation contract. Look at the main purpose — if warehousing, e-commerce fulfillment, or logistics management is the main service, the GST treatment changes entirely.
18.2.2 — Mode of Transport ⇧ Index
🚢✈️🚂🚛 Mode of Transport — Multi-Modal Transport
FREE CHOICE
Carrier may freely choose mode of transport
OBLIGATED MODE
Carrier may be obligated to transport via specific mode under contract
MULTI-MODAL
Multi-modal transport (MMT) = more than one mode of transport
Governed by Multi-modal Transportation of Goods Act, 1993
📝 A single consignment moving by truck, then ship, then truck again = MMT. One contract, multiple transport modes, one carrier responsible throughout.
18.2.3 — Forwarding Note: The GTA Trigger ⇧ Index
📄 Forwarding Note — Mandatory Document & GTA Trigger
Carriage by Road Act 2007 (effective 20 Jul 2010) | Section 8
Forwarding Note must contain — 4 Mandatory Particulars
(i) Identity of goods
(ii) Identity of Parties
(Consignor & Consignee)
(iii) Origin & destination of journey
(iv) Extent of risks assumed
Common Names — By Mode of Transport
ROAD
Lorry Receipt (LR)
SEA
Bill of Lading
AIR
Airway Bill
RAIL
Railway Receipt
LEGAL CHARACTER — DOCUMENT OF TITLE
Forwarding note is admitted in law as 'document of title'
Transfer of title is given effect when titleholder makes endorsement on the forwarding note in favour of any person
📝 This is very important for GST place of supply and time of supply. While goods are in transit, title can be transferred merely by endorsing the bill of lading / LR in favour of a new buyer. The new buyer becomes the owner while goods are still at sea or in transit.
💡 Goods transported by sea from Mumbai to Rotterdam. While on 'high seas', Indian exporter endorses the Bill of Lading to a Singapore buyer. Singapore buyer is now the owner and presents the Bill of Lading at Rotterdam port. This is a valid title transfer — no physical possession was needed.
SECTION 8 — CARRIAGE BY ROAD ACT, 2007
(1) Every consignor shall execute a goods forwarding note in prescribed form & manner — including declaration about value of consignment and goods of dangerous/hazardous nature.
(2) Consignor is responsible for correctness of particulars furnished in the goods forwarding note.
(3) Consignor shall indemnify the common carrier against any damage suffered by reason of incorrectness or incompleteness of particulars.
📝 Section 8 is mandatory — every consignor must issue a forwarding note whenever a common carrier is appointed. This is not optional. The forwarding note obligation exists by statute — not by choice of the carrier or consignor.
⚠️ AUTO GTA TRIGGER
When a 'common carrier' is appointed → forwarding note is implicitly and mandatorily issued (by statute)
→ Transporter cannot escape being a GTA (Goods Transport Agency)

GTA definition (Entry 9 & 11 of 11/2017-CT(Rate) dated 28 Jun 2017):
"goods transport agency means any person who provides service in relation to transport of goods by road and issues consignment note, by whatever name called."
📝 The KEY word is 'issues consignment note.' Since Section 8 of Carriage by Road Act makes issuance of forwarding note MANDATORY when a common carrier is appointed — the transporter automatically becomes a GTA by operation of law, regardless of what they call themselves or try to structure their contract as.
💡 A transporter tries to avoid GTA classification by structuring the contract as 'logistics management service' without explicitly issuing a consignment note. When Carriage by Road Act mandates the forwarding note — the transporter is still a GTA and GTA-RCM still applies. The structuring attempt fails.
18.2.4 — Reverse Charge Liability ⇧ Index
💰 Reverse Charge on GTA — The Legal Chain
ATTEMPT TO AVOID GST
Omitting to issue forwarding note is thought to be an ingenious way to overcome GTA-RCM liability
— especially between distinct persons or related persons
📝 Some taxpayers think: "If I don't issue an LR/consignment note, I am not a GTA, so there is no RCM." This reasoning is flawed — the statute imposes the obligation to issue the forwarding note.
THIS DOES NOT WORK
When transporter is implicitly a GTA (fact furnished by statute) → person liable to pay for transportation is necessarily liable to discharge GTA-RCM tax
Where applicable
📝 Statute says the forwarding note MUST be issued. So in law, it IS issued even if physically not prepared. Therefore GTA obligation exists. Therefore RCM applies. No document = no escape.
LEGAL CHAIN — HOW RCM FLOWS
Common carrier appointed
→ Section 8 (Carriage by Road Act) mandates forwarding note
→ Transporter is GTA (by statutory definition)
→ Person paying for transportation = liable for RCM on GTA services
→ Cannot be avoided by omitting to issue forwarding note
💡 Company A sends goods from factory to warehouse via a truck. Truck is a 'common carrier.' No LR is issued. Company A thinks there is no GTA-RCM. Wrong — Carriage by Road Act mandates the forwarding note. Transporter is a GTA by law. RCM liability exists for Company A.
18.3.1 — Delivery v. Transport ⇧ Index
📦 Delivery v. Transport — Critical Legal Distinction for GST Place of Supply
TRANSPORTATION
Goods put into transportation = when handed over to common carrier for purpose of carrying to destination
Goods remain 'in transportation' until handed over to intended Consignee at specified destination
📝 The moment you hand goods to a carrier = transportation has begun. It does not end until the carrier hands over to the recipient at the destination.
DELIVERY
Expression of great legal significance — cannot be furnished by visual observation of state of goods or extent of completion of transportation
Delivery = when common carrier is discharged of obligations
📝 Delivery is NOT when the truck arrives. Delivery is the legal act of the carrier handing over and being discharged of responsibility. Until that moment, the carrier is accountable for any shortage or loss.
Section 10 IGST Act — Four Expressions using 'Movement' and 'Delivery'
§10(1)(a)
'Movement' of goods
= location where movement 'terminates' for delivery
§10(1)(b)
'Principal place of business' of third party who issues instructions for their 'delivery' to person who is not the buyer
§10(1)(c)
'Location' of goods at time of their 'delivery' to recipient
§10(1)(d)
'Place' of installation or assembly of goods
⚠️ IMPORTANT DISTINCTION
'Delivery to recipient' in §10(1)(c) refers to Supplier's obligations (under contract of supply) towards Recipient of supply
Movement is NOT the same as Delivery
Must identify which expression is used in each context — legal connotation or visual observation?
📝 Movement = physical/legal transportation (carrier's world). Delivery = discharge of Supplier's obligation to Recipient (supply contract's world). These are two different legal concepts operating in two different contexts — confusing them leads to wrong place of supply determination.
💡 Supplier in Mumbai sells to Buyer in Delhi. Goods handed to carrier in Mumbai = movement starts (for carrier's purposes). Goods reach Delhi and carrier hands over = delivery to recipient (for GST place of supply purposes). Place of supply = Delhi (where movement terminates for delivery) under §10(1)(a).
18.3.2 — Movement v. Journey ⇧ Index
🛣️ Movement v. Journey — Legal Transportation vs Physical Travel
JOURNEY
Physical travel undertaken by the conveyance
📝 Journey = truck physically moving on road. Stops at dhaba = journey interrupted. Breakdown = journey interrupted.
MOVEMENT
Legal transportation of goods
Commences: when goods handed over to common carrier
Continues: until carrier hands over to Consignee (or holder of title)
= Legally concluded when common carrier hands over
📝 Movement = legal status of goods. Even if the truck is stopped for a check, the goods are legally 'in movement.' Movement continues uninterrupted — legally — through stops, inspections, and breaks.
✓ EVENTS THAT DO NOT INTERRUPT MOVEMENT
• Stoppage in transit for rest and refreshment
• Inspection at statutory check-points
• Change of conveyance with necessary permits to enter certain States
• Break-down of vehicle

In all these cases — journey may or may not continue, but legally, movement continues uninterrupted.
📝 This is critical for the e-way bill validity period under GST. The clock for e-way bill validity runs on movement, not physical journey. If goods are stuck at a checkpost due to inspection — movement is legally uninterrupted — e-way bill validity should not expire due to this stoppage.
💡 Truck carrying goods from Pune to Delhi stops for 12 hours at a toll plaza due to police inspection. E-way bill is valid for 1 day (24 hours). The 12-hour detention at checkpost should NOT count against e-way bill validity — movement is legally uninterrupted during statutory detention.
18.3.3 — Interception of Conveyance ⇧ Index
🚨 Interception of Conveyance — GST Penalty under Section 129
INSPECTION DOES NOT END MOVEMENT
Goods mandated to be accompanied by documents
Interception for inspection ≠ end of movement
Detention by order due to infraction of law = interrupts movement
Movement resumes on grant of release order
📝 Routine inspection checkpoint = movement continues. But if GST officer detains goods by order because documents are wrong or goods appear to be tax-evaded = movement legally interrupted until release order is passed.
OFFENCE ON CONSIGNMENT vs CONVEYANCE — INDEPENDENT
GST offence involving consignment is independent of offence involving conveyance
To attract penalty u/s 129 CGST Act:
Suffice that consignment is found wanting on correctness & completeness of documentation (Rule 138A)
📝 The consignment (goods) is penalised separately from the vehicle (conveyance). You can penalise the goods without penalising the carrier. Carrier's vehicle is released separately.
PENALTY ON CONVEYANCE — LIMITS
No penalty can be imposed on the conveyance under Section 129
Section 129 requires common carrier to be complicit in commission of offence
Conveyance is released on discharge of lower of:
(i) Penalty on consignment, OR
(ii) ₹1 lakh
📝 The carrier's truck is not penalised just because the goods on it are improperly documented. To penalise the truck/conveyance, the officer must prove the carrier was complicit (knew about and participated in the tax evasion). Paying ₹1 lakh (or penalty on consignment, whichever is lower) releases the truck without proving complicity.
💡 A truck carries goods with wrong HSN code in e-way bill. GST officer intercepts — consignment penalty = ₹5 lakh. To release the truck, carrier pays lower of ₹5 lakh or ₹1 lakh = ₹1 lakh. Truck released. Carrier has not admitted complicity.
✓ RISKS OF CARRIER EXCUSED
Risks of common carrier are excused once goods are no longer in movement due to supervening action of statutory authorities
📝 If the government seizes/detains the goods — carrier is no longer responsible for the goods from that point. The statutory intervention breaks the carrier's chain of responsibility.
18.3.4 — Complicit in Illegal Transport ⇧ Index
⚠️ Complicit in Illegal Transport — What Makes Carrier Liable?
To Make Common Carrier Complicit — Complex Ingredients Required
INGREDIENT 1
Knowledge beyond ordinary duty to collect information about consignment
📝 Carrier must have known about tax evasion — beyond what any reasonable carrier would know from basic documentation.
INGREDIENT 2
Directions to transport without 'any' documentation = incompatible with carrier's innocence
📝 If someone tells the carrier 'transport this, no papers needed' — that is a red flag. A carrier who proceeds anyway cannot claim innocence.
Boundary of Common Carrier's Due Diligence
✓ WITHIN CARRIER'S DUTY
Availability of 'basic' documentation (Rule 138A CGST Act)
Payment or assurance of hire charges
Clarity as to nature of consignment (not contraband / hazardous)
✗ BEYOND CARRIER'S DUTY
Investigating Consignor's compliance with GST
Verifying correctness of tax treatment of goods
Checking whether HSN / GST rate applied is correct
📝 A truck driver cannot be expected to verify whether the goods he carries are correctly classified under GST. That goes beyond any reasonable duty of a carrier.
IF CARRIER IS COMPLICIT — CONSEQUENCES
Independent of actionable cause against Consignor or Consignee →
• Power of detention or seizure of consignment
• Carrier is liable to detention or seizure of the conveyance

Confiscation of conveyance u/s 130 CGST Act ≠ double jeopardy when consignment is also liable to confiscation

Without rigours of notice + adjudication + appeal:
Carrier may discharge penalty of ₹1 lakh (or penalty on consignment, if lower) and release conveyance under proviso to Section 129(6) CGST Act

This penalty is in respect of conveyance — NOT the consignment. Penalty on conveyance is determined based on penalty applicable to consignment (or ₹1 lakh, whichever is lower).
📝 Even if the carrier is found complicit — the law gives an escape route: pay ₹1 lakh (or the consignment penalty if lower) and get the truck released. The carrier does not have to go through the full adjudication process. This proviso to Section 129(6) is the carrier's get-out-of-detention card.
💡 Truck carrying goods where the supplier has committed GST fraud. Officer intercepts and seizes both goods and truck. If truck owner pays ₹1 lakh under Section 129(6) proviso, truck is released immediately — without waiting for final adjudication of the goods case which may take months/years.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 18 | Carrier Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision name.
GTA Auto-Trigger — Carriage by Road Act, 2007 (Section 8) + GTA Definition
Statutory Provision | Carriage by Road Act 2007 (effective 20 Jul 2010) | Read with Entry 9 & 11 of 11/2017-CT(Rate) dated 28 Jun 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
It is implicit when a 'common carrier' is appointed that goods being transported are covered by a 'forwarding note'. And when this fact is furnished by statute, transporter cannot escape being a GTA. GST applies on reverse charge basis in case of services of goods transport agency. Omitting to issue such a forwarding note is not an ingenious way of overcoming GST liability — when it is implicit that the transporter is a GTA and this fact is furnished by statute, person liable to pay for transportation is necessarily liable to discharge tax on reverse charge basis, where applicable.
When to Use — When GTA-RCM is disputed on the ground that no consignment note was issued. Argue: Section 8 of Carriage by Road Act mandates forwarding note whenever a common carrier is appointed. The statutory obligation means the consignment note is deemed to have been issued. Transporter IS a GTA. RCM applies.
Carrier = Agent, NOT Intermediary — Section 24(vii) CGST Act
Statutory Principle | CGST Act | Para 18.2.1
Key Principle
Independent carriers are 'agents' but not 'intermediaries' as understood in GST. Carriers are not answerable to the tax treatment in paragraph 3, Schedule I to Central GST Act. Similarly, carriers are not answerable to compulsory registration under section 24(vii) of Central GST Act. Payer of remuneration to carrier is not determinative of the Principal who the Agent represents. Duty of carrier is determinative of nature of services supplied.
When to Use — When Department argues that a carrier is an 'intermediary' (attracting place of supply of intermediary services) or that a carrier must compulsorily register under Section 24(vii). Counter: carriers are not intermediaries in GST sense. They supply transportation directly. Duty of carrier — not who pays — determines the nature of service.
Composite Supply vs Mixed Supply — Carrier's Value-Added Services
GST Principle | CGST Act | Para 18.1
Key Principle
Value added services of a carrier must be incidental to be 'composite supply' with transportation being the principal supply. These value-added services cannot be substantive as substantive services run the risk of rendering the transaction to be regarded as 'mixed supply'. A carrier is one whose object of contract is to ferry goods from point A to point B — transportation simplicitor. Offering to transport goods by Seller of those goods does not convert the Seller into a Carrier.
When to Use — When carrier includes loading, packing, insurance and Department treats it as mixed supply taxed at highest rate. Counter: if transportation is the principal supply and other services are incidental — composite supply, taxed at GTA rate. Also use to distinguish ECOM/LPG dealer from carrier (Eg. 591, 592).
Movement vs Delivery — IGST Act Section 10 Place of Supply
Statutory Principle | Section 10 IGST Act | Para 18.3.1
Key Principle
'Delivery' is an expression of great legal significance and not one that can be furnished by visual observation of the state of the goods or the extent of completion of their transportation. Delivery is when and where the common carrier is discharged of obligations. 'Delivery to recipient' in Section 10(1)(c) refers to Supplier's obligations towards Recipient of supply. Movement is not the same as delivery. It is important to identify which expression is used in each context and whether that expression is one of legal connotation or of visual observation. Goods continue to be 'in transportation' until they are handed over to the intended Consignee at the specified destination.
When to Use — In place of supply disputes under Section 10 IGST Act where Department argues place of supply based on physical location of goods during transit. Counter: movement terminates at delivery point (legal discharge of carrier's obligation) — not at an intermediate inspection point or godown.
Movement Continues During Statutory Stoppage — E-Way Bill Validity
Legal Principle | Carriage by Road Act 2007 | Para 18.3.2 & 18.3.3
Key Principle
Journey is physical travel undertaken. Movement is legal transportation. Goods commence movement once handed over to a common carrier and continue to remain in movement until their delivery is legally concluded when carrier hands over to Consignee. Stoppage in transit for rest and refreshment or for inspection at statutory check-points or for change of conveyance or due to break-down — none of these instances stops or interrupts movement of goods. Where there is stoppage of movement for these reasons, the journey may or may not continue but legally, their movement continues uninterrupted. Movement is interrupted only by order of law enforcement due to infraction — and resumes on grant of release order.
When to Use — When Department argues e-way bill has expired because goods were stationary for hours at a checkpost or during detention. Counter: legal movement is uninterrupted during statutory detention/inspection. E-way bill validity should not be counted during statutory detention period. Also use in transit loss insurance disputes.
Carrier Not Complicit — Due Diligence Boundary | Section 129 CGST Act
Statutory Principle | Section 129 & 130 CGST Act | Para 18.3.4
Key Principle
To implicate a common carrier to be complicit in the commission of offence involving evasion of tax on the consignment being transported requires a complex array of ingredients to be established. Foremost is knowledge beyond ordinary duty to collect information about consignment. Availability of 'basic' documentation (Rule 138A), payment or assurance of hire charges and clarity as to nature of consignment (not being contraband or hazardous) would be the boundaries of common carrier's due diligence. Expecting the carrier to investigate the Consignor's compliance with GST or other Carrier laws would lie beyond these boundaries of common carrier's duties. No penalty can be imposed on the conveyance under section 129 — section 129 requires common carrier to be complicit in commission of said offence. Conveyance is released on discharge of lower of penalty on consignment or ₹1 lakh.
When to Use — When Department seeks to seize and penalise the carrier's vehicle/conveyance along with the consignment. Counter: carrier's due diligence is limited to basic documentation and hire payment. Carrier cannot be expected to investigate GST compliance of consignor. No penalty on conveyance under Section 129 unless carrier is proved complicit. Release of conveyance available under Section 129(6) proviso on payment of ₹1 lakh (or consignment penalty if lower).
Mandatory Requirements of Carrier Laws Override Contractual Arrangements
Legal Principle | Carrier Laws Welfare Legislation | Para 18.1
Key Principle
Carrier laws are welfare matters legislations and for that reason they are exposed to purposive construction but the mandatory requirements in these laws prevail and any contractual arrangement to bypass these requirements will fall foul with the GST treatment applicable. Roles and responsibilities of independent carrier are dictated by statute and freedom to enter into contracts in derogation of the terms in governing statute will be void. Coupled with the legal fiction of 'distinct persons', it becomes necessary to overlay the mandatory requirements in Carrier law to this legal fiction in GST and fill the missing pieces to satisfy those requirements.
When to Use — When assessee or Supplier tries to contract out of GTA classification by creative structuring (e.g., calling the arrangement 'logistics service', 'supply chain management' etc. to avoid GTA-RCM). Counter: mandatory requirements of Carriage by Road Act prevail over contractual labels. The welfare legislation purpose overrides creative structuring. GTA-RCM applies regardless.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 19 — Motor Vehicles Act

🚗 Chapter 19 — Motor Vehicles Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Included
Prohibited / Excluded / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.1 Relevance to GST
19.2.1 Motor Vehicle — Definition & Case Law
19.2.2 Two-Wheeler — Four Tests
19.2.3 Body Building
19.2.4 Ambulance
19.2.5 Refrigerated Van & HSN Tariff Table
19.3.1 Mark of Registration
19.3.2 Section 17(5) — ITC Block
19.3.3 Pre-Owned Vehicles
19.3.4 Para 4(b) — Schedule II Diversion
19.3.5 Taxi Operator
19.3.6 Section 9(5) — ECOM & Taxi Hailing
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🚗 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 — Relevance to GST
Welfare Legislation | Legislation by Reference | ITC Block | Tariff Classification
📝 Motor vehicles are "poorly understood" — even courts have had a checkered history on what counts as a motor vehicle. Since GST tariff classification AND credit restrictions (Section 17(5)) are directly linked to whether something is a "motor vehicle," correctly identifying what falls within that definition is critical for GST.
CHECKERED HISTORY
Identity of motor vehicle made contingent on whether article requires to be assigned a mark of registration — this line of thinking has been overturned
📝 Courts earlier said: if it needs a registration plate, it's a motor vehicle. This is now wrong. Registration is not the test — the statutory definition is the test.
OVERTURNED TO ACCOUNT FOR:
(i) Vehicles for use within bounded premises
(ii) Machinery mounted on mobile skids — not road-ready, no registration
📝 A factory battery vehicle or a crane on skids does not need registration — but may still be a motor vehicle under the statutory definition. Registration requirement is no longer the test.
GST LINK — DUAL RELEVANCE
(i) Tariff classification linked to article being 'motor vehicle'
(ii) Credit restrictions under Section 17(5) linked to 'motor vehicle'
📝 Whether something is a motor vehicle determines: (a) which HSN code it falls under (rates vary 18-28% + cess), and (b) whether ITC on its purchase is blocked. Both consequences are huge — get it wrong and it costs crores.
LEGISLATION BY REFERENCE
MV Act 1988 and Rules are welfare matters' legislations but due to approach of 'legislation by reference' in GST, it becomes important to understand meaning of each article under this law
📝 "Legislation by reference" means GST borrows the definition from MV Act without rewriting it. So MV Act's definition, as interpreted by courts under MV Act, governs GST as well. There is no room for negotiation — the express words demand their conclusion.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE
Where there is a statutory definition made applicable, it is legislation by reference, and there is no room for negotiation or search for reasoning to apply the conclusion that the express words demand.
19.2.1 — Motor Vehicle — Statutory Definition & Case Law ⇧ Index
⚙️ "Motor Vehicle" — Section 2(28) MV Act, 1988
Bose Abraham v. State of Kerala | In re BMW | Golf Carts | Electric Buggies
Section 2(28) — "motor vehicle" or "vehicle" means any mechanically propelled vehicle adapted for use upon roads whether the power of propulsion is transmitted thereto from an external or internal source and includes a chassis to which a body has not been attached and a trailer; but does not include a vehicle running upon fixed rails or a vehicle of a special type adapted for use only in a factory or in any other enclosed premises or a vehicle having less than four wheels fitted with engine capacity of not exceeding twenty-five cubic centimetres.
📚 Key Case Law — Judicial Development of Definition
Bose Abraham v. State of Kerala & Anr.
AIR 2001 SC 835
QUESTION
Whether excavators and road rollers are motor vehicles for entry tax purposes
HC APPROACH (Wrong test)
Guided by: (i) mark of registration required, (ii) cannot operate without driver's license
SC HELD
Affirmed HC but corrected reasoning — these equipment were "suitable for use on roads" albeit to construct the road itself → motor vehicles
📝 The Supreme Court confirmed road rollers/excavators are motor vehicles — but the correct reason is that they are "suitable for use on roads" (even if the road they use is the one they are building). Registration/license is NOT the test.
In re BMW
2006 (193) ELT 138 (AAR)
QUESTION
Whether CKD kit of motor car without seats is still a motor car
HELD
CKD kit of motor car without seats must still be understood as motor cars — as understood in common parlance after carefully balancing divergent decisions in CC v. Maestro Motors Ltd. 2004 (174) ELT 289 (SC)
📝 CKD = Completely Knocked Down kit — parts imported in disassembled form. Even without seats (incomplete), it is still a "motor car" in common parlance and for customs/GST classification. Assembly does not change its essential nature.
EMERGING QUESTIONS
Electric golf carts — capable of use on roads but devoid of doors and other road-worthy safety features — are they 'suited' for use on roads?
Battery operated 'buggy' used within factory, club or estate premises with more seating capacity — motor vehicle or not?
📝 The critical test remains: "adapted for use upon roads." A golf cart can physically go on a road but lacks safety features required for road use. The answer will significantly affect ITC eligibility and tariff rate for such vehicles.

💡 If a factory electric buggy is a motor vehicle → Section 17(5) blocks ITC on its purchase. If it is NOT a motor vehicle (adapted only for enclosed premises) → ITC may be available as plant and machinery.
19.2.2 — Two-Wheeler — Four Tests ⇧ Index
🏍️ Two-Wheeler & Three-Wheeler — Four Tests for "Motor Vehicle"
Section 2(28) | Plain Words | Registration NOT a Factor
📝 Two and three-wheelers raise special questions because the definition excludes vehicles with less than 4 wheels if engine capacity is 25cc or less. The four tests below determine whether a two/three-wheeler is a "motor vehicle" — all four must be examined. Importantly, registration mark and driver's license are NOT among the factors.
TEST (a)
Is propulsion from a power source — internal or external?
📝 Internal = engine/motor inside. External = connected to external power (like tram). Both qualify. Purely human-powered (bicycle) = no.
TEST (b)
Is it constructed on a chassis (to be attached with a body)?
📝 Chassis = the base frame on which the vehicle body is mounted. Even if body not yet attached, the chassis itself is included in motor vehicle definition.
TEST (c)
Is it specially adapted (by negative construction) to be used within enclosed spaces?
📝 "Negative construction" = specifically designed to ONLY work inside a factory/enclosed space. If it is only adapted for enclosed use, it is NOT a motor vehicle. Factory forklifts, for example, are excluded.
TEST (d)
Is its engine capacity less than 25cc when it has less than four wheels?
📝 25cc or less engine + less than 4 wheels = NOT a motor vehicle. This excludes small mopeds/toy vehicles. A standard motorcycle (100-200cc) clearly passes this test and IS a motor vehicle.
💡 E-scooter with external battery power: (a) ✓ power source (external), (b) ✓ chassis, (c) ✗ not enclosed-only, (d) need to check cc capacity → likely a motor vehicle → ITC blocked u/s 17(5).
CONCLUSION
Neither requirement for mark of registration nor operator having driver's license are factors in this definition.
Based on tests applied, two and three-wheelers fall within 'motor vehicle'.
📝 Even an unregistered two-wheeler or one ridden without a license is still a "motor vehicle" for GST purposes. The tests are mechanical/functional — not administrative/regulatory.
19.2.3 — Body Building ⇧ Index
🔧 Body Building — Independent Process | Job Work in GST
Central Excise vs GST Treatment | HSN 9988 | Section 2(68) CGST Act
📝 Body building = fitting a truck/bus body onto a chassis. Under Central Excise, this was treated as "manufacture." Under GST, it has been reclassified as "job work" — an independent process. This distinction dramatically affects the HSN code, rate, and ITC availability.
CENTRAL EXCISE (Old)
Chapter 87, First Schedule, Central Excise Tariff Act 1985 — Para 5:
"Building a body or fabrication or mounting of fitting of structures or equipment on the chassis falling under heading 8706 shall amount to 'manufacture' of a motor vehicle."
📝 Under old excise law — body building = manufacture = treated as making a new motor vehicle. Attracted excise duty as a motor vehicle. This has changed in GST.
GST (Current)
Body building rendered into a job-working activity classifiable under HSN 9988 under entry 26(ic) of notification 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017
📝 In GST, body building is treated as the body builder doing "job work" on the customer's chassis (goods). It is a service — not manufacture of a motor vehicle. Completely different tax treatment.
Section 2(68) CGST Act — "job work" means any treatment or process undertaken by a person on goods belonging to another registered person and the expression "job worker" shall be construed accordingly.

Schedule II, Para 3 — "Any treatment or process which is applied to another person's goods is a supply of services."
IMPORTANT CONDITION
Entry 26(ic) refers to 'job work' — eligible for concessional rate only when Recipient is a 'registered person' as required in Section 2(68) CGST Act
📝 If the customer (owner of the chassis) is a registered GST person — body building is job work at concessional rate. If the customer is unregistered — different rate applies. This is a critical distinction.
💡 Eg.594: Body building on truck chassis for a factory (registered person) to build an employees' bus → taxable under HSN 9988 entry 26(ic) at 18%.
💡 Eg.595: Body building on truck chassis for an educational institution (unregistered person) to build students' bus → taxable under HSN 9988 entry 26(iv) at 8%.
19.2.4 — Ambulance ⇧ Index
🚑 Ambulance — Two Routes of Manufacture | HSN 8703 vs 8707
Direct OEM Manufacture vs Indirect Body-Builder Manufacture
ROUTE (a) — DIRECT
Direct manufacture by OEM as an ambulance
HSN 8703
Entry 400 — Schedule III
GST: 18% | Cess: NIL
