📝 If a company (OEM like Tata, Force) directly manufactures an ambulance — classified under 8703 (motor cars and passenger vehicles). 18% + no cess.
💡 Eg.598: OEM manufactures ambulance (cleared as such) → tariff 18% + 0%.
ROUTE (b) — INDIRECT
Indirect manufacture by body builder as ambulance on a truck chassis OR by modification of car, van, LCV or SUV
HSN 8707
Entry 169 — Schedule IV
(Bodies for motor vehicles)
📝 When a body builder takes a chassis and builds an ambulance body on it — the body is classified under 8707. The chassis separately under 8706. The completed ambulance may attract mixed supply rules.
Section 2(72) CGST Act — "manufacture" means processing of raw material or inputs in any manner that results in emergence of a new product having a distinct name, character and use and the term "manufacturer" shall be construed accordingly.
MODIFICATION ≠ MANUFACTURE
Modifying vehicles is NOT a manufacturing activity — functionality and identity being unaffected by modifications as per definition in Section 2(72) CGST Act.
Modification is to create some incremental utility but not one that can have a different HSN code.
📝 A modification (like converting a van to an ambulance by adding stretcher, oxygen fittings) does not create a "new product with distinct name, character and use." The van remains a van — its HSN code does not change. Manufacture = new product with different identity, not mere modification.
💡 Eg.596: An SUV modified to be a mobile office for a CEO continues to remain the same vehicle. Does not secure a distinct name, character or use.
💡 Eg.597: A four-seater car modified into a two-seater car does not amount to manufacture of a new article.
19.2.5 — Refrigerated Van & HSN Tariff Table ⇧ Index
❄️ Refrigerated Van — Direct vs Indirect Manufacture | Mixed Supply Risk
HSN 8704 vs 8707 | Composite Supply Test | OEM vs Integrating Dealer
DIRECT MANUFACTURE
OEM manufactures refrigerated van directly → HSN 8704
Entry 401 — Schedule III
GST: 18% | Cess: NIL
INDIRECT MANUFACTURE
Truck chassis + body building by separate dealer → HSN 8707
Entry 169 — Schedule IV
⚠️ MIXED SUPPLY RISK
Body building is an independent manufacturing activity → HSN codes of chassis (8706) and body (8707) will NOT harmonize
Unless OEM manufactures special utility vehicle
📝 Composite supply requires a principal supply and ancillary supply as part of one bundle. If body builder and chassis supplier are different — the "integrating dealer" is not in a position to provide product warranty → no composite supply → mixed supply rules apply → taxed at highest rate.
💡 Eg.599: Dealers (integrators) who supply (i) Bus chassis at 28% + 15% and (ii) Body building at 18% + 0% → attract tax as 'mixed supply' and taxable at 28% + 15% and NOT 18%.
📊 Motor Vehicle HSN Codes — GST Tariff Reference Table
Description HSN GST Cess Notes
Road tractors870128%NILFarm tractors, highway tractors
Bus870228%15%Private/commercial buses
Bus (public transport)870218%15%Lower GST for public transport buses
Motor car870328%15% to 22%Sedan, SUV, hatchback — cess varies by engine size
Ambulance8702 or 870318%NILDepends on OEM (direct) vs body-builder (indirect) route
Truck870428%NILGoods transport vehicles
Chassis (of 8701 to 8705)870628%NILBare chassis — body not yet attached
Bodies (of 8701 to 8705)870728%NILBodies separately — bus body, truck body
Refrigerated van870418%NILConcessional rate — cold chain vehicles
Specialized vehicles *870518%NILBreakdown lorries, crane lorries, fire fighting, concrete-mixer, road sweeper, spraying lorries, mobile workshops, mobile radiological unit
Work trucks **870918%NILSelf-propelled, not fitted with lifting/handling equipment, used in factories/warehouses/dock areas/airports for short distance transport of goods; tractors on railway station platforms
19.3.1 — Mark of Registration ⇧ Index
🏷️ Mark of Registration — Purpose, Effect & Limitations
Bona Fide Ownership | NOT Test for Motor Vehicle Identity
📝 Registration mark is like a SIM card activation — it is not the thing itself, just an administrative step that activates it for use on public roads. A vehicle not yet registered is still a motor vehicle. Registration confirms ownership assertion — it does NOT confirm fitness or create the "motor vehicle" status.
PURPOSE
Affixation of mark of registration is for purposes of regulating trade in motor vehicles — akin to SIM card being 'activated'
📝 Just as a SIM card is a SIM before it's activated, a motor vehicle is a motor vehicle before registration. Registration is just the formal "activation" for public road use.
NOT YET USED
Motor vehicles not yet 'put to use' will not bear mark of registration
Vehicles for demonstration or test drive will not have mark of registration (Kerala exception vide circular 6th Aug 2018)
📝 Demo vehicles and test drive vehicles have no registration — but they are still motor vehicles. Kerala made a specific exception allowing certain demo vehicles to not bear registration mark.
WHAT REGISTRATION AFFIRMS
Affixation of mark of registration is:
✗ NOT assurance of fitness for use on road
✓ IS affirmation that such assertion has been made by a bona fide owner of the vehicle
📝 Registration says: "This person claims to be the genuine owner and has registered the vehicle." It does NOT say the vehicle is safe, fit for roads, or that ITC is admissible on it.
PRE-OWNED TEST
Test of pre-ownership is based on assertion of ownership by assigning mark of registration — except demonstration/test vehicles which are pre-owned even without registration mark (except in Kerala)
📝 Pre-owned = previously owned. If a vehicle has a registration in someone's name — it is pre-owned. Even demo vehicles without registration can be pre-owned for GST purposes.
19.3.2 — Section 17(5) — ITC Block on Motor Vehicles ⇧ Index
🚫 Section 17(5) CGST Act — ITC Ineligibility on Motor Vehicles
Policy Restriction | Not a Test | Repairs & Maintenance also Blocked
CORE RULE
Certain kinds of motor vehicles are ineligible to ITC under Section 17(5) — even when they are 'used' in business, and even when allowed as a business deduction under allied laws
📝 Even if you use a car 100% for business — even if your Income Tax allows depreciation on it — Section 17(5) CGST Act still blocks ITC. These are two separate laws with separate rules.
NOT A USAGE TEST
Inadmissibility of credit is not based on a test — it does NOT imply these motor vehicles are somehow not used in course or furtherance of business
It is just a policy restriction
📝 The ITC block is a pure policy call — Parliament decided that motor vehicles (subject to exceptions) should not carry ITC. The block does not mean the vehicle is not for business. Both can be true simultaneously.
CASCADING BLOCK
Motor vehicles ineligible to ITC are also ineligible to ITC in respect of their repairs and maintenance
📝 If you cannot claim ITC on buying the vehicle — you also cannot claim ITC on repairing it, servicing it, or maintaining it. The block extends to all ancillary services related to the blocked vehicle.
💡 Company car (not used for transport of passengers commercially) — ITC blocked on purchase AND on insurance, tyre replacement, service, repairs. All ancillary supplies blocked.
DEFINITION DETERMINES SCOPE
Refer examination of definition of 'motor vehicle' — what falls within = ITC blocked
What falls outside = may be eligible as plant & machinery
📝 This is why the definition of motor vehicle is so critical. A factory electric buggy that is NOT a motor vehicle → ITC eligible as plant and machinery. A buggy that IS a motor vehicle → ITC blocked.
19.3.3 — Pre-Owned Vehicles ⇧ Index
🔄 Pre-Owned Motor Vehicles — Output Tax Even Without ITC
Notification 8/2018-CT(Rate) | Rule 32(5) CGST Rules | Cess Exemption
📝 Even if you couldn't claim ITC when you bought the motor vehicle, you still have to pay GST when you sell it as a "pre-owned" vehicle. However, the GST rules provide a margin-based scheme to avoid double taxation — you pay GST only on the profit margin (selling price minus purchase price).
OUTPUT TAX
Disposal of motor vehicles, even when ITC was not availed, is liable to payment of output tax
📝 You bought a car, couldn't claim ITC. Now you sell it. You must charge GST on the sale. You are not exempt just because you didn't get ITC. ITC block ≠ output tax exemption.
RATE — TWO OPTIONS
(i) Notification 8/2018-CT(Rate) dated 25th Jan 2018
(ii) Rule 32(5) of CGST Rules — margin-based valuation
📝 Rule 32(5) allows GST to be paid only on the margin (selling price minus depreciated book value), not on the full transaction value. This is the margin scheme for second-hand goods including vehicles.
CESS EXEMPTION
Full exemption of Cess vide entry 42A to notification 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate), dated 28th June 2017 as amended from 25th Jan 2018
📝 When selling a pre-owned motor vehicle — Compensation Cess is fully exempt. Only GST (at applicable rate, on margin or full value as per scheme chosen) is payable. No cess to be charged.
JUDICIAL TEST
Important to refer to judicial consideration regarding motor vehicle based on facts — even when legislation made reference to MV Act
📝 Even in pre-owned vehicle disputes — courts look at the facts of whether the article actually IS a motor vehicle as per the definition (not just what the parties call it).
19.3.4 — Para 4(b) — Schedule II: Diversion of Business Assets ⇧ Index
↗️ Para 4(b) Schedule II — Diversion of Motor Vehicle to Non-Business Use
Supply of Services | HSN 9971 Rate Applies
📝 Para 4(b) of Schedule II CGST Act creates a fiction: when a business asset (like a motor vehicle) is diverted from its intended business use to personal use or use by someone outside the business — even without consideration — this diversion is treated as a "supply of services." This prevents businesses from buying vehicles as assets and then diverting them for personal use tax-free.
Schedule II, Para 4(b) — "where, by or under the direction of a person carrying on a business, goods held or used for the purposes of the business are put to any private use or are used, or made available to any person for use, for any purpose other than a purpose of the business, whether or not for a consideration, the usage or making available of such goods is a supply of services."
TRIGGER
Diversion of business assets from intended 'end use' → attracts incidence of tax as 'supply of services' under Para 4(b)
COVERS MOTOR VEHICLES
Where pre-owned vehicles are supplied by being diverted for non-business use — the supply is to be 'treated' as supply of services even though object of contract of sale is goods (motor vehicles)
📝 The vehicle is goods in reality — but the law creates a legal fiction treating this diversion as supply of services. The fiction overrides the physical form of the transaction.
RATE
Rate of tax applicable on disposal of goods 'treated' to be supply of services will adopt the 'same rate of tax as applicable to sale of goods' under HSN 9971
📝 Even though it becomes a "service" by legal fiction — the rate used is NOT the standard 18% service rate. It uses the same rate as the goods (motor vehicle) — i.e., whatever rate applies under HSN 9971 for sale of goods by the same description.
💡 Company car (ITC not availed) diverted to MD's personal use → treated as supply of services under Para 4(b). Rate = same as if sold as goods (motor car) under HSN 9971. Not 18% standard service rate.
19.3.5 — Taxi Operator ⇧ Index
🚖 Taxi Operator — Legal Authority Required | Non Est if Unlicensed
Commercial Driver's License | Colour Code | Lawful Authority to Contract
📝 Operating a taxi is not just about having a vehicle — it requires specific legal authority: a commercial driver's license AND the correct colour code for the vehicle's registration mark. Without these, operating as a taxi is illegal and the transaction is non est (does not exist in law). This has profound GST implications for ITC claims and output tax.
TWO LEGAL REQUIREMENTS
(i) Obtaining a commercial driver's license or badge
(ii) Operate vehicles with a distinct colour code for its mark of registration
📝 A private car (white plate) cannot be used as a taxi. It must be a commercial vehicle (yellow plate/yellow number). And the driver needs a commercial license — not just a personal driving license.
NON EST IF UNLICENSED
To claim that a motor vehicle is being used for passenger transport — requires legal authority to do so.
Without such authority (operator AND vehicle) — would be illegal and non est in law
📝 Non est = does not exist. An unlicensed taxi operation is void in law. You cannot claim ITC based on an operation that is legally non-existent. And you cannot use a non-existent operation as defence against GST demand.
ITC MOTIVE IRRELEVANT
Motivated by loss of ITC or inspired to claim ITC — offering to transport passengers would fall foul with MV Act
Two-wheelers: Karnataka Electric Bike Taxi Scheme 2021 counters contrary assertion
📝 You cannot simply put a car on Ola/Uber and say "I'm providing passenger transport service to claim ITC." Without commercial license and yellow plate, the operation is illegal. Motive to save/claim tax does not legitimize an illegal operation.
💡 Company buys SUV. Registers it as personal (white plate). Employee drives it. Claims ITC under Section 17(5) exception for "transport of passengers." → Illegal — needs commercial license and yellow plate → ITC claim fails.
19.3.6 — Section 9(5) — ECOM & Taxi Hailing Service ⇧ Index
📱 Section 9(5) CGST Act — E-Commerce Operator's GST Liability for Taxi Hailing
Notification 17/2017-CT | ECOM = Deemed Supplier | Driver ≠ Supplier for GST
📝 Section 9(5) is the provision that makes Ola/Uber/Rapido (ECOM operators) liable to pay GST on taxi hailing services — instead of the driver (actual supplier). This is a legal fiction in GST. Three parties are involved: ECOM (Ola/Uber), Driver, Customer. ECOM pays GST on entire transaction value but this does NOT affect the Driver's obligations under Motor Vehicles Act (license, colour code, etc.).
Three Parties in Taxi Hailing Service
Operator (ECOM)
Ola / Uber / Rapido
→ LIABLE to discharge GST on entire transaction value
Driver (Supplier)
Actual service provider
→ EXCUSED from coming within compliance requirements under GST law (by legal fiction)
Customer (Recipient)
Passenger
→ Pays fare (consideration)
CONTRACTUAL PRIVITY
Important to notice 'contractual privity' between the various actors involved
ECOM is notified for purposes of Section 9(5) under Notification 17/2017-CT dated 28th June 2017
📝 Contractual privity = who has a direct contract with whom. In Ola: Customer contracts with Ola (ECOM). Ola contracts with Driver. Customer and Driver have no direct contract. This chain determines who pays GST.
LEGAL FICTION LIMIT
This legal fiction in GST will NOT interfere with the Driver's obligations under Motor Vehicles' law
(commercial license + colour code requirements still apply to Driver)
📝 Even though Ola pays the GST — the Driver still needs a commercial license and yellow-plate vehicle under MV Act. GST fiction changes who pays tax — it does NOT change who needs to comply with traffic/vehicle laws.
💡 An Ola driver uses a white-plate car and personal license → illegal under MV Act → even though Ola pays GST under Sec 9(5) → the Driver's non-compliance is a separate violation. GST legal fiction does not cure MV Act violations.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 19 | Motor Vehicles Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name or provision.
Legislation by Reference — No Room for Negotiation | Motor Vehicle Definition
Legal Principle | Para 19.1 | MV Act, 1988 | GST Tariff Classification & Section 17(5)
Key Principle
Where there is a statutory definition made applicable, it is legislation by reference, and there is no room for negotiation or search for reasoning to apply the conclusion that the express words demand. The identity of a motor vehicle is not contingent on whether the article requires to be assigned a mark of registration. This line of thinking has been overturned to account for vehicles for use within bounded premises and machinery mounted on mobile skids which are not road-ready.
When to Use — When Department or assessee tries to argue that an article is/is not a motor vehicle based on whether it has a registration plate, or uses analogical reasoning. Counter: the statutory definition is legislation by reference — express words govern. Registration is irrelevant to the definition.
Bose Abraham v. State of Kerala & Anr. | AIR 2001 SC 835
Supreme Court of India | 2001 | Entry Tax on Motor Vehicles | Excavators & Road Rollers
Key Ratio
The correct test for a motor vehicle is not whether mark of registration was required or whether it could be operated without a driver's licence. The correct test is whether the equipment is "suitable for use on roads" — albeit to construct the road itself. Neither requirement for mark of registration nor operator having driver's licence are factors in the statutory definition.
When to Use — In disputes about whether road construction equipment (graders, compactors, road rollers) are motor vehicles for GST tariff and ITC purposes. The test is "suitable for use on roads" — not registration. Cite Bose Abraham to establish excavators/road rollers = motor vehicles.
Body Building = Job Work, NOT Manufacture | HSN 9988 vs 8706
Legal Principle | Para 19.2.3 | Notification 11/2017-CT(Rate) | Section 2(68) CGST Act
Key Principle
Unlike Central Excise where building a body on a chassis amounts to "manufacture" of a motor vehicle, GST has rendered body building into a job-working activity classifiable under HSN 9988 under entry 26(ic) of notification 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate). It is important to note that entry 26(ic) refers to 'job work' — eligible to concessional rate only when the Recipient is a 'registered person' as required in Section 2(68) of Central GST Act.
When to Use — When Department raises demand on body building activity treating it as manufacture/sale of motor vehicle at 28%. Counter: GST specifically reclassified body building as job work under HSN 9988. Rate depends on whether recipient is registered (18%) or unregistered (8%). Central Excise precedents on manufacture do not apply in GST.
Modification ≠ Manufacture | Section 2(72) CGST Act
Legal Principle | Para 19.2.4 | Section 2(72) CGST Act | Eg.596, 597
Key Principle
Modifying vehicles is not a manufacturing activity in view of the functionality and identity being unaffected by such modifications as per definition in Section 2(72) of Central GST Act. Modification is to create some incremental utility but not one that can have a different HSN code. Manufacture requires emergence of a new product having a distinct name, character and use.
When to Use — When Department demands GST at motor vehicle rate (28%) on vehicle modification/customization activity. Counter: modification does not result in a new product with distinct name/character/use. HSN code does not change. An SUV converted to mobile office remains an SUV. A four-seater converted to two-seater remains the same vehicle.
Mixed Supply on Indirect Ambulance/Refrigerated Van — Bus Chassis + Body Building
Legal Principle | Para 19.2.5 | Eg.599 | Composite vs Mixed Supply
Key Principle
Body building is an independent manufacturing activity. Therefore, unless the OEM manufactures a special utility vehicle, the HSN codes will not harmonize since the 'integrating-dealer' is not in a position to provide product warranty. Dealers (integrators) who supply (i) Bus chassis at 28% + 15% and (ii) Body building at 18% + 0% will attract tax as 'mixed supply' and taxable at 28% + 15% and not 18%.
When to Use — When integrating dealers try to classify combined bus/truck chassis + body building supply as composite supply at the lower 18% rate. Counter: body building is independent activity, HSN codes do not harmonize, no unified product warranty possible → mixed supply → taxed at highest rate of 28% + 15%. Only OEM direct manufacture achieves 18%.
Section 17(5) — ITC Block is Policy, NOT a Usage Test
Legal Principle | Para 19.3.2 | Section 17(5) CGST Act | Cascading Block on Repairs
Key Principle
Inadmissibility of credit is not based on a test and it does not also imply that these motor vehicles are somehow not used in the course or furtherance of business. It is just a policy restriction that credit is not allowed on motor vehicles. Motor vehicles that are ineligible to credit are also ineligible to credit in respect of their repairs and maintenance.
When to Use — (i) When assessee tries to claim ITC on motor vehicles by arguing business use. Counter: Section 17(5) is a policy block — business use is irrelevant. (ii) When assessee claims ITC on repairs/insurance/maintenance of blocked vehicles. Counter: repairs and maintenance of blocked vehicles are also blocked — the cascading block applies. (iii) When Department tries to argue that ITC block means vehicle is not "used in business" — counter: the two are independent.
Taxi Operator Without Legal Authority — Non Est | Para 4(b) & HSN 9971
Legal Principle | Para 19.3.5 | MV Act 1988 | Section 9(5) CGST | Karnataka Electric Bike Taxi Scheme 2021
Key Principle
To claim that a motor vehicle is being used for passenger transport requires legal authority to do so. Without such authority — for operator and vehicle — would be illegal and non est in law. Motivated by loss of input tax credit or inspired to claim input tax credit, offering to transport passengers would fall foul with MV Act. This legal fiction in GST (Section 9(5)) will not interfere with the Driver's obligations under Motor Vehicles' law.
When to Use — When an assessee (or Department) relies on "passenger transport" characterization of a private vehicle (white plate, personal license) to either claim ITC exception under Section 17(5) or to classify supply as passenger transport service. Counter: without commercial license and correct colour code — the operation is non est. GST legal fiction (Section 9(5) ECOM liability) does not cure MV Act non-compliance.
Para 4(b) Schedule II — Diversion of Vehicle = Supply of Services at Goods Rate
Legal Principle | Para 19.3.4 | Schedule II Para 4(b) | HSN 9971 | CGST Act
Key Principle
Diversion of business assets from intended end use attracts incidence of tax as 'supply of services' in terms of paragraph 4(b), Schedule II to Central GST Act. Where pre-owned vehicles are supplied by being diverted for non-business use, the supply is to be 'treated' as supply of services even when the object of contract of sale is goods — motor vehicles. Rate of tax applicable on disposal of goods 'treated' to be supply of services will adopt the 'same rate of tax as applicable to sale of goods' under HSN 9971.
When to Use — When company vehicle (ITC not availed) is diverted to MD/director's personal use or given to an outsider for non-business purposes. Argue: Para 4(b) Schedule II deems this a "supply of services." Rate = same as goods rate under HSN 9971 (not standard 18% service rate). Also use when Department tries to argue such diversion is not a supply at all.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 20 — Competition Act

⚖️ Chapter 20 — Competition Act

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Valid / Permitted
Prohibited / Caution / GST not affected
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
20.1 Relevance to GST
20.2 Essentials
20.2.1 Purpose
20.2.2 Consumer
20.2.3 Price
20.2.4 Service
20.3 Investigation
20.3.1 Scope
20.3.2 Due Process
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
20.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
⚖️ Competition Act — Relevance to GST
Welfare Matters' Legislation | CCI replaces NAA from 1 Dec 2022
KEY CHANGE
From 1st Dec 2022 — Competition Commission of India (CCI) will replace the National Anti-Profiteering Authority (NAA) under Section 171 of Central GST Act.
Vide Notifications 23 and 24/2022-Central Tax, dated 23rd Nov. 2022.
📝 The anti-profiteering mechanism under GST is now enforced by CCI — the same body that oversees competition law. This makes it even more important to understand how Competition Act principles interact with GST.
How Competition Act 'Feeds' Missing Ingredients into GST:
OPPORTUNITY COST
When something is available and its free — monetary value is ascribed to the transaction based on opportunity cost of that offering — to bring actors within the operation of this law
📝 'Free' doesn't mean no value in Competition law. If you could have sold it but gave it free — the market price becomes the notional value. This can be used to impute consideration even in GST contexts.
UNFAIR TRADE PRACTICES
Cognizance taken for:
• Predatory pricing
• Other forms of abuse of dominant position
📝 Predatory pricing = pricing below cost to kill competitors. Under Competition Act, this has monetary value implications. In GST — declared value may be challenged if it is artificially low due to dominant position abuse.
⚠️ CRITICAL LIMITATION
Competition Act = welfare matters' legislation → purposive construction applies → permits 'lifting the corporate veil' but does NOT authorise re-imagining the contract to support allegations of anti-competitive behaviour.

Strict interpretation that applies in regular GST compliance CANNOT be eclipsed by anti-abuse investigative tools available in Competition Act.

Assertions in law or admissions by Suppliers under Competition Act cannot alter the fact that there is 'no supply' as regards GST — since this legal fiction cannot pollinate the strict construction that GST demands.
📝 CCI may find anti-competitive behaviour and impute a notional price. But that finding does NOT automatically create a GST liability. GST is governed by strict construction — the legal fiction of imputed value under Competition Act cannot be transplanted into GST unless GST law itself so provides.
20.2.1 — Purpose of Competition Act ⇧ Index
🎯 Purpose — Why Competition Act Exists
PREAMBLE
"An Act to provide, keeping in view of the economic development of the country, for the establishment of a Commission to prevent practices having adverse effect on competition, to promote and sustain competition in markets, to protect the interests of consumers and to ensure freedom of trade carried on by other participants in markets, in India, and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto."
Four Objects of Competition Act:
Prevent practices having adverse effect on competition
Promote and sustain competition in markets
Protect interests of consumers
Ensure freedom of trade for all market participants
WHY RELEVANT TO GST
Must understand purpose of this Act and locate the confines of the limited area of overlap with GST — to appreciate:
(i) Extents of positions taken in proceedings under this Act
(ii) Sanctity of positions applicable to GST vide Contract law, Sale of Goods Act and other legislations
📝 Competition Act proceedings may produce findings about price, dominance, or market behaviour — but those findings only carry over to GST to the extent that GST law itself recognises them. The overlap is limited.
20.2.2 — Consumer ⇧ Index
👤 "Consumer" — Section 2(f) Competition Act
STATUTORY DEFINITION — Section 2(f)
"Consumer" means any person who —

(i) Buys any goods for a consideration which has been paid or promised or partly paid and partly promised, or under any system of deferred payment — includes any user of such goods other than the person who buys, whether purchase is for resale, commercial purpose or personal use;

(ii) Hires or avails of any services for a consideration which has been paid or promised or partly paid and partly promised, or under any system of deferred payment — includes any beneficiary of such services other than the person who hires, whether such services are availed with approval of first-mentioned person, whether hiring is for commercial purpose or personal use.
WIDE SCOPE
Includes users even if they did not buy/hire themselves — if used with approval of buyer.
Includes purchase for commercial purpose (not limited to personal use).
Includes deferred payment arrangements.
📝 Under Competition Act — 'consumer' is very widely defined. Even a B2B buyer is a 'consumer' here — unlike Consumer Protection Act which has narrower scope.
GST LIMITATION
'Buys' and 'hires' in this Act CANNOT be given a meaning beyond the contours of Contract law

(Gannon Dunkerley ibid — SC — 46th Amendment context: 'sale' cannot be defined expansively beyond limits of Sale of Goods Act)
📝 Even though Competition Act defines 'consumer' broadly, the underlying acts of buying and hiring must still satisfy Contract law requirements. No consideration = no contract = no consumer = no supply in GST terms.
KEY RULE
Making every User a 'Consumer' under Competition Act (via legal fiction) cannot — under strict GST construction — convert a non-supply into a supply. Legal fiction of Competition Act cannot pollinate GST.
📝 CCI may treat a free user as a 'consumer' for anti-profiteering purposes. But GST says: if there is no consideration, there is no taxable supply. The CCI finding does NOT create GST liability by itself.
20.2.3 — Price ⇧ Index
💰 "Price" — Section 2(o) | Valuable Consideration vs Imputed Value
DEFINITION — Section 2(o)
"Price", in relation to the sale of any goods or to the performance of any services, includes every valuable consideration, whether direct or indirect, or deferred, and includes any consideration which in effect relates to the sale of any goods or to the performance of any services although ostensibly relating to any other matter or thing.
📝 Price in Competition Act is extremely wide — even consideration ostensibly for something else (like a 'maintenance fee' that is really for the goods) is captured. This helps detect disguised pricing arrangements that distort competition.
VALUABLE CONSIDERATION
Not imputed consideration — but valid consideration, even if in non-monetary form.

Refer Currie v. Misa ibid — 'benefits-detriment' theory: every benefit can be accepted as consideration — subject to limitations that private motivations cannot be consideration.
📝 Consideration doesn't have to be money — barter, data, exchange of services all qualify. But a purely private motivation (like love and affection) does not qualify as consideration in law.
OSTENSIBLE RELATING
The expression "although ostensibly relating to any other matter or thing" — takes this Act into a completely different realm compared to Contract law or GST treatment.

Benefits flowing can — due to this legal fiction — be imputed to take cognizance of any anti-competitive behaviour of Suppliers by rendering every User on par with Consumer.
📝 This phrase is the key power of Competition Act — it can look through the label of a payment and find its true economic character. But this power to impute value is LIMITED to Competition Act proceedings — it does NOT flow into GST.
⚠️ GST FIREWALL
Neither assertions in law nor admissions by Suppliers under Competition Act can alter the fact that there is 'no supply' as regards GST — this legal fiction cannot pollinate the strict construction that GST demands.
FREE SERVICES / NO REVENUE SCENARIO
Mere accrual of benefits to a User cannot be elevated to be 'consideration' contrary to the tests in Contract law.
When the benefits are NOT consideration → the User is NOT a Consumer.

Where there is no revenue (yet) → it is not even a business, because business is one which has demonstrated income generating capacity.
📝 Google/LinkedIn type platforms that appear 'free' may be under Competition Act scrutiny — but for GST, if there is truly no consideration flowing from the user, there is no taxable supply to or by that user. Tax law looks at actual revenue, not theoretical opportunity cost.
💡 Google: Ostensible business = Technology enterprise | Business in law/fact = Advertisement intermediary
💡 LinkedIn: Ostensible business = Technology enterprise | Business in law/fact = HR intermediary
Under Competition Act — the real business character is examined. Under GST — it is the principal source of revenue that determines the tax treatment.
20.2.4 — Service ⇧ Index
🔧 "Service" — Competition Act vs GST
DEFINITION — Section 2(u) Competition Act
"Service" means service of any description which is made available to potential users and includes the provision of services in connection with business of any industrial or commercial matters such as banking, communication, education, financing, insurance, chit funds, real estate, transport, storage, material treatment, processing, supply of electrical or other energy, boarding, lodging, entertainment, amusement, construction, repair, conveying of news or information and advertising.
📝 The Competition Act definition of 'service' attempts to be universal — it covers almost every economic activity. This is much wider than the GST definition under Section 2(102) of Central GST Act, which has its own specific inclusions and exclusions.
COMPETITION ACT — WELFARE LAW
Looks at Supplier's skills and competencies to identify business character

Test: 'Object of contract' — examines the 'substance' of the offering by persons under inquiry
📝 Competition Act investigates: What does this entity actually do? What capabilities define it? It looks at the whole picture including ostensible and real business character.
GST — TAX LAW
Looks at principal source of revenue to determine tax treatment

Where there is NO revenue yet → NOT even a 'business' for GST purposes → business requires demonstrated income generating capacity
📝 GST focuses on what generates income — not what the entity is capable of. A free service platform that earns from advertising pays GST on advertising services — not on the 'free' user services.
⚠️ KEY DIVERGENCE
Universality attempted in Competition Act definition of 'service' dwarfs the definition in Section 2(102) of Central GST Act.
For this reason — test of 'object of contract' becomes even more important since service looks into the 'substance' of the offering under Competition Act — whereas GST demands strict construction of what the supply actually is and for what consideration.
20.3.1 — Scope of Investigation ⇧ Index
🔍 Investigation — Scope & Burden of Proof
Investigation under Competition Act is LIMITED to Three Areas:
Section 3
Anti-competitive agreements
📝 Agreements that restrict competition — price-fixing, market sharing, bid-rigging between competitors.
Section 4
Abuse of dominant position
📝 When a dominant player misuses its market power — predatory pricing, unfair conditions, denial of market access.
Section 5
Combination by acquisition
📝 Mergers and acquisitions that may reduce competition in the market — requires CCI approval beyond threshold.
⚠️ INGREDIENTS — HIGHEST DEGREE OF PROOF
All three expressions contain 'ingredients' that have to be established with the highest degree of proof since any investigation must first establish the applicability of the law to the facts of an entity.
📝 'Ingredients' = specific legal elements that must each be separately proved. Like a recipe — every ingredient must be present. You cannot simply allege 'anti-competitive behaviour' — each element of the offence must be proved to the highest standard.
💡 Example (600): "I have committed murder" is NOT a statement of fact but an opinion. 'Murder' has a statutory definition and contains 'ingredients'. Admission requires knowledge of definition and availability of impeccable evidence to establish each ingredient. At best, death of victim alone can be established by this statement.

💡 Example (601): "Agreement is anti-competitive" is an opinion — an allegation. Allegations when disputed require proof. Proof must be such that a Court, and no less, must admit to being sufficient to support the allegations beyond reasonable doubt — not mere preponderance of probability.

💡 Example (602): "Abuse of dominant position" is also an opinion. Requires first establishing the existence of 'dominant position' itself.
20.3.2 — Due Process ⇧ Index
⚖️ Due Process — Investigation Procedure
Complaint or Information
→
Examine prima facie cause for investigation
Notice of intent to investigate
→
Direct investigation with areas of inquiry
Review investigation report and objections
→
Pass Orders
NATURE OF PROCEEDING
Being a proceeding that can inflict adverse consequences on the person being investigated → due process cannot be any less than a trial — although without the rigours of rules of procedure and evidence.
📝 Even though Competition Act proceedings are quasi-judicial (not full court trials), the stakes are high enough that the person being investigated must be treated like a defendant in a trial — with all procedural protections.
First Principles on Common Law — Must be Applied:
(a)
Burden of proof rests on State assisted by Complainant
No burden to prove innocence
📝 Presumption of innocence. The CCI/DG must prove the case — the accused party does NOT have to prove it is innocent.
(b)
Evidence in support of allegations must stand scrutiny as to admissibility and probative value
📝 Not all evidence is acceptable — it must be admissible (legally collected, proper form) AND probative (actually proves what it claims to prove).
(c)
Principles of natural justice will apply
📝 Audi alteram partem (hear the other side) and nemo judex in causa sua (no one should be a judge in their own cause) — both apply.
(d)
Remedy by way of appeal is also allowed
INDEPENDENCE
Authority (CCI) is independent of the Investigative Agency (Director General).
The basis on which the investigation was authorised is subject to judicial review.
📝 CCI (which decides) is separate from DG (which investigates). This separation ensures fairness. And if CCI authorised an investigation without proper grounds — courts can review that very authorization. The investigation itself can be challenged at the root.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 20 | Competition Act ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before CCI, NAA/Anti-Profiteering Authority, and GST adjudicating officers. State them confidently and cite the provision/authority.
Competition Act Legal Fiction Cannot Pollinate GST — Strict Construction Firewall
Legal Principle | Chapter 20.1 | Applicable in all Anti-Profiteering and CCI proceedings with GST implications
Key Principle
Strict interpretation that applies in regular compliance in GST cannot be eclipsed by anti-abuse investigative tools available in Competition Act. Neither assertions in law nor admissions by Suppliers under Competition Act can alter the fact that there is 'no supply' as regards GST is concerned since this legal fiction cannot pollinate the strict construction that GST demands. Being a welfare matters' legislation, purposive construction applies to Competition Act — but this does not authorise re-imagining the contract to support the allegation of anti-competitive behaviour.
When to Use — When CCI or anti-profiteering authority tries to use a Competition Act finding (e.g., imputed value of free service, admission by competitor) to create or enhance a GST demand. Counter: findings and legal fictions under Competition Act (a welfare legislation) cannot override GST's strict construction. GST liability must be determined by GST law — not imported from Competition Act proceedings.
Free Service = No Consumer + No Supply in GST | No Revenue = No Business
Legal Principle | Chapter 20.2.2 & 20.2.3 | Gannon Dunkerley (SC) — ibid | Contract Law Tests
Key Principle
Mere accrual of benefits to a User cannot be elevated to be 'consideration' contrary to the tests in Contract law. When the benefits are not consideration, the User is not a Consumer. In Competition Act, 'buys' and 'hires' cannot be given a meaning beyond the contours of Contract law — as affirmed in Gannon Dunkerley in the context of 46th Amendment — that 'sale' was not permitted to be defined in an expansive manner beyond the limits of Sale of Goods Act. And where there is no revenue (yet) then it is not even a business because business is one which has demonstrated income generating capacity.
When to Use — When Department taxes free services (like platform services, loyalty programmes, free trials) on the basis that Competition Act treats users as 'consumers'. Counter: free services = no consideration = no supply in GST. Competition Act's expansive treatment of free users as 'consumers' cannot be imported into GST. Also use when Department treats a startup/pre-revenue entity as a 'business' for GST purposes.
"Ostensibly Relating" — Price Definition Limited to Competition Act Proceedings
Legal Principle | Section 2(o) Competition Act | Chapter 20.2.3 | Currie v. Misa — benefits-detriment theory
Key Principle
The expression "although ostensibly relating to any other matter or thing" in the definition of 'price' under Section 2(o) of Competition Act takes this Act into a completely different realm compared to Contract law or treatment in GST. Benefits flowing can, due to this legal fiction in these words, be imputed to take cognizance of any anti-competitive behaviour of Suppliers — but by rendering every User to be on par with Consumer for purposes of this Act, neither assertions in law nor admission by Suppliers, can alter the fact that there is 'no supply' as regards GST is concerned since this legal fiction cannot pollinate the strict construction that GST demands.
When to Use — When Revenue uses Competition Act's broad price definition to impute consideration in transactions where none actually exists or where consideration relates to an ancillary matter. Counter: the 'ostensibly relating' fiction is peculiar to Competition Act and is for welfare/consumer protection purposes only. It does not extend to GST valuation — GST valuation follows Section 15 CGST Act which requires actual consideration.
Ingredients Must Be Established to Highest Degree of Proof — Beyond Reasonable Doubt
Legal Principle | Chapter 20.3.1 | Examples 600, 601, 602 | Common Law Principles
Key Principle
All expressions in Competition Act — anti-competitive agreements, abuse of dominant position, and combination by acquisition — contain 'ingredients' that have to be established with the highest degree of proof since any investigation must first establish the applicability of the law to the facts of an entity. An allegation of anti-competitive agreement or abuse of dominant position is an opinion, not a statement of fact. Proof must be such that a Court, and no less, must admit to being sufficient to support the allegations beyond reasonable doubt and not mere preponderance of probability. Abuse of dominant position also requires first establishing the existence of 'dominant position' itself.
When to Use — In any proceeding before CCI or anti-profiteering authority where an allegation has been made without proper proof of each ingredient. Cite to challenge the very invocation of the law. Especially useful when: (a) 'dominant position' is assumed without being established; (b) 'anti-competitive agreement' is alleged based on parallel conduct alone without proof of coordination; (c) DG report relies on circumstantial evidence that does not meet the 'beyond reasonable doubt' standard.
Due Process — Burden on State + Natural Justice + Judicial Review of Investigation
Legal Principle | Chapter 20.3.2 | Common Law Principles | CCI Act
Key Principle
Being a proceeding that can inflict adverse consequences on the person being investigated, the due process cannot be any less than a trial although without the rigours of rules of procedure and evidence. The first principles on Common Law must be applied: (a) burden of proof rests on the State assisted by Complainant and there is no burden to prove innocence; (b) evidence in support of allegations must stand scrutiny as to their admissibility and probative value; (c) principles of natural justice will apply; and (d) remedy by way of appeal is also allowed. The Authority is independent of the Investigative Agency (Director General). And the basis on which the investigation was authorised is subject to judicial review.
When to Use — In CCI proceedings when: (a) DG issues a sweeping investigation order without establishing prima facie case — challenge the very basis of investigation by judicial review; (b) Natural justice is violated — DG or CCI passes order without hearing the party; (c) CCI acts on inadmissible or hearsay evidence — challenge probative value; (d) Burden is placed on investigated party to prove innocence — counter: burden is on State/Complainant. Also use in anti-profiteering proceedings where the same principles of due process should apply since NAA/CCI proceedings can inflict adverse financial consequences.
Ostensible Business vs Business in Law/Fact — Object of Contract Test (Service Definition)
Legal Principle | Chapter 20.2.4 | Applicable in GST classification disputes for digital/platform businesses
Key Principle
The universality that is attempted to be given to the definition of 'service' in Competition Act seems to dwarf the definition in Section 2(102) of Central GST Act. For this reason, the test of 'object of contract' becomes even more important since 'service' in Competition Act looks into the 'substance' of the offering by persons under inquiry. There are any number of instances where welfare legislations look at the Supplier's skills and competencies whereas tax law looks at principal source of revenue. And where there is no revenue (yet) then it is not even a business because business is one which has demonstrated income generating capacity.
When to Use — In GST classification disputes for digital platforms, technology companies, and new-age businesses. Example: CCI identifies Google as an 'advertisement intermediary' but Google classifies its GST supply as 'technology services'. Counter using Competition Act's own finding: object of contract is what really determines the supply — Google's principal revenue source is advertising, not technology licensing. Also cite to argue that for GST rate classification, the substance of the offering (principal source of revenue) governs, not the ostensible description in the contract.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 21 — Intellectual Property Laws

💡 Chapter 21 — Intellectual Property Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Wrong
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
21.1 Relevance to GST
21.2.1 Copyright
21.2.2 Trade Mark
21.2.3 Industrial Design
21.2.4 Patent
21.2.5 Semiconductor Layout
21.2.6 Geographic Indications
21.2.7 Plant Varieties
21.2.8 Confidential Information
21.2.9 TRIPs
21.3.1–21.3.5 Sectoral: Lyrics/Music/Films/Broadcast/Performer
21.3.6–21.3.9 Sectoral: Statutes/Case Law/Assignment/License
21.3.10–21.3.15 Sectoral: Article/Societies/Franchise/Goodwill/VDA
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
21.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
💡 Intellectual Property Laws — Relevance to GST
Tangible vs Intangible | Goods vs Services | Temporary vs Permanent Transfer
ISSUE 1 — GOODS
When 'goods' are not limited to tangible property → GST requires clarity as to HSN of goods
📝 Intangible property like software, patents, trademarks can also be 'goods' in GST. Identifying correct HSN is critical for rate and exemption.
ISSUE 2 — SERVICES
When 'property' does not refer to subject matter but rights over certain subject matter → and when object of contract is not 'goods' → it will be 'services'
📝 Licensing an IP right (not selling the IP itself) = supply of services. The distinction between 'right to use' and 'transfer of ownership' determines goods vs services.
ISSUE 3 — LEGAL FICTION
'Temporary transfers' declared to be services while 'permanent transfers' are goods
→ Do not assume assignment is permanent without establishing it
📝 GST law deems temporary licensing = service. Permanent assignment = goods. But most copyright assignments have a 5-year limit or lifetime of author — so they are NOT truly permanent.
⚠️ CRITICAL GST ALERT
IP properties are not the object of contract but are inherently included when a business is transferred.
Being a private matters' legislation → IP laws are subject to strict construction, except to extent of anti-avoidance and abuse of inherent rights.
Certain aspects merit attention to expose failings in understanding the scope of IPRs and their tax treatment.
📝 When a business is sold as a going concern (exempt from GST), the IP embedded in that business transfers automatically — without a separate taxable event for IP. But if IP alone is transferred independently, full GST treatment applies.
21.2 — Overview: All 9 Types of Intellectual Property Rights ⇧ Index
IPR Type Governing Act What is Protected Duration Key GST Relevance
Copyright
(§21.2.1)		 Copyright Act (inherent — no registration needed) Literary, dramatic, musical & artistic works; cinematograph film; sound recordings
Idea has no copyright — reduced physical form does		 60 years after lifetime of author Temporary license = service. Permanent assignment debatable — limited to 5 years or lifetime. Copyright is a negative right — active only on infringement.
Trade Mark
(§21.2.2)		 Trade Marks Act, 1999 Visual symbol of identification of manufacturer applied to articles offered for sale. Distinctiveness is key. Renewable (sector-limited) Grant limited to 'sector' of articles. Use beyond sector by another is allowed. Infringement relief includes destruction of unauthorised stock.
Industrial Design
(§21.2.3)		 Designs Act, 2000 Aesthetic design of article or 3D model (not mere drawing). Novelty and originality are key. 15 years Drawing of design ≠ subject matter of protection. The article itself or 3D model is. Can be exploited by owner or licensed.
Patent
(§21.2.4)		 Patent Act, 1970 New and unique invention — enables disclosure without fear of copying. Patent is real property (intangible asset). Originally 14 years; now 20 years Patent = intangible asset = real property. Licensing = service. Generic duplicates permissible after expiry. Bad faith second inventions not protected.
Semiconductor Layout
(§21.2.5)		 Semiconductor IC Layout Designs Act, 2000 IC layout designs — efficiency of IC design. Different from industrial design under Designs Act. Specific to registration Registration criteria: originality, innovativeness, inherently distinctive, distinguishable.
Geographic Indications
(§21.2.6)		 GI of Goods (Registration & Protection) Act, 1999 Geographic indicators — mark of origin of article (not trademarked). Non-assignable. Renewable GI is non-assignable — it belongs to the region, not an individual. Cannot be part of a business transfer/assignment for GST purposes.
Plant Varieties
(§21.2.7)		 Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmer's Rights Act, 2001 (sui generis) New varieties of plants — gene sequence-based uniqueness. Breeders hold rights. Per registration; royalty payable to Government to retain registration Farmers not barred from re-sowing. Only registration-holders (breeders) can assign for commercial reproduction. Royalty to Government = consideration for retention of registration.
Confidential Information
(§21.2.8)		 No specific statute — common law + contractual Trade secret, non-public financial info, payroll data, business model, financing method. NDA makes limitation explicit. Until owner publishes it (then ceases) Injunctive relief + damages even without NDA. Exception: disclosure in course of discharging agreed obligations or required by public authority. Breach = actionable prejudice.
TRIPs
(§21.2.9)		 Trade Related IP Rights Agreement (Berne Convention 1971 included) Computer programs protected as literary works (machine-readable or not) Per category TRIPs Sections: 1=Copyright, 2=Trademarks, 3=GI, 4=Industrial Designs, 5=Patents, 6=Topographies of ICs, 7=Undisclosed Information
21.2.1 — Copyright (Detailed) ⇧ Index
©️ Copyright — Nature, Scope & GST Implications
CORE CONCEPT
Copyright = "right to copy" subject to consent of author, for consideration or otherwise.
No regulatory approval required — copyright is inherent in the work.
Exists in: (i) Literary, dramatic, musical & artistic works (ii) Cinematograph film (iii) Sound recordings
📝 Copyright arises automatically the moment a work is created and reduced to physical form. You do not need to register it anywhere. It is born with the work.
⚠️ KEY RULE — IDEA vs EXPRESSION
There is NO copyright in an idea
When that idea is reduced to some physical form → copyright emerges in the idea visible in this physical form
📝 The idea of a detective story has no copyright. But writing it as a novel creates copyright in that novel. The copyright is in the expression — not the underlying idea.
💡 Eg.603: Architectural drawings must be reduced to a three-dimensional model with a two-dimensional scale drawing to avail copyright. Copyright is in the model and drawing — not in the idea.
Eg.604: Story idea or teacher's explanation will enjoy copyright protection when reduced in writing and published as a book/article. Live discourse has NO copyright.
COPYRIGHT = NEGATIVE RIGHT
Active protection only when there is (i) infringement (ii) complaint and (iii) suit for injunction and damages
Unless there is infringement → no active protection
Presumption against infringement due to prohibition in law
📝 Unlike a trademark which you actively use to identify goods, copyright just sits there protecting you if someone copies. You don't need to enforce it continuously — but it only 'activates' when someone actually infringes.
4 Ways Copyright Can Be Exploited
(a) Reproduction in multiple copies
(b) Conversion into visual presentation (film / enactment)
(c) Translation into other languages
(d) Adaptation in context
⚠️ EXPLOITATION RISK
As exploitation takes original work farther from original form → existence of copyright must be tested on the anvil of substantive similarity without impinging on others' copyright to their own development of associated ideas.
21.2.2 — Trade Mark (Detailed) ⇧ Index
™️ Trade Mark — Registration, Sector Limitation & GST
WHAT IT IS
Visual symbol of identification applied to articles offered for sale to indicate identity of a particular manufacturer
Distinctiveness is key to protection
💡 Eg.605: Double stitch design on back pocket of jeans (various brands) is a trademark of each brand.
Eg.606: Dancing doll motif is a trade mark of Oreo cookie.
SECTOR LIMITATION
Registration is limited to 'sector' of articles produced
Grant does NOT prevent rest of world from using that mark in a different sector
💡 Eg.607: Swoosh is Nike's trademark for shoes/sportswear/garments. Swoosh can be registered by another company manufacturing surgical implements — different sector.
CROSS-SECTOR EXPANSION
Trademark holders expand into non-core sectors to prevent new entrants — those sectors sometimes become profitable too
💡 Eg.608: 'Wills' is a tobacco company trademark. But Wills brand of garments has also become popular.
Eg.609: Liquor company producing ads for bottled water / music CDs / garments (surrogate advertising).
INFRINGEMENT RELIEF
Not limited to profits and damages — also includes destruction of stock of articles bearing unauthorized use of trade mark
Mala fide registration and hoarding are barred
21.2.4 — Patent (Detailed) ⇧ Index
🔬 Patent — Intangible Asset & GST Treatment
WHAT IT IS
Granted for (i) uniqueness and (ii) non-obviousness of invention
Enables disclosure of invention without fear of copying
Patent is real property in and of itself
Patented invention = intangible asset
📝 Patent is actual property — like land or a building. It can be bought, sold, licensed, mortgaged. It is an asset on the balance sheet.
DURATION
Originally 14 years — now revised to 20 years from date of invention
After expiry → generic duplicates freely produced in same market
📝 After the 20-year window, the invention becomes public domain. Anyone can make and sell it — hence the pharma industry's cycle of branded drugs becoming generic medicines.
OBLIGATIONS & LIMITS
Patent-holders cannot hoard the invention
Must work the invention to serve society
(Mass-produce OR license to others)
Bad faith second inventions to counter generics — protection denied
📝 You cannot sit on a patent and block everyone else — you must either produce the invention yourself or license others to produce it. Society's interest comes first.
21.2.5–21.2.6 — Semiconductor Layout & Geographic Indications ⇧ Index
🔌 Semiconductor Layout (21.2.5) & 🌍 Geographic Indications (21.2.6)
SEMICONDUCTOR LAYOUT
Semiconductor IC Layout Designs Act, 2000
Protects efficiency of IC design
NOT same as industrial design under Designs Act
Registration criteria: (i) Originality (ii) Innovativeness (iii) Inherently distinctive (iv) Distinguishable
📝 IC design = the 'map' of electronic circuits on a chip. Better IC design = better performance (speed, power efficiency). This Act protects that map — not the chip itself.
GEOGRAPHIC INDICATIONS (GI)
GI of Goods (Registration & Protection) Act, 1999
Mark of origin of article — not a trademark
Non-assignable — belongs to the region
Goods produced within scope of registration may bear the GI mark
📝 Darjeeling tea, Alphonso mango, Kolhapuri chappal — these are GI marks. They belong to the producers of that region collectively. No single person can own or assign a GI mark.
💡 GI mark cannot be part of a business sale/transfer — it is non-assignable. So in a GST 'going concern' transfer, GI rights cannot transfer — they stay with the region.
21.2.7–21.2.8 — Plant Varieties & Confidential Information ⇧ Index
🌱 Plant Varieties (21.2.7) & 🔒 Confidential Information (21.2.8)
PLANT VARIETIES
Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmer's Rights Act, 2001
Sui generis legislation — unique, no parallel
Gene sequence-based uniqueness
Farmers: NOT barred from re-sowing their produce
Breeders: Pay royalty to Government to retain registration and license for commercial reproduction
📝 Sui generis = one of its kind. This law exists only to protect plant breeders. Royalty paid by breeder to Government = price for retaining the registration, not a tax.
CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION
Trade secret, non-public financial info, payroll data, business model, financing method
Injunctive relief + damages even without NDA
NDA/confidentiality agreements only make the limitation more explicit
EXCEPTION — WHEN DISCLOSURE IS PERMITTED
(i) Natural to course of discharging agreed obligations
(ii) Required to a public authority by law
CEASES TO BE CONFIDENTIAL
When owner deliberately or otherwise publishes the said information
📝 Prejudice to owner is sufficient to bring action — you don't need to prove actual loss. The moment your confidential business model is leaked to a competitor, you can sue — even if you haven't suffered monetary loss yet.
21.3.1–21.3.5 — Sectoral Insights: Lyrics, Music, Films, Broadcast, Performer ⇧ Index
🎵 Sectoral Insights — Entertainment & Media Copyright
21.3.1 LYRICS
Copyright in lyrics = independent of copyright in sound recording
Lyrics printed = subject of copyright
Same lyrics expressed in musical notations = copyright in the recording
Music ≠ Song
Music = sound; Song = presentation of lyrics musically
📝 The lyricist, music composer, and sound recording producer each hold separate and independent copyrights in the same song.
21.3.2 MUSIC
Music ≠ sound recording
Composer = author of music
Producer = author of sound recording (music with/without lyrics)
Live performance has NO copyright
Recording of live performance = has copyright
Changed by Sections 38, 38A, 38B Copyright Act
📝 A concert performer who sings live owns no copyright in that live performance. But if it is recorded — the recording has copyright with the producer.
21.3.3 FILMS
Copyright in cinematographic film = with producer
Film copyright ≠ copyright in sound recording for film
Film copyright ≠ copyright in story, dialogues, lyrics, background score
Storyboard is developed to establish copyright
Reference: AV Meyappan ibid
📝 A film has multiple overlapping copyrights. Producer owns the film. Director, writer, lyricist, composer each own their contributions separately. GST licensing deals must specify which copyright is being licensed.
21.3.4 BROADCAST RIGHTS
Broadcaster obtains license from copyright owner of individual songs or other original work → creates compilation/playlist
Broadcaster's copyright = with respect to the playlist after it is broadcast once
Applies to: TV programs, OTT series, music countdown shows
📝 When a radio station creates a playlist for its show, the station has a separate copyright in that playlist — not in the underlying songs, but in the arrangement/selection itself.
21.3.5 PERFORMER'S RIGHTS
Protected under Sections 38, 38A, 38B Copyright Act
Similar to broadcast rights
That specific rendition = subject matter of copyright
Subsists until 50 years from beginning of calendar year next following the year of performance
📝 Section 38: A performer (singer, actor, dancer) gets a special right — 'performer's right' — in their specific performance. Even if the song belongs to the producer, the performer's rendition is separately protected.
21.3.6–21.3.9 — Statutes, Case Law, Assignment & License ⇧ Index
📚 Statutes, Case Law, Assignment & License — Copyright Dimensions
21.3.6 STATUTES
Legislative work published in Official Gazette
Brief commentary / inserts with relevant decisions = copyright of publisher
Purpose of publishing original work — even if it does not sell — is to establish copyright
📝 Bare Acts are in public domain. But a publisher's annotated version with case notes and commentary has copyright in those additions.
21.3.7 CASE LAW
Citation of case law belongs to publisher
Decision is of the Court
Headnotes = copyright of publisher
Full text of decision appended to headnote
Headnotes are different for each published version — but full text of decision remains unaltered
📝 The Supreme Court's judgment itself is public domain. But LexisNexis or SCC headnotes for that judgment are copyrighted by the respective publisher. Copying headnotes without permission = infringement.
21.3.8 ASSIGNMENT
Copyright owners assign exploitation rights to commercial entity for consideration
May be general OR specific (language/duration/geography)
Even though intended to be permanent → usually subject to 5-year limit or lifetime of author
⚠️ GST CRITICAL
Must NOT assume assignment is permanent without establishing it
Permanence is limited by maximum duration of copyright
After copyright expiry → IP still with author but in permissively unprotected condition → assignment meaningless
📝 If assignment is not truly permanent — it is a temporary transfer = services under GST. Do NOT treat it as goods (permanent transfer) without proof of permanence.
21.3.9 LICENSE
Permission to do something that involves use of copyright
Assignment of copyright itself is NOT within scope of this authorization

Exclusive license = license severable from article
Implied license = article and license to IP embedded are inseparable
💡 Eg.611: Laptop manufacturer gets assignment of copyrights in the OS to be loaded in each laptop manufactured.
Eg.612: Customer who purchases the laptop gets a single non-transferable, royalty-free license to use the OS in the course of using the laptop.
21.3.10–21.3.15 — Copyrighted Article, Societies, Creative Commons, Franchise, Goodwill & VDA ⇧ Index
🏢 Copyrighted Article, Societies, Franchise, Goodwill & Virtual Digital Assets
21.3.10 COPYRIGHTED ARTICLE
Ownership of copyright ≠ ownership of copyrighted article
Author owns the book (copyright)
Customer owns a copy of the book (containing author's copyright)
Owning a copy ≠ owning the copyright
💡 Eg.613: One can purchase MsOffice software for ₹30,000 — but this does NOT imply ownership of the MsOffice itself.
📝 Buying a book or software gives you a copy — not the copyright. The copy is taxable as goods; the copyright/license is taxable as a separate supply (service if temporary).
21.3.11 COPYRIGHT SOCIETIES
Section 33 Copyright Act → establishment of copyright societies
Authors join as members to redress grievances
Grant permissions for standard uses (general use and broadcasting)
💡 Eg.614: IPRS (Indian Performing Right Society) — literary works of music.
Eg.615: IRRO (Indian Reprographic Rights Org.) — photo-copying works.
Eg.616: ISRA — performing artists.
Eg.617: RMPL — sound recording.
21.3.12 CREATIVE COMMONS
Where creative persons permit free reproduction of their work
Opposite of copyright
Sometimes referred to as 'copylefted'
📝 Creative commons = the author says 'use my work freely.' It is the opposite of 'all rights reserved.' GST on supply of copylefted work = supply without consideration = outside scope of GST (unless commercial).
21.3.13 FRANCHISE
Requires 'secret sauce' forming pith of business
Includes: (i) Patented invention (ii) License to use trademark (iii) Disclosure of confidential info
Unless all elements present → cannot be a franchise business
⚠️ GOING CONCERN — GST TRAP
Transfer of business as going concern = exempt from GST
BUT Franchisee CANNOT make such transfer — IPRs are vested with Franchisor not Franchisee
Franchisee can transfer assets & liabilities — NOT the franchise itself
Unless securing franchise is pre-condition → transfer of Franchisee's business ≠ going concern transfer
💡 Eg.618: Car wash cannot be replicated under franchise (no secret sauce / IP).
Eg.619: Pizza chain can be under franchise (patented recipe + trademark).
21.3.14 GOODWILL
Aggregation of various rights
Individual rights can be protected; entire goodwill cannot be protected under single legislation
Goodwill = real property & asset (can be bought, sold, destroyed, valued)
'Passing off' = usurpation of someone else's goodwill (criminal misappropriation)
💡 Eg.620: 'adidas' spelt as 'adidas' on similar looking shoes = passing off.
Eg.621: Blue-green colour label on packaged water to take advantage of resemblance to Bisleri water colour = passing off.
📝 Goodwill is an asset but no single law protects it as a whole. When goodwill is transferred in a business sale, its valuation for GST purposes requires identifying which rights (trademark, trade name, customer relationships) are being transferred.
21.3.15 VIRTUAL DIGITAL ASSETS
Expenditure ≠ asset
Asset = real property = rights qua over a thing
Rights = enforceable in law against infringement or threatened infringement

VDA = decentralized & virtual structure of validation without oversight of regulator or regulation
Unless Court of law assistance available → no right is really enforceable
CONCLUSION
VDA may be immutable but is NOT enforceable
If not enforceable → not a right
If not a right → not a property
If not a real property → not an asset
No protection = No right
📝 Cryptocurrency may have market value — but if no court can protect your ownership rights in it, it is not legally 'property.' This has enormous implications for GST on crypto transactions and ITC on crypto-related expenditure.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 21 | Intellectual Property Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision or principle.
Temporary vs Permanent Transfer — Goods vs Services Legal Fiction
GST Principle | Schedule II CGST Act | Para 21.1 & 21.3.8
Key Principle
Legal fiction has not spared 'temporary transfers' by declaring them to be services while implying that 'permanent transfers' are goods. Assignment may be general or specific (language, duration or geography) for their exploitation. Even though assignment may be intended to be permanent, it is usually subject to time limit of five (5) years or lifetime of author. For this reason, assignment must not be assumed to be permanent, at least not for GST purposes, without establishing that the assignment is permanent. Permanence is limited by the maximum duration of the copyright.
When to Use — When assessee or Department classifies copyright assignment as 'permanent transfer of IP' (goods) to claim concessional rate or exclusion. Counter: copyright assignment is almost always limited to 5 years or lifetime of author — making it a temporary transfer = services. GST at service rate, not goods rate. Establish permanence before applying goods classification.
Ownership of Copyrighted Article ≠ Ownership of Copyright
Legal Principle | Copyright Act | Para 21.3.10 | Eg. 613
Key Principle
Ownership of copyright is not the same as ownership of a copyrighted article. Author has ownership of a book. Customer who has purchased the book has ownership of a copy of the book which contains the author's copyright. Ownership of a copy of the copyrighted article is not the same as ownership of the copyright. Purchase of the article includes non-transferable and royalty-free license to the IP that is integral to the identity of that article.
When to Use — When Department argues that sale of software/book/music CD includes embedded IP as a separate taxable service (GST on both the CD and the copyright license). Counter: purchase of article includes an implied, non-transferable, royalty-free license to embedded IP. There is only one supply — sale of the article. No separate taxable license supply arises.
Franchise Transfer ≠ Going Concern Transfer — IPR Vests with Franchisor
GST Principle | Schedule III / Notification 12/2017 | Para 21.3.13
Key Principle
Transfer of business as a going concern is exempt from GST. But a Franchisee cannot make such a transfer since IPRs are vested with Franchisor and not with Franchisee. Franchisee can, at best, transfer all assets and liabilities but not the franchise itself — that has been directly secured from the Franchisor. Unless securing franchise is a pre-condition, transfer of business of Franchisee will not be a going concern transfer at all.
When to Use — When a Franchisee tries to transfer their franchise business as a 'going concern' (exempt from GST). Counter: the core IP (patent, trademark, trade secrets) vests with the Franchisor — not the Franchisee. Franchisee can only transfer physical assets. Without the IP (the 'secret sauce'), there is no going concern. GST applies on the transfer.
Virtual Digital Assets — Not Real Property, Not an Asset
Legal Principle | Para 21.3.15
Key Principle
Expenditure is not an asset. Asset is that which is real property. Real property is that which represents rights qua over a thing. Rights are those enforceable in law against infringement or threatened infringement. At the very heart of virtual digital assets lies their decentralized and virtual structure of validation without coming under the oversight of a regulator or a regulation. Unless the assistance of a Court of law is available, no right is really an enforceable right. It may be immutable but unless it is enforceable, it is not a right. And when it is not an enforceable right, it is not property. That which is not a real property, notwithstanding that there is a well-accepted market to buy and sell, it is not an asset. No protection, no right.
When to Use — In GST disputes involving cryptocurrency, NFTs, or other VDAs — where assessee claims ITC on VDA-related expenses or Department seeks to tax VDA transactions. Use to argue: VDA without regulatory oversight has no enforceable rights = not real property = not an asset. GST analysis of VDA transactions must start from this foundational conclusion.
Copyright — Negative Right | Active Only on Infringement
Legal Principle | Copyright Act | Para 21.2.1
Key Principle
Copyright is therefore a negative right. Existence of copyright will be examined only when there is infringement, complaint and suit for injunction and damages. Unless there is infringement, there is no active protection to its owner because there is a presumption against infringement due to prohibition in law. Copyright encourages disclosure with assurance of protection in the event of infringement. There is no copyright in an idea — when that idea is reduced to some physical form then copyright emerges in the idea visible in this physical form.
When to Use — When Department seeks to impose GST on copyright licensing transactions where no active exploitation or infringement is occurring. Also useful to establish when a copyright is commercially active vs. dormant. Use the 'idea vs expression' principle (Eg. 603, 604) to challenge claims that a mere idea (business plan, concept note) carries assignable copyright value for GST purposes.
IP Inherently Included in Business Transfer — Not a Separate Taxable Supply
GST Principle | Para 21.1 | Going Concern Transfer
Key Principle
Often, intellectual properties are not the object of contract but are inherently included when a business is transferred. Being a private matters' legislation, IP laws are subject to strict construction, except to the extent of anti-avoidance and abuse of inherent rights. Intangible immovable properties are considered in this deliberation. Similarly, deliberation on intangible movable properties is necessary to be meaningful.
When to Use — When Department demands GST separately on IP (copyright, trademark, know-how) transferred as part of a going concern sale (which is exempt). Counter: IP is inherently included in the business transfer — it is not the object of the contract but an incident of it. The going concern exemption covers the bundle including embedded IP. Separate GST on IP component is not warranted.
GI Mark — Non-Assignable, Cannot Be Part of Business Transfer
Legal Principle | GI of Goods Act 1999 | Para 21.2.6
Key Principle
Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999 grants registration and protection to 'geographic indicators' which is not a registrable mark under Trade Marks Act. GI mark is non-assignable. Once registration is granted, goods produced within the scope of such registration are permitted to bear this mark. GI is not a trademark — it is a mark of the origins of the article. Use of a registered GI mark demonstrates the virtues associated with the article produced in a given geographic region.
When to Use — When a business transfer involves use of a GI mark (Darjeeling tea, Alphonso mango producer). Counter: GI mark is non-assignable — it belongs to the region's producers collectively, not to any individual. Therefore it cannot be valued or transferred as part of any business transfer. No GST can be levied on transfer of GI rights — because GI rights cannot be transferred at all.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 22 — Medical Laws

🏥 Chapter 22 — Medical Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Exempt / Valid / Permitted
NOT Exempt / Caution / Taxable
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
22.1 Relevance to GST
22.2.1 Medicaments
22.2.2 Recognized Pharmacopeia
22.2.3 Medical Education
22.2.4 Exemption
22.2.5 Authorized Medical Practitioner
22.2.6 Para-medic
22.2.7 Clinical Establishment
22.3 Sectoral Insights
22.3.1 PHC
22.3.2 SHC
22.3.3 Tertiary Care
22.3.4 Quaternary Care
22.3.5 Diagnostic Centre
22.3.6 Clinical Trials
22.3.7–10 Other/Bone-setter/Midwifery/Unauth.
22.3.11 Diagnosis
22.3.12–14 Treatment/Care/Surgery
22.3.15–21 Pregnancy/Mental/Transplant etc.
22.3.22 Teaching Hospitals
22.4 Regulatory Oversight
22.4.1 Non-clinical Establishments
22.4.2 Medical Tourism
22.4.3 Incorporeal Form
22.4.4 Rent-a-Hospital
22.4.5 Health-tech
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
22.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🏥 Medical Laws — Relevance to GST
Healthcare exemption | Narrow scope, Liberal application is perilous
⚠️ KEY WARNING — NARROW EXEMPTION
Exemption in GST in respect of healthcare services occupies a narrow space but its liberal application is perilous.
Care must be taken to identify the nature of services provided — do NOT allow curative effects alone to motivate claim of exemption.
Every substance that is curative is NOT medicament. Every procedure that is curative is NOT healthcare service.
📝 Just because something heals or cures does NOT make it a 'healthcare service' exempt from GST. The law has a very specific and narrow meaning of healthcare service — and stretching it too liberally can lead to incorrect exemption claims.
WHY MEDICAL LAWS MATTER FOR GST
Healthcare is heavily regulated. Assertions made under those regulations greatly affect the tax positions taken in GST. Understanding nature of assertions made under those regulations helps validate tax positions taken. Curative effects alone are not a reliable basis to claim GST exemption.
22.2.1 — Medicaments ⇧ Index
💊 Medicaments — 5-Parameter Test
To be a Medicament — ALL parameters must be satisfied (empirically & pharmacologically):
1. Existence of physiological condition
(requires medical intervention)
2. Under any of 5 recognized medical pharmacopeia
3. Prescriptive by diagnosis
4. Curative effects
5. Ingestion in specified dosage
RULE There can be NO medicament that is prescribed to be ingested by a healthy person. Pre-requirement: there must be some physiological condition requiring medical intervention.

Dietary prescriptions do NOT become medicaments just because they are curative and were not part of previous diet.
📝 Supplements, herbal drinks, or diet changes — even if a doctor recommends them — are NOT medicaments unless they satisfy all 5 parameters. The mere fact that they cure is not enough.
💡 Eg.622: Citrus fruits → NOT medicaments (though cure Vitamin C deficiency/scurvy).
💡 Eg.623: Sugar water → NOT medicament (though alleviates hypo-diabetic condition).
💡 Eg.624: Brandy with warm water → NOT medicament (relief comes as bronchial dilator).
💡 Eg.625: Coffee → NOT medicament (caffeine dilates microcapillaries — relief mechanism identified, but not a medicament).
Drugs & Cosmetics Act — Key Definitions:
COSMETIC — Sec 2(aaa)
Article intended to be rubbed/poured/sprinkled/sprayed on or introduced into human body for cleansing, beautifying, promoting attractiveness or altering appearance
📝 Cosmetics enhance appearance — they are NOT medicaments even if they have some curative side-effects.
DRUG — Sec 4(b)
(i) Medicines for internal/external use of humans or animals — for diagnosis, treatment, mitigation or prevention of disease/disorder including repelling mosquitoes
(ii) Substances to affect structure/function of human body or destroy vermin/insects causing disease
(iii) Substances as components of drug including empty gelatin capsules
(iv) Devices for diagnosis/treatment/mitigation/prevention of disease
📝 Drug definition is very wide — includes devices too. But only REGULATED drugs (with approval) are admitted as medicaments for GST purposes. Approval is key — approval dictates HSN code to be applied.
CRITICAL RULE
Only regulated drugs are admitted as medicaments. Chapter 30 of Customs Tariff includes all medicaments — for adult, paediatric or veterinary use.
Approval determines drugs. Underlying therapeutic benefits are NOT open to challenge once approval is granted.
Ineffectiveness of a medicament on a particular user does NOT alter its identity as a 'drug'.
Goods consumed in provision of services will NOT result in transfer of property and will be a supply of services.
💡 Eg.626: Turmeric added to food for anti-septic properties → NOT a drug (naturally occurring, in diet).
💡 Eg.627: Nutritional yeast consumed for B-complex → NOT a drug (nutritional, not regulated).
22.2.2 — Recognized Medical Pharmacopeia ⇧ Index
📚 Recognized Medical Pharmacopeia — 5 Systems
(a) Allopathy
