(b) Homeopathy
(c) Ayurveda
(d) Unani
(e) Siddha
NOT RECOGNIZED
Naturopathy = ingenious combination of various traditional cures and procedures — has brought great benefits but is NOT a recognized pharmacopeia.

Customary cures are NOT recognized medicament because recognition requires certain validation of course of treatment and documentation based on certain standards.
📝 Only treatment recognized under one of these 5 systems can qualify as 'healthcare service' for GST exemption. Treatments outside these 5 — however beneficial — are NOT exempt.
💡 Eg.628: Kashaya or Kada is a home remedy for flu but since formulation is documented, tested, validated and prescriptive — it will NOT be a medicament (lacks regulatory approval).
💡 Eg.629: Cold pack on forehead is a prescribed course of treatment (although not a medicament) for controlling fever — it is healthcare service, not a medicament.
22.2.3 — Medical Education ⇧ Index
🎓 Medical Education — GST Implications
Regulatory Framework for Medical Education:
National Medical Commission
(formerly MCI 1934 — dissolved 2010)
UG & PG medical programs
National Board of Education
Teaching hospitals → super-specialization (DNB — Diplomate National Board)
Under Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
Indian Nursing Council Act, 1947
Nursing diploma/degree/PG/certificates
Includes midwifery programs
Paramedical Board of India, MoHRD
Lab technician, medical records technologist, dialysis technician, OT technician etc.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Education is NOT necessarily provided in a class-room environment. Education imparted by carrying out actual procedures cannot be taught in class-room — it is done by practical work.
Practice-oriented, PG and super-speciality programs in healthcare are imparted in an approved Medical Establishment. In other words, 'hospital is the educational institution' for such programs.
📝 When a hospital provides practical training to medical students, it is acting as an educational institution — not just a healthcare provider. This affects GST treatment — educational services may be separately exempt under Entry 66 of exemption notification.
22.2.4 — Exemption ⇧ Index
✅ GST Exemption for Healthcare Services — Entry 74
EXEMPT
GST exemption is limited to (i) Diagnosis (ii) Treatment and (iii) Care
Exception for: (a) Hair transplant (b) Cosmetic surgery (c) Plastic surgery
BUT NOT EXCEPTED when required due to: (i) Congenital defects (ii) Developmental abnormalities (iii) Injury (iv) Trauma
📝 Even cosmetic surgery is EXEMPT if done to correct a birth defect, development abnormality, injury, or trauma. It is only elective cosmetic surgery (for enhancement/aesthetics) that loses exemption.
Supplier of Exempt Services — Entry 74 requires BOTH:
ELEMENT 1 — THE SUPPLY
Service must be diagnosis, treatment or care — must fall within defined scope
ELEMENT 2 — THE SUPPLIER
Supplier making the supply must be:
(a) Clinical establishment
(b) Authorized medical practitioner (AMP)
(c) Para-medic
⚠️ BOTH MUST BE PRESENT
Supplier must have 'legal authority' — not merely expertise. Reference to (i) supply involved AND (ii) supplier making the supply are BOTH found in Entry 74. When one of them is present but NOT the other → exemption is NOT available.
📝 Experience and expertise alone do NOT qualify someone as a 'supplier' for healthcare exemption. Legal authority (AMP registration, para-medic qualification) is separately required. A highly skilled bone-setter without AMP qualification cannot claim exemption.
22.2.5 — Authorized Medical Practitioner (AMP) ⇧ Index
👨‍⚕️ Authorized Medical Practitioner (AMP) — Legal Authority, Not Just Expertise
HOW TO BECOME AMP
After completing theoretic and practical study → graduate must enrol with State Council → subject to governance regulations on practice and professional conduct → entitled to offer healthcare services as AMP
AMP IN MEDICAL ESTABLISHMENT
Practice in form of company or firm (Medical Establishment) is permitted — provided the field of medicine is only practiced by AMP.
No medical procedure can be conducted sans an AMP who will be answerable for all legal consequences.
Even where expert consultant attends — an AMP must be designated in each case.
Medical Establishments with full-time AMP = Registered Medical Practitioner (RMP) — identified with each case attended.
AMP or RMP who will be answerable in tort for vicarious liability in medico-legal cases involving professional negligence or misconduct.
📝 The AMP is the legally accountable person for every patient interaction. A company or hospital cannot practice medicine — only the AMP in it can. This is crucial for GST exemption — healthcare services must be under the seal and authority of an AMP.
KEY GST RULE
Healthcare services do NOT lose their identity based on form of organization when an AMP is available on record and services are offered under the seal and authority of said AMP.
Merely because establishment does NOT have Clinical Establishment registration is NOT fatal to their claim for GST exemption — because the services do not become any less than healthcare services provided by AMP.
📝 No Clinical Establishment registration? Still OK for GST exemption IF services are genuinely provided under an AMP's authority and seal. The AMP is the identity of the healthcare service — not the registration certificate.
22.2.6 — Para-medic ⇧ Index
🩺 Para-medic — Legal Authority Required
DEFINITION
Persons holding recognized diploma or other qualifying certificate issued by Ministry of HRD, Government of India = a 'para medic'. Nature of their services may involve direct engagement (privity of contract) with Patient OR via an AMP.
TWO MODES OF SERVICE
Para medic services may be:
(i) Pursuant to a course of treatment or care prescribed by an AMP — with para medic implementing it; OR
(ii) Directly engaged (privity of contract with Patient) — for specific para-medical services
💡 Eg.630: Physiotherapist may be directly engaged for post-surgery rehabilitation.
💡 Eg.631: Home-testing of blood glucose levels may be offered directly by technician (phlebotomist) drawing blood — reports given to Diabetologist for monitoring.
💡 Eg.632: Under instructions of AMP, radiology technician may operate x-ray machine to take image and send it to AMP within Medical Establishment for further diagnosis.
⚠️ ANCILLARY SUPPORT ≠ PARA-MEDIC
Ancillary support services in a Medical Establishment will NOT be considered as para medic even if their services are based on instructions of AMP or para medic.
Test of legal relations is important:
• Master-servant relationship → tax consequences of master prevail over actions of servant too
• Principal-principal relationship → authority of immediate service provider prevails
Ostensible authority to contract must be tested — asserted authority not be readily admitted while examining applicability of GST exemption.
📝 A hospital cleaner, food supplier, or security guard at a hospital is NOT a para-medic. Even if they follow hospital rules. The test is the legal relationship — are they providing healthcare services under qualified para-medical authority? If not, their services are taxable.
22.2.7 — Clinical Establishment ⇧ Index
🏥 Clinical Establishment — Definition & GST Test
GST EXEMPTION NOTIFICATION — Sec (s)
"Clinical establishment" means a hospital, nursing home, clinic, sanatorium or any other institution by whatever name called, that offers services or facilities requiring diagnosis or treatment or care for illness, injury, deformity, abnormality or pregnancy in any recognised system of medicines in India, or a place established as an independent entity or a part of an establishment to carry out diagnostic or investigative services of diseases.
CENTRAL ACT — Section 4(c)
Clinical establishment means: (i) Hospital, maternity home, nursing home, dispensary, clinic, sanatorium or institution offering services requiring diagnosis, treatment or care for illness, injury, deformity, abnormality or pregnancy in any recognised system of medicine — administered or maintained by any person or body of persons, whether incorporated or not; OR (ii) Place established as independent entity or part of establishment in sub-clause (i), in connection with diagnosis or treatment of diseases where pathological, bacteriological, genetic, radiological, chemical, biological investigations or other diagnostic or investigative services — usually carried on by any person, incorporated or not.

Includes clinical establishments owned/controlled/managed by: (a) Government/dept; (b) Trust, public or private; (c) Corporation/society under Central/Provincial/State Act; (d) Local authority; (e) Single doctor.
Does NOT include Armed Forces clinical establishments.
TRUE TEST — Not 'Location' but 'Infrastructure & AMP'
Test is NOT of the 'location' but the 'infrastructure and accountability of an AMP'.
It is often considered that clinical establishment registration is the final word on GST exemption — THIS IS WRONG.
Rural eye camp / blood donation camp → healthcare services performed there — but no such registration is feasible (no such registration will be granted).
Registration as a Clinical Establishment is NOT determinative of the identity of Supplier of healthcare services — the AMP located in such Clinical Establishment is.
📝 A doctor doing a rural eye camp or a tele-medicine consultation doesn't have a 'clinical establishment' registration — but services are still healthcare services. The AMP's authority determines the exemption, not the registration certificate.
💡 Eg.633: Anesthesiologist does not practice in her own clinic but visits various hospitals and participates during surgery — still provides healthcare services.
💡 Eg.634: Tele-medicine is super-specialist services offered by one AMP to other on-site AMPs while providing diagnosis, treatment or care to a Patient.
22.3 — Sectoral Insights Overview ⇧ Index
🔍 Sectoral Insights — Healthcare Delivery Models & GST Treatment
Healthcare delivery operates across a spectrum. GST treatment depends on identifying: (i) who is providing the service (AMP/para-medic/clinical establishment); (ii) what is being provided (diagnosis/treatment/care); and (iii) the legal relations between parties (privity of contract).
22.3.1–22.3.4 — PHC / SHC / Tertiary / Quaternary Care ⇧ Index
🏥 Healthcare Delivery Tiers — GST Treatment
PHC
Primary Health Centre
Smallest unit
AMP must be designated
Only clinical examination & consultations — no pathology/radiology/in-patient
Can be in residence, not necessarily commercial
Where AMP not designated → NOT a PHC
SHC
Secondary Health Centre
More than PHC: in-patient services + minor/major-OT
Nursing homes, maternity centres, hospitals
Surgery with in-house or consulting Surgeons
Main-stay of healthcare establishments
Protocol-based diagnosis & treatment
Deviation = disciplinary proceedings
TC — Tertiary Care
SHCs offering super-specialities:
Surgical oncology, multiple-organ impairment, nuclear medicine, organ transplant, complex procedures
Usually 'referral' hospitals
Generally no direct admissions without prior AMP evaluation in SHC
📝 Certain conditions (cancer, HIV) CANNOT be treated by graduate doctor (MBBS) — it would be professional misconduct. Must be referred to specialist/TC.
QC — Quaternary Care
Experimental medication
Extension of TC — referral hospitals
Treatment may lack standardized protocols
Transparent with ICMR & Ministry of Health
Experimental medication: stem cell therapy (hematopoietic cell / bone marrow / cord blood) under ICMR guidelines
NOT 'goods' for GST — customized solutions = 'services'
📝 Experimental treatments at QC level are customized — not off-the-shelf products. Therefore they are 'services' even if medication is involved. QC is like a job-worker to TC-SHC.
22.3.5 Diagnostic Centre | 22.3.6 Clinical Trials ⇧ Index
🔬 Diagnostic Services & Clinical Trials — GST Treatment
22.3.5 — Diagnostic Centre
EXEMPT
Diagnosis = healthcare service even without treatment following from it. Direct engagement (privity of contract) with Patient NOT necessary to constitute diagnosis where diagnostic procedure requires presence of Patient.
📝 A pathology lab or radiology centre doing tests is providing healthcare service — even if the doctor who will treat is different. The act of examination/testing is itself exempt.
B2B engagement (lab-to-doctor) also exempt from GST.
Person carrying out examination/administering tests must be AMPs in field of Pathology or Radiology.
Report must be under authority and license of AMP concerned — paramedical technician may operate equipment but CANNOT issue report.
💡 Eg.635: Pathology lab conducting tests of biomedical samples (blood, sputum, tissue) = diagnostic service.
💡 Eg.636: Radiology centre taking x-ray = diagnostic service.
💡 Eg.637: General health check whether for selling insurance policy, employment selection or routine check = diagnostic service.
💡 Eg.643: Covid home sample collection by agency at ₹1,400 + lab AMP issues report at ₹1,000 — still healthcare service if sample collection agency comprises paramedical technicians.
22.3.6 — Clinical Trials
TWO POSSIBLE CHARACTERIZATIONS
(a) Clinical trial = service supplied by Clinical Establishment TO Pharma Company
(b) Clinical trial = payment of healthcare services by Pharma Company on behalf of Patients
ANSWER
Lies in terms of engagement between the 3 parties. Supply in GST = not benefits accruing but actual flow of consideration from Recipient to Supplier.
Clinical trials on residue of biomedical samples from tested Patients or from Donor Patients = service simplicitor between Pathology lab and Pharma Company.
All clinical trials CANNOT be painted with same brush and denied GST exemption.
📝 Simplicitor = simply, without additional conditions. Tax treatment turns on the terms of engagement with sponsoring entities. Each clinical trial arrangement must be separately analysed.
22.3.7–10 — Other Care / Bone-setters / Midwifery / Unauthorized Treatment ⇧ Index
⚠️ Care vs Cure | AMP Authority Threshold
22.3.7 — Other Care
Care must be differentiated from cure:
• Physiotherapy ≠ chiropractic adjustments
• Personal gym instructor (keto/paleo diet) ≠ dietician services
📝 Just being in the health/wellness space does NOT qualify as healthcare service. The specific nature of what is offered and by whom determines GST treatment.
22.3.8 — Bone-setters
NOT EXEMPT
Treatment for dislocation/fracture by vaidyas of bone-setters will NOT be exempt from GST as healthcare service because they are NOT AMP.
No matter how scientific, accurate, or curative.
📝 Legal authority (AMP qualification) is the threshold — curative results alone cannot grant exemption.
22.3.9 — Midwifery
EXEMPT
Specific branch of para medical study and training → exempt from GST provided person providing services has legal authority to provide these services.
NOT by experience elder in the village who is akin to a vaidya.
22.3.10 — Unauthorized Treatment
NOT EXEMPT
Healthcare services exempt ONLY when provided by persons with legal authority or capacity to contract. GST exemption will evade any over-enthusiasm.
Duration of care uncertain by nature. If offered as 'package' — careful enquiry needed to determine if 'wellness' is paraded as 'healthcare' for unjustified exemption claim.
💡 Eg.638: Lab technician CANNOT prescribe treatment for hypertension — even if by experience typical course is well known.
💡 Eg.639: Pharmacist CANNOT prescribe medicaments — even if typical course known.
💡 Eg.640: Ayurvedic healthcare 4-day couples package in Himachal Pradesh resort needs enquiry into object of healthcare and its conformity with pharmacopeia — may be mixed supply.
22.3.11–22.3.14 — Diagnosis / Treatment / Care / Surgery ⇧ Index
🔬 Core Healthcare Concepts — GST Exemption Scope
DEFINITION — Sec (zg)
"Health care services" means any service by way of diagnosis OR treatment OR care for... [disjunctive 'or' — ANY of these three is sufficient for exemption]
22.3.11 — DIAGNOSIS
Means of detecting a condition by: clinical examination / pathological examination of biomedical samples / radiological imaging / combination of above.
Supplier need NOT reach diagnosis — only perform examination or conduct tests and provide results.
Diagnosis = interpretation of results (by AMP).
Conducting examination (radiology) or tests (pathology) = sub-set of diagnosis.
B2B engagement also exempt.
📝 Exemption is available for (i) conducting examination/tests AND (ii) interpreting results to provide a diagnosis. Treatment commences much later — but exemption starts at diagnosis.
22.3.12 — TREATMENT
Treatment does NOT always 'treat' the disease/condition — it may only address 'symptoms'. Course of treatment determined by healthcare experts — not common understanding.
Expression 'treatment' must be examined in light of specific course determined by healthcare experts.
💡 Eg.644: Paracetamol reduces temperature but does NOTHING to alleviate underlying viral infection.
💡 Eg.645: Analgesics cloud pain receptors but do NOT remedy the fracture causing pain.
22.3.13 — CARE
Care ≠ Cure. Care may result in cure. But care does NOT have to bring about cure. Care does not cease if cure does not result.
Care is itself the service — cure being just the outcome.
Palliative care = maintenance, delay of disease progression and residual care WITHOUT any attempt to remedy the underlying cause.
Care conforming to recognized pharmacopeia = EXEMPT. Care not conforming = NOT exempt.
💡 Eg.646: Chronic diabetes treated with medication that brings down blood glucose — but after medication wears off and food intake, levels may increase again. Treatment was provided and the cure was effective but for that duration only.
22.3.14 — SURGERY
When organ irretrievably affected and imminently capable of affecting other organs or quality of life → Surgeon may advise excision. This fits expression 'treatment' for purposes of GST because it is the recognized course of treatment.
Surgery without consent = punishable under IPC (Sec 52, 80, 81, 83, 88, 90, 91, 92, 304-A, 337, 338).
Consensual/optional surgery must be essential course of treatment where benefits > avoiding surgical removal.
💡 Eg.647: Irretrievably infected appendix must be excised — surgical removal is recognized course of treatment.
💡 Eg.648: Tonsillectomy cannot be opted unless documented tonsillitis has recurred 3+ times in a year showing predisposition.
22.3.15–22 — Pregnancy | Mental Health | Organ Transplant | Vaccination | Palliative | Geriatric | Alternate | Teaching ⇧ Index
🌐 Specific Healthcare Categories — GST Treatment Quick Reference
SectionCategoryGST TreatmentKey Rule / Note
22.3.15 Pregnancy EXEMPT GST specifically includes pregnancy within scope of exemption. Pre-conception, pre-natal and post-natal procedures fully included within 'treatment' for GST exemption.
22.3.16 Mental Health EXEMPT Mental Health Act, 1987 provides regulatory framework. Full spectrum of diagnosis, treatment and care for psychological conditions. Temporary impairment of mental balance = defence in IPC if actions cause injury or death to others.
22.3.17 Organ Transplant EXEMPT Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act, 1994 — retrieval or transplantation cannot be undertaken unless establishment is registered and persons, circumstances and consent with regulatory oversight are met. Cannot be done lightly even among family — personal rights of Donor relevant.
22.3.18 Vaccination EXEMPT Vaccination = inducing auto-immune system to accelerate antibody production to resist viral exposure. This is recognized course of 'treatment'. Persons opting out and still surviving do NOT render vaccination any less a healthcare treatment for those who opt in.
22.3.19 Palliative Care EXEMPT (if conforming to pharmacopeia) Care = maintenance + delay of disease progression + residual care without attempt to remedy underlying cause. Care does NOT become less merely because its curative effects do not remove the underlying cause. Palliative care extends to mental health care and support programs. Age is NOT a factor.
22.3.20 Geriatric Care NOT healthcare service Geriatric care is NOT healthcare service although efficiently provided by Medical Establishment. Care for super-senior citizens with non-limiting ailments — main requirement is accommodation and infrastructure for mobility limitations. Akin to 'old age home' services — see Entry 9D in GST exemption notification. Eg.649: Wheelchair accessible premises = age-related limitation NOT a disease.
22.3.21 Alternate Treatment & Care DEPENDS on pharmacopeia recognition Innovative approaches NOT recognized in pharmacopeia are NOT exempt even if they show curative results. It is the recognition that is attached to protocol and consequent liability for maladministration that qualifies for exemption. Eg.650: Keto/paleo diet = NOT exempt; bariatric invasive treatment = IS healthcare service. Eg.651: Yoga & meditation per recognized pharmacopeia (ayur) by authorized persons = healthcare service.
22.3.22 Teaching Hospitals EXEMPT — Entry 66 (Educational) SHC-TC-QC can provide educational services — exempt under Entry 66. Approved teaching hospitals provide practical teaching for MBBS-MD-MS-DNB-MCh and paramedical students. Key test: is the teaching hospital the Supplier of educational services resulting in law-recognized qualification, OR is it engaged by educational institution to provide practical training?
22.4 — Regulatory Oversight ⇧ Index
🏛️ Regulatory Oversight — GST & Healthcare Structures
CORE PRINCIPLE
It is important to locate (i) privity of contract (ii) object of contract (iii) primary liability for quality of services. These facts must be gleaned out of the T&Cs on the website or mobile application (where health-tech is involved).
22.4.1–22.4.5 — Non-clinical / Medical Tourism / Incorporeal / Rent-a-Hospital / Health-tech ⇧ Index
⚖️ Regulatory Oversight — Five Key Issues
SectionIssueGST RuleKey Principle
22.4.1 Non-clinical Establishments May still be EXEMPT AMPs come under oversight of regulators. Establishments come in for scrutiny for healthcare activities without AMP. Without holding out that healthcare services are being provided — massage, relaxation, laughter-therapy and other wellness = NOT healthcare services. But unregistered Clinical Establishments will NOT cease to be exempt from GST where services offered are under seal and authority of an AMP albeit practicing in any incorporeal form.
22.4.2 Medical Tourism CAUTION — Mixed Supply Risk Medical tourism = travel to different cities or countries to avail specific healthcare services. Does NOT involve visit to tourist destination. If it does → services run risk of being 'mixed supply' and forego exemption available in GST for healthcare services. SHCs can now organize ancillary support services where principal supply continues to be healthcare.
22.4.3 Practice in Incorporeal Form EXEMPT AMPs permitted to engage practice in any form of organization — partnership firm, company, society or trust. Healthcare services do not lose their identity based on form of organization when AMP is available on record and services offered under seal and authority of AMP. Merely because establishment does NOT have CE registration is NOT fatal to their claim for GST exemption.
22.4.4 Rent-a-Hospital
(Inverted Billing)		 NOT EXEMPT — Renting of Infrastructure Where privity of contract with Patient is with AMP and NOT SHC — inverted billing pattern. Services of SHC will NOT be admissible to GST exemption as it is akin to rent-a-hospital — since between AMP and Hospital, renting of infrastructure is the object of contract and NOT healthcare services.
Patient→Hospital→AMP: both Hospital and AMP = healthcare services (exempt).
Patient→AMP→Hospital: Hospital = renting infrastructure (TAXABLE).
22.4.5 Health-tech ANALYSE 3 TESTS Health-tech companies often assert they provide healthcare services. GST authorities look for CE registration as if final word — incorrect. CE registration will be granted if basic facilities established AND there are healthcare services where CE registration is NOT required too. Locate (i) privity of contract (ii) object of contract (iii) primary liability for quality. If facts reveal liability is NOT assumed — healthcare is NOT the object — and therefore privity (for healthcare services) is NOT with health-tech establishment.
⚖️ Rent-a-Hospital — Billing Flow Comparison
✓ NORMAL — Both EXEMPT
Patient
→
Hospital
→
AMP
✗ RENT-A-HOSPITAL — Hospital TAXABLE
Patient
→
AMP
→
Hospital
(renting infra)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 22 | Medical Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings on healthcare GST exemption disputes. State them confidently and cite the provision/authority.
Curative Effects Alone — NOT Reliable Basis to Claim GST Exemption
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.1 | Entry 74 GST Exemption Notification for Services
Key Principle
Every substance that is curative is not medicament and every procedure that is curative is not healthcare service. Exemption in GST in respect of healthcare services occupies a narrow space but its liberal application is perilous. Care must be taken to identify the nature of services provided and not allow curative effects alone to motivate claim of exemption. Curative effects alone are not a reliable basis to claim exemption from GST.
When to Use — When assessee claims GST exemption for any wellness, dietary, herbal, naturopathy, or alternate therapy service by merely pointing to curative results. Counter: curative results alone are insufficient — the service must be within recognized medical pharmacopeia, provided by AMP/para-medic/clinical establishment, and must constitute diagnosis, treatment or care specifically.
Entry 74 — BOTH Supply AND Supplier Must Be Present for Exemption
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.2.4 | Entry 74 GST Exemption Notification for Services
Key Principle
Supplier must have 'legal authority' — not merely expertise. Reference to (i) the supply involved and (ii) the supplier making the supply, are both found in the exemption under Entry 74 to exemption notification. When one of them is present but not the other, the exemption is not available.
When to Use — (a) When someone without AMP/para-medic qualification claims healthcare exemption merely because the service rendered has curative value. Counter: legal authority, not expertise, is the threshold. (b) When AMP claims exemption for a supply that is not diagnosis/treatment/care (e.g., cosmetic surgery without medical necessity). Counter: supply must also qualify — both limbs must be satisfied simultaneously.
Medicament — 5-Parameter Test | Approval is Key — Dictates HSN Code
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.2.1 | Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940 | Customs Tariff Chapter 30
Key Principle
For there to be a medicament, certain parameters have emerged based on tests both empirically and pharmacologically applied: existence of physiological condition, under recognized pharmacopeia, prescriptive by diagnosis, curative effects, and ingestion in specified dosage. There can be no medicament that is prescribed to be ingested by a healthy person. Only regulated drugs are admitted as medicaments. Approval determines drugs. Underlying therapeutic benefits are not open to challenge once approval is granted. Ineffectiveness of a medicament on a particular user does not alter its identity as a drug. Approval is key and approval dictates HSN code to be applied.
When to Use — In disputes about classification of products as 'medicaments' under HSN Chapter 30 vs food/cosmetic/supplement. Cite to establish that only regulated and approved drugs are medicaments. Also use to counter Revenue's challenge to therapeutic benefit of an approved drug — once approved, therapeutic basis is not open to challenge by Revenue. Approval determines HSN, not Revenue's assessment of efficacy.
AMP Authority — CE Registration NOT Determinative | AMP in CE Determines Identity
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.2.5 & 22.2.7 | Clinical Establishments (R&R) Act, 2010
Key Principle
Registration as a Clinical Establishment is not determinative of the identity of Supplier of healthcare services — it is the AMP located in such Clinical Establishment. There may be an establishment without a Clinical Establishment registration but will still be exempt from GST where healthcare services are provided under the seal of an AMP. Merely because the establishment does not have a Clinical Establishment registration is not fatal to their claim for GST exemption because the services offered do not become any less than healthcare services provided by AMP. The test is not of the 'location' but the 'infrastructure and accountability of an AMP'.
When to Use — When Department denies GST exemption solely on the ground that Clinical Establishment registration is not available. Counter: CE registration is only evidence — it is not determinative. If AMP is present on record and services are offered under AMP's seal and authority, exemption is available. Also use for rural clinics, eye camps, tele-medicine — where CE registration is practically not available but AMP is present.
Para-medic — Ancillary Support ≠ Para-medic | Test of Legal Relations
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.2.6 | Entry 74 GST Exemption Notification
Key Principle
Ancillary support services in a Medical Establishment will not be considered as para medic even if their services are based on instructions of AMP or para medic. Test of legal relations is important. Where a master-servant relationship is found to exist, then the tax consequences attendant to the authority of the master will prevail over actions of the servant too. But where principal-principal relationship exists, then the authority of the immediate service provider will prevail. Ostensible authority to contract must be tested and asserted authority not be readily admitted while examining applicability of GST exemption.
When to Use — When a hospital claims GST exemption for housekeeping, security, catering, or administrative staff on the ground that they follow AMP/hospital instructions. Counter: the test is the legal relationship — whether they are providing healthcare services under qualified para-medical authority. Master-servant (following instructions) does NOT make an ancillary staff a para-medic. Also use to challenge health-tech companies that claim para-medic status for their app-based service providers.
Rent-a-Hospital — Inverted Billing | Privity of Contract with Patient Determines Exemption
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.4.4 | Object of Contract Test
Key Principle
Where privity of contract with Patient is with AMP and not SHC, there is an inverted billing pattern. Services of SHC will not be admissible to GST exemption as it is akin to rent-a-hospital, since between AMP and Hospital, renting of infrastructure is the object of contract and not healthcare services. Trace direction in which payment flow (of consideration) takes place to locate GST treatment applicable. Hospital pays remuneration to Consultant out of amounts collected from Patient — in this case both payees will be admitted as providing healthcare services as the object of contract between Hospital and AMP is akin to main-contractor and sub-contractor relationship.
When to Use — When a hospital rents out its operation theatre/facilities to visiting surgeons who then bill patients directly. Revenue will argue that hospital's charges to AMP are 'renting of immovable property' — taxable. Support this Revenue position (or defend the hospital) based on who has privity of contract with the Patient. If Patient → Hospital → AMP: both exempt. If Patient → AMP → Hospital: hospital's supply is taxable renting of infrastructure.
Wellness vs Healthcare — Mixed Supply Risk | Object of Contract Test
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.3.10 & 22.4.2 | Object of Contract Test
Key Principle
If any healthcare service is offered as a 'package' more careful enquiry will be necessary to determine if 'wellness' is paraded as 'healthcare' to make unjustified claim to the exemption. Services like massage, relaxation, laughter-therapy and other forms of wellness will not be healthcare services without holding out that healthcare services are being provided. Alternate treatment and care that are not conforming to recognized pharmacopeia may well be caring but are not exempt from GST as 'care' within the scope of definition of 'health care services'. If it does involve visit to tourist destination, services run risk of being 'mixed supply' and forego exemption available in GST for healthcare services.
When to Use — (a) When a resort/spa claims healthcare exemption for its Ayurvedic wellness packages. Challenge: object is tourism/wellness, not healthcare — check if AMP prescribed the treatment and if pharmacopeia is followed. (b) When medical tourism involves bundled travel and treatment — risk of mixed supply denial of exemption. (c) When assessee bundles non-medical services (food, accommodation, transport) with medical treatment and claims composite healthcare supply.
Health-tech — Three Core Tests | CE Registration NOT Final Word
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.4.5 | Health-tech / Digital Health Platforms
Key Principle
For health-tech companies, it is important to locate (i) privity of contract (ii) object of contract (iii) primary liability for quality of services. These facts must be gleaned out of the T&Cs on the website or mobile application. GST authorities looking for 'clinical establishment' registration as if that is the final word on facts before allowing exemption is incorrect — CE registration will be granted only when basic facilities are established and there are healthcare services where CE registration is not required too. Once facts reveal that liability is not assumed then healthcare is not the object and therefore privity (for healthcare services) is NOT with the health-tech establishment.
When to Use — When health-tech companies (apps, platforms, telemedicine aggregators) claim GST exemption as healthcare service providers. Apply three tests from T&Cs: (1) Is the health-tech company in privity of contract with patient for healthcare? (2) Is healthcare the object of contract — or is it merely facilitation/technology? (3) Has the health-tech company assumed primary liability for quality of healthcare? If facts show healthcare liability is not assumed — health-tech is NOT the supplier of healthcare and cannot claim Entry 74 exemption.
Diagnosis — B2B Exempt | Sub-contractor Scope = Sub-set of Main Contract
Legal Principle | Chapter 22.3.11 | Entry 74 & Examples 641, 642, 643
Key Principle
Diagnosis alone without offering treatment based on the diagnosis is also healthcare services and admissible for GST exemption. B2B engagement will also be exempt from GST. Person carrying out examination or administering tests are required to be AMPs themselves in the field of Pathology or Radiology. Sub-contractor's scope will always be a sub-set of main-contractor's scope under a given contract for works. As long as the expert carrying out examination (radiology) or administering tests (pathology) and the expert interpreting the results and arriving at the diagnosis are both AMPs, both are exempt from GST for providing healthcare services.
When to Use — When Revenue taxes B2B diagnostic services (pathology lab supplying to doctor/hospital) on the ground that the patient is not the direct recipient. Counter: B2B engagement is also exempt — diagnosis is a subset of healthcare; privity with patient is not necessary when biomedical samples are provided. Also use to establish that sample collection agencies comprising paramedical technicians remain part of the healthcare service chain.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 23 — Education Laws

🎓 Chapter 23 — Education Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Exempt / Valid
Taxable / Not Exempt
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
23.1 Relevance to GST
23.2.1 Recognized Education
23.2.2 Complex Exemption Construct (Entry 66)
23.2.3 Recipient v. Beneficiary
23.2.4 Form of Organization
23.2.5 Charity v. Education
23.2.6 University v. Institution
23.3.1 Composite Supply
23.3.2–23.3.4 Religious / Medical / Two Suppliers
23.3.5–23.3.7 Home School / Value Added / Student Exchange
23.4 Outliers: Contract Educators / Exam Prep / Integrated / Sports / Edtech
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
23.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🎓 Education Laws — Relevance to GST
Entry 66 | Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) | Karnataka Education Act 1983
⚠️ THE HIDDEN COST — ITC LOSS
Education is expected to be exempt from GST — but the sector is unaware that ITC loss it endures and passes on to Customers is estimated to be in excess of 11%.
No educational institution can afford to remain unregistered in GST — not for any other reason, but because no educational institution is free of taxable turnover (coupled with exempt turnover) to trigger requirement to register and be exposed to audit enquiry.
📝 Every school/college has some taxable income (rent of premises, sale of books, canteen, etc.) mixed with exempt tuition fees. The combined turnover triggers GST registration. Being registered = opportunity to assert self-assessment correctness = result may be NIL tax payable in many years.
TEMPLATE STATUTE
Karnataka Education Act, 1983 is referred as a template to examine the legal contours surrounding educational institutions in this deliberation.
Strict construction applies — scope of exemption under Entry 66 to Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) dated 28 Jun 2017 must be done with great circumspection.
📝 Exemptions are construed strictly — you cannot assume education is exempt without checking every condition of Entry 66. The Karnataka Education Act provides the framework to understand what a legally recognized educational institution looks like.
23.2.1 — Recognized Education: The Golden Test ⇧ Index
🏛️ Recognized Education — The Only Gateway to Exemption
Exemption = Outward supplies that result in QUALIFICATION RECOGNIZED BY LAW
📝 This is the golden test. If the education does not lead to a qualification that law recognizes (like a degree, diploma, or certificate recognized by statute), no exemption. Hobby classes, personality development, coaching = not exempt.
PARA 2(y) — DEFINITION OF "EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION"
Institution providing services by way of:
(i)
Pre-school education and education up to higher secondary school or equivalent
(ii)
Education as a part of a curriculum for obtaining a qualification recognised by any law for the time being in force
(iii)
Education as a part of an approved vocational education course
⚠️ TWO-CONDITION RULE FOR EXEMPTION
Education that is EITHER (i) not essential to the grant of qualification recognized by law
OR (ii) not compulsory for all to undergo in the course of pursuing the program for grant of qualification recognized by law
— alone will qualify for this exemption
📝 Only courses that are ESSENTIAL (not optional) AND COMPULSORY FOR ALL students in the program qualify as 'recognized education' for exemption. Elective, optional, value-added, or supplementary courses that are not mandated for all → taxable.
⚠️ SKILL DEVELOPMENT — EXCLUDED FROM ENTRY 66
Skill development is covered under entries 69, 70 and 71 of the same exemption notification
NOT to be admitted for exemption under Entry 66
Strict interpretation — affirmed in Govt. of Kerala v. Mother Superior Adoration Convent [2021] 126 taxmann.com 68 (SC)
After refining interpretation in CC v. Dilip Kumar & Co. [2018] 95 taxmann.com 327/69 GST 239 (SC)
📝 Skill development has its own separate exemption entries (69/70/71). You cannot use Entry 66 (education exemption) to claim exemption for skill training. They are different baskets — do not mix them.
23.2.2 — Complex Exemption Construct: Entry 66 Decoded ⇧ Index
📋 Entry 66 — Clause-by-Clause Breakdown
⚠️ IMPORTANT Self-assessment applies only to (i) outward supplies AND (ii) inward supplies where RCM is applicable. Suppliers covered by Entry 66(b) must independently avail this exemption. All other outward supplies and inward supplies are NOT covered by this exemption.
Clause
Entry 66		 Supply Type What is Exempt Scope / Limitation
(a) Outward supply Exemption applies when the beneficiary is 'students, faculty or staff' All educational institutions under Para 2(y)
(aa) Outward supply Exemption limited to conduct of entrance exam where consideration is designated as 'entrance fee' From 25 Jan 2018
(b)(i) Inward supply Transportation services availed from an operator where charges are activity-based (per person per month) and NOT time-based (fixed per month, regardless of number of persons) Pre-school up to HSC only (NOT university or vocational course)
(b)(ii) Inward supply Catering services Pre-school up to HSC only
(b)(iii) Inward supply Security, cleaning or house-keeping services 'on premises' Pre-school up to HSC only
(b)(iv) Inward supply Admission process (student evaluation procedure — NOT student referral program) and conduct of examination Pre-school up to HSC only
(b)(v) Inward supply Online educational journals or periodicals Only university (from 25 Jan 2018)
⚠️ CRITICAL LIMITATION  Entry 66(b) inward supply exemptions are limited to pre-school up to HSC (except online journals for universities). Universities, colleges, vocational training institutions get NO inward supply exemption under Entry 66(b) — except online journals. ITC blocked = irrecoverable cost.
23.2.3 — Recipient v. Beneficiary ⇧ Index
👨‍👩‍👧 Recipient v. Beneficiary — Who is the Payer of Consideration?
RECIPIENT
Person responsible to pay the consideration
= Section 2(93) CGST Act
In education: PARENT (who pays the fee)
Fee demand can be made ONLY on person who contracts with the educational institution
📝 Recipient = the one with the contract. Normally the parent signs the school admission form and is contractually liable to pay fees. The school can only recover fees from the parent — not from a donor or sponsor who informally pays.
BENEFICIARY
Person who actually enjoys/benefits from the service
In education: STUDENT
Exemption in Entry 66(a) is for beneficiary = students, faculty or staff
📝 The student benefits from education but may not be the one paying. GST exemption flows to outward supplies where beneficiary is student — regardless of who pays. The exemption is for the student's benefit, not the payer's.
Donor / Sponsor Scenarios — Privity of Contract is Key
SCENARIO 1
Donor who offers to pay fee for poor students → Education services supplied to Donor → Exempt
💡 Eg.652: Education services is supplied to Donor who offers to pay fee for poor students → exempt from tax.
SCENARIO 2
Fee deposited by Donor directly to School where Parent is the contractual party → Education services supplied to Parent → Exempt
💡 Eg.653: Education services supplied to Parent where fee is deposited by Donor directly to School → exempt from tax.
VARYING PAYER
Every year the Payer may vary — but that does NOT become 'other income' of the institution
Used to settle fee due from students or write-off unpaid fee
📝 Assertions on facts cannot be assumed — they will neither be constant every year nor for all students being sponsored. Identify privity of contract each time.
23.2.4 — Form of Organization: Mandatory Legal Structure ⇧ Index
🏢 Form of Organization — Legal Structure Determines Exemption Eligibility
Karnataka Education Act — Section 2(14): "Educational Institution"
Means any institution imparting education referred to in Section 3
Includes private educational institution — but DOES NOT INCLUDE an institution under direct management of University or Central Government or a tutorial institution
📝 Tutorial institutions (coaching classes) are explicitly excluded. Even if they teach the same subjects as a school — they are NOT educational institutions under the Act. No exemption available to them.
Rule 3A — Karnataka Educational Institution Rules 1997
Permitted Forms of Organization for Educational Institutions
✓ 1
Society registered under Karnataka Societies Registration Act, 1960
✓ 2
Charitable trust registered under Indian Trusts Act, 1882
✓ 3
Company registered under Section 8 of Companies Act, 2013 (Central Act 18 of 2013)
✓ 4
Non-profit motive limited liability partnership registered under LLP Act, 2008 (Central Act 6 of 2009)
⚠️ GST CONSEQUENCE
Where the educational institution is of a different form although carrying out educational activities → it would NOT be eligible to this exemption

If the form of the institution is not as above → there is no question of entertaining any further deliberation as to the admissibility of exemption.
📝 Form and structure of the organization is determined by law. You cannot run an educational institution as a private limited company (Pvt Ltd) or a partnership firm and claim GST exemption — even if you do legitimate educational work.
23.2.5 — Charity v. Education ⇧ Index
❤️ Charity v. Education — Different Concepts, Different Exemptions
CHARITY
Refers to (i) objects declared before a regulator AND (ii) actual end-use of funds raised
📝 Charity = you declare your objectives (e.g., educating the poor) to a regulator AND you actually spend the money on those objectives. Both conditions must be met.
EDUCATION
Refers only to end-use of funds raised
The 'source' of funds must be: (i) consideration for making supplies (ii) supplies within activities of qualification recognized by law (iii) supplies made by Supplier who is recognized by law
📝 For education exemption, it's about what you DO with the money — you provide qualifying education. Where it came from (donation, fee, grant) matters for identifying the supply, but the exemption flows from what service is provided.
⚠️ DONOR IS NOT SUPPLIER OF EDUCATION
Donor paying school fees for poor students is NOT making outward supplies of education.
Donor is merely paying the dues of students by depositing money to the institution which is providing education.
Exemption to Donor may (i) fall within Entry 1 if conditions satisfied OR (ii) fall outside scope of levy for being voluntary contribution — NOT under Entry 66.
💡 Eg.654: Donor paying school fees for poor students is NOT making outward supplies of education. Donor is merely paying the dues of students by depositing money to the institution which is providing education.
📝 Exemption cannot be assumed without knowing exactly how and which exemption applies. A donor's payment is a voluntary contribution — not a consideration for educational service. Different exemption basket applies to donors.
23.2.6 — University v. Institution ⇧ Index
🏛️ University v. Institution — The Critical Distinction for Exemption
📝 The qualification recognized by law is conferred by the UNIVERSITY — not by the college/institution that teaches. This separation has massive GST implications on which entity's supply is exempt and which is taxable.
Five Key Attributes of University for GST Purposes
(a) University itself can impart education and be Supplier of exempt services
(b) University can grant recognition to another independent institution which engages in imparting education
CRITICAL (c)
Qualification recognized by law will be conferred on students by the UNIVERSITY — NOT by the institution imparting education
(d) UGC Act gives recognition to various kinds of private universities
(e) Conduct of affairs requires: (i) Board of Studies (ii) Board of Examination (iii) Governing Council (iv) Management — each role separate
✓ BOARD OF STUDIES (BoS) TEST — THE KEY
Course that is approved by BoS = admitted as part of 'qualification recognized by law'
Course that is NOT approved by BoS or is offered independent of minimum requirements of course curricula as per University Regulations = NOT admitted as part of 'qualification recognized by law'
💡 Eg.655: Subjects must be approved by Board of Studies to be admitted as part of 'qualification recognized by law'.
Eg.656: Value-added courses must be examined for compliance with University Regulations to be admitted.
Eg.657: 'Value added courses' independent of minimum requirements of course curricula → NOT admitted → Taxable.
Eg.658: 'Optional' courses independent of minimum requirements → NOT admitted → Taxable.
23.3.1 — Composite Supply in Education ⇧ Index
📦 Composite Supply — Exemption Extends to All Constituent Supplies
GST EXEMPTION — COMPOSITE SUPPLY PRINCIPLE
GST exemption is applicable to 'composite supply' whether the constituent supplies are offered by the educational institution in a consolidated manner or individually, whether offered mandatorily for all students or optionally for some students.
Care must be taken to locate the recognition of every course offered by Board of Studies (BoS).
📝 If the educational supply is exempt, ALL incidental/composite components (stationery, uniform, transport, meals charged as part of annual fee) are also exempt — because the principal supply (education) is exempt. The composite package follows the principal supply's tax treatment.
⚠️ HSN NOTE
Reference to HSNs 9992 and 9963 in column (2) to entry 66 is inconsequential to guide the determination of scope of this exemption because:
(i) No reference is made in the head of the notification to HSN codes appearing in column (2)
(ii) Not ALL supplies are covered under these two HSN codes when the expressly listed supplies are covered in column (3) by a 'description'
📝 Do not rely on HSN codes to determine scope of education exemption. The description in column (3) of the notification is what governs — not the HSN. This is an important technical point for assessment and litigation.
💡 Eg.659: Notebooks and uniform included along with annual composite fee → also exempt since principal supply (education) is exempt.
Eg.660: Composite fee for (i) boarders will include hostel accommodation and mess charges and (ii) day scholars will include transport charges — and entire composite fee will be exempt.
23.3.2–23.3.4 — Religious Education, Medical Education & Two Suppliers ⇧ Index
⛪ Religious, Medical Education & Two-Supplier Scenarios
23.3.2 RELIGIOUS EDUCATION
Religious education necessary for pupil to learn Scriptures and be admitted as a religious Priest → would NOT be admitted to the exemption
(does not result in qualification recognized by law)
May be classified as: (i) No supply OR (ii) Charitable activity under Entry 1 — "advancement of religion"
📝 Religious training for becoming a priest = not recognized by any secular law = not exempt under Entry 66. May qualify under Entry 1 (charitable activity) if conditions met.
23.3.3 MEDICAL EDUCATION
Education is exempt from GST — not restricted to classroom setting
Specialization and super-specialization imparted by Teaching Hospital
May be exempt under BOTH:
→ Entry 74 (health care services)
→ Entry 66 (educational services)
Restricting exemption to one entry only = contrary to notification terms
📝 A teaching hospital is both a healthcare provider AND an educational institution simultaneously. Both exemptions apply to the two different types of services it provides. Do not restrict it to only one.
23.3.4 TWO SUPPLIERS
One supply can have TWO suppliers
University grants qualification recognized by law
Recognized institution (College) imparts teaching
Both legs of one supply = exempt
📝 University provides the degree (exempt). College provides the teaching (exempt). The student benefits from both. Two separate suppliers, one supply — both exempt under Entry 66.
💡 Educational institution imparts classroom training + hospital provides practical training for paramedical course. Two suppliers, both exempt. Care: (i) privity of contract (ii) flow of consideration (iii) object of each supplier — all must be identified.
23.3.5–23.3.7 — Home School, Value Added Education & Student Exchange ⇧ Index
🏠 Home School | 📊 Value Added Education | 🌍 Student Exchange
23.3.5 HOME SCHOOL
Non-formal education recognized in law
Must NOT be a 'tutorial institution'
Tutorial institution = Section 2(39): Unrecognised institution run by not less than 2 persons for systematically imparting education to 20+ persons in any subject with view to prepare them for examination
✓ EXEMPT
Open university & distance education = exempt (conducted by recognized educational institution)
✗ NOT EXEMPT
Where course requires full-time education → self-study would NOT meet requirements for exemption
📝 Home school = parents teaching kids at home = legally recognized if parents do it. But if you run a coaching class for 20+ students to prepare for exams = tutorial institution = NOT exempt.
23.3.6 VALUE ADDED EDUCATION
Exempt vs Taxable — Criteria Table
CriteriaExemptTaxable
Course definedYesYes
Linked to curriculaYesYes
Approved by BoSYesNo
Adds value to StudentsYesYes
Optional for given courseNoYes
Open for external participationNoYes
Impact on final course resultYesNo
Fee charged extraNoYes
📝 Where subjects/courses are non-composite in nature (optional, external participation allowed, extra fee charged) → they would NOT be exempt. BoS approval is the single most decisive criteria.
23.3.7 STUDENT EXCHANGE
Location where education is imparted is NOT as relevant as the institution under whose authority education is imparted and the nexus with 'qualification recognized by law'
✗ NOT EXEMPT
Students of foreign university arrive at Indian institution + consideration charged from foreign university for enrichment of non-students of Indian institution → exemption NOT available
(Exemption = supplier is educational institution + beneficiary is student/faculty/staff)
📝 If an Indian college charges a foreign university for hosting foreign students, the Indian college is supplying a service to the foreign university — not to its own students/faculty. Exemption conditions not met.
23.4 — Outliers: Contract Educators, Exam Prep, Integrated Education, Sports Academy & Edtech ⇧ Index
⚠️ Outliers — Special GST Analysis for Non-Standard Education Scenarios
23.4.1 CONTRACT EDUCATORS
Tuition or part-time educators are NOT exempt from GST
Educational institution 'collaborates' with corporate educators (institution = skin for contract educators)
4 RELEVANT FACTORS
(a) Educational institution exempt from its own revenue
(b) Share of revenue with corporate educators = inward supply to institution — NOT covered under Entry 66(b) except clause (b) extent
(c) Share of revenue ≠ diversion of income by overriding charge
(d) Prohibited activities examined based on purposive construction even where innovative contract-writing is employed
📝 Creative contract writing to make a corporate trainer look like it's 'inside' the institution will not fool GST. Purposive construction of Education laws will expose the true arrangement.
23.4.2 EXAM PREP
Coaching for competitive exams is TAXABLE with full benefit of admissible ITC
Object of ContractGST Treatment
Coaching class for competitive examsEducational service — NOT exempt
Pre-recorded videos for competitive examsEducational service — NOT exempt (OIDAR if supplier outside India)
Study material and explanatory videosEducational service — NOT exempt
Study material + videos + laptopMixed supply of educational service and hardware — NOT exempt
📝 Object of contract is always unmistakable. Taxpayers often force-fit into exempt category and retain ITC without utilizing it. First principles: exam prep ≠ recognized education = taxable.
23.4.3 INTEGRATED EDUCATION
Institution offers additional preparation for competitive exams as part of course fee for 'qualification recognized by law'
When coaching for competitive exams included in course fee → unlikely to be 'composite supply' → liable to be treated as 'mixed supply'
5 RISK FACTORS
(i) Object of contract (ii) Non-severability of components of supply (iii) Advertisement of terms including past results (iv) Extent of requirement within 'qualification recognized by law' (v) Terms of other supplies limiting their scope
📝 If a college advertises that it prepares students for IIT-JEE/NEET alongside its degree course — the competitive exam coaching is NOT part of the degree curriculum. Two unconnected supplies bundled together = mixed supply = highest rate applies.
23.4.4 SPORTS ACADEMY
Sports + education bundled → risk of being taxed as 'mixed supply'
Two unconnected supplies brought together unnaturally and supplied for a single price CANNOT be explained away as 'composite supply'
Omission to claim ITC will NOT procure exemption under this entry
📝 A sports academy that also provides regular education is offering two separate services. Sports training is not incidental to education. Bundling them for a single fee = mixed supply = taxed at highest rate. You cannot get exemption by choosing not to claim ITC.
23.4.5 EDTECH
Education is exempt ONLY when: (i) Supplier is an educational institution (ii) Supply is education (iii) Beneficiary is student, faculty or staff

Use of technology is welcome BUT claiming exemption in edtech enterprise is disingenuous given vagaries of funding and restrictions under Education Act
Accumulation of ITC = area of interest (sunk costs discussion)
📝 Edtech platforms (like BYJU's, Unacademy) are NOT educational institutions under the law. They are technology companies. Their supply of online education content = taxable service. The three conditions for exemption are not met.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 23 | Education Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision or case name.
Strict Construction of Exemption — Mother Superior Adoration Convent Principle
Supreme Court | Govt. of Kerala v. Mother Superior Adoration Convent [2021] 126 taxmann.com 68 (SC) | Read with CC v. Dilip Kumar & Co. [2018] 95 taxmann.com 327/69 GST 239 (SC)
Key Principle
Where strict construction is to be applied in taxation, scope of exemption under entry 66 to notification 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate), dated 28th June 2017 must be done with great circumspection. Exemption is applicable in respect of outward supplies that result in qualification recognized by law. Education that is either not essential to the grant of qualification recognized by law or not compulsory for all to undergo in the course of pursuing the program for the grant of qualification recognized by law, alone will qualify for this exemption. Skill development is covered under entries 69, 70 and 71 and is not to be admitted for exemption under entry 66.
When to Use — When assessee claims exemption for skill training, coaching, value-added courses, optional courses under Entry 66. Counter: strict construction applies — exemption only for courses that are essential AND compulsory for all students pursuing the qualification recognized by law. Skill development has its own basket (entries 69/70/71) — cannot use Entry 66.
Form of Organization — Mandatory Pre-Condition for Exemption
Legal Principle | Karnataka Education Act 1983 | Rule 3A | Para 23.2.4
Key Principle
Educational institutions to be in accordance with this Act — no educational institution shall be established or maintained otherwise than in accordance with the provisions of this Act or the rules made thereunder. With the negative construct, educational institution to be recognized must be established in accordance with this (or applicable) law. Where the educational institution is of a different form although carrying out educational activities, it would not be eligible to this exemption. If the form of the institution is not as above (society, charitable trust, Section 8 company, non-profit LLP), then there is no question of entertaining any further deliberation as to the admissibility of exemption.
When to Use — When Department denies exemption to an educational institution or when assessee wrongly claims exemption. First checkpoint: Is the institution established in the permitted form (society / charitable trust / Section 8 company / non-profit LLP)? If not — no exemption, full stop. Private limited company, partnership firm running a school = no exemption under Entry 66.
Recipient v. Beneficiary — Privity of Contract Determines Tax
GST Principle | Section 2(93) CGST Act | Para 23.2.3
Key Principle
With the definition of recipient in section 2(93) of Central GST Act being person responsible to pay the consideration, recipient of educational services would be the Parent whereas the beneficiary of services would be the Student. It is important to differentiate who is the payer of consideration. Fee demand can be made only on that person who contracts with the educational institution. Every year the Payer may vary, but that does not become 'other income' of the educational institution which is used to either settle fee due from students or write-off unpaid fee. Assertions on facts cannot be assumed — they will neither be constant every year nor for all students being sponsored.
When to Use — When Department treats donor's payment of student fees as 'other income' of the school (liable to GST). Counter: donor's payment settles the contractual liability of the parent (who is the recipient). It is not a separate supply by the school to the donor. Identify privity of contract to determine who the true recipient is and whether exemption applies.
Board of Studies Approval — The Single Most Decisive Test
GST Principle | Para 23.2.6 | Eg. 655–658
Key Principle
Qualification recognized by law will be conferred on students by the university and not by the institution imparting education. Course that is approved by Board of Studies cannot be inquired for any nexus with the curricula of institution offering 'value added courses' independent of minimum requirements of course curricula as per University Regulations — such courses would not be admitted as part of 'qualification recognized by law'. The conduct of affairs requires a structure comprising of Board of Studies, Board of Examination, Governing Council and Management, with each of their roles being separate.
When to Use — In disputes about whether a specific course or subject offered by a college/institution is within the scope of the education exemption. The test: Is the course approved by the university's Board of Studies? If not — the course is not part of 'qualification recognized by law' — it is taxable regardless of how educationally valuable it is.
Composite Supply — HSN Codes Are Inconsequential for Education Exemption
GST Principle | Entry 66 Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) | Para 23.3.1
Key Principle
GST exemption is applicable to 'composite supply' whether the constituent supplies are offered by the educational institution in a consolidated manner or individually, whether offered mandatorily for all students or optionally for some students. Reference to HSNs 9992 and 9963 in column (2) to entry 66 is inconsequential to guide the determination of the scope of this exemption for the reason that (i) no reference is made in the head of the notification to HSN codes appearing in column (2) of the table in this notification and (ii) not all supplies are covered under these two HSN codes when the expressly listed supplies are covered in column (3) by a 'description'.
When to Use — When Department raises demand arguing that notebooks, uniform, transport, hostel charges are separately taxable because they fall under different HSN codes. Counter: HSN codes in column (2) are inconsequential. The description in column (3) governs. Where education is the principal supply, the entire composite fee (including notebooks, uniform etc.) is exempt under the composite supply principle.
Mixed Supply vs Composite Supply — Sports Academy / Integrated Education Trap
GST Principle | Para 23.4.3 & 23.4.4
Key Principle
Where the coaching for competitive exams is included as part of the course fee for the 'qualification recognized by law', it is unlikely to be admitted as 'composite supply' and is liable to be treated as 'mixed supply'. Two unconnected supplies are unnaturally brought together and supplied for a single price — this cannot be explained away as 'composite supply' to avail exemption. Omission to claim input tax credit will not procure exemption under this entry. The risk is real and attention must be paid to (i) object of contract (ii) non-severability of components of supply (iii) advertisement of terms including information about past results (iv) extent of requirement within scope of 'qualification recognized by law' (v) terms of other supplies limiting their scope.
When to Use — When a coaching institute or sports academy claims GST exemption by bundling competitive exam coaching/sports training with regular qualifying education as 'composite supply.' Counter: bundling two unconnected supplies (degree education + IIT coaching / sports training) and charging a single price = mixed supply = taxed at highest rate. Five risk factors must be examined. Not claiming ITC does not convert a taxable supply into an exempt one.
Edtech / Contract Educators — Three Conditions for Exemption Must ALL Be Met
GST Principle | Entry 66 | Para 23.4.1 & 23.4.5
Key Principle
Education is exempt when (i) supplier is an educational institution (ii) supply is education (iii) beneficiary is student, faculty or staff. Use of technology is welcome but given the vagaries of funding and the restrictions under the Education Act, it would be disingenuous to claim this exemption in edtech enterprise. Tuition or part-time educators are not exempt from GST. Share of revenue with corporate educators is inward supplies to the educational institution which is not covered under entry 66(b) except to the extent of clause (b). Prohibited activities will be examined based on purposive construction even where innovative contract-writing is employed.
When to Use — When edtech platforms, online learning companies, or contract educators claim GST exemption on their services. Counter: all three conditions must simultaneously be met. An edtech platform is not an educational institution — condition (i) fails. A corporate educator is not an educational institution — condition (i) fails. Purposive construction will expose the true arrangement regardless of how the contract is written.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 24 — Internet Intermediaries

🌐 Chapter 24 — Internet Intermediaries

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Included
Prohibited / Taxable / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
24.1 Relevance to GST
24.2.1 Trade — Online Agreed, Offline Fulfilled
24.2.2 Supplier-Recipient — Intermediary is Neither
24.2.3 Cash-Back (Eg.661 & 662)
24.2.4 Agent-as-Supplier (Schedule I Para 3)
24.2.5 Tax Treatment — Three Routes
24.3.1 Information Portals (Eg.663-664)
24.3.2 Intermediary-as-Supplier (Section 9(5))
24.3.3 Cash Custody — The Pivot
24.3.4 Intermediary-of-Intermediaries (Eg.668)
24.3.5 Discounts by Intermediaries
24.3.6 Profit Protection
24.3.7 Free is Not Free
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
24.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🌐 Internet Intermediaries — Relevance to GST
E-Commerce | Least Understood Sector | Two GST Treatments | IT Rules 2021
📝 E-commerce is the least understood sector in GST — partly because the business model is fluid (promoters can "pivot" the business), and partly because synonymous expressions confuse analysis. GST is very specific: it picks only a very small sub-set of internet intermediary businesses for special treatment. All other intermediary-like businesses must be weeded out of the discussion.
WHY POORLY UNDERSTOOD
(i) Atypical business model — flexibility in promoters' hands to 'pivot'
(ii) Synonymous expressions used to describe the business cause confusion
📝 A platform can pivot from marketplace to inventory model overnight. And words like "e-commerce operator," "marketplace," "aggregator," "platform" are used interchangeably — but each has different GST consequences.
GST SELECTS — TWO TREATMENTS ONLY
(i) Tax to be paid by Intermediary (Section 9(5) — ECOM is deemed supplier)
(ii) Tax to be paid by actual Supplier but collected at source by Intermediary (Section 52 — TCS)
📝 GST does not treat all e-commerce operators the same. There are only two special treatments: (a) Sec 9(5) — ECOM pays as deemed supplier; (b) Sec 52 — actual supplier pays, ECOM collects TCS. All other situations = normal supply rules apply.
WELFARE vs PRIVATE LAW
IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 — welfare legislation — uses expressions like 'social media intermediary', 'significant social media intermediary'

In GST (private matters' legislation) — contractual authority of internet intermediaries must be gathered with precision
📝 IT Rules 2021 define intermediaries for public safety/content regulation. GST looks at the same entity through a completely different lens — what is its contractual authority in the trade? The two are independent. Welfare law definitions cannot be imported into GST.
DELIBERATION PURPOSE
Expose the differentiators so that look-alikes can be eliminated from the tax treatment that ought to apply — and to those that fall within the 'pith' of the internet intermediary business
📝 The analysis is designed to identify what truly is an internet intermediary (for GST purposes) and what merely looks like one. Many platforms claim intermediary status to avoid GST liability — the tests below separate genuine intermediaries from imposters.
24.2.1 — Trade — "Online Agreed, Fulfilled Offline" ⇧ Index
🤝 Trade — Online Agreement, Offline Fulfilment
Intermediary's Role | Indemnity | Non-Payment by Promisee
📝 In e-commerce, the contract is agreed online but the actual delivery of goods/services happens offline (physically). The intermediary's role is to bring the two contracting parties together — Promisor (Supplier) and Promisee (Recipient). Even deep involvement by the intermediary does not make it the Promisor.
INTERMEDIARY'S ROLE
To bring binding obligations between the actual Supplier (Promisor) and eventual Recipient (Promisee)

Deep and pervasive involvement — including:
• Assurances of reliability
• Indemnity in case of default by Supplier/Recipient
📝 Even if the platform guarantees delivery, gives refund assurance, or indemnifies the buyer against fraud — none of this makes the platform the "Supplier." The platform is still just a bridge between buyer and seller.
ELEVATED RESPONSIBILITIES ≠ PROMISOR
Not even elevated responsibilities convert Intermediary to be the Promisor of the supplies and displace the actual Promisor from that position qua the Promisee
📝 Qua = in the capacity of. Even a very involved intermediary (like Amazon with FBA — Fulfilled by Amazon) is not the supplier of the goods. The seller remains the Promisor.
INDEMNITY CLARIFICATION
Indemnity = contract of assurance
Indemnifier will NOT become the Promisor — performance cannot be fulfilled by any person but the Promisor only
Indemnifier is only a recourse for Promisee in event of failure by Promisor or vice versa
📝 If Amazon gives a refund when a seller fails to deliver — Amazon is indemnifying the buyer. But Amazon does not thereby become the seller. Indemnity = backup guarantee, not assumption of the supply obligation.
NON-PAYMENT RULE
In case of non-payment by Promisee after supply completed by Promisor — payment by Intermediary to Promisor is NOT consideration for supply but to settle unpaid consideration that was due by Promisee
📝 If the buyer doesn't pay and the platform pays the seller — the platform has discharged the buyer's obligation (not its own obligation as buyer). This has implications for who is liable to GST and on what value.
24.2.2 — Supplier-Recipient — Intermediary is Neither ⇧ Index
🔄 Supplier-Recipient — Intermediary Occupies Neither Position
Even if Entire Payment Collected | Even if Storage/Packing/Transport Facilitated
📝 The core principle: an intermediary is not the Supplier (Promisor) even if it collects all the money — and it is not the Recipient (Promisee) even if it facilitates storage, packing, transportation. The intermediary is the bridge, not the party to the trade.
NOT THE PROMISOR
Intermediary is not the Promisor — not even if the entire payment from Promisee is collected by Intermediary which is to be transferred to Promisor
📝 Flipkart collects ₹10,000 from the buyer for a phone. Flipkart holds the money and later remits to the seller. Flipkart is not the seller just because it collected the money. It is a payment intermediary for this leg.
NOT THE PROMISEE
Intermediary is not the Promisee — not even if fulfilment of supplies is facilitated such as storage, packing or transportation responsibilities are assumed by Intermediary
📝 Amazon stores goods in its warehouse (FBA), packs them, ships them. Amazon has storage and transport responsibility. Yet Amazon is not the "buyer" of those goods. It is still only facilitating the seller's supply.
CORE TEST
Online trade transactions still require a Promisor (Supplier) and Promisee (Recipient) and a valid contract with an object that is in existence.
Supplier = Promisor whose Offer is accepted by Offeree.
Intermediary is not the Promisor and not the Promisee.
24.2.3 — Cash-Back (Eg.661 & Eg.662) ⇧ Index
💰 Cash-Back — Two Separate Supplies, Cannot be Merged
Eg.661: ECOM → Recipient | Eg.662: ECOM → Supplier | Two Structures, Different GST
📝 "Cash-back" is when an intermediary pays money to either the buyer or seller as part of a promotion. This creates a second supply separate from the primary supply of goods. Each supply is taxed independently. Merging them or treating them as reducing the primary transaction value is impermissible.
Scenario A — Cash-Back to Recipient (Buyer) | Eg.661
Eg.661: Mobile phone is sold by ABC to XYZ for ₹25,000 and ECOM transfers ₹2,000 to XYZ.
SupplySupplierRecipientTaxable ValueObject
PrimaryABCXYZ₹25,000Goods
SecondaryXYZECOM₹2,000Not goods
RULE
It is impermissible to merge two supplies and apply a single tax treatment.
It is no one's case that the goods are sold for ₹23,000.
HSN, tariff rate, credit restrictions, time and place of supply and valuation will mutatis mutandis apply to each supply.
📝 Mutatis mutandis = with the necessary changes. Each supply must be examined independently. The cash-back of ₹2,000 is not a "discount" on the phone sale — it is a separate supply of something (not goods) from XYZ to ECOM.
Scenario B — Cash-Back to Supplier (Seller) | Eg.662
Eg.662: Mobile phone is sold by ABC to XYZ for ₹25,000 and ECOM transfers ₹5,000 to ABC.
Three transactions but still only two supplies
(a) Supply of goods ₹25,000
(b) Payment of ₹5,000 by ECOM to Supplier
(c) Supply of 'something' (not goods) for ₹5,000 where Recipient's obligation met by ECOM
TWO POSSIBILITIES
(a) Acceptance-failure test: if ₹5,000 not paid, Supplier refuses to supply → then ₹5,000 is additional consideration for primary goods supply → total taxable value = ₹30,000

(b) ₹5,000 is for separate transaction between ECOM as Recipient and Supplier (Promisor) for a secondary supply (listing services)
📝 The acceptance-failure test: would the seller refuse to sell if ECOM didn't pay this ₹5,000? If yes — it is part of the goods sale price (total = ₹30,000 is taxable). If no — it is a separate listing service fee that ECOM pays the seller.
SupplySupplierRecipientTaxable ValueObject
PrimaryABCXYZ₹25,000 + ₹5,000Goods
SecondaryXYZECOM₹5,000Not goods
⚠️ SECTION 9(4) EXPOSURE
ECOM must be mindful of liability under Section 9(4) CGST Act (until exempt from 13th Oct 2017).
Promisor (Supplier of primary supply) should aggregate amounts collected from Intermediary with that collected from Promisee as the total consideration of primary supply of goods.
24.2.4 — Agent-as-Supplier (Schedule I, Para 3) ⇧ Index
🏪 Agent-as-Supplier — Schedule I Para 3 | Buy-Sell Trade Fiction
Value-Added Services | Del Credere Agent | FDI Restrictions
📝 Schedule I Para 3 creates a legal fiction: when an Agent handles goods of the Principal AND receives/delivers goods on behalf of the Principal — this is treated as a "buy-sell trade" between Principal and Agent. If an intermediary's value-added services tip this scale — it becomes a deemed supplier with full GST consequences.
Schedule I, Para 3 — Agent will be 'deemed' to be Supplier if agent is involved in handling the goods of the Principal and goods received or delivered on behalf of Principal will constitute a 'buy-sell trade' between Principal and Agent.
TRIGGER
Where Intermediaries extend scope of their value-added services to such an extent that it tips the scales and brings them within the operation of this legal fiction
📝 Tipping point: if the platform not only lists but also handles inventory, takes delivery, manages returns, etc. — at some point it moves from "facilitating" to "dealing." Once it crosses that line, it becomes a deemed supplier under Schedule I Para 3.
NATURE OF VALUE-ADDED SERVICES
(i) Canvassing agent or del credere agent or deemed reseller
(ii) Warehouse-keeper or co-packer or common carrier
(iii) All of above → risk of mixed supply in GST
📝 Del credere agent = agent who guarantees payment by the buyer. If an intermediary is a del credere agent — it is taking on the seller's credit risk. That is more than mere facilitation. Each engagement model needs individual inquiry.
OUTSOURCING DOESN'T EXCUSE
Outsourcing any one or more of these value-added services does NOT excuse the Intermediary — because privity of contract cannot be disturbed easily and outsourcing is akin to sub-contracting and not novation of contract
📝 If the platform sub-contracts its warehousing to a third party — the platform still remains contractually responsible to the buyer. You cannot escape your GST obligations by outsourcing what you are contractually bound to deliver.
FDI CAUTION
FDI restrictions may come in the way of Intermediaries acting as resellers. Where 'buy-sell trade' is compelled by legal fiction, facts in the business may not fall foul with FDI regulations
📝 FDI policy restricts foreign e-commerce companies from being "resellers." If GST legal fiction deems them a reseller under Schedule I Para 3 — does that violate FDI norms? The chapter notes this tension but suggests the fiction is for GST purposes only, not FDI.
24.2.5 — Tax Treatment — Three Routes ⇧ Index
🔀 GST — Three Tax Treatment Routes for Intermediaries
Section 9(5) | Section 52 | Fee on Own Service | Cash Custody is the Pivot
📝 GST prescribes exactly three tax treatments for intermediaries — no more, no less. Which route applies depends critically on whether the intermediary has "cash custody" of the trade value. Cash custody is the pivot that determines the intermediary's GST obligations.
ROUTE (a) — SECTION 9(5)
Where primary supply is notified under Section 9(5) → output tax payable by Promisor (Supplier) must be discharged by Intermediary
(Intermediary = deemed Supplier)
📝 Taxi (Ola/Uber), food delivery (Swiggy/Zomato for restaurants), accommodation booking (for unregistered hotels) — ECOM pays GST as if it were the supplier. The actual supplier is relieved of GST compliance but not of the contractual obligation to supply.
💡 Swiggy is notified under Sec 9(5) for restaurant supply. When you order biryani from a restaurant through Swiggy — Swiggy pays the GST on the entire order value. The restaurant does not separately charge GST to Swiggy.
ROUTE (b) — SECTION 52 (TCS)
Where primary supply is NOT notified → output tax payable by Promisor (Supplier) but Intermediary is liable to collect tax at source — provided 'cash custody' is with Intermediary
