📝 For all e-commerce supplies not covered by Sec 9(5) — the seller pays GST but the platform must collect 1% TCS (Tax Collected at Source) from the seller's payout and deposit it with the government. This applies only if the platform holds the cash.
💡 Amazon seller sells a book for ₹500. Amazon holds the money. Amazon deducts 1% TCS (₹5) before remitting ₹495 to the seller. Seller then gets credit for the ₹5 TCS in their GST return.
ROUTE (c) — OWN SERVICE FEE
Where Intermediary does NOT have 'cash custody' → neither (a) nor (b) applies → Intermediary is liable to discharge tax only on the fee charged for its own services
📝 If the platform never touches the trade money (buyer pays seller directly, platform is just a listing/information service) — the platform only pays GST on its own commission/fee. No TCS, no deemed supplier obligations.
💡 A property listing website where buyer and seller contact each other directly and transact independently — the website only charges a listing fee. GST only on that listing fee. No TCS obligation.
⚠️ KEY — CASH CUSTODY IS THE PIVOT
Routes (a) and (b) apply only when Intermediary has 'cash custody'.
Route (c) applies when Intermediary does NOT have cash custody.
Determining who holds the cash at the moment the trade is concluded is the single most important factual question in internet intermediary GST disputes.
24.3.1 — Information Portals (Eg.663 & Eg.664) ⇧ Index
ℹ️ Information Portals — Not E-Commerce Operators
No Cash Custody | No Trade Conclusion | Only Listing/Publishing
📝 Many websites claim to be "information portals" to avoid e-commerce operator classification and TCS obligations. The test is simple: does the portal (a) gain cash custody of the trade, AND (b) conclude the trade on its platform? If neither — it is only an information listing service.
✓ INFORMATION PORTAL
Visitor gets all information about courses and colleges → exits platform and concludes transaction with selected college directly → Intermediary is only information portal
→ NO special GST treatment (a) or (b)
📝 The test: does the trade conclude ON the platform, or does the user leave and transact directly? If they leave — the platform is just an information service. GST only on its own listing fee.
💡 Eg.663: policymarket.com lists various insurance policies and shows comparative premia — but to buy a policy, Customer exits the website and concludes trade on hcfdlife.com. policymarket.com is only an information portal.
✗ BECOMES E-COMMERCE OPERATOR
Same website now collects premia and offers discounts by sharing the commission earned → it will be an e-Commerce Operator and liable to tax under Section 9(5) OR TCS compliance under Section 52
📝 The moment the portal starts collecting the premium (cash custody) — it crosses the line from information portal to e-commerce operator. The very act of holding the trade money triggers TCS or Sec 9(5) obligations.
💡 Eg.664: Same policymarket.com now collects premia and offers discounts — it becomes an e-Commerce Operator and liable to tax under Sec 9(5) or TCS compliance under Sec 52.
24.3.2 — Intermediary-as-Supplier | Section 9(5) ⇧ Index
🔄 Section 9(5) — "As If" Fiction | Intermediary Displaces Supplier
Legal Fiction Limited to Supplier-Customer Transaction | Circular 167/23/2021-GST
📝 Section 9(5) says the ECOM operator pays GST "as if he is the supplier." The words "as if" are key — it is a legal fiction, not reality. When the law uses "as if" — fact is replaced by fiction. And when Intermediary is the Supplier by fiction, the Supplier (Promisor) is no longer the Supplier for GST purposes. But contractual obligations continue.
"AS IF" FICTION MECHANICS
"As if" language → fact is replaced by fiction
When Intermediary = Supplier → Supplier (Promisor) is NO LONGER the Supplier
"As if the Promisor is not the supplier" is implicit in Section 9(5)
📝 You cannot have two Suppliers simultaneously — one in fact and one by legal fiction. The fiction replaces the fact. So when Ola pays GST as deemed supplier — the driver is no longer the supplier FOR GST PURPOSES (but remains the contractor for service delivery).
CONTRACT OBLIGATION SURVIVES
This legal fiction is only for tax treatment — contractual obligation must continue to be fulfilled by Promisor to Promisee (Customer)
📝 The driver still has to complete the ride. The restaurant still has to deliver the food. Sec 9(5) only changes who pays the tax — NOT who fulfils the contract. Both are independent obligations.
NO DOUBLE TAXATION
Once entire transaction value has been subject to GST (in hands of Intermediary) → transaction inter se Intermediary and Supplier cannot once again be taxed
Cumulative taxable value would otherwise exceed 100% of transaction value
📝 If Ola pays GST on the full ride fare (₹100) — Ola cannot also charge GST on its commission (₹20) received from the driver. That would mean ₹120 of GST on a ₹100 transaction. The law prevents this double taxation.
💡 Eg.665: Listing fee or commission charged by Intermediary to Supplier is cost of access to online market — inward supply availed by Supplier though tax on outward supply is discharged by Intermediary acting as Supplier.
CIRCULAR AFFIRMATION
Any charges payable by Supplier (Promisor) to Intermediary will continue to be taxable — tax position affirmed in Circular 167/23/2021-GST dated 17th Dec 2021 (Para 6)
📝 The commission/listing fee that the driver pays Ola or the restaurant pays Swiggy — that is a SEPARATE inward supply to the driver/restaurant. That remains taxable. Circular 167 confirms this. The no-double-taxation rule only applies to the customer-facing transaction.
💡 Eg.666: Convenience fee charged by Intermediary to Customer is total consideration payable for 'composite supply' by Intermediary acting as Supplier. Listing fee charged by Intermediary to Supplier (Promisor) continues unaffected by legal fiction.
24.3.3 — Cash Custody — The Decisive Pivot ⇧ Index
💵 Cash Custody — The Single Most Important GST Test
Section 9(5) | Section 52 | Social Media ≠ E-Commerce | Eg.667
📝 The entire edifice of internet intermediary GST treatment rests on one factual question: does the intermediary have "cash custody" — i.e., does it hold the money paid by the buyer? Without cash custody, the intermediary cannot discharge output tax (Sec 9(5)) or collect TCS (Sec 52). It is only a non-intermediary support services supplier.
WITHOUT CASH CUSTODY
Intermediary is merely an information listing service
Cannot discharge output tax under Sec 9(5)
Cannot collect TCS under Sec 52
WITH CASH CUSTODY
Intermediary is in a position to either:
(i) Discharge output tax in terms of legal fiction in Section 9(5), OR
(ii) Collect tax at source under Section 52
SOCIAL MEDIA ≠ E-COMMERCE OPERATOR
Social networking and interaction portal where Content Creators post views, photos or lectures that Users consume creating a large amount of traffic that enables monetization by (i) data collation or (ii) advertisement — is NOT an e-Commerce Operator
📝 Facebook, Instagram, YouTube — their revenue model is advertising and data monetization, not holding cash from buyer-seller trades. They do not have cash custody of any buyer-seller transaction. Therefore they are NOT e-commerce operators for Sec 9(5)/52 purposes.
💡 Eg.667: Social networking and interaction portal where Content Creators post views, photos or lectures that Users consume — enabling monetization by data collation or advertisement — is NOT e-Commerce Operator.
24.3.4 — Intermediary-of-Intermediaries ⇧ Index
🔗 Intermediary-of-Intermediaries — Can There Be Two Levels of TCS?
Section 52 | One Agent Concludes | Agent-Sub-Agent Only | Eg.668
📝 When a supplier lists on Platform A (e.g., O-Yeah Rooms), and Platform A is itself listed on Platform B (e.g., BRED.com), the question is: can both platforms have TCS obligations? The answer is NO — there cannot be two levels of TCS. Only ONE intermediary can have cash custody at any time.
💡 Eg.668: Hotel accommodation of Raj Exotica is listed on platform of O-yeah Rooms (Intermediary A) who in turn lists on BRED.com (Intermediary B). One to has 'cash custody' will be liable to tax under Section 9(5) or TCS compliance under Section 52.
CANNOT BE TWO INTERMEDIARIES
While more than one Agent can canvass inventory belonging to one Principal — when a trade is concluded, there must be either:
(i) Only one Agent concluding that trade, OR
(ii) Two Agents must have 'agent and sub-agent' relationship inter se
📝 When the hotel booking is made through BRED.com, either BRED.com concludes the trade (holds cash) or it is sub-agent of O-yeah Rooms which holds cash. Both cannot simultaneously hold cash and have independent TCS obligations.
NEITHER IN Eg.668
In the hotel example — it is neither (i) nor (ii). There is no intermediary-of-intermediaries in this structure.
Of the two Intermediaries, the one who has 'cash custody' will be liable to comply with GST
NON-CASH INTERMEDIARY
The other (without cash custody) would only be a non-Intermediary support services supplier — paying GST on its own service fee to the cash-holding intermediary
📝 O-yeah Rooms lists on BRED.com. If BRED.com has cash custody — BRED.com pays GST/TCS. O-yeah Rooms is just providing a listing support service to BRED.com and pays GST only on its own fee to BRED.com.
RESALE OF SERVICES
Non-transparency of resale prices and fee of each Intermediary can neither alter nature of supplies involved nor can it imply either of them to be the Supplier of primary supply
📝 Even if you don't know what each platform charges — that opacity doesn't change who the real supplier is. The legal analysis of who has cash custody and who is the promisor must be done on the actual contractual facts.
24.3.5 — Discounts by Intermediaries ⇧ Index
🏷️ Discounts by Intermediaries — Cannot Alter Primary Supply Contract Price
Two Supplies Must be Identified Separately | Merging = Erroneous in GST
📝 When an intermediary offers a "discount" on a product — it is NOT reducing the seller's price. The seller-buyer contract price remains what it is. The intermediary is making a separate payment from its own listing fee/commission revenue. These are two separate supplies and must be taxed separately. Merging them is contractually wrong AND GST-wrong.
CANNOT ALTER PRIMARY SUPPLY PRICE
When Intermediary is NOT the Supplier — consideration for primary supply cannot be altered by Intermediary
Discounts offered by Intermediary cannot diminish the contract price of the primary supply
📝 Amazon Paytm cashback of ₹200 on a ₹1,000 phone. The phone was sold by seller for ₹1,000. GST is on ₹1,000 — not ₹800. The ₹200 cashback is Amazon/Paytm's separate expenditure, not a price reduction on the phone.
LISTING FEE / COMMISSION
Intermediary may be entitled to collect listing fee or commission from Supplier under a secondary supply — and some part of this may be 'shared' with Recipient of primary supply
→ Two supplies must be identified and subject to tax treatment on respective taxable values
📝 Amazon charges commission from seller (secondary supply: seller → Amazon). Amazon may share some of this commission with the buyer as cashback. Both transactions need independent GST analysis.
MERGING IS WRONG
Merging and muddling is incongruous in Contract law and erroneous in GST law
Lower payment made by Recipient needs careful unbundling and taxing of respective supplies in respective hands
📝 Merging the two transactions = treating the seller as if they sold at ₹800 = wrong. The seller sold at ₹1,000. The platform chose to give ₹200 from its own pocket. These are separate economic events and separate GST events.
24.3.6 — Profit Protection ⇧ Index
🛡️ Profit Protection Scheme — Additional Consideration for Primary Supply
Acceptance-Failure Test | Listing Services | Deep Discount Assurance
📝 "Profit protection" = an intermediary promises the seller that if you offer deep discounts exclusively on our platform, we will pay you extra to compensate for the loss in margin. This payment by the intermediary to the supplier is actually additional consideration for the goods supply — because without it, the supplier would not offer the deep discount.
Acceptance-Failure Test Applied to Profit Protection Payments
TEST (a)
Would Supplier continue to be listed on the platform even when NO payment was made by Intermediary? OR be ousted quickly?
TEST (b)
Would Supplier refuse to offer deeply discounted prices for the primary supply on the platform without the profit protection payment?
CONSEQUENCE IF TESTS FAIL
If any payments by Intermediary are 'profit protection' scheme — then Supplier would be liable to tax on the total amount received for the primary supply:
(i) partly from Recipient (Customer) AND (ii) partly under this scheme from Intermediary.

Supplier must aggregate amounts collected from Intermediary with that collected from Promisee as the total consideration of primary supply of goods.
📝 If the acceptance-failure test shows the seller wouldn't offer ₹799 price without Flipkart's ₹200 "profit protection" payment — then the phone was really sold for ₹999 (₹799 from buyer + ₹200 from Flipkart). GST should be on ₹999, not ₹799.
💡 A fashion brand lists on Myntra at ₹500 (MRP ₹800). Myntra pays the brand ₹150 as "profit protection." Without this ₹150, brand would not offer 37.5% discount. → Acceptance-failure test shows ₹150 is part of goods sale consideration. Total taxable value = ₹650 (₹500 + ₹150), not ₹500.
24.3.7 — Free is Not Free ⇧ Index
🆓 "Free is Not Free" — Consumer is the Commodity
Data Disclosure | Viewership | VoIP | GST Accepts Transaction Price
📝 Free search, free mail, free content — none of this is truly free. The user gives something in return: their data, their attention, their consent to receive advertisements. The customer is the commodity. But this is NOT a barter in law — private motivations (data being extracted) do not form consideration in a contract. GST taxes transaction price, not notional values for unrelated parties.
THE PHENOMENON
Customers acquired by offering: (i) high quality + (ii) free-of-charge services like search, mail, content sharing, news, utilities
Where these utilities form object of enforceable contract → look for consideration that is liable to tax
📝 If there is an enforceable contract (terms of service accepted) where the user gives something of value — consideration may exist. But the question is whether what the user "gives" (data, attention) constitutes consideration in contract law.
NOT A BARTER
Private motivations do NOT form consideration in a contract
When utilities offered free-of-charge → consumer is the commodity who accepts offering and agrees to data disclosure or viewership of push notifications / advertisements
This is NOT a barter
📝 Google offering free search in exchange for your data — Google has a private motivation (data monetization) but there is no contractual obligation on the user to give data. The user just uses the service. No barter, no consideration, no taxable supply from Google to user (or user to Google).
VoIP EXAMPLE
TRAI permitted VoIP services (below PSTN quality) free-of-charge to data customers — provided full disclosure of quality and use of data network AND devices cannot merge VoIP signals into PSTN network
📝 WhatsApp/Skype calls — allowed free because they are below PSTN quality and full disclosure made. Customers accept data network usage in exchange. TRAI recognized this is NOT a phone call — it is a data application. No telecom GST applies.
GST APPROACH
GST has accepted transaction price as the taxing base
Applies notional values only when related persons OR distinct persons are involved
For unrelated parties: transaction price = taxable value
"If something is available and its actually free, then you are the commodity."
📝 For arm's length transactions between unrelated parties — GST taxes what was actually paid. No imputation of notional value for free services to unrelated users. The concern arises only between related/distinct persons where the transaction price may be artificially low.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 24 | Internet Intermediaries ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision or principle.
Intermediary Cannot Become Promisor — Even with Deep Involvement
Legal Principle | Para 24.2.1 & 24.2.2 | Core of Internet Intermediary Analysis
Key Principle
Not even elevated responsibilities of an Intermediary — including assurances of reliability and undertaking to indemnify in case of default — convert the Intermediary to be the Promisor of the supplies and displace the actual Promisor from that position qua the Promisee. Intermediary is not the Promisor, not even if the entire payment from Promisee is collected by Intermediary. Intermediary is not the Promisee, not even if fulfilment of supplies is facilitated such as storage, packing or transportation responsibilities are assumed by Intermediary.
When to Use — When Department demands GST from e-commerce platform as if it is the supplier of goods (not the third-party seller). Cite to establish: collecting money + facilitating logistics does NOT make the platform the supplier. The seller remains the Promisor. Platform pays GST only on its own commission/fee (Route c) unless specifically notified under Sec 9(5).
Two Supplies Cannot Be Merged — Each Supply Taxed on Its Own Merits
Legal Principle | Para 24.2.3 | Cash-Back Analysis | Eg.661
Key Principle
It is impermissible to merge two supplies and apply a single tax treatment. Each supply is liable to tax treatment on its own merits. HSN, tariff rate, credit restrictions, time and place of supply and valuation will mutatis mutandis apply to each supply. It is no one's case that the goods are sold at a lower price merely because cash-back is offered. Merging and muddling is incongruous in Contract law and erroneous in GST law.
When to Use — (i) When Department treats cashback as reducing the primary supply value and demands GST on reduced value. Counter: two separate supplies, each taxed independently. (ii) When assessee treats cashback as reducing GST liability. Counter: cashback = secondary supply of services, taxed separately. Primary supply taxed at full contract price.
Cash Custody — The Single Decisive Test for ECOM GST Obligations
Legal Principle | Para 24.2.5 & 24.3.3 | Section 9(5) & Section 52 CGST Act
Key Principle
Only when Intermediary holds custody to the cash involved in the trade, Intermediary is in a position to either discharge output tax in terms of legal fiction in section 9(5) or collect tax at source under section 52 of Central GST Act. Where Intermediary does not have 'cash custody' then neither of the above applies and Intermediary is liable to discharge tax only on the fee charged for its own services.
When to Use — When Department demands TCS compliance or Sec 9(5) liability from a platform that does NOT hold trade cash. Counter: cash custody is the decisive test. Without cash custody, neither Sec 9(5) nor Sec 52 applies. The platform is only a listing/information service — GST only on its own fee. Also use to establish that social media platforms (no cash custody from buyer-seller trades) are not e-commerce operators.
Section 9(5) "As If" Fiction — Supplier Displaced, No Double Taxation | Circular 167/23/2021-GST
Legal Principle | Para 24.3.2 | Section 9(5) CGST Act | Circular 167/23/2021-GST dated 17th Dec 2021 (Para 6)
Key Principle
Where the law uses the expression "as if" then fact is replaced by fiction. And when Intermediary is the Supplier then the Supplier (Promisor) is no longer the Supplier. This is an obvious extension of the legal fiction. Legal fiction cannot be expanded beyond the terms for which it is enacted. Since the legal fiction in section 9(5) addresses the treatment of transaction between Supplier and Customer, transaction between Intermediary and Supplier cannot be altered by this legal fiction. Once the entire transaction value has been subject to GST albeit in the hands of Intermediary, there remains nothing more in the transaction value to be taxed. Any charges payable by Supplier (Promisor) to Intermediary will continue to be taxable — affirmed in Circular 167/23/2021-GST.
When to Use — (i) When ECOM operator already paying GST under Sec 9(5) is also asked to pay GST on commission/listing fee — counter: double taxation = cumulative value exceeds 100%, legal fiction cannot be expanded. (ii) When supplier claims its commission payment to ECOM is not taxable because ECOM pays GST under Sec 9(5) — counter: Circular 167 affirms commission to ECOM remains separately taxable as inward supply of listing service.
Acceptance-Failure Test — Profit Protection Payments are Additional Consideration
Legal Principle | Para 24.3.6 | Profit Protection Schemes | Deep Discount Platforms
Key Principle
Payment by Intermediary must be subject to 'acceptance-failure' test to see if: (a) Supplier would continue to be listed on the platform even when no payment was made by Intermediary, or be ousted quickly; or (b) Supplier would refuse to offer deeply discounted prices for the primary supply on the platform. When it is so, Supplier would be liable to tax on the total amount received for the primary supply — partly from Recipient (Customer) and partly under this profit protection scheme from Intermediary. Promisor (Supplier) should aggregate amounts collected from Intermediary with that collected from Promisee as the total consideration of primary supply of goods.
When to Use — In cases where e-commerce platforms pay "profit protection," "brand support," "marketing support," or "sales support" fees to suppliers. Apply acceptance-failure test: if supplier would not offer the deep discount without the payment, the payment = additional consideration for supply. Total GST taxable value = price paid by customer PLUS amount received from platform. The supplier must aggregate both.
Information Portal vs E-Commerce Operator — Trade Must Conclude on Platform
Legal Principle | Para 24.3.1 | Eg.663 & Eg.664 | Section 9(5) & Section 52
Key Principle
When visitor to the platform gets all information and exits the platform to conclude the transaction with the selected party directly, then the Intermediary is only an information portal and not one who would attract any of the special tax treatment applicable in GST to Intermediaries. The differentiating factor is whether the Intermediary gains 'cash custody' of the trade. Without cash custody, Intermediary is merely an information listing service and GST applies only on its own fee.
When to Use — When Department demands TCS compliance from a comparison portal, listing website, or aggregator that does NOT collect payments. Counter: no cash custody = information portal only. The moment the same portal starts collecting payments — it crosses into e-commerce operator territory. Eg.663/664 establishes this line precisely.
Intermediary-of-Intermediaries — Only ONE Cash Custody Holder | No Two-Level TCS
Legal Principle | Para 24.3.4 | Eg.668 | Section 52 CGST Act
Key Principle
While it is possible that more than one Agent can canvass inventory belonging to one Principal, when a trade is concluded, there must be either (i) only one Agent concluding that trade or (ii) the two Agents must have 'agent and sub-agent' relationship inter se. Of the two Intermediaries involved, the one who has 'cash custody' will be liable to comply with GST requirements. The other would only be a non-Intermediary support services supplier. Non-transparency of resale prices and fee of each Intermediary can neither alter nature of supplies involved nor imply either of them to be the Supplier of primary supply.
When to Use — In hotel aggregator-of-aggregator cases (OTAs listing on meta-search engines). Counter demand for double TCS: only one intermediary can have cash custody. The other is a support services supplier. The intermediary WITH cash custody pays TCS/Sec 9(5). The other pays GST on its own support service fee only.
"Free is Not Free" — Consumer is the Commodity | GST Taxes Transaction Price Only
Legal Principle | Para 24.3.7 | Unrelated Parties | GST Valuation Rules
Key Principle
Private motivations do not form consideration in a contract. When utilities are offered free-of-charge, the consumer is the commodity who accepts the offering and agrees to data disclosure or viewership of push notifications or advertisement. This is not a barter. GST has accepted transaction price as the taxing base and applies notional values to be imputed only when related persons or distinct persons are involved. Someone said it well: "if something is available and its actually free, then you are the commodity."
When to Use — When Department tries to impute notional value to "free" services offered by internet platforms to unrelated users (free search, free email, free navigation). Counter: private motivations (data extraction) are not consideration in contract law. GST taxes transaction price for unrelated parties — which is zero. Notional value is imputed only for related/distinct persons under Rule 28/30.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 25 — Insurance Laws

🛡️ Chapter 25 — Insurance Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Caution
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
25.1 Relevance to GST
25.2.1 Actors — Insurer, Insured & Supplier
25.2.2 Privity of Contract
25.2.3 Loss Payee (Eg.669 & Eg.670)
25.2.4 IRDA Licensee
25.3.1 Contract of Assurance — Instantaneous Supply
25.3.2 Contract of Repair
25.3.3 Right of Subrogation (Section 79 Marine)
25.3.4 Credit Shopping (FAQ 75 CBIC)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
25.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🛡️ Insurance Laws — Relevance to GST
Insurance Act, 1938 | Marine Insurance Act, 1963 | Private Matters Legislation
📝 The single most important GST principle in insurance: compensation paid to the insured is NOT liable to GST. Settlement of an insurance claim is compensation — not consideration for a supply. Beyond this, insurance law illuminates several GST issues: ITC eligibility, time of supply, privity of contract in repair claims, and IRDA licensing requirements for valid assurance contracts.
CORE PRINCIPLE
Compensation is NOT liable to GST
Settlement of insurance claim is compensation to Insured — not a taxable supply
📝 When an insurer pays out on a claim — that payment is compensation for a loss. It is not payment for a service. The insurer is not "buying" anything. Therefore GST does not apply on the compensation payment itself.
LOSS DETERMINATION
Determination of loss takes various factors into account and compensation addresses each of these factors
Impact of ITC greatly alters profitability of Insurers
📝 When calculating how much compensation to pay, the insurer must consider: depreciation, salvage value, ITC that the insured could have claimed on repairs. If insured can claim ITC on repair cost — compensation is lower (tax-exclusive). If not — compensation is higher (tax-inclusive).
ITC ASYMMETRY
Restrictions that apply to Insured in claiming ITC do NOT apply on all fours to Insurers
Insured may be blocked from ITC; Insurer may still claim ITC on same repair service
📝 A person whose car insurance claim leads to garage repair — the person (insured) may not get ITC on the repair. But the insurance company (if it pays the garage directly) may claim ITC on the same repair service. Different tax positions for the same underlying supply.
PRIVATE MATTERS LEGISLATION
Insurance Act 1938 and Marine Insurance Act 1963 — subject to strict construction
Assertions admitted under these legislations will have a bearing on tax treatment of underlying transactions in GST
📝 What you assert to IRDA (insurance regulator) about your insurance product will be used in GST proceedings. The two laws are private matters' legislations — strict interpretation governs. Regulatory assertions bind the parties in GST.
REINSURANCE NOTE
Reinsurance is a big but innocuous part of insurance business — the insurer reinsures its own risk. GST treatment of reinsurance follows the same principles as direct insurance.
25.2.1 — Actors — Insurer, Insured & Supplier ⇧ Index
👥 Three Actors — Insurer, Insured & Supplier
Section 2(9) Insurance Act | Insurer = IRDA Licensee | Supplier = Independent Third Party
INSURER
Section 2(9) Insurance Act
Licensed by IRDA to enter into a trade to indemnify a contingent event based on independent assessment of its likelihood
📝 Insurer = insurance company (LIC, New India, HDFC Ergo etc.) licensed by IRDA. They take on the risk of a contingent (uncertain future) event happening and agree to pay if it does.
💡 Section 2(9) covers: (a) individual/body corporate incorporated outside India carrying on insurance business in India; (b) Indian body corporate; (c) person with Lloyd's underwriter contract — but NOT principal agent, chief agent, special agent, insurance agent or provident society.
INSURED (Policy-Holder)
One who is exposed to the contingency and enters into a contract of assurance with Insurer
In contract: Promisor = Insurer (Offeror) | Promisee = Insured (Offeree)
📝 The Insurer makes the offer (I will cover this contingency for this premium). The Insured accepts the offer. Uniquely — in insurance, the Insurer is the Promisor (offeror) and the Insured is the Offeree/Promisee.
SUPPLIER
Supplier of parts for repair or replacement of article insured is NEITHER the Insurer NOR the Insured
Supplier is an independent entity who is NOT a party to the contract of assurance
📝 When your car is damaged and sent to a garage — the garage (Supplier of repair services) has NO contract with the insurance company. The garage's contract is only with the car owner (Insured). This contractual reality has important GST implications for ITC and invoice issuance.
CONTINGENCY CLARIFICATION
Contingency may be fire or other natural causes that impede the expected course of events — capable of causing loss to the Insured.
Incident → remediation could be: (i) repairs OR (ii) replacement
This loss is assured by Insurer in exchange for a premium (consideration in the contract of assurance).
25.2.2 — Privity of Contract ⇧ Index
🔗 Privity of Contract — The Critical Chain
Insurer — Insured — Supplier | Insurer Has NO Privity with Supplier
📝 Privity of contract = having a direct contractual relationship. The privity chain in insurance has a gap — the Insurer and Supplier have NO direct contract. This gap creates the GST complexity in credit shopping and claim settlement.
Privity Chain in Insurance
✓ Insurer ↔ Insured
Contract of Assurance
(Premium paid by Insured)
✓ Insured ↔ Supplier
Supply of repair/replacement parts
(Insured may have originally bought from Supplier)
✗ Insurer ↔ Supplier
NO privity
Insurer has NO contractual relationship with Supplier
Supplier is an independent entity
📝 This gap between Insurer and Supplier is the fundamental contractual reality of insurance. The garage has no contract with the insurance company. The car owner (insured) is in the middle.
PREMIUM STRUCTURE
Payment of premium creates the contract of assurance
Premium may be paid in advance → on expiration of term, renewal is automatic
Insurer is Promisor (Offeror) | Insured is Promisee (Offeree)
📝 The Insurer makes the offer — "I will cover X contingency for Y premium." The Insured accepts. The Insured cannot structure the policy or decide the premium — they can only decide whether to accept the Insurer's offer.
INSURED'S LIMITED ROLE
Insured is NOT in a position to structure the policy of insurance and decide the consideration (premium)
Insured only knows about the contingency and would like to be covered
Insurer is the one who is able to devise the policy and determine the premium
📝 You cannot tell your insurance company: "I want to pay ₹5,000 premium for ₹20 lakh cover." The insurer actuarially determines the premium based on risk assessment. The insured takes it or leaves it.
25.2.3 — Loss Payee (Eg.669 & Eg.670) ⇧ Index
💳 Loss Payee — Nominated Third Party for Compensation
Two Contracts | No New Privity | Compensation Belongs to Insured | Accounting Tables
📝 "Loss Payee" = a person named in the insurance policy to receive the compensation payment when a claim is settled, instead of the Insured. The Loss Payee can be a bank (lender), a leasing company, or any third party named by the Insured. But naming someone as Loss Payee does NOT create any new contractual privity between Insurer and Loss Payee — and the compensation still belongs to the Insured.
BASIC RULE
Any person may be named as 'Loss Payee' in insurance policy
When contingent event occurs → Insurer is discharged of obligations by making payment to 'loss Payee' named in the policy
📝 The person paying the premium is the default Loss Payee. But the Insured can instruct the Insurer to pay compensation to a third party instead. Insurer follows these instructions — like a trustee paying per beneficiary's instructions.
💡 Eg.669: Account holder nominates child instead of spouse as nominee. Bank is discharged on obligations when balance is paid to nominee. Bank does not determine succession under applicable law.
BANK AS LOSS PAYEE
Lender (Bank) imposed as condition in loan agreement:
(i) Borrower insures primary security (asset financed) from perils under fire policy
(ii) Lender be named as 'loss Payee'

Under contract of assurance — Insurer obliged to pay 'to or to order of' Insured → in terms of condition, Lender's name endorsed as Loss Payee
📝 Bank says: "I gave you loan to buy machinery. You must insure it and name me as Loss Payee. If the machine is destroyed, I want to be paid first." This is a standard banking condition to protect loan security.
💡 Eg.670: Company A borrowed from ABC Bank to purchase machinery. In fire policy, ABC Bank named as 'loss Payee' so that compensation is first paid to Bank who retains outstanding dues and remits balance (if any) to Company A. Insurer is NOT privy to loan agreement.
NO NEW PRIVITY CREATED
Inclusion of third-Party as 'loss Payee' does NOT create any privity of contract between Insurer and Lender
Compensation paid by Insurer to Lender does NOT make the compensation 'belong to Lender'

'Compensation' is to defray loss — inextricably linked to person who is 'entitled' to compensation = Insured
📝 The bank receives the money as an agent/trustee of the Insured — not in its own right. The bank uses the money to repay the loan (which the Insured owed) and returns the balance to the Insured. The bank is NOT the beneficial owner of the compensation.
📊 Accounting Treatment — Eg.670 (Loss Payee = Bank)
Table A — Books of Insured
Compensation receivableDr.100
To Asset *Cr.100
Term loan from BankDr.100
To Compensation receivable *Cr.140
📝 * Credit to asset (and to other income) must be reworked based on accumulated depreciation and realization from disposal of wreck adjusted with compensation approved.
Table B — Books of Lender
BankDr.100
To Term loan of Company ACr.140
📝 Care must be taken NOT to account the compensation in books of Lender. Compensation received by bank reduces Company A's loan — not the bank's income.
⚠️ GST COMPLIANCE — INSURED'S RESPONSIBILITY
When compensation is collected 'to order of' Insured, compliance with Section 17(5)(h) (in case of total loss) or Section 18(6) (in case of partial loss and disposal) of CGST Act will have to be ensured by Insured.
It is also NOT permissible for Insured to credit entire compensation as other income.
Compensation is NOT consideration — refer detailed illustration under 'compensation is not consideration' where intricate GST implications are exposed in the details of arriving at compensation to be settled.
📝 When insured goods are totally destroyed — the insured must reverse ITC under Sec 17(5)(h). When partially lost/sold as scrap — Sec 18(6) applies. Both are the Insured's compliance obligations, not the Insurer's. The bank receiving compensation as Loss Payee does not trigger these obligations in the bank's books.
25.2.4 — IRDA Licensee ⇧ Index
📜 IRDA Licensee — No Insurance Without License | Void Ab Initio if Unlicensed
Section 2C + Section 3 | Insurance Act | Indemnity/Guarantee Contracts Must Be Examined
📝 No one can carry on insurance business in India without an IRDA license. This is an absolute prohibition. Any attempt to provide "insurance" by an unlicensed entity — even if dressed up as indemnity or guarantee — is void ab initio. Contracts of indemnity and guarantee must be carefully examined to ensure they are not covert insurance contracts.
Section 2C(1) + Section 3: No person shall, after commencement of this Act, begin to carry on any class of insurance business in India and no insurer carrying on any class of insurance business in India shall, after expiry of three months from the commencement of this Act, continue to carry on any such business, unless he has obtained from the Authority a certificate of registration for the particular class of insurance business.

Provided also that no insurer other than an Indian insurance company shall begin to carry on any class of insurance business in India under this Act on or after the commencement of the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999.
ABSOLUTE PROHIBITION
No form of 'assurance' akin to insurance against any contingency by way of indemnity or guarantee can be provided by any unlicensed entity
If such insurance is extended — same will be void ab initio for being in direct violation of Insurance Act
📝 Void ab initio = void from the very beginning. If Company X (not an insurance company) offers to "insure" your goods against fire damage for a "fee" — that contract is void. No rights, no obligations, no GST treatment can flow from it.
EXAMINE CAREFULLY
Care must be taken to re-examine:
• Contracts of indemnity
• Contracts of guarantee
These may be disguised insurance contracts
📝 Some entities try to offer "extended warranty," "damage protection," or "credit guarantee" without IRDA license. If these are truly insurance contracts in substance (covering contingent events for consideration), they are void. The substance governs, not the label.
VALID INDEMNITY (Eg.671)
Indemnity is valid if contingency indemnified is non-working of equipment supplied
(Supplier guarantees its own equipment will work — not a contingent event, it is a warranty)
📝 A manufacturer offering a warranty that their equipment will work = valid indemnity (not insurance). The contingency (equipment not working) is within the manufacturer's control. This is warranty/after-sales service — not insurance.
💡 Eg.671: Indemnity is valid if contingency indemnified is non-working of equipment supplied.
VALID GUARANTEE (Eg.672)
Guarantee is valid if contingency is default in repayment of loan by Subsidiary which may in turn be due to destruction of factory by fire accident
(Bank guarantee — not insurance)
📝 A bank guarantee for loan repayment is valid — even if the underlying reason for default might be a fire. The guarantee is against the credit risk (default), not directly against the fire risk. Bank guarantees are not insurance.
💡 Eg.672: Guarantee is valid if contingency is default in repayment of loan by Subsidiary which is may in turn be due to destruction of factory by fire accident.
25.3.1 — Contract of Assurance — Instantaneous Supply ⇧ Index
⚡ Contract of Assurance — Instantaneous Supply | Time of Supply = Policy Issuance
Section 16(2)(b) CGST | ITC Immediately Available | Premium = Not Return for Compensation
📝 This is one of the most important GST principles for insurers: the insurance supply is COMPLETE on the day the policy is issued. Not when the premium is fully paid. Not when the claim is settled. Not at the end of the policy term. The supply is instantaneous — and this has direct consequences for ITC (Section 16(2)(b)) and time of supply.
NATURE OF PREMIUM
Payment of premium is NOT in return for compensation
Payment of premium is for the assurance that should the named contingency occur, specified amount of compensation will be settled
📝 You pay premium for the peace of mind that IF something happens, you will be compensated. Not to buy the compensation itself. The premium buys the assurance (the promise) — not the payout.
TIME OF SUPPLY — KEY RULE
Supply by Insurer is complete immediately on issuance of policy
Payment of compensation is NOT the test of completion of supply
Condition in Section 16(2)(b) CGST Act will be satisfied immediately on issuance of policy and not be deferred until end of term
📝 Section 16(2)(b) requires that goods/services are received before ITC can be claimed. For insurance — the "supply" is received the moment the policy is issued. So ITC can be claimed immediately. The insured need not wait for the policy to expire or a claim to be made.
💡 Company buys fire insurance policy on 1st April. Policy covers April to March. GST paid on premium. ITC eligible from 1st April itself (policy issuance date) — not 31st March end of term. Supply complete on day of issuance.
REFUND OF PREMIA
Refund of premia paid in case of termination of contract does NOT make:
• Contract performance by Insurer incomplete on date of issuance of policy, OR
• Render the contract one of continuous supply
📝 If you cancel a policy mid-term and get a refund — the insurance company's supply is not retroactively incomplete. The supply was complete on day 1. The refund is for the unexpired portion — it does not change the nature of the original supply as instantaneous.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Contract of assurance is an instantaneous supply which is performed as soon as policy is issued.
It is NOT a continuous supply.
📝 Continuous supply = supply spread over time (like telecom, electricity). Insurance is NOT continuous supply — even though the policy runs for a year. The supply of "assurance" is instantaneous at issuance. This affects time of supply, ITC, and reverse charge calculations.
25.3.2 — Contract of Repair ⇧ Index
🔧 Contract of Repair — Insured's Choice | Compensation ≠ Automatic
Volition | No-Claim Bonus | Insurer Cannot Compel | Repair/Replacement Options
📝 One of the most misunderstood aspects of insurance: compensation is NOT automatically payable just because the named contingency occurred. The Insured has a choice — claim or not claim. This choice (volition) belongs to the Insured. And if the Insured decides to claim, the Insurer cannot deny the claim unless there are specific delinquencies.
INSURED'S CHOICE
There is no compulsion that whenever the named contingency occurs, the Insured must claim compensation
Insured may choose NOT to claim insurance and instead claim 'no claim bonus' — which may be more than the claim itself
📝 If your car has a minor scratch and claim would cost you your no-claim bonus (NCB) discount — you might choose to pay for the repair yourself and preserve the NCB. This is your choice. The contingency occurred but you chose not to claim.
VOLITION IS KEY
Compensation is NOT automatically payable even in absence of a claim although the named contingency has occurred
Volition or choice is the key aspect — this choice is with Insured and NOT with Insurer
📝 Volition = free exercise of will. The Insured exercises their will to claim or not. The Insurer cannot initiate payment without a claim. This is the foundational principle that compensation is demand-driven (by Insured), not automatic.
IF CLAIM IS MADE
Insured may pay for the repairs or replacement to Supplier (repair shop/parts dealer)
And where a claim is made → Insurer cannot deny the claim unless there are other delinquencies in the claim
📝 Once you validly claim — the insurer must pay. The only ground for denial is "delinquencies" — like misrepresentation, non-disclosure of material facts, exclusion clause applying, etc. Insurer cannot arbitrarily deny valid claims.
💡 Insured's car damaged in accident. Insured sends car to preferred garage. Insured claims from insurance. Insurer cannot say "you should have used our network garage" unless the policy specifically requires it and Insured violated that term (delinquency).
25.3.3 — Right of Subrogation (Section 79 Marine Insurance Act) ⇧ Index
⚖️ Right of Subrogation — Insurer Steps Into Insured's Shoes After Claim Settlement
Section 79 Marine Insurance Act | Total Loss vs Partial Loss | Title Transfer | NOT Listed in Section 7(1)(a) CGST
📝 Subrogation = after the Insurer pays the full insurance claim (total loss), the Insurer gets the rights over whatever remains (wreck/scrap) of the insured article. The title to the wreck passes to the Insurer — not by sale, not by gift — but by the legal doctrine of subrogation. This is NOT one of the 8 forms of supply listed in Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act.
Section 79 Marine Insurance Act — Right of subrogation:
(1) Where the insurer pays for a total loss, either of the whole, or in the case of goods of any apportionable part, of the subject-matter insured, he thereupon becomes entitled to take over the interest of the assured in whatever may remain of the subject-matter so paid for, and he is thereby subrogated to all the rights and remedies of the assured in and in respect of that subject-matter as from the time of the casualty causing the loss.
(2) Subject to the foregoing provisions, where the insurer pays for a partial loss, he acquires no title to the subject-matter insured, or such part of it as may remain, but he is thereupon subrogated to all rights and remedies of the assured in and in respect of the subject-matter insured as from the time of the casualty causing the loss, in so far as the assured has been indemnified, according to this Act, by such payment for the loss.
TOTAL LOSS
Insurer pays full claim → Insurer becomes entitled to take over interest of Assured in whatever remains of subject-matter (wreck/scrap)
Insurer is subrogated to ALL rights and remedies of Assured
→ Title of wreck passes to Insurer
📝 Car completely destroyed in accident. Insurer pays full claim. The burnt wreck of the car is now the Insurer's property. The Insurer can sell the wreck as scrap and keep the proceeds. The Insured has been fully compensated and has no further right to the wreck.
PARTIAL LOSS
Insurer pays for partial loss → Insurer acquires NO title to subject-matter insured or remaining part
BUT Insurer is subrogated to rights and remedies of Assured to extent Assured has been indemnified
📝 Car partially damaged. Insurer pays for repairs. The car still belongs to the Insured. But Insurer can step into Insured's shoes to recover damages from the third party who caused the accident (to the extent Insurer paid).
GST — TITLE TRANSFER BY SUBROGATION
Passing of title is NOT by 'subrogation' which is NOT listed in the 8 forms of supply in Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act
→ Not a taxable supply by the Insured to the Insurer
📝 The 8 forms of supply in Sec 7(1)(a) are: sale, transfer, barter, exchange, licence, rental, lease, disposal. Subrogation is not on this list. So when the Insured's title to the wreck passes to the Insurer by subrogation — there is no "supply" and no GST.
INSURER'S DISPOSAL OF WRECK
Insurer will now dispose the wreck or scrap to a dealer OR authorize any other person (Insurer or Supplier/repair shop) to take care of disposal and account for the proceeds to Insurer
📝 Once Insurer has title to the wreck (by subrogation after total loss) — Insurer sells the wreck as scrap. GST applies on the Insurer's sale of the wreck/scrap (this is a regular sale by Insurer as seller). The Insurer must account for this sale and the GST on it.
25.3.4 — Credit Shopping (CBIC FAQ 75) ⇧ Index
🛒 Credit Shopping — Insurer Claims ITC on Repair Despite No Privity
CBIC FAQs Dec 2018 | FAQ 75 | Invoice Addressed to Insurer | Incongruence with Privity
📝 "Credit shopping" = when an insurer claims ITC on repair/replacement services even though the Insurer has NO contractual privity with the Supplier (repair shop). CBIC's own FAQ 75 (Dec 2018) permits this — but the chapter questions whether this is legally sound given the fundamental absence of privity between Insurer and Supplier.
CBIC FAQ 75 — PERMITS ITC
CBIC FAQs December 2018 permits Insurer to claim ITC when claims are settled directly with Supplier towards cost of repairs or replacements
FAQ 75 — "Whether ITC will be allowed on motor garage services used by insurance company for claim settlement? Yes, ITC will be allowed on services of motor garage used by an insurance company for claim settlement."
📝 CBIC's administrative position: when insurer pays the garage directly for repairs on the insured's car, the insurer can claim ITC on the repair service. Practically this is a very significant benefit — insurer's ITC reduces the effective cost of claims.
THE INCONGRUENCE
Supplier (repair/replacement) will issue tax invoice addressed to Insurer and delivering the parts to Insured

This appears to be completely incongruous with the contractual privity that Supplier has with Insured and NOT with Insurer
📝 The garage's contract (for car repair) is with the car owner (Insured) — not with the insurance company. So how can the garage issue a tax invoice to the insurance company? The invoice recipient should match the contractual party receiving the service.
THE TENSION
When it is unequivocal that there is no contractual privity between Insurer and Supplier — in case of repair or replacement of insured article — the permission to claim ITC creates an interesting tension

Author's note: This is incongruous with privity principles
📝 The CBIC FAQ creates a practical convenience — but it conflicts with basic contract law. The author flags this as a potential litigation risk: Supplier has privity with Insured, not Insurer. Invoice to Insurer for a service rendered to Insured may be legally questionable.
💡 Car accident. Repair shop fixes insured's car. Invoice is raised in insurer's name. Insurer pays and claims ITC. But: repair shop's contract was with the car owner (Insured), not the insurer. The invoice-recipient mismatch creates a legal incongruence that FAQ 75 glosses over.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 25 | Insurance Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the statutory provision or principle.
Compensation is NOT Liable to GST — Settlement of Insurance Claim ≠ Consideration
Core Principle | Para 25.1 | Insurance Act 1938 | Private Matters Legislation
Key Principle
Compensation is not liable to GST. Settlement of insurance claim is compensation to Insured — not consideration for a supply. Compensation is to defray loss and is inextricably linked to the person who is 'entitled' to compensation, which is the Insured. Compensation is not the consideration payable by the Insurer to Insured for any supply made by Insured. Payment of premium is not in return for compensation — payment of premium is for the assurance that should the named contingency occur, specified amount of compensation will be settled.
When to Use — When Department attempts to levy GST on insurance claim settlement amounts received by policyholders. Counter: compensation is not consideration for any supply — it is a settlement of loss. GST does not apply. Also use when arguing that insurance payout received by a business is not "consideration" that should be added to the taxable value of any supply.
Supplier Has No Privity with Insurer — Independent Entity Outside Contract of Assurance
Core Principle | Para 25.2.1 & 25.2.2 | Insurance Act 1938 | Privity of Contract
Key Principle
Supplier of parts for repair or replacement of article insured is neither the Insurer nor the Insured. Supplier is an independent entity who is not a party to the contract of assurance. Insurer has privity with Insured. Insured has privity with Supplier. Insurer has no privity with the Supplier. Locating contractual privity is important to identify the GST treatment applicable.
When to Use — (i) When Department demands Insurer to reverse ITC on repair services because "the service was received by the Insured, not the Insurer" — the no-privity rule supports denial. (ii) When examining who is the proper recipient of the repair service for GST purposes — the privity chain determines this. (iii) To challenge the legal basis of CBIC FAQ 75's ITC permission for Insurers.
Loss Payee — No New Privity | Compensation Belongs to Insured | Section 17(5)(h) & 18(6)
Core Principle | Para 25.2.3 | Section 17(5)(h) & Section 18(6) CGST Act
Key Principle
Inclusion of third-party as 'loss Payee' does not create any privity of contract between Insurer and Lender. Compensation paid by Insurer to Lender does not make the compensation belong to Lender. 'Compensation' is to defray loss and that is inextricably linked to person who is 'entitled' to compensation. Loss Payee is 'to order of' Insured. So compensation paid to Lender is actually paid to Insured who in turn used that money to discharge outstanding loan. When compensation is collected 'to order of' Insured, compliance with Section 17(5)(h) (total loss) or Section 18(6) (partial loss and disposal) of CGST Act will have to be ensured by Insured.
When to Use — (i) When Bank (Loss Payee) accounts for insurance compensation as its own income — counter: compensation belongs to the Insured (borrower), not the bank. (ii) When there is a dispute about who must ensure GST compliance (ITC reversal under 17(5)(h) or 18(6)) on destruction/disposal of insured goods — it is the Insured's obligation, not the Loss Payee bank's. (iii) When examining if compensation receipts attract GST in bank's hands — they do not, as it belongs to the Insured.
IRDA License is Mandatory — Unlicensed Insurance is Void Ab Initio
Core Principle | Para 25.2.4 | Section 2C & Section 3 Insurance Act | Eg.671 & Eg.672
Key Principle
No form of 'assurance' akin to insurance against any contingency by way of indemnity or guarantee can be provided by any unlicensed entity. Where there is any such insurance extended in the guise of indemnity or guarantee, the same will be void ab initio for being in direct violation of Insurance Act. Care must be taken to re-examine contracts of indemnity and contracts of guarantee. Indemnity is valid if contingency indemnified is non-working of equipment supplied (Eg.671). Guarantee is valid if contingency is default in repayment of loan (Eg.672).
When to Use — (i) When examining "extended warranty," "damage protection," or "credit protection" products offered by non-insurance companies — if these cover contingent events for consideration, they may be unlicensed insurance = void = GST treatment of underlying contract is disrupted. (ii) To distinguish valid indemnity (warranty by supplier) and valid bank guarantee from disguised insurance contracts. (iii) To establish that GST cannot apply on premiums for unlicensed insurance products — the contract is void ab initio.
Insurance = Instantaneous Supply | ITC Available on Policy Issuance | Not Continuous Supply
Core Principle | Para 25.3.1 | Section 16(2)(b) CGST Act | Time of Supply
Key Principle
Insurance is a contract of assurance — supply by Insurer is complete immediately on issuance of policy. Payment of compensation is not the test of completion of supply. Condition in Section 16(2)(b) of Central GST Act will be satisfied immediately on issuance of policy and not be deferred until end of term. Refund of premia paid in case of termination of contract does not make the contract performance by Insurer incomplete on date of issuance of policy or render the contract one of continuous supply. Contract of assurance is an instantaneous supply which is performed as soon as policy is issued.
When to Use — (i) When Department denies ITC on insurance premium saying service not "received" until policy expires or claim arises — counter: supply is instantaneous on policy issuance, Section 16(2)(b) satisfied on day 1. (ii) When Department treats insurance as "continuous supply" for TDS/reverse charge timing purposes — counter: insurance is NOT continuous supply, it is instantaneous. (iii) When time of supply is disputed — time of supply = date of invoice (policy issuance), not claim date.
Right of Subrogation — Title Transfer Not Listed in Section 7(1)(a) | No Supply
Core Principle | Para 25.3.3 | Section 79 Marine Insurance Act | Section 7(1)(a) CGST Act
Key Principle
Once claim is settled, wreck or scrap remaining of the article insured belongs to Insurer. Passing of title is not by 'subrogation' which is not listed in the eight (8) forms of supply in section 7(1)(a) of Central GST Act. Section 79 of Marine Insurance Act: where the insurer pays for a total loss, he thereupon becomes entitled to take over the interest of the assured in whatever may remain of the subject-matter so paid for, and he is thereby subrogated to all the rights and remedies of the assured. For partial loss — insurer acquires no title to subject-matter but is subrogated to all rights and remedies of the assured to the extent indemnified.
When to Use — (i) When Department argues that transfer of wreck from Insured to Insurer (after total loss settlement) is a "supply" by the Insured = liable to GST — counter: title passes by subrogation, which is NOT listed in the 8 forms of supply in Section 7(1)(a). No GST. (ii) When distinguishing total loss (Insurer gets title to wreck) from partial loss (Insurer gets no title, only subrogated rights). (iii) When Insurer disposes the wreck — that disposal is the Insurer's supply and GST applies on that sale.
Volition — Insured's Choice | Compensation Not Automatic | Insurer Cannot Deny Valid Claim
Core Principle | Para 25.3.2 | Contract of Repair | No-Claim Bonus Principle
Key Principle
There is no compulsion that whenever the named contingency occurs, the Insured must claim compensation. Insured may choose not to claim insurance instead claim 'no claim bonus' which may be more than the claim itself. The point is that compensation is not automatically payable even in the absence of a claim although the named contingency has occurred. Volition or choice is the key aspect — this choice is with Insured and not with Insurer. Insured may pay for the repairs or replacement to Supplier. And where a claim is made, Insurer cannot deny the claim unless there are other delinquencies in the claim.
When to Use — (i) When Department argues that the mere occurrence of an insured event = automatic taxable supply — counter: volition is with the Insured. Compensation arises only when claimed, not automatically. (ii) When analyzing whether an insurance claim creates a GST obligation — it does not by itself; it is the Insured's volitional claim that triggers the compensation, not the contingent event. (iii) When insurer tries to deny claim without valid ground — cite: insurer cannot deny claim except for specific delinquencies.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 26 — Banking Laws

🏦 Chapter 26 — Banking Laws

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Exempt / Valid / Permitted
Taxable / Caution / NOT Exempt
Condition / Test / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
26.1 Relevance to GST
26.2.1 Bank & Bank-like
26.2.2 Chit Fund
26.2.3 Fintech
26.2.4 Pre-paid Instruments (PPI)
26.2.5 White Label PPI
26.2.6 PPI Distribution
26.3.1 FEMA — Current Account
26.3.2 Netting Off Receivables
26.3.3 Nostro & Vostro
26.3.4 Guaranteed Repatriation
26.3.5 Softex
26.3.6 Merchanting Trade
26.3.7 Merchant Exports
26.3.8 Deemed Exports
26.3.9 GR Waiver
26.3.10 Non-exports
26.3.11 Overseas Intermediaries
26.3.12 FDI
26.3.13 ODI
26.4.1 Non-banking Branches
26.4.2 NPA
26.4.3 Digital Wallet
26.4.4 Redeemable Wallet
26.4.5–7 Loyalty / Sale of Liability / Coupons
26.4.8 Voucher v. Money
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
26.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
🏦 Banking Laws — Relevance to GST
Penumbral regions of banking business | Transaction in Money vs Taxable Services
⚠️ KEY WARNING
GST is NOT applicable to 'transactions in money' — but currency translation, lending, credit and other activities are TAXABLE and selective exempt.
One cannot ASSUME that an arrangement involves a transaction in money without knowing exactly how it fits within the contours.
Understanding the penumbral regions of banking business is important — without overburdening with a detailed coverage of banking per se.
📝 Penumbral region = the grey area between light and shadow — the boundary zone. In banking, the boundary between 'transaction in money' (no GST) and 'taxable service' (GST payable) is NOT clear-cut. Each arrangement must be examined in detail.
NO GST
Transactions in money
(Schedule III)
GST APPLICABLE
Currency translation
Lending
Credit activities
Other banking services
SELECTIVE EXEMPT
Interest on deposits/loans/advances
(cannot be routinely applied to NBFCs)
26.2.1 — Bank and Bank-like | NBFC Types ⇧ Index
🏛️ Bank vs Bank-like Entities — Key Distinctions
BANK
Body corporate duly — even when not incorporated under Companies Act.
Organized in any manner — but ONLY when licensed under RBI Act.
License = for the Legal Entity — not for each branch or location.
Regardless of nature of activities in a given branch.
📝 A company with 'banking' in its objects clause is NOT a bank unless it has an RBI license. The license covers the entire entity — all branches automatically come under it.
NBFC — Non-Banking Financial Company
Company registered under Companies Act 1956 engaged in:
• Loans and advances
• Acquisition of shares/stocks/bonds/debentures/securities
• Leasing, hire-purchase, insurance, chit business

Does NOT include: institutions whose principal business is agriculture, industrial activity, purchase/sale of goods (other than securities), providing services, or purchase/construction of immovable property.

Residuary non-banking company = company receiving deposits in lump sum or instalments by way of contributions.
10 Types of NBFCs (by principal business):
Asset finance | Investments (incl. securities) | Lending | Infrastructure finance | Systemically important core investment | Infrastructure debt fund | Micro finance institution | Factors | Mortgage guarantee | Non-operative financial holding
📝 GST exclusion for 'transactions in money' and exemption for 'interest on deposits, loans or advances' cannot be routinely applied to NBFCs — each NBFC type must be separately examined.
26.2.2 — Chit Fund ⇧ Index
💰 Chit Fund — Savings-cum-Credit Plan
DEFINITION — Section 2(b) Chit Funds Act
"Chit" means a transaction whether called chit, chit fund, chitty, kuree or by any other name by or under which a person enters into an agreement with a specified number of persons that every one of them shall subscribe a certain sum of money (or a certain quantity of grain) by way of periodical instalments over a definite period and that each such subscriber shall, in his turn, as determined by lot or by auction or by tender or by foreman or in such other manner as may be specified in the chit agreement, be entitled to the prize amount.
📝 A chit fund is a group savings pool. Each member contributes monthly. One member gets the entire pool each month (through bidding/lottery). Members who need money early 'bid' by offering a discount. The discount is shared among all as 'interest' on their subscriptions.
Chit Fund Flow — How it Works:
Organized by
'foreman'
→
Contribution by
'subscribers'
→
Periodic subscription
up to 'term' of plan
Collections held
'in trust'
→
One-time credit to
'bidder' out of collections
→
Bid (discount) distributed
to subscribers (excl. bidder)
Repayment by
paying remaining subscriptions
→
Subscriptions continue
→
Remuneration to foreman
= 'fee or commission'
KEY GST POINTS
Foreman's fee/commission: paid out first from the discount — only residual discount distributed to subscribers.
Chit funds monitored as Miscellaneous Non-banking Company — NOT regulated by RBI.
GST on net income of organizer — subject to Rule 31A of Central GST Rules.
Chit Fund vs Prize Scheme — Key Distinctions:
CriteriaChit FundPrize Scheme
Contribution is 'repayable deposit' (not irretrievable expense)YESNO
Collections held 'in trust' and 'on account of' contributorsYESNO
Contractual privity inter se contributorsYESNO
Claim of credit (or prize) is right of contributor (bidder or prize winner)YESYES
Credit (or prize) is 'application' by organizer out of collections held 'in trust'NOYES
⚠️ CAUTION
Care must be taken to uncover the 'facts' in each plan and not assume every plan operates like a chit fund plan and subject it to GST treatment on 'net income' of organizer and disparage policy wisdom in Rule 31A of Central GST Rules.
26.2.3 — Fintech ⇧ Index
📱 Fintech — Online Lending | Two-player Structure
STRUCTURE
Online canvassing and Borrower engagement (by fintech) presenting offline credit involves two players due to licensing requirement for lending activities — non-regulated technology offerings by internet economy enterprises is one way of describing finance-technology ('fintech') business.
RBI digital lending guidelines dated 2 Sept. 2022 + Working Group guidelines dated 10 Aug. 2022 throw light on the nature and contours of fintech enterprises.
Regulated Enterprise
(Bank/NBFC)
Borrower
Lending Service
Provider (Fintech)
⚠️ KEY GST RULES FOR FINTECH
(i) Must locate privity of contract and persons who have legal remedies under 'debtor-creditor' relationship
(ii) Identify role of intermediary(ies) in customer (borrower) acquisition and cross-cancelling supplies via cash-back and other plans

It is NOT possible to assume and admit lending service provider (intermediary) to be the regulated enterprise (Bank or NBFC) or vice versa.
Interest exemption in GST CANNOT be availed by intermediary — nor can NBFC each commission from new business referral.
📝 A fintech app that connects borrowers with banks is NOT a bank. It cannot claim the interest exemption. Its commission income from loan referral is taxable as a service. The Bank/NBFC (regulated entity) is the lender; the fintech is merely the intermediary.
INFORMATION vs RELIABLE FACTS
Fintech enterprises must provide 'information' that must then be validated to derive 'reliable facts' for purposes of applying appropriate GST treatment. Description of business activities by these actors cannot be relied upon as admission of facts contrary to the regulations that permit or restrict overenthusiastic revenue recognition. Ostensible legal authority to enter into contracts is determinative of kinds of revenue each is entitled to assert or collect.
26.2.4 — Pre-paid Instruments (PPI) ⇧ Index
💳 Pre-paid Instruments (PPI) — Stored Currency | Section 2(75) GST
PSS ACT PURPOSE
"An Act to provide for the regulation and supervision of payment systems in India and to designate the Reserve Bank of India as the authority for that purpose and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto."
PSS Act is a welfare matters' legislation.
WHAT IS PPI
PPI = stored currency (nothing less). It is actually legal money of fiat currency but with the convenience of carrying and usage — especially for online trade.
PPI = instrument recognized by RBI — within definition in Section 2(75) CGST Act.
PPI is money — it is NOT an actionable claim or securities.
Any instrument operating as a settlement system must be pre-paid.
Balance in wallet = stored currency → fiat currency loaded → IS a PPI.
PPIs cannot be issued by unregulated entities.
📝 A pre-paid wallet like Paytm wallet (when loaded with real money) is legal money in digital form. When you load ₹100 into it, ₹100 of real currency is deposited. The wallet balance is not 'points' or 'rewards' — it is actual money. Therefore it is NOT a supply, NOT taxable — it is just a form of money.
⚠️ NOT PPI
Wallet balance credited with value-to-use (not stored currency) is NOT a PPI.
Loyalty points credited into a digital wallet — unless wallet balance represents stored currency, it will NOT be a PPI (at least not to extent credited with value-to-use).
💡 Eg.673: Notification that reads "download mobile application and get ₹100 into your wallet" is NOT PPI since it is NOT stored currency balance. PPI balance in this wallet is ₹ NIL.
VOUCHER vs PPI vs MONEY — MUTUAL EXCLUSIVITY
PPIs are "instruments recognized by RBI" → are money for GST purposes (Section 2(75)).
Vouchers = "instruments with obligation to be accepted as consideration" → NOT money.
Money and vouchers are mutually exclusive — they cannot be the same thing.
26.2.5 & 26.2.6 — White Label PPI | PPI Distribution ⇧ Index
🏷️ White Label PPI & PPI Distribution
White Label PPI
Licensees under PSS Act offer PPI license to offer co-branded PPIs with appearance that every Merchant has their own PPI.
PSS Act does NOT prohibit such arrangements as long as there is ONE regulated entity.
📝 A merchant can brand a PPI with their own logo — but behind the scenes, a licensed PPI issuer is managing the actual currency. The merchant is just the face; the licensee carries the regulatory accountability.
PPI Distribution
RULE PPIs cannot be 'traded'. Regulated entities must remain accountable until redemption. At time of issue — PPIs are NOT 'activated'. Activation by KYC-OTP-Email verification at time of redemption. Intermediaries can procure and distribute 'inactive' PPIs.

Key features:
(a) Licensee collects face value of ₹1,000 — issues 'inactive' e-card
(b) Intermediary demands commission of ₹100 — 'cuts and pays' ₹900 to Licensee
📝 Distribution of PPIs is neither resale nor a transaction in money — the intermediary is providing a distribution service, not selling money. Commission earned by intermediary is taxable as service.
💡 Eg.674: ABC Ltd. issues payment of ₹10,000 to Bank requesting demand draft (DD). Bank issues DD for ₹9,800 (after deducting commission of ₹200). Two undeniable transactions have taken place although only one may be apparent.
💡 Eg.675: Cars (not registered with RTO) can be sold via dealers to Customers.
💡 Eg.676: LPG cylinders must be sold directly by licensed-refiners or licensed-bottlers even though delivery facilitated by dealerships (para 3, Sch. I CGST).
💡 Eg.677: SIM card can be resold via distributors and is NOT PPI.
IMPORTANT
Distribution of PPIs is NEITHER resales NOR transactions in money.
Decision in Premier Sales Promotion (P) Ltd. v. UoI (WP 5560/2022) respectfully needs reconsideration.
26.3 FEMA — Overview & 26.3.1 Current Account Transactions ⇧ Index
🌐 FEMA — Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999
Enabling legislation | Great extent of delegation through RBI Regulations & Master Directions
KEY REGULATIONS TO STUDY
(a) Foreign Exchange (Export of Goods and Services) Regulations
(b) Foreign Exchange (Import of Goods and Services) Regulations
Along with Master Directions issued by RBI from time to time.
FEMA — Two Types of Transactions [Section 2(e) & 2(j)]:
CAPITAL ACCOUNT TRANSACTION — Sec 2(e)
Transaction which alters assets or liabilities INCLUDING contingent liabilities outside India of persons resident in India OR assets or liabilities in India of persons resident outside India — includes transactions referred to in sub-section (3) of section 6.
📝 Capital account = changes your ownership of assets/liabilities abroad (or foreign entities' assets in India). FDI, ODI, external borrowings — all capital account.
CURRENT ACCOUNT TRANSACTION — Sec 2(j)
Transaction other than capital account transaction — includes:
(i) Payments due in connection with foreign trade, other current business, services, short-term banking and credit facilities in ordinary course of business
(ii) Payments due as interest on loans and as net income from investments
(iii) Remittances for living expenses of parents, spouse and children abroad
(iv) Expenses in connection with foreign travel, education and medical care of parents, spouse and children
📝 Current account = recurring payments for normal business activities. Export proceeds, import payments, service fees — all current account. These require Form A2 for outbound remittances.
FORM REQUIREMENTS — Outbound Remittances
Current account transactions with outbound remittance of foreign exchange → must comply with Form A2 (self-certificate + CA certificate — Form 15 CA/CB) regarding:
(i) Characterization of income
(ii) Applicability or inapplicability of withholding tax (TDS) under Section 195 of Income-tax Act

Import of goods in non-physical form (brand name, copyright, IP) → must be accompanied by Form A2 and CA certificate due to non-availability of bill of entry.
No outbound remittance will be passed through authorized dealer (bank) without either Form A1 or Form A2 and other accompanying documents as prescribed.
26.3.2 Netting Off | 26.3.3 Nostro & Vostro | 26.3.4 Guaranteed Repatriation | 26.3.5 Softex ⇧ Index
🔄 FEMA — Export & Repatriation Framework
SectionTopicKey RuleGST Impact
26.3.2 Netting Off Receivables Transactions requiring outbound + inbound FX remittance between SAME two persons involving import AND export of goods or services (NOT interchangeably of goods with services) — permitted to be 'net off' under supervision of authorized dealer (bank) per A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 08 dated 4 Dec. 2020. DANGER: Definition of 'export of services' u/s 2(6) IGST Act demands repatriation of FX — this condition is NOT 'subject to' such netting off provisions. Claim for export of services would DENY zero-rated benefits if netting off is adopted.
26.3.3 Nostro & Vostro Nostro (ours) = one bank's money held by another bank. Vostro (yours) = customer's money held by (and owed by) a bank. OPGSP (Online Payment Gateway Service Provider) permitted to collect FX and credit in Indian currency into beneficiary accounts — up to USD 10,000 (exports) and USD 2,000 (imports) per transaction per A.P. Circular 16, 24 Sep 2015. Eg.678: PayPal is an example of OPGSP + PI operating in India. Repatriation in Indian Rupees of proceeds = export of services where specific/general exception permitted by RBI (Sec 2(6)(iv) IGST + IGST Amendment Act, 2018 from 1 Feb 2019). Most updated RBI regulation must be referred. No assumption can be made about admissibility of 'export' benefits when remittances are via OPGSP/PI and funds credited in Indian currency to beneficiary account.
26.3.4 Guaranteed Repatriation Exporters of goods liable to action under FEMA for failure to repatriate export proceeds within time prescribed by RBI in Master Directions on Export of Goods and Services. At time of export — Customs (nodal agency) demands execution of 'GR Form' (PP for postal exports, Softex for software exports) — undertaking that stated currency will be repatriated via banking channels within stated timelines. Every export must be accompanied by GR/PP/Softex form and reconciled with each remittance on EDMPS system ('closure of export bills'). Zero-rated benefits for export of goods contingent on proper GR form execution and closure of export bills. Failure to repatriate = FEMA action + loss of GST zero-rating benefits.
26.3.5 Softex Softex certificate = ONLY in respect of offshore exports (exports made FROM India) — NOT onshore exports (exports by personnel stationed on foreign soil, Customer site outside India). Software exporter must be registered with Director, Software Technology Parks of India (STPI), Ministry of IT — whether or not registered as STP unit. For GST purposes, export of services of 'distinct person' limited to export for which Softex certification is allowed. 'Adjusted Total Turnover' (ATT) in formula for Rule 89 CGST Rules CANNOT include onshore export turnover. Eg.679: Export of software services from Bangalore unit = USD 100,000 (offshore — Softex certified). Onsite services from USA branch = USD 200,000 (onshore — no Softex). Total Legal Entity turnover = USD 300,000. But ATT for Rule 89 refund formula = limited to USD 100,000 of offshore services only. Critical impact on ITC refund calculation.
26.3.6–26.3.10 Merchanting Trade | Merchant Exports | Deemed Exports | GR Waiver | Non-exports ⇧ Index
🚢 Export-Related Transactions — Types & GST Treatment
TypeDefinition & MechanicsGST Treatment
26.3.6
Merchanting Trade		 Purchase from Japan + Sell to Germany (goods NOT entering India). RBI permits remittance to Supplier (Japan) only after collection of advance/guarantee from Customer (Germany). No Customs bill of entry for import leg + no shipping bill for export leg. Not the same as High Sea Sales (HSS): HSS = sale while goods are en route to India, between two buyers both in taxable territory. Eg.683: "Bill to London, ship to Delhi" = NOT export. Eg.684: "Bill to Delhi, ship to London" = IS export if Seller is Exporter in Shipping Bill. NOT liable to GST — supply NOT taking place within 'taxable territory'. But must be REPORTED in returns (included in turnover belonging to taxable person in India). Listed in Para 7, Schedule III CGST Act.
HSS: excluded from GST per Para 8, Schedule III CGST Act.
26.3.7
Merchant Exports		 Export of goods by a taxable person in India who has NO production capabilities — undertakes back-to-back export of goods procured from another taxable person in India. Exports by merchant tied to supporting manufacturer or trading-supplier. Merchant exporter has no value to add except uplift the price on sale to overseas Customer. Special GST dispensation vide Notification 41/2017-IGST(Rate) & 40/2017-CT(Rate) dated 23 Oct 2017 — merchant exporters permitted to procure supplies of GOODS ONLY (not services) on payment of 0.1% IGST or 0.5% each of CGST-SGST — subject to conditions listed.
26.3.8
Deemed Exports		 NOT actual exports but deemed to be exports for GST purposes by legal fiction authorized under Section 147 CGST Act. Notification No. 48/2017-CT dated 18 Oct 2017 issued — Suppliers permitted to supply at 0% GST to eligible Recipients subject to conditions in Circular 14/14/2017-GST dated 6 Nov 2017. Supply at 0% GST. Special dispensation applies ONLY to goods — NOT to services supplied as deemed exports.
26.3.9
GR Waiver		 Where there is clear commercial understanding that export proceeds will NOT be repatriated → exporter must secure 'letter of waiver' from authorized dealer (bank) in respect of GR Form BEFORE presentation of goods at port of export. Without GR waiver letter, Customs is NOT authorized to permit export of those goods. Eg.680: Samples sent to overseas (potential) Customers on non-returnable basis = GR waiver required.
Eg.681: Demo equipment sent on returnable basis = GR waiver required.
Eg.682: Tested sample residue sent to overseas Customer = GR waiver (export of testing services).
26.3.10
Non-exports		 Earning in foreign exchange is NOT ipsi dixit (not by itself) admitted as 'exports' (of goods or services). For FEMA: earning FX is necessary and often sufficient. For GST: earning FX must be accompanied by repatriation into India within prescribed time limits. Currency translation must occur IN INDIA (not outside India). FX must arrive through banking channels into India and be converted into Indian currency at time of credit to beneficiary's account. If current translation occurs outside India and Indian currency arrives into India → NOT admitted as repatriation in foreign exchange. Failure to properly repatriate FX = services NOT treated as 'export of services' → zero-rating benefits denied → GST payable on what were assumed to be zero-rated supplies.
📝 Ipsi dixit = by one's own assertion. Just because you earned USD/EUR doesn't make it an export in GST law — the money must physically arrive into India through banking channels.
26.3.11 Overseas Intermediaries | 26.3.12 FDI | 26.3.13 ODI ⇧ Index
🌍 Foreign Entity GST Implications — Overseas Structures
26.3.11 — Overseas Intermediaries
