Intermediaries facilitating trade between two other persons — location is important to determine GST treatment accurately.
Remittances from India to overseas intermediaries: neither any GST nor any withholding of taxes.
Overseas remittances to intermediaries must be examined for:
(i) Bona fides
(ii) Proportionality of remuneration to efforts
(iii) Proximity and association with Payer in India
Due to presumptions in PMLA — validation of outward remittances becomes significant.
📝 PMLA = Prevention of Money Laundering Act. Even legitimately structured overseas commissions must be validated to ensure they are not disguised profit repatriations attracting different tax consequences.
26.3.12 — FDI (Foreign Direct Investment)
DPIIT publishes FDI Policy. RBI issues Directions on flow of various forms of capital. Governing regulations apply depending on form of enterprise.
FED Master Direction No. 10/2015-16 (1 Jan 2016) — prescribes conditions for overseas Entities to set up in India as: (a) Liaison office (b) Branch office (c) Project office

Reference to FDI guidelines important in GST context to confirm whether enterprise in India (of overseas Entity) is a 'taxable person' — tested based on assertions made at time of entry into India under FDI guidelines to DPIIT and RBI.
Three Offices Comparison:
Liaison Office: information channel to Parent | NIL capital | Financial assistance from Parent = main income | No supplies → No GST
Branch Office: Trading and permitted services | Revenue from trading & services | Taxable supplies → GST applicable
Project Office: Execution of project awarded to Parent | Revenue from execution in India | Taxable supply as per project → GST applicable
26.3.13 — ODI (Overseas Direct Investment)
FEM (Overseas Investment) Regulations, 2022 — regulations for Indian entities setting up overseas presence as separate legal entity or extension (liaison/branch office).
Assertions made to RBI under ODI regulations affect tax position in respect of transactions outside India.
IGST Act Section 8 Explanations 1 & 2:
Expln.1: Establishment in India + another establishment outside India = treated as establishments of distinct persons.
Expln.2: Person carrying on business through branch or agency or representational office in any territory = treated as having establishment in that territory.
Read with export of services (Section 2(6) IGST): exports to overseas branch office will NOT be admitted to zero-rated benefits u/s 16(1)(a) IGST Act to the exporting distinct person in India.
📝 If an Indian company has a branch office abroad, supply from India to that branch is treated as supply between two 'distinct persons' — NOT as export of services. Zero-rating benefits denied. This is a significant trap for IT companies, consulting firms, and manufacturing exporters with overseas offices.
26.4.1–26.4.3 — Non-banking Branches | NPA | Digital Wallet ⇧ Index
🔍 Sectoral Insights — Banking Specific GST Issues
26.4.1 — Non-banking Branches
Branches of banks that do NOT offer banking services — being integral of the legal entity holding banking license. Nature of operations determines GST treatment applicable. Where option under Section 14(4) CGST Act is availed → applies to entire legal entity.

Activities of non-banking branches = 'deemed supply' under Section 7(1)(c) read with Para 2, Schedule I CGST Act.
📝 CBIC BFSI FAQ Dec 2018 Q.55: Management oversight/stewardship activities performed by Head Office to Branch in India = supply of services by Head Office even where no consideration charged and no expenditure in books of Branch. Supply between distinct entities = taxable supply even without consideration.
26.4.2 — NPA (Non-Performing Assets)
Loans not serviced (by paying interest and principal as due) = classified as 'non-performing assets'. Provision required in financials per prescribed norms (based on age since classification as NPA).

NPAs = basically doubtful receivables. Being 'beneficial interest in movable property not in possession or claim to a debit' → receivables in books = 'actionable claims'.
Sale of doubtful receivables = NOT liable to GST due to exclusion in Para 6, Schedule III CGST Act.

Companies purchasing NPAs take action against Debtors in the name of Creditors in accordance with applicable law.
📝 When a bank sells its bad loans (NPAs) to an ARC (Asset Reconstruction Company) — that is a sale of actionable claims. Schedule III Para 6 excludes actionable claims (other than lottery, gambling, betting) from GST. So no GST on NPA sale.
26.4.3 — Digital Wallet
Digital wallet = a PPI that operates remotely and away from bank account — NOT digital banking on website or mobile application.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) = channel to transact via mobile phone from registered bank account → NOT a digital wallet.
Pre-paid SIM (subscribed identification module) for mobile connection = NOT a PPI even when supplied in 'inactive' state.
Banks do NOT require PSS Act license but due to deep technology dependency — banks co-brand PPIs with fintech companies.
Review of T&Cs on website/mobile app reveals if PPIs are used by fintech under a 'rent-a-bank' model.
📝 UPI is like a remote control for your bank account — not a separate wallet. A digital wallet (like Paytm wallet) is separate from the bank account and holds loaded currency independently.
26.4.4 Redeemable Wallet | 26.4.5–7 Loyalty Points / Sale of Liability / Coupons | 26.4.8 Voucher v. Money ⇧ Index
💎 Payment Instruments — GST Classification Master Table
Three Types of Wallet Instruments — Comparison:
CriteriaStored Value (PPI)Value-to-useLoyalty Points
IssuerLicensee (Bank/NBFC)AnyMerchant
RedeemerMerchantMerchantMerchant
AdditionsFiat currency onlyTerms offeredTerms offered
DeletionsRedemptionRedemptionRedemption
ExpiryNOT allowedAllowedAllowed
Re-credit to bankAllowedNOT allowedNOT allowed
Status at issueInactiveActive-bearerActive-locked
RedemptionOnlineOnlineOffline
Resale of liabilityNOT allowedNOT allowedAllowed
AccountabilityLicenseeIssuerAny
26.4.4 — Redeemable Wallet:
Digital wallet bearing stored value into which value-to-use is intermixed, but segregation data is maintained.
Where balance represents stored value → IS a digital wallet (PPI).
Where balance ONLY represents value-to-use → NOT a digital wallet but only redeemable wallet = either (i) future discount or (ii) cross-cancelling supplies.

Redeemable wallet where different merchant is the redeemer — liability on redemption due to Merchant B is paid by Merchant A; debt due to Customer is admitted by Merchant A and settled by discharging Customer's dues to Merchant B on redemption spend.
26.4.5 — Loyalty Points | 26.4.6 — Sale of Liability | 26.4.7 — Redeemable Coupons:
LOYALTY POINTS — Key GST Features
(a) Merchant X concludes trade → pays GST on Supply A → credits Customer's account with loyalty points
(b) Customer enters into another trade → pays for Supply B with loyalty points (GST paid on Supply B as well)
(c) Amount payable to Customer (creditor) under Supply A settled with amount receivable from Customer (debtor) on Supply B

Underlying structure must be determined by unbundling: (i) contractual privity (ii) object of contract (iii) flow of consideration (iv) attendant GST treatment.
SALE OF LIABILITY — Key Features
Merchant X carries liability of ₹100 crores → Intermediary Y buys liability for ₹85 crores → Merchant X saves ₹15 crores → Intermediary Y runs redemption campaign (water bottles/airline tickets/hotel/vacation at 25% discount) → Intermediary Y saves ₹25 crores − ₹15 crores spent = nets ₹10 crores.
Merchants P, Q, R, S issue invoice to Intermediary Y (paying GST, claimed as ITC without Section 17(5) restrictions).
REDEEMABLE COUPONS — Contingent Liability
Redeemable coupons = contingent liability — uncertainty whether Customer will return. Since Merchant T is NOT licensed, redeemable coupons are NOT PPI under PSS Act.
Where Issuer is Redeemer → loyalty points program operates as 'future discount'.
Key feature: Trade for ₹1,000 → GST paid → Coupon issued for ₹200 → Customer returns for ₹2,000 trade → Merchant invoices ₹1,800 and pays GST on ₹1,800 (not ₹2,000).
26.4.8 — Voucher v. Money — Mutually Exclusive:
MONEY (PPI)
Recognized by RBI (Sec 2(75) CGST Act)
= Stored fiat currency
Time of supply event specified in Sec 12 or 13
Loaded at face value
Re-creditable to bank
No expiry permitted
IS a form of legal tender
VOUCHER
"Instrument with obligation to be accepted as consideration" (Sec 2(118) CGST)
= Value-to-use ONLY
Another time of supply event for goods and services separately
Issuer accepts obligation
Redeemer is agnostic to who pays fiat currency
NOT stored value or loaded currency
NOT legal tender
Premier Sales Promotion (P) Ltd. v. UOI (WP 5560/2022)
Held: 'vouchers' in GST are neither 'goods' nor 'services'.
Coupon given on making purchase at 'full price' = NOT 'money'. It is a claim to discount on future purchase.
Next purchase made 'net of coupon' price — not at 'full price'.
Although outcome (neither goods nor services) is satisfactory — 'what is it?' remains unanswered. With time of supply and valuation mechanism prescribed — Issuers, Intermediaries and Merchants must stand trial on these aspects.
💡 Eg.685: Loyalty points credited at Establishment A (petrol) — redeemed at any associate establishment. Next purchase at Establishment B = 'full price' where 'adjusted price' collected from Customer (claiming redemption of points) and 'adjustment of price' collected from Establishment A.
💡 Eg.686: Cash-back paid by bank for making purchase of petrol is NEITHER 'money' NOR 'loyalty' points. It IS consideration paid by bank for supply of (loyalty) services received from Customer.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 26 | Banking Laws ⇧ Index
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings on banking, fintech, FEMA, and payment instrument GST disputes.
Transaction in Money vs Taxable Banking Service — Cannot Assume Without Examining Contours
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.1 | Section 2(75) CGST Act | Schedule III
Key Principle
GST is not applicable to 'transactions in money' but currency translation, lending, credit and other activities are taxable and selective exempt. One cannot assume that an arrangement involves a transaction in money without knowing exactly how it fits within the contours. GST exclusion available for transactions in money and exemption available in respect of interest on deposits, loans or advances cannot be routinely applied.
When to Use — When assessee claims that their banking/NBFC activity is a 'transaction in money' and hence not subject to GST. Counter: the mere fact that money is involved does not automatically make it a 'transaction in money' excluded from GST — the specific arrangement must be examined against the statutory definition. Also use to challenge blanket interest exemption claims by NBFCs without examining the specific nature of their lending activity.
PPI = Stored Currency | Money and Vouchers are Mutually Exclusive
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.2.4 & 26.4.8 | Section 2(75) & 2(118) CGST Act | PSS Act
Key Principle
PPI as a settlement is actually legal money of fiat currency but with the convenience of carrying and usage. PPIs fit within the definition in Section 2(75) being "instrument recognized by RBI" — it is not an actionable claim or securities. Since vouchers are "instruments with obligation to be accepted as consideration", vouchers cannot be both money and vouchers. Money and vouchers are mutually exclusive. Balance in wallet that represents stored currency is a PPI; balance that only represents value-to-use is not a digital wallet but only a redeemable wallet — either future discount or cross-cancelling supplies.
When to Use — When Department taxes PPI loading as a supply or argues that PPI commission is a 'transaction in securities'. Counter: PPI is money (Section 2(75)) — not securities. Also use to distinguish digital wallets (stored currency = money) from value-to-use wallets (redeemable wallet = future discount or cross-cancelling supply — different GST treatment).
Fintech — Interest Exemption Cannot Be Availed by Intermediary | Privity Determines GST Treatment
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.2.3 | RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2 Sept 2022)
Key Principle
It is not possible to assume and admit lending service provider (intermediary) to be the regulated enterprise (Bank or NBFC) or vice versa. Interest exemption in GST cannot be availed by intermediary nor can NBFC earn each commission from new business referral. Description of business activities by these actors cannot be relied upon as admission of facts contrary to the regulations that permit or restrict overenthusiastic revenue recognition. Ostensible legal authority to enter into contracts is determinative of kinds of revenue each is entitled to assert or collect.
When to Use — When a fintech platform claims interest exemption for itself or when it claims it is 'lending' (not providing intermediary services). Counter: fintech is not the regulated lender — it cannot inherit the interest exemption of the Bank/NBFC. Each party's revenue stream must be determined by their legal authority to contract. Also use when fintech's cash-back, discount, or commission arrangements obscure the underlying taxable supply.
Netting Off Receivables — Denies Zero-Rating for Export of Services
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.3.2 | Section 2(6) IGST Act | A.P. (DIR Series) Circular No. 08 dated 4 Dec 2020
Key Principle
Definition of 'export of services' in Section 2(6) of Integrated GST Act demands repatriation of foreign exchange and this condition is not 'subject to' such netting off provisions under FEMA Regulations. As such, claim for export of services would deny zero-rated benefits if netting off is adopted.
When to Use — When a company exports services AND imports services from the same counterparty overseas, and nets off the receivable against the payable instead of actually remitting/receiving the foreign exchange through banking channels. Counter: netting off is permitted under FEMA but it defeats the GST repatriation condition — the export of services ceases to be zero-rated. This is a significant trap for IT companies, software exporters, and trading companies with mirror transactions with overseas group entities.
Softex — ATT for Rule 89 Excludes Onshore Export Turnover
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.3.5 | Rule 89 CGST Rules | Section 2(6) IGST Act
Key Principle
For GST purposes, export of services of 'distinct person' will be limited to export of services for which Softex certification is allowed. And 'adjusted total turnover' (ATT) in the formula for purposes of Rule 89 of Central GST Rules cannot include onshore export turnover. This is an important implication that is exposed via Softex verification. Export of software services from unit in India is offshore services. Export of onsite services from branch established abroad is onshore services. Softex certification only covers offshore services.
When to Use — When an IT company claims ITC refund under Rule 89 for zero-rated exports and includes onshore services (provided by India-based employees deputed to client site abroad) in the ATT denominator. Counter: ATT cannot include onshore export turnover — only Softex-certified offshore exports qualify. This reduces the refund entitlement and any excess ITC refund claimed must be reversed.
ODI / Distinct Person — Exports to Overseas Branch NOT Zero-Rated
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.3.13 | Section 8 Explanation 1 & 2 IGST Act | Section 2(6) IGST Act
Key Principle
Read together with the definition of 'export of services' in Section 2(6) of Integrated GST Act, exports from Indian entity to overseas branch office will not be admitted to zero-rated benefits under Section 16(1)(a) of Integrated GST Act to the exporting distinct person in India. Section 8 Explanation 1: person having an establishment in India and any other establishment outside India — such establishments shall be treated as establishments of distinct persons. Explanation 2: A person carrying on a business through a branch or an agency or a representational office in any territory shall be treated as having an establishment in that territory.
When to Use — When an Indian company supplying services to its own overseas branch/liaison office/project office claims zero-rating (Section 16(1)(a) IGST). Counter: the overseas establishment is a 'distinct person' under Section 8 Explanation 1 — supply between distinct persons is not 'export of services' (Section 2(6)(v) excludes supplies between distinct persons). Zero-rating not available — supply is treated as domestic supply between distinct persons and GST is payable.
Chit Fund vs Prize Scheme — Factual Examination Required Before Applying Rule 31A
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.2.2 | Rule 31A Central GST Rules | Chit Funds Act, 1982
Key Principle
Care must be taken to uncover the 'facts' in each such plan and not assume every plan operates like a chit fund plan and subject it to GST treatment on 'net income' of organizer and disparage the policy wisdom in Rule 31A of Central GST Rules. The key distinction is that in chit funds, contribution is a 'repayable deposit' and collections are held 'in trust' and 'on account of' contributors, with contractual privity inter se contributors — whereas in prize schemes, collections held in trust are applied by the organizer.
When to Use — When Revenue treats any savings-cum-prize scheme as a chit fund and attempts to apply Rule 31A net income computation to it. Counter: the specific scheme must satisfy all the characteristics of a chit fund (repayable deposit, in-trust collections, inter se privity) — not just superficially resemble one. Also use when assessee tries to claim chit fund treatment for a prize scheme to reduce their GST base.
NPA Sale — Excluded from GST as Actionable Claim | Schedule III Para 6
Legal Principle | Chapter 26.4.2 | Para 6, Schedule III CGST Act
Key Principle
NPAs are basically doubtful receivables. Being 'beneficial interest in movable property not in possession or claim to a debit', receivables in the books are 'actionable claims'. Sale of doubtful receivables is not liable to GST due to exclusion in paragraph 6, schedule III to Central GST Act.
When to Use — When Department attempts to levy GST on the sale of NPAs (bad loans) from a bank to an Asset Reconstruction Company (ARC). Counter: NPAs are actionable claims. Sale of actionable claims (other than lottery, gambling, betting) is excluded from GST under Schedule III Para 6. Also use when bank restructures loans or assigns receivables to third parties — the transfer of receivables/actionable claims is not a supply liable to GST.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📖 Ch 28 — Criminal Laws

Chapter 28 — Criminal Laws

Evidence Law · Criminal Procedure · Jurisprudence | Pages 463–487 | Source: Allied Laws — GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

🗺️ Legend
⬛ Root Node
🔵 Level 1 Branch
✅ Permitted / Taxpayer Protection
❌ Prohibited / Revenue Overreach
⚠️ Condition / Test / Warning
🟣 Optional / Presumption Route
ℹ️ Neutral Reference / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 Index — Chapter 28 | Criminal Laws | Evidence & Procedure
28.1 Relevance to GST — Four Instructions
28.2.1 Fact — Definition & Proof
28.2.2 Assumption About Fact
28.2.3 Adverse Inference — GST Self-Assessment Shield
28.2.4 Undisputed Facts
28.2.5 Presumption of Innocence
28.2.6 Presumption of Non-Compliance
28.2.7 Judicial Notice
28.2.8 Burden of Proof
28.2.9 Onus of Proof — Burden vs Onus
28.2.10 Burden of Exception
28.2.11 Special Knowledge
28.2.12 Bona Fides of Executive Action
28.2.13 Two Incorrect Interpretations
28.2.14 Statement Before Police Officer
28.3.1–28.3.3 Prosecution · Arrest · Trial
28.3.4–28.3.6 Admission · Curative Power · Disclosure
28.4.1 Self-Incrimination
28.4.2 Death of Taxpayer
28.4.3 Key Authorities
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
28.1 — Relevance to GST | Four CBIC Instructions
⬆ Index
CRIMINAL LAWS — RELEVANCE TO GST
Search, seizure, arrest, prosecution, summons — Section 134 CGST: tried by Judicial Magistrate of First Class
GST Officers appraised about applicable jurisprudence by Government to prevent misapplication through four CBIC Instructions:
(a) Instruction 1/2020-21
dated 2 Feb 2021
Inspection
(b) Instruction 1/2022-23
dated 25 May 2022
Search
(c) Instruction 2/2022-23
dated 17 Aug 2022
Arrest
(d) Instruction 3/2022-23
dated 17 Aug 2022
Summons
📝 These four CBIC instructions are your first line of defence in any search, arrest or summons proceeding. If Revenue deviates from these instructions — the proceeding itself can be challenged as without authority and vitiated by non-compliance with CBIC's own guidelines.
28.2.1 — Fact | Definition & What Must Be Proved
⬆ Index
FACT — Section 3 Indian Evidence Act
Fact is NOT understanding — it is belief in existence/non-existence supported by evidence
SECTION 3 — FACT MEANS AND INCLUDES
(1) Anything, state of things, or relation of things, capable of being perceived by the senses
(2) Any mental condition of which any person is conscious
Illustrations: (a) Objects arranged in certain order in certain place = fact (b) That a man heard/saw something = fact (c) That a man said certain words = fact (d) That a man holds certain opinion, intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently = fact (e) That a man has a certain reputation = fact
FACT IS NOT SIMPLE
Facts not easy to prove. More complex the thing — more substantive must be the evidence.
Facts left undisputed do not require further proof.
'Dispute' as to alleged fact is essential to bring burden on person making the assertion to establish its truth.
📝 If Revenue makes an allegation — taxpayer's FIRST move is to DISPUTE it (simply plead: 'Denied'). This puts the burden of proof squarely back on Revenue. Taxpayer need not explain — just dispute.
FACTS IN ISSUE
"Any fact from which — either by itself or in connection with other facts — the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability or disability asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding necessarily follows."

RELEVANT FACT = One fact connected with another in the ways referred to in provisions of the Act relating to relevancy of facts.
📝 'Facts in issue' are the battleground in any GST proceeding. Revenue must establish the EXISTENCE of each taxing ingredient as a 'fact in issue' — not merely assert that tax is payable. The assertion without proof = no fact in issue = demand fails.
28.2.2 — Assumption About Fact
⬆ Index
ASSUMPTION ABOUT FACT
Fact may be obvious — but it CANNOT be assumed. Interpretation is NOT fact — it is opinion.
CANNOT BE ASSUMED
Even obvious facts CANNOT be assumed. Cannot proceed on assumption about existence or non-existence of things or state of things.
GST is NOT a game of chance that counter-Party may lack skill to contest errors of facts.
📝 Just because Revenue thinks something is 'obvious' — they still must PROVE it. Revenue cannot say 'it's obvious that supply was made' and then demand GST. The supply must be established as a fact through evidence — not assumed.
INTERPRETATION ≠ FACT
Interpretation is opinion — not fact. There is NO compulsion that only ONE interpretation must exist — both can be above obvious standards of reasonableness.
Interpretation adopted by taxpayer would be implausible rarely — Courts support many interpretations.
Interpretation is application of law to facts — not a fact itself.
📝 Revenue often says 'the correct interpretation is X — so the fact is X.' But interpretation is OPINION, not fact. There can be multiple valid interpretations. Unless taxpayer's interpretation is IMPOSSIBLE — it remains valid. Revenue must show taxpayer's interpretation has NO judicial support at all.
SECTIONS 75(7) AND 107(11)
By denying curative power to adjudication and appellate authorities — GST has created occasion for either side to derive advantages from factual errors left uncontested.
Bulwark = pleading of error at any late stage of proceedings.
📝 In GST, there is NO curative power like Section 222 CrPC. But taxpayer can raise a ground at any late stage — even in appeal. So errors in SCN that went uncontested can still be raised in appeal proceedings — they are not waived simply by silence at SCN reply stage.
28.2.3 — Adverse Inference | GST Self-Assessment Shield
⬆ Index
ADVERSE INFERENCE — TAXPAYER'S SILENCE
No adverse inference flows from taxpayer's silence when tax position is challenged
SELF-ASSESSMENT DISCLOSURE IS SUFFICIENT
Duty of disclosure sufficiently discharged when ALL applicable returns are filed on Common Portal:
(a) Table 4 GSTR-9 = admission of taxability including debit notes / credit notes
(b) Table 5 GSTR-9 = claim of exemption or non-taxability
(c) ECrL movement = Tables 6–8 & 13 GSTR-9
(d) Table 9 GSTR-9C = rate-wise breakdown of turnover
(e) Table 12 GSTR-9C = alignment of books with ECrL
(f) Table 5 GSTR-9C = valuation adjustments, deemed supplies
(g) Financial statements appended to GSTR-9C = disclosure complete
📝 Once all returns are filed — taxpayer has discharged disclosure duty. Revenue is FREE to examine and derive interpretation. Revenue CANNOT demand taxpayer to explain their interpretation or justify their tax position. That duty is Revenue's — not taxpayer's.
REVENUE CANNOT — ADVERSE INFERENCE BARRED
Revenue CANNOT make 'adverse inference' from taxpayer's silence after returns filed and contemporaneous books available.
To outsource inquiry to taxpayer raises questions about validity of conclusions reached.
Adversely affects reliability of evidence introduced by taxpayer and relied upon by Revenue without corroboration.
Supply CANNOT be inferred or presumed — must be shown to exist.
Eg 704: ADT-2 issued "since no justification with documentation furnished about other income" — contrary to law. Omission to offer 'satisfactory' explanation is NOT sufficient to support demand.
💡 GST Hearing Key Argument: "The demand is based on adverse inference from omission to explain 'other income' appearing in financial statements. This is contrary to established law. Supply cannot be inferred or presumed. Revenue must establish supply by independent evidence — not by demanding taxpayer to self-incriminate through explanation."
4 INGREDIENTS FOR MALA FIDES — ALL FOUR MUST EXIST
To impute mala fides in taxpayer's omission to maintain/produce information (excluding non-production of maintained books):
(a) Non-payment of some tax (or claim of credit/refund)
(b) Knowledge about said tax (or inadmissibility)
(c) Use of device to render detection impossible or cumbersome
(d) Gains from this misadventure shown to arise to taxpayer
Jus in rem — Revenue duty not to allow single taxpayer getaway with unjust advantage
📝 ALL four ingredients must co-exist to impute mala fides. Revenue must specifically allege and prove each. Missing even ONE ingredient = no mala fides = no Section 74 jurisdiction = Section 73 applies (shorter limitation + lower penalty). This is the most critical threshold in every GST evasion allegation.
ACTIVE CONCEALMENT ≠ PASSIVE SILENCE
Deficiencies in record-keeping does NOT ipsi dixit imply fraud or suppression.
Deliberate withholding of facts = not permissible. But investigation cannot be outsourced to taxpayer by Revenue — that would be abdication of duty of State.
Uberrima fidei (utmost good faith) or duty to disclose does NOT apply once litigation commences.
📝 Active concealment (hiding books, destroying records) = punishable. Passive silence (not volunteering explanations beyond what returns show) = fully protected right. The two are legally distinct. Revenue must show ACTIVE steps to hide — not mere failure to explain.
28.2.4 — Undisputed Facts | Evidence Act Principles in GST
⬆ Index
UNDISPUTED FACTS — SECTION 58
Facts is NOT synonymous with truth — Evidence Act principles are equitable principles of Common Law applicable to taxation
CHUHARMAL MOHANANI v. CIT AIR 1988 SC 1384 + Bombay HC in J.S. Parkar v. V.B. Palekar 94 ITR 616
While rigours of Evidence Act not applicable to tax proceedings — principles of Evidence Act being equitable principles of Common Law ARE applicable to taxation. Section 110 of Evidence Act embodies a salutary principle of common law jurisprudence which could be attracted to a set of circumstances.
📝 This SC judgment is the gateway for importing Evidence Act principles into GST proceedings. Even though formally Evidence Act does not apply to GST — its PRINCIPLES (burden of proof, presumption of innocence, adverse inference rules etc.) are equitable principles that apply universally to ALL quasi-judicial proceedings.
SECTION 58 — ADMITTED FACTS NEED NOT BE PROVED
"No fact need to be proved in any proceeding which the parties thereto or their agents agree to admit at the hearing, or which before the hearing they agree to admit by any writing, or which by any rule of pleading in force at the time they are deemed to have admitted by their pleadings."

Each person (taxpayer or Revenue) free to guide outcome by passive inaction — not necessarily by positive/affirmative action. No burden of overenthusiasm imposed on taxpayer to guide Revenue from misleading the course of proceedings.
💡 GST Application: If Revenue's SCN contains erroneous factual allegation — taxpayer need not correct it proactively. Simply plead denial. Revenue must prove the allegation. Omission to appeal erroneous demand does NOT imply acceptance of Revenue's interpretation — Section 120(3) CGST Act is explicit on this. The same taxpayer is not precluded from agitating same demand in a subsequent tax period.
28.2.5 — Presumption of Innocence | Self-Assessment & Bona Fides
⬆ Index
PRESUMPTION OF INNOCENCE
Self-assessment takes within itself presumption about bona fides of taxpayer's interpretation
SECTION 4 — THREE LEVELS OF PRESUMPTION
'May presume' = Court may regard fact as proved unless and until disproved — or call for proof
'Shall presume' = Court shall regard fact as proved unless and until disproved (rebuttable presumption)
'Conclusive proof' = When one fact declared conclusive proof of another — Court shall on proof of one regard the other as proved — shall NOT allow evidence to disprove it
BONA FIDE SELF-ASSESSMENT = PRESUMPTION OF INNOCENCE
Self-assessment takes within itself presumption of bona fides. When bona fide view of law entertained in self-assessment:
— Imputation of mala fides CANNOT be lightly made
— Assumption of guilt is NOWHERE within reckoning
When interpretation accepted by Courts but overturned by later Superior Court decision = demand under Section 73 ONLY (no Section 74)
No allegation of special circumstances can be alleged for Section 74
📝 If taxpayer took a position that was supported by some Court decision (even a lower court) — that is conclusive evidence of bona fide interpretation. Section 74 (fraud/suppression) cannot be invoked. Only Section 73 applies — shorter limitation, lower penalty. This is your shield in almost every major GST demand.
EVEN PRESUMPTION CANNOT OPERATE AS ASSUMPTION
Even when statute expressly provides for a presumption — not even such a presumption can operate as an assumption.
Section 135 CGST: Court compelled to presume mens rea exists — but this remains REBUTTABLE presumption — not assumption of guilt.
Revenue cannot treat presumption of non-compliance as equivalent to proved non-compliance.
📝 Even where GST law says 'presume' — the accused/taxpayer can still REBUT it. Presumption merely shifts the evidentiary burden for that particular ingredient. Once taxpayer provides credible explanation — the presumption falls. It is NOT a conclusive finding of guilt.
28.2.6 — Presumption of Non-Compliance | Shifting of Onus
⬆ Index
PRESUMPTION OF NON-COMPLIANCE
Anti-evasion provisions do NOT superimpose presumption of non-compliance — guilt or wrongdoing
ONLY REBUTTABLE PRESUMPTION
Presumption of non-compliance = only rebuttable presumption. Where offence requires animus — Section 135 CGST compels Court to presume mens rea. But this remains rebuttable — not converts into assumption.
Notice based on 'appearance of wrongdoing' must NOT cause taxpayer to rush to explain without first DISPUTING the allegation.
📝 When a demand notice arrives — the correct response is first to DISPUTE each allegation. By rushing to explain without disputing — taxpayer shifts the onus onto themselves. Once allegation is disputed — Revenue must prove it. Then taxpayer can explain.
CONTRADICTORY ASSERTIONS = PRESUMPTION AGAINST TAXPAYER
Where two related assertions are contradictory → presumption runs in favour of Revenue.
Where assertions on same fact-in-issue made before another regulator → any contradiction before GST authorities stands impeached by presumption in favour of Revenue.
Eg 705: Output tax discharged via DRC-3 raises presumption that interest is payable on underlying demand.
Eg 706: Debit note issued in 2022-23 with reference to tax invoice of 2019-20 raises presumption of interest payable on gross value for the interval.
Eg 707: Non-repatriation of export of services proceeds raises presumption of output tax on inter-State supplies.
💡 Practical Application: Whatever position you take before Income Tax / ROC / FEMA / Customs — must be consistent with GST position. A discrepancy between FEMA declaration and GST return on export proceeds will be used by Revenue to raise presumption against you.
28.2.7 — Judicial Notice | Section 56–57
⬆ Index
JUDICIAL NOTICE — SECTION 56
Facts judicially noticeable need not be proved — can be taken at any time during adjudication or appeal
13 CATEGORIES OF FACTS WHERE JUDICIAL NOTICE MUST BE TAKEN (Section 57)
(1) All laws in force in India (2) All public Acts passed by Parliament of UK (3) Articles of War for Indian Army/Navy/Air Force (4) Course of proceedings of Parliament/State Legislatures (5) Accession and sign manual of Sovereign of UK (6) All seals of English/Indian Courts, Courts of India, Notaries Public (7) Accession to office, names, titles, functions of persons filling public office — if notified in Official Gazette (8) Existence, title and national flag of every State or Sovereign recognized by GOI (9) Divisions of time, geographical divisions of world, public festivals, fasts and holidays notified in Official Gazette (10) Territories under dominion of GOI (11) Commencement, continuance and termination of hostilities between GOI and any State or body of persons (12) Names of members/officers of Court and all advocates, attorneys, proctors, vakils, pleaders (13) Rule of road on land or at sea
📝 Category 7 is most important for GST: If an officer's appointment is notified in the Official Gazette — the adjudicating authority MUST take judicial notice that such officer has the authority and jurisdiction. This also means if the appointment is NOT notified — the authority can be challenged. No separate proof needed.
KEY GST APPLICATION
No requirement that judicial notice must be taken in the NOTICE for it to be taken into consideration in adjudication or appellate proceedings. Such notice can be taken DURING adjudication or appeal. State of affairs being facts-in-issue can be taken cognizance of at any time — and assertions made before other regulators become evidence of HIGH QUALITY to impact treatment applicable in GST.
28.2.8 — Burden of Proof | Section 101
⬆ Index
BURDEN OF PROOF — SECTION 101
"Burden rests on the person who makes any assertion" — Static, does not shift
Narayan Bhagwantrao Gosavi Balajiwale v. Gopal Vinayak Gosavi 1960 AIR SC 100
Burden of proof becomes academic when both Parties introduce evidence. Counter-Party must ALLOW burden of proof to be discharged by Party enjoined with this burden BEFORE introducing rebuttal and its own evidence.
📝 In GST proceedings: Revenue makes allegation (bears burden). Revenue should present ALL evidence FIRST. Then taxpayer replies. Taxpayer must NOT jump to explain or rebut until Revenue has actually discharged its burden of establishing the allegation prima facie. Many taxpayers make the mistake of explaining before Revenue has established anything.
DEGREE OF PROOF ON REVENUE
Must establish allegations plausible (NOT merely possible). Prima facie to establish allegations plausible — Not merely possible.
Plausible = in the given set of facts — the outcome alleged is the ONLY explanation available.
Possible = theoretically possible (insufficient for Revenue's burden)
📝 'Possible' and 'plausible' are legally distinct. Revenue must show that evasion is PLAUSIBLE — meaning it's the ONLY logical conclusion from the facts. If taxpayer can show an innocent explanation is equally plausible — Revenue's case fails at burden stage.
TWO GST EXAMPLES
Eg 708: When taxpayer explains GSTR-3B turnover unreported = zero-rated supplies → taxpayer LIABLE to produce evidence to substantiate compliance with definition of 'export of services.' (Taxpayer claimed exception — bears burden to prove it)
Eg 709: When taxpayer disputes correctness of valuation of inter-branch supplies and takes support of Rule 28 of CGST Rules → Revenue's allegation that (i) deemed supplies omitted and (ii) such deemed supplies are taxable = would NOT require any proof (because by invoking Rule 28, taxpayer has admitted deemed supply exists)
💡 Eg 709 warning: If taxpayer invokes Rule 28 to justify valuation — taxpayer has implicitly admitted deemed supply exists. Revenue then doesn't need to prove supply existed — it's admitted! Only the quantum is disputed. Taxpayer must be careful not to inadvertently shift burden through its own pleadings.
28.2.9 — Onus of Proof | Burden vs Onus | Section 102
⬆ Index
BURDEN vs ONUS — CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Ei qui affirmat, non ei qui negat, incumbit probatio — He who invokes the aid of law should be first to provide his claim
FeatureBURDEN (Section 101)ONUS (Section 102)
Substantive provisionSection 101Section 102
ShiftingSTATIC — does not shift as burden is imposed by lawDYNAMIC — shifts back and forth as onus linked to material introduced by either Party
Degree of proof requiredPrima facie to establish allegations plausible (not merely possible)Substantial and satisfactory
Additional proof requiredRequired if allegation is disputedRequired in rebuttal if allegation is undisputed or admitted
ACCUSED/TAXPAYER'S DUTY
Must ONLY plead their position — accept or reject (the allegation) — and thereafter ALLOW Revenue to bring home the allegations with a mountain of evidence to support the allegation.
Allegations need only to be 'REJECTED' to satisfy the defence needed to be advanced by accused.
To do more would cause shifting of onus and tantamount to satisfaction of burden of accuser.
There is NO duty 'to prove innocence' on the accused.
📝 This is the single most important principle for GST litigants: SAY ONLY 'DENIED.' Then let Revenue prove. Once Revenue presents evidence — THEN you rebut. Do NOT try to disprove allegations before Revenue has even established them. That would shift onus from Revenue to you — which is exactly what Revenue wants.
Eg 710: GSTR-1 > GSTR-3B asserts output tax NOT discharged → raises presumption in Revenue's favour under Section 79 → but taxpayer can explain by rectifying under Rule 88C
Eg 711: GSTR-2A < GSTR-3B raises NO presumption that taxpayer must disprove. But taxpayer's assertion of bona fides of inward supplies implies acceptance of non-compliance with Sec 16(2)(c) to extent of mismatch — be careful what you admit!
28.2.10 — Burden of Exception | Section 103
⬆ Index
BURDEN OF EXCEPTION — SECTION 103
Claiming Schedule III exclusion or Section 11 exemption — burden falls on TAXPAYER
THE RULE
Section 103 requires that applicability of exclusion falls on one CLAIMING the exclusion — because it admits that treatment prescribed is to follow applicability of exclusion cannot be satisfied.
Assertion that transaction falls within any of the exceptions = exclusion in Schedule III or exemption under Section 11 — falls on TAXPAYER — because claim of exception is admission that Section 9 CGST Act stands attracted.
📝 The moment taxpayer says 'my supply is exempt' or 'my transaction falls in Schedule III' — taxpayer has admitted that GST would otherwise apply. Now taxpayer must PROVE the exemption/exclusion. Revenue's burden to show supply exists is discharged by taxpayer's own admission of supply — only the exception remains to be proved by taxpayer.
Eg 712: Considering received from sale of land eligible for exclusion under Para 5, Schedule III — burden on taxpayer to produce:
(i) Written document of sale of land
(ii) Document conveying absolute title (not lesser)
(iii) Document duly executed and registered
WITHOUT this — presumption runs in favour of Revenue to DENY exclusion from tax.
💡 Practical: When claiming Schedule III Para 5 exclusion for land sale — produce registered Sale Deed showing absolute title conveyance. Mere Agreement to Sell will NOT satisfy burden. If Sale Deed is not produced — Revenue can deny exclusion and demand GST on full consideration.
Eg 713: Other income in financial statements raises NO presumption — financial statements are document of book-keeping and accounting disclosure (unlike GSTR-1 which IS primary record of self-assessment in GST).
Eg 714: Turnover claimed exempt under entry 74 Notification 12/2017-CT(Rate) — requires taxpayer to produce:
(i) Contractual privity between Patient and taxpayer
(ii) Object of contract = healthcare services
(iii) Taxpayer licensed as medical practitioner/paramedic OR location registered as clinical establishment
28.2.11 — Special Knowledge | Section 106
⬆ Index
SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE — SECTION 106
If information that can establish a fact remains with any person — burden to prove its existence/non-existence rests on HOLDER
Regardless of who the burden rests on — if the information (that can establish a fact or fact-in-issue) remains with any person, then burden to prove existence or non-existence rests on the HOLDER of that information. Section 106 places this burden to deny any advantage by denial of information that cannot otherwise be obtained by counter-Party.
📝 This cuts BOTH ways. If taxpayer has information about their own business that Revenue cannot access — taxpayer bears burden to disclose it (if claiming exception/exemption). But equally — if Revenue has search/seizure material that taxpayer cannot access — Revenue bears burden to disclose it in adjudication proceedings.
Eg 715: Taxpayer explains turnover in Table 5F GSTR-9 = 'pure agency' reimbursements → burden on taxpayer since proof of this assertion is within taxpayer's SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE and CANNOT be proved by Revenue
Eg 716: Credit notes issued = suo motu sanction of refund by taxpayer → burden on taxpayer to demonstrate every credit note is compliant with Section 34 CGST Act
Eg 718: Omission to explain interpretation of Section 16(4) does NOT cast burden on taxpayer — once details of date of filing GSTR-3B returns are evident on Common Portal = undisputed
28.2.12 — Bona Fides of Executive Action | Section 114
⬆ Index
BONA FIDES OF EXECUTIVE ACTION
Presumption in favour of Government action — but Court CAN examine if reasons have rational connection to purpose
SECTION 114 — COURT MAY PRESUME
Court may presume existence of any fact which it thinks likely to have happened — regard being had to the common course of natural events, human conduct and public and private business, in their relation to facts of the particular case.
Illustration (e): That judicial and official acts have been regularly performed
Illustration (g): That evidence which could be and is NOT produced would, if produced, be unfavourable to person who withholds it
Illustration (h): If man refuses to answer a question he is not compelled to answer — answer if given would be unfavourable to him
📝 Section 114(e) — presumption that official acts are regularly performed — is a double-edged sword. Revenue invokes it to say 'our search/audit was valid — presume we followed procedure.' But Sec 114(g) cuts back: if Revenue has evidence but does NOT produce it in adjudication — presume it would have been unfavourable to Revenue!
CIT v. SCINDIA STEAM NAVIGATION — Mandamus
Unless 'demand for justice' was made to authority concerned and DENIED — no writ of mandamus lies. Taxpayer must not proceed on assumption that injustice will be done unless it can be shown to satisfaction of Court that injustice is imminent and further delay will cause injustice, restitution will be impossible and approaching Court would be of no effect.
📝 Before filing a Writ Petition against a GST demand — taxpayer must first 'make demand for justice' to the concerned authority (file appeal, exhaust remedies). Writ petition without exhausting GST appellate remedies = premature. Court may not entertain it.
VEXATIOUS SEARCH
There is NO provision in GST law for 'vexatious search.' In fact — there is a PRESUMPTION that searches conducted were bona fide (Section 114(e) Evidence law).
This brings sharper focus to requirement to DEMAND JUSTICE — especially when exceptional powers are sought to be invoked.
Eg 719: Inspection under Section 67(1) demanding (i) financial statements (ii) GSTR-2A v. GSTR-3B and GSTR-1 v. GSTR-3B reconciliation (iii) input credit register — bears all indicia of 'roving enquiry' and NOT 'inquiry into evasion.'
💡 GST Hearing: When search/inspection is clearly a fishing expedition — cite Eg 719 pattern: demand for GSTR reconciliation + financial statements during Section 67(1) inspection = roving enquiry = not authorised under Section 67(1). Bona fides of such inspection can be challenged.
28.2.13 — Two Incorrect Interpretations | Section 75(7) Safeguard
⬆ Index
TWO INCORRECT INTERPRETATIONS
Both taxpayer AND Revenue can be wrong — due process tests viability of Revenue's interpretation, not truth discovery
Interpretation adopted by taxpayer AND that canvassed by Revenue CAN BOTH be incorrect interpretation of law. Due process in GST is to challenge taxpayer's interpretation and put notice 'on trial' the alternate interpretation canvassed by Revenue. Not for any adjudication/appellate forum to accept either — but to determine if the one agitated is sustainable in law and on facts, or not.

Due process of law is NOT discovery of the truth. It is to test the viability of the alternate interpretation canvassed challenging the self-assessment.
📝 This means: Even if taxpayer's interpretation is wrong — Revenue must PROVE its own interpretation is CORRECT. If Revenue's interpretation is also not sustainable — the demand cannot be confirmed even if taxpayer's position was erroneous. Confirmation of demand requires Revenue's interpretation to be RIGHT — not just taxpayer's to be WRONG.
SECTION 75(7) SAFEGUARD
Demand for TAX must show ALL taxing ingredients needed to attract incidence. It does NOT suffice that sweeping statements are made as if that would be sufficient to support the demand.
Demand CANNOT be confirmed under WRONG HSN/classification — Section 75(7) bars it.
Eg 722: GST demanded at 18% on 'other income' = failure of demand — cannot be saved by omission to show taxing ingredients needed to attract incidence
Eg 723: SCN demanding payment of output tax under HSN 8704 — cannot be confirmed in adjudication under HSN 9971. Falls foul of Section 75(7).
💡 Section 75(7) is one of the most powerful weapons. If SCN demands GST at 18% as 'service' and later Revenue tries to confirm it as 'goods at 12%' — Section 75(7) bars this. Demand must be confirmed on the SAME ground and rate as raised in SCN. Revenue gets ONE shot — cannot change classification in adjudication.
CORRECT TAX MUST BE PAID — 'ANY TAX' WON'T DO
Payment of 'any' tax does NOT satisfy GST liability. Sections 77 CGST + 19 IGST makes this explicit. Correct tax must be paid.
Demand for output tax cannot be made on PRESUMPTION even if tax payable has in fact not been paid.
Eg 720: Taxpayer discharging GST at 18% (goods) cannot be excused when correct answer is services at 18%
Eg 721: Taxpayer reversing ITC under Section 17(5)(h) cannot be excused for non-payment of output tax on disposal of inputs
28.2.14 — Statement Before Police Officer | Section 24 | Sec 70 CGST
⬆ Index
STATEMENT BEFORE POLICE OFFICER vs PROPER OFFICER
Tofan Singh v. State of Tamil Nadu (Cr.Apl.) 152/2013 — Proper Officer in GST is NOT a Police Officer
SECTION 24 PROTECTION — STATEMENT BARRED
Section 24 denies statement made before Police Officer admission as evidence in trial. This acknowledges 'threat, inducement or perception of use of force' in securing statements. Since Proper Officer in GST is NOT Police Officer (Tofan Singh) — Sections 24–30 exclusion does NOT avail in respect of statements made before Proper Officer in GST — whether during audit under Section 65 or inquiry under Section 67 read with summons under Section 70 CGST Act.
📝 Statements recorded before GST officers under Section 70 are NOT protected by Section 24 Evidence Act. But they are still the WEAKEST form of evidence — because they may contain interpretation errors. Statement on oath can be wrong if the person making it has a wrong understanding of the legal interpretation.
SECTION 70 STATEMENT — WEAKEST EVIDENCE
Statement recorded under Section 70 = the WEAKEST form of evidence. Allegation in GST = interpretation of facts AND application of statutory provision to arrive at tax treatment. Any person making statement — even under oath and with full confidence — CAN BE WRONG about interpretation of this new law.
Such statements admissible if and only if opportunity of cross-examination is afforded in adjudication before admitting as true representation.
Omission to demand opportunity of cross-examination = only raises presumption in favour of Revenue's tax treatment — not conclusive proof.
📝 If you made a statement under Section 70 that was later wrong — you can challenge it. The statement has no conclusive probative value unless Revenue provides opportunity of cross-examination in adjudication. Always demand this right under Section 67(10) CGST read with Section 165(5) CrPC via Commissioner's equivalent powers.
ADMISSION vs CONFESSION
Admission = any 'ingredient' of offence
Confession = entire 'offence' admitted
Confession requires precise understanding of (i) ALL ingredients making up the offence AND (ii) quality of evidence needed to demonstrate existence of every one of those ingredients.
Bald confessions lack probative value — 'no person can offer to be accused' without having committed the offence.
Admissions must be recorded before Magistrate under Section 164 CrPC.
📝 During investigation, if a GST officer extracts a 'statement' that taxpayer 'admits liability' — examine carefully: Is it an admission of ONE ingredient (like 'yes I received money') or a confession of entire offence? Admission of receipt of money does NOT constitute admission of taxable supply unless ALL other ingredients are also established.
28.3.1–28.3.3 — Prosecution · Purpose of Arrest · Purpose of Trial
⬆ Index
CRIMINAL PROCEDURE — PROSECUTION · ARREST · TRIAL
GST prosecution tried by Judicial Magistrate of First Class — Section 134 CGST
28.3.1 PROSECUTION
'Prosecution' = follow course prescribed — NOT criminal trial alone. Statement under Section 70 tempered with 'reliability' only to extent admissible per Section 136 CGST. Admissibility of evidence = sine qua non — cannot go untested. No 'assumption of offence.'
Without DISCLOSURE of investigative material — adjudication can be assailed for being 'conducted in the dark' — findings perverted by non-disclosure.
Cross-examination demand under Section 67(10) read with Section 165(5) CrPC via Commissioner's powers = mandatory disclosure of all investigative material before adjudication.
📝 Right to cross-examine witnesses/sources is fundamental. If Revenue relies on third-party information (like DGARM data, ITC mismatch list) — taxpayer has right to demand this material and cross-examine its basis before it is used against taxpayer in adjudication.
28.3.2 PURPOSE OF ARREST
Arrest = to establish identity of accused AND THEN conduct investigation. Arrest is NOT commencement of sentence. Pre-trial detention subject to bail. Statutory bail after 90 days even in capital offence cases if charge-sheet not filed. Right to life (Article 21) cannot be rendered illusory even to accused.
PRE-TRIAL REMAND
Only until preliminary investigation completed — for collection of evidence OR securing deposition of material witnesses.
Risk of inducement to witnesses may be grounds for remand — but this risk CANNOT last entire trial duration.
As soon as necessary evidence from witnesses secured — risk stands de-risked and remand must end.
💡 GST arrest challenge: If arrested for GST offence — demand: (a) What is the specific evidence collection purpose? (b) Once that evidence is collected — release must follow. (c) 90-day statutory bail right is absolute if charge-sheet not filed. (d) Article 21 protections apply to GST accused.
28.3.3 PURPOSE OF TRIAL
Six-step process: (1) Private complaint or FIR → (2) Police report → (3) Charge-sheet → (4) Framing of charges → (5) Trial → (6) Judgment.
GST equivalent: (1) Audit enquiry → (2) Findings (ADT-2) → (3) Allegations with evidence (SCN) → (4) Evaluation of evidence to support allegations → (5) Findings on allegations → (6) Order.
📝 This GST-CrPC parallel is the most powerful tool. At SCN stage (step 3) — Revenue must ALLEGE with EVIDENCE. At adjudication (step 4-5) — Revenue must EVALUATE and FIND. Order without proper findings on EACH allegation = infirm order. Taxpayer can challenge for non-application of mind.
28.3.4–28.3.6 — Admission of Liability · Curative Power · Disclosure of Investigative Material
⬆ Index
ADMISSION · CURATIVE POWER · DISCLOSURE
Section 240 CrPC — Framing of charge | Section 222 CrPC — No GST equivalent
28.3.4 ADMISSION / DENIAL OF OFFENCE
Section 240 CrPC: Accused must be 'put at notice' on exact nature of charges/allegations to answer. Accused is NOT obliged to 'prove innocence' — only to DECLARE position: accept or reject charges. Evidence of rebuttal required only AFTER prosecution has entered evidence and made out strong case. If prosecution case not made out — accused not obliged to introduce any rebuttal.
GST parallel: Position of taxpayer = 'accept or reject' allegations so Revenue can first establish its case.
📝 At personal hearing before FAA/GSTAT: Taxpayer need only state their position (accept or dispute each allegation). Revenue must then DEMONSTRATE prima facie case. Only after that does taxpayer need to present detailed rebuttal. Do NOT present your entire case before Revenue has shown its hand.
28.3.5 CURATIVE POWER
Section 222 CrPC: When offence proved is included in offence charged — person may be convicted of minor offence though not specifically charged.
NO GST EQUIVALENT in Section 75 CGST Act.
Section 75(7) CGST = absolute SAFEGUARD: Demand for tax must show ALL taxing ingredients. Sweeping statements insufficient. Cannot change classification/rate after SCN.
Taxpayer who fails to object to curative attempts in adjudication orders — imperil their reply and forego this relief.
💡 Eg 724–726: SCN demanding GST at 18% on 'other income,' staff welfare expenses, creditors balance = demand fails entirely at adjudication for non-showing of taxing ingredients. These cannot be 'cured' by adjudicating officer under correct classification. Section 75(7) = absolute bar. This ends the demand.
28.3.6 DISCLOSURE OF INVESTIGATIVE MATERIAL
Magistrate OBLIGED to allow accused examination of ALL investigative material prosecution relies upon during trial. Section 165 CrPC applies to GST searches (Section 67(2)) by designation of Commissioner equivalent to person in Section 165(5). GST makes it MANDATORY for adjudication to commence only after disclosure of all investigative material in any search proceedings under Section 67(2) CGST.
Key cases: State of Rajasthan v. Rehman AIR 1960 SC 210 | S. Narayanappa v. CIT AIR 1967 SC 523 | CCT v. RS Jhaver AIR 1968 SC 59.
📝 If Revenue searched your premises under Section 67(2) — they MUST disclose all material collected during that search to you BEFORE adjudication begins. Adjudication without disclosure = adjudication 'in the dark' = can be challenged as violating natural justice and Section 165(5) CrPC read with CGST.
28.4.1 — Self-Incrimination | Article 20(3) | Tofan Singh
⬆ Index
SELF-INCRIMINATION — ARTICLE 20(3)
No person accused of offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself — Miranda rights parallel
ARTICLE 20 — THREE PROTECTIONS
(1) No conviction except for violation of law in force at time of commission of act charged — no penalty greater than that prescribable at time of commission
(2) No person shall be prosecuted and punished for same offence more than once (double jeopardy)
(3) No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be witness against himself (self-incrimination)
TOFAN SINGH — INAPPLICABILITY IN TAXATION
Rule against self-incrimination does NOT apply to taxation matters as held in Tofan Singh v. State of Tamil Nadu (Cr.Apl.) 152/2013.
BUT: taxpayers must be vigilant not to make admission of interpretation of law or facts.
📝 Self-incrimination protection doesn't apply in tax — but INADVERTENT ADMISSION protection is your equivalent shield. Don't admit interpretations of law. Don't admit facts that Revenue hasn't established. Make available contemporaneous records — but nothing more.
SECTION 70 STATEMENT — WEAKEST EVIDENCE (AGAIN)
Statement recorded under Section 70 = weakest form of evidence. Allegation in GST = interpretation of facts AND application of statutory provision. Any person making statement — even truthfully — CAN be wrong on interpretation.
Such statements admissible only if opportunity of cross-examination afforded in adjudication. Statements made and relied upon are admissible if and only if cross-examination opportunity is afforded.
Taxpayer has no responsibility to admit any interpretation of law or facts proposed by any proceedings.
💡 During summons under Section 70: Make factual statements truthfully. But NEVER admit to legal conclusions like 'Yes, this is taxable' or 'Yes, I have suppressed this.' Those are legal interpretations — not facts. Leave legal conclusions for the adjudication forum — not summons proceedings.
28.4.2 — Death of Taxpayer | Section 93 CGST
⬆ Index
DEATH OF TAXPAYER — SECTION 93 CGST
'Death abates all things' — Order 22 Rule 1 CPC — but Section 93 CGST carves important exceptions
SECTION 93 — TWO SCENARIOS
(a) Business CONTINUED after death by legal representative or other person → such person SHALL be liable to pay tax, interest or penalty due from deceased
(b) Business DISCONTINUED (before or after death) → legal representative liable to pay — out of estate of deceased — to extent estate is capable of meeting the charge
Applies whether liability determined before or after death.
CRITICAL LACUNAE IN CGST
There is NOTHING in Section 75 CGST that permits notice to be served on 'legal heirs.' And there is NO provision in Section 107 or 112 CGST for legal heirs to prefer appeal in respect of demands confirmed against deceased taxpayer.
Notice delivered to legal heirs under Section 169 = constructive service on taxpayer — but delivering to legal heirs as constructive service to taxpayer is NOT the same as serving notice 'on behalf of' taxpayer to legal heirs.
📝 If a taxpayer dies mid-proceeding — Revenue faces significant procedural hurdles. Notice cannot be served 'on legal heirs' under Section 75 — only constructive notice to taxpayer via legal heirs under Section 169. Legal heirs have NO statutory right to appeal under Sections 107/112. To protect themselves, legal heirs should proactively appear in proceedings, obtain temporary login credentials and file appeal — or challenge dismissal for want of locus standi.
28.4.3 — Key Authorities | Search · Natural Justice · Opinion Formation
⬆ Index
KEY JUDICIAL AUTHORITIES — SEARCH & NATURAL JUSTICE
Eight landmark authorities — operational with full force in GST proceedings
Matajog Dobey v. HC Bhari AIR 1956 SC 44
"A discretionary power is not necessarily a discriminatory power and abuse of power is not easily to be assumed where the discretion is vested in the Government and not in a minor official."
S. Narayanappa v. CIT AIR 1967 SC 523
"The belief must be held in good faith: it cannot be merely a pretence. The reasons for the belief must have a rational connection or a relevant bearing to the formation of the belief and must not be extraneous or irrelevant to the purpose of the section."
📝 Before any search — Revenue must form a genuine belief (not mere pretence) that incriminating material exists. The REASONS for that belief must be rationally connected to the purpose of the search provision. Pretextual searches = infirm.
Barium Chemicals v. AJ Rana AIR 1972 SC 591
"The words 'consider is necessary' postulate that the authority concerned has thought over the matter deliberately and with care and it has been found necessary as a result of such thinking to pass the order. If the impugned order shows no careful thinking or proper application of mind — the essential requisite to the makings of the order would be held to be non-existent."
📝 Any search authorization, summons or adjudication order that shows no application of mind = non-existent as a matter of law. Revenue cannot rubber-stamp orders. Each order must show independent thinking. Boilerplate/template orders with no case-specific reasoning = infirm.
CCT v. RS Jhaver AIR 1968 SC 59
"Safeguards provided in Section 165 also apply to searches under sub-section (2). The empowered officer must have reasonable grounds for believing that anything necessary for recovery of tax may be found; he must be of opinion that such thing cannot be otherwise got without undue delay; he must record in writing grounds of his belief; and he must specify in writing so far as possible the thing for which search is to be made."
💡 FOUR CONDITIONS for valid GST search (Section 67(2)):
(1) Reasonable grounds for belief
(2) Cannot be otherwise obtained without undue delay
(3) Written recording of grounds of belief
(4) Written specification of the thing for which search is to be made
ALL four must be satisfied. Missing any ONE = search is infirm.
State of Rajasthan v. Rehman AIR 1960 SC 210
"Search is a process exceedingly arbitrary in character, stringent statutory conditions are imposed on the exercise of the power. Section 165 conditions govern searches." — All four conditions of Section 165 must be satisfied.
Rajesh Kumar v. Dy. CIT (2007) 2 SCC 181
"When by reason of action of a statutory authority, civil or evil consequence ensue — principles of natural justice are required to be followed. In case of denial of principles of natural justice in a statute, the same may also be held to be ultra vires Article 14 of the Constitution."
Narayan Govind Gavate v. State of Maharashtra AIR 1977 SC 183
Opinion/satisfaction of authority can be challenged on three grounds ONLY:
(1) Mala fides
(2) Authority did not apply its mind to material on which it formed opinion/satisfaction
(3) Material was so insufficient that no man could reasonably reach that conclusion
Court can interfere only where NO material at all or material SO insufficient that no man could reasonably reach that conclusion.
CIT v. Scindia Steam Navigation Co. Ltd. 1961 AIR SC 1633
"No mandamus will be issued unless the applicant had made a distinct demand on the appropriate authorities for the very reliefs which he seeks to enforce by mandamus and that had been refused."
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 28 | Criminal Laws | Evidence & Procedure ⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, CESTAT and in Written Submissions. State them confidently and cite the authority.
Revenue Cannot Make Adverse Inference from Taxpayer's Silence | Self-Assessment is Sufficient Disclosure
Para 28.2.3 | Example 704 | CBIC Position
Key Ratio
Revenue cannot make 'adverse inference' from taxpayer's silence after returns are filed and contemporaneous books and records are made available. Interpreting information made available is Revenue's responsibility. Supply cannot be inferred or presumed. In order to fasten any demand, supply must be shown to exist, not inferred, and can certainly not be shown by making adverse inference from taxpayer's omission, deliberate or not. ADT-2 issued 'since no justification with documentation furnished about other income appearing in financial statements' — this demand is contrary to law and omission to offer 'satisfactory' explanation is not sufficient to support the demand.
When to Use — When SCN or adjudication order is based on taxpayer's failure to explain 'other income' or reconciliation differences. Cite to establish: Returns filed = disclosure complete. Revenue must independently investigate and prove supply — cannot extract the investigation from taxpayer. Entire demand based on 'taxpayer did not explain' = fails at threshold.
Four Ingredients for Mala Fides — ALL Must Co-Exist | Section 74 Jurisdiction Test
Para 28.2.3 | Section 74 CGST Act | Four-ingredient Test
Key Ratio
To impute mala fides in taxpayer's omission to maintain or produce information, the following four ingredients are necessary: (a) Non-payment of some tax or claim of some credit or refund; (b) Knowledge about said tax or inadmissibility to credit or refund; (c) Use of device to render detection impossible or cumbersome; and (d) Gains from this misadventure shown to arise to taxpayer. All four must exist simultaneously. Deficiencies in record-keeping does not ipsi dixit imply fraud or suppression. Active concealment is different from passive silence.
When to Use — When SCN invokes Section 74 (fraud/suppression) without establishing all four ingredients. Argue: Missing even ONE ingredient = no mala fides = Section 74 inapplicable = only Section 73 can apply (shorter limitation, lower penalty). Revenue must SPECIFICALLY allege and PROVE each of the four ingredients — general allegation of 'suppression' without specifics = infirm invocation of Section 74.
Section 75(7) — Demand Must Show ALL Taxing Ingredients | Wrong HSN Cannot Be Corrected in Adjudication
Para 28.2.13 | Section 75(7) CGST Act | Examples 722, 723
Key Ratio
Demand for tax must show all the taxing ingredients needed to attract the incidence. It does not suffice that sweeping statements are made as if that would be sufficient to support the demand. GST demanded at 18% on 'other income' is a failure of demand which cannot be saved in adjudication or appeal for omission to show the taxing ingredients needed to attract the incidence of tax. Failure of demand can conclude with demand being dropped in later proceedings and no tax being recoverable at all due to lapse of limitation to issue a revised SCN. SCN issued demanding payment of output tax under HSN 8704 cannot be confirmed in adjudication under HSN 9971 — this would fall foul of Section 75(7).
When to Use — In every SCN reply and appeal — specifically plead: 'The SCN fails to establish each of the taxing ingredients required under Section 7(1)(a) read with Section 9 of CGST Act. Sweeping demand without establishing: (i) supply (ii) by taxable person (iii) in course of furtherance of business (iv) against consideration fails at threshold. Section 75(7) bars confirmation of demand on different grounds than raised in SCN.'
S. Narayanappa v. CIT AIR 1967 SC 523 — Belief Must Have Rational Connection | Search Cannot Be Pretextual
Supreme Court of India | 1967 | AIR SC 523 | Para 28.4.3
Key Ratio
The belief must be held in good faith: it cannot be merely a pretence. To put it differently it is open to the Court to examine the question whether the reasons for the belief have a rational connection or a relevant bearing to the formation of the belief and, are not extraneous or irrelevant to the purpose of the section.
When to Use — Challenging validity of search/seizure under Section 67(2) or summons under Section 70 where the 'belief' or 'reasons' of the authorizing officer are not discernible or are pretextual. Cite to demand disclosure of reasons for belief recorded in writing. If reasons shown are irrelevant to purpose of section — entire search action falls. Also apply to adjudication orders that show no application of mind.
CCT v. RS Jhaver AIR 1968 SC 59 — Four Conditions for Valid Search | Section 165 CrPC Governs GST Search
Supreme Court of India | 1968 | AIR SC 59 | Para 28.4.3 | Para 28.3.6
Key Ratio
Safeguards provided in Section 165 of CrPC also apply to searches made under sub-section (2) of Section 67 CGST Act. These safeguards are: (i) the empowered officer must have reasonable ground for believing that anything necessary for the purpose of recovery of tax may be found in any place within the jurisdiction; (ii) he must be of the opinion that such thing cannot be otherwise got without undue delay; (iii) he must record in writing the grounds of his belief; and (iv) he must specify in such writing so far as possible the thing for which search is to be made. The power to search under sub-section (2) is not arbitrary.
When to Use — When challenging search and seizure conducted under Section 67(2) CGST Act. Demand production of: (a) Written record of grounds of belief (b) Specification in writing of thing for which search was made (c) Show that officer independently formed the belief (not on dictation of superiors). If ANY of these four conditions are not satisfied — the search is infirm and all material collected = tainted evidence = cannot support demand.
Barium Chemicals v. AJ Rana AIR 1972 SC 591 — Application of Mind | Template Orders = Non-Existent
Supreme Court of India | 1972 | AIR SC 591 | Para 28.4.3
Key Ratio
The words 'considers is necessary' postulate that the authority concerned has thought over the matter deliberately and with care and it has been found necessary as a result of such thinking to pass the order. If the impugned order were to show that there has been no careful thinking or proper application of mind as to the necessity of obtaining and examining the documents specified in the order, the essential requisite to the makings of the order would be held to be non-existent.
When to Use — Where adjudication order, search authorization, summons, or any official order is clearly a template with no case-specific reasoning. Argue: No application of mind = order non-existent in law. In appeal against ex-parte adjudication orders — cite to show essential pre-condition (proper evaluation of evidence) was not satisfied — order must be set aside for non-compliance with principles of natural justice.
Presumption of Innocence in Self-Assessment | Section 74 Cannot Apply to Bona Fide Interpretation
Para 28.2.5 | Section 4 Indian Evidence Act | Chuharmal Mohanani v. CIT AIR 1988 SC 1384
Key Ratio
Self-assessment takes within itself presumption about bona fides of taxpayer's interpretation of law. There is no assurance of accuracy in self-assessment — it is for this reason that penalties are applicable on detection of erroneous discharge of liability due to mala fides. When a certain interpretation is accepted by Courts and is overturned by a later decision of a Superior Court, remains a demand under Section 73 and no allegation of existence of special circumstances can be alleged to demand under Section 74. As such, there is a presumption of innocence when bona fide view of this law is entertained in self-assessment. Imputation of mala fides cannot be lightly made, and assumption of guilt is nowhere within reckoning.
When to Use — When Revenue invokes Section 74 despite taxpayer's interpretation having judicial support (even from any High Court, CESTAT, AAR or AAAR). Cite to argue: Bona fide interpretation supported by any judicial authority = presumption of innocence = Section 74 inapplicable = only Section 73 (3 year limitation, Section 73(5) reduced penalty). Demand Revenue to prove MALA FIDES specifically — not just wrong interpretation.
Statement Under Section 70 — Weakest Evidence | Cross-Examination Must Be Afforded
Para 28.2.14 & 28.4.1 | Section 70 CGST Act | Tofan Singh v. State of Tamil Nadu Cr.Apl. 152/2013
Key Ratio
Statement recorded under Section 70 of Central GST Act is the weakest form of evidence that may be relied upon in support of allegation due to allegation in GST being an interpretation of facts and application of statutory provision to arrive at a particular tax treatment. Any person making a statement, even under oath and with full confidence in the correctness of the statements being made truthfully, can be wrong when it comes to interpretation of this new law. Such statements are admissible if and only if opportunity of cross-examination is afforded in adjudication before admitting those statements to have any probative value.
When to Use — When Revenue relies on Section 70 statement as primary evidence for demand. Argue: (a) Statement = weakest evidence (b) Demand cross-examination opportunity under Section 67(10) read with Section 165(5) CrPC (c) Until cross-examination provided — statement has NO probative value (d) Any admissions in statement are of interpretation of law — not of facts — and interpretation can be wrong. Revenue cannot make demand primarily on basis of Section 70 statement without corroborating evidence.
Rajesh Kumar v. Dy. CIT (2007) 2 SCC 181 — Natural Justice Implied Even Without Express Provision
Supreme Court of India | 2007 | 2 SCC 181 | Para 28.4.3
Key Ratio
When by reason of action of the part of a statutory authority, civil or evil consequence ensue, principles of natural justice are required to be followed. In such an event, although no express provisions is laid down in this behalf, compliance of principles of natural justice would be implicit. In case of denial of principles of natural justice in a statute, the same may also be held to be ultra vires Article 14 of the Constitution.
When to Use — In any GST proceeding where taxpayer is denied: (a) Opportunity to hear evidence relied upon (b) Cross-examination of witnesses (c) Access to search/seizure material before adjudication (d) Fair notice of exact allegations. Even if CGST Act has no express provision requiring natural justice in a specific context — cite Rajesh Kumar: natural justice is IMPLICIT in all quasi-judicial proceedings causing civil consequences. Denial = ultra vires Article 14.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | GST Litigation Practice
📖 Ch 29 — General Clauses Act, 1897

⚖️ Chapter 29 — General Clauses Act, 1897

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Saved
Prohibited / Void / Lost
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference / Statutory Text
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / Case
📋 Index of Sections
29.1 Relevance to GST
29.2.1 Interesting Definitions
29.2.2 Effective Date (Section 5)
29.2.3 Repeal and Saving (Section 6)
29.2.4 Prior Publication of Law (Section 23)
29.2.5 Meaning of 'Service' (Section 27)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
29.1 — Relevance to GST ⇧ Index
⚖️ General Clauses Act, 1897 — Relevance to GST
Article 367 of the Constitution | Interpretation of Central Acts
CONSTITUTIONAL REACH
Applicable even to interpret the Constitution of India as per Article 367
Cannot be overlooked in any deliberation on tax law
📝 When the Constitution itself uses a word that GCA defines — GCA meaning applies. This makes GCA one of the most powerful interpretive tools in any legal dispute including GST.
WHY IT MATTERS IN GST
Tax law being amended often → needs sound understanding of:
(i) 'Effective date' of amendments
(ii) Nature of amendment proposed
📝 GST amendments happen frequently. Whether a GST amendment applies from today, tomorrow, or retrospectively is determined by GCA principles — not by assumptions.
CRITICAL DISTINCTION
Effects on matters of substantive law are NOT the same as effects on matters of procedure that are amended
📝 Amending a rule about refund procedure = can apply to past claims. Amending a substantive right (like ITC eligibility) = cannot take away vested rights. GCA draws this line clearly.
29.2.1 — Interesting Definitions (Section 3) ⇧ Index
📖 Key Definitions — Section 3, General Clauses Act, 1897
Statutory meanings that eliminate common preconceptions
📝 Knowing how a statute defines a certain expression helps overcome the more common yet inapplicable understanding that is often ascribed in the course of interpretation of statutes. These definitions apply across all Central Acts — including GST.
Section 3
Sub-clause		 Term Defined Statutory Definition GST Relevance / Plain English
3(18) "Document" Shall include any matter written, expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means which is intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter 📝 Extremely broad. Any writing on any surface = document. In GST — an e-way bill on a phone screen, a WhatsApp message confirming a supply, a sticky note with price — all are 'documents.'
3(21) "Financial Year" Shall mean the year commencing on the first day of April 📝 April 1 to March 31. This is the standard for GST annual return period, audit period, and limitation calculations involving 'financial year.'
3(22) "Good Faith" A thing shall be deemed to be done in "good faith" when it is in fact done honestly, whether it is done negligently or not ⚠️ Critical for penalty defence. Negligent conduct can still be in 'good faith' if done honestly. This means bona fide mistakes (honest but negligent) can attract good faith defence against penalties and prosecution.
3(26) "Immovable Property" Shall include land, benefits to arise out of land, and things attached to the earth, or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth ⚠️ Consistent with Registration Act and TPA. Benefits to arise out of land (mining rights, fishing rights) = immovable property. ITC blockage on attached plant and machinery flows from this definition.
3(29) "Indian Law" Shall mean any Act, Ordinance, Regulation, rule, order, bye-law or other instrument which had the force of law in any Province of India or Part A/Part C State — but does not include any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom or any Order in Council made under such Act 📝 Clarifies 'Indian law' vs. foreign/colonial law. Relevant when interpreting references to 'law in force' in GST exemption notifications — 'law' means Indian law only.
3(35) "Month" Shall mean a month reckoned according to the British calendar 📝 Calendar month (January = 31 days, February = 28/29 days etc.) — NOT a 30-day period. Limitation periods in GST expressed in months use calendar months. E.g., 'within 3 months' = 3 calendar months from trigger date.
3(36) "Movable Property" Shall mean property of every description, except immovable property 📝 Residual definition — everything that is not immovable property = movable property. Goods under GST are movable property (with some exceptions for attached plant and machinery).
3(42) "Person" Shall include any company or association or body of individuals, whether incorporated or not ⚠️ Very wide. Unincorporated associations, clubs, AOPs, BOIs — all are 'persons' under GST. No entity can escape GST liability by claiming it is not a 'person.'
3(51) "Rule" Shall mean a rule made in exercise of a power conferred by any enactment, and shall include a Regulation made as a rule under any enactment 📝 CGST Rules = 'rules' under this definition. A rule cannot exceed the power conferred by the parent Act (CGST Act). Any rule that exceeds the Act's power = ultra vires.
