3(54) "Section" Shall mean a section of the Act or Regulation in which the word occurs 📝 When a notification or rule says 'under Section X' — it refers to that section of the parent Act being administered. Avoids ambiguity in cross-references.
3(66) "Year" Shall mean a year reckoned according to the British calendar 📝 Calendar year (Jan–Dec) vs. Financial year (Apr–Mar). When GST law says 'year,' it means calendar year unless context clearly indicates financial year. Know which one applies.
29.2.2 — Effective Date of Enactments (Section 5) ⇧ Index
📅 Effective Date — When Does a Law/Amendment Actually Take Effect?
Section 5, General Clauses Act, 1897
LEGISLATIVE PROCESS
Introduction of Bill → Reading in Lower House (Lok Sabha) → Reading in Upper House (Rajya Sabha) → Presidential Assent
Date affixed on an Act or notification giving its effect must be understood to be the day before — and NOT the date of publication or notification
📝 If a GST amendment notification says it is effective from "1st January 2024" — it comes into operation at the end of 31st December 2023 (midnight). Practically, this means the law operates from the start of 1st January 2024. The publication date and effective date are NOT the same.
§5(1) — NO DATE SPECIFIED
Where any Central Act is NOT expressed to come into operation on a particular day → it shall come into operation on the day on which it receives Presidential assent
📝 If Parliament passes an Act but does not specify a commencement date → it becomes law the moment the President signs it. Automatic commencement on assent.
§5(3) — CONSTRUCTION RULE
Unless the contrary is expressed → a Central Act or Regulation shall be construed as coming into operation immediately on the expiration of the day preceding its commencement
📝 Law effective from "1st April" = comes into force at the stroke of midnight ending 31st March = beginning of 1st April. This is the precise legal moment of commencement. Important for limitation calculations and transitional supply treatment.
✓ GST APPLICATION
Effective date determines: (i) Which GST rate applies to a supply (ii) Whether an exemption applies (iii) Whether an ITC restriction is in force (iv) Whether a limitation period has commenced
Getting the effective date wrong = wrong tax position = potential demand + interest + penalty
💡 GST rate change notification issued on 30th September 2023 — effective from 1st October 2023. Under Section 5(3), the new rate operates from the expiration of 30th September = start of 1st October 2023. A supply on 30th September midnight = old rate. A supply on 1st October 00:01 = new rate.
29.2.3 — Repeal and Saving (Section 6) ⇧ Index
🔄 Repeal and Saving — Vested Rights Survive Repeal
Section 6, General Clauses Act, 1897
📝 When a law is repealed or a rule is amended — the old law doesn't simply vanish. Vested rights, obligations, liabilities, and pending proceedings under the old law survive. Section 6 is the automatic saving clause in all Indian legislation.
CORE PRINCIPLE — SECTION 6
Repeal of a legislation/sub-ordinate rule/notification also involves determination of the date it is to take effect.
Vested rights cannot be obliterated by such repeal
'Repeal' involves 'saving' of vested rights and liabilities too
5 Things a Repeal SHALL NOT Affect — Section 6(a) to (e)
§6(a)
Revive anything NOT in force or existing at the time repeal takes effect
§6(b)
Affect the previous operation of any enactment so repealed or anything duly done or suffered thereunder
§6(c)
Affect any right, privilege, obligation or liability acquired, accrued or incurred under any enactment so repealed
§6(d)
Affect any penalty, forfeiture or punishment incurred in respect of any offence committed against any enactment so repealed
§6(e)
Affect any investigation, legal proceeding or remedy — any such proceeding may be instituted, continued or enforced, and any penalty may be imposed as if the repealing Act had not been passed
⚠️ RIGHT TO APPEAL — VESTED RIGHT IN GST LAW
Right to appeal is a vested right in GST law
Non-filing within statutory limitation OR non-payment of pre-deposit → renders appeal non est (as if never filed)
Legislature will NOT labour to save this right against the Appellant's will
To appeal = right, but fraught with pre-conditions (limitation + pre-deposit)
📝 Once the right to appeal vests (i.e., an adverse order is passed), Section 6 protects that right even if the appeal provision is subsequently amended. But the taxpayer must exercise the right within time and pay pre-deposit — otherwise the right lapses non est.
Refund / ITC Rules Amended — Which Version Applies?
(e.g. Amendment to Rule 89(5) or Rule 96B)
Rights Accrued Accrued Rights Availed Which Law Governs
Before amendment Before amendment Old law — vested right fully preserved
Before amendment After amendment Old law for substantive right — but new procedural law may govern how the right is exercised
After amendment After amendment New law — no vested right under old law
💡 Hoosein Kasam Dada (India) Ltd. v. State of MP — AIR 1953 SC 221: Matters of substantive alteration of rights cannot disturb vested rights.
Garikapatti Veeraya v. N. Subbiah Choudhury — AIR 1957 SC 488: Vested rights can be affected by rules of procedure — but not substantive rights.
29.2.4 — Prior Publication of Law (Section 23) ⇧ Index
📰 Prior Publication — Rules Must Be Published Before Enforcement
Section 23, General Clauses Act, 1897
CORE PRINCIPLE
Failure to adhere to applicable law cannot be established without first showing that the said law was duly informed to those liable to adhere
📝 You cannot penalise someone for violating a rule that was never properly published or communicated to them. Prior publication is a pre-condition to enforcement. This is a fundamental rule of natural justice embedded in the statute.
5 Requirements Under Section 23 — Prior Publication Process
§23(1)
Authority having power to make rules shall, before making them, publish a draft for information of persons likely to be affected
§23(2)
Publication shall be in such manner that authority deems sufficient, or as Government concerned prescribes
§23(3)
A notice shall be published specifying a date on or after which the draft will be taken into consideration
§23(4)
Authority shall consider any objection or suggestion received from any person before the date specified
§23(5)
Publication in the Official Gazette of a rule purporting to have been made after previous publication shall be conclusive proof that the rule has been duly made
⚠️ 'REASONS TO BELIEVE' & 'PRIOR AUTHORIZATION' — GST SAFEGUARDS
Where law lays down requirements such as 'for reasons to believe' or 'with prior authorization' → even bona fide exercise of authority will be tainted with illegality if:
(i) No such reason recorded, or
(ii) Without said authorization

GST lays down such safeguards against enthusiastic exercise of authority contrary to law.
Taxpayers are within their rights to demand justice by calling into question the satisfaction of those pre-conditions by Executives suspected of acting in excess of authority conferred by Legislature.
📝 If a GST officer conducts a search 'with reasons to believe' but has NOT recorded those reasons in writing before the search — the entire search is illegal even if the officer genuinely believed there was evasion. Taxpayers can challenge the very foundation of the action. The pre-condition must be satisfied BEFORE the action, not explained after.
💡 Section 67 CGST Act — search and seizure 'with reasons to believe.' If Joint Commissioner issues authorization without recording reasons, the search is without authority of law. All material seized = inadmissible. Demand based on such search = legally unsound at its foundation.
29.2.5 — Meaning of 'Service' of Notices (Section 27) ⇧ Index
📬 Meaning of 'Service' — Valid Communication of Notices & Orders
Section 27 GCA | Section 155 & 169 Central GST Act
SECTION 155 CGST ACT — VALIDITY OF SERVICE
Validity is NOT limited to 'mode' of service but also the selection of the one among 14 available modes in Section 169 of CGST Act such that it meets the ends of justice — that is, to put the Party at notice (or communication or order)
📝 Section 169 CGST Act lists 14 modes of service of notice. But just using any mode is not enough — the mode chosen must actually put the taxpayer on notice. Revenue cannot choose an obscure or impossible mode and then claim the taxpayer was duly served.
SECTION 27 GCA — SERVICE BY POST
Where any Central Act authorizes/requires any document to be served by post → the service shall be deemed to be effected by properly addressing, pre-paying and posting by registered post a letter containing the document → and unless the contrary is proved, to have been effected at the time at which the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of post
📝 If Revenue sends a notice by registered post to the correct address — it is deemed served when it would normally be delivered. Revenue does not need to prove actual delivery. BUT the taxpayer can rebut this by proving non-delivery.
⚠️ REVENUE'S RESPONSIBILITY — CANNOT WASH HANDS OFF NOTICE
Revenue cannot discharge its duty by merely putting it in transmission.
Revenue remains responsible to show that it has been 'duly communicated'.
Party who is not well-versed with computers and use of Common Portal, due to nature of business involved in, cannot be expected to rapidly upgrade skills to accept electronic service.
Choice of mode of service exposes the bona fides of Revenue's actions.
Revenue cannot 'wash its hands off' the notice after having sent an e-mail or by uploading on Common Portal.
📝 Just uploading a notice on the GST Common Portal or sending an email does NOT mean the taxpayer was duly served — especially if the taxpayer has no habit of checking the portal. Revenue must ensure actual communication, particularly when limitation and penalty consequences flow from the notice date.
DATE OF SERVICE vs MODE OF SERVICE
Date of service = question of fact
Selection of unconscionable and shocking mode of service = question of law that begs to be redressed
Purpose of notice = to set the law in motion → one that cannot be lightly discharged
Especially when rights to put forward a defence (or prefer appeal) can be lost due to lapse of limitation
📝 If Revenue deliberately uses an ineffective mode (like uploading on portal when it knows the taxpayer doesn't have internet), the date of deemed service can be challenged as a question of law — not just fact. This is powerful in appeal proceedings where limitation is disputed.
NATURAL JUSTICE — APPELLATE REMEDY DOES NOT CURE DEFECT AT ORIGINAL STAGE
"If one accepts the contention that a defect of natural justice in the trial body can be cured by the presence of natural justice in the appellate body this has the result of depriving the member of his right of appeal from the expelling body. If the rules and the law combine to give the member the right to a fair trial and the right of appeal, why should he be told that he ought to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal? Even if the appeal is treated as a hearing de novo, the member is being stripped of his right to appeal to another body from the effective decision to expel him."
📝 In plain terms: getting a fair appeal does not undo the injustice of a defective original notice/hearing. The taxpayer had a right to both — a fair original proceeding AND a fair appeal. Denying fair hearing at original stage = loss of the right to appeal from that unfair decision, even if appellate stage is fair.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 29 | General Clauses Act ⇧ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the section/case name.
Good Faith Defence — Section 3(22) General Clauses Act
General Clauses Act, 1897 | Section 3(22) | Para 29.2.1
Key Statutory Definition
A thing shall be deemed to be done in "good faith" when it is in fact done honestly, whether it is done negligently or not. Negligent conduct can still be in 'good faith' if done honestly. Revenue cannot establish absence of good faith merely by showing carelessness or negligence — it must establish dishonesty or fraudulent intent.
When to Use — When Department levies penalty under Section 74 CGST Act (fraud, suppression, wilful misstatement) on a taxpayer who made a mistake but not dishonestly. Counter: Section 3(22) GCA defines good faith as honest conduct even if negligent. A bona fide error in ITC classification, rate application, or return filing = good faith = no Section 74 penalty. Section 73 (genuine mistake) applies, not Section 74.
Vested Rights Survive Repeal/Amendment — Section 6 General Clauses Act
General Clauses Act, 1897 | Section 6 | Hoosein Kasam Dada (India) Ltd. v. State of MP — AIR 1953 SC 221 | Garikapatti Veeraya v. N. Subbiah Choudhury — AIR 1957 SC 488
Key Ratio / Principle
Repeal of a legislation or a sub-ordinate rule or notification also involves determination of the date it is to take effect. However, vested rights cannot be obliterated by such repeal. As such, 'repeal' involves 'saving' of vested rights and liabilities too. Repeal shall not affect any right, privilege, obligation or liability acquired, accrued or incurred under any enactment so repealed, nor any investigation, legal proceeding or remedy — and any such investigation, legal proceeding or remedy may be instituted, continued or enforced as if the repealing Act had not been passed. Matters of substantive alteration of rights cannot disturb vested rights. But vested rights can be affected by rules of procedure.
When to Use — (i) When Department denies ITC refund claiming rule 89(5) was amended and the amended (restrictive) version applies to the pre-amendment claim. Counter: right to refund accrued before amendment — vested right — saved by Section 6 GCA. Old rule governs. (ii) When Department tries to invoke amended penalty provisions for acts done before the amendment. Counter: penalty provisions at the time of offence apply — saved by Section 6 GCA.
Effective Date — Act Operates from Day Preceding Commencement — Section 5(3) GCA
General Clauses Act, 1897 | Section 5(3) | Para 29.2.2
Key Principle
Date affixed on an Act or a notification giving its effect must be understood to be the day before and not the date of publication or notification. Unless the contrary is expressed, a Central Act or Regulation shall be construed as coming into operation immediately on the expiration of the day preceding its commencement. Consequentially effects on matters of substantive law are not the same as the effects on matters of procedure that are amended.
When to Use — (i) In rate change disputes — when supply straddles a rate change date and both parties argue which rate applies. The law operates from expiration of the day preceding commencement — i.e., from midnight. (ii) When Department applies an amendment retrospectively claiming it was 'effective from' a past date. Counter: effective date is construed as day preceding — not the date of Gazette publication.
Revenue Cannot 'Wash Its Hands Off' — Duly Communicated Service — Section 27 GCA
General Clauses Act, 1897 | Section 27 | Section 155 & 169 CGST Act | Para 29.2.5
Key Principle
Validity of service is not limited to 'mode' of service but also the selection of the one among fourteen available modes in section 169 of Central GST Act such that it meets the ends of justice — that is, to put the Party at notice. Revenue cannot discharge its duty by merely putting it in transmission but remains responsible to show that it has been 'duly communicated'. Revenue cannot 'wash its hands off' the notice after having sent an e-mail or by uploading on Common Portal. Date of service is a question of fact but the selection of unconscionable and shocking mode of service is a question of law that begs to be redressed — especially when rights to put forward a defence or prefer appeal can be lost due to lapse of limitation.
When to Use — (i) When Department claims notice was duly served by portal upload and limitation for appeal has expired. Counter: portal upload alone ≠ duly communicated. Revenue must show actual communication. Mode of service = question of law. Limitation cannot start from a non-communicative service. (ii) When taxpayer is not computer-savvy and Department used only electronic mode. Counter: party not well-versed with computers cannot be expected to rapidly upgrade skills to accept electronic service.
Natural Justice Defect at Original Stage Cannot Be Cured by Fair Appeal
Legal Principle | Para 29.2.5 | Natural Justice Doctrine
Key Ratio
If one accepts the contention that a defect of natural justice in the trial body can be cured by the presence of natural justice in the appellate body — this has the result of depriving the member of his right of appeal from the expelling body. If the rules and the law combine to give the member the right to a fair trial and the right of appeal, why should he be told that he ought to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal? Even if the appeal is treated as a hearing de novo, the member is being stripped of his right to appeal to another body from the effective decision to expel him.
When to Use — When Department/Appellate Authority argues that since the taxpayer had a fair hearing before the Commissioner (Appeals), any defect in the original SCN/adjudication (lack of opportunity, improper notice) is cured. Counter: taxpayer had a right to both a fair adjudication AND a fair appeal. Defective original proceedings cannot be cured at appellate stage — the right to challenge the original order from a position of having received fair hearing is lost forever.
'Reasons to Believe' and 'Prior Authorization' — Pre-Conditions Cannot Be Assumed
General Clauses Act 1897 | Section 23 | Section 67 CGST Act | Para 29.2.4
Key Principle
Failure to adhere to applicable law cannot be established without first showing that the said law was duly informed to those liable to adhere. Where the law lays down requirements such as 'for reasons to believe' or 'with prior authorization', even bona fide exercise of authority will be tainted with illegality if there are no such reasons recorded or without said authorization. GST lays down such safeguards against enthusiastic exercise of authority contrary to law. Taxpayers are within their rights to demand justice by calling into question the satisfaction of those pre-conditions by Executives suspected of acting in excess of authority conferred by Legislature.
When to Use — When Revenue conducts search, seizure, or survey without properly recording 'reasons to believe' (Section 67 CGST Act) or without proper prior authorization. Counter: bona fide exercise without recorded reasons = tainted with illegality. The pre-condition is not a formality — it is a safeguard. Taxpayer can demand to see the recorded reasons. If reasons are not contemporaneously recorded = entire action = ultra vires = all material gathered = legally inadmissible.
'Person' Includes Any Unincorporated Association — Section 3(42) GCA
General Clauses Act, 1897 | Section 3(42) | Para 29.2.1
Key Statutory Definition
"Person" shall include any company or association or body of individuals, whether incorporated or not. No entity can escape GST liability by claiming it is not a 'person.' Unincorporated associations, clubs, associations of persons, bodies of individuals — all are 'persons' and therefore 'taxable persons' if they make taxable supplies exceeding the threshold.
When to Use — (i) When an unregistered club, RWA, cooperative society, or AOP claims it is not a 'person' under GST and therefore not liable to register or pay GST. Counter: Section 3(42) GCA — 'person' includes any body of individuals whether incorporated or not. GST registration and liability apply. (ii) In disputes about whether a JV (joint venture) or AOP is a taxable person — GCA definition settles it in favour of Revenue's position that it is a taxable person.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
